facts: Georgia lawmakers revoked a $5,000 tax credit for no-emission vehicles in 2015.
In 2015, Georgia legislators imposed an over $200 annual registration fee for EV owners.
In 2023, Republican state lawmakers implemented a tax on public EV charging stations expected to take effect in 2027.
Congressional Republicans passed President Donald Trump’s “big, beautiful” taxation and budget bill in May that would end federal subsidies bolstering Georgia’s EV manufacturing industry.
If the Senate passes the bill and Trump signs it, the measure might slow EV sales while making owning a battery-powered vehicle more costly.
Republican-controlled Georgia General Assembly legislators are not eager to replace proposed federal rollbacks with state incentives.
State Sen. Steve Gooch (R-Dahlonega) stated that consumers should buy the vehicle of their choice without government subsidies.

State Sen. Frank Ginn, vice chairman of the Senate Economic Development and Tourism Committee, said the Legislature's plans depend on the state’s needs at the time but does not believe ending federal incentives will slow EV manufacturing production in Georgia.
Industry observers disagree with Ginn, saying removal of Biden-era incentives could hurt manufacturing projects across the state.
Ginn noted tax credits make sense to jump-start a new industry but companies must eventually navigate the market independently.
Supporters of EV subsidies fear that without new state incentive programs, Georgia could lose jobs and fall behind other states as an EV manufacturing hub.
State Sen. Emanuel Jones (D-Decatur), a car dealer, said it appears government is stepping away when it should be pushing the industry forward.
The reconciliation bill targets provisions from the Inflation Reduction Act signed by President Joe Biden in 2022.
Proposed rollbacks include sunsetting a $7,500 tax break for new U.S.-manufactured electric vehicles by the end of 2026.
The bill phases out production subsidies for manufacturers of batteries and other clean energy inputs.
The measure ends a tax credit for EV charging stations.
The bill imposes an annual $250 fee to compensate for taxes not paid by EV owners at the gas pump.
Georgia’s rise as an EV manufacturing hub began before the late 2022 passage of the Inflation Reduction Act.
Rivian announced plans in 2021 to build a factory 40 miles east of Atlanta.
Hyundai announced plans for its “Metaplant” outside of Savannah around the same time as Rivian's announcement.
Federal incentives have aided Georgia’s plan to become a clean technology hub, with the industry employing 82,000 Georgians as of 2024.
GOP state lawmakers and Gov. Brian Kemp have not expressed enthusiasm for government tax breaks despite federal subsidies accelerating EV and clean tech in Georgia.
Gov. Kemp aimed to make Georgia a leader in producing electric vehicles and parts but criticized the IRA as a “reckless tax and spending spree.”
Kemp stated the IRA disproportionately favors particular manufacturers by “picking winners and losers.”
If passed, Georgians would pay more when purchasing some new EVs.
Owners of battery-powered vehicles in Georgia would pay more than $450 annually between state and federal fees if the tax bill passes.
Longtime EV advocate Don Francis, who works at an e-mobility nonprofit, is conflicted about the congressional bill.
Francis acknowledged the EV tax credit should end eventually but argued the proposed annual fee is not comparable to gas pump taxes paid over a year.
Francis said the fees make consumers want to buy gas guzzlers to save money and are shortsighted as more people shift away from gas-powered vehicles.
The more than 1,000-page bill could change significantly in the Senate where Republicans have limited margin for lost votes.
Congressional Republicans hope to pass the legislation by July 4.
source_title: If Congress Cuts EV Credits, Could Georgia Pick Up the Slack?

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group Georgia EV Facility
facts: On May 20, 2022, Governor Brian P. Kemp and Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung announced that Hyundai Motor Group will open its first fully dedicated electric vehicle (EV) and battery manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group will invest $5.54 billion to open a state-of-the-art U.S. smart factory at the Bryan County Megasite.
Non-affiliated Hyundai Motor Group suppliers will invest approximately another $1 billion in the project.
The project will deliver approximately 8,100 new jobs to Georgia’s coastal region.
The megasite is a premier 2,923-acre site purchased jointly by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (JDA) and the State less than a year ago.
Construction on the new facility is expected to begin in January 2023.
Full production is expected in the first half of 2025, with an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Wages throughout the project will be well-paying advanced manufacturing positions competitive in the local market.
Hyundai Motor Group cited speed-to-market, workforce, and the state’s ability to meet the company’s carbon neutrality standards as reasons for choosing Georgia.
Georgia is home to an existing network of Hyundai subsidiaries and suppliers.
The Bryan County Megasite was developed by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA over several years in partnership with the state to reduce barriers to speedy operations.
Industrial utilities are adjacent to the site, and extensive due diligence reports have been completed.
Hyundai Motor Group is headquartered in Seoul, Korea, and employs 250,000 people worldwide.
The Group’s vehicles are sold in 193 countries through 5,000 dealerships and showrooms.
At the new Georgia facility, Hyundai Motor Group plans to produce a diverse range of innovative full electric vehicles, with details of production models to be shared later.
Through the battery manufacturing facility, the Group aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV battery and other EV components in the U.S. market.
When nearing the planned 2025 start of operations, Hyundai Motor Group will seek local vendors and suppliers.
Bryan County is the fastest-growing county in Georgia, and the strategic purchase of the Bryan County Megasite in 2021 was the largest in state history.
The site is adjacent to Interstate 16 with immediate access from I-95 and I-16 to 250 major metro areas.

The site is less than 30 miles from the Port of Savannah, the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S., with two Class I rail facilities on-site provided by Georgia Central Railway.
Georgia is the Southeastern leader for EV registrations per 1,000 registered automobiles and offers more EV charging outlets per capita than anywhere else in the Southeast.

The Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance is a public-private-nonprofit initiative focused on growing the electric mobility ecosystem in the state and strengthening Georgia’s position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) was represented by Project Manager Taylor Walden and Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim, along with the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA, Georgia DOT, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
Under Governor Kemp’s leadership, GDEcD has focused on putting Georgia at the forefront of electric mobility.

At the new facility, Hyundai Motor Group plans to implement advanced intelligent manufacturing technologies currently under test at the Group’s innovation hub, including intelligent A.I.-equipped manufacturing and data-driven processes.
The plant will support Hyundai Motor Group’s efforts to achieve its carbon neutrality target through the active use of renewable energy for the facility.
Since 2020, Georgia has announced more than 20 EV-related projects that will invest more than $13.54 billion and create nearly 18,000 jobs in the state.
Hyundai Motor Group's mobility brands include Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.

facts: Georgia’s mobility ecosystem connects innovative original equipment manufacturers and suppliers across the entire supply chain, producing and recycling materials that deliver an integrated, circular system.
As a rapidly growing e-mobility hub, Georgia is strategically expanding its critical minerals, battery production, automotive and aerospace manufacturing, and recycling sectors.
Through collaboration and innovation, the state fosters industry growth through strategic infrastructure updates and workforce development.
Georgia Tech ranks No. 1 for Energy among public universities and in the top 5 for materials science and engineering, according to U.S. News and World Report.
Georgia has 1,200 miles of federally designated EV-ready Alternative Fuel Corridors and 2,400+ stations according to the Department of Energy.
The Port of Brunswick is the leader in roll-on/roll-off cargo in the U.S., according to Georgia Ports Authority.
Efficient charging infrastructure is a key driver for electric vehicle adoption, and Georgia is committed to expanding EV infrastructure across the state.
Georgia-based EnviroSpark works across public and private sectors, making charging accessible to EV drivers nationwide.

The Electric Highway Coalition is an initiative between six major energy companies, including Southern Company, to connect drivers to a seamless network of charging stations along major highways, ensuring Georgia’s businesses, communities, and technology are prepared for the future.
Georgia is powering electric mobility technology through partnerships like The Ray Intelligent Highway.

Eighteen miles along Interstate 85 serves as a real-world testing facility and proving ground for safe and renewable transportation technology, providing insights into freight mobility through a program that has expanded nationwide.
Autonomous vehicle testing is underway in a variety of Georgia communities eager to advance mobility solutions.
The City of Chamblee has partnered with Georgia Tech to identify how Shared Autonomous Vehicles (SAVs) could affect public transit usage.
Gwinnett County and the City of Woodstock are both planning for how autonomous vehicles will affect streets and highways.
Peachtree Corner’s Curiosity Labs is a living smart city laboratory with a 1.5-mile intelligent highway for autonomous vehicle testing.
The publicly funded lab operates all-electric autonomous shuttles, e-scooters, a solar roadway in real-time and gathers data as the technology interacts with motorists, cyclists, and pedestrians.
By working to gather and analyze how the technology performs day to day, Curiosity Labs’ engineers continue to connect Georgia to the future.
Located in the heart of the Southeast, companies in Georgia benefit from proximity to major manufacturers and suppliers located in the state and surrounding area.
Our integrated supply chains built with producers and recyclers reduce companies’ reliance on global networks while encouraging collaboration and growth.
Georgia is partnering with neighboring states Alabama and Mississippi to advance the transportation network, through a cooperative known as MAGNET.

The University of Georgia, the University of Alabama, and Mississippi State University, along with Georgia Power, Alabama Power, and Mississippi Power, are partnering to communicate across academia, industry, and government and identify how a collaborative advanced automotive manufacturing ecosystem will drive the entire region forward.
Georgia’s proven record of investing early in resources, infrastructure, and workforce skills supports manufacturing’s future.

In keeping with this tradition, the Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (EMIA) was created to expand the state’s electric mobility ecosystem and strengthen its role in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.
The alliance includes partners from the private and public sectors, government agencies, electric utilities, education institutions, nonprofits, and other stakeholders.
source_title: Advanced Mobility | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp joined President and Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and President and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America José Muñoz to celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s historic impact on Georgia.
An update was provided on the progress of Hyundai Motor Group's Metaplant America facility.
The facility will officially start vehicle production in the fourth quarter of 2024.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai has been an incredible partner to the state, helping recruit businesses, giving back to communities, and working with colleges and universities to connect Georgians with opportunities.
Hyundai and Genesis electric vehicles on display included the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60, and Electrified GV70.
The hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck will be used for logistics needs between Hyundai’s manufacturing facilities and suppliers.
Governor Kemp officially declared February 26 as Hyundai Day throughout the state.
The General Assembly passed a Resolution commending Hyundai Motor Group’s dedication to Georgia and recognizing February 26 as Hyundai Day at the State Capitol.
José Muñoz stated that Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of great-paying American jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America project was announced in May 2022.
LG Energy Solution was named as the joint venture partner in the manufacture of battery components.
An additional expansion of the battery facility was announced.
Hyundai and SK On are building an EV battery manufacturing facility in Bartow County to supply Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. plants.
Since the initial announcement, Hyundai Motor Group’s planned investment in Georgia has reached more than $12.6 billion.
The planned investment will bring the creation of 12,000 direct, long-term jobs.

Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones and House Speaker Jon Burns attended the celebration along with legislative leaders, GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson, and officials from the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones applauded Governor Kemp, state agencies, and the University System of Georgia, especially Georgia Tech, for their partnership with Hyundai.
Lt. Governor Jones stated that stakeholder commitment enabled Hyundai's positive impact and creation of thousands of jobs, supporting Georgia's position as the number one state to do business.
Up to 300,000 Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia EVs are expected to be produced annually at the Metaplant.
17 HMGMA suppliers have announced projects across 10 Georgia counties spanning beyond southeast Georgia.
Further supplier announcements are expected.
Supplier facilities are expected to create more than 6,700 new jobs and more than $2.5 billion in investment.
Supplier facilities will be operational prior to the opening of HMGMA.
Speaker Jon Burns stated that Hyundai's Metaplant will have a generational impact as a force multiplier for investments in education, healthcare, housing, and transportation.
The Metaplant site is nearly 3,000 acres.
Four building exteriors are complete and outfitting for internal operations is underway.
The Metaplant’s architecture incorporates technologically advanced elements and design features representing the Savannah area, Hyundai’s brand identity, nature, and clean energy.
HMGMA's full-time employees, called "Meta Pros," are being hired rapidly for training on machinery, office/clerical work, and other roles.
HMGMA currently has 566 full-time employees.
Approximately 75 percent of the workforce is from the Greater Savannah area.
An additional 37 employees are from Georgia but outside the local area.
Georgia Quick Start broke ground on its customized training center at the Metaplant site on February 8, 2024.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the groundbreaking fulfilled a commitment to Hyundai and Georgians to train individuals in high-skill careers as Meta Pros.
HMGMA partnered with local technical colleges to prepare students for entry-level employment at the Metaplant.

Electrical Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate of Credit (EVP TCC) training is available at Coastal Pines Technical College’s Baxley and Waycross campuses, Southeastern Technical College’s Vidalia campus, Savannah Technical College, and Ogeechee Technical College.
TCSG Commissioner Greg Dozier stated that technical colleges are providing comprehensive training programs tailored to the growing EV industry in Georgia.

In September 2023, Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Company signed a memorandum of understanding to collaborate on research for sustainable mobility, including developing a hydrogen economy, workforce development, and smarter cities.
U.S. News & World Report ranked Georgia Tech as the top public university and No. 3 nationally in energy and fuel research.
Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera stated that the partnership will advance science powering key sectors and produce the next-generation workforce needed for these industries.
Since 1998, Hyundai has directed over $6 million towards local Georgia communities through philanthropic programs and partnerships.
The company is promoting STEM programs in local K-12 schools and hosting educational programs on alternative energy sources.
Hyundai supports local organizations dedicated to affordable homeownership and focuses on environmental stewardship.

Trip Tollison of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority stated that Hyundai has given more than $1 million to regional nonprofits and formed partnerships with local educational institutions.
Trip Tollison noted Hyundai committed to $7.59 billion in capital investment in HMGMA and will create 8,500 jobs.
More than 550 Meta Pros have already been hired, with 75 percent coming from the Savannah region.
Beginning with mass production, electricity used to power the Metaplant will come from 100 percent clean power sources.
This aligns with Hyundai’s commitment to carbon neutrality by 2045.
A 41-acre park will be featured in front of the plant, including trails, running tracks, environmental art displays, sports fields, and amenities for employees.
HMGMA is Hyundai Motor Group’s first dedicated Electric Vehicle mass-production plant located in Bryan County, Georgia.
The plant will utilize a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem.
All production processes including order collection, procurement, logistics, and production will be optimized utilizing AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system will create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
Hyundai Motor Group has about 250,000 employees worldwide and operates mobility brands including Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.

links.context: Deputy Press Secretary for Governor Kemp
Communications Manager for Georgia Department of Economic Development

source_title: Leaders Celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s Historic Impact on Georgia | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Seoyon E-HWA will create 740 new direct and indirect jobs.
Seoyon E-HWA will invest almost $76 million in a new manufacturing facility in Chatham County.
The announcement was made on February 1, 2023, in Atlanta.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that Georgia is working to ensure it becomes the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Seoyon E-HWA was established in 1972 and operates 32 corporations across North America, South America, Europe, and Asia.
The company supplies door trim, headlining, seats, C/PAD, bumpers, and other parts to Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, Mercedes-Benz, Ford, Volkswagen, and more.
Seoyon’s existing LaGrange location will continue to support 630 full-time jobs.
Seoyon E-HWA Chairman Mr. Ryu Yang-seok stated the company is an automobile manufacturer in countries including Korea, Europe, and India.
The new facility will be located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center, a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site.
The new manufacturing facility will produce automotive interior parts such as door trims, headlining, and tailgate trims.
Production at the new facility is expected to begin in October 2024.
More than two-thirds, or approximately 500, of the announced jobs will be permanent roles in human resources, development, quality, and production personnel.

Savannah Economic Development Authority President and CEO Trip Tollison stated the location ideally positions Seoyon E-HWA close to the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, the Port of Savannah, Interstates I-16 and I-95, and more.
Georgia Ports Authority Executive Director Griff Lynch noted that Seoyon is a valued customer using the Port of Savannah to supply its manufacturing facility in West Georgia.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Savannah Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Hyundai’s new EV facility is drawing an experienced and dedicated supplier network to the region.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced over $21 billion in investment and 26,700 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers for the Hyundai project have already exceeded the $1 billion in investment predicted from other locations connected to the project around the state by more than $600 million.
source_title: Seoyon E-HWA Joins Growing List of Hyundai Suppliers | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on April 11, 2023 that Seohan Auto Georgia will create at least 180 new jobs.
Seohan Auto Georgia will invest more than $72 million in a new manufacturing facility in Liberty County.
The new manufacturing facility will be located at the Dunwoody Court Site in the Tradeport East Business Center, a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site.
Seohan Auto Georgia Corporation is a Tier-1 automotive parts supplier for Hyundai Motor Group America.
The company is based in South Korea and operates production facilities around the world, including multiple facilities in the United States.
CEO Jung Kee Koo stated that sustained EV market growth suggests accelerated changes to the automotive market are inevitable.
Jung Kee Koo believes Georgia will be the center of the EV industry and a new frontier for Seohan's future with limitless opportunities.
The new facility is scheduled to begin production in late 2024.
The facility will produce H/shafts, axles, and brake systems for OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.

Liberty County Development Authority Chairman Allen Brown described Seohan and its affiliated enterprises as responsible, thorough, and progressive organizations striving to advance innovative technology.
GDEcD Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team on the project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Liberty County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that the transition to e-mobility creates a window of opportunity for companies and states remaining on the forefront.
Pat Wilson stated that communities investing in speed-to-market solutions, like Liberty County with the Tradeport East Business Center, give Georgia a competitive advantage.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers connected to Hyundai's project were predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
Projects announced to date have already exceeded the $1 billion supplier investment prediction by nearly $1 billion.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry, building on assets that make the automotive industry successful.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and 27,800 jobs in Georgia.
Seohan Auto Georgia Corporation is a new subsidiary of Korea Movenex Co., Ltd, which was established in 1974.
Korea Movenex employs more than 2,800 employees globally and possesses advanced technology and experience in the automotive parts industry.

Korea Movenex strives to create new value by developing advanced technology and supplying parts to collaborative partners rather than simply transferring value through distribution or sales operations.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Seohan Auto Georgia Announces 180 New Jobs in Liberty County | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: On October 31, 2023, Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that auto parts company Hwashin will establish a new manufacturing facility in Dublin.
The project will create over 460 new jobs and more than $176 million in investment in Laurens County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated the goal is to become the "e-mobility capital of the nation" and noted Hwashin joins a growing ecosystem of companies bringing good jobs to Georgians.
Hwashin operates as a Tier-1 supplier specializing in crafting chassis and body parts for Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, and international original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
The company is headquartered in Korea and has established production facilities spanning the United States, Asia, and South America.
Seo Jin Jung, CEO of Hwashin, stated the company aspires to shape a future global city focused on electric vehicles (EVs) drawing from its 50-year expertise.

Jung noted Hwashin's commitment to being a reliable partner to customers and an engaged contributor to the local community, expressing gratitude to the State of Georgia and Laurens County for their cooperation.
The new Georgia facility is scheduled to begin production in late 2025.
The facility will be located at the Georgia Highway 257 Rail Served Site, which is the first Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Select site in the state.
A "GRAD Certified Select" status indicates that a site has met or exceeded more rigorous certification requirements.
The facility will primarily focus on manufacturing chassis components for OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and Kia Georgia.
Dublin Mayor Joshua Kight stated the city is pleased to welcome Hwashin and praised the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority for demonstrating why the area is a great place to do business.
Mayor Kight noted the manufacturing facility will become an economic engine for the region, leading to more employment opportunities and quality growth.
Jeff Davis, Chairman of the Laurens County Board of Commissioners, stated Hwashin is the seventh international company to call Dublin and Laurens County home.

Davis highlighted the partnership between Laurens County, the City of Dublin, and the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority, noting the project will provide new career opportunities and develop increased opportunities in housing and retail development.
Georgia Ports Authority President and CEO Griff Lynch stated significant investments from Hwashin and other automotive suppliers are delivering career opportunities for Georgians.
Lynch noted the Georgia Ports Authority welcomes Hwashin as the newest member of its manufacturing community to efficiently connect companies to international markets.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on the project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Hwashin will have direct interstate access to at least two long-term automotive partners from its chosen site.
Wilson noted locating on a GRAD Select site allows the company to pursue an accelerated construction timeline.
Wilson emphasized that decades of elected leadership have supported infrastructure and workforce development, areas where Georgia excels against competition.
Over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, collaboration, innovation, and positioned itself as a leader in the automotive and mobility industry.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility OEMs resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state.
As the electric vehicle market grows, Georgia has pursued the entire supply chain, creating more than $25.1 billion in investments and 29,000 jobs since 2020.
Since its inception in 1975, Hwashin Co., Ltd. has held a prominent position in the Korean auto parts industry for nearly five decades.
The company maintains a specialized focus on chassis and body parts while actively engaging in research and development.
Hwashin has expanded its product offerings to encompass lightweight aluminum components and eco-friendly solutions aligned with global automotive industry trends.
Hwashin Co., Ltd. employs more than 4,200 personnel worldwide.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Auto Parts Supplier Hwashin to Bring 460 Jobs to Middle Georgia | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: GDEcD’s Global Commerce division supported 218 locations or expansions during the first half of Fiscal Year 2023 (July 1 through December 31, 2022).
These projects are expected to create approximately 17,500 new jobs and more than $13 billion in investment for the state.
The automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment after advanced manufacturing during this period.
Advanced manufacturing, automotive, aerospace, and food processing accounted for nearly 75 percent of new jobs and 84 percent of investments.
Planned investments from these projects increased by more than 38 percent compared to the same period last year.
Areas outside the 10-county metro Atlanta region accounted for 85 percent of jobs created and 92 percent of investments from these economic development projects.
In October, state and local officials joined company representatives for the groundbreaking of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) EV facility.
Korean Ambassador to the U.S. Tae-yong Cho attended the HMGMA groundbreaking.
The HMGMA EV facility will be located in Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham, and Effingham Counties.
The HMGMA EV facility is expected to support more than 8,100 direct jobs.
Three off-site HMGMA suppliers announced locations following the groundbreaking.
These supplier additions swiftly surpassed the initial $1 billion in investment predicted from them.
International companies accounted for 23 percent of state projects since July 1, 2022, contributing to a closed-loop electric mobility supply chain.
SK On and Hyundai Motor Group announced a partnership for a facility in Bartow County.
The SK On and Hyundai Motor Group project is among the top five largest projects by investment in Georgia’s history.
Next-generation developer FREYR Battery announced a facility in Coweta County.
The FREYR Battery facility is among the top five largest projects by investment in Georgia’s history.
Battery recycler Sung-Il Hitech joined Georgia’s electric mobility ecosystem.
Battery recycler Denkai America joined Georgia’s electric mobility ecosystem.
GDEcD utilizes a best-in-class job training program called Georgia Quick Start to help companies solve workforce challenges.
GDEcD offers GRAD certified sites that support accelerated project timelines for companies.
GDEcD Deputy Commissioner for Global Commerce Kristi Brigman stated that companies use Georgia Quick Start to build talent and workforce from the ground up.

links.context: Details of state-announced economic development projects

source_title: Gov. Kemp: Key Industries Sustain Momentum for State's Economic Development | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Kia Georgia celebrated a historic milestone on May 30, 2024, when it rolled an all-electric 2025 EV9 off its assembly line in West Point, Ga.
The three-row SUV is the first electric vehicle ever produced in Georgia.
The launch marks the culmination of a $200 million expansion at Kia’s plant.
The expansion has added about 200 jobs at Kia’s plant.
SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon, President and CEO for Kia North America and Kia America, introduced the vehicle.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp and James Watson, Kia Georgia’s Vice President of Production, drove the EV9 off the assembly line.
SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon stated that the launch is the latest example of Kia’s commitment to the U.S. market and the state of Georgia.
SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon noted that the EV9 will be in excellent hands with the team members at Kia Georgia, citing their track record for assembling award-winning, world-renowned products.
Georgia Quick Start worked with Kia to design and develop an “Introduction to EV Vehicles” course for new and current Kia employees on the EV9 production line.
Georgia Quick Start also developed an EV safety course for new and current Kia employees on the EV9 production line.
Stuart Countess, President and CEO of Kia Georgia, stated that Kia aims to change the future for the better and be a company people feel inspired by and want to work for.
More than 15 years ago, Quick Start helped Kia start up its operation making cars at the West Point location.
Quick Start is currently helping Kia add electric vehicles to its award-winning lineup.
Kia's headquarters are located in Seoul, South Korea.
source_title: Kia Georgia Launches EV9 - Georgia Quick Start

facts: Quick Start provides customized training for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America’s (HMGMA) leading tier-one supplier, Hyundai Mobis.
Quick Start’s Eastern Operations around Savannah, Georgia, are highly active as the opening day for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) approaches.
Suppliers are an essential part of HMGMA, with Hyundai Mobis serving as the largest tier-one supplier.
Hyundai Mobis manufactures front and rear end chassis modules, cockpit modules, and key parts used to build batteries for electric vehicles.
Training provided to Mobis includes company-specific assembly courses, mechatronics, and electrical safety awareness.
Trainees also receive safety and organizational development courses, including Lean, leadership training, and conflict resolution.
Quick Start is utilizing its main Advanced Manufacturing Training Center in Pooler, Georgia, to deliver the training.
An interim facility is being used while the new Hyundai Mobility Training Center of Georgia is under construction adjacent to the Metaplant.
The Metaplant, which will host Mobis’s operations in the Savannah area, is scheduled to begin vehicle production by early 2025.
Hyundai Mobis is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis - Georgia Quick Start

facts: Quick Start provides customized workforce training as an incentive for companies investing and creating jobs in Georgia.
To begin a training partnership, Quick Start meets with company leadership, subject-matter experts, and operations teams to define the scope of training needs.
Different job tasks require different modes of training.
With an emphasis on visual learning and hands-on training, Quick Start maps out training plans unique for each company.
“Flexible and responsive” have been Quick Start’s watchwords for decades.
Quick Start delivers the training whenever and wherever it makes sense for each company project.
Quick Start upholds the highest standards of confidentiality to protect a company’s intellectual property.
source_title: What We Do - Georgia Quick Start

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) is soliciting proposals from National Laboratories and industry partners for radical innovations in American battery manufacturing leadership.

Manufacturing competitiveness is a priority for the Trump Administration, and DOE will directly fund National Laboratories to establish public-private partnerships to solve engineering challenges for advanced battery materials and devices.
The initiative focuses on de-risking, scaling, and accelerating adoption of new technologies.

The Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy’s Advanced Manufacturing Office and Vehicles Technologies Office will jointly invest up to $12 million in projects addressing capability gaps for enhanced lithium-ion batteries, next-generation lithium-ion batteries, and next-generation lithium-based battery technologies.
Projects must fall within four focus areas: Materials processing and scale-up; Innovative / advanced electrode and cell production; Designer materials and electrodes; and Formation.
Proposals are limited to submission by the National Laboratories.
Full applications from National Laboratories are due on July 17, 2020.
Individual project awards will range from $500,000 to $3 million over 24 to 36 months.
A 50/50 cost-share is required between DOE and the private partner, which can include an in-kind contribution.
Funds will be awarded directly to National Laboratories to support work with companies under Cooperative Research and Development Agreements (CRADAs).
The funding opportunity is part of the Energy Storage Grand Challenge, a DOE-wide effort to create and sustain global leadership in energy storage utilization and exports.
The initiative aims to establish a secure domestic manufacturing supply chain that does not depend on foreign sources of critical materials.
Ames National Laboratory capabilities include Electrode Manufacturing and Battery Cell Testing; contact is Iver Anderson.

Argonne National Laboratory capabilities include Cell Manufacturing, Electrode Manufacturing, Electrode Materials Scale-up, Electrolyte Manufacturing / Scale-up, Failure Analysis Testing, Machine Learning, and Materials Processing; contacts are James Miller and Venkat Srinivasan.
Brookhaven National Laboratory capabilities include Electrode Manufacturing and Electrode Materials Scale-up; contact is Xiao-Qing Yang.
Kansas City National Security Campus capabilities include Battery Cell Testing; contact is Rocco Covello.
Idaho National Laboratory capabilities include Electrode Manufacturing, Failure Analysis Testing, Battery Abuse Testing, and Machine Learning; contact is Seth Snyder.

Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory capabilities include Advanced Formation, Electrode Manufacturing, Electrolyte Manufacturing / Scale-up, and Materials Processing; contacts are Thomas Kirchstetter and Vince Battaglia.
Los Alamos National Laboratory capabilities include Electrode Materials Scale-up and Electrode Manufacturing; contact is Rodney Borup.
National Energy Technology Laboratory capabilities include Electrode Materials Scale-up and Electrode Manufacturing; contact is Bryan Morreale.

National Renewable Energy Laboratory capabilities include Advanced Formation, Cell Manufacturing, Electrode Manufacturing, Electrolyte Manufacturing / Scale-up, Materials Processing, and Battery Abuse Testing; contact is John Farrell.
Oak Ridge National Laboratory capabilities include Cell Manufacturing, Electrode Manufacturing, Electrolyte Manufacturing / Scale-up, and Battery Cell Testing; contact is Claus Daniel.
Pacific Northwest National Laboratory capabilities include Cell Manufacturing and Electrode Materials Scale-up; contact is Johnathan Holladay.
Sandia National Laboratories capabilities include Electrode Manufacturing, Cell Manufacturing, and Battery Abuse Testing; contact is Christopher Moen.
SLAC Accelerator Laboratory capabilities include Advanced Formation, Electrode Materials Scale-up, Materials Processing, and Machine Learning; contact is Steve Eglash.
Interested industry partners should reach out directly to National Laboratory contacts about opportunities to collaborate.
source_title: Energy Department to Fund National Laboratories to Establish Industry Partnerships for Battery Manufacturing Innovation
