entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On EV Battery Cell Production Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group announced plans to establish an EV battery cell production joint venture in the US with partner SK On.
Affiliates Hyundai Motor Company, Kia Corporation, and Hyundai Mobis each approved the joint venture plan at their respective board meetings on April 25, 2023.
In November 2022, Hyundai Motor Group signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) with SK On to secure EV battery supply for North America.
The joint venture's total investment is expected to be approximately USD 5 billion.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On will each hold a 50 percent stake in the joint venture.
Both parties plan to establish an EV battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia.
The new plant will be located close to Hyundai's U.S. production facilities: Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama, Kia Georgia, and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The plant is expected to start manufacturing battery cells in the second half of 2025.
The facility will have an annual production capacity of 35 GWh.
This capacity is sufficient to support the production of 300,000 EVs.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble battery packs using cells from the new plant.
Hyundai Mobis will supply the assembled battery packs to the Group's U.S. manufacturing facilities for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
The joint venture aims to accelerate Hyundai Motor Group's electrification efforts and bolster its position as an EV leader in the U.S. market through a stable battery supply.
SK On has been a strategic partner for Hyundai Motor Group.
Both companies have previously collaborated on the production of globally renowned EV models, including the Hyundai IONIQ 5, Kia EV6, and Genesis GV60.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On to Establish EV Battery Cell Production Joint Venture in US

facts: Provides one of the world's most advanced batteries for electric vehicles based on technological expertise and manufacturing experience.
Provides batteries of exceptional quality to the global market based on electric vehicle battery manufacturing experience and high-nickel technology.
Applies strictest standards to all production and supply processes with reliable performance and safety as the most important value.
Provides various types of batteries depending on the client's needs.
Owns a global battery supply network centered on production bases in key markets, such as North America, Europe, and Asia.
Uses local production processes and supply networks to collect stable raw materials and efficiently produce and supply products, strengthening global production competence.
Replicates a proven process integrating production experience and expertise to new production bases to effectively expand production capacity.
Upholds strict safety and quality management standards to provide highest quality batteries to customers worldwide.

Continually enhances manufacturing performance and product attributes through automation, including process automation, artificial intelligence, smart factory operation, logistics automation, and digitalization.
The NCM9 battery is developed by SK On and produced by SK Battery America.
The NCM9 battery derives its name from key components nickel, manganese, and cobalt (NCM), with the '9' signifying a nickel composition nearly reaching 90%.
The elevated nickel content of the NCM9 battery translates to superior energy density and an extended driving range.
The NCM9 battery is a preferred choice for enhanced performance.
source_title: Product < Business < SK battery America

facts: SK Battery America Inc. exposed employees at its Commerce plant to serious and potentially disabling safety and health hazards, according to a U.S. Department of Labor workplace safety inspection.
OSHA cited SK Battery America Inc. with six serious violations and one other-than-serious violation.

The company subjected employees to continuous noise levels exceeding the 8-hour time-weighted average of 85 A-weighted decibels by not developing a monitoring program, nor establishing an audiometric testing program, including a valid baseline audiogram.
SK Battery America Inc. exposed employees working with cobalt, nickel and manganese to respiratory hazards by failing to complete a workplace hazard assessment.
The company failed to ensure employees were given clean, disinfected and sanitary respirators for work involving cobalt, nickel and manganese.
SK Battery America Inc. failed to store respirators properly to protect them from material contamination.
The company failed to make certain that people working with corrosive materials had access to eyewash stations and emergency showers.
SK Battery America Inc. left workers handling bags of nickel powder vulnerable to respiratory hazards by not providing feasible administrative or engineering controls to reduce exposure levels.

OSHA Area Office Director Joshua Turner stated that the lithium battery industry has experienced unprecedented growth, bringing heightened responsibility for safety at the forefront of innovation, and emphasized that materials used to produce lithium batteries can cause debilitating and permanent health issues.

Turner noted that OSHA’s continued efforts to identify emerging hazards are key in helping the industry eliminate employee exposures, warning that employers must prioritize worker safety alongside technological advancement.
OSHA has proposed $75,449 in penalties against SK Battery America Inc., an amount set by federal statute.
SK Battery America Inc. employs about 3,100 people at its two battery manufacturing plants in Commerce.
SK Battery America Inc. is a subsidiary of SK On, a global electric vehicle battery manufacturer.
SK On is part of the Seoul-based SK Group, South Korea’s second-largest conglomerate, which includes companies in energy, advanced materials, biopharmaceuticals and digital business.
SK Battery America Inc. has contested the OSHA findings before the independent Occupational Safety and Health Review Commission.
source_title: US Department of Labor finds electric vehicle battery manufacturer exposed employees to unsafe nickel levels, other mixed metals at Commerce plant | U.S. Department of Labor

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) has accelerated its official start of production to the fourth quarter of 2024.
The grand opening celebration for HMGMA is targeted for the first quarter of 2025, moving up from a previously announced first quarter of 2025 date.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp, First Lady Marty A. Kemp, and Hyundai executives celebrated Hyundai Motor Group's positive impact on the state, officially marking February 26, 2024, as Hyundai Day.
The Georgia General Assembly passed a Resolution commending Hyundai Motor Group's dedication to the state and recognizing February 26, 2024, as Hyundai Day at the State Capitol.
Hyundai and Genesis electric vehicles on display included the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60, and Electrified GV70.
A hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck was displayed for use in logistics between Hyundai's manufacturing facilities and suppliers.

José Muñoz, president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and president and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, stated that Hyundai's investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of great-paying American jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that Hyundai helps recruit businesses, gives back to communities, and works with world-class colleges and universities to connect Georgians with opportunities.

Oscar Kwon, president and CEO of HMGMA, called the project the largest economic development project in Georgia's history and credited the team's rapid progress to the Meta Pros and support from state and local partners including the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Joint Development Authority.

Attendees at the celebration included Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones, House Speaker Jon Burns, legislative leaders, Department of Economic Development Director Pat Wilson, and officials from the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $7.59 billion to develop HMGMA, which will create 8,500 direct jobs.
Hyundai and SK On are building a $5 billion EV battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia, that will employ 3,500 workers.
A Center for Automotive Research study projects Hyundai's investments in Georgia will create nearly 40,000 jobs and generate $4.6 billion in individual earnings annually.
HMGMA continues hiring efforts and has developed educational partnerships with area colleges to create an employment pipeline.
The Electric Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate (EVP TCC) is being offered at four area technical colleges as part of this collaboration.
Students who successfully complete the EVP TCC program receive preferred status when applying for positions at HMGMA.
Hyundai is actively developing a hydrogen mobility value chain at HMGMA, as announced at CES 2024.
Through the Clean Logistics Project in Georgia, the Metaplant will utilize XCIENT Fuel Cell tractors to manage logistics for the plant and its suppliers.
The XCIENT is Hyundai's commercialized class 8 6x4 fuel cell electric model with a proven record of real-world application and over 6.25 million accumulated miles.
Hyundai is developing hydrogen refueling infrastructure needed to support this fuel cell activity, which is anticipated to stimulate economic growth and create job opportunities in the state.
HMGMA is Hyundai Motor Group's first dedicated electric vehicle mass-production plant and is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
The facility will feature a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem to realize customer value.
The Georgia facility will become an intelligent manufacturing plant where order collection, procurement, logistics, and production are optimized using AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system is designed to create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.

Hyundai Motor America supports Hyundai Motor Company's Progress for Humanity vision and operates significant U.S. facilities including its North American headquarters in California, the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama assembly plant, HMGMA in development in Georgia, and several R&D facilities.
Combined with operations from 835 independent dealers, Hyundai's U.S. operations contribute $20.1 billion annually and 190,000 jobs to the U.S. economy according to a recent economic impact report.

links.context: More information on Hyundai Motor America

source_title: Hyundai Announces New Production Start Date at Metaplant America During Hyundai Day in Georgia

facts: Rivian held a formal groundbreaking ceremony at its manufacturing site outside Social Circle, Georgia, on September 16, 2025.
The new facility represents a multi-billion dollar investment.
The plant is expected to create 7,500 jobs by 2030.
The project will also create 2,000 construction jobs.
Customer vehicle production at the Georgia facility is expected to begin in 2028.
Rivian plans to manufacture the midsize, 5-seater R2 SUV and R3 crossover at the new plant.
The facility will be built in two phases.
Each phase will result in 200,000 units of annual production capacity.
The total annual production capacity for the completed facility is expected to be up to 400,000 vehicles.
The plant's output will support the sale of American EVs in international markets.
An analysis conducted by IMPLAN estimates the project will generate an additional nearly 8,000 indirect jobs.
Collectively, the 15,000+ direct and indirect jobs are expected to generate over $1 billion in labor income annually.
The project is expected to support suppliers, vendors, and small businesses in Jasper, Morgan, Newton, and Walton Counties and the surrounding region.
Billions in economic benefits are anticipated from the project across these counties.
Rivian is developing partnerships with universities, technical colleges, and local, regional, and state institutions as it scales operations.
The manufacturing site covers nearly 2,000 acres.
The site will utilize modern construction techniques and advanced environmental management.
The facility is intended to feature recreational trails for employees and customers, including a dedicated Rivian experience trail.
On September 14, 2025, Rivian held a community event at the Stanton Springs Plant site welcoming nearly 1,000 future neighbors and friends.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp, First Lady Marty Kemp, and Georgia Speaker of the House Jon Burns attended the groundbreaking ceremony alongside other state and local officials.
Rivian Founder and CEO RJ Scaringe stated the facility will support global expansion and provide scale for millions of future drivers in all-electric vehicles domestically and overseas.

Jerry Silvio, Chairman of the Joint Development Authority of Jasper, Morgan, Newton & Walton Counties, noted the project aligns with the JDA's 25-year vision for high-tech manufacturing and good-paying jobs.
source_title: Rivian Holds Kickoff Ceremony for Georgia Plant, Accelerating American Manufacturing

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Seoyon E-HWA, a global auto parts supplier that manufactures both interior and exterior components, will create 740 new direct and indirect jobs.
Seoyon E-HWA will invest almost $76 million in a new manufacturing facility in Chatham County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated he promised to bring even more jobs and opportunity to Georgians following the announcement of the largest economic development project in Georgia history last year.
Governor Brian Kemp aims to ensure Georgia is the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Governor Brian Kemp claims Georgia is the No. 1 state for business.
Seoyon E-Hwa was established in 1972.
Seoyon E-Hwa operates 32 corporations across North America, South America, Europe, and Asia.
Seoyon E-Hwa supplies door trim, headlining, seats, C/PAD, bumpers, and other parts to Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, Mercedes-Benz, Ford, Volkswagen, and more.
Seoyon’s LaGrange location will continue to support 630 full time jobs.
Seoyon E-Hwa Chairman Mr. Ryu Yang-seok stated the company is an automobile manufacturer in various countries such as Korea, Europe, and India.
Seoyon E-Hwa plans to supply electric vehicle parts at its new Chatham County, Georgia location.
The new facility will be located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center, a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site.
The new manufacturing facility will produce automotive interior parts such as door trims, headlining, and tailgate trims.
Production at the new facility is expected to begin in October 2024.
More than two-thirds, or approximately 500, of the announced jobs will be permanent roles in human resources, development, quality, and production personnel.

Savannah Economic Development Authority President and CEO Trip Tollison stated the facility is ideally positioned in close proximity to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, the Port of Savannah, Interstates I-16 and I-95.
Georgia Ports Authority Executive Director Griff Lynch noted Seoyon is a valued customer that uses the Port of Savannah to supply its manufacturing facility in West Georgia.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Savannah Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated this is Seoyon's second Georgia facility.
Pat Wilson noted the commitment nearly doubles the number of jobs Seoyon supports in the state.
Hyundai’s new EV facility is drawing an experienced and dedicated supplier network to the region, adding to the state’s industry expertise and shaping a larger skilled workforce.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced over $21 billion in investment and 26,700 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers have already exceeded the $1 billion in investment predicted from other locations connected to the project around the state by more than $600 million.
source_title: Seoyon E-HWA, a Hyundai supplier, to locate in Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center – Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) announced a state-of-the-art vehicle manufacturing facility and battery joint venture two years ago.
The HMGMA project represents an estimated $7.59 billion in investment.
The HMGMA project will create 8,500 new jobs for the Savannah region over the next eight years.
The HMGMA facility is on track to begin full production in fourth quarter 2024.
Suppliers to HMGMA announced investments totaling more than $2.5 billion across 11 counties in Georgia as of July 2024.
HMGMA supplier investments span west and middle Georgia.
Kia Georgia's lineup expanded to include the EV9, which is the first electric car expected to roll off the line in Georgia.
The Kia EV9 began rolling off the line at Kia Georgia's existing facility in West Point in May 2024.
Kia Georgia has an annual production capacity of 350,000 units.
Kia Georgia manufactures the Telluride SUV, Sorento SUV models, Sportage SUV models, and the all-electric EV9 SUV.
The battery joint venture is a collaboration between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solutions.
The Hyundai-LG Energy Solutions battery JV will support production of 300,000 units of Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EVs annually at full operations.
SK Battery America operates existing facilities that support EV manufacturers across the Southeast.
Qcells is a solar panel manufacturer located in northern Georgia.
Georgia is advancing its position as a central hub for the "Battery Belt."
The State of Georgia has hosted a Korean consular presence since 1971.
The State of Georgia has maintained continuous diplomatic representation in Seoul since 1985.
Governor Brian P. Kemp conducted his first economic development mission to Korea in 2019.
The Korea Trade-Investment Promotion Agency (KOTRA) opened its Atlanta office in 2022.
The State of Georgia received the James A. Van Fleet Award from The Korea Society in 2023.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development and the Korea International Trade Association (KITA) both received a 2023 Van Fleet Award.
Metro Atlanta ranks among the top five U.S. metros for its Korean American community size.
Korean communities are actively growing across the state of Georgia.
The "Seoul of the South™" food tour, organized by Explore Gwinnett, fosters ties between Gwinnett County's hospitality industry and the local Korean community.
The Seoul of the South™ food tour is featured in Taste of the South Magazine and regularly sells out.
Six dates of the Seoul of the South™ food tour scheduled between May and September 2024 sold out by March 2024.
Explore Gwinnett developed a DIY Seoul of the South™ guide to accommodate demand for experiencing Korean food and culture.
Since 2022, Hyundai Motor Group has donated over $2 million to regional nonprofits in the Savannah area, focusing on safety and education.
HMGMA engaged students from the Savannah College of Art and Design in multiple community projects.
HMGMA student projects included designing a parade float for the 200th Anniversary of the St. Patrick's Day Parade and designing the Bryan County water tank.
Hyundai Motor Group sponsored a two-week summer study abroad program to Korea for Columbus State University students in 2024.
The Columbus State University summer study abroad program featured Korean history, culture, and its growing economic importance to Georgia and the United States.
Daily direct flights operate between Atlanta and Seoul via Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport.
The Delta Air Lines-Korean Air joint venture is celebrating its sixth year of operation.
The Delta Air Lines-Korean Air joint venture connects travelers to more than 290 destinations in the Americas and 80 destinations in Asia.
Georgia is the only U.S. state where tourism visitation from South Korea has recovered to and exceeded 2019 levels.
Visitors from South Korea to Georgia totaled 33,500 in 2022, representing a year-over-year increase of more than 360% according to Tourism Economics data.
Estimated spending by Korean visitors in Georgia in 2022 surpassed pre-pandemic 2019 levels.
source_title: How the Georgia-Korea Connection Energizes Opportunities | Georgia

facts: Archer Aviation Inc. (NYSE: ACHR) is constructing a high-volume manufacturing facility in Covington, Georgia.
The facility's buildout spans nearly 400,000 square feet.
Construction remains on track to be completed by the end of 2024.
Archer completed the first build phase earlier this year, which included grading and foundation work.
The company is nearing completion of phase two, involving the erection of exterior walls and roof.
Phase three (interior fit out) and phase four (equipment load in and power on) are on track to be completed in the coming months.
Once complete, the facility will support production of up to 650 Midnight aircraft annually in partnership with Stellantis.
This production volume makes it one of the largest manufacturing facilities by volume in the aviation industry.
Archer recently showcased its Midnight aircraft at Atlantic Aviation’s new terminal at DeKalb–Peachtree Municipal Airport in Atlanta.

Guests at the showcase included government officials from Georgia’s Office of the Governor, Georgia’s Congressional Offices, the City of Atlanta, Georgia’s State Legislature, the Federal Aviation Administration, and other local governments.
Archer’s buildout is taking place in close partnership with Stellantis to mass-produce the Midnight aircraft together.
Adam Goldstein, CEO and founder of Archer, stated that the company aims to be the first in the eVTOL industry to achieve scaled manufacturing.
The Midnight aircraft is built around Archer's proprietary electric powertrain.
It is designed to offer passengers a sustainable, low-noise, and safe alternative to ground transportation.
The aircraft travels at speeds up to 150 mph.
It is designed for back-to-back flights of 20 to 50 miles with minimal charge time in between.
The Midnight is 100 times quieter than a helicopter at cruising altitudes and nearly inaudible from the streets below.
The aircraft features redundant systems, allowing Archer to target safety levels similar to commercial airliners.
It is designed to carry a pilot, up to four passengers, and carry-on luggage.
source_title: Archer Aviation Showcases Midnight Aircraft In Atlanta As It Nears Completion Of Its High-Volume Manufacturing Facility In Covington, Georgia

facts: Hyundai broke ground on its Georgia electric vehicle factory in fall 2022.
At the time of the groundbreaking, Hyundai staged a car equivalent of a fashion show at Savannah's Enmarket Arena.
Hyundai had not yet announced what models it would build at what would become the Metaplant.
Locals flocked to an open house in search of hints about future production.
The centerpiece display showcased an all-electric SUV, a concept car known as SEVEN.
source_title: Coming to streets near you: Hyundai’s made-in-Georgia, all-electric SUV | Chattanooga Times Free Press

facts: In Tbilisi, passing cars display the slogan “Build Your Dreams,” which is BYD’s branded motto.
BYD is China’s largest automaker.
Chinese vehicles are increasingly visible on streets in Tbilisi and other Georgian cities.
Official data for January-July 2025 shows China accounted for just over 10 percent of Georgian imports, ranking fourth behind Turkey, the United States, and Russia.
Passenger cars are Georgia’s largest import product, accounting for 20 percent of all imports.
The auto sector is significant for domestic demand and Georgia’s role as a regional re-export hub.
China’s increasing presence underscores its strategic interest in gaining a larger share of the country’s auto market.
While Georgia’s annual imported car numbers have remained steady, the share of electric vehicles is surging.
During the first half of this year, the country imported 3,616 EVs, an 88 percent jump from 1,922 during the same period last year.
Until recently, China did not rank among Georgia’s top five car-supplying countries.
China is now the second-largest EV supplier after the US.
Overall imports of Chinese cars, both electric and conventional, have risen roughly 79 percent this year.
Electric vehicles still make up a relatively small share of the overall auto market in Georgia, where nearly every second person owns a car.
Most Georgian consumers have long relied on cheap, second-hand cars from the US that are easy to repair locally.
EVs do not fit this local repair model, which helps explain why the US remains the country’s main car supplier.
China has captured nearly 12 percent of Georgia’s EV market in just a couple of months.
Demand for EVs is rising sharply due to strong appeal and lower running costs compared to gasoline-powered cars.
Widespread EV adoption requires charging infrastructure, which Georgia currently lacks.
Market signals suggest that charging infrastructure development is about to change.
Chinese EVs are more affordable than their Western-made counterparts, positioning them to capture an increasing share of the Georgian market.
Growing Chinese economic influence goes hand in hand with politics, specifically the ruling Georgian Dream party’s pro-China stance.
Prime Minister Irakli Kobakhidze praised Communist China as the “best example of state modernization” and argued that Beijing sets a positive example for other global powers.
The BYD Center in Tbilisi has recently taken over the former headquarters of the United National Movement, now a major opposition force.
The building was built in 2010 for party operations and slipped out of the opposition’s hands amid financial and political pressures from the government.

Transparency International Georgia noted in its latest report that as relations with the US and EU deteriorate, Georgian Dream is strengthening alliances with authoritarian regimes hostile to the US, such as China.
Irakli Machaidze is a contributing writer covering the South Caucasus for Eurasianet.
source_title: As Georgia’s electric vehicle market grows, China powers ahead | Eurasianet

facts: Kia’s Georgia plant was the Korean automaker’s first manufacturing operation in the U.S. and, at the time, the largest economic project in Georgia.
Kia’s $1.2 billion facility brought thousands of jobs to Troup County, a once-struggling textile area that had one of the highest unemployment rates in the state.
Kia’s presence made Georgia a player in auto manufacturing again, a legacy dating back to 1915 when Ford established a manufacturing plant and its Southeastern headquarters in Atlanta.
Kia will celebrate a first this spring with a $200 million investment.
Kia’s new three-row electric SUV, the Kia EV9, will be the first EV manufactured in Georgia.
Georgia is emerging as an EV manufacturing hub thanks to Kia’s ongoing commitment and Hyundai’s multibillion-dollar investment.
Imola, an Italian manufacturer of customized EVs, announced plans to open an EV manufacturing plant in Fort Valley within the next two years.
Rivian, a California-based truck manufacturer, has put its planned $5 billion Georgia investment on hold.

Chris Smith, chief project implementation officer and general counsel of Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, stated that once up to full capacity, the metaplant will have the capacity to produce 300,000 vehicles a year.
Kristi Brigman, deputy commissioner of global commerce at the Georgia Department of Economic Development, said the five largest projects in the state’s history have all been related to e-mobility.
Since 2018, projects related to the e-mobility supply chain have created about 32,200 new jobs in Georgia and $27 billion in investment.
The Hyundai Metaplant in Ellabell in Bryan County is the largest of these projects and is ahead of schedule.
The project was announced in 2022 and now represents an investment of more than $7.5 billion in Georgia, counting investments by suppliers and partners.
The investment includes a venture with Korean conglomerate LG Energy Systems to make battery packs at the site.
The plant broke ground in October 2022.
In February, Hyundai made it official that the first EVs will be produced in the fourth quarter of 2024, ahead of the previous announced 2025 target.
Chris Smith noted that the start of production will not mean the facility will be complete by 2024.
The battery joint venture will come online sometime in 2025.
At full capacity, the metaplant has room to expand to produce 500,000 vehicles if demand dictates.
At full employment, there will be 8,500 Hyundai Motor Group employees onsite at the plant.
The majority of supplier jobs created by these projects are outside the Atlanta-Fulton County area.

Hwashin, a Tier-1 supplier that makes chassis and body parts for entities such as Hyundai Motor Group, Kia and international OEMs, will open a new manufacturing facility bringing over 460 new jobs and more than $176 million in investment to Dublin and Laurens County.
The Hwashin facility is scheduled to begin production in late 2025.
Seoyon E-Hwa’s $96 million investment will create 740 new direct and indirect jobs in Chatham County.
Seoyon E-Hwa’s new manufacturing facility, located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center, will produce automotive interior parts such as door trims, headlining and tailgate trims.
Production at the Seoyon E-Hwa facility is expected to begin in October 2024.

DAS Corp., which designs and makes seating systems, safety seating components and seating structures, is building a new manufacturing facility that will create 300 new jobs and generate more than $35 million in investment in Candler County.
DAS is a key supplier for Kia Georgia and Hyundai.
Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. will build a new $30 million manufacturing plant creating 200 new jobs in Candler County.
Doowon Group designs and makes automotive climate control systems for EVs and traditional vehicles and will be a key supplier for Kia Georgia and Hyundai.
The Doowon manufacturing facility will be located in the Metter-Candler County I-16 Industrial Park.
Anovion Technologies announced a new manufacturing facility that will create more than 400 jobs and over $800 million in investment in Decatur County.
Anovion is a supplier of synthetic graphic anode materials for lithium-ion batteries, with production expected to start in 2025.
Woory Industrial Co., Ltd. will open a new manufacturing facility creating more than 130 new jobs and an estimated $18 million in investment in Dublin-Laurens County.
The Woory facility is the company’s first in the U.S. and will produce electrical heaters, control units, and actuators for both EV and traditional carmakers in North America.
Kristi Brigman stated that Georgia has a reputation as a very business-friendly environment and its logistics network (rail, airport, and port) is absolutely critical to growth.

While Georgia offers tax incentives competitive with other states, Chris Smith said tax incentives were not as much of a deciding factor for Hyundai as geographic position, port access, and a pre-earmarked site.

Hyundai was drawn to Georgia’s skilled workforce, available largely due to Georgia Quick Start, an internationally acclaimed workforce training program that provides customized training free-of-charge to qualified new, expanding and existing businesses.
Georgia Quick Start is a division of the Technical College System of Georgia and has more than 50 years of experience in automotive-related advanced manufacturing.
Quick Start works with companies including Kia, Toya Tire, Kumho Tire, Hyundai Powertech and Hyundai Motor Group, specializing in automotive transmissions.
In February, Georgia Quick Start broke ground on the first training center in Georgia dedicated exclusively to the EV market.
The training center is located on state property adjacent to the metaplant near Ellabell and will train employees for Hyundai as well as affiliate companies such as Mobis, Glovis and Transys.
Georgia Quick Start has facilitated the establishment of new companies and industries in Georgia and played an important role when those companies planned expansions.
source_title: Georgia’s Battery-Powered Future - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric transportation industry.
Since 2018, 35 EV-related projects have contributed $23 billion in investments in Georgia.
In May 2022, Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) announced plans to open its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in Georgia.
HMG began construction on the new facility in January 2023.
Full production at the HMG facility is expected in the first half of 2025.
The HMG facility will have an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
In December 2021, Rivian announced its largest manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Rivian's Georgia facility is slated to start production in 2026.
Rivian's Georgia facility will have an annual capacity of 400,000 vehicles.

Georgia is rapidly expanding across multiple EV supply chain segments including cathode and anode manufacturing, chemical and mineral solutions, battery manufacturing, metals and aluminum manufacturing, and battery recycling.
Georgia's diverse workforce is prepared to meet all skill levels from high-end to assembly to hourly.
The local workforce includes experienced engineers, electricians, machinists, software developers, and cybersecurity specialists.
State-sponsored training facilities, top-notch education programs, and nationally ranked colleges provide Georgia businesses with talent for the EV industry.
At the high school level, Georgia's Department of Education is working with industry leaders to develop the Electric Vehicle Career Pathway.
The Electric Vehicle Career Pathway includes EV-specific coursework to prepare students for careers in the industry.
In 2021, more than 4,000 EVs were sold in Georgia.
EV sales in Georgia represented a total annual growth of 29% in 2021.
As of September 2022, more than 42,500 electric vehicles were registered in Georgia.
Public and private entities are offering incentives and suggesting policies to support continued growth of the electric mobility sector.
Georgia is 6th in the nation for public EV charging stations.
Georgia offers more than 1,500 individual public EV charging outlets.
Georgia has more outlets per capita for EV charging than anywhere in the Southeast.
Georgia has more than 1,200 miles of federally designated EV-ready Alternative Fuel Corridors along its interstates and highways.
Georgia is due to receive $135 million in federal funds from the infrastructure spending bill passed by Congress to build EV charging stations.
The state continues to foster public-private partnerships and other business models to continue the statewide expansion of charging infrastructure.
Georgia has a proven record of investing early in resources, infrastructure, and job skills needed to support the future of manufacturing.
The Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance was created to grow the electric mobility ecosystem in the state.
The alliance aims to strengthen Georgia's position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.
The Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance is led by the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
The alliance includes partners from the private and public sectors, government, electric utilities, education, nonprofits, and other stakeholders.
On December 16, 2021, Governor Brian Kemp announced that Rivian Inc. would expand its manufacturing operations with a second U.S. plant in Georgia.
Rivian will invest $5 billion in a carbon-conscious campus in Georgia.
On May 23, 2023, Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hanon Systems would expand operations in Georgia during an economic development trip to Israel.
Georgia is at the epicenter of the nation's electric vehicle revolution.
Georgia's system of higher education is working to drive the EV industry forward.
An Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE) Tax Credit offers a $250 rebate.
A Plug-In Electric Vehicle Charging Rate is available through the Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance.
source_title: Electric Vehicle Manufacturing | Shenzhen Car Connector Co.,Ltd

facts: Rivian announced it is increasing its initial production capacity to 300,000 vehicles annually for its future manufacturing facility in Stanton Springs North, Georgia.
The increased capacity represents a 50 percent increase over the initially planned capacity of 200,000 units.
The capacity increase is intended to facilitate a lower cost per unit and provide significant room for future expansion in later phases.
Rivian has worked with the U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Energy Dominance Financing (EDF) to update its DOE loan.
The total DOE loan is aligned with the updated facility design and roadmap at up to $4.5 billion ($4,006 million principal and $494 million capitalized interest) for the initial phase.
Vertical construction at the Georgia plant is expected to commence in 2026.
Rivian expects to first draw on the DOE loan by early 2027.
Vehicle production at the facility is expected to begin in late 2028.
Preparations are underway for the development of the stamping press area, one of the most capital-intensive and technically demanding projects within the plant from a construction perspective.
Progress on the facility’s primary buildings is expected to accelerate this summer as they begin to take shape.
Rivian recently announced a partnership with Uber.
The company is expected to build up to 50,000 robotaxis at its Georgia plant beginning in late 2028 under the partnership with Uber.
Rivian Founder and CEO RJ Scaringe stated that the R2 vehicle model dramatically expands their market opportunity.
RJ Scaringe noted that thousands of dedicated people will soon work in the Georgia plant to help scale American manufacturing and ensure U.S. leadership in innovation and technology.
The capacity increase is expected to boost cost efficiency, support thousands of U.S. manufacturing jobs, and bolster American tech leadership on the global stage.
source_title: Rivian Announces Optimized Capacity Plan for Georgia Plant - Green Stock News

facts: Hyundai Motor Group’s Metaplant is located in Ellabell, Ga.
The facility represents a US $12.6 billion investment in U.S.-based manufacturing of EVs and batteries.
Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 SUVs are assembled on surgically spotless assembly lines at the plant.
Consumer adoption of EVs had started slowing as of the reporting period.
The U.S. federal government’s $7,500 clean-car tax credit was being phased out.
The Ioniq 9 is a three-row SUV tailored to the United States’ plus-size tastes.
Each vehicle features a 110.3-kilowatt-hour battery pack that sits below the floor.
The battery pack is shielded to prevent or limit damage in a collision.
The battery is part of an advanced 800-volt architecture for ultrafast DC charging.
A $4.3 billion joint venture between Hyundai and LG Energy Solution is building a battery plant near the Savannah-area factory.
The joint venture battery plant is on track to produce lithium-ion cells for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis models in 2026.
The battery facility encompasses 11 low-roofed buildings covering 697,000 square meters (70 hectares) with pale green walls designed to blend into the Georgia countryside.
The Metaplant is backed by $2.1 billion in state subsidies.
It is the largest public development project in Georgia’s history.
The plant covers 70 hectares and is the centerpiece of Hyundai’s $12.6 billion total investment in the state.
U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents raided the battery factory in September, detaining and deporting more than 300 South Korean workers.
The plant's creation was accelerated by the Biden administration’s pro-EV policies.
It was the centerpiece of Republican Gov. Brian Kemp’s bid to make Georgia “the electric mobility capital of the country.”
Hyundai executives call the Metaplant North America’s most automated car factory and the most advanced full-scale factory among Hyundai Motor Co.’s 12 global manufacturing facilities.
The facility rivals or surpasses Japan’s most advanced plants, such as those operated by Toyota.
Automakers are enlisting armies of AI-controlled robots that can potentially work 24/7 without asking for raises or lunch breaks to compete with China.
The factory may eventually employ 8,500 people directly and 7,000 satellite workers.
The plant has an annual capacity of 500,000 cars, which is more than Tesla’s Texas Gigafactory but less than Tesla’s Shanghai plant.
During the past summer, 1,340 humans were sufficient to operate the assembly lines for two Ioniq models.
“Meta Pros” working on the assembly lines earn an average of $58,100 a year, which is 35 percent higher than the average in Bryan County, Ga.
The cavernous welding hall houses about 475 robots piecing together car chassis.
A steel stamping plant uses robots to stamp roofs and body panels and stow them in overhead racks without requiring ear protection due to its quiet operation.
Human workers park their cars beneath solar roofs that generate up to 5 percent of the plant’s electricity.
A fleet of 21 Hyundai-owned Xcient hydrogen fuel-cell trucks carries parts from suppliers with zero tailpipe emissions.
Hyundai’s goal is to obtain 100 percent of the Metaplant’s energy from renewables by 2030.
The body build line adapts to changing production mixes to meet customer orders and has built-in flexibility to assemble future models.
The Metaplant’s welding shop houses about 500 industrial robots that piece together the “body-in-white” prior to adding subassemblies like suspension, power train, body trim, and interior.
Hyundai is experimenting with humanoid robots to perform welding tasks to reduce labor costs and scrapped materials.

“Body-complete” robots mount front doors onto Ioniq 5s using machine vision and laser-measurement systems to ensure exact fit and install mounting bolts to exact torque specifications validated for safety and quality standards.
The factory utilizes 850 robots to unload parts from trucks at the docks.
About 300 automated guided vehicles (AGVs) glide silently across the factory floor without tracks, trained to stop for humans.
AGVs use cameras and sensors to navigate, slow or stop to avoid collisions, and emit spoken warnings to human workers in their path.
An AI-based procurement-and-logistics system allows AGVs to allocate and deliver parts to workstations for “just in time” delivery.

Adjustable-height carriers elevate Ioniq 5s for access to central fasteners and plugs for suspension components and the high-voltage battery prior to the “marriage” of upper and lower vehicle sections.
On the final assembly line, an Ioniq 9’s “top hat” is married to a lower “skateboard” structure containing electric motors, battery, and suspension.
Finished cars undergo testing including a water bath for leaks and a quick road test outdoors.
A “collaborative” robot is being used for the first time at a Hyundai factory to install bulky, heavy doors on the assembly line without needing fencing or isolation.
Spot, robotic quadrupeds from Hyundai-owned Boston Dynamics use camera vision, sensors, and “athletic intelligence” to autonomously recharge and inspect body welds for defects.
Atlas, a humanoid robot from Boston Dynamics, is being trained to sense environments and manipulate parts in complex sequences for future deployment in global factories.
A backup station allows high-voltage battery fasteners to be installed manually if automated systems require servicing, ensuring the assembly line keeps running.

Freshly painted Ioniq 5s and 9s move along a conveyor on a windowed bridge visible from Interstate 16 in Ellabell, Ga., as an intentional glimpse of factory operations for drivers seeing their tax dollars at work.
Hyundai is already building a second battery plant in Georgia and a steel plant in Louisiana as part of an expanded pledge of $21 billion in U.S. investment through 2028.
The first Ioniq 5 rolled off the Metaplant line on 3 October 2024.
Production of the larger Ioniq 9 kicked off in March 2025.
Immediately after final inspection, a human driver takes the car for its first road test on a track just outside the factory.
source_title: The Hyundai Metaplant: A New Era in EV Manufacturing - IEEE Spectrum

facts: The 2025 Kia EV9 is the first electric vehicle assembled in the state of Georgia.
The EV9 joins the award-winning Telluride, Sorento, and Sportage SUVs currently assembled at Kia’s West Point facility.
Assembly of the all-electric 2025 EV9 three-row SUV began on Thursday, May 30, 2024, at Kia Georgia’s assembly plant in West Point.
The vehicle was introduced by SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon, President and CEO for Kia North America and Kia America.
The EV9 was driven off the assembly line by Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp and James Watson, Kia Georgia’s Vice President of Production.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the expansion of Kia’s operations in Georgia and the addition of the new product line is an example of state and local partners working with a valued job creator.
Governor Kemp thanked Kia Georgia team members for choosing the Peach State as they work to become the e-mobility capital of the nation.
The EV9 joins the lineup of SUVs currently being assembled on Kia Georgia’s assembly line: Telluride, Sorento, and Sportage.
This launch is the culmination of a more than $200 million expansion.
The expansion added approximately 200 new jobs at the plant.
The expansion provides flexibility to assemble both EVs and traditional products along the same assembly line.
SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon stated that the launch is the latest example of Kia’s commitment to the U.S. market and Georgia.
Through Plan S, Kia aims to be a leader in the e-mobility sector for years to come.
The EV9 has won World Car of the Year, World EV of the Year, and North American Utility Vehicle of the Year.
Stuart Countess is President and CEO of Kia Georgia.
Countess noted that almost 15 years ago, on Nov. 16, 2009, the first Kia vehicle rolled off the same assembly line.
Countess credited the Kia Georgia team with driving milestones and turning challenges into opportunities.
Kia Georgia is adding its flagship EV to the facility to enter a new era with a focus on assembling high-quality vehicles in West Point.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Corporation, which is based in Seoul, Korea.
Kia Georgia has an annual capacity of 350,000 units.
Kia Georgia is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Ga.
Kia Georgia began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
Kia Georgia produces the Telluride SUV, Sorento SUV (excludes HEV and PHEV models), Sportage SUV (excludes HEV and PHEV models), and the all-electric EV9 SUV.
source_title: Kia Georgia begins assembly of the 2025 Kia EV9 All-Electric SUV - Kia Georgia

facts: Kia Georgia has started production of the 2027 Telluride HEV at its West Point facility.
The ceremonial first vehicle represents the plant’s 5 millionth unit.
The 2027 Telluride HEV is the first hybrid electric vehicle assembled in the state of Georgia.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp attended the production start event.
The West Point plant is the sole global production site for the Telluride model.
The Telluride has been manufactured at the West Point facility since its launch in 2019.
The West Point facility has operated since 2009.
The West Point facility has grown into one of the largest employers in the region.

Sean Yoon, president and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that reaching five million vehicles and introducing Kia’s first US-assembled HEV is a testament to both Kia’s storied history and its direction going forward.
source_title: Kia Georgia hits five million units as 2027 Telluride rolls out | Automotive World

facts: EV charging in Atlanta sits at a turning point in 2026.

Effective January 1, 2026, the City of Atlanta's 2025 EV Readiness Ordinance (25-O-1011) mandates EV infrastructure in new construction and major renovations across commercial, multifamily, and residential developments.
The ordinance introduces three defined tiers: EVSE Capable, EVSE Ready, and EVSE Installed.
Commercial and multifamily developments must include at least 20% EVSE Installed spaces and 20% EVSE Capable spaces.
City-owned projects must meet higher thresholds: 25% EVSE Installed and 50% EVSE Capable.

The ordinance applies to both parking structures and surface parking lots, and includes detailed fire safety provisions such as emergency shutoff switches, equipment spacing, and ventilation requirements for enclosed garages.
EV charging in Atlanta runs on two primary charging levels: Level 2 (240V, adds 11-54 miles/hour) and Level 3/DCFC.
Georgia law now allows energy dispensed by chargers to be priced based on kilowatt-hour (kWh) usage versus time-based usage.
Georgia Power transitioned to kWh pricing in 2023, so charging prices are now based on what a driver's vehicle actually consumes.
J1772 is the standard North American AC charging connector for Level 2 AC and is compatible with most non-Tesla EVs.
CCS (Combined Charging System) is the standard DC fast-charging connector compatible with most non-Tesla EVs.
NACS (North American Charging Standard, Tesla connector now industry-wide) supports Level 2 + DC Fast and is compatible with Tesla + newer 2025+ EVs.
CHAdeMO is the Japanese DC fast charging standard compatible with Nissan LEAF and older models.
Older EVs with CCS charging ports need a CCS-to-NACS adapter to access Tesla Superchargers, with quality adapters typically costing $100 to $200.
Midtown Atlanta is one of the city's densest zones for EV charging stations in Atlanta.
Colony Square has free, dedicated EV charging stations.
The Atlanta Botanical Garden has eight EV chargers for guests.
The Woodruff Arts Center is near several parking decks with EV chargers.
The Four Seasons Hotel Atlanta offers two EV charging stations for guests.
ChargePoint operates an extensive network of Level 2 stations across parking decks near Centennial Olympic Park, the Georgia Aquarium, and the World of Coca-Cola.
Level 3 DC fast charging at the Atlantic Station Parking Deck in Downtown Atlanta costs $0.30 per minute.
Buckhead offers a concentration of Atlanta EV charging stations tied to luxury retail and hospitality.
The St. Regis Atlanta offers three charging stations, including two Tesla outlets, for guests.
Buckhead Village District features two EV charging stations alongside its designer boutiques and restaurants.

Avalon in Alpharetta, about 30 minutes north of downtown, offers a shopping and dining district with 16 EV charging stations, including a Tesla Supercharger capable of delivering up to 200 miles of range in just 15 minutes.
ChargePoint covers offices, malls, and hotels with Level 2 + DC Fast at per kWh pricing set by host.
EVgo focuses on urban DC fast charging in city core at per kWh + session fee.
Tesla Supercharger covers highway corridors + destination with DC Fast (up to 250 kW) at per kWh pricing.
Electrify America covers highway + big-box retail with DC Fast (up to 350 kW) at per kWh or per minute pricing.
Blink covers community locations and parking with Level 2 + DC Fast at per kWh or per hour pricing.
The Georgia State Transportation Board approved a $24.4 million grant to build 26 new charging stations around the state.

The grant is part of the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) Program, funded through the Infrastructure Investment in Jobs Act, which allocated $5 billion nationwide over five years for states to expand EV charging networks.
Six privately owned companies will design, install, finance, operate, and maintain stations equipped with DCFC ports for at least five years.
Each site will include four DCFCs capable of operating simultaneously, 24 hours a day, seven days a week.
Depending on the vehicle, drivers will be able to fully recharge in as little as 20 minutes.
One goal of this initiative is to make public EV fast charging available every 50 miles along the entire I-85 route.
Georgia can keep spending $135 million in federal funds allocated for EV charging infrastructure, including sections of Interstates 16 and 95.
Georgia Power, the dominant utility serving metro Atlanta, offers time-of-use (TOU) rates that reward off-peak charging.
Georgia Power's EV program offers peak rates of $0.20/kWh and off-peak rates of $0.09/kWh, a 56% savings when charging overnight.
Georgia imposes a per-kWh excise tax on electricity distributed by public charging stations, which is factored into the prices at the station.
Atlanta's dense urban core means a significant share of EV drivers live in multifamily buildings.
Electric vehicles present new challenges for older apartment and condo developments that require retrofitting and new power sources to offer chargers.
Charging during off-peak hours (typically late evening to early morning) reduces both grid demand and cost.
Time-of-use rates can reduce charging costs by 30–50% when charging overnight.
Atlanta's destination charging at hotels, malls, and attractions is often Level 2.
A 2–3 hour visit to Lenox Square or Phipps Plaza in Buckhead can add 30–60 miles of range at no extra cost.
Both I-85 and I-75 are designated EV charging corridors through Georgia.
The Georgia Department of Transportation has nominated eight interstates and two US Route/State Highways as EV charging corridors that are now classified as EV ready.
Stations near Mercedes-Benz Stadium, Hartsfield-Jackson Airport, and Atlantic Station see heavy use during events and peak travel periods.
Level 2 public charging typically runs $0.20–$0.40 per kWh.
DC fast charging at EVgo starts at $0.34 per kWh plus a session fee.
Certain destination charging locations, including Colony Square in Midtown, offer free Level 2 charging.
The City of Atlanta's 2025 EV Readiness Ordinance (25-O-1011) requires new commercial and multifamily developments to include at least 20% fully installed EV charging spaces.
Tesla Superchargers in Atlanta are open to non-Tesla EVs.
Drivers with CCS connectors need a CCS-to-NACS adapter, which typically costs $100–$200.
Newer 2025+ EVs with native NACS ports connect directly to Tesla Superchargers.
Tesla charges non-member drivers a higher per-kWh rate, while a monthly membership reduces that cost.
Atlanta EV charging stations on the Tesla network include locations at Atlantic Station and across the Buckhead and Midtown corridors.

The Atlanta Regional Commission's federally funded CFI grant program is placing free public charging stations at everyday destinations including shopping centers, pharmacies, and government service centers across the 19-county metro region.
There are 2153 total Level 2 Stations in Atlanta, representing 92% of Level 2 Stations.
There are 181 total Level 3 Stations in Atlanta, representing 8% of Level 3 Stations.
The percentage of free stations is 22%, with a total number of free stations at 514.
The total number of charging stations in Atlanta is 2334.
Main networks operating in the area are ChargePoint, EnviroSpark, and Blink.

Real-time availability, payment, and connector filtering across networks

source_title: Atlanta, Georgia EV Charging Stations Info | ChargeHub

facts: Aims to recycle lithium-ion batteries from vehicles and other uses.
Will build its first large plant in suburban Atlanta, investing $43 million.
Hiring 150 people for the new facility.
Battery Resourcers is based in Worcester, Massachusetts.
The company will take over an existing building in Covington, Georgia.
Plans to ramp up to full operation by August.
Betting that the battery supply chain, currently concentrated in Asia, is shifting to other regions.
Claims its recycling process will be cleaner and cheaper than mining new materials.
CEO Michael O’Kronley stated that automotive manufacturers have many discarded batteries and scrap with few responsible and cost-effective disposal options.
The recycling process involves taking apart, discharging, and shredding batteries before processing the material.
Produces recovered metals including lithium, cobalt, nickel, and manganese, as well as graphite and other engineered ingredients for new batteries.
Uses methods claimed to recover more than 97% of metals with zero toxic waste.
Recovered material outperforms newly mined ingredients.
The Covington plant will have an annual recycling capacity of 30,000 metric tons (33,000 tons) of batteries.
This capacity equals approximately 70,000 vehicle batteries per year.
The project is part of Georgia officials' efforts to build an electric vehicle supply chain.
SK Innovation is building a $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, northeast of Atlanta, which will employ 2,600 workers.
Rivian Automotive announced plans for a $5 billion battery and assembly plant just east of Covington projected to employ 7,500 workers.
Vice President of Marketing & Government Affairs Roger Lin stated the Georgia site was chosen for its proximity to EV and battery makers and its strong workforce.
Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that battery recycling is a vital part of the EV supply chain and reducing reliance on unstable global regions is crucial.
The company operates pilot plants in Westborough, Massachusetts, and Novi, Michigan, in addition to Worcester.
Plans to build two additional plants in Europe by the end of the year.
Aims to eventually expand operations to Asia with a goal of processing 150,000 metric tons (165,000 tons) of material per year.
Has raised more than $90 million from investors.
Signed a contract to process spent batteries from Honda and Acura vehicles.
Investors include the venture capital arms of Jaguar Land Rover and Hitachi.
The company will receive a competitive incentive package from Georgia.
Can claim an income tax credit allowing an annual deduction of $3,000 per job from state income taxes, up to $2.25 million over five years.
Workers must earn at least $28,000 a year to qualify for the income tax credit.
May also receive local property tax breaks.
source_title: $43M battery recycling plant set for Georgia, hiring 150 | AP News

facts: Ascend Elements is a Massachusetts-based maker of battery materials that announced a deal to reprocess old batteries into cathode material.
The company reached a billion-dollar deal with an unnamed automaker to supply recycled cathode material.
Cathode material accounts for more than one third of the expense of a finished EV battery pack.
Ascend is building a new cathode plant in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
The contract covers cathode for at least 40 gigawatt hours (GWh) of batteries per year.
The contracted volume is enough to power 750,000 electric cars.
In 2021, the contracted volume would have satisfied the entire US EV industry.
BloombergNEF data indicates five times as much cathode will be needed by 2025.
This contract marks the second major deal for US-made cathode.
The deal follows a similar agreement between Nevada-based Redwood Materials Inc. and Panasonic Holdings Corp. in November.
Most of the world’s cathode currently comes from China.
Demand for US-sourced battery materials is increasing due to Inflation Reduction Act requirements that EV batteries be built in North America using materials processed by close US trading partners.
Those incentives and others pushed by the Biden administration have spurred more than $70 billion of investments since last August along America’s Battery Belt.
The Battery Belt stretches roughly from Michigan to Georgia.
Ascend CEO Mike O’Kronley stated that the level of manufacturing investment in the US is unprecedented in decades.
O’Kronley said this represents one of the very first commercial realizations of what the US is trying to do nationally.
Ascend was founded in 2015 by two battery researchers at Worcester Polytechnic Institute.
The founders developed a process for extracting complex cathode chemistries directly from shredded battery materials without individually extracting each mineral.
Ascend operates a recycling plant in Covington, Georgia.
The Georgia plant recycles battery materials out of discarded consumer electronics and manufacturing scrap from plants run by Honda Motor Co., Hitachi Ltd., and others.
Ascend plans to begin installing manufacturing equipment at its Kentucky facility by the end of the year.
Cathode deliveries will start in the fourth quarter of next year.
Full-volume production is scheduled for 2025.
The contract is for a minimum of $1 billion of cathode delivered over no more than five years.
The buyer has options to expand purchases to $5 billion of cathode under similar terms.
Ascend said the unnamed automaker requested not to be named.
O’Kronley worked in auto manufacturing for 30 years before becoming CEO at Ascend in 2020.
In 2016, O’Kronley served as head of corporate strategy at A123 Systems LLC, a producer of lithium-ion batteries.
While at A123, O’Kronley received early samples of recycled cathode from Ascend and oversaw testing against a leading supplier.
The testing showed that Ascend’s material performed better than the material A123 was buying.
Following successful testing, A123 became an investor in Ascend.
O’Kronley joined Ascend’s board after the investment.
Ascend plans to close a Series D funding round this summer to finance additional factory expansion.
It has not yet been decided whether additional operations will be built in Kentucky or elsewhere.
The company’s growth was assisted by $480 million in matching grants from the US infrastructure law.
O’Kronley described the grants as key to proving the company could build a factory and translate into commercial contracts.
source_title: US battery belt grows with $1 billion Kentucky cathode deal - MINING.COM

facts: Anovion announced plans to invest $800 million to build a factory in southwest Georgia.
The company is trying to become a major American-based supplier of graphite for electric batteries.
The factory will hire 400 workers.
Anovion Technologies is based in Chicago.
The company will produce synthetic graphite anodes in Bainbridge, Georgia.
Production of synthetic graphite anodes for lithium-ion batteries is scheduled to start in 2025.
The plant supports Georgia's initiative to recruit electric vehicle manufacturers and their suppliers.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated the announcement furthers his goal of making Georgia the nation's electric mobility capital.
Most battery graphite is currently manufactured outside the United States.
Georgia economic development officials report over 40 EV-related projects announced in the state since 2020.
These projects pledge $22 billion in investment and 28,000 jobs.
Anovion was formed last year by combining the graphite businesses of Pyrotek and Amsted Graphite Materials.
The company received new investment from private equity firm Monomyth Group.
Anovion operates existing facilities in Sanborn, New York, and Clarksburg, West Virginia.
The company previously announced plans to build a graphite factory near Muscle Shoals, northwest Alabama.
The Georgia factory will have a production capacity of 44,000 tons (40,000 metric tonnes).
Anovion received a $117 million grant under the federal infrastructure law last year.
The grant will help finance the Georgia plant and improvements at existing facilities.
The company described planned pay levels only as "competitive."
Anovion CEO Eric Stopka stated the company was attracted to Georgia by its workforce, research universities, and growing solar energy production.
CEO Eric Stopka stated the company looks forward to securing the country's electrified future with support from Georgia and federal partners.
Anovion executives aim to reach 150,000 tons of production in the coming years.
Synthetic graphite is manufactured from carbon products like petroleum coke (an oil refining byproduct) heated to super-high temperatures.
The manufacturing process requires huge amounts of electricity, making power costs a key location factor.
Anovion aims to become carbon neutral by using renewable energy and more efficient furnaces.
CEO Eric Stopka cited Georgia's proximity to low- and carbon-free energy sources as a key reason for the location choice.
Anovion may qualify for a state income tax credit allowing an annual deduction of $3,500 per job for five years.
The tax credit requires workers to earn at least $31,300 annually.
The potential tax credit value could reach $7 million.
Anovion may qualify for property tax breaks from Bainbridge and Decatur County.
The state of Georgia will pay an undisclosed amount to train workers.
The announcement marks the second major industrial project in two years for Bainbridge, located in the far southwest corner of Georgia.
Danimer Scientific, a biodegradable plastic maker, announced a $700 million factory and 400 jobs in Bainbridge in 2021.
Decatur County Commission Chairman Pete Stephens stated diversifying the industry mix is necessary for competitiveness and growth.
source_title: Anovion, maker of graphite for electric batteries, plans $800 million factory in southwest Georgia | AP News

facts: Stryten Energy LLC and Largo Clean Energy Corp. formed the Storion Energy, LLC joint venture on February 4, 2025.
The joint venture will deliver price-competitive vanadium electrolyte via a unique leasing model to drive rapid commercialization and adoption of vanadium redox flow batteries.
Stryten Critical E-Storage LLC signed agreements with Largo Clean Energy Corp., a subsidiary of Largo Inc., to establish the joint venture.
Storion Energy’s mission is to remove barriers to entry for battery manufacturers by enabling domestic sourcing of price-competitive components for flow batteries, including vanadium electrolyte.
The company combines access to high-quality vanadium supply from the only operating Western Hemisphere vanadium mine with domestic electrolyte production.
This establishes a fully integrated vertical supply chain for utility-scale vanadium redox flow batteries used in long-duration energy storage applications.
Storion will leverage a proprietary continuous manufacturing process and patented purification process to offer high-quality, domestically produced electrolyte.
The initiative supports the DOE’s goal to deliver reliable energy at $0.05/kWh by 2030.

Through exclusive access to Largo Physical Vanadium Corp., Storion offers a vanadium electrolyte leasing model to help VRFB compete with up-front capital costs of lithium-ion battery energy storage systems.
Mike Judd, CEO and President of Stryten Energy, announced the formation aims to bring a cost-effective domestic supply chain for vanadium electrolyte and component parts.
Judd noted VRFBs are safe and reliable long-duration energy storage solutions that provide energy resilience for commercial and industrial applications requiring continuous power.
Vanadium electrolyte typically constitutes a large portion of the overall VRFB system cost depending on market prices.
Largo Physical Vanadium’s leasing model allows investors to invest in physical vanadium assets while they are stored in VRFBs.
Storion holds exclusive rights to offer LPV-owned vanadium to the market as leased vanadium electrolyte.
Francesco D’Alessio, President of LCE, stated the joint venture accelerates LPV’s vanadium electrolyte leasing offering to the North American long-duration energy storage market.
D’Alessio noted Storion’s competitive VRFB pricing model is expected to challenge lithium dominance for utility-scale deployments and secure U.S. leadership in this energy infrastructure application.
VRFB technology provides safe and reliable long-duration energy storage for greater than four hours to ensure grid stability and facilitate renewable utilization across the U.S.
With proper maintenance, VRFBs can last more than 20 years without electrolyte losing storage capacity.
The battery's longevity complements wind and solar installation lifespans.
The infinitely recyclable electrolyte offers a near-limitless cycle life.
Systems are independently scalable in power and capacity.
There is potential for substantial cost reduction as discharge time increases.
Storion Energy has locations in Alpharetta, Georgia and Wilmington, Massachusetts.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, and provides energy storage solutions across Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military, and Government sectors.
Stryten’s technologies include advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.
Largo Physical Vanadium Corp. aims to provide exchange-traded investment exposure to physical vanadium for steel, aerospace, and energy storage industries.
LPV’s strategy includes actively supplying vanadium to VRFB end users to advance renewable energy integration in long duration storage.
Largo Inc. is a globally recognized supplier of high-quality vanadium and ilmenite products sourced from its Maracás Menchen Mine in Brazil.
Largo holds 50% ownership of Storion Energy and trades on the Nasdaq Stock Market and Toronto Stock Exchange under the symbol “LGO”.
source_title: Storion Energy Launched to Establish a Domestic Supply of Flow Battery Components for Long-Duration Energy Storage

facts: South Korean automotive giant Hyundai Motor Group confirmed plans to build a roughly $5.5 billion electric vehicle assembly and battery plant near Savannah.
The facility will eventually employ about 8,100 workers.
This marks the second major electric vehicle factory announcement in Georgia since December.
State economic development officials are working to turn Georgia into an important manufacturing hub for battery-powered automobiles.
Hyundai officials and Gov. Brian Kemp outlined future operations during a ceremony near the Georgia coast.
The plant will be located on a nearly 3,000-acre, state-owned megasite along I-16 in Bryan County.
Hyundai officials stated they will break ground in 2023 and start production in 2025.
The facility will have an annual capacity for 300,000 EVs.
The new plant will be highly automated and feature state-of-the-art technology with high automation levels.
Hyundai Motor Co. CEO Jaehoon Chang said the facility will be one of the most advanced EV plants in the world.
The project includes a battery manufacturing facility.
Georgia and South Korea share a long history of economic cooperation.
Hyundai has invested $5.1 billion in the U.S. since 2013.
The deal follows pushback over Rivian's planned plant in Bryan County.
Rivian's Georgia plant was initially planned for Bryan County but faced opposition from local residents concerned about traffic and infrastructure impacts.
No cars will roll off the line at the Hyundai plant in Bryan County until 2025.
The project is part of a broader push to attract automotive and battery manufacturing to Georgia.
Rivian, an electric vehicle startup, announced it would build its first U.S. factory in Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai confirms details of electric vehicle factory to be built in South Georgia

facts: Woory Industrial Co., Ltd. is a South Korea-based company that makes heating, ventilation and air conditioning components and systems.
Woory Industrial will invest $18 million into a new manufacturing facility in Dublin, Georgia.
The new facility will create more than 130 jobs.
Gov. Brian Kemp welcomed Woory Industrial as the latest job creator to help South Georgia on the path to becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation.

Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the emerging industry continues to bring historic levels of new jobs and investment to communities across the state thanks to a highly-skilled workforce and reliable infrastructure network.
Gov. Brian Kemp expressed confidence that Woory will have a long and beneficial partnership with Dublin-Laurens County.
The company is developing a new line of products for EVs and hydrogen-fueled cars.
Woory Industrial will supply the Hyundai Metaplant in Bryan County.
Woory Industrial will also supply Rivian's EV facility planned for Social Circle near Athens.
Dublin Mayor Joshua Kight stated that fostering quality and sustainable growth is a goal of the city of Dublin.
Mayor Joshua Kight noted that Woory’s commitment to place an existing manufacturing building back into service helps promote infill development while creating new career paths for the community.
The facility will be located at 404 Kellam Road in Dublin, Georgia.
This facility will be Woory Industrial's first manufacturing location in the United States.
The facility will produce electrical heaters, control units and actuators for EV and internal combustion carmakers in the states.
Operations at the new facility are slated to begin in November.
Fred Williams, chair of the Board of Directors of the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority, welcomed Woory to the Dublin-Laurens County community.
Fred Williams highlighted that Dublin and Laurens County has a rapidly growing automotive sector thanks to its geographic location on Interstate 16 and access to a skilled and trained workforce.
Fred Williams noted that Woory's partnership with area education systems and training providers will ensure training programs are in place to help fill Woory's talent pipeline for years to come.

facts: Savannah will be an EV epicenter thanks to a Hyundai assembly plant slated to open in 2025.
Bryan County will be the epicenter for EV production as Hyundai is set to build its first EV plant in the U.S. by 2025.
Georgia Ports Authority terminals in Savannah and Brunswick will move vehicles from the facility around the world.
Hyundai's local facility is the biggest economic development project in state history.
The facility is part of a larger, industry-wide movement away from fossil fuel-powered cars and trucks and toward electric vehicles.
Jae Hoon Chang, Hyundai Motor’s president and CEO, stated during the Bryan County facility announcement that “This high-tech EV plant represents the future of our business.”
Competitors aggressively pushing into the EV market alongside Hyundai include Toyota, Ford, and BMW.
Toyota will have 70 models to choose from in the coming years and introduced the all-new 2023 bZ4X EV at $42,000.
Nissan announced the Nissan Ambition 2030 campaign to make “40% of U.S. vehicle sales fully electric, with even more to be electrified” by 2030.
Nissan has sold more than 600,000 LEAFs globally and more than 173,000 in the U.S., with its newest EV, the Ariya, joining the lineup in fall 2022.
Average EV price points are $56,500 compared to $47,000 for gas-powered models.
The Nissan LEAF is priced at $27,800, approximately $4,000 less than the gas-powered Nissan Altima.
The Hyundai Kona EV and Hyundai Kona are priced the same at around $34,000.
President Joe Biden signed an executive order in December 2021 to “reach net zero emissions economy-wide by no later than 2050.”
The Obama administration's Climate Action Plan previously aimed to reduce carbon dioxide emissions by 32% from 2005 levels by 2030.
Gas prices hit all-time highs above $5 a gallon in some U.S. markets in June, driving consumer interest in EVs as an economic alternative.
Automakers are prioritizing sleek exterior designs and modern aesthetics for EVs to address early criticism of compact and boxy styling.
General Motors’ Cadillac LYRIQ features a 33-inch cabin display and is positioned with futuristic exterior styling.
Ford's F-150 Lightning was developed based on prospective buyer feedback requesting a modern look for the EV pickup truck version.
Hyundai's IONIQ model has been noted by Edmunds.com for its eye-catching exterior styling.

facts: Hyundai will open an auto plant and EV battery factory in Bryan County in 2025.
The South Korean automaker will invest $5.5 billion and employ 8,100 workers in facilities to be built on a megasite property located adjacent to Interstate 16 near Ellabell.
Trip Tollison serves as president and CEO of the Savannah Economic Development Authority (SEDA).
SEDA signed an agreement with Hyundai on April 25, 2022, at 10:32 a.m.
Tollison described signing the agreement with Hyundai as a big moment that changed Savannah's economy forever.
Hyundai Motors announced plans on Friday to build a $5.5 billion manufacturing facility near Savannah.
The site will employ 8,100 workers between an electric vehicle assembly plant and an adjacent EV battery factory.
The project is the largest single economic development project in Georgia history.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group’s Georgia supplier list has grown with three additional auto parts suppliers announcing plans to establish assembly plants in the state.
Hyundai Industrial Co., a subsidiary of Hyundai Motor Group, is spending more than $24 million to acquire a building in Newnan, GA.
Hyundai Industrial Co. plans to retrofit the acquired facility to produce armrests and headrests for Hyundai’s EV assembly plant under construction in Ellabell, as well as for other OEMs.
Hyundai’s EV assembly plant under construction in Ellabell has an estimated cost of $5.5 billion.
The new Hyundai Industrial Co. factory is expected to create 100 jobs.
South Korean company Hwashin plans to establish a $176 million plant in Dublin, GA.
Hwashin’s new plant will create more than 460 jobs.
Hwashin is a Tier 1 supplier that specializes in crafting chassis and body parts for Hyundai and its subsidiary Kia, as well as for other OEMs.
Production at Hwashin’s Dublin plant is expected to begin in late 2025.
Daechang Seat Corp. USA announced it is investing $72.5 million to build an assembly plant in Savannah, GA.
Daechang Seat Corp. USA’s new plant will create more than 500 jobs.
Daechang Seat Corp. USA specializes in automotive seat frames.
Daechang Seat Corp. USA will join two other auto parts manufacturers, Seoyon E-HWA and PHA, at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The latest investments bring the Peach State an estimated $2.5 billion from a total of 14 suppliers.
The growing EV network is also creating more than 6,000 jobs.
Hyundai Motor Group invested additional funds to increase its supplier network amid the ongoing construction of its EV manufacturing campus.
In September, Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution invested an additional $2 billion toward the $4.3 billion of their joint battery cell manufacturing plant.
Production at the joint battery cell manufacturing plant is slated to begin at the end of 2025.
The joint battery cell manufacturing plant will have enough capacity to produce 300,000 EVs annually.
Hyundai broke ground on its manufacturing campus over a year ago.
Construction has recently begun on Hyundai’s production-related buildings at the campus.
The nearly 3,000-acre megasite will use renewable resources to power its mass production.
Using renewable resources for mass production will help Hyundai reach its goal of becoming carbon neutral by 2045.
