facts: Hyundai Motor Company recently unveiled Phase 2 production goals for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The plant is on pace to manufacture 500,000 hybrids and electric vehicles by 2028 at its site in Ellabell.
HMC made the announcement last week, saying the additional $2.7 billion investment will create 3,000 direct and indirect jobs in Georgia.
Hyundai's initial investment, which was announced in 2022, was $5.5 billion.
A Hyundai official stated that a recent raid should not impact future production of vehicles at the site.

facts: Synthetic graphite maker Anovion Technologies plans to invest $800 million in a manufacturing facility in Bainbridge, Georgia.
The company’s first large-scale facility is expected to produce 40,000 metric tons of synthetic graphite annually for lithium-ion batteries.
CEO Eric Stopka stated that existing infrastructure makes Southwest Georgia an attractive location for Anovion’s new facility.
CEO Eric Stopka noted that proximity to other battery and EV manufacturing plants will allow the company to further reduce its carbon footprint.
The 1.5 million-square-foot facility will be located near low- and carbon-free energy sources.
The facility site will offer short supply chains and access to rail infrastructure.

CEO Eric Stopka said Anovion conducted a thorough search to identify a location addressing energy, transportation, logistics, and human capital needs to produce high-quality and sustainable synthetic graphite anode material.
Operations at the new Georgia facility are expected to come online in late 2025.
The company plans to hire for over 400 jobs in technical, production, and engineering roles, as well as administrative and management positions.
Anovion Technologies received $117 million from the Department of Energy last year under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
The DOE funding will go toward building the new Georgia facility and expanding its hydro-powered manufacturing facility in Sanborn, New York.
The expanded Sanborn, New York site will provide support and research in addition to production.
Since receiving its federal grant in October, Anovion Technologies has begun signing supply deals with battery makers.
In November, Anovion Technologies partnered with American Battery Factory to supply materials for the latter's lithium-iron phosphate battery production network.
Hyundai Motor Group is building an EV and battery manufacturing hub near Savannah, Georgia.
Battery maker SK On announced plans last month for a $5 billion EV battery manufacturing facility in Bartow County, Georgia.

facts: Korean electric motor manufacturer Seojin Mobility will invest US $300 million in its first Mexico plant.
The Mexico plant is now under construction in the northern state of Nuevo León.
The first stage of construction is expected to be completed by February 2025 at a cost of $200 million.
The second $100 million phase will involve the construction of hybrid motors.
Seojin Mobility anticipates the plant, once operational, will create 300 jobs.
A groundbreaking ceremony was held on March 28 in the city of Escobedo, just north of Monterrey.
Byung Hyuk Kang, general director of Seojin Mobility México, and José Antonio Quiroga, the local municipal president, presided over the groundbreaking ceremony.
Seojin Mobility will build motors for electric vehicles for the Hyundai plant in Savannah, Georgia.
Seojin Mobility has plans to collaborate with Kia Motors’ operations in the city of Pesquería, 36 kilometers west of Escobedo.
When asked about building motors for the Tesla plant in Santa Catarina (27 kilometers southwest of Escobedo), Byung Hyuk said that is unlikely, though not impossible.
Seojin Mobility will focus on four Hyundai models in the United States.
Kia Motors has a plan for a small model electric vehicle in 2025.
Seojin Mobility is headquartered in Siheung, South Korea.
Seojin Mobility is the primary supplier for Hyundai and Kia and also has contracts with GM, Renault, Ferrari and Volvo.
The proximity of the Tesla plant presents several advantages for Seojin Mobility.
Byung Hyuk stated that with Tesla being nearby, they might find an opportunity to develop new products.
The new plant will serve to strengthen Seojin’s association with Hyundai and Kia México and develop the local electric vehicle market.
Seojin Mobility chose Escobedo for its location near the industrial center of Monterrey and just 150 miles from Laredo, Texas.
Seojin Mobility chose Escobedo due to the presence of a qualified workforce.
Escobedo has been preparing to take advantage of nearshoring by creating an industrial district.
The municipality’s development plan includes the construction of two hotels at a cost of $1 billion.

facts: Bolt Energy USA is the leading golf cart battery manufacturer in the US and Canada.
Customers can buy high quality, reliable, and the most powerful golf cart lithium batteries in the industry from Bolt Energy.
The company serves Central Georgia, Augusta, Statesboro, Griffin, McDonough, Savannah, Pooler, and Aiken, South Carolina.
Business hours are Monday through Friday from 8 am to 5 pm, and Saturday from 8 am to 12 pm.

facts: Aspen Aerogels Inc., a Massachusetts-based electric vehicle supplier, has canceled plans to build a manufacturing facility in Bulloch County near Statesboro.
The company announced the cancellation during an investor call on Thursday.
Instead of the Georgia plant, Aspen Aerogels will increase production capacity at its existing East Providence, R.I., facility.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp originally announced the Statesboro project in 2022.
The canceled project was planned to involve a $325 million investment.
The canceled project was expected to create more than 250 jobs.
Production at the canceled Statesboro plant was originally expected to begin in late 2023.
Aspen Aerogels manufactures thermal barriers designed to insulate electric car batteries from fires.
source_title: Company would have created 250 jobs in Statesboro, cancels plans to build there

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on January 5, 2023 that Ecoplastic Corporation will create 456 new jobs in a new manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
The company will invest approximately $205 million in the new facility.
The company will serve the Hyundai Metaplant from Statesboro, where it will have close proximity to Georgia's ports system.
Ecoplastic Corporation was established in 1984 and is a Korea-based company that manufactures plastic automobile parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) and other suppliers.

Ecoplastic is a Tier-1 supplier for Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) and Kia, engaging in the manufacturing of automotive interior and exterior plastic parts including bumpers, consoles, trims, and plastic molds.

Shang Han, President and CEO of Ecoplastic, stated the company is establishing a production facility for electric vehicle parts in Bulloch County to provide a stable supply of high-quality products to client HMGMA.
Ecoplastic’s new facility will be located at 4822 Highway 301 South Register near Statesboro.
The new plant will be capable of injection molding and painting, producing exterior parts, bumpers and garnishes, interior parts, consoles, and trims for electric vehicles.
The company expects to begin operations by October 2024.
Ecoplastic will be hiring for HR, finance, parts development, quality control, facility maintenance, injection, and paint production positions.
Billy Allen serves as the Development Authority of Bulloch County Chair.

Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy at the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team, represented the agency on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Ecoplastic has decades of experience as a supplier for Kia and Hyundai and will help build the supplier network for the Hyundai metaplant in Bryan County.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry, with more than 30 EV-related projects announcing more than $21 billion in investment and more than 26,300 jobs since 2020.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground on October 25 on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in neighboring Bryan County.

Offsite suppliers connected to the Hyundai project were predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state, and projects announced to date have already exceeded this amount.
Ecoplastic Corporation was established in June 1984 as Apollo Ind Co., Ltd. and established its first Technical Research Center in 1987.
The company was publicly listed on the Korean Stock Exchange in March 2000.
Ecoplastic currently employs more than 770 employees and recorded sales revenue of more than $700 million in 2021.
source_title: Longtime Kia and HMG Supplier to Build Manufacturing Facility in Bulloch County | Development Authority of Bulloch County

facts: Former President Joe Biden initiated a $5 billion program in 2022 aimed at building 500,000 electric vehicle chargers across the nation.
President Trump signed the "Unleashing American Energy" executive order terminating the EV charger program earlier this month.
WJCL 22 News interviewed Rep. Buddy Carter of Georgia's 1st Congressional District regarding the impact of federal decisions on Georgia's economic outlook.
Rep. Buddy Carter stated that the current administration has "hit the ground running" and had a positive effect on Georgia's economy.
Trump's administration signed 68 executive orders, including one to eliminate the EV vehicle goal by 2030.
The National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Formula Program, which would have facilitated nationwide construction of EV chargers, was eliminated.
Hyundai is producing EV vehicles in Georgia, bringing scrutiny to the state's future EV infrastructure.
Rep. Buddy Carter stated that the future of EVs in Georgia is strong and bright, predicting a market will exist for them.
Rep. Buddy Carter criticized government intervention in consumer choices, stating that when the government picks winners and losers, consumers lose.
EV supplier Aspen Aerogels halted production at a potential new manufacturing plant in Bulloch County.
Rep. Buddy Carter expressed disappointment that Aspen Aerogels decided not to pursue their project further.
WJCL 22 News asked Sen. Jon Ossoff about the Trump administration's actions damaging Georgia's economy through the repeal of energy and manufacturing policies.
Rep. Buddy Carter noted that a frequent question he gets is "What's next?" and remarked that with Donald Trump, it changes every day and every minute.
The United States Department of Energy maintains a map of all EV charging stations nationwide.
source_title: Senator and congressman weigh in on Georgia's economic future - WJCL

facts: The Chatham-Siler City Advanced Manufacturing Site is located in North Carolina’s Research Triangle region.
It is a North Carolina certified, shovel-ready megasite.
The site encompasses more than 1,300 acres.
The CAM Site sits just 12 minutes from Toyota’s electric vehicle battery manufacturing facility in central North Carolina.
The site is served by Duke Energy to support large-scale industrial operations.
It is positioned at the geographic center of North Carolina.
The site is directly adjacent to U.S. Highway 421.
U.S. Highway 421 is currently in the process of becoming Interstate 685.
The highway conversion provides enhanced regional and national connectivity for advanced manufacturing investment.
The site is ideally situated to access skilled talent, transportation options, suppliers, and consumers.
source_title: Chatham-Siler City Advanced Manufacturing Site - Chatham County Economic Development Corporation

facts: Seoyon E-HWA, a global auto parts supplier, is investing almost $76 million in a new manufacturing facility in Chatham County.
Seoyon E-Hwa chairman Ryu Yang-seok stated hope that the facility will provide new jobs in the region and contribute to making Savannah a central city for automobile development and production.
Governor Brian Kemp announced on Feb. 1 that Seoyon’s new facility will be located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The new facility will create 740 new jobs.

Governor Brian Kemp stated he promised last year when announcing the largest economic development project in Georgia history that it was just the start in bringing more jobs and opportunity to Georgians.
Established in 1972, Seoyon E-Hwa operates 32 corporations in North America, South America, Europe, and Asia.
The company supplies door trim, seats, bumpers, and other parts to Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, Mercedes-Benz, Ford, and Volkswagen.
Savannah Economic Development Authority president Trip Tollison stated Seoyon E-HWA has a proven track record of supplying the automotive industry and will provide well-paying jobs for the region.
Approximately 500 of the announced jobs will be permanent roles in human resources, development, quality, and production personnel.
In October, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County for its first EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that projects like this will continue to help Georgia become the e-mobility capital of the nation and benefit communities in every zip code.
source_title: Seoyon E-HWA invests $76 million in Chatham County facility  - Grice Connect

facts: Battery company SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at a manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday amid automakers' changing electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for EVs.

Friday marked the last working day for 958 plant employees, about 37% of its workforce, according to a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
Impacted workers will be paid through May 6.
The plant will continue to employ about 1,600 workers after the reductions.
SK opened the $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022.
The Korean company notably supplied batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the F-150 Lightning in December.

The layoffs occur as the U.S. electric vehicle market is at a standstill amid the Trump administration steering federal support away from electrification in favor of more lax automotive emissions policies and a broader agenda supporting the oil and gas industries.
SK Americas spokesperson Joe Guy Collier said the workforce reduction was made to align operations to market conditions.
Collier stated, "SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and to building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing."
The company is pursuing a range of future customers, including the Battery Electric Storage System arena.
Ford plans to scrap the fully-electric version of its iconic pickup truck and opt for an extended-range version of the vehicle.
A Ford spokesperson said it could not comment on supplier personnel actions.
SK and Ford had together previously invested $11.4 billion in joint battery plants in the U.S.
The battery maker ended the joint venture with Ford in December.
SK is also a supplier to Volkswagen.
Georgia U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff stated, "Let's be clear: these were battery manufacturing jobs and now they're gone," and added that Trump's war on electric vehicles is hurting Georgia's economy.

SK has invested significantly in Jackson County in Georgia in recent years as automakers shored up plans to spend billions to develop and build EVs and the federal government under former President Joe Biden supported efforts to build out a domestic EV supply chain.
In June 2020, SK announced plans to pour $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
Gov. Brian Kemp's office said the June 2020 expansion would create 600 jobs.
SK and Hyundai are still jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has attracted other massive EV manufacturing investments, including Rivian's $5 billion factory and Hyundai's own $7.6 billion factory complex among them.
Few states benefited more than Georgia from Biden's signature climate law, the Inflation Reduction Act, which accelerated a rush of green energy projects.
The 33 additional projects announced by the end of 2024 were the most nationwide, according to E2, an environmental business group.
Exact figures differ, but projects in Georgia topped $20 billion, pledging more than 25,000 jobs.

Qcells, a unit of South Korea's Hanwha Solutions, said Friday that it had resumed normal production after temporarily reducing hours and pay for some workers last year because U.S. customs officials had been detaining imported components needed to make solar panels.
EV demand, while still growing, has not met automakers' ambitious expectations in recent years.
EVs accounted for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. in 2025, much the same as a year earlier.
Automakers have been reevaluating their multibillion-dollar electrification plans as financial losses mount and demand shifts.

Manufacturers including Ford, General Motors, Stellantis and others — along with others across the EV supply chain — have reneged on factory, investment and product plans, laid off workers and, instead, pivoted some of those efforts to hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles.
Hybrids and more efficient gasoline-powered vehicles are seemingly more palatable for mainstream buyers concerned about EV driving range and charging infrastructure availability.
Under President Donald Trump, Congress has eliminated tax credits of up to $7,500 for consumers' purchases of new or used EVs.

The administration has also announced plans to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules for automakers, essentially eliminating any federal incentive for auto companies to make their vehicle fleets cleaner.
source_title: EV battery maker SK lays off nearly 1,000 workers at Georgia Plant - CBS Atlanta

facts: SK Battery America is a subsidiary of South Korea-based SK On.
SK Battery America is laying off hundreds of workers from its production facility in Commerce, Georgia.
The layoffs are occurring as electric vehicle battery demand slows and federal incentives dry up.
In December, parent company SK On announced it would expand its focus from EV batteries to stationary energy storage.
This strategic shift followed the collapse of a joint venture between SK On and Ford.
The company filed a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) on Friday, notifying the state of job cuts affecting 958 people.
Affected workers were notified they will receive pay and benefits for the next 60 days.
Friday, March 6, was the last day of work for the affected employees.
The Commerce facility previously employed 2,566 workers.
The layoffs reduced the site's headcount to approximately 1,600 employees.
Joe Guy Collier, SK Battery America’s spokesperson, stated the company will assist affected workers during the transition.
The workforce reductions coincide with broader demand challenges in the electric vehicle battery industry.
Spokesperson Joe Guy Collier indicated the company is transitioning to diversify its business beyond EV batteries.
The company stated it remains committed to Georgia and building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
SK Battery America is actively pursuing future customers in the Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) market.
According to the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas, many automakers and battery companies are cutting back due to slower sales and reduced federal incentives.
Industry players are shifting focus to meet growing data center demand for backup power and energy storage systems.
Customer Ford announced in December that it is shifting its electrification plans to include hybrid vehicles and smaller, more affordable EVs.
Ford also announced plans to grow its energy storage business.
Ford expects to record a $19.5 billion write-down resulting from its strategic shift.
Ford dissolved its BlueOval SK battery joint venture with SK On.
Ford canceled production of the electric F-150 Lightning due to sluggish sales.
In 2023, SK Battery America announced plans to expand its Commerce site workforce by several hundred employees.
The company had previously set a hiring goal of 2,600 employees at the Commerce site to meet rising EV demand.
The Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) fined SK Battery America twice for exposing workers to respiratory hazards.
Law firm Strauss Borrelli announced an investigation into SK Battery America for allegedly failing to properly notify the 958 affected employees under the WARN Act.
The WARN Act mandates that employers provide written notice to workers at least 60 days prior to plant closures or mass job cuts.
Spokesperson Joe Guy Collier stated that the workforce reduction complies with WARN Act guidelines.

facts: A South Korean battery manufacturer filed a federal lawsuit and a trade complaint alleging theft of trade secrets against a rival company planning a sprawling factory in northeast Georgia.
The facility is planned to employ 2,000 workers.
The dispute pits two of South Korea's largest conglomerates against each other in U.S. court and before a top U.S. trade body.
LG Chem alleges SK Innovation stole their technology.
LG Chem requests that a federal judge in Delaware and the U.S. International Trade Commission step in to block SK from importing lithium-ion batteries into the U.S.
LG Chem also requests blocking SK from importing equipment for manufacturing and testing batteries into the U.S.
SK Innovation's CEO Lee Jae-hyun said GL was trying to block SK from importing batteries and equipment for manufacturing and testing.
SK Innovation is confident that the U.S. International Trade Commission will find in their favor and protect them from unfair competition.
SK Innovation, a South Korean conglomerate worth $17.1 billion in 2025, is planning a sprawling factory in northeast Georgia that will employ up to 2,000 workers.
The project is part of the state's effort to become a global leader in battery manufacturing and attract automotive companies.
The state has invested $50 million in workforce training and infrastructure for the project.
Georgia announced a Joint-Construction Contract for funding and tax credits to support the project.
The contract is not yet approved, but work is ongoing.

facts: Daesol Ausys is a South Korean auto supplier planning to build a $72 million plant in Georgia to manufacture electric vehicle parts.
The company announced plans on a Tuesday to construct a factory in West Point, Georgia, specifically to produce luggage boards and covers for EV compartments.
Daesol Ausys currently supplies automotive parts to Hyundai, Kia, and General Motors.
This new facility will be the third factory in the same West Point industrial park built by members of the company's corporate family.
Daehan Solution Co. opened a $1 million factory in West Point in 2008 to manufacture headliners and other sound insulation parts for the nearby Kia plant.
Daesol Material opened a $35 million factory in West Point in 2017 to produce automotive fiber padding.
Daehan Solution currently employs 350 full-time workers, while Daesol Material employs 50 workers.
Daehan Solution also operates a manufacturing plant near Montgomery, Alabama, that supplies parts to the Hyundai assembly plant there.
The announcement follows Hyundai's 2022 plan to build a $5.5 billion electric vehicle and battery assembly plant in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah.
The Hyundai Ellabell site is projected to grow to 8,100 employees and is slated to begin vehicle production in 2025.
EV suppliers have pledged to invest nearly $2.2 billion and hire 5,000 people for the broader Georgia project ecosystem.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that the state's automotive industry employs over 50,000 Georgians and will continue expanding as e-mobility suppliers choose locations across the state.
Daesol Ausys is scheduled to begin production at its new West Point facility in December 2024.
The new plant will hire more than 140 workers.
Daesol Ausys will pay hourly wages ranging from $15 to $20 per hour.
The state of Georgia will fund worker training for the project.
The state provided $500,000 last year to help Harris County clear and grade the site and build a road, with the county matching the funds using sales tax collections.
The city of West Point and Harris County are granting eight years of property tax breaks worth a projected $5.7 million.
Daesol Ausys could qualify for $875,000 in state income tax credits, calculated at $1,250 per job over five years, provided workers earn at least $31,300 annually.

entity_name: Georgia’s First Solar-Powered Electric Vehicle Charging Station at West Point Visitor Information Center
canonical_name: Georgia’s First Solar-Powered Electric Vehicle Charging Station at West Point Visitor Information Center
facts: A partnership including The Ray C. Anderson Foundation and Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia took the first step in imagining “The Ray,” the Highway of the Future.
Georgia’s first solar-powered PV4EV (photovoltaic for electric vehicle) charging station was installed at the Visitor Information Center (VIC) in West Point.
The innovative charging station is powered by the SpotlightTM Solar LIFT.
The EV charger is a level 3 “quick charge” station, capable of providing 80 percent + battery charge in less than 45 minutes for most EVs.
It is the first in what is imagined to be a series of infrastructure upgrades that will make The Ray, a 16-mile section of I-85 in West Georgia, a model for the highway of the future.
Students from the Georgia Tech School of Architecture studied ways to create a sustainable highway.
They researched technologies including solar panels built into highways, asphalt that generates electricity as cars travel over, and air filtering plant walls to reduce highway emissions.
Georgia ranks 2nd in the nation for registered electric vehicles.
John Lanier, executive director and Ray Anderson’s grandson, stated the installation of the PV4EV is a progressive move to help promote more broad adoption of EV’s in Georgia.
The charging station’s placement at the West Point VIC makes travel more convenient for electric vehicle drivers between West Georgia and Alabama.
The Ray is a 16-mile stretch of road between exits 2 and 18 in Troup County.
The Ray is named for Ray C. Anderson, founder of the global carpet tile manufacturer Interface, Inc.
Anderson had an environmental epiphany in 1994 at the age of 60 and challenged Interface to become the “first name in industrial ecology.”
Anderson led his company on a journey coined as “Mission ZeroⓇ,” influencing other business and industry leaders that it was both the right and the smart thing to do.
The Ray is envisioned as a way to change the way we think about our infrastructure in the future.
A dedication ceremony for the charging station took place on October 14th at the West Point Visitor Information Center.
A Kia Soul was plugged into and charged by the PV4EV charging station during the ceremony.

Attendees included representatives from the Ray C. Anderson Foundation, Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Hannah Solar, LLC, Board of the Georgia Department of Transportation, Mayors of LaGrange and West Point.

Randy Jackson, KMMG’s senior vice president of human resources and administration, stated Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia is proud to be a leader when it comes to taking care of their environment and is at the forefront of the Mission Zero Corridor project to usher in regenerative, restorative and sustainable highways of tomorrow.
Environment is one of the pillars that KMMG’s community relations program is built on, and this project fits into their goals with the potential to improve the region for decades to come.

Partners include The Ray C. Anderson Foundation, Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG), Hannah Solar, LLC, Spotlight Solar, City of West Point, GA, LaGrange-Troup County Chamber of Commerce, Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT), Georgia Department of Economic Development, Georgia Tech School of Architecture and the Georgia Conservancy.
source_title: West Point Visitor Information Center Features Georgia’s First Solar-Powered Electric Vehicle Charging Station, Allowing Greater Travel Across GA and AL | Ray C. Anderson Foundation

facts: Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and major players in Georgia’s ever-growing automotive industry.
A Hyundai representative did not immediately respond to a message seeking comment on the project.
A spokesperson for SK On said the two companies are still reviewing options and discussing details of their collaboration, with more details to be shared in the future.
More than 548,000 electric vehicles were sold in the United States during the first nine months of this year, up 70% from the same period in 2021, according to automotive data firm Kelley Blue Book.
Electric vehicles accounted for about 5.4% of all new car sales in the first three quarters of this year, up from 2.7% last year.
David Clayton serves as Executive Director of Clemson University International Center for Automotive Research.
Clayton noted that EV-related businesses benefit from clustering in one area for shipping logistics and attracting a workforce, especially during times of low unemployment.
Clayton stated the Southeast population is growing and drawing people from all over the country and the world, which bodes well for companies navigating a tight job market to find workers.
The federal government has ramped up incentives to produce more electric vehicles and batteries in the United States.
The Inflation Reduction Act pledged $369 billion to accelerate the nation’s transition away from fossil fuels.
Timothy Lewin is CEO of Georgia Tech Strategic Energy Institute.
Lewin said the IRA is leading to increased investment in domestic manufacturing, even among foreign companies like Hyundai that have faced short-term obstacles regarding aspects of the law.
Lewin called the Bartow battery facility announcement "Exhibit A of what the IRA is trying to achieve" and noted it suggests industrial policy to stimulate US manufacturing investment is working.
Georgia has recruited start-up Rivian, which plans a $5 billion factory about an hour east of Atlanta that will employ 7,500 workers.
Hyundai broke ground last October at its sprawling battery and electric vehicle plant along I-16 in Bryan County.
Hyundai plans to hire 8,100 workers and produce 300,000 electric vehicles annually in the first phase of its Bryan County plant.
Hyundai's production capacity is expected to rise to 500,000 vehicles annually, including several new EV models, according to company officials.

State and local leaders cited Hyundai’s jobs, investment on site, and commitment to create thousands more jobs at suppliers around Georgia as justification for a record $1.8 billion stimulus package for the Bryan County plant.
Two major Hyundai suppliers have announced plans to build plants near the Georgia coast.
The Bartow County Battery facility would likely receive a similar package of incentives, including grants, tax breaks, free infrastructure, and worker training.
Some economists and observer groups have criticized state and local incentives as extravagant, claiming these projects would be completed without them.
Good Jobs First found that states and local governments contributed nearly $13.8 billion in incentives to attract at least 51 electric vehicle and battery manufacturers in recent years.
Georgia has set aside about $3.3 billion for Hyundai and Rivian through various grants, tax credits, worker training, land, and infrastructure.
Pat Wilson is the Economic Development Commissioner.
Wilson told the American Jewish Committee that Georgia has been working with SK On on this project for more than six months.
SK On sought to expand its manufacturing capacity in the United States.
The Biden administration wants to expand battery production in the United States, but much of the supply chain does not exist domestically and is dominated by China.
Wilson stated, “We don’t produce as many batteries as we do in the US,” regarding domestic production capabilities.
Wilson said SK On and Hyundai will utilize the Georgia Technical College system and Georgia Quick Start worker training program to help workers prepare for the factory of the future.
Wilson described Bartow as a metro county and one of the fastest growing counties in the state, noting it is in a good place to provide the needed workforce.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) and SK On have selected a site in Bartow County for a new EV battery manufacturing facility to supply Hyundai’s EV plants across the country.
The battery plant will create more than 3,500 new jobs through an investment of $4 billion to $5 billion.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and key players in Georgia’s ever-growing automotive industry.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated his administration has been focused on bringing jobs and opportunity to communities across the state that may have been overlooked, noting that SK and HMG share this goal.
SK On was established just last year as the lithium-ion battery subsidiary of SK Innovation, South Korea’s largest energy company.
SK Innovation currently employs more than 2,000 Georgians at a battery plant in Commerce.
The new facility in Bartow County is expected to begin operations in 2025.
EV-related projects in Georgia since 2020 total about $17 billion in investment and account for more than 22,800 new jobs.

Georgia Commissioner of Economic Development Pat Wilson stated the state is creating a fully integrated supply chain for automotive manufacturers while also connecting battery manufacturers with recyclers to close the loop on battery manufacturing.

The state Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce Team worked in partnership with Georgia EMC, the Development Authority of Bartow County, the Cartersville-Bartow County Department of Economic Development, and the Technical College System of Georgia’s Quick Start program to land the project.
The Hyundai EV plant being built near Savannah in Bryan County is the largest economic development project in Georgia history.
The Hyundai EV plant near Savannah represents a $5.5 billion investment expected to create 8,100 jobs when fully built out.

facts: Georgia DOT scheduled a public information open house for its proposed US 411 Connector project in Bartow County on Thursday, March 18, 2010, from 4 to 7 p.m.
The meeting was held at the Cartersville Civic Center, located at 435 West Main Street in Cartersville.
The proposed project calls for a new US 411 to I-75 connection by constructing a rural freeway on a new location between the existing US 411/US 41 interchange and State Route (SR) 20 east of I-75.
Plans include a new I-75 interchange as part of the project.
Interchange improvements are also planned at US 41.
The project would begin at the intersection of US 411 and US 41 and extend to SR 20 east of I-75, covering a distance of 7.31 miles.
Bartow and Floyd County residents were encouraged to attend the meeting to express their thoughts and preferences on the proposed project.
source_title: Open house for proposed U.S. 411 connector in Bartow Co. | WGAA Radio

facts: Ascend Elements, a Massachusetts-based advanced battery recycling company, opened a plant in Covington, Georgia in March 2023 that grinds used batteries into black mass powder.
The company is currently building a plant in Hopkinsville, Kentucky called Apex 1 to refine black mass into battery materials.
Ascend canceled plans to produce cathode active materials (CAM) at the Kentucky facility and agreed to cancel a $164 million grant for that portion won from the Department of Energy.
The company intends to convert the CAM space into a lithium carbonate production line using proprietary technology rolled out at its Covington plant early this year.
Apex 1 will still produce precursors to CAM known as pCAM, an effort aided by a separate $316 million grant from the DOE.
Ascend plans to invest about $1 billion in the Kentucky project, according to spokesperson Thomas Frey.
The company can sell its pCAM to specialized CAM manufacturers or to electric-vehicle and battery manufacturers who want their suppliers to use that particular material.
Ascend will be the only large-scale manufacturer of pCAM in America, which spokesperson Thomas Frey noted makes the company appealing due to tariffs.
The broader slowdown in U.S. EV manufacturing buildout and Detroit automakers pulling back on EV production enthusiasm pushed back timelines for the battery supply chain, including CAM and pCAM.
Ascend extended its timeline for Apex 1 from the end of 2025 to the third quarter of 2026 to allow for a more cost-effective construction process.
Commissioning is underway for the new lithium carbonate line at Ascend’s Covington factory, which should begin commercial production in the next few months.
The Covington plant has struggled with used batteries catching fire during delivery and processing.
Firefighters responded to a Feb. 20 conflagration in a tractor trailer delivering used batteries to the Covington site; the fire consumed the trailer but did not jump to the adjacent building.
That incident was the 14th time Ascend’s Covington plant called in emergency teams, according to plant manager Andrew Gardner.
The cause of the fires seems to have been batteries that were not properly packed or discharged prior to shipping.

Ascend stopped operations for 10 days to work closely with the Fire Department and the mayor on safety and went on a blitz with customers to redo training on how to pack end-of-life batteries and scrap.
Competitor Freyr Battery (now T1 Energy) had aspired to build a battery factory in Georgia but canceled plans amid the market slowdown.
source_title: Facing headwinds, Ascend shifts plans for battery… | Canary Media

facts: Rivian indefinitely delays plans to build a $5 billion EV plant east of Atlanta.
Instead, Rivian initially will begin building its new R2 midsize SUV model at its plant in Normal, Ill.
The company stated the Georgia plant remains an extremely important part of its strategy to scale production of R2 and R3.
Timing for resuming construction is expected to be later to focus its teams on the capital-efficient launch of R2 in Normal, Illinois.
Rivian announced plans to build the $5 billion plant in Georgia in December 2021 amid much fanfare.
It was the largest economic development project ever to come to the Peach State at the time.
The Georgia project was surpassed five months later by an announcement that Hyundai would build a $5.5 billion EV plant west of Savannah.

In exchange for creating 7,500 jobs, state and local economic development agencies offered Rivian $1.5 billion in incentives including tax credits, a 25-year no-cost lease, and $198.1 million in site and road improvements on 1,978 acres.
Thus far, Rivian has made two payments in lieu of taxes (PILOT) to the Joint Development Authority (JDA) of Jasper, Morgan, Newton, and Walton Counties on the property totaling $3 million.
The most recent PILOT payment was made on March 1.
The economic development agreement with Rivian requires that it meets 80% of its $5 billion investment commitment and 7,500 jobs commitments.
Rivian must maintain those commitments by Dec. 31, 2030, through 2049.
Rivian has committed to maintaining those timelines.

The state Department of Economic Development and JDA wrote in a joint statement that Rivian has restated its commitment to Georgia, and the state and JDA are in steady communication with Rivian regarding its manufacturing plans at Stanton Springs North.
Rivian estimated that shifting production of the R2 to Illinois from Georgia will save the company more than $2.25 billion.
The savings are expected to come from capital expenditures, product development investment, and supplier sourcing opportunities.
source_title: Rivian pauses construction of Georgia electric vehicle plant

facts: ABM Equipment released a study ranking U.S. industrial job centers using metrics including total manufacturing employment, annual wages, manufacturing jobs as a percentage of the workforce, and employment growth rates.
The company took data from the U.S. Census Bureau and U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics to conduct the analysis.
Metrics were weighted and combined to create a 100-point scoring system highlighting both current and future industrial growth of the cities.
Atlanta, GA ranked 10th on the list with an 81 total score.

entity_name: Global Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure Market
canonical_name: Global Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure Market
facts: Global electric vehicle charging infrastructure market size was valued at USD 28.21 billion in 2025 and is projected to grow from USD 33.32 billion in 2026 to USD 90.5 billion by 2032.
The industry is projected to reflect sustained growth at a steady CAGR of 18.12% during 2026-2032.
Rivian broke ground on its USD 5 billion plant at Stanton Springs North, Georgia, on September 16, 2025, targeting 400,000 vehicles annually and 7,500 jobs by 2030.

General Motors committed USD 4 billion across plants in Michigan, Kansas, and Tennessee, designating Factory ZERO as its dedicated EV assembly hub targeting over 2 million U.S.-produced vehicles per year.
In 2025, global EV sales reached approximately 20.7 million units, with the U.S. accounting for 1.6 million combined BEV and PHEV sales.
The U.S. public charging stock grew 20% in 2024 to just under 200,000 public charging points.
The United States has allocated USD 5 billion under the NEVI Formula Program via the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law to expand nationwide charging infrastructure and improve highway connectivity.
According to the U.S. Department of Energy, DC fast charging stations cost between USD 10,000–USD 40,000 per unit in hardware, with installation adding another USD 4,000–USD 51,000 per port.

ChargePoint introduced a next-generation AC Level 2 charging architecture featuring bidirectional charging, Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) capability, and charging speeds up to 19.2 kW in North America and 22 kW in Europe in April 2025.
SOLUM launched 50 kW modular EV power units designed for scalable, flexible, and cost-efficient charging station deployment at CES 2025 in Las Vegas in January 2025.

The global electric vehicle charging infrastructure industry is moderately fragmented, with the top five players Tesla, Charge Point, Shell Recharge, ABB, and Electrify America collectively holding around 45% of the global market share.

Key players in the electric vehicle charging infrastructure industry include Tesla, ChargePoint, ABB, Shell Recharge, Siemens, BP Pulse, EVgo, Blink Charging, Electrify America, EVBox, Schneider Electric, Tritium, IONITY, Wallbox, and Enel X.
Commercial sector alone contributes approximately 56% share of the overall revenue in the global electric vehicle charging infrastructure industry.
Wall Mounted chargers account for approximately 57% share of the electric vehicle charging infrastructure industry.
source_title: EV Charging Infrastructure Market Growth Outlook 2026-32

facts: Georgia has secured more than $27 billion in private investment tied to electric vehicles and battery manufacturing over the past several years.

State programs like the Quality Jobs Tax Credit and Investment Tax Credit helped offset capital costs for multibillion-dollar facilities.

The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 includes a $7,500 consumer tax credit tied to North American assembly.

Georgia Institute of Technology plays a central role in battery research and manufacturing innovation for the EV sector.
Technical colleges have introduced accelerated programs tied directly to employers like Rivian.
The K-12 system is evolving with early-stage career pathways aimed at preparing students for EV-related roles before graduation.
In early 2026, SK Battery America cut nearly 1,000 jobs at its Georgia facility.

High vehicle prices and interest rates have slowed EV adoption in parts of North America.
Market uncertainty has raised questions about whether EV production capacity will outpace demand.
Gaps in charging networks remain, particularly in rural areas.
Large-scale plants place significant strain on local resources, including water and energy supply.
Future changes in federal incentives or state policy could alter the economics that underpin current investments.
Georgia now leads the nation in announced EV and battery investments.
Georgia is on pace to rival Michigan in battery production capacity by the end of the decade.
By 2030, Georgia could be producing hundreds of thousands of electric vehicles annually.
Georgia is building a modern auto industry from the ground up that is integrated, electrified, and closely tied to global supply chains.

facts: Rivian Automotive is in late-stage discussions to open a vehicle manufacturing and battery plant east of Atlanta along Interstate 20.

facts: Major new public investments in Southern manufacturing continue to present opportunities to benefit local workers and communities.
A long-standing Southern economic development model has historically prioritized corporate power and profits over workers and communities.
This failed economic model is rooted in the legacies of slavery, anti-Black racism, and the suppression of worker organizing.
The South lags all other U.S. regions on most indicators of economic health, including job growth and wages.
Southern workers and their families experience significantly higher rates of poverty than in other parts of the country.

Southern politicians have promoted a development model characterized by low wages, regressive taxation, lax environmental regulations, a weak social safety net, and opposition to unions to attract business.
The automotive manufacturing industry has been a key recipient of public subsidies, receiving billions of dollars from Southern states in recent decades.
This system of low taxation and corporate giveaways starves essential public goods like education and social safety net programs.
Weak or nonexistent environmental regulations have contributed to toxic sites and resource degradation that disproportionately affect Black and brown families.
Some announced manufacturing projects have been cancelled or reduced in size after the Trump administration’s slashing of federal supports for strategic industries.
Many projects launched during the Biden administration continue to move forward despite federal support uncertainties.
Manufacturing investments in traditional industries and nascent ones like electric vehicle (EV) and EV battery manufacturing are spurring significant job growth in some Southern communities.
Community benefits agreements can be powerful vehicles for communities to secure lasting local economic benefits from major industrial development at new and existing facilities.
A CBA is a legally enforceable contract between a private developer or company and a local coalition detailing how a project will benefit workers and the community.

Benefits in a CBA can include commitments to strong labor standards, respect for workers’ rights to organize, equitable workforce recruitment, training, and hiring practices, affordable housing, environmental protections, child care, and public parks.
CBAs mitigate risks for project developers by ensuring local project support and addressing important concerns early on.
Failure to engage local communities can lead to strong opposition, interruption of development, obstacles to obtaining siting permits or rezoning approvals, or significant legal costs.
In June 2024, developers shelved plans for a $1.3 billion data center in Indiana after facing significant local opposition over environmental concerns.
Strong CBAs ensure that new industrial investments generate good manufacturing jobs that pay a living wage and expand pathways to unionization.
The fights to secure CBA gains help forge strong, durable labor-community coalitions needed to reshape the political fabric of Southern communities.
Between 2017 and 2023, manufacturing construction doubled in the East South Central Census division (Alabama, Kentucky, Tennessee, and Mississippi).
The West South Central division (Arkansas, Louisiana, Oklahoma, and Texas) has the highest amount of manufacturing construction spending of any division in the U.S.

Large federal investments through the Inflation Reduction Act, Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, and CHIPS and Science Act accelerated a long-term trend of manufacturing industries locating in the South.

These federal investments included both direct public subsidies and tax credits to businesses investing in key clean energy manufacturing industries like batteries, electric vehicles, solar panels, and wind energy products.
Some recent federal investments include incentives meant to encourage strong labor standards on projects receiving public funds.
Since the third quarter of 2023, more than $125 billion worth of clean energy manufacturing investments were announced across Georgia, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee, Kentucky, and Texas.
Advancing even a portion of these announced projects would result in thousands of jobs for Southern workers.
The South has retained the largest share of U.S. manufacturing employment of any region; in 2024, 35% of U.S. manufacturing employment was in the South.
Since 2010, manufacturing employment in the South has grown by 17%, the quickest growth of any region.
Manufacturing jobs became "good jobs" historically thanks to relatively high levels of unionization during the mid-20th century, which improved wages, benefits, and working conditions.
Unionization in manufacturing has fallen in all regions since 1983, but the South has almost without exception had the lowest unionization rate of any region.
Every Southern state except Maryland and Delaware has passed anti-union right-to-work laws that make it harder for workers to form, join, and sustain unions.
Right-to-work laws suppress unionization rates and have driven down wages for both union and nonunion workers alike across the South.
In 2025, Tennessee Republicans passed legislation prohibiting any company that enters into a CBA from receiving state economic development funds.

This Tennessee legislation aims to discourage a coalition of West Tennessee residents and allied groups calling on Ford and SK Innovation to negotiate a CBA covering its massive BlueOval electric vehicle and battery manufacturing complex.
Community Benefits Plans (CBPs) demonstrate how a company applying for public funds will ensure a proposed project provides benefits to workers and community members.
Many federal agencies required companies to submit CBPs to receive certain grant funds designated by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act or the Inflation Reduction Act.
CBPs are not legally binding commitments but can lay important groundwork for a CBA and provide leverage for community benefits coalitions.
Community benefits coalitions bring together multiple labor and community-based organizations representing interests of those most affected by a proposed new development.

Project Labor Agreements (PLAs) are legally binding agreements in the construction industry that establish hiring procedures, help enforce prevailing wages, support dispute resolution, and can require contractors to hire through union hiring halls.
Community Workforce Agreements (CWAs) are a type of PLA which include community-oriented commitments like equitable workforce development.
Union Neutrality or Card Check agreements commit employers to remaining neutral regarding union organizing and respecting workers’ ability to use voluntary recognition options.
Labor peace agreements involve unions agreeing not to engage in picketing or work stoppages during organizing in exchange for securing employer commitments to neutrality and card check.

CBAs do not require government action and can shape outcomes of major projects even in contexts where state legislators have preempted local governments from establishing job quality or environmental standards.
Cities like Detroit and Cleveland have ordinances requiring developers of projects using public resources to engage in a community benefits plan process.
In 2005, Atlanta passed an ordinance specifying worker and community benefits for the Beltline redevelopment.
A CBA can be negotiated alongside a PLA to ensure pathways to quality jobs for local residents during operational phases, including future expansions.

To be successful, a CBA must include defined enforcement mechanisms that hold all parties accountable, clearly establish obligations, set metrics for measuring progress, and mandate ongoing monitoring of compliance.
If a company or coalition fails to make good-faith efforts on commitments, an arbitration process is initiated.
A newly established union at the project site is well-positioned to monitor CBA commitments and hold the company accountable over the long term.
Building broad-based, durable coalitions and leverage necessary to compel private interests to engage in CBA negotiations is a challenging, long-term, resource-intensive organizing project.

Vastly expanding the capacity of broad-based coalitions and grassroots organizations to gradually build community and worker power in Southern communities is the most essential ingredient for transforming existing power imbalances.
source_title: Community benefits agreements can turn Southern manufacturing investments into good jobs and shared prosperity | Economic Policy Institute

Austin is strategically located near FREYR’s operating U.S. solar module manufacturing facility in Wilmer, Texas.
Austin provides access to an emerging talent pool within the booming, business-friendly Texas economy to support the Company’s growth aspirations.

Daniel Barcelo serves as FREYR’s Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer.

The company intends to bring more than 1,000 new American jobs to the Texas economy as it builds teams in Austin and Wilmer.
FREYR plans to work with partners across industry and government to invest in critical U.S. infrastructure.

facts: Georgia is accelerating growth in electric vehicle (EV) parts and power manufacturing on the ground alongside EV aircraft development in the skies.

Archer Aviation, a California-based company developing electric-powered vertical takeoff and landing aircraft, announced on Nov. 14 that it will manufacture its air EVs next to Covington Municipal Airport.
Plant construction and production at the Archer Aviation facility are scheduled to start in 2024.
Archer plans to invest $118 million over 10 years for the facility.
The facility will initially build up to 650 four-passenger aircraft per year.
Archer eventually plans to employ 1,000 people at the facility.
The Archer Aviation facility could expand to manufacture 2,300 airplanes annually.
Archer’s aircraft take off and land like helicopters but fly as airplanes within a metro area.
Archer CEO Adam Goldstein stated that creating urban air mobility networks across major cities nationwide requires ramping production of their aircraft.
Archer CEO Adam Goldstein noted that the new facility represents a huge step toward meeting production challenges.
Delta Air Lines invested in Archer competitor Joby in October to help launch an air taxi service to and from airports.
Joby expects to start commercial service for its air taxis in 2025.
Two auto parts suppliers announced plants that will supply Hyundai’s $5.5-billion Metaplant America EV facility.
Hyundai’s Metaplant America EV facility broke ground in Bryan County on Oct. 25.
Joon Georgia expects to invest $317 million and employ 630 people at a facility outside Statesboro, with operations beginning in mid-2024.

The Hyundai Mobilis plant will supply the nearby Metaplant America facility.

Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson called Joon’s announcement “a landmark moment as we drive Georgia’s automotive industry into the future.”

The FREYR Battery investment is projected to grow to $2.6 billion with more than 720 jobs by 2029 if a planned second phase proceeds.
FREYR Battery manufactures batteries for EVs but focuses heavily on electric storage to allow EV charging with clean energy during periods without solar or wind power.

facts: FREYR Battery, a developer of clean, next-generation battery cell production capacity, will invest $2.57 billion into Georgia's sustainable technology ecosystem.
FREYR Battery will create 723 new jobs over the next seven years at a manufacturing facility in Coweta County.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that job creators and innovators from all over the world bring their operations to Georgia because they know they will have success here.
Gov. Brian Kemp expressed pride in welcoming FREYR to Georgia as the latest company to bring transformational investments and opportunity to communities.
Gov. Brian Kemp referred to Georgia as the No. 1 state for business and looked forward to FREYR's growing impact on Coweta and the surrounding region.
FREYR is dedicated to supporting a domestic supply chain for renewable power sources.
FREYR's battery cells can be used for stationary energy storage, electric mobility, and additional applications.
FREYR co-founder and CEO Tom Einar Jensen stated the company is deeply committed to decarbonizing the transportation and energy sectors with U.S. partners.

Tom Einar Jensen noted that as they advance their U.S. expansion plan with key stakeholders, they expect to make meaningful investments to spur job creation and develop localized, decarbonized supply chains in the U.S. to enhance energy security and economic activity.
FREYR's new facility will be located at the Bridgeport Industrial Park site.
The Bridgeport Industrial Park site has been zoned for a mix of heavy and light industrial use since 2014.
The company will hire technicians, operators, and engineers as well as executive and administrative roles.
The facility will be called Giga America.
Giga America will have a planned first phase production of approximately 34 gigawatt hours.
The facility will use the U.S.-based 24M Technologies SemiSolidTM platform to simplify the production process and increase manufacturing efficiency.
FREYR is currently evaluating clean power supply solutions for the new facility, including the potential use of renewable energy from solar installations.
Coweta County Development Authority Chair John Daviston expressed pleasure at welcoming FREYR Battery to the community.
John Daviston highlighted the opportunities the venture brings to the area and the addition of quality, highly-skilled jobs that will significantly stimulate the local economy.
Metro Atlanta Chamber President and CEO Katie Kirkpatrick expressed excitement about welcoming FREYR Battery to metro Atlanta, which has quickly become a hub for battery cell research and production.
Katie Kirkpatrick noted that FREYR will benefit from the region's vibrant research ecosystem and diversity of talent.
Project Manager John Soper represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with Coweta County Development Authority, the Metro Atlanta Chamber, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that companies like FREYR are driving a global revolution based on sustainable technology and solutions.
Pat Wilson noted that electrification is changing how we power entire cities beyond EVs and mobility.
Pat Wilson mentioned that to support electrification in home and commercial storage, they are developing a battery ecosystem with manufacturers, recyclers, and customers.
Pat Wilson stated that Georgia and its communities are ready to support a sustainable future for generations to come.

facts: Monomyth Materials has acquired the assets of NanoGraf.
NanoGraf is a Chicago-based silicon anode company spun out of Northwestern University and Argonne National Laboratory.
The acquisition pairs SiOx technology and a defense-validated IP portfolio with Monomyth's domestic synthetic graphite business.
Financial terms for the acquisition were not disclosed.
The NanoGraf assets become the foundation of a new subsidiary, M2Innovations.
M2Innovations is focused on advanced battery materials and prototype cell development from R&D through pilot scale.
M2Innovations sits alongside M2Graphite, Monomyth's synthetic graphite business.
M2Graphite was simultaneously rebranded from Anovion Technologies.
M2Graphite handles commercial-scale production.
The combination covers essentially the full active anode stack.
Graphite and silicon together make up virtually all of the active material in a lithium-ion anode.
Both are currently dominated by Chinese supply.
Over 90% of active anode material is sourced from China today.
China accounts for more than 60% of global SiOx capacity.
NanoGraf's SiOx technology is already deployed in consumer electronics and military applications.
The technology has validation from federal defense and energy agencies, including the Department of Energy.
M2Graphite was assembled from graphite assets formerly held by Amsted Industries and Pyrotek.
Amsted remains a minority shareholder.
The Bainbridge, Georgia site is slated to be the first of several facilities.
The site is backed by three planned capital programs of more than $1 billion each.
Monomyth has invested more than $100 million in the platform since 2022.
Francis Wang, who led NanoGraf through its growth stage, will join the M2Innovations advisory board.

facts: Qcells closed on a $1.45 billion Energy Department loan guarantee to support its solar panel manufacturing facility in Cartersville, Georgia, announced Dec. 19.
The facility will create 1,650 full-time jobs.
The facility will generate 3.3 GW of solar panels annually.
The annual output is enough to power 500,000 homes and reduce CO2 emissions by over five million tons per year.
Qcells began some production at the Cartersville site this past spring.
Qcells plans to start full-scale operations at the facility in 2025.
The facility will produce solar components for an end-to-end supply chain, including ingots, wafers, cells, and finished solar panels.
The Cartersville factory is the largest ingot and wafer plant ever built in the U.S., according to the DOE.
The project has a potential sales output of more than $2 billion.
Qcells is eligible for Inflation Reduction Act incentives, such as the Section 45X Advanced Manufacturing Production Tax Credit.
The Cartersville factory will supply solar panels for distributed and utility-scale projects.
In January, Qcells signed an eight-year, 12 GW solar and engineering, procurement and construction agreement with Microsoft, fulfilled by panels made in Cartersville.
Hanwha Qcells USA, Inc. opened a separate solar factory in Dalton, Georgia, in 2019, located about an hour from Cartersville.

Qcells committed to spending nearly $2.8 billion to build out a complete and sustainable solar supply chain in the U.S., first announced in January 2023.

The Biden-Harris administration is racing to finalize Energy Department clean energy loans while the president is still in office.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are beginning to build an electric vehicle battery manufacturing facility in Kingston, GA (northwest of Atlanta).

facts: Tesla supplier CelLink has received a $362 million loan from the US Department of Energy.

The funding will be used to construct a new manufacturing facility in Georgetown, Texas.
Construction of CelLink’s manufacturing facility is anticipated to create 165 construction jobs.
When fully operational, the project is expected to create more than 1,200 permanent jobs.
Wiring harnesses are key components of cars, acting as sets of wires and related equipment that relay information and carry electricity throughout vehicles.
CelLink’s flexible circuit wiring harnesses are called “flex harnesses.”
Flex harnesses are lighter, smaller, faster to produce, and can be less expensive than conventional wiring harnesses.
CelLink’s technology is now in demand in the electric vehicle (EV) and general automotive markets.
The technology also has applications across numerous other industries, including aviation, space, consumer electronics, and appliances.

facts: Troup County is experiencing explosive growth driven by Kia and its suppliers in the southwest part of the county, alongside distribution centers like Walmart and UPS.
The Troup Strategy Center (formerly the Troup County Center for Strategic Planning) leads workforce development initiatives.
The Troup Strategy Center was incorporated in 2009.
The Troup Strategy Center launched strategic plan "The Shift Forward" in May 2023.

The Shift Forward focuses on expanding infrastructure countywide, overcoming employment barriers including daycare and transportation, supporting public schools, and providing diverse housing opportunities.
Maryanne Lovejoy serves as executive director of the Troup Strategy Center.
Scott Malone is president of the Development Authority of LaGrange.
Kelley Bush is director of existing industry and workforce development for the Development Authority of LaGrange.

Workforce development challenges in the county include a 21% poverty rate, low educational attainment, lack of training, generational poverty, illiteracy, and high school scheduling conflicts preventing college/career academy enrollment.
Attainable housing is critical to filling the talent pipeline for growing jobs in the area.
Troup County has 4,000 housing units entitled, in permitting, and/or under construction, a milestone that took seven years to reach.

Kia sees the North American market as part of its long-term market strategy and has had great success growing market share in the U.S.
Kia credits partnerships with state agencies, including the Georgia Ports Authority, for its success.
