facts: Lovejoy Capital, an Atlanta investment firm specializing in luxury outdoors rentals, could bring “glamping,” EV chargers and fishing docks to Lake Tobesofkee.
Atlanta investors arrived at the shores of Sandy Beach at Lake Tobesofkee on Thursday night with a proposal to build modern improvements around the lake.
They were met by about 50 Lake Tobesofkee residents, employees and other lake frequenters who voiced concerns about everything from park fees to lake maintenance infrastructure.
The company approached Macon-Bibb County with the proposal last June.

Murphy Talmadge, one of Lovejoy Capital’s founders and a descendant of former Georgia Gov. Herman Talmadge, noted that the project is in its very early stages and said the purpose of the town hall was to hear from the community to inform its agreement with the county.
He said Lovejoy will fill the demand for “glamping” and extend visitors’ trips to the park by offering amenities such as concession stands or a puzzle maze.
Talmadge told attendees, “We want more people using the things y’all are already using.”
Lovejoy Capital LLC was formed in February 2024, according to its business records filed with the state.
The company has members experienced with “outdoor hospitality ventures across 36 states,” according to its presentation.
The firm’s website displays a blank login page.
The company has several other ongoing projects, including a similar proposal in Troup County for improvements to Pyne Road Park.
Lovejoy Capital has not yet completed any of these projects, according to spokesperson Thornton Kennedy.
Lovejoy plans to build dozens of rental units at Lake Tobesofkee, including holiday cabins and British Landpods, which are movable, compact off-the-grid cabins.
The company will be responsible for managing reservations for park stays, according to Lovejoy’s proposal.

One of the main components of the project would be adding a tourist maze to Sandy Beach, connecting to playgrounds and other family activities meant to enhance the time families already spend at the beach.
Lovejoy proposed building bike racks, EV charging stations, a trail system and additional fishing docks around the park.
Talmadge said, “The main thing we add is hospitality. These places already have great facilities.”
Nothing is set in stone yet, according to Kennedy.
Lovejoy Capital would provide the funding while the county would provide its facilities.
The terms of this public-private partnership have yet to be finalized and will be more clear once Lovejoy receives an interim agreement from the county, as required by the county.
In exchange for its funding, Lovejoy would collect revenue from the rental units.
The county would receive a portion of that revenue and benefit from a hotel-motel tax.
According to Lovejoy’s proposal, the project is estimated to cost about $23 million.
Talmadge said the company still has to conduct a feasibility study and hammer out the revenue share agreement with the county.

The developers’ plans sounded a familiar tune to the residents of Lake Tobesofkee, who were meeting a short ways away from the now defunct Sandy Beach water park, which closed in 2023 after several ownership changes and a series of operational challenges.
Phil Martin, a captain at The Sailing Center at Lake Tobesofkee, which teaches kids and adults with developmental disabilities how to sail, said he thought the proposed changes were positive.
He came to the meeting curious about what would become of the park’s employees.
Talmadge reassured residents that lake fees wouldn’t go up and people working at the lake would keep their jobs.
Tony Thomaston made one thing clear: leave the duck ponds alone.
He said he’s seen how the ponds can be a safe place for families to have “clean fun.”
Thomaston held a kid’s fishing derby there last month and has lived on Saint Anthony’s Drive for seven years.
He said he’s weary about Lovejoy’s promise to keep fees down because the project is ultimately an investment with expectations of a financial return.
Thomaston said he wants everyone to be able to use the parks, but the county should take a look at and work with what’s already there.
“I want growth, we need growth, our county needs growth for sure,” Thomaston said, “But not at the expense of families.”
The next step for Lovejoy Capital will be presenting to the Macon-Bibb County Board of Commissioners.
The investment firm will then determine the scope of the project and enter into an interim agreement to begin further planning.
source_title: Luxury outdoors investor takes first steps in Lake Tobesofkee improvement project – The Macon Melody

facts: Hyundai Motor’s manufacturing plant in Georgia recently started producing the IONIQ 5.
The South Korean automaker’s facility in Georgia is located west of Savannah and named the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
Hyundai spokesperson Bianca Johnson said the company held an “employee-focused celebration” last week.
A grand opening celebration for HMGMA is set for the first quarter of 2025.

After validating its production processes to ensure its vehicles meet Hyundai Motor Group’s high-quality standards, HMGMA has started initial production of customer vehicles ahead of schedule, according to spokesperson Bianca Johnson.
The first Hyundai electric vehicle (EV) to roll off the HMGMA assembly line is the IONIQ 5.
The Hyundai IONIQ 5 was the second best-selling electric vehicle in the United States in the first half of 2024, if Tesla EVs are excluded from the count.
Hyundai has partnered with long-time Tesla supplier LG Energy Solution (LGES) on the HMGMA facility.
The South Korean companies are expected to employ 8,500 people at the plant.
Hyundai aims to produce up 300,000 EVs per year in Georgia.
The HMGMA plant will also be producing the batteries for the 300,000 EVs.
source_title: Hyundai Georgia plant starts IONIQ 5 production

facts: Hyundai recorded 901,686 in total domestic sales in 2025.
Ira Gabriel holds the title of senior group manager for Hyundai Motor Company.
Ira Gabriel stated that Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America will help the company reach a target goal to produce more than 80% of its vehicles sold in the United States domestically by 2030.
The current domestic production percentage stands at 40%.
Ira Gabriel described the facility as the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
The facility is engineered to scale to 500,000 units by 2028.
The facility's capacity supports the production of Hyundai, Genesis and Kia EV and hybrid vehicles.
Localized manufacturing enhances availability, affordability and supply-chain resilience, which are key enablers of Hyundai's 2026 and long-term sales ambitions.
source_title: Hyundai's Georgia metaplant: US production, EV sales, battery innovation

facts: A U.S. agency plans to reexamine the environmental permit for Hyundai Motor Group’s EV factory in Georgia ahead of its planned opening in October.

The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (Usace) agreed to reassess the permit for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (Hmgma) after a conservation group alleged that the impact of the plant on the local water supply was not properly assessed.
Hmgma released a statement saying it is “aware of the request” and will cooperate with the reassessment.
Hmgma stated it is confident that Usace will supply the relevant data in a timely manner and will assist them as needed.
Hmgma added that it adheres to internationally recognized standards and guidelines to reduce environmental impacts and protect communities.
The plant is built on a 2,923-acre site in Bryan County, Georgia.
The HMGMA plant is scheduled to start production in early 2025 or sooner.
The plant is set to have an annual capacity of 300,000 vehicles, including Hyundai, Genesis and Kia EVs.
A letter from Usace said that the plant’s 2022 permit did not address the factory’s plans to withdraw up to 6.6 million gallons of local underground water per day.
The plan to withdraw water emerged earlier this year when the Georgia Environmental Protection Division considered a proposal to provide four new wells for the factory’s water supply.
Usace stated that the "permit is still valid [at this time]" and that the agency "has not requested that the permittee stop work."
The new factory is expected to create about 8,100 new jobs, according to Hmgma.
Georgia officials say it’s the largest economic development project the state has ever seen.
source_title: Hyundai’s Georgia EV plant faces permit review over water impact concerns

facts: HL-GA Battery Company LLC represents a groundbreaking collaboration between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution.
Both parent companies are global leaders committed to driving innovation in the electric vehicle (EV) industry.
The company is manufacturing state-of-the-art lithium-ion battery cells for electric vehicles to advance sustainable mobility.
The facility is strategically located at the HMGMA Metaplant site near the city of Savannah, Georgia.
Upon completion, the factory will boast a production capacity of 30GWh.
The 30GWh capacity is enough to power 300,000 electric vehicles annually.
HL-GA Battery Co. is listed as a Trustee Member and categorized under Aerospace, Auto Industry Suppliers by the Savannah Chamber.
The facility signifies a transformative investment in EV battery technology, setting new standards in efficiency, sustainability, and innovation.
source_title: HL-GA Battery Co. | Savannah Chamber

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp drove the new hybrid vehicle off the production line on Tuesday.
The Kia Sportage Turbo-Hybrid is the first Kia and first hybrid electric vehicle to be produced at the Ellabell Metaplant.
It is the third vehicle manufactured at the site since it began production in 2022, joining HMGMA’s electric Hyundai IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9.
While the Sportage Hybrid has been produced in Korea, this marks the first time it is being manufactured in the U.S.

Kia North America and America President and CEO Seungkyu (Sean) Yoon, Kia Georgia President and CEO Stuart Countess, and about 200 HMGMA employees known as Meta Pros joined Gov. Kemp to celebrate the vehicle reveal.
The hybrid Kia Sportage debuted by traveling to the stage on an Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR), a machine used throughout HMGMA’s production processes.
Two yellow “robot dogs” greeted guests at the event.
Gov. Brian Kemp called the reveal a historic moment.

Kemp stated that mutual success is the direct result of a proven partnership approach creating long-lasting relationships between Kia America, HMG’s Metaplant, the local community, and hardworking Georgians.
Kemp expressed looking forward to the new vehicle joining the Georgia-grown lineup and helping support jobs for communities for years to come.
The new vehicle’s production marks the first step in HMGMA’s pivot to producing hybrid vehicles.
Chris Susock, chief manufacturing officer for Hyundai Motor North America, stated the plant’s original intention was to manufacture all-electric vehicles.
There has been a sharp decline in U.S. electric vehicle demand, especially following the expiration of federal tax credits outlined in U.S. President Donald Trump’s “big beautiful bill.”
Midway through construction, officials decided to shift focus on making the plant adaptable to hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) production to balance market shifts.
Susock emphasized that the pivot focuses on flexibility in automation and technology to switch powertrain mix patterns on a dime and adapt to the market very quickly.
Last month, HMGMA shipped about 6,000 units outside the facility.
This shipment volume represents about 50% of the plant’s capacity.
The plant is ramped up to reach full capacity of about 580,000 units by 2028.
Susock stated that product planning will now rely on forecasts, and HMGMA has the capability and technology to build requested models quickly.
Future builds could include more Kias, different HEVs, or other models depending on demand.
Yoon noted that Kia is seeing higher demand for hybrids, especially SUVs including the Sportage, Carnival, and Telluride.
Tony Heo, President and CEO of HMGMA, stated that employees received extensive training to produce the new vehicle.
Many team members traveled to Kia facilities in West Point, Georgia, and South Korea to learn best practices for Sportage Hybrid production.
Both HMGMA and Kia’s plant in West Point, GA, emphasize the creation of high-quality jobs and economic opportunities for their local communities and across the state.
Yoon stated that the Metaplant in Savannah is a clear testament to confidence in Georgia’s future as an automotive powerhouse.
Yoon expressed great confidence that the Metaplant will continue strong momentum with the Sportage Hybrid quickly becoming one of Kia’s highest volume models.
source_title: Georgia’s Hyundai Metaplant begins producing third car - Bryan County News

facts: The state of Georgia and local governments will give Rivian Automotive $1.5 billion of incentives to build a 7,500-job, $5 billion electric vehicle plant east of Atlanta.
The size of the incentive package reflects the largest single industrial announcement in Georgia history.
Rivian pledges to reach full investment and job targets by the end of 2028.
Jobs at the new plant will pay an average of $56,000 a year, plus benefits.
The state hopes Rivian will anchor an entire electric vehicle industry in Georgia.
Greg LeRoy, executive director of Good Jobs First, stated it is the largest incentive package any American state has ever given to an auto plant.
Rivian is based in Irvine, California.
Rivian is a startup manufacturer of electric trucks and commercial delivery vans.
The company challenges established automakers like Ford and General Motors and electric vehicle leader Tesla.
Rivian is already producing vehicles at its existing facility in Normal, Illinois.
Rivian hopes to break ground on the Georgia plant as early as this summer.
Rivian plans to begin production in Georgia in 2024.
The company aims to produce 400,000 vehicles a year in Georgia.
Rivian stated the long-term economic partnership promises to deliver value to the company, Georgians, and future generations.
The plant site covers 2,000 acres (800 hectares).
The site is located between Social Circle and Rutledge, about 45 miles (70 kilometers) east of downtown Atlanta.
The state took over planning and zoning for the project after opponents overwhelmed Morgan County officials.
Residents have voiced concerns about possible well-water contamination, light pollution, and the disruption of wildlife and farmland.
A site plan shows Rivian responding to concerns by shifting the plant away from wetlands and agreeing to limit light pollution.
Former U.S. Sen. David Perdue is challenging Kemp in the May 24 Republican primary and lines up with opponents of the project.
George Soros bought $2 billion worth of shares around the time Rivian chose Georgia but owns only 2% of Rivian, with no evidence he influenced the plant location.
Perdue criticized the deal, saying it pads Soros' pockets and leaves hardworking Georgians footing the bill instead of helping small businesses.
Governor Kemp reiterated his backing for Rivian during a debate, supporting 7,500 jobs going to rural Georgia.
Local governments have agreed to $700 million in property tax breaks for the project.
Rivian plans to make more than $300 million in payments in lieu of taxes over 25 years beginning in 2023.
The state would spend $200 million to buy the site, grade it, build road improvements including a new Interstate 20 interchange, and extend utilities.
Georgia would spend $62.5 million to build a dedicated training center for the plant.
Georgia would spend a projected $27 million on providing job training.
Other benefits include a $200 million income tax credit at $5,250 per job over five years.
If Rivian did not owe that much state corporate income tax, Georgia would give personal income taxes collected from workers instead.
Georgia estimates sales tax exemptions will save Rivian $175 million on machinery and $105 million on construction materials.
Kia received more than $450 million in incentives for its plant in West Point, southwest of Atlanta.
Georgia promised SK Innovation $300 million in incentives for a $2.6 billion, 2,600-worker battery plant northeast of Atlanta.
The state has protections to claw back money if Rivian falls below 80% of promised investment or employment.
The state touts a $420 million annual payroll from the project.
An analysis shows there will be 8,000 more jobs created elsewhere in the state with a total impact of more than $7 billion.
LeRoy said incentives will cost $200,000 per job and state and local governments will never collect enough increased taxes to cover that.
Rivian currently plans two consumer models: the R1T pickup with a base price of $67,500 and the R1S SUV with a base price of $70,000.
Amazon owns 18% of Rivian and has ordered 100,000 delivery vans, launching the company into commercial vehicles.
Rivian raised $11.9 billion in a stock offering on Nov. 10 to finance the new plant.
Rivian's shares have fallen 70% since the stock offering as investors worry about production delays.
source_title: Georgia sets $1.5B in aid for electric vehicle maker Rivian | AP News

canonical_name: Rivian Georgia Electric Vehicle Manufacturing Facility
facts: Rivian aims to increase initial capacity at its future electric vehicle factory near Social Circle, Georgia by 50%, from 200,000 EVs a year to 300,000.
The company announced the capacity increase in an April 30 press release.

Construction is set to resume this year and is being funded by a $4.5 billion loan commitment by the Department of Energy’s Office of Energy Dominance Financing for the initial phase aimed at bolstering American manufacturing and construction jobs.
Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe stated, “We’re very excited to partner with the U.S. Department of Energy to grow our manufacturing footprint in Georgia.”

Scaringe noted that thousands of dedicated people will soon work in the Georgia plant, instrumental to Rivian’s growth as they scale American manufacturing and work to ensure that the U.S. retains its leadership in innovation and technology.
Increasing production capacity at the plant will lead to lower cost per unit and provide significant room for future expansion in phases.
As construction resumes this spring, Rivian expects to draw on the DOE loan by early 2027 and start production at the Georgia EV factory in late 2028.

Preparations have already begun for the development of the plant’s stamping press area, which Rivian says is one of the most capital-intensive and technically demanding projects from a construction perspective.
The work on the stamping press area will accelerate this summer, per the release.
Rivian continues to work with its partners in the communities surrounding the factory site as it transitions to vertical construction.
The construction of Rivian’s Georgia EV factory faced roadblocks.
In March 2024, Rivian announced it was pausing construction on the facility in order to conserve cash to launch and deliver its new R2 SUV.
First deliveries of the R2 SUV begin this spring.
At the time of the pause, the company said it was moving R2 production to its existing plant in Normal, Illinois, to expedite sales.
Rivian aims to grow its sales volume and ramp up production of the more affordable R2, which starts at $48,490.
In August 2025, Rivian said it was able to get the factory project back on track after it won final approval of a $6.6 billion DOE loan in January of that year.
The initial loan commitment from the DOE, which was first announced in November 2024, has been revised to up to $4.5 billion.
The revised loan includes $494 million capitalized interest for the initial phase.
Rivian said the updated loan amount is better aligned with the updated facility design and roadmap.
The Georgia plant is also slated to assemble the R2 in late 2028 as part of Uber’s investment in Rivian to supply it with robotaxis.
In March, the ride-hailing company announced it would invest up to $1.25 billion in Rivian and purchase 10,000 fully autonomous R2 SUVs to add to the Uber platform.
The deal includes an option for Uber or one of its fleet partners to purchase an additional 40,000 R2s in 2030.
Although the deal is still subject to regulatory approval, Uber has committed to an initial $300 million investment in Rivian.
Last July, Rivian announced it was building a new East Coast headquarters in Atlanta to support its operations in the state, including the EV plant.
source_title: Rivian raising Georgia EV plant’s initial capacity by 50% to 300K vehicles a year | WardsAuto

facts: Rivian will resume prep work on its planned Georgia factory in August and is looking to break ground early next year.
The restarted effort comes months after the Biden administration’s Department of Energy approved a $6.6 billion loan meant to fund construction.
Rivian has invested more than $80 million in the project as of June 20, 2025, up from $41 million in July 2024.
The project has created 46 full-time jobs so far.
Rivian will begin installing “deep utilities” in August, with “vertical construction” set to begin in the first quarter of 2026.
The company is reaching out to existing suppliers to see which ones might want to co-locate near the Georgia factory.
Rivian asked the state’s economic development department for a list of suppliers already in the region that may be able to help build the R2 SUV and R3 hatchback at the factory when it opens in 2028.
Rivian’s founder and CEO RJ Scaringe met with the state’s governor Brian Kemp at the end of May.
The company’s corporate affairs director told the governor’s office that the meeting was a “top priority” for the company.
Rivian spokesperson Peebles Squire said the meeting between Scaringe and the governor was a “regular check-in.”
Squire stated they discussed ongoing work in Georgia, gave general project updates, and discussed ways to continue a strong partnership with the state.
The governor’s press secretary did not respond to requests for comment.
Rivian first announced the Georgia factory shortly after its IPO in late 2021.
The company originally planned to start construction in 2022 and have vehicle production up and running by 2024.
Rivian promised to invest $5 billion in the facility.
In May 2022, Rivian lined up $1.5 billion in state incentives to help make that happen.
The factory quickly faced local opposition.
The project took a back seat as Rivian worked around supply chain shortages during the ramp-up of its R1T pickup truck and R1S SUV at its original factory in Normal, Illinois.
Rivian pushed back the timeline for the Georgia project in favor of expanding the Normal factory.
For the Normal factory expansion, Rivian nabbed $827 million in incentives from Illinois.
The company announced this delay in 2024 when it showed off the R2 SUV and R3 hatchback for the first time.
In late 2024, Rivian announced it had secured the $6.6 billion loan from the Department of Energy’s Loan Programs Office.
The loan would be coming from the Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing program.
That same program helped Tesla navigate the Great Recession more than a decade ago.
The loan agreement was finalized just a few days before Donald Trump was sworn in for his second term.
By that point, the deal had become a target of some people in the new president’s orbit.
Vivek Ramaswamy said he wanted to look into clawing back the loan.
After Trump took office, his administration froze all kinds of spending.
Some of those freezes were reversed by lower district courts, while others have remained in place as the Supreme Court has mostly allowed the president to operate more freely.
In February, Governor Kemp told a local news station he wasn’t sure of the status of the loan.
Squire said Rivian continues to work “with DOE and the administration to bring thousands of quality, good paying jobs back to the United States.”
Squire stated electric vehicles are a global strategic industry and the U.S. should maintain its leadership role in new technologies.
A few weeks into March, Rivian began coordinating with the governor’s staff for a face-to-face between Kemp and Scaringe.
The meeting was originally slated to take place on April 9 but had to be rescheduled because the Rivian CEO had a “personal conflict come up.”
Andrew Capezzuto, the corporate affairs director for Rivian, said the meeting was “a top priority” in an apologetic email about the rescheduling.
As Capezzuto hashed out a new time, he was also in regular contact with Georgia’s economic development department (GCED).
On April 8, Capezzuto told GCED they were interested in picking back up on supplier conversations.

Capezzuto asked GDEcD to provide an overview of existing suppliers within Georgia and the greater Southeast region to evaluate the supplier base and determine which suppliers to consider locating in Georgia to support the SSN facility.
Squire said Georgia and the Southeast have a very strong automotive supplier base and Rivian wants to leverage that base to optimize logistics costs and reinforce a strong supply chain.
Squire added it’s good for jobs, regionally and nationally, and promotes American manufacturing and economic development.
As Rivian ramps up supplier activity, the company is starting to hire workers to support the buildout of the factory.
It has posted seven open roles to LinkedIn within the last month, including one for construction manager.
source_title: Rivian restarting work on its Georgia factory, emails show | TechCrunch

facts: Kia executives met with Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp and state leaders on Feb. 13 to showcase the first electric vehicle built in Georgia.
A fleet of three-row electric EV9 SUVs, fresh off assembly lines at Kia’s West Point factory and recently highlighted in a Super Bowl ad, was parked outside the State Capitol (Gold Dome).
The display signals the Peach State's appeal as Kia, Hyundai, and Rivian ramp up plans to mass produce EVs across Georgia.
Steven Center, chief operating officer and executive vice president for Kia America, expressed pride in Kia being first with the EV9 leading the pack.
“We’re very proud of that, and we’re very excited for Georgia because of its big future in e-mobility,” Center said.
The EV9 is essentially an electric version of Kia’s popular three-row gas-powered Telluride crossover.
The EV9 starts at $56,395 and is currently on sale.
Kia’s West Point plant is undergoing a $200 million expansion to incorporate EV9 production onto its current assembly lines.
The expansion adds 200 jobs.
Stuart Countess, head of Kia’s operations in West Point, stated the expansion is on pace to officially open in the spring.
Production lines at the West Point plant will be able to switch between producing gas-powered vehicles and plug-in SUVs.
“We have flexibility, so that we can move as the market is moving in either direction,” Countess said.
The West Point factory has an annual production capacity of about 350,000 vehicles.
Kia has shifted its focus toward hiring and training workers as part of the expansion.
Kia recently launched its Veterans Technical Apprenticeship Program, which company officials discussed with state leaders.
Kia and other automakers are facing renewed unionization campaigns, with the United Auto Workers specifically targeting Southern factories including the West Point plant.
Steven Center stated that Kia brass does not expect union representation efforts to succeed.
“We have a good relationship with people in manufacturing, so there’s no upside for them to have (union) representation,” Center said.
source_title: Kia Debuts First Georgia-Made EV at State Capitol - TT

facts: Kia invested $200 million to expand operations at its plant in West Point, Georgia.
The expansion occurred 10 months prior to the release of the EV9.
Kia released the EV9, the first electric vehicle manufactured in the state of Georgia.
Gov. Brian Kemp drove the EV9 off the production line at the West Point plant on a Thursday.
Gov. Brian Kemp congratulated the Kia Georgia team for boosting the state’s EV portfolio, strengthening the battery belt, and advancing the climate agenda.
Transportation activities accounted for 37.4 percent of U.S. CO2 emissions from fossil fuel combustion in 2022, according to the EPA US Greenhouse Gas Inventory.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Georgia is working to become the e-mobility capital of the nation.
The EV9 went on sale at the end of 2023.
Prior to Georgia production, EV9s were shipped from South Korea and sold in all 50 states.
The EV9 has received industry accolades including World Car of the Year, World EV of the Year, and North American Utility Vehicle of the Year.
Sen. Jon Ossoff congratulated the Kia Georgia team at the rollout ceremony.
Sen. Jon Ossoff stated that Georgians are proud of the cars produced in Georgia and made in America.
Manufacturing the vehicle on American soil makes it at least partially eligible for a $7,500 EV tax credit under the Inflation Reduction Act.
The $7,500 EV tax credit requires the EV to be made in America.
The second requirement for the tax credit relates to battery components.
Anne Blair, VP of the Electrification Coalition, stated that 50% of mineral components in an EV must now be sourced and processed in the U.S. or a trade partner country.
Kia has not confirmed whether the Georgia-assembled EV9 will qualify for the full $7,500 tax credit.
Kia Georgia spokesman Patrick Sands stated the company expects the Georgia-assembled EV9 to qualify for available incentives in the near future.
Kia will provide additional details regarding tax credit qualification at a later date.
The EV9 batteries take eight hours to charge on a 220V Level 2 circuit.
The EV9 battery comes with a 10-year/100,00-mile warranty.
Federal regulations mandate a minimum eight-year warranty for EV batteries.
The EV9's MSRP ranges from $55,000 to $70,000, depending on trim level and incentives.
The plant's expansion in July added 200 jobs.
The new jobs support EV production as well as the Telluride, Sorento, and Sportage lineups.
The West Point plant is listed as the fifth largest employer on the 'Choose Columbus' website.
Approximately 86 people from Columbus work at the Kia Georgia plant in West Point.
source_title: Kia at West Point just made history, rolling out first-ever EV made in Georgia, an EV9 SUV | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: On Feb. 24, 2026, Kia Georgia team members marked a historic day at its West Point facility with the start of production for the all-new 2027 Kia Telluride.

The ceremonial 2027 Kia Telluride represented two additional major milestones: the first hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) assembled in the state of Georgia and the 5 millionth vehicle produced at Kia Georgia.
A ceremony for the milestone was attended by Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp, team members, and state/community leaders.

Sean Yoon, President and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that as the only plant in the world that builds the Telluride, the launch reflects the strength of the West Point team, customer trust, and Kia’s commitment to building the future of mobility in Georgia.
Sean Yoon noted that reaching five million vehicles and introducing Kia’s first US-assembled HEV is a testament to Kia’s history and direction going forward.
Since beginning production in 2009, Kia Georgia has grown into one of the region’s largest employers and a cornerstone of automotive manufacturing in the Southeast.
The Telluride is one of the industry’s most awarded vehicles, has become a flagship model for the brand, and is a symbol of quality built in West Point.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that since its introduction in 2019, the Telluride has been exclusively Georgia-made and has become one of Kia's most successful vehicles.
Governor Kemp attributed the Telluride's success to a proven partnership approach creating long-lasting relationships between Kia Georgia, the local community, and the state.
Stuart Countess, Kia Georgia’s President and CEO, emphasized that the day’s milestones were made possible by the dedication of team members at Kia Georgia.

Stuart Countess stated that five million vehicles are the result of famous teamwork and highlighted the plant's ability to produce world-class gasoline-powered vehicles, EVs, and now HEVs as a testament to versatility and talent in West Point.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Corporation, which is based in Seoul, Korea.
Kia Georgia is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Ga., and has an annual capacity of 350,000 units.
Kia Georgia began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
Kia Georgia produces the Telluride, Sorento (excludes HEV and PHEV models), Sportage (excludes HEV and PHEV models) SUVs, and the all-electric EV9 and EV6.
source_title: Kia Georgia celebrates trio of major milestones, including Start of Production for 2027 Kia Telluride AUTOJUNCTION - Autojunction

facts: The all-electric EV9 is assembled in Kia’s West Point, Georgia plant.
The EV9 qualifies for a $7,500 federal EV tax credit.
Sales of the EV9 are anticipated to begin in the second quarter of 2025.
The 2026 Kia EV9 Nightfall Edition debuted on April 16, 2025, at the 2025 New York International Auto Show.
The EV9 is offered on a dual-motor Land AWD trim with standard six-passenger seating and an available seven-passenger configuration.
The EV9 Nightfall Edition features exclusive 20-inch Gloss Black wheels wrapped in 275/50R-20 all-season tires.
A standard Boost function increases torque from 443 lb.-ft to 516 lb.-ft, allowing acceleration from zero to 60 mph in 4.5 seconds.
The EV9 generates 379 horsepower from its dual motors.
The EV9's adoption of the North American Charging Standard allows owners to access the Tesla Supercharger network and, with an optional adapter, existing Combined Charging System networks.
Kia America is headquartered in Irvine, California.
Kia serves as the "Official Automotive Partner" of the NBA and WNBA.
Kia sells its vehicles through a network of over 775 dealers in the U.S.
The EV9 all-electric three-row SUV is assembled in the United States from U.S. and globally sourced parts.
Steven Center serves as COO & EVP at Kia America.
“This award-winning SUV continues to be a landmark win for the Kia brand,” said Steven Center, COO & EVP of Kia America.
source_title: 2026 KIA EV9 NEW NIGHTFALL EDITION ENHANCES THREE-ROW ELECTRIC SUV WITH DISTINCTIVE STYLING, BOOSTED ACCELERATION

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group & SK On Joint Battery Facility
facts: Hyundai Motor Group has officially announced it has inked a deal with the state of Georgia and battery manufacturer SK On to build a new electric vehicle battery production plant.
The project is one of Georgia's most significant economic development projects in its history.
It will create an estimated 3,500 new jobs.
It involves approximately $4-$5 billion worth of investment in Bartow County.
Bartow County is located about an hour northwest of Atlanta.
The announcement comes only a couple of weeks after Hyundai and SK On signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) outlining their strategic partnership.
Governor Brian Kemp stated: "Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and key players in our state's ever-growing automotive industry."

Governor Kemp added: "Since day one, my administration has been focused on bringing jobs and opportunity to communities across the state that may have been overlooked in the past. SK and HMG share this goal, and we're proud they are choosing to invest even further in this No. 1 state for business."
Specific details of the companies' partnership are still being ironed out, but the project is definitely happening.
The new facility will be located at Bartow Centre, a manufacturing and industrial site on Highway 411.
Construction should be getting underway relatively soon because operations are set to begin in 2025.
SK On is a subsidiary of South Korea's SK Innovation.
SK On was established in 2021.
SK On already employs over 2,000 Georgia residents at an existing facility in Commerce.
Last summer, SK Battery America announced a partnership with Work for Warriors Georgia, a program dedicated to hiring veterans, service members, and their families.
Hyundai has been very aggressive with its EV lineup, most recently with unveiling the Ioniq 6 GT at last month's LA Auto Show.
Hyundai Motor Group includes the Kia and Genesis brands.
Hyundai has a massive vehicle production plant located in nearby Alabama.
Along with this new battery plant, Hyundai announced last May plans to invest $5.54 billion in a separate and also new dedicated EV manufacturing facility in Bryan County, Georgia.
The automaker broke ground on the Bryan County facility in October.
All told, Hyundai's EV-related projects announced since 2020 amount to nearly $17 billion in investment and over 22,800 new jobs in the state.
source_title: Hyundai Announces Major EV Battery Facility To Be Built In America

facts: A Commerce electric vehicle battery manufacturing plant laid off over a third of its workforce.
958 employees were let go at the SK Battery America plant.
Impacted workers will still be paid through May 6.

Jackson County Commission Chairman Marty Clark called the layoffs difficult news for the community and noted that while Jackson County's economic outlook is stable, the decision affects hardworking families.
The Korean company opened the $2.6-billion plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January of 2022.
Georgia U.S. Senators Jon Ossoff (D) and Raphael Warnock (D) issued statements on Friday regarding the layoffs.
Senator Jon Ossoff stated that battery manufacturing jobs are gone and blamed Trump's war on electric vehicles for hurting Georgia's economy, noting Georgians are losing jobs.

Senator Raphael Warnock said the Trump Administration is putting politics over people, praised Georgia workers for making the state a leader in advanced manufacturing, criticized the administration for slashing clean energy investments and imposing harmful tariff taxes that make manufacturing in Georgia more expensive, and vowed to fight for Georgia workers and protect investments.
SK Americas spokesperson Joe Guy Collier stated the workforce reduction was made to align operations to market conditions.

Collier said SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing, and is pursuing future customers including the Battery Electric Storage System arena.
The Associated Press provided original reporting on March 6, 2026 @ 4:50 p.m. by Alexa St. John and Jeff Amy.
SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at its manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday amid automakers’ changing electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for EVs.
The layoffs mark the last working day for 958 plant employees, about 37% of its workforce, according to a WARN notice filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
The plant will continue to employ about 1,600 workers after the layoffs.
SK notably supplied the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck before Ford announced plans in December to cancel the fully electric version of the truck.

The U.S. electric vehicle market is at a standstill as the Trump administration steers federal support away from electrification toward lax automotive emissions policies and an agenda supporting oil and gas industries.
The City of Commerce and the Jackson County commission chair did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Ford scrapped the fully-electric version of its F-150 pickup in December, opting for an extended-range version, and a Ford spokesperson declined to comment on supplier personnel actions.
SK and Ford previously invested $11.4 billion in joint battery plants in the U.S., but SK ended the joint venture in December.
SK is also a supplier to Volkswagen.
SK has invested significantly in Jackson County, Georgia, as automakers developed EVs and the Biden administration supported a domestic EV supply chain.
In June 2020, SK announced plans to pour $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta, which Gov. Brian Kemp’s office said would create 600 jobs.
SK and Hyundai are still jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has attracted massive EV manufacturing investments, including Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s own $7.6 billion factory complex.
Few states benefited more than Georgia from Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act, which accelerated a rush of green energy projects.

According to environmental business group E2, 33 additional EV-related projects were announced nationwide by the end of 2024, with Georgia projects topping $20 billion and pledging more than 25,000 jobs.

Qcells, a unit of South Korea’s Hanwha Solutions, resumed normal production on Friday after temporarily reducing hours and pay last year due to U.S. customs officials detaining imported components for solar panels.
EV demand has not met automakers’ ambitious expectations, with EVs accounting for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. in 2025, matching the prior year.
Automakers have been reevaluating multibillion-dollar electrification plans as financial losses mount and consumer demand shifts.

Manufacturers including Ford, General Motors, Stellantis, and others across the EV supply chain have reneged on factory, investment, and product plans, laid off workers, and pivoted to hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles.
Hybrids and more efficient gasoline-powered vehicles are becoming more palatable for mainstream buyers concerned about EV driving range and charging infrastructure availability.
Under President Donald Trump, Congress eliminated tax credits of up to $7,500 for consumers’ purchases of new or used EVs.
The Trump administration announced plans to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules for automakers, eliminating federal incentives for cleaner vehicle fleets.
source_title: SK Battery lays off 958, Jackson Co. Commission Chair: "difficult news for our community" - AccessNorthGA

facts: Construction at the HL-GA Battery Company joint venture between Hyundai and LG Energy Solution has been paused.
Homeland Security Investigations targeting of four individuals led to the detainment of about 475 people on Thursday, Sept. 4.
On Sept. 5, a U.S. Department of Justice press release referred to the HSI and ICE raid at HL-GA Battery Company, LLC at 9730 US Highway 280 in Ellabell, Ga, as Operation Low Voltage.
The warrant for the Sept. 4 raid named Andreina Fuentes-Tovar, Kevin Zavaleta-Ramirez, David Zavaleta-Ramirez and Julio Gonzalez Alvarado as "Target Persons."
Approximately 400 officers across multiple federal and Georgia agencies assisted with the operation, according to ICE public affairs officer Lindsay Williams.
The battery plant was first announced on Friday, May 26, 2023, as part of a state economic agreement with Hyundai to build an EV manufacturing plant and affiliate facilities in Bryan County.
Production of EV batteries was slated to begin later this year once construction had been completed on what eventually came to be known as HL-GA Battery Company.
LG was responsible for the governance of the HL-GA facility, including during its construction, until the past week, according to HMGMA Communication and Public Relations Manager Bianca Johnson.
LG officials announced they are cooperating fully with authorities and have paused construction on the site.
The warrant identifies the HL-GA plant as a 35-acre site within the larger Hyundai megasite campus in Ellabell, Georgia.
The Hyundai megasite also houses or will house five Hyundai affiliates: Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai Glovis, Hyundai Transys, Hyundai Steel, and Hyundai Material.

HMGMA officials have requested that media outlets refer to the location as "the Bryan County megasite," "the Hyundai megasite," or "the Hyundai vehicle manufacturing site in Bryan County" because HMGMA is just one independent operation onsite.
Hyundai's economic agreement with Georgia requires all companies on the megasite to create and sustain a combined 6,800 to 8,500 permanent jobs by Dec. 31, 2031.
The agreement mandates that the megasite jobs' salaries must average $58,105.
The battery plant initially committed to fulfilling 1,600 of the required jobs and later committed to 400 more for a total of 2,000.
HMGMA officials have stated it aims to hire around 2,600 workers.
Roughly 300 of the detained individuals were reported to be Korean nationals.
LG Communications and Government Affairs Manager Phil Lienert confirmed that 47 of the detained individuals are LG employees, with 46 being Korean and one Indonesian.
About 250 detainees are affiliated with HL-GA's partner companies, most of whom are Korean according to Lienert.
Individuals detained in the Sept. 4 operation were immediately taken to Folkston Detention Center in South Georgia.
In June, Charlton County approved a $50 million contract with ICE to expand Folkston Detention Center, which would make it the largest detention center in the country.
Some detained workers may have relied on the U.S. Visa Waiver Program or B-1 business visas to enter the United States for stays of 90 days or less without a traditional visa.
Travelers under the Visa Waiver Program must have a valid Electronic System for Travel Authorization (ESTA).

Atlanta-based immigration lawyer Charles Kuck affirmed that many Korean detainees from the HL-GA plant were in the country on B-1 visas, which may be used for business consulting, contract negotiation, or short-term training.
As of publication, no evidence confirmed that any detained person used these authorizations to work at or contract for work at the HL-GA plant.
The warrant for Operation Low Voltage was filed on Tuesday, Sept. 2, and commanded execution on or before Sept. 14.
U.S. Magistrate Judge Christopher Ray of the Southern District of Georgia signed the warrant.
Assistant United States Attorney Tania Groover, on behalf of U.S. Attorney Meg Heap, filed a request to unseal the warrant on Thursday, Sept. 4.
Groover, along with Ryan Bondura, Kelsey Scanlon, and E. Greg Gilluly, Jr., are investigating the case.

The warrant stated officials were seeking to seize documents related to ownership and management of HL-GA and contractors TK LLC, Autorica LLC, SBY America, Beyond Iron Construction LLC, and Steel Brothers Development Inc.
Potential documentation sought included time cards, payroll information, photographs of employees, Visas, permanent resident cards, employment authorization cards, passports, and birth certificates.
Beyond Iron Construction LLC's site address is listed as 1 LG Road, Ellabell, Georgia, 31308, with a mailing address at 3229 Weyhill Trail, Buford, Georgia, 30519.
Whether the construction pause will impact EV production at HMGMA remains unknown.
HMGMA had asserted it will produce 500,000 units annually once fully operational.
Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO José Muñoz stated in March at the metaplant's grand opening that production would include hybrid vehicles at what was initially touted as an all-EV plant.
The HSI and ICE operation followed other recent challenges to the EV industry, including lower than expected consumer demand over the past year.
In February, the Trump Administration froze $5 billion for EV charger infrastructure for America's highways, which had been appropriated by Congress under the Biden Administration.
The funding freeze lasted about six months until June when a federal judge ruled in favor of several states that sued the federal government over withholding the funds.
In February, Aspen Aerogels Inc. pulled out of its contract to construct a $325-million plant in Statesboro.
The Trump Administration removed federal tax credits for EVs, meaning after Sept. 30, credits worth $4,000 for used EVs or $7,500 for new ones will no longer be available to consumers.
source_title: ICE warrant for Hyundai megasite sought four "target persons"

facts: U.S. officials stated that the majority of the 475 arrestees were Korean nationals, prompting concern from the South Korean government.
Several federal and state law enforcement agencies raided the construction site of a Hyundai joint-venture in Georgia, arresting 475 individuals for alleged immigration violations.
The EV battery plant is known as HL-GA Battery Company.
HL-GA Battery Company is a joint venture between Hyundai and LG Energy Solution.
The plant is located next to Hyundai’s “Metaplant” in Ellabell, Georgia, southwest of Savannah.
The plant was slated to open by the end of this year or early 2026.
The plant will supply batteries for the Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 EV SUVs.
DHS spokesperson Steven Schrank stated the arrests were made for entering the U.S. unlawfully, overstaying visas, and working unlawfully.
No charges have been filed yet as the investigation continues.
The raid is part of President Donald Trump’s controversial crackdown on unlawful immigration.
A spokesperson for LG told the New York Times that both LG employees and Hyundai employees were arrested.
Hyundai told the New York Times separately that it believed none of its employees were arrested.
Hyundai told Fox News that as of today, it understands that none of those detained are directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company.
Hyundai stated it prioritizes the safety and well-being of everyone working at the site and complies with all laws and regulations wherever it operates.
Schrank said some of the arrested individuals worked for subcontractors rather than directly for either parent company.
Schrank said some number of U.S. citizens and lawful residents were detained and are now being released.

DHS spokesperson Steven Schrank stated the operation underscores a commitment to protecting jobs for Georgians and Americans, ensuring a level playing field for compliant businesses, safeguarding economic integrity, and protecting workers from exploitation.
DHS told CNN it executed a search warrant as part of an ongoing criminal investigation into allegations of unlawful employment practices and other serious federal crimes.

ICE officials told the BBC the raid culminated a multi-month criminal investigation involving developed evidence, conducted interviews, gathered documents, and presented evidence to a court to obtain an official search warrant.
source_title: Massive Federal Raid at Hyundai's Metaplant Complex In Georgia, 475 Arrested

facts: Immigration enforcement agents stormed a Hyundai electric vehicle battery production plant in Georgia last Friday, citing a shockingly large number of illegal workers and raising concerns about workforce security.
Hyundai Motor Company stated it is aware of the immigration enforcement action that took place at the construction site of its supplier, HL-GA Battery Company in Bryan County, Georgia.
Hyundai stated it is "closely monitoring the situation while working to understand the specific circumstances."
Based on Hyundai's current understanding, none of those detained are directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company.
Hyundai committed to full compliance with all laws and regulations in every market where it operates, including employment verification requirements and immigration laws.
Hyundai expects the same commitment to legal compliance from all its partners, suppliers, contractors, and subcontractors.
Hyundai stated that the safety and well-being of everyone working at its sites is a top priority.
Hyundai views incidents like this as reminders of the importance of robust oversight throughout its entire supply chain and contractor network.

Hyundai is reviewing its processes to ensure all parties working on its projects maintain high standards of legal compliance, including thorough vetting of employment practices by contractors and subcontractors.
North America Chief Manufacturing Officer Chris Susock will now assume governance of the entire megasite in Georgia.
Hyundai will conduct an investigation to ensure all suppliers and their subcontractors comply with all laws and regulations.
Hyundai stated it has zero tolerance for those who don't follow the law.
Hyundai continues to invest in American manufacturing and create thousands of jobs in full accordance with U.S. law, reflecting values of treating all people with dignity and respect.
source_title: Hyundai Raid

entity_name: HL-GA Battery Company at the Hyundai megasite
canonical_name: HL-GA Battery Company at the Hyundai megasite
facts: A news conference to announce the results of an ICE raid at the HL-GA Battery Company at the Hyundai megasite was held in Savannah, Ga., around 10:30 a.m. on September 5, 2025.
The investigation resulted in the arrest of 475 individuals who worked for a variety of contractors and subcontractors on the site.
The majority of those arrested were Korean nationals who entered the U.S. illegally through various means, including illegal border crossings, visa waiver violations, and visa overstays.
HSI Special Agent in Charge Steven Schrank stated that the operation underscores a commitment to protecting jobs for Georgians and Americans.
Most of the arrested individuals were transported to the Folkston ICE Processing Center.
Charges have not yet been filed, and officials described the situation as an ongoing investigation with no wrongdoing accused at this time.
There were no substantial uses of force, resulting in only one person overheating and one agent suffering a minor laceration.
HSI Special Agent in Charge Steven Schrank confirmed this was the largest single-site operation in the history of Homeland Security Investigations in the country.

The press conference featured participants including US Attorney Margaret E. Heap, ICE Supervisory Detention and Deportation Officer R. Brandon Eccles, FBI Assistant Special Agent in Charge Brian Ozden, ATF Resident Agent in Charge Joseph L. Waller, DEA Assistant Special Agent in Charge Brian A Villella, and U.S. Marshal Brett Hall.

According to a press release, several individuals attempted to flee the location by running into a sewage pond on the premises, with agents using a boat to retrieve them and one individual attempting to flip the boat over before being captured as illegal workers.

The investigation was led by Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), with assistance from U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) Enforcement and Removal Operations, FBI, ATF, DEA, IRS Criminal Investigations, DOL Office of Inspector General, U.S. Marshal Service, and Georgia State Patrol.
Assistant United States Attorneys Tania Groover, Ryan Bondura, Kelsey Scanlon, and E. Greg Gilluly, Jr., are investigating the case.
source_title: ‘The largest single-site operation in history’: HSI, ICE reveals number of Hyundai megasite arrests

facts: Hyundai Mobis is one of the world's largest auto suppliers.
Hyundai Mobis is building a new $205 million EV battery plant in Montgomery, Alabama.
The new plant is intended to feed the electric vehicle production process.
Hyundai Mobis has long been the largest tier 1 supplier to Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis currently supplies full vehicle modules and assemblies from its existing 83-acre site in Montgomery.
The new facility will be a second site measuring 450,000 square feet located near HMMA.
The new facility will serve as a vehicle battery module plant for Hyundai and Kia.
Company officials expect the new plant to employ 400 people with "competitive" salaries.

H.S. Oh, vice president of the electric powertrain business for Hyundai Mobis, stated that strong production capacity is key as the EV market continues to grow and allows Hyundai Mobis to see continued growth in the market.
H.S. Oh said the new Montgomery facility will be an important step in that process and expressed excitement for the project to be under way.
Construction at a site just off Interstate 65 in Montgomery is expected to start as early as December.
EV battery assembly production is expected to start in 2024.
HMMA will provide the land for the new Hyundai Mobis plant.
Hyundai announced earlier this year it planned to pour $300 million into its Montgomery plant to start EV production there.
The $300 million investment includes production of an all-electric Genesis GV70 and a hybrid electric Santa Fe.
HMMA President and CEO Ernie Kim said the new Hyundai Mobis plant would help Hyundai accelerate its transition to electric vehicles.
Montgomery Mayor Steven Reed thanked county, state, and HMMA leaders for helping secure the new plant.
Mayor Reed said HMMA President Kim "didn't want to lose (the Hyundai Mobis project) to Georgia" and sounded like an Alabama fan.
Mayor Reed stated, "Well, we didn't lose to Georgia."
Alabama Gov. Kay Ivey said the electric battery plant announcement "speaks volumes as to what we as a city and as a state are all about."
Gov. Ivey told company leaders that the expansion will help production at Hyundai and provide some 400 more Alabama families a good living, guaranteeing those workers will not let them down.
Hyundai's Alabama supply chain has been under scrutiny in recent months.
Hyundai Global President and COO Jose Munoz told Reuters the company plans to "sever relations" with two Alabama suppliers accused of employing underage workers: SL Alabama and SMART Alabama LLC.
Jose Munoz also told Reuters he ordered a more comprehensive investigation into potential labor violations across Hyundai's supply chain.
Hyundai spokesman Ira Gabriel told the Montgomery Advertiser that the investigation is ongoing.
source_title: Supplier Hyundai Mobis will build a $205M EV battery plant in Montgomery

facts: Hyundai Mobis announced the construction of a $205M EV battery plant outside Montgomery.
The factory will be 450,000 square feet.
The facility will manufacture 200,000 battery modules for electric vehicles.
The battery modules are intended for EVs produced in Alabama and Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis announces construction of $205M EV battery pla...

canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Battery Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai and LG Energy Solution are set to invest $2 billion more into their joint venture battery plant near Savannah.
The additional investment will create 400 jobs above the companies' initial pledge.
In May, the companies stated the battery cell facility located on the site of the Hyundai Metaplant in Ellabell would require an investment of $4.3 billion.
State officials clarified that the $4.3 billion figure was not new capital but part of a previously announced $5.5 billion total for the battery facility and adjacent EV factory.
On Thursday, the companies announced an additional $2 billion investment in the battery facility, bringing the full project total to $7.59 billion.
With the additional investment, the Metaplant is expected to employ 8,500 people across the battery and EV assembly facilities.
This employment figure represents an increase from the 8,100 jobs initially promised.
The incremental investment is located in Bryan County.

José Muñoz, president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Co. and president and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, stated the investment reflects a commitment to create a sustainable future powered by American workers.
It remains unclear how the new capital will be deployed, specifically whether it will accelerate production timelines or expand factory capacity ahead of future growth.
According to Governor Brian Kemp's office, the factory will initially produce 30 GWh of battery cells annually.
This annual capacity matches the figure announced when the partnership was publicized earlier in the year.
The facility's output is initially pegged to power 300,000 EVs.
This production capacity matches the initial capacity planned for the Hyundai Metaplant.
The battery cells produced by the 50-50 joint venture will be sent to Hyundai Mobis.
Hyundai Mobis is located on the 2,900-acre Metaplant site.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble the cells into modules before integration into the EV assembly process.
The project adds to numerous South Korean investments worth billions of dollars and thousands of jobs in Georgia.
Affiliated suppliers have announced over $2 billion in investments for nearby factories intended to supply the company's first dedicated U.S. EV plant.
Hyundai is ramping up electric model production at its existing Alabama factory.
Kia is ramping up electric model production at its existing Georgia factory.
Kia is investing $200 million in West Point for EV9 production.
The EV9 is the first all-electric seven-seater SUV introduced in the U.S.
Kia's EV9 production is targeted to be completed by the end of 2024.
Hyundai started manufacturing the electrified Genesis GV70 in Montgomery, Alabama earlier this year.
The U.S. began subsidizing domestic EV production through purchase incentives in the Inflation Reduction Act passed last August.
Due to their Korean origin, none of Hyundai Motor Group's EVs currently qualify for the subsidies, except when leased.
Company leaders and Korean diplomats have visited Georgia over the last year to lobby for qualification.
Hyundai received a $1.8 billion incentive package for its $5.5 billion plant.
It is unclear if the additional $2 billion commitment requires a separate incentive package.
source_title: Hyundai, LG to Put $2B More Into Georgia Battery Joint Venture  - Global Atlanta

facts: Hyundai Mobis announced the beginning of construction of a new EV-dedicated battery system plant in Indonesia.
The company plans to complete the Indonesian plant by the first half of next year.
Hyundai Mobis intends to use Indonesia as a beachhead for the ASEAN market to become a leader in the electrification ecosystem, which includes cells, battery systems, and OEMs.
A groundbreaking ceremony for the battery system plant was held in Bekasi, West Java, on the outskirts of Jakarta on May 31.
The ceremony was attended by about 150 participants.

Attendees included Mochamad Ridwan Kamil (Governor of West Java Province), Ambassador of the Republic of Korea Lee Sang-deok, Oh Heung-sub (Senior Vice President of Electrification at Hyundai Mobis), Indonesian Government officials, industry leaders, and local journalists.
The Indonesian battery system plant will serve as a foundation for Hyundai Mobis' global electrification business, particularly targeting the ASEAN market.
The Indonesian government is accelerating efforts to secure leadership in the EV market by leveraging the country's ample natural resources.
The demand for EVs in the ASEAN region, centered on Indonesia, is increasing rapidly.
Hyundai Mobis will use battery cells supplied by the joint venture HLI Green Power for the plant.
The plant will convert these cells into modules that include a controller and a heat controller.
The completed large battery systems will be supplied to global automakers.
Hyundai Mobis will act as an intermediary to complete the EV ecosystem through this plant.
The Indonesian plant will be located in the Deltamas industrial complex, approximately 60 km southeast of Jakarta.
The site is located 3 km from Hyundai Motor Company’s Indonesian plant and 10 km from HLI Green Power to maximize delivery efficiency.
Hyundai Motor Group has invested a total of USD 60 million for the plant.
The plant will be built on a land area of approximately 33,000 square meters.
The battery systems will initially be supplied to flagship EV models scheduled for release in Southeast Asia next year.
The Indonesian government provided high support for administrative matters related to the plant's construction, aligning with its policy to strengthen the local EV market and ecosystem.
The Indonesian government provided support regarding construction approvals, environmental assessments, logistics costs, and tax benefits.
Oh Heung-sub, Senior Vice President of Electrification at Hyundai Mobis, thanked the Indonesian government for its support.
Oh Heung-sub stated that "Hyundai Mobis will become a partner for Indonesia and will play a pivotal role in establishing the EV ecosystem."
Hyundai Mobis holds half of the stake in the joint battery cell venture HLI Green Power (Hyundai Mobis 25%, Hyundai Motors 15%, Kia 10%).
HLI Green Power is a joint battery cell venture currently being built near Hyundai Mobis' local plant.
Hyundai Mobis aims to expand and develop the Indonesian plant into a long-term strategic base for major global customers due to the rising ASEAN market.
Hyundai Mobis currently produces EV components, including battery systems, in Korea and Europe (Czech Republic, Slovakia).
In October of the previous year, Hyundai Mobis announced a plan to invest USD 1.3 billion by 2030 to expand its electrification production base in North America.
Hyundai Mobis is currently constructing five additional electrification component plants in Alabama and Georgia, USA.
The global expansion will complete an electrification value chain linking Asia (Korea and Indonesia), Europe, and North America.
The expansion is expected to reinforce Hyundai Mobis' capability to respond to the EV market.
Hyundai Mobis is the global No. 6 automotive supplier, headquartered in Seoul, Korea.
The company has expertise in sensors, sensor fusion in ECUs, and software development for safety control.
Hyundai Mobis' products include components for electrification, brakes, chassis and suspension, steering, airbags, lighting, and automotive electronics.
Hyundai Mobis operates its R&D headquarters in Korea with four technology centers in the United States, Germany, China, and India.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis Breaks Ground On New Battery System Plant - Invest Indonesia

facts: Hyundai Motor Group inaugurated its $7.6 billion electric vehicle (EV) manufacturing plant in Georgia on March 26.
It is the first factory built under Chairman Chung Eui-sun’s leadership.
The project marks a major expansion in the U.S. market, two decades after his father, Honorary Chairman Chung Mong-Koo, opened the company’s Alabama plant to begin local production.
The new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) aims to strengthen the company’s U.S. footprint amid rising concerns over tariffs under the Trump administration.
Hyundai is increasing local production to reduce trade risks and strengthen its position in its largest overseas market.
Construction of the 11,760,000㎡ facility began in October 2022 and was completed in two and a half years.
The plant has an initial production capacity of 300,000 vehicles per year.
An additional 200,000-unit expansion is approved.
Combined with Hyundai’s Alabama plant (360,000 units) and Kia’s Georgia plant (340,000 units), Hyundai’s U.S. production capacity will reach 1.2 million units annually.
Based on its 2024 U.S. sales of 1.71 million vehicles, approximately 70% of Hyundai’s American sales will now be locally assembled.
The new factory is located within a four- to five-hour drive from Hyundai’s existing U.S. plants, enabling logistical efficiencies and economies of scale in parts procurement.
The inauguration comes at a critical time as Hyundai faces declining sales in South Korea and other global markets, with the U.S. remaining its key growth driver.
Trump administration’s push for higher tariffs on imported vehicles has raised uncertainty for foreign automakers.
Chairman Chung emphasized that the investment was about long-term commitment rather than immediate tariff concerns.

At the ceremony, Chairman Chung said: “Hyundai Motor Group invests not only in technology and automobiles but, above all, in relationships. We are here not just to build a factory but to put down roots.”
In a Q&A session, Chung dismissed suggestions that the plant was built in response to tariff risks.
Chung stated: “Rather than preparing for tariffs, we see this as part of a broader effort to manufacture EVs with low-carbon steel in the U.S.”
This refers to Hyundai’s plans to use locally sourced sustainable materials.

Addressing Trump’s planned reciprocal tariffs set to take effect on April 2, Chung said: “Tariffs are a matter between governments. While individual companies will negotiate, the real discussions will begin after April 2.”
The HMGMA facility will initially produce Hyundai’s Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 models before expanding to include all Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EVs.
Initially intended for EVs, the plant will start producing hybrids in 2026 to address slowing demand.
Previously, Hyundai’s two U.S. factories primarily produced internal combustion vehicles, with limited hybrid and EV output.
The new plant significantly enhances Hyundai’s ability to meet growing U.S. demand for eco-friendly cars.
The plant incorporates cutting-edge automation and AI-driven manufacturing technologies.
It is Hyundai’s first “software-defined factory,” digitizing the entire production process.
AI-powered cameras detect panel defects.
Boston Dynamics’ four-legged Spot robots inspect vehicle specifications.
Hyundai plans to introduce the humanoid robot “All New Atlas” for trial operations.
Hyundai envisions HMGMA as an advanced EV production hub.
The company is constructing a battery cell plant in partnership with SK On and LG Energy Solution.
Four Hyundai affiliates operate facilities within the complex: Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai Glovis, Hyundai Steel, and Hyundai Transys.
Hyundai Mobis supplies battery systems.
Hyundai Glovis manages logistics.
Hyundai Steel produces automotive-grade steel.
Hyundai Transys manufactures seats.

The inauguration was attended by Georgia Governor Brian Kemp, U.S. Representative Buddy Carter, Georgia Institute of Technology President Ángel Cabrera, and South Korean Ambassador to the U.S. Cho Hyun-dong.
Hyundai executives present included Chairman Chung, President and CEO Jose Muñoz, and Kia CEO Song Ho-sung.
500 attendees were present at the inauguration ceremony.
source_title: Hyundai opens first EV-only US plant in Georgia, braces for trade shifts

facts: Hyundai announced it will supply fuel cell trucks to its Georgia EV plant in cooperation with its subsidiary Glovis America.

Glovis America provides logistics operations for the operation and manages project cargoes and project freight logistics involving oversized, heavy-lift or dangerous goods in various amounts and types.
When in operation, new Xcient hydrogen fuel-cell Class 8 heavy-duty trucks will transport vehicle parts from HMGMA suppliers across the region to the factory in Georgia.
The 21 Xcient trucks will represent more than one-third of the Glovis America truck fleet at the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
When introduced to the US market, the Xcient fuel cell truck was revealed to run on two 90 kW hydrogen fuel cell systems (totaling 180 kW power) and a 350 kW electric motor.
The Xcient fuel cell truck allows for over 450 miles of range.
A mobile hydrogen refuelling station will be set up at the Megasite in Georgia to fuel the trucks.
A more permanent hydrogen production and refueling station is planned for the Megasite, though it is being developed and will be announced at a later date.
Oscar Kwon serves as the CEO of HMGMA.
Oscar Kwon stated that HMGMA is committed to creating sustainable transportation solutions.

Oscar Kwon noted that the Hyundai Xcient hydrogen fuel-cell trucks supporting the Metaplant release zero emissions and offer a cleaner alternative to traditional freight logistics methods used at other manufacturing facilities.
source_title: Hyundai to supply Georgia EV plant via fuel cell trucks - electrive.com

facts: Hyundai Motor Group plans to buy SoftBank's remaining 9.65% stake in Boston Dynamics for approximately $325 million.
The acquisition would make Boston Dynamics a fully owned Hyundai subsidiary after the expected June 22, 2026, board approval.
Hyundai already controls more than 90% of Boston Dynamics through Hyundai Motor, Kia, Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai Glovis, and Executive Chair Euisun Chung.
The deal strengthens Hyundai Motor Group's long-term strategy in robotics, AI, factory automation, and future mobility technologies.
A Hyundai board meeting is expected on June 22, 2026, to approve the acquisition.
The transaction is linked to a put option that was agreed when Hyundai first acquired a controlling stake in Boston Dynamics in 2021.
Full ownership gives Hyundai complete control over product development, investment decisions, intellectual property, and future commercialization without needing minority shareholder approval.
Hyundai acquired an 80% controlling stake in Boston Dynamics in 2021 in a deal that valued the robotics company at around $1.1 billion.
Earlier in 2026, Hyundai announced plans to deploy Boston Dynamics’ humanoid Atlas robots at its electric vehicle manufacturing facility in Georgia.
The company has also increased investments in AI infrastructure and robot manufacturing.
Complete ownership could allow Hyundai Motor Group to integrate robotics across manufacturing, logistics, warehouse automation, and future mobility products much faster.
Boston Dynamics is known globally for the Spot quadruped robot and Atlas humanoid robot, technologies that are expected to support industrial automation over the next decade.
Hyundai has been expanding beyond traditional vehicle manufacturing by investing billions of dollars into AI, robotics, and smart factories during 2026.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Eyes Full Ownership of Boston Dynamics in $325M SoftBank Stake Buyout | Meyka

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution plan to invest 5.7 trillion won ($4.3 billion) to build an electric vehicle (EV) battery plant in the United States.
The investment is part of a push to “drive the EV transition in America” and take advantage of tax credits provided by the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
The two companies signed a memorandum of understanding to produce EV batteries in the United States and “further accelerate Hyundai’s electrification efforts in North America.”
The memorandum of understanding was signed on May 26, according to a statement.
Hyundai and LGES will each hold a 50 percent stake in the joint venture.
The new plant will be located in Bryan County, Savannah, Georgia.
The facility site is where Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is currently being built.
The plant will have an annual capacity of 30 GWh.
The 30 GWh capacity is enough to power 300,000 electric vehicles.
Construction of the factory will begin in the second half of 2023.
Battery production at the facility will start at the end of 2025 at the earliest.
Jaehoon Chang, CEO of Hyundai Motor Co., stated they will create a strong foundation to lead the global EV transition through establishing a new EV battery cell plant with LG Energy Solution.
Youngsoo Kwon, CEO of LG Energy Solution, said the two auto and battery leaders are “ready to drive the EV transition in America.”
The joint venture marks Hyundai’s second battery partnership with LG.
Hyundai previously announced the construction of a $1.1 billion battery cell plant located in Karawang New Industry City, near Jakarta, Indonesia, in 2021.
Automakers and battery companies are swiftly ramping up EV battery production in North America driven by President Joe Biden’s IRA.
The IRA provides $369 billion in renewable energy incentives.
The incentives include a $7,500 federal EV tax credit for cars assembled in North America.
The incentives are part of President Joe Biden’s efforts to reduce reliance on China.

To qualify for the tax credits, a certain percentage of the battery’s critical materials and components must be sourced domestically or from countries with which the United States has a free trade agreement.
Sen. Jon Ossoff (D-Ga.) recently led a trade mission to South Korea to discuss the deal with the two companies.
Ossoff welcomed the announcement in a statement on Thursday.
Ossoff stated that Georgia now ranks second nationwide in new investment from IRA manufacturing incentives.
Securing the LG-Hyundai battery investment to Georgia was a key goal of Ossoff's trade delegation to South Korea last month.
Ossoff noted that the IRA’s manufacturing incentives continue to bring jobs and investment to Georgia.
Ossoff stated his goal remains to make Georgia the world leader in advanced energy production.

Ossoff thanked LG Chairman Koo Kwang-mo, LG Energy Solutions President David Kim, Hyundai Chairman Chung Eui-sun, Senator Reverend Warnock, and all local and state officials for making the announcement possible.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group, LG Energy Jointly Investing $4.3 Billion in EV Battery Plant in Georgia | The Epoch Times

entity_name: LG Energy Solution-Hyundai Joint Battery Cell Plant
canonical_name: LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group Joint Battery Cell Plant
facts: LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group struck a joint venture deal to build a $4.3 billion battery cell plant, the companies announced on Friday.
The factory will be located in Savannah, Georgia.
Production is slated to begin at the end of 2025.
The facility will have enough capacity to support the production of 300,000 EVs a year.
The two South Korea-based companies will each take a 50% stake in the venture and facility.
Hyundai will assemble battery packs using cells from the plant to be sent to its U.S. manufacturing sites.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution have worked together in the EV battery sector since 2009, when Hyundai introduced its Elantra Hybrid EV.
LG Energy Solution provided batteries for the Elantra along with Hyundai’s Kona Electric and IONIQ 6 EVs.
In September 2021, the companies broke ground on a battery cell manufacturing plant in Karawang, Indonesia.
The $1.1 billion Indonesian plant is set to start production in the first half of 2024.
The companies stated that the new memorandum of agreement will strengthen Hyundai’s and LG Energy Solution’s ongoing EV battery partnership.
LG Energy Solution CEO Youngsoo Kwon said, “Two strong leaders in the auto and battery industries have joined hands, and together we are ready to drive the EV transition in America.”
Kwon added that LG Energy Solution will advance product competitiveness and global operational expertise to offer sustainable energy solutions to customers.
Hyundai’s upcoming Georgia EV campus has been attracting other South Korea-based auto suppliers to the state.
Georgia announced its eighth Hyundai supplier to set up shop near its EV campus on Tuesday.
source_title: LG Energy Solution, Hyundai to build $4.3B joint battery cell plant in Georgia | Construction Dive

entity_name: LG Energy Solution Vertech and Qcells
canonical_name: LG Energy Solution Vertech and Qcells
facts: LG Energy Solution Vertech and Qcells announced a multi-year commitment to install reliable, affordable, American-made storage products for Qcells’ development projects across the country.
The agreement includes 5GWh of lithium-ion energy storage projects for utility-scale sites, along with lifecycle services from LG Energy Solution Vertech.
The joint projects are scheduled for installation between 2028 and 2030.
All projects under the agreement will meet U.S. domestic content requirements.
In May 2024, Qcells previously entered into battery purchase agreements with LG Energy Solution Vertech totaling 4.8 GWh of BESS projects developed across the U.S.
