The partnership secures energy storage systems meeting domestic content requirements because LG Energy Solution’s battery energy storage products are manufactured in Michigan and Qcells’ solar panels are made in Georgia.
Leveraging made-in-America products supports U.S. manufacturing jobs, strengthens the nation’s clean-energy supply chains, enhances grid reliability, and reduces exposure to trade risks and tariffs.
Chris Hodrick serves as the CEO of Qcells EPC.
Qcells operates the largest solar panel manufacturing facility of its kind in the United States, located in Dalton, Georgia.
The company is constructing a second factory in Cartersville, Georgia, dedicated to manufacturing ingots, wafers, cells, and finished panels.
The Cartersville facility represents the largest investment in U.S. solar history aimed at creating an integrated, sustainable domestic supply chain.
Once fully operational, Qcells’ annual solar panel production capacity in the U.S. will reach 8.4 gigawatts.
This production capacity equates to nearly 46,000 panels manufactured every day.
The daily output is sufficient to power more than 1.3 million American homes each year.
Jaehong Park serves as the CEO and President at LG Energy Solution Vertech.
The energy storage systems in this commitment utilize LG Energy Solution’s containerized lithium-ion battery solutions with LFP cells manufactured at U.S. facilities.
LG Energy Solution is currently manufacturing up to 16.5 GWh of battery energy storage cells in Holland, Michigan.
LG Energy Solution’s production across sites in North America is set to ramp up to approximately 50GWh by the end of 2026.
source_title: Press Release | Media | LG

facts: Aurubis AG launched a groundbreaking metal recycling facility in Richmond, Georgia.
The project represents an $800 million investment.
It marks Europe's largest copper producer's first major US copper investment on American soil.
The plant is designed to process up to 180,000 metric tons of complex recycling materials annually.
Production at the facility began in September 2025.
Full operational capacity is expected by the first half of 2026.
When fully operational, the plant will produce 70,000 tons of high-grade blister copper with 98-99% purity annually.
The facility recovers valuable nickel, tin, and precious metals (including gold, silver, and platinum group metals) from waste streams.
Input materials include electronic waste, copper cables, metal-bearing products, and printed circuit boards from consumer and industrial sources.
Processing technologies employed include thermal processing, mechanical processing, advanced sorting, metallurgical recovery, and highly automated systems.
The facility's 70,000-ton annual production capacity will offset approximately 7-8% of current US copper imports when operating at full capacity.
Domestic copper demand in the United States reaches approximately 1.8 million tons annually, with roughly 50% currently imported.
Aurubis has explicitly stated intentions to prioritize US customers for the copper produced at Richmond.
The facility will offer direct sales to US customers at competitive market rates and integrate into Aurubis' international smelting network for specialized applications.
Copper recycling at the facility typically requires up to 85% less energy than primary production from mined ore.
The plant creates high-skill technical positions in metallurgical processing.
The plant supports logistics and administrative roles.
The plant generates indirect employment through supplier relationships.
Aurubis CEO Toralf Haag stated that the company sees significant potential for additional projects in the United States due to high availability of recycling material and good local conditions.

The facility supports strategic industries including defense and military applications, renewable energy infrastructure, electric vehicle manufacturing, and critical telecommunications and grid infrastructure.
Industry experts anticipate the Richmond facility may represent just the first phase in a broader North American strategy for Aurubis.
The plant's environmental performance will significantly exceed regulatory requirements while establishing new standards for the industry.
source_title: Aurubis US Copper Recycling: Transforming Domestic Supply

facts: Aurubis AG of Hamburg, Germany, and the SMS Group of Dusseldorf, Germany, have signed a contract for the construction of Aurubis Richmond, a multimetal recycling plant in Georgia.
Aurubis announced its plans to build the plant Nov. 10, noting that construction would start in mid-2022 in Augusta, Georgia, in Richmond County.
Upon commissioning, which is scheduled for the first half of 2024, the plant will process about 90,000 metric tons of complex recyclables annually.
The two companies have agreed to work together on the greenfield project, with the SMS group planning the facility concept, delivering it and implementing it on-site.

The SMS Group is delivering the technology for the top-blown rotary converter, or TBRC, which Aurubis describes as a state-of-the-art piece of equipment for processing complex recyclables to recover copper, nickel, tin, zinc, precious metals and platinum group metals.
The scope of delivery also includes the sampling and off-gas cleaning facilities.
The construction of Aurubis Richmond is the starting point of a longer term collaboration between Aurubis and the SMS Group.

Aurubis and the SMS Group signed a cooperation agreement to quickly implement the planning, construction and development of additional modular recycling facilities in Europe and North America in the case of a positive investment decision from Aurubis.

Hans Rosenstock, managing director of Aurubis Richmond, says the investment reinforces their ambitions to continue expanding the recycling of complex, valuable metal-bearing materials and returning them to the material cycle.

Hans Rosenstock states that the SMS Group is an extremely competent partner for the setup of the site, supporting custom-fit solutions for ecologically sustainable business activity and accelerated decarbonization.
Michael Rzepczyk, a member of the management team at the SMS Group, says they are pleased to bring the state-of-the-art recycling plant in Richmond to life together with Aurubis.

Michael Rzepczyk notes that both partners are united by a strategic orientation toward continued growth in the circular economy and the recycling business, as well as contributing to climate protection with new technologies.

Michael Rzepczyk states that their intelligent and efficient digital concepts enable them to optimize production processes, and that this modular facility sets new international benchmarks in recycling electronic scrap and is planned for additional sites.
Aurubis says the construction of Aurubis Richmond taps a market with strong growth potential and expands its international integrated smelter network.
The investment of about 300 million euros, or $33.8 million, will help Aurubis meet its sustainability targets.

Aurubis Richmond is expected to generate an annual contribution to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of about 80 million euros, or $90.2 million, at full production capacity starting in fiscal year 2025/26.
The site will process circuit boards, copper cable and other materials containing metals will into 35,000 metric tons of blister copper annually, the company says.
Aurubis will further process the intermediate products into various industrial and precious metals at its European smelter sites and also will sell them directly in the U.S.
source_title: Aurubis taps SMS to construct Georgia recycling plant - Recycling Today

facts: Aurubis plans to capitalize on megatrends such as electrification, artificial intelligence, and the modernisation of energy infrastructure.
The group unveiled a revised corporate strategy entitled "Performance 2030: Forging resilience. Leading in multi-metal" at its annual capital market day held in London on Wednesday.
Aurubis aims to strengthen its position as a leading recycler and supplier of copper and other metals with greater emphasis on operational excellence, profitability, and sustainable value creation.

CEO Toralf Haag stated the company is focusing on expanding its strong market presence while continuing to set industry standards in processing complex raw materials, metal recovery, technological leadership, sustainability, and operational excellence.
After a phase of investment-driven expansion, Aurubis is shifting its focus to generating targeted returns from new assets and strengthening cash flow.
Aurubis is one of only a handful of companies able to recover 20 different metals from a wide range of primary and secondary raw materials.
The company's integrated smelter network in Europe and the US provides scale, flexibility, and operational resilience.
Aurubis intends to further optimise its multi-metal production by improving material flows, alleviating bottlenecks, and maximising metal yields.
Particular emphasis is being placed on expanding recycling operations in the US.
The company recently commissioned the "Aurubis Richmond" site in Georgia.
CEO Toralf Haag described the Richmond site as "the first greenfield smelter in the US in more than a century".
The Georgia plant represents the group's largest investment outside Europe at around €740m.
The facility is designed to process about 180,000 tonnes of complex recycling materials such as printed circuit boards, copper cables, and other metal-bearing products.
Aurubis expects its US subsidiary to contribute around €170m to annual Ebitda from the 2027/28 financial year.

Toralf Haag noted that their timely entry into the US market, unique capabilities, and a supportive funding environment form a strong foundation for further growth in one of the most attractive metal markets in the world.
Aurubis sees little potential for growth in battery recycling at the moment and announced it will not make any further investments in this segment for the time being.

The company stated that while technical feasibility of recovering key valuable materials from black mass has been demonstrated, the European black mass market is still in an early stage of development.
Aurubis intends to pursue an "asset-light" approach in cooperation with partners to minimise risk in this slow-growing market.
Completed and ongoing investment projects are expected to help raise the group's return on capital employed (ROCE) in the operating business to 15 per cent in the longer term.
For the current 2025/26 financial year, which began on 1 October, management is targeting an operating ROCE of 7-9 per cent.
Aurubis is forecasting operating Ebitda of €580-680m and operating earnings before tax (EBT) of €300-400m for the current financial year.
The company announced a change in its dividend policy, with the payout ratio to rise to up to 30 per cent of operating post-tax group earnings from the 2025/26 financial year on.
Salzgitter remains Aurubis's largest shareholder with a holding of nearly 30 per cent, while drugstore chain founder Dirk Rossmann held around 11 per cent as of 6 October.
source_title: Aurubis presents corporate strategy: focus on efficiency, stronger returns and US growth

facts: German company Aurubis has brought its multi-metal recycling facility in Georgia online after two years of work.
The $800 million facility, named Aurubis Richmond, is located in Augusta.
It is the first-ever secondary smelter of its kind for multi metal recycling in the United States, according to a press release.
The plant will have a processing capacity of more than 180,000 tons of complex materials annually, including circuit boards, copper cables and other metal-containing products.
The plant will produce blister copper, which will later be processed into industrial and precious metals at the company’s European smelter sites.
Some of the final product will be sold back into the U.S. market.
Aurubis CEO Toralf Haag said the smelter will allow them to keep strategically important metals in the economy, making U.S. supply chains more independent.

Haag added that the new site shows how Aurubis combines profitable growth and sustainable business activity, following their ambition to responsibly transform raw materials into value for an innovative and sustainable world.
The original plan called for a smaller capacity, but in 2023 Aurubis decided to double the plant’s metals recovery capacity by adding a second recycling module.
This first stage of the plant was scheduled to come online in the first half of 2024.
The second stage is slated for 2026.
David Schultheis, president and managing director of Aurubis Richmond, said the facility is going to become a major player in the North American multimetal supply chain.
source_title: Aurubis starts up Georgia smelter - Resource Recycling

facts: Duckyang is an electric mobility parts supplier of automotive battery modules and energy storage systems to SK Battery America (SKBA).
Duckyang will invest $10 million in opening its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Jackson County.
The new facility will create 285 jobs in Braselton, Georgia.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the investment and stated that Duckyang is creating high-paying, advanced automotive manufacturing jobs for Georgians in Jackson County.
Governor Kemp noted that Georgia has the best workforce training programs in the nation and has been the number one state for business for eight years.
Duckyang is headquartered in Ulsan, Korea.
Duckyang supplies cockpit modules to global customers including Hyundai, Kia, Renault Samsung Motors, Genesis, and Mobis.
Cockpit modules supplied by Duckyang are integrated auto parts comprised of more than 130 subcomponents located near the driver’s seat.
Opening the Georgia facility will allow Duckyang to better serve its regional automotive customers.

Dong-in Son, CEO of Duckyang Ind. Co. Ltd., stated that entering the U.S. electric vehicle battery market with SKBA will allow cooperation with other automotive companies like Hyundai and Kia Motors that have U.S. manufacturing bases.

Dong-in Son expected business expansion opportunities to supply electric vehicle parts and interior parts, including cockpit module, door trim panel module, and other flagship parts to global automakers based in the United States.
Duckyang’s new facility will be 230,000 square feet and located at 984 Broadway Avenue in Braselton.
The company will hire for positions in production, maintenance, logistics, and warehouse management.
Individuals interested in careers with Duckyang are encouraged to contact the company for additional information.
Town Manager and Clerk of Braselton Jennifer Scott stated that the town looks forward to welcoming Duckyang and is delighted to see a new manufacturing facility in their town.

John Scott, Director of Economic Development for the Jackson County Area Chamber of Commerce, stated that companies like Duckyang are helping Jackson County and Georgia secure a place in the automotive industry's future as it moves toward electric vehicles.
Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce division on the project.
The project was facilitated in partnership with the Jackson County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia is focused on growing the entire electric mobility supply chain.
Commissioner Wilson noted that strong relationships with SK Battery America and other Korean companies like Duckyang are helping secure local supply of needed parts for companies in the state.
The State of Georgia facilitated more than $1.3 billion in investment and more than 2,700 jobs from South Korean companies during 2020 alone.
Georgia remains a national leader in advanced manufacturing and continues to attract business globally.
source_title: Korean Automotive Manufacturer Duckyang to Open Company's First U.S. Facility in Jackson County - Middle Georgia CEO

facts: Korean supplier Duckyang Industrial is set to invest $10 million to open a manufacturing facility in Braselton, Georgia.
The Braselton facility will be the company's first such facility in the U.S.
source_title: Supplier Duckyang to open $10 million plant in Georgia - Rubber News

facts: A Georgia Republican who endorsed Donald Trump is concerned about the potential repeal of the Inflation Reduction Act, which has brought billions of dollars of clean energy investment to Georgia.

Last Tuesday, 18 House Republicans sent a letter to House Speaker Mike Johnson emphasizing the importance of maintaining energy tax credits in the Inflation Reduction Act if Trump were elected in November.
Donald Trump has stated he would repeal the Inflation Reduction Act on his first day in office.
Donald Trump has called electric vehicle mandates the "death of the U.S. auto industry."
The Inflation Reduction Act has put more than $352 billion into clean energy across 47 states.
Georgia has received $24 billion in clean energy investment according to Climate Power, a climate communication organization.
Georgia has created 30,661 jobs from clean energy investment according to Climate Power.
Climate Power states that Georgia leads the United States in clean energy job creation.
Rep. Buddy Carter of Georgia's 1st District, which encompasses Georgia's coast and inland to Waycross, was one of 17 co-signers of the letter.
Georgia's 1st Congressional District has received $7.6 billion, which is more than a third of the state's clean energy manufacturing investment.
Rep. Buddy Carter stated that companies must find a balance between rolling back the worst portions of the Inflation Reduction Act and ensuring stability for continued U.S. investment.
The Hyundai EV Metaplant and LG Metafactory projects in Southeast Georgia are worth $7.5 billion and bring 8,500 jobs.
The Seoyon E-HWA EV Parts project invested $76 million and brought 740 jobs.
These current and ongoing project numbers are according to a new clean energy map by Thirdway, a nonprofit think tank dedicated to climate policy.
The letter warns that a full repeal of the Inflation Reduction Act would create a worst-case scenario where billions in taxpayer dollars are spent with next to nothing in return.
The letter states that energy tax credits have spurred innovation, incentivized investment, and created good jobs across many parts of the country.
The letter was spearheaded by Rep. Andrew Garbarino of New York, who is the co-chair of the bipartisan House Climate Solutions Caucus.
Hyundai chose Georgia for its access to ports and rails in Savannah.
Hyundai specifically broke ground in Georgia because of the tax credits laid out in the Inflation Reduction Act, according to an Associated Press report.
Rep. Buddy Carter stated that Hyundai decided to come to his district before the passage of the Inflation Reduction Act.

Rep. Buddy Carter expressed excitement over Hyundai as a strong partner in Georgia's First Congressional District, noting the decision predates the act and emphasizing pushing pro-growth policies to beat China.
Georgia has seen tax benefits from the act beyond business investment according to government studies.
The IRS recently published new data highlighting tax credit benefits at the household level.

Climate Power's analysis states that 87,000 Georgia families benefited from more than $148 million in tax credits to lower costs of clean energy and energy efficiency upgrades to their homes during 2023.
Home improvements covered by the credits include heat pumps, insulation, windows, and efficient air conditioning.
source_title: Georgia GOP rep. wants to save Biden’s energy tax credits. Trump says he’ll repeal them - AOL

facts: The Hyundai Metaplant is a $7.56 billion electric vehicle factory located in rural Bryan County.
More than 1,471 workers have been hired at the Hyundai Metaplant.
The Korean manufacturer is pushing to get the first car off the assembly line by December.
Georgia and southeast coastal counties are subsidizing Hyundai and its suppliers with $2.1 billion in tax breaks, construction costs, discounted land, and other perks.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the subsidy deal would produce good-paying jobs for Georgians.
Hyundai Metaplant, the LG joint-venture battery facility, and five affiliated Hyundai suppliers must collectively invest $5.545 billion.
The entities must hire at least 8,500 workers by 2031.
The hired workers must have an average annual salary of $58,105.
The created jobs must be retained until 2048.
Only two of the eight publicly available economic agreements for companies at or near the Metaplant site promise average salaries higher than $58,000.
The two companies promising salaries over $58,000 are Hyundai Metaplant and Hyundai Mobis.
Six other companies bound by the state development benefits have average annual salaries lower than the suggested retail price of the electric SUVs their workers build.
Two seat-building workers at the Metaplant earn $20 per hour.
The Hyundai subsidiary aims to produce 300,000 electric vehicles per year.
The annualized pay for these workers equals $41,600.
The 2024 Hyundai IONIQ 5 electric SUV, which will be built at the plant, has a suggested retail price of $41,800.
The $58,105 figure represents an average annual salary plus benefits for all workers on site.
The $58,105 average pay scale is above the average worker salaries in Coastal Georgia counties.
2022 U.S. Census Bureau data reported average worker salaries in Coastal Georgia counties ranging from $30,686 in Bulloch County to $46,305 in Effingham County.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) pays production workers a base salary of $22.40 per hour, or about $46,592 annually.
HMGMA maintenance positions start at $30.70 per hour.
HMGMA implemented a new wage structure for hourly employees in January 2024.
The new structure is designed to help workers reach an annual salary of $62,524 within four years.
Six other companies in the Metaplant economic agreement set their pay independently of HMGMA.
HMGMA will not collect salary data from on-site affiliates until March 2025.
The first annual report to the state regarding salaries is due in March 2025.
Bianca Johnson is an HMGMA spokesperson.
HMGMA does not coordinate with its affiliates on pay and has no plans to do so.
Of the 1,471 new workers reported in HMGMA's biannual count on June 30, 859 are employed directly by HMGMA.
The remaining 616 new hires work for affiliated companies.
If the seven companies fail to create 8,500 jobs averaging $58,105 by 2031, HMGMA risks losing billions in public aid.
Failure to meet job and salary targets could force HMGMA to repay part of its incentive package.
HMGMA is confident average pay will reach or exceed $58,105 by December 31, 2031.
The performance period for the job and salary requirements ends on December 31, 2031.
More than a half-dozen other suppliers have promised to create almost 4,500 additional jobs in separate state agreements.
Hyundai-affiliated parts manufacturer Hyundai Mobis promises the highest average pay of $61,642 annually plus benefits, or nearly $30 per hour.
South Korean company Seohan Auto Georgia will pay workers an average salary of $31,304 annually plus benefits, or about $15 per hour.
Seohan Auto Georgia's Liberty County plant will build Hyundai's brake systems and drive shafts.
The seat-building team's pay temporarily increased from $20 to $22 per hour without warning.
Two weeks later, an HR representative informed the 35-person seat-building team that the $2/hour raise was an error.
The company stated the salary bump would be deducted from future paychecks.
The Hyundai Metaplant site spans 3,000 acres and remains an active construction zone.
Workers are forced to use a makeshift parking lot at the site.
Industrial nails litter the site, causing vehicle damage like punctured tires.
The company does not provide a safe parking zone or offer to pay for vehicle damages.
The company does not provide shuttle transportation for workers.
Many seat-building workers have finished repaying the mistaken raise, and their pay has returned to $20 per hour.
Seohan Auto Georgia employs 188 workers averaging $31,304 a year plus benefits ($15/hr).
PHA employs 402 workers averaging $37,440 a year plus benefits ($18/hr).
Seoyon E-Hwa Savannah employs 500 workers averaging $37,440 a year plus benefits ($18/hr).
Sewon America employs 740 workers averaging $37,440 a year plus benefits ($18/hr).
Joon Georgia employs 630 workers averaging $45,000 a year plus benefits ($21.65/hr).
Ecoplastic Corporation employs 456 workers averaging at least $45,000 a year plus benefits ($21.65/hr).
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (including affiliate suppliers and the joint-venture battery facility) employs 8,500 workers averaging $58,105 a year plus benefits ($28/hr).
Hyundai Mobis employs 1,578 workers averaging $61,642 a year plus benefits ($30/hr).
source_title: As workers train, Hyundai Metaplant salary picture takes shape - The Current

facts: The $50 million project will add 98,250 square feet to the facility in Rincon, GA.
The expansion will create 100 new jobs.
This marks the first expansion by a Hyundai supplier since the launch of Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in late 2024.
Sewon America is a subsidiary of Korean-based Sewon Precision Industry Co.

Misti Martin, Deputy Commissioner of Global Commerce at the Georgia Department of Economic Development, called the expansion a "full-circle moment and incredible milestone for the Metaplant’s supplier network."
Matt Saxon, Chairman of the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority (ECIDA), stated that Sewon America chose Effingham County for its second U.S. facility.
Rincon Mayor Kevin Exley expressed excitement about more quality jobs in the community and looked forward to partnering with Sewon to ensure local citizens can take advantage of the positions.
Robert Vazquez, Director of Human Resources at Sewon America, said the expansion will allow the company to better serve customers and add new capabilities to operations.
Sewon America operates one of the largest automobile body parts manufacturing plants in North America.
The company opened its first Georgia facility in LaGrange in 2008, which has since doubled in size.
Sewon America began operations in Rincon in 2025.
With the expansion, Sewon will continue to supply electric vehicle (EV) body parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The project was supported by the Georgia Department of Economic Development, the City of Rincon, and the Effingham County Board of Commissioners.
source_title: Sewon America Launches $50 Million Expansion in Rincon, Bringing 100 New Jobs and Advanced Manufacturing Capabilities | Effingham County Development Authority

entity_name: Georgia Clean Energy & EV Supply Chain Investments
facts: Since the passage of the clean energy plan, Georgia has seen 41 new clean energy projects invest $23.88 billion.
These projects have created over 30,600 jobs across the state.
Many of these clean energy jobs pay a higher average salary and are unionized positions.
Companies have partnered with local schools to establish workforce training and skills development programs.
Qcells has made several investments in northwest Georgia to boost U.S. solar manufacturing.
The company said the passage of the Inflation Reduction Act was “crucial” to its success and decision to expand manufacturing in the U.S.
Since the clean energy plan passed, Qcells has added over 500 new jobs at its Dalton manufacturing facility.
Qcells announced plans to create 2,000 new jobs at a manufacturing facility in Cartersville.
Qcells is creating 3,800 direct jobs across Bartow and Whitfield counties.
Qcells is supporting nearly 3,000 additional indirect jobs in surrounding communities.
The two factories are projected to create over $2 billion in total sales/output in Bartow and Whitfield Counties annually.
Qcells is actively working to attract and train a local workforce.
Qcells partnered with Georgia Quick Start to provide customized job training programs.
Training sessions emphasize worker safety.
Qcells is attracting workers from the region’s once-thriving carpeting industry.
Qcells is offering opportunities for growth.
In March 2023, Hanwha Advanced Materials announced a $147 million investment to open a solar encapsulent materials manufacturing facility in Bartow County.
The facility will supply Qcell’s nearby Cartersville factory.
Production begins at the end of 2024.
The new factory will employ 160 workers.
It will be the only U.S. manufacturer of ethylene vinyl acetate, which increases solar panel durability.
In November 2022, Hyundai and SK On signed a memorandum of understanding to open an EV battery cell plant in Bartow County.
The plant aims to meet demand for battery cells that qualify for Inflation Reduction Act tax incentives.
The two companies formalized the agreement in May 2023.
They announced they would invest $5 billion in the facility.
The plant will create 3,750 jobs.
Jobs have an average annual salary of over $53,000.
This salary is higher than the current average income in Bartow County.
The plant opens in the second half of 2025.
It will produce enough batteries to support 300,000 EVs per year.
In February 2024, SolarCycle announced plans to invest $344 million in a new solar glass manufacturing facility in Cedartown.
The company plans to create more than 600 new jobs at the facility.
It will be the first in the U.S. to use recycled materials from retired solar panels to make new solar glass.

The Chair of Polk County’s Development Authority celebrated the investment, saying it would have a positive economic impact by providing quality jobs and strengthening opportunities in housing and retail development in the area.
SolarCycle chose to invest in Northwest Georgia because of its proximity to major domestic solar panel manufacturers.
SolarCycle chose the location for access to key shipping infrastructure.
The company announced its Cedartown facility after receiving a contract from Qcells to recycle decommissioned panels.
In partnership with Georgia Northwestern Technical College, the State of Georgia, and other community organizations, SolarCycle will offer 60 apprenticeship positions.
Apprenticeships ensure training is available for students of all economic backgrounds.
The City of West Point was on a downward economic slide in the 2000s, driven by losses in the textile industry before Kia announced it would open an auto manufacturing plant in the city in 2006.
In July 2023, Kia announced a $200 million investment in its West Point manufacturing facility.
The investment will create 200 jobs.
The facility includes the Kia Georgia Training Center.
The training center includes robotics, welding and electronics labs, classrooms, and equipment for training on state-of-the-art programmable logic controllers.
In May 2023, Anovion announced its plans for its first synthetic graphite anode materials manufacturing facility in Bainbridge.
The facility represents an $800 million investment.
It will create over 400 high-paying quality jobs.
Anovion’s average wage is $63,000 a year.
Anovion will provide scholarships for students in the area to attend Southern Regional Technical College.
Bainbridge’s Executive Director of the Developmental Authority, Rick McCaskill, said it is difficult to keep young people in the city after they graduate.
New high-paying clean energy jobs and scholarships are hoped to be an incentive to have residents develop their careers in the community.
In January 2024, Imola Automotive USA announced it would open an EV manufacturing facility in Fort Valley.
When operational, the plant would employ 7,500 workers.
The plant will provide a technology training program for EV workers.
The majority of the jobs created would pay $46 per hour.
Jobs only require a G.E.D or high school diploma.
There are opportunities for training and career advancement.
These jobs would raise the median income in Fort Valley.
Imola Automotive Group signed a neutrality agreement with the UAW.
The company said allowing workers to unionize was “in line” with its priorities.
In April 2024, Syensqo broke ground on a battery-grade polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) manufacturing facility near Augusta.
PVDF is a critical battery component that helps EVs go further.
The plant was partially supported by a $178.2 million Bipartisan Infrastructure Law-funded Department of Energy grant to support the U.S. battery supply chain.
Syensqo created 100 highly skilled jobs.
Syensqo supported 500 local construction jobs.
Syensqo announced it would invest $1 million to support workforce development and training programs for local economically disadvantaged, underrepresented, and rural communities.
As part of that investment, Syensqo will make contributions to local higher education institutions, including Augusta Technical College.
In February 2023, Seoyon E-HWA announced a new EV components manufacturing facility in Savannah.
The company broke ground on the $76 million facility in April 2023.
Production is scheduled for October 2024.
The new facility will supply parts for the nearby Hyundai Metaplant.
It will create 740 jobs, including 500 permanent roles.
In September 2023, Seoyon announced a partnership with Savannah Technical College to provide job training programs.
Seoyon E-Hwa President Chung Yun Kim said it was a great opportunity for the company to “hire very skilled employees from the esteemed Savannah Technical College.”
As of March 2024, Seoyon is actively hiring.
Seoyon participated in a job fair for Hyundai suppliers.
In February 2023, Sewon America announced a $300 million investment to open an EV body part manufacturing facility in Rincon.
This project is the largest known private investment in the City of Rincon.
The facility will create 740 new jobs.
Sewon America is collaborating with Savannah Technical College on a workforce training program.
The training program graduated its first students in January 2024.
Sewon America contributed to local educational and infrastructure funds in the county.
As of March 2024, Sewon is already actively hiring in the community.
Sewon participated in a job fair for Hyundai suppliers in Georgia.
source_title: Clean Energy Investments Are Revitalizing Georgia Communities | Climate Power

facts: ARCO Design/Build has completed a 100,000-square-foot expansion for Sewon America in Rincon, Effingham County.
The project is valued at $50 million.
The expansion is expected to bring approximately 100 new jobs to the area.
The project supports the production of automotive components tied to electric vehicles.
Sewon America is a subsidiary of South Korea-based Sewon Precision Industry.
Sewon America manufactures automotive body components and supplies parts for major manufacturers, including Hyundai Motor Group’s nearby Metaplant.
The facility was completed on an accelerated timeline, allowing operations and equipment to begin moving in ahead of full completion.
Local leaders say the project highlights rapid growth along the Savannah industrial corridor.

Brandt Herndon with the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority stated that the project strengthens the region's position in the electric vehicle supply chain while bringing quality jobs and long-term opportunity to the community.
The expansion reflects increasing demand across Georgia for manufacturing facilities that can be built quickly and efficiently to support expanding electric vehicle production.
source_title: New $50M plant expansion brings 100 jobs, boosts EV industry in Effingham County

facts: Hanon Systems shares the global market for thermal management components for vehicles with Japan's Denso.
The company recorded KRW 2.4625 trillion in sales and KRW 53.2 billion in operating profit in the fourth quarter of last year (consolidated).
Fourth-quarter sales rose 5% from the same period last year, while operating profit fell 48.9%.
Based on annual performance, Hanon System's sales last year rose 10.8% from the previous year to 9.5593 trillion won, and its operating profit rose 8.1% to 277.2 billion won.
The company is investing $40 million (about 53 billion won) in Georgia to build a new factory.
The company is investing $170 million (about 227 billion won) in Tennessee to build a new factory.
Hanon Systems invested 443 billion won in R&D last year, up 14% from the previous year.
Over the past five years, the amount of money spent on R&D has reached 1.87 trillion won.
Last year, the depreciation cost of Hanon System totaled 598 billion won, which is 2.2 times the annual operating profit.
Hanon Systems acquired Magna International's hydraulic control business unit for $1.23 billion (about 1.64 trillion won) in 2019.
Depreciation costs from the Magna acquisition alone amount to about 30 billion won per year.
EV investment is expected to begin to pay off in earnest in the second half of this year.
Hyundai Motor Group will begin mass production of electric vehicles at a plant dedicated to electric vehicles (HMGMA) in the U.S. in the second half of this year.
Sales of thermal management parts will begin at Hanon System's new plant in Georgia when Hyundai begins production.
Hyundai Motor Group will introduce electric vehicles using next-generation platforms starting next year, and Hanon System will supply parts for next-generation thermal management systems.
Mercedes-Benz and BMW are expanding their heat pumps to next-generation electric vehicle models.
The heat pump technology recycles heat generated from a battery for indoor heating and minimizes the degradation of battery performance in winter when installed in an electric vehicle.
As of the end of last year, the order balance of Hanon System totaled $51 billion (about 68 trillion won), of which about 70% were electric vehicle parts.
Currently, electric vehicle parts account for 26% of the total sales of Hanon System.
Sales of electric vehicle parts increased 17% in a year to 2.48 trillion won last year.
Hanon Systems supplied 3 million electric air compressors (e-compressors) to automakers last year.
The e-compressor maintains the battery pack at a safe operating temperature by circulating refrigerant in electric vehicles.
Rolls-Royce electric vehicle 'Spector' uses an e-compressor from Hanon System.
Hanon Systems presented KRW 10 trillion in sales and KRW 400 billion in operating profit as its earnings forecast for this year.
Compared to last year's performance, the projected sales and operating profit increased by 4.6 percent and 44.3 percent respectively.
Hanon System presented 11 trillion won in sales and 600 billion won in operating profit as next year's forecast.
The company expressed confidence that it can increase its operating profit ratio by 40-50% for the second consecutive year.
source_title: Hanon Systems, which shares the global market for thermal management components for vehicles with Ja.. - MK

facts: A job fair is set for 10 a.m.-4 p.m. on Thursday, April 4.

Hyundai Metaplant and area suppliers are holding the job fair.
3,500 jobs are available at the event.

facts: Joon Georgia Inc. Dba Ajin Georgia is a leading automotive parts manufacturer and Hyundai supplier.
The company operates a state-of-the-art facility in Statesboro, Georgia.
Joon Georgia is ramping up production and workforce hiring to meet growing demand for automotive and EV components.
The company completed and launched an 850,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Statesboro, Georgia.
The new Statesboro facility represents a $317 million investment.
The facility is expected to employ up to 630 workers.
Joon Georgia manufactures stamped metal assemblies and automotive electronic components.
The company primarily supplies Hyundai Motor Group and other automotive OEMs.
As the first confirmed supplier for Hyundai’s Metaplant America, Joon Georgia is positioned at the forefront of the region’s automotive and EV manufacturing expansion.
The company leverages advanced manufacturing technologies to deliver high-quality automotive components.
Joon Georgia was founded in 2024.
The company is pursuing strategic efforts to expand its market presence and reach new customer segments.

facts: Georgia’s EV industry is currently hiring, with high demand and wide-open career possibilities across multiple levels and sectors.
The job postings page currently displays 0 results for available positions.

facts: Auto parts maker Seohan Auto Georgia plans to invest $72 million in a new manufacturing plant in Midway, Georgia.
The South Korea-based auto parts manufacturer will produce H/shafts, axles and brake systems for OEMs.
The company will supply parts for Hyundai’s nearby EV and battery manufacturing hub.
Seohan Auto Georgia’s upcoming facility is expected to create 180 jobs.
Production at the new facility is slated to start in late 2024.
Seohan Auto Georgia is the seventh Hyundai supplier to flock to the Peach State since the automaker broke ground on its upcoming $5.5 billion EV campus in October.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp called Hyundai’s plant the “largest economic development project in state history.”
Gov. Kemp estimated the site would bring at least 8,100 jobs to Georgia’s coastal region.
Georgia officials had predicted Hyundai’s Metaplant would attract offsite suppliers to invest an estimated $1 billion.
Hyundai’s suppliers have outpaced that estimate, with plans to invest nearly $2 billion in the state, according to the April 11 announcement.

Gov. Kemp stated in a statement earlier this month that “The Hyundai Metaplant is and will continue to be an economic driver for the entire region, creating more jobs for hardworking Georgians in communities like Liberty County.”
In November, Joon Georgia became the first supplier to announce plans for the state, with a $317 million production facility in Statesboro.
Later that month, Hyundai Mobis became the second Hyundai supplier, with its plans for a $926 million EV power electric systems plant.
Since January, Ecoplastic Corporation, Seoyon E-Hwa, Sewon America and PHA have each announced plans for manufacturing plants of their own to supply Hyundai’s manufacturing hub.
source_title: Seventh auto supplier follows Hyundai to Georgia | Manufacturing Dive

facts: Korea-based Ecoplastic Corp., a supplier of plastic parts like exterior bumpers and interior consoles, trims and molds, is set to build a $205 million plant in Bulloch County to supply the Hyundai Motors dedicated electric vehicle plant set to be built nearby.

Founded in 1984 and located in the North Gyeongsang Province, Ecoplastic is a tier-one supplier for both Hyundai and Kia Motors, sending parts directly into the final assembly plants of both Korean auto makers, which have plants in Alabama and Georgia, respectively.
The new facility near Statesboro will include injection molding and painting operations.
The facility will create 456 new jobs when it opens in October 2024.
The opening occurs two years after the groundbreaking for Hyundai's Meta Plant.

Ecoplastic makes molds for headlights and taillights for the Hyundai Sonata and Avante (the overseas version of the Elantra), as well as bumpers for the Genesis luxury brand and radiator grilles, wheel covers, tailgate garnishes and roof racks for other models.
Ecoplastic has 770 employees and recorded $700 million in revenues during 2021, according to a news release from Gov. Brian Kemp’s office.
Ecoplastic is part of the Seoul-based SECO Automotive family of companies, which also includes Seojin Industrial, SeojinCam and Komos, which supplies steering wheels to Hyundai in Alabama.
Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, stated that Ecoplastic's move to Statesboro makes it the third announced supplier for Hyundai’s new plant.
The benefit of the Hyundai plant in Bryan County is rippling far beyond its 2,900-acre site where the auto maker is spending $5.5 billion.
Bulloch County, along with Bryan, Effingham and Chatham counties, will receive briefings on best practices for welcoming new Korean neighbors from Jeanne Charbonneau.
Jeanne Charbonneau is a volunteer who has served for 17 years as the point of contact for newly arrived South Koreans at Hyundai’s first U.S. plant in Montgomery, Ala.
source_title: Ecoplastic to Invest $205M in Facility Near New Hyundai Plant  - Global Atlanta

facts: CEO Youngdoo “Thomas” Kim leads Ecoplastic America.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on February 5, 2024 that Ecoplastic Corporation will create 456 new jobs and invest approximately $205 million in a manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
Ecoplastic America is an automotive parts manufacturer dedicated to bringing its expertise to Georgia with a new plant in Bulloch County.
The company operates as a tier-1 supplier for Hyundai.
Ecoplastic is a Korean-based auto parts manufacturer investing $205 million into the Bulloch County facility.
CEO Youngdoo “Thomas” Kim says Bulloch County is making the first impression automotive enthusiasts want to see.
source_title: Community – Ecoplastic

facts: Ascend Elements has opened its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility.
The $50 million Base 1 facility is located in Covington, Georgia.
The Base 1 facility is one of the largest electric vehicle battery recycling facilities in North America.
Lithium-ion battery recycling is becoming an increasingly important part of the EV battery materials supply chain as demand for electric vehicles increases.
The facility began partial operations in August 2022.
The facility has an annual capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
The processing capacity is equal to 70,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
Ascend Elements CEO Mike O'Kronley stated that the facility is an important part of America's EV battery infrastructure and that it is just the start.
Ascend Elements CEO Mike O'Kronley emphasized the industry's need to continue building a closed-loop battery materials supply chain to make electric vehicle batteries cleaner and more sustainable.
Ascend Elements recovers up to 98 percent of the critical battery metals in used electric vehicle batteries and gigafactory manufacturing scrap.

By using its Hydro-to-Cathode process to recycle used lithium-ion batteries and scrap, Ascend Elements can reduce the carbon footprint of new electric vehicle battery cathode materials by up to 90 percent.
The facility is powered by renewable energy.
The facility features onsite wastewater recycling and treatment systems.
The facility currently employs about 100 team members.
Ascend Elements plans to increase the facility's headcount to 185 by 2024.
source_title: Ascend Elements opens EV battery recycling facility in Georgia

facts: Ascend Elements is committed to making US EV manufacturing more self-sufficient and sustainable.
Many of the materials used by the EV industry are imported from different countries.
To make US EV manufacturing more self-sufficient, it’s critical to produce more materials domestically and to recycle existing EV materials.
The EV battery manufacturing process is very energy and carbon intense.
Ascend Element’s vertically integrated business model reduces 90% of the carbon emissions associated with the production of materials that go into every EV battery.
That’s driven primarily by a patented Hydro-to-Cathode® process.
Ascend Elements begins by recycling the metals in scrap EV batteries and delivering an engineered material that goes into new EV batteries.
Ascend Elements’ existing EV battery recycling facility in Georgia will provide the engineered materials to a new site in Kentucky.
The Kentucky site will produce enough materials to feed 750,000 EV batteries a year.
The Kentucky facility will go live by the end of 2024 and enter large-scale industrial production in 2025.
To decrease business risks, Ascend Elements teamed with EY US.
As Ascend Elements created plans for its new Kentucky site, the team recognized that everything was theoretical.
The company wanted to ensure that once the site was up and running, it would operate as effectively as possible.
In response, Ascend Elements dedicated time and effort to developing a material flow strategy.
The industrial engineering team mapped out every material movement.
Although their work was very thorough, Ascend Elements wanted additional validation from outside professionals.
Together, Ascend Elements, with the assistance of EY US, engaged in a logistics simulation exercise to map all of the material movements.1
The relationship has been a logical one for EY US since the company is committed to sustainability and decarbonization of supply chains.
Developing and sustaining a profitable and growing industry for recycling and remanufacturing of batteries is a focus area for EY US.
The relationship with Ascend Elements started from a shared vision.
source_title: Insights on the US EV battery materials market | EY - US

facts: Ascend Elements achieved a significant milestone in domestic critical minerals recovery last month by producing >99% pure, recycled lithium carbonate (Li2CO3) from black mass for the first time at commercial scale.
Black mass is a dark, powdery material recovered from used lithium-ion batteries during the recycling process.

The company operated a commercial-scale (3 kiloton per year) lithium recovery line at its battery recycling facility in Covington, Georgia last month to demonstrate the commercial viability of lithium recovery and recycling.
Ascend Elements plans to produce >15,000 metric tons of recycled Li2CO3 per year in the United States and Europe by 2027.

In addition to the 3,000 metric-ton-per-year lithium recovery line in Covington, Georgia, Ascend Elements plans to operate a state-of-the-art battery materials facility and advanced lithium recovery line at a new location in Europe.
Linh Austin, President and CEO of Ascend Elements, stated that the company is at the center of three macro trends: Electrification, localization and critical minerals.

Austin noted that producing recycled lithium carbonate at commercial scale in the United States and Europe will advance energy independence, critical minerals security and electrification for the Western world.
Lithium carbonate is a highly valuable critical mineral listed on the official U.S. federal government list of critical minerals published in August 2025.
Lithium carbonate is used to manufacture lithium-ion batteries, glass and ceramics.
According to Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, 98% of U.S. lithium carbonate imports come from South America.
Europe also imports most of its lithium carbonate from South America and China.
The United States and Europe currently have limited capacity to mine and process lithium carbonate.
Austin thanked the company's team in Georgia for working tirelessly on the project.
Recovering lithium from black mass is the most efficient way to source this critical mineral, with the concentration of lithium in black mass being 10 times higher than in Chilean brine pools.

A recently published life cycle assessment found Ascend Elements’ lithium recovery process produces 86% lower carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2e) emissions and 97% lower particulate matter 2.5 (PM 2.5) emissions than spodumene mining.
The same assessment found the process produces 37% lower CO2e emissions and 81% lower PM 2.5 emissions than Chilean brine extraction.
By 2030, Ascend Elements aims to achieve 99% lower CO2e emissions and up to 99.7% lower PM 2.5 emissions through additional decarbonization efforts.
Founded in 2015, Ascend Elements is a leading provider of domestic critical minerals and engineered battery materials for the lithium-ion battery industry.
The company produces Li2CO3 and NMC pCAM made from recycled battery materials.

Ascend Elements’ patented Hydro-to-Cathode® direct cathode precursor synthesis technology produces new pCAM material from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods, resulting in improved economics and lowered carbon emissions.
source_title: Ascend Elements | Ascend Elements Produces Recycled Lithium Carbonate from Used Li-ion Batteries for First Time at Commercial Scale

facts: Battery Resourcers is changing its name to Ascend Elements.
The company is based in Westborough.
The company operates as a lithium ion battery recycling and manufacturing company.
The company's technology reclaims critical battery elements from scrap batteries and elevates them to customized, battery-active materials with higher value and performance.
The name change is part of a long-term strategy that includes total rebranding and a website redesign.
The company is seeing increased demand for its products and cathode active materials (CAM) manufacturing process as electric vehicles increase in popularity.
The company previously announced plans to construct a $43-million plant in Georgia.
The planned Georgia facility will be North America’s largest lithium-ion battery recycling base.
Roger Lin serves as Ascend Elements’ vice president of global marketing and government relations.
Roger Lin stated that the name Ascend Elements and newly launched branding capture the company's mission to elevate the value of elements in the lithium-ion battery supply chain.
source_title: Battery Resourcers rebranding to Ascend Elements

facts: Anovion Technologies is a climate tech-driven advanced materials company focused on clean energy innovation.
The company produces synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries.
Eric Stopka serves as the Chief Executive Officer of Anovion.
Anovion announced plans to build a new manufacturing facility in Georgia.
The Georgia facility will initially produce 40,000 metric tons of synthetic graphite anode materials annually.
Total operational capacity for the Georgia facility is expected to be achieved by late 2025.
The manufacturing facility in Georgia will span 1,500,000 square feet.
Anovion expects to create approximately 400 high-quality local clean energy jobs at the Georgia site.
Hiring for the Georgia facility is scheduled to begin in late 2025.
Eric Stopka cited Georgia's strong pipeline of technical workers as a key factor in the site selection.
Georgia was chosen for its existing and planned proximity to low- and carbon-free energy sources, access to rail infrastructure, highly skilled workforce, and business-friendly environment.
Anovion views Georgia as part of a green energy corridor focused on job creation, community development, and investment in people.
The company gained qualification for electric vehicle (EV) applications for its synthetic graphite products.
Commercial production of Anovion's synthetic graphite began in early 2021.
The new Georgia facility will utilize state-of-the-art processes and proprietary furnace technologies to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
The facility will leverage the decarbonizing energy mix in the Southeast to enhance sustainability.
Anovion's expansion strategy includes the Anovion Center of Excellence (ACE) in Sanborn, New York.
Anovion also operates a hydro-powered manufacturing facility near Niagara Falls.
These domestic expansions support Anovion's goal of securing critical materials for US lithium-ion battery production.
Anovion received a $117M grant from the US Department of Energy to scale production capacity and construct the Georgia factory.

Synthetic graphite produced by Anovion powers electric vehicles on the road today, as well as energy storage systems, personal e-mobility, medical devices, military and aerospace applications, and other industrial uses.
Anovion's in-house testing capabilities allow it to understand how manufacturing parameters and methods impact battery performance from raw materials to finished batteries.
Historically, much of the graphite used by the US for lithium-ion batteries has been produced overseas, a paradigm Anovion aims to change.

Growing consumer demand for EVs and other electrified applications, along with government policies like the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law and Inflation Reduction Act, are driving increased investment in a clean energy economy.

Anovion is a founding member of the Battery Materials & Technology Coalition (BMTC), which advocates for significant investments and smart policy to strengthen the sustainable domestic upstream battery supply chain in North America.
source_title: Anovion Technologies: Job Creation and U.S. Battery Leadership

facts: Anovion Technologies has announced that it will invest $800 million into a factory located in Bainbridge, Georgia.
Anovion Technologies is based in Chicago and was created last year when Pyrotek and Amsted Graphite Materials merged together.
The company operates existing factories in Sanborn, New York, and Clarksburg, West Virginia.
Anovion recently announced plans to construct a facility near Muscle Shoals, Alabama.
The Georgia factory will employ 400 workers.
The Bainbridge facility is expected to begin production on synthetic graphite anode in 2025.
According to Georgia officials, this project is one of more than 40 EV-related projects initiated in the state since 2020.
Anovion Technologies is a supplier of graphite for electric batteries.

CEO Eric Stopk stated that the company looks forward to providing solutions that will further secure the country's electrified future with the support of partners in Georgia and the federal government.
source_title: Electric Battery Graphite to Be Produced in Georgia | National Oil and Lube News

facts: Republican Georgia Governor Brian Kemp announced that Anovion Technologies would be building a new manufacturing facility in Bainbridge.
The project is projected to create more than 400 jobs.
The project involves over $800 million in investment in Decatur County.
Anovion is a supplier of premium synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries.
Anovion is a climate tech-driven advanced materials company.
Anovion is one of the first qualified U.S. suppliers of synthetic graphite anode materials for use in e-mobility applications.
Synthetic graphite anode materials are the largest battery material used in electric vehicles by mass.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia is fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that job creators recognize every corner of Georgia has the resources and workforce needed for success.
Anovion’s new facility will be 1,500,000 square feet.
The facility will utilize proprietary furnace technologies.
The facility is located at Downrange Industrial Park in Bainbridge.
The facility will be the first of its size and scale in North America.
The facility will produce synthetic graphite for use in batteries that power EVs, electric storage systems, electronics, military applications, and other products.
Operations are expected to begin in late 2025.
Eric Stopka is the Chief Executive Officer of Anovion.
This will be Anovion's first large-scale expansion manufacturing facility in Georgia.
The state’s strong technical workforce and top-tier schools will help advance research partnerships for Anovion.
The state’s resources will contribute to a highly skilled talent pipeline in support of Anovion’s innovation and climate technology jobs.
Anovion is encouraged by the region’s growing availability of clean energy, which will increase sustainability for its manufacturing process.
Anovion aims to secure the country’s electrified future with support from partners in Georgia and the Federal government.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry, building on assets that make the automotive industry successful.
Since 2020, 40 EV-related projects have announced more than $21.9 billion in investment in Georgia.
Since 2020, those 40 EV-related projects have announced 28,000 jobs in Georgia.
More than 10 of these projects are battery manufacturers, recyclers, and suppliers.
The battery-focused projects represent approximately $8.5 billion in investment.
The battery-focused projects represent 6,000 jobs.
