facts: Solvay Specialty Polymers plans to build a $850 million plant in Augusta, Georgia to manufacture key lithium-ion EV battery parts, announced last Thursday.
The polymer company will produce EV-battery grade binders and separator coatings made from polyvinylidene fluoride.
With the new site, Solvay claims it will have the largest production capacity of the lithium-ion chemical ingredient in North America.
The project is a joint venture between Solvay and chemical manufacturer Orbia, which will supply raw materials.
The companies plan to open two production sites, with both expected to be operational by 2026.
Belgium-based Solvay stated the joint venture would fill a “significant supply gap” for the EV battery part.
Solvay noted the project builds off “favorable conditions promoting regional production and material security.”

Last month, Solvay received a $178 million grant for the facility through the Battery Materials Processing and Battery Component Manufacturing and Recycling program, funded under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.

Mike Finelli, President of Solvay Growth Initiatives and Chief North American Officer, stated: “We are proud to receive the federal government’s support for manufacturing technologies that will provide the U.S. with critical raw materials for building an independent, sustainable EV value chain.”
Rivian made the single largest economic investment in Georgia’s history last year with plans for a $5 billion EV production facility in East Augusta, according to the governor’s office.
Last month, Hyundai broke ground on a $5.5 billion “mega campus” in Savannah, Georgia, where it will manufacture batteries and a diverse line of EVs.
Denkai America will locate its North American headquarters and new manufacturing facility in the Augusta area, focusing on EV battery foil.
source_title: Georgia to get $850M EV battery parts plant | Manufacturing Dive

facts: Qcells has just begun manufacturing solar cells at its new factory in Cartersville, Georgia.
The company already produces solar modules at the Cartersville facility.
The Cartersville facility will operate as a vertically integrated production facility for solar modules.
When fully operational, the facility will add 3.3 GW of vertically integrated ingot capacity.
When fully operational, the facility will add 3.3 GW of wafer capacity.
When fully operational, the facility will add 3.3 GW of cell capacity.
When fully operational, the facility will add 3.5 GW of module capacity.
Qcells' total U.S. output will hit 8.6 GW by the end of Q3 2026.
The total U.S. output is approximately the energy needed to power roughly 1.3 million American homes for a year.
This will be the first and only manufacturing facility in the United States to produce ingot, wafer, cell, and module components under one roof.
Qcells can claim tax credits for each of these parts and the panel as a whole.
The Biden administration established large subsidies for producing solar panels and solar PV components in the U.S.
These subsidies have survived various Trump administration attacks on pro-cleantech policies.
Having all production in one spot makes for very competitive products from Qcells.
Customers will benefit from an easier path to qualifying for the domestic content tax credit.
Customers will benefit from greater supply certainty for their projects.
The domestic content bonus is 10% under the Investment Tax Credit.
Because the major components of each module are made domestically, customers can pursue qualifying projects with greater confidence on pricing, supply and sourcing.
Qcells expects strong interest in Cartersville-produced modules due to growing demand for fully domestic solar equipment.
The company is already at full module production at the factory.
The factory is able to produce 16,700 solar PV modules a day.
Full solar cell production will be achieved in the fall of this year.
Once fully operational, it will be the largest solar cell production facility in the history of the United States.
It will also become the biggest ingot and wafer production facility in the country.
By Q3 2026, Cartersville will make 3.3 GW each of ingots, wafers, cells, and 3.5 GW of modules a year.
The Dalton factory tripled module capacity to 5.1 GW in late 2023.
Together with the expanded Dalton factory, Qcells' total module capacity in Georgia will reach 8.6 GW a year.
Combined with Dalton, Qcells will produce 47,000 panels a day.
The combined output is approximately the energy needed to power roughly 1.3 million U.S. homes for a year.
The Cartersville investment is bringing thousands of skilled manufacturing jobs to Northwest Georgia.
Together with the expanded Dalton campus, Qcells' Georgia operations are expected to employ nearly 4,000 people.
This includes an estimated 3,800 direct jobs across Bartow and Whitfield Counties.
source_title: Largest Solar Cell Factory In USA Starts Production in Georgia - CleanTechnica

facts: The Hyundai electric vehicle factory near Savannah is known as the Metaplant and is a 20-month-old manufacturing facility.
Acclimation time with Spot, a doglike robot, is part of the onboarding process for trainees at the Metaplant.
Susan Williams heads the factory’s training program through Quick Start, a state-funded workforce development organization.
Spot would walk up beside new hires and startle them, which is why acclimation time is necessary to prevent screaming on the factory floor.
The Hyundai Metaplant is currently seeking full-time and part-time factory employees.
Open roles include automation, robotics, maintenance, logistics, production and quality positions.
Susan Williams confirmed that the plant will be hiring for all of these listed positions.
Quick Start is described as Georgia’s “secret weapon” for economic development.
The training program follows a “train like you work” philosophy.
source_title: Hyundai’s Georgia Metaplant uses Quick Start to train workers

facts: Qcells is the first and only polysilicon-based solar manufacturer to achieve Electronic Product Environmental Assessment Tool (EPEAT)’s Low Carbon Solar standard.
Qcells obtained initial EPEAT status for its solar panels last year, after years of work hitting the stringent low-carbon threshold.
EPEAT is a globally recognized ecolabel for electronics and technologies that validates sustainable production practices to help customers select low-carbon solutions.
Qcells achieved Low Carbon status for two products: a residential solar panel and a community, commercial and industrial (CCI) panel.
Both the residential and CCI panels are assembled at Qcells’ Dalton, Georgia manufacturing facility.
Initial EPEAT registration for these two panels was achieved in June 2024.
Qcells’ EPEAT registered modules have a ~73% lower carbon footprint or lower carbon intensity than imported solar products from China.
Compared to an average utility-scale 100 MW solar PV project, a same-size PV project using Qcells’ EPEAT registered panels would have 37% lower emissions for the same power output.
Qcells’ EPEAT registered solar panels are 37% more sustainable than a global standard solar panel.
Qcells outperforms standard modules on sustainability by nearly 40%.

Kelly Weger, Senior Director of Sustainability at Qcells, stated that the company wants its solar products to be the most sustainably built products on the market and remains fully committed to manufacturing complete clean energy solutions.
It took more than two years of collecting extensive data for Qcells’ products to meet EPEAT’s rigorous standards.
Only 30% of solar products are expected to be able to meet the minimum criteria to achieve the EPEAT ecolabel.
Qcells leads as the first domestic manufacturer to provide EPEAT-registered residential solar panels.
Qcells plans to continue expanding its number of products on the EPEAT registry manufactured at its Dalton and Cartersville, Georgia manufacturing facilities.
EPEAT registration measures social and environmental impacts of products from extraction to end of life using lifecycle-based criteria that adjust as sustainability standards evolve.
EPEAT registered products are listed on the Global Electronic Council’s EPEAT registry for customers with ambitious sustainability goals.
EPEAT’s online registry helps drive supply chain improvements by simplifying the procurement of sustainably manufactured low-carbon solar panels.

Qcells is one of the world’s leading clean energy companies, recognized as a manufacturer of high-performance solar cells and modules, intelligent storage systems, and large-scale renewable energy projects.
Qcells provides renewable electricity retail services and packages to end customers worldwide.
The company is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea (Global Executive HQ) with international manufacturing facilities in the U.S., Malaysia, and South Korea.
Qcells serves utility, commercial, governmental, and residential markets through a global business network spanning Europe, North America, Asia, South America, Africa, and the Middle East.
source_title: Qcells Becomes First Polysilicon-Based Solar Manufacturer in the U.S. to Earn Low Carbon Solar Distinction - Qcells North America

facts: Freyr Battery announced it abandoned plans to build a battery factory in Coweta County, Georgia.
The Georgia battery factory was originally announced in 2022.
The first phase of the Georgia factory was expected to produce approximately 34 GWh of lithium-ion batteries.
The estimated capital investment for the first phase was $1.7 billion.
Freyr Battery is moving its global corporate headquarters to Austin, Texas.
The company plans to sell the 368-acre site of the planned Georgia battery factory to an undisclosed party.
The sale of the Georgia site is expected for gross sales proceeds of $50 million.
The Georgia site sale transaction is expected to close on February 15, 2025.
Freyr expects estimated net proceeds from the site sale to total $22.5 million following repayment of state and local grants.
The Georgia project was planned as a multi-phase clean battery manufacturing project.
After the first phase, additional investment in the Georgia project would amount to $2.6 billion by 2029.
Over the lifetime of the Georgia project, the company had estimated it would bring more than 720 U.S. jobs to Georgia.
Freyr was motivated to build the Georgia factory based on the strength of the U.S. renewable energy and electric vehicle industries coupled with tax incentives from the Inflation Reduction Act.
Freyr currently has a gigafactory under construction in Norway called the Giga Arctic project.
The Giga Arctic project in Norway has a planned nameplate capacity of 29 GWh.
Late last year, Freyr announced it entered into an agreement to acquire the U.S. solar manufacturing assets of Trina Solar for $340 million.
The acquired Trina Solar assets include a 5 GW, 1.35 million square foot solar module manufacturing facility in Wilmer, Texas.
The Wilmer, Texas solar facility started production in November 2024.
The Wilmer, Texas facility is expected to ramp up to full production this year with 30% of capacity already contracted with U.S. customers.
This acquisition is the first step in Freyr's plan to build out a vertically integrated domestic manufacturing footprint.
The next phase of Freyr's domestic footprint will include a 5 GW solar cell facility.
Freyr is currently selecting a site for the 5 GW solar cell facility and is targeting Q2 2025 for the start of construction.
Freyr anticipates that the first Freyr cells would roll off the line in the second half of 2026.
The decision to pull out of the Georgia project is a direct consequence of uncertainty created by Donald Trump’s stance on tariffs and his threats to repeal the Inflation Reduction Act.
A company statement noted that the stability and growth of the renewable sector depend on clear, consistent policy rather than political volatility.
The company stated that businesses cannot justify billion-dollar investments when a single individual’s agenda puts the entire industry at risk.
The company characterized the decision as stemming from reckless policymaking that undermines American jobs, energy independence, and long-term economic growth.
source_title: Freyr Battery abandons plans for Georgia battery factory - pv magazine USA

facts: Freyr Battery decided to abandon its plans for a $2.6 billion lithium-ion battery plant in Coweta County, Georgia.
The canceled plant would have created more than 700 jobs.
The cancellation decision was communicated in a letter to county leaders.
Reasons cited for the withdrawal include rising interest rates, falling battery prices, changes in management, and a realignment of the company’s near-term strategic goals.
Freyr will now focus on its newly-opened, 5 GW solar cell fab that it bought for $340 million from Trina Solar in November 2024.
This cancellation marks the second gigafactory plan in the U.S. shelved within a week.
A couple of days earlier, Kore Power abandoned efforts to build a $1.2 billion battery plant in Buckeye, Arizona.
The cancellation highlights the difficulty in breaking into a highly-competitive, low-margin business such as battery manufacturing.
Globally, 2024 saw the highest gigafactory capacity cancellations on record with more than 312 GWh of projects worldwide shelved by the end of November.
These global cancellations have been primarily from less experienced Tier 3 manufacturers in China.
Plans for Freyr’s Giga America plant were initially laid down in November 2022.
The initial plan called for a $1.7 billion investment to build the manufacturing facility with an annual output of 34 GWh.
At the time of that announcement, the Coweta County project was the second-largest battery factory in planning worldwide.
By 2029, the projected investment was expected to surpass $2.6 billion, with subsequent expansion of annual manufacturing capacity.
Giga America was expected to produce lithium-ion cells for both electric vehicles and energy storage applications.
Construction on the 368-acre (149-hectare) site never began.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development conveyed a $7 million grant to buy a site for Freyr in Newnan.
Freyr is now expected to repay the $7 million state grant.
Freyr was founded in 2008 and had an ambitious 200 GWh production target by 2030 across gigafactory projects in Europe and the U.S.
In 2023, Freyr decided to minimize all investments in its flagship Giga Arctic project in Mo I Rana, Norway, as well as other European projects to focus only on scaling in the U.S.
To maximize eligibility for U.S. tax benefits of the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), Freyr moved its corporate headquarters from Norway to Newnan.

The company announced Austin, Texas, as the location for its new global headquarters to geographically align its workforce with operations and strategy for providing solar modules made in the United States.
Freyr entered into a definitive agreement to sell its 368-acre site in Coweta County, Georgia, to an undisclosed party for gross sales proceeds of $50 million.
The site sale transaction is expected to close on February 15, 2024.
Estimated net proceeds to Freyr from the sale are expected to total $22.5 million following repayment of previously received state and local grants.
source_title: Freyr cancels $2.6 billion gigafactory in Georgia, sells site - Energy Storage

facts: FREYR has selected Austin, Texas, as its global headquarters.
The company selected the new headquarters to geographically align its workforce with its operations and strategy.
Freyr Battery sold its Georgia site for USD 50 million.
source_title: Freyr Battery, Norway announces new global HQ, sells Georgia site for USD 50 Million - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: Trina Solar announced it was investing more than $200 million to establish the Wilmer plant in September 2023.
About a year later, global banking company Standard Chartered secured a $235 million green loan to finance the development and construction of the 1.35-million-square-foot plant.
Once it’s completed, the Wilmer facility will create up to 1,500 jobs and source polysilicon from the U.S. and Europe, according to Trina Solar’s September 2023 press release.
The site will also have seven production lines, Freyr’s SVP of Communications Amy Jaick said in an email.
Production on the plant began Nov. 1, 2024.
Freyr announced on Feb. 10 it selected Austin, Texas, as its new global headquarters.
The location brings the company closer to the Wilmer site, which is in the Dallas-Fort Worth metropolitan area.
Freyr is also currently developing a 5 GW solar cell manufacturing facility in the U.S., Jaick said.
Freyr plans to announce the plant’s location in the coming months and production is expected to begin in the first half of 2026.
The company expects to spend $850 million on the new facility as well as create 1,800 jobs, according to the presentation.
The Wilmer plant and solar cell factory are expected to be fully eligible for Inflation Reduction Act production tax credits, which Freyr plans to apply for.
However, the future of the tax credits under Trump is unclear, given the president’s vocal opposition to the subsidies.
source_title: Freyr Battery Cancels $2.6B Georgia Battery Factory Plans

facts: Archer Aviation has selected a site east of Atlanta, Georgia, for its Midnight electric takeoff and landing aircraft (eVTOL) project.
The chosen location is a tract adjacent to a municipal airport in Covington spanning about 100 acres.
The Archer facility will consist of three buildings.
Georgia's incentive package for the project is worth about one-third of the company's pledged $118 capital investment.
The project is expected to create 1,000 incentivized jobs.
Archer chose Georgia over a competing site in Dallas-Fort Worth.
Georgia's existing ecosystem of some 800 aerospace companies and a growing EV sector was a key factor in the decision.

Tosha Perkins, Archer’s chief people and partnerships officer, stated that the talent pool for aerospace, automotive, electric vehicle, and general manufacturing was the number one reason for choosing Georgia.
The airport location was favored for its benign weather, which is good for testing.
Georgia's technical colleges and other organizations came together to provide training and workforce development support beyond the incentive package.
Archer has a partnership with a local chapter of the Future Farmers of America (FFA) where trees cleared from the site are being used to make furniture for the facility.
The company's current target is to move into the facility by the end of the year.
Commercialization of the Midnight aircraft is targeted for 2025.
Archer Aviation has raised more than $1 billion but has not yet turned a profit.
The company's current headquarters is in Santa Clara, California.
Tosha Perkins described the Midnight as having 12 propellers that take off and land like a helicopter and fly like a plane.
Archer's vision for the Midnight includes using the aircraft as rideshare or an "air taxi."
source_title: Georgia Intelligence Report - Aerospace & Aviation: Innovative Archer Aviation Has Landed in Georgia: Relationships are key to another E-mobility project.

facts: Archer Aviation Inc. is a pioneer in electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft.
Archer Aviation announced plans to build its manufacturing facility in Covington, Georgia, next to the Covington Municipal Airport.
Archer has also announced recently its new partnership with Molicel to supply battery cells for…
The announcement regarding the facility selection and partnership was published on November 19, 2022.
source_title: Archer Aviation Selects Georgia For Manufacturing Facility Next to Covington Municipal Airport

facts: Archer Aviation's Midnight electric air taxi successfully completed its inaugural uncrewed, untethered hover flight.
The test flight marks a critical step towards FAA type certification and commercial launch by 2025.
The five-seat eVTOL is designed for rapid, short-hop flights.
The Midnight aims to be cost-competitive with ground rideshare.
Archer has partnerships with United Airlines.
Stellantis is a manufacturing partner for the Midnight.
Archer has a significant contract with the U.S. Air Force.
Archer is rapidly advancing its flight test program.
Archer is constructing a large production facility in Georgia.
The company received a special airworthiness certificate from the FAA in August to begin testing the electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) design.
A nonconforming Midnight prototype completed its inaugural test flight.
source_title: Archer’s Midnight Electric Air Taxi Completes Inaugural Test Flight

facts: Archer Aviation Inc. (NYSE: ACHR) announced plans to locate its manufacturing facility in Covington, Georgia at a site adjacent to the Covington Municipal Airport.
Archer plans to initially build out a 350,000 square-foot facility on a 96-acre site.
The initial facility will be capable of producing up to 650 aircraft per year.
The planned eVTOL aircraft manufacturing facility is expected to create over 1,000 jobs over the long term.
Construction is expected to be completed in the first half of 2024.
Initial production is expected to begin in the second half of 2024.
The facility will be capable of being expanded by an additional 550,000 square feet.
The expansion is estimated to support production of up to 2,300 aircraft per year.
Archer completed a comprehensive survey of potential sites across the United States to select the location for its first manufacturing facility.

Key considerations for site selection included the availability of talent in the local labor market, utility availability, ability to conduct seamless flight test operations, construction costs, and logistics.
Archer will receive an incentive package that includes land conveyance, tax incentives, and a Georgia REBA grant.
Financing for the project is anticipated to be provided by Synovus, the largest bank headquartered in Georgia.
Adam Goldstein serves as Archer's CEO.
Governor Brian P. Kemp, Bob Kosek, Sara Gershon, Serra Hall, and Frank Turner were involved in the site selection process.
Georgia's established leadership in the aerospace industry was essential to landing Archer Aviation in Newton County.
Archer can take advantage of Georgia's signature workforce training program Quick Start to help fill over 1,000 long-term jobs in Covington.
Georgia is home to more than 800 aerospace companies.

The state maintains a robust aerospace education pipeline that includes a dozen high schools offering training, five technical colleges with aviation programs, and several universities offering degrees in aerospace engineering.

The Georgia Center of Innovation for Aerospace, a strategic arm of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, has been cultivating an ecosystem of opportunities for innovative companies like Archer for more than 10 years.
Archer is designing and developing electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft for use in urban air mobility networks.
Archer's team is based in Santa Clara, CA.
source_title: Archer Aviation Selects Site in Georgia Adjacent to the Covington Municipal Airport for its Manufacturing Facility - Green Stock News

facts: Archer reached an agreement in principle on key terms of a contract manufacturing relationship with Stellantis.
Stellantis will contribute up to approximately $400 million to help scale Archer’s Midnight manufacturing to 650 aircraft annually.
The commitment includes an obligation to cover manufacturing labor costs and certain capital expenditures at Archer’s Georgia manufacturing facility through 2030.
This contribution is in addition to Stellantis’ $55 million equity investment in Archer received on July 1, 2024.
Stellantis will fund up to approximately $370 million of the anticipated manufacturing labor costs necessary to support the planned production ramp to 650 aircraft annually.
In exchange for funding labor costs, Stellantis will receive Archer shares on a rolling quarterly basis based on total labor costs incurred and Archer’s then-current future stock price.
Stellantis will contribute identified initial incremental manufacturing capital expenditures for the initial ramp of Midnight production, estimated to be up to $20 million.
Archer intends to issue Stellantis $30 million of performance warrants that will vest based on Stellantis' achievement of certain performance milestones under the contract manufacturing relationship.
Archer’s issuance of any equity to Stellantis pursuant to this relationship is expected to be subject to approval by Archer’s stockholders.
Archer secured $230 million in additional equity capital since the end of Q2 2024.
The new equity capital includes a $175 million investment from strategic and institutional investors, including United.
Moelis & Company LLC acted as exclusive placement agent for the equity investment.
Archer unveiled plans for a Los Angeles air taxi network with take-off and landing locations at LAX, USC, Orange County, Santa Monica, Hollywood Burbank, Long Beach, and Van Nuys.
Archer is coordinating with the Los Angeles Rams and Hollywood Park, a 300-acre district centered around SoFi Stadium, for a planned exclusive vertiport.
Archer’s goal is to begin its Los Angeles network operations by as early as 2026.
The U.S. Department of Defense accepted the military airworthiness assessment of Archer’s Midnight aircraft this month.
Archer delivered its first Midnight aircraft to the U.S. Air Force as part of an AFWERX Agility Prime contract valued at up to $142 million.
Future Flight Global plans to purchase up to 116 Midnight aircraft worth up to $580 million, bringing Archer’s indicative order book to nearly $6 billion.
The planned aircraft purchase supports the joint goal of launching electric vertical take-off and landing aircraft operations in key global markets in Europe and Asia.
Archer’s Q2 2024 total operating expenses were $121.2 million on a GAAP basis and $96.4 million on a Non-GAAP basis, with a net loss of $106.9 million and cash and cash equivalents of $360.4 million.
Archer’s Q3 2024 financial estimates project non-GAAP total operating expenses between $90 million and $100 million.
CEO Adam Goldstein stated that Archer is working to enable commercial operations worldwide and aims for commercialization as early as next year.

links.context: Shareholder letter and live webcast access
Full press release and source version

source_title: Archer Announces Key Terms Of Contract Manufacturing Relationship With Stellantis–Representing Up To ~$400M To Help Scale Midnight Production To 650 Aircraft Annually–Secures $230M In Additional Equity Capital From Strategic & Institutional Investors | Nasdaq

facts: Three new businesses are coming soon to Right to Work Georgia: Brightmark, Duckyang, and IC Biomedical.
Each business will add a new location somewhere within the state of Georgia.
Brightmark will be locating in Macon-Bibb with a $680 million investment.
Brightmark is investing $680 million soon in Right to Work Macon-Bibb County, Georgia.
Brightmark will build its next plastics renewal facility in Macon-Bibb.
The advanced recycling facility will be the world’s largest and will utilize proven plastics renewal technology.
Duckyang is investing $10 million soon toward its first US manufacturing location in Right to Work Braselton, Georgia.
Duckyang's new location will create 285 new jobs.
In Georgia, partners are focused on growing the entire electric mobility supply chain.
Strong relationships with SK Battery America and other Korean companies like Duckyang are helping secure a local supply of needed parts for the automotive industry moving toward electric.
Partners in Jackson County supported the Duckyang project.
IC Biomedical is investing millions toward a new manufacturing facility in Right to Work Cartersville, Georgia.
IC Biomedical's new facility will create over 80 new jobs.
Georgia was chosen for IC Biomedical due to a highly trained workforce, transport links, and state infrastructure committed to the life science sector.

IC Biomedical joins metro Atlanta’s growing life sciences community alongside assets like Emory School of Medicine, Morehouse School of Medicine, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, Georgia Tech, and many businesses engaged in research, development, manufacturing, and distribution of life science related products and supplies.
source_title: Recap: What Businesses are Coming to Right to Work Georgia? | NILRR

facts: Kia Motors America operates an assembly plant in Troup County producing 340,000 vehicles a year.
Kia recently dropped “Motors” from its name and is now known simply as “Kia America.”
The Korean automaker has a $25 billion plan to transition to electric vehicles soon.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced a new statewide initiative on July 20 aimed at strengthening Georgia’s status as a leader in the electric mobility industry.

The Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (EMIA) will bring industry executives together with state leaders in government, education, utilities and nonprofits to look for ways to build up an industry that already has enjoyed some major successes.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated: “Georgia has a proven track record of investing early in the resources and infrastructure needed to connect it to the world and develop jobs of the future.”
Gov. Brian Kemp stated: “The Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance will ensure that our state is positioned to continue leading the nation in the rapidly growing electric mobility industry.”
Georgia already boasts two SK Innovation electric vehicle battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County.
SK Innovation's investment in the Jackson County facilities is nearly $2.6 billion.
Dutch electric-vehicle charging company Heliox decided to base its North American headquarters in Atlanta.
Turkish electric-vehicle parts manufacturer TEKLAS plans to open its first North American plant in Gordon County.
German-owned GEDIA, a manufacturer of lightweight auto-body parts, plans to open a plant in Whitfield County.
South Korea-based Duckyang, an electric mobility parts supplier, will build two plants near Braselton.
Chris Clark is president and CEO of the Georgia Chamber of Commerce.
Projections show electric vehicles in Georgia will balloon from just 7% of the market in 2025 to 30% by 2030, 58% by 2040 and 96% by 2050.
Chris Clark stated: “Kia, SK and all these companies have to got to build out these vehicles.”
Chris Clark stated: “We want Georgia to do it.”
Pat Wilson is commissioner of the state Department of Economic Development.
Pat Wilson stated that 55,000 jobs in Georgia are connected in some form to the auto industry.
Many of those auto industry jobs involve manufacturing vehicles with traditional internal-combustion engines.
Pat Wilson stated: “We’re probably going to see more change in the next 20 years in the auto industry than we’ve seen in the past 100 years.”
Pat Wilson stated: “We’re focused on public policy that can help drive that transition.”
Participants in the EMIA will be divided into five committees.
The five committees will set objectives in five categories: supply chain, infrastructure, workforce development, innovation and policy.
The Carl Vinson Institute at the University of Georgia will host committee meetings.
The auto industry will be represented in the initiative by executives from Kia, Cox Automotive, Porsche, and Mercedes-Benz USA.
Porsche has its North American headquarters in Hapeville.
Mercedes-Benz USA is based in Sandy Springs.
Executives from Georgia Power and the state’s electric membership cooperatives (EMCs) will be involved.
The public sector will be represented by the state Department of Transportation and the Technical College System of Georgia.
The Technical College System of Georgia will take part in discussions on workforce development.
Pat Wilson stated: “We’re looking at this as a true public-private partnership.”
Outcomes from the initiative could come in the form of legislation for the General Assembly to consider, new policy initiatives or a combination of the two.
Lawmakers approved a tax credit for purchasers of electric vehicles back in the 1990s.
The state eliminated the EV tax credit in 2015 as EV sales soared.
Georgia Rep. Chuck Martin is from R-Alpharetta and was the chief sponsor of the 2015 bill.
Rep. Chuck Martin stated the 2015 tax credit was mainly generating sales for the Nissan Leaf electric vehicle.
Rep. Chuck Martin stated: “It wasn’t helping drive innovation.”
Rep. Chuck Martin stated: “It was just subsidizing a purchase.”
Chris Clark agreed a tax credit to stimulate sales of EVs is no longer needed.

Chris Clark expects some form of tax credit proposal could come out of the new initiative that will neither incentivize EV sales or offer the jobs tax credit Georgia traditionally has provided industries.
Chris Clark stated: “It’s a new industry, a new economy, and our old jobs tax credit is not going to move the needle for these guys.”
Chris Clark stated: “We’re going to have to listen to the industry.”
Chris Clark envisions new tax breaks that would incentivize gasoline stations to install charging stations for EVs.
Chris Clark envisions new tax breaks that would encourage homebuilders to put chargers in new houses.
Rep. Chuck Martin stated state government needs to be careful not to get ahead of the industry by pushing too aggressively for a transition to all electric vehicles.
Rep. Chuck Martin stated: “Innovation will get us there.”
Rep. Chuck Martin stated: “But in some places, there won’t be the electric grid or charging stations for that to happen.”
Pat Wilson said it’s uncertain what solutions the committees will hit upon.
Pat Wilson stated: “We want to go into this with a blank slate and really listen to the professionals.”
The EMIA committees will meet this fall.
The goal is to make recommendations in time for the 2022 General Assembly session starting in January.
source_title: Kemp launches effort to boost Georgia’s electric mobility industry |

facts: Duckyang USA plans to invest $10,000,000.00 to build out 230,000 square feet of new space in Braselton, Georgia.
The company plans to occupy the new space at 984 Broadway Avenue in Braselton on or about January 1, 2022.
Duckyang Ind. is a leading auto component provider founded in July 1977 in Ulsan, Korea.
The company's founding belief was that “manufacturing is the foundation for industrial activity which drives the nation’s economy.”
Duckyang remains committed to its motto of “responsibility, creativity, and cooperation.”
The company emphasizes a moral and transparent management system focused on developing employees into leading creative talents.
Dong-in Son serves as the Chief Executive Officer of Duckyang USA.
State and local economic development sources confirmed the investment and occupancy plans.
source_title: Duckyang USA to spend $10,000,000.00 to occupy 230,000 square feet of space in Braselton Georgia. - intelligence360

facts: Georgia’s incentives portfolio fosters economic growth by offering tax credits and exemptions that reduce costs for companies establishing new locations or expanding current ones.
The corporate income tax rate dropped to 4.99% in 2026.
Georgia was the first Southeastern state to adopt single-factor apportionment.
Companies can tap into Job Tax Credits by creating new jobs in qualifying industries.
In certain locations, Job Tax Credits can reduce payroll-withholding obligations to generate cash flow for businesses.
Quality Jobs Tax Credits are earned for new jobs that pay 110% or more of the county’s average wage.
Georgia exempts sales and use taxes on manufacturing-integral items including machinery, repair parts, molds, dies, tooling, raw materials, packaging, and supplies.
Energy essential to manufacturing operations is exempt from the State’s 4% sales and use tax.
Additional local exemptions for manufacturing energy vary by jurisdiction from 1% to 3%.

Distribution centers may qualify for a sales and use tax exemption on equipment used for handling, moving, or storing goods if they invest at least $5 million in expanding or establishing a new facility.
Most counties exempt local property tax for manufacturers’ goods held for 12 months or less, including goods to be shipped within a year.
Companies must apply for the freeport exemption each year.
There is no state property tax on inventory or any real or personal property in Georgia.
source_title: Georgia’s Incentives | Georgia

entity_name: State of Georgia Economic Development & Workforce Initiative
facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp delivered the keynote address at the second annual Governor's Workforce Summit on September 13, 2024.
Georgia was named the No. 1 state for business for the 11th consecutive year by Area Development magazine.
The summit put forth the goal of making Georgia the Top State for Talent.
Georgia Quick Start earned a No. 1 ranking for the 15th year in a row for its nationally recognized workforce development program.
Area Development’s annual poll considers approximately 50 leading site consulting firms from across the U.S. and evaluates 14 different factors to determine the Top State for Business.
The second annual Governor's Workforce Summit was hosted at the Georgia World Congress Center in Atlanta.
Georgia MATCH is described as the largest direct college admission program in the nation.
Georgia MATCH sent a letter to every high school senior in the state informing them of spots at University System of Georgia (USG) and Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG) institutions.
TCSG saw a preliminary year-over-year enrollment increase of 9.3% this fall following last year's program rollout.
USG saw a preliminary year-over-year enrollment increase of 6.2% this fall following last year's program rollout.
The enrollment increases occurred despite challenges from the federal government's mishandling of the FAFSA rollout.

Dennis Shea, Publisher of Area Development, stated that Georgia has maintained top 10 rankings in survey subcategories for well over a decade, including available workforce, cost of doing business, cooperative and responsive state government, energy availability and costs, logistics and infrastructure, and site readiness programs.
Site selectors recognize that Georgia provides the whole package for companies choosing to grow or expand in the state.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia does economic development differently than anywhere else by prioritizing partnerships and investments in infrastructure, education, and workforce development.
Between July 1, 2023, and June 30, 2024, GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 429 facility expansions and new locations.
These supported expansions and new locations totaled more than $20.3 billion in investment.
The investments resulted in plans for 26,900 new jobs statewide.
Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) serves as the state’s sales and marketing arm and lead agency for attracting new business investment and encouraging industry expansion.
source_title: Gov. Kemp Celebrates Top State for Business Ranking | Georgia

entity_name: U.S. Department of Energy Transportation Technologies Office
canonical_name: U.S. Department of Energy Transportation Technologies Office (TTO)
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy's Transportation Technologies Office (TTO) has awarded more than $570 million since 1998 through its funding opportunities in support of Technology Integration Program projects across the country.

These project awards contribute to empowering communities to choose the transportation energy sources that work for them, facilitating implementation of alternative fuel vehicles and infrastructure, and building the workforce for our transportation energy future.
The program supports integrating alternative fuel vehicles and fueling infrastructure in urban and rural communities.

The program supports implementing living lab projects that demonstrate and assess new mobility solutions that maximize the return on investment to mobility systems in terms of time, cost, energy, and opportunity.
The program supports developing alternative fueling and charging stations along busy transportation corridors.
The program supports providing stakeholder choice in vehicle and fueling options.
The program supports securing real-world data on total cost of ownership for alternative fuels.
The program supports planning community-driven transportation.
The program supports understanding transportation electrification in public and private fleets.
source_title: Clean Cities and Communities: Funded Projects

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Georgia-based engineering and manufacturing company Yancey Engineered Solutions will invest $5.7 million in a new manufacturing facility in Cordele.
The investment will create 300 new jobs over the next several years in Crisp County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Yancey provides solutions and services that keep Georgia moving, supporting everything from small businesses to large manufacturers across every industry.
Governor Brian Kemp noted the new facility will provide direct opportunity for hardworking Georgians in Crisp County and help drive further growth statewide.
Yancey Engineered Solutions was founded in 2004 and provides power generation packaging systems.
Yancey Engineered Solutions is a division of Yancey Bros. Company, the authorized Caterpillar dealer for Georgia.
Trey Googe, Yancey’s CEO, stated that Yancey Bros. has served Cordele and Crisp County for decades primarily with Caterpillar earthmoving equipment, parts, and service.
Trey Googe expressed excitement to add new teammates from the Cordele/Crisp community and manufacture products here that help fill a need in a growing and important market.
Yancey’s new facility will be located at 502 South Midway Road in Cordele.
The company plans to refurbish the former Big Tex manufacturing facility.
Yancey Engineered Solutions plans to hire welders, quality control technicians, assembly workers, and drivers.

Cordele Mayor and Crisp County-Cordele Industrial Development Authority Vice-Chair Wesley Rainey stated that Cordele is excited by Yancey’s decision to redevelop the Big Tex facility and hopes to rehire many former employees of Big Tex.
Wesley Rainey described the project as a great development for Cordele.

James R. Dowdy, III, Chairman of the Crisp County Board of Commissioners and the Cordele-Crisp Industrial Development Authority, stated that the community is thankful for Yancey’s decision to land there.
James R. Dowdy, III noted the project demonstrates that economic development efforts are paying dividends and are consistent with citizens’ visions of growth.

Senior Regional Project Manager Christy Bozeman represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development on this competitive project in partnership with the Cordele-Crisp Industrial Development Authority, Crisp County Power Commission, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Yancey Bros. has shaped Georgia’s story for a century and is continuously evolving to serve industry needs.
Pat Wilson noted that Yancey Engineered Solutions represents an exciting addition to Yancey’s portfolio that helps provide reliable energy to users.
Yancey Bros. Co. is headquartered in Austell, Ga., and is recognized as the nation’s oldest Caterpillar dealer.
Yancey Bros. Co. celebrated its Centennial anniversary in 2014 and has been family owned and operated for 107 years.
Yancey Bros. Co. offers Caterpillar, Weiler, and Blue Bird equipment plus many other products, parts and services across the state of Georgia.
The company operates through 29 facilities in 21 Georgia communities.
source_title: Yancey Engineered Solutions Bringing 300 New Jobs to Crisp Co. | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on May 3, 2024 that GF Casting Solutions AG will invest more than $184 million in a new manufacturing facility in Augusta.
GF Casting Solutions AG is a division of Georg Fischer AG, located in Schaffhausen, Switzerland.
The company produces lightweight components for the mobility and energy industries.
The project will create 350 new jobs for Richmond County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated Georgia is proud to carry the title of the No. 1 state for business to companies across the globe.
GF Casting Solutions develops and produces parts made from aluminum, magnesium, iron, and super alloy for light vehicles, trucks, aerospace, energy, off-highway vehicles, and industrial applications.
Carlos Vasto, President of GF Casting Solutions, stated building a High Pressure Die Casting Facility is a very long-term investment to complete their footprint in the global e-mobility market.
The new facility will be state-of-the-art and LEED-certified at the Augusta Corporate Park, a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) site.
The facility will produce cast aluminum parts with a special focus on large structural components for the automotive industry.
Operations are expected to begin in 2027.
GF Casting Solutions will hire for roles in manufacturing and automation over the next few years.
Jobs will be posted as they become available.
Augusta Mayor Garnett Johnson stated this is the second large European investment in the Augusta Corporate Park and highlighted city investments in water, sewer, roads, and workforce growth.

Development Authority of Augusta Chairman Wayne Gossage thanked the Augusta Economic Development Authority for bringing in industries with new jobs and ensuring existing industries have resources to grow.
Assistant Director Virginia Sengewald and Project Manager Caroline Knowles represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on the project.
The project was completed in partnership with the Augusta Economic Development Authority, Georgia Power, State of Georgia Europe Office, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated European companies demonstrate a wealth of business experience combined with an intentional focus on innovation driving the market forward.
Since 2018, Georgia has attracted more than 32,200 new jobs and over $27.3 billion in investments in the e-mobility, clean energy, and battery-related supply chains.
