facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) announced the 12 winning teams for Phase 2 of the 2025 Collegiate Wind Competition (CWC) on January 17, 2025.

The CWC is an annual competition held since 2014 that prepares students from colleges and universities across the United States for jobs in the wind energy industry by giving them real-world experience in technical design and project management.

Phase 2 winners include Brigham Young University, California Maritime Academy, California Polytechnic State University, Iowa State University, Johns Hopkins University, Kansas State University, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Pennsylvania State University, Rice University, University of Massachusetts Lowell, The University of Texas at Dallas, and Virginia Tech.
Finalists were selected from 35 Phase 1 winners based on submissions in prototype wind turbine design, turbine testing, hypothetical offshore wind farm planning, and community/industry outreach.

Each winning team receives a cash prize of up to $15,000 per team and advances to Phase 3 to refine wind turbine prototypes, present hypothetical wind farm sites, and illustrate community outreach plans.
Teams not selected can still participate as learn-along teams but are ineligible for any awards.
The final event will be held at the American Power Association's CLEANPOWER 2025 Conference and Exhibition in Phoenix, Arizona, where overall first-, second-, and third-place teams will be selected.

According to the National Laboratory of the Rockies' 2024 National Wind Energy Workforce Assessment, total wind energy workers are projected to reach about 258,000 by 2030, requiring the industry to train and hire up to 125,000 new workers.
The competition is funded by DOE's Wind Energy Technologies Office and managed by the National Laboratory of the Rockies.
source_title: Twelve Teams Selected To Advance to 2025 Collegiate Wind Competition Final Event | Department of Energy

facts: By 2030, the U.S. will need 28 million EV charging ports to support 33 million EVs.
The National Laboratory of the Rockies (NLR) estimates that by 2030, there will be 33 million EVs on the road and 28 million EV charging ports will be needed to support them.
The majority of charging will be at home and work, with the public network for opportunity charging and less common long trips.
Of the 28 million charging ports, 25.7 million (92%) are expected to be private Level 1 (L1) and Level 2 (L2) chargers at single-family homes.
Additionally, there will be an estimated 2.1 million (7.6%) public and private L2 chargers at multifamily homes, workplaces, stores, restaurants, and hotels.

Estimates show 182,000 (~1%) direct current (DC) fast charging ports would be needed at public charging stations, primarily to support those with no access to consistent off-street parking, as well as for long-distance travel.
L1 and L2 EV charging are expected to account for 80% of all EV charging in 2030.
NLR expects L1 and L2 charging to handle 80% of all EV charging duties by 2030.
Sufficient energy from the electric grid may be needed to support 33 million EVs on the road by 2030.
The majority (64%) of EV charging is estimated to take place at single-family homes using L1 and L2 charging, which is typically the most cost-effective and convenient type of charging.
DC fast charging infrastructure is expected to support 20% of EV charging needs.
Sixty percent of the 33 million EVs expected by 2030 are projected to be in suburban areas, according to a 2023 NLR report.
Of the remainder, 20% will be in rural locations and another 20% in urban areas.
The charging network needs for EVs are different depending on whether EV owners live in rural, suburban, or urban areas.

While electricity from public DC fast chargers would be the most utilized in urban areas (40%), in rural and suburban areas, L1 and L2 chargers in single-family homes are expected to meet the majority of EV electricity needs (82% and 64%, respectively).
Although DC fast charging receives a lot of attention, increasing the availability of L1 and L2 charging is critical to enable consumer EV choice.
source_title: EVGrid Assist: Charts and Figures | Department of Energy

facts: Gov. Andy Beshear congratulated Vista Metals as Kentucky’s metals industry continues to grow at a rapid pace.
Vista Metals is a market leader in the manufacturing of specialty aluminum products primarily supporting aerospace, commercial aircraft, defense, automotive, and general industrial applications.
Vista Metals plans to locate a new specialty aluminum products operation in Bowling Green, creating over 50 well-paying full-time jobs behind an approximate $60 million investment.
Initial site preparation for the new facility is already underway.
Vista Metals will commence with a phased build-out to support growth in its core markets.
Gov. Andy Beshear stated that Kentucky’s metals sector growth is not slowing down and highlighted Warren County as among the leading communities for industry growth.
Vista Metals plans to acquire more than 100 acres for the new facility.
The company will construct a 75,000-plus-square-foot facility.
Jobs created at the facility will include roles in operations, planning, engineering, and site management.

Andy Primack, CEO of Vista Metals, announced the new Kentucky location and emphasized Bowling Green’s strong industrial business base, qualified manufacturing talent pipeline, business-education interaction, and long-term community development plan.

Kelly Thomas, president of Vista Metals, stated the investment underscores a commitment to the aerospace industry and robust supply chains as OEMs look to more than double commercial aircraft build rates over the next two decades.

Vista Metals intends to enable customers to test and qualify the new facility ahead of projected growth surges while continuing ongoing investments in people, processes, and equipment at existing facilities.
VMC Specialty Alloys LLC (dba Vista Metals Georgia) operates under a licensing agreement with Fontana, California-based Vista Metals Corp.

Along with Vista Progressive Metals LLC, which produces aluminum lithium products, the group of companies are market leaders in specialty aluminum products for aerospace, commercial aircraft, defense, automotive, and general industrial applications.
Warren County Judge/Executive Doug Gorman noted the region’s strength in supporting new business and highlighted Warren County’s position as a hub for innovation and manufacturing excellence.
Bowling Green Mayor Todd Alcott stated the community has the resources, skilled workforce, and robust infrastructure needed to ensure the company’s success.

Ron Bunch, CEO of the Bowling Green Area Chamber of Commerce, thanked project partners including Atmos Energy, TVA, Warren County Water District, Warren RECC, Scotty’s Contracting & Stone, and Presidents Club investors.
Vista Metals’ investment builds on the best four-year period for economic growth in Kentucky state history.

Since the beginning of Gov. Beshear’s administration, more than 1,000 private-sector new-location and expansion projects have been announced totaling over $30.3 billion in investments and creating more than 52,000 jobs.
This investment figure is the highest secured during the tenure of any governor in Kentucky’s history.
The average incentivized hourly wage in Kentucky topped $26 in consecutive years for the first time in 2022 and 2023.
Gov. Beshear has solidified Kentucky as the electric vehicle battery production capital of the United States through several major projects.
Ford Motor Co. and SK On announced a transformative $5.8 billion, 5,000-job BlueOval SK Battery Park in Hardin County.
AESC announced a $2 billion, 2,000-job gigafactory project in Warren County.
Toyota announced a $1.3 billion investment in Scott County.
INFAC North America announced a $53 million investment in Taylor County.
The Governor’s administration secured the largest General Fund budget surplus and Rainy Day Fund, as well as the most jobs filled in state history.
Kentucky set the record for the longest period with the lowest unemployment rates in state history last year.
Kentucky secured rating increases from major credit rating agencies Fitch Ratings and S&P Global Ratings.
Moody’s Investors Service upgraded Kentucky’s credit outlook from stable to positive.
Site Selection magazine ranked Kentucky third nationally and first in the South Central region for economic development projects per capita in its 2023 Governor’s Cup rankings.
Previously, Site Selection placed Kentucky first in the South Central region and top 5 nationally in its 2023 Prosperity Cup ranking for state-level economic development agencies.
Gov. Beshear announced a “Supply Kentucky” initiative aimed at boosting job growth, reducing costs, and providing more security in the supply chains of Kentucky companies.
The Kentucky Economic Development Finance Authority (KEDFA) preliminarily approved a 15-year incentive agreement with Vista Metals under the Kentucky Business Investment program.
The performance-based agreement can provide up to $2.5 million in tax incentives based on capital investment and job creation.
KEDFA also approved Vista Metals for up to $500,000 in tax incentives through the Kentucky Enterprise Initiative Act (KEIA).
KEIA allows approved companies to recoup Kentucky sales and use tax on construction costs, building fixtures, equipment used in research and development, and electronic processing.

Vista Metals is entitled to receive resources from Kentucky’s workforce service providers, including no-cost recruitment and job placement services, reduced-cost customized training, and job-training incentives.
source_title: Gov. Beshear: Vista Metals To Invest $60 Million in Aluminum Casting Facility in Bowling Green, Creating Over 50 Full-Time Jobs | Kentucky Cabinet for Economic Development

entity_name: U.S. Department of Energy Critical Minerals and Energy Innovation Funding Opportunity
canonical_name: Critical Material Innovation, Efficiency, and Alternatives (FOA DE-FOA-0003105)
facts: On May 19, 2026, the U.S. Department of Energy’s Critical Minerals and Energy Innovation announced $45.7 million for 19 projects to address and fill domestic critical minerals and materials supply chain gaps.
The total funding amount for the opportunity is $150,000,000.

Funding specifically focuses on supporting development of novel technologies that will be used in the production of critical materials by developing pilot-scale facilities for processing magnesium and rare earth minerals elements.
According to the U.S. Geological Survey, more than 95% of the U.S. supply for rare earth elements comes from foreign sources.
Over 50% of most critical minerals come from foreign sources.
At least 14 critical minerals come exclusively from foreign sources.

DOE’s Critical Material Innovation, Efficiency, and Alternatives funding opportunity is providing federal funding to build a secure domestic supply of critical minerals from sources across the United States, including ore deposits, mine and industrial waste, and recycled materials.
These projects build on an unprecedented surge in federal support for domestic critical minerals supply chains under the Trump Administration.

In August 2025, DOE announced a series of funding opportunities totaling nearly $1 billion to advance and scale mining, processing, and manufacturing technologies across key stages of critical minerals and materials supply chains.

Two projects selected for negotiation fall under Area of Interest 1B: Phase II – Pilot scale facility development and construction are focused on pilot-scale processing from a continuous bench-scale operation to kickstart pre-commercial/commercial production.
USA Rare Earth is one of the two projects selected for negotiation under AOI-1B.
Big Blue Technologies is one of the two projects selected for negotiation under AOI-1B.

Seventeen projects selected for negotiation fall under Area of Interest 3: Next Generation Technologies and will develop innovative technologies and practices to diversify commercially viable and sustainable domestic sources of critical materials.

AOI-3 technologies focus on optimizing affordability, environmental emissions, and resource usage or intensity within extraction, production, separation, processing, refining, alloying, manufacturing, or recycling technologies.
Southwest Research Institute is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.
Princeton University is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.
Battelle Memorial Institute is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.
National Laboratory of the Rockies is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.
Idaho National Laboratory is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.
Argonne National Laboratory is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.
Columbia University is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.
Ohio University is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.
The University of North Dakota is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.
Savannah River National Laboratory is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.
Iowa State University is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.

The University of Idaho is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.

Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory is selected for negotiation under AOI-3.
Project selections table lists May 19, 2026 for AOI-1B and AOI-3.
Project selections table lists Jan. 15, 2025 for AOI-2.
Project selections table lists Sept. 26, 2024 for AOI-5.
Project selections table lists Aug. 8, 2024 for AOI-4.
The FOA number for this funding opportunity is DE-FOA-0003105.
The responsible office is Critical Minerals and Energy Innovation.

facts: The Department of Energy recognized Volvo Group North America for its leadership in energy efficiency at the New River Valley assembly plant near Roanoke, Va.
Volvo Group is a Better Buildings, Better Plants partner.
Volvo has already achieved 16 percent savings across its U.S. plants.
Volvo continues working toward its goal of becoming 25 percent more energy efficient within ten years.
source_title: At its Largest Truck Plant, Volvo Recognized for Leadership in Energy Efficiency | Better Buildings & Better Plants Initiative

facts: Demonstrate Deploy Decarbonize 2024 (Deploy24) is the second annual gathering of decision makers from across the private and public sectors.
Attendees include senior industry executives, capital allocators, community leaders, and others across the clean energy supply chain.
The event focuses on accelerating the deployment of critical energy and decarbonization technologies and supply chains in the United States.
Deploy24 is hosted by the Department of Energy (DOE).
The event builds private-public dialogue through keynote addresses and off-the-record workshops organized by scalable technology.
Deploy Dialogues were launched as part of Deploy23 to provide an independent platform for dialogue around Pathways to Commercial Liftoff reports and sectors.
DOE will host several Deploy Dialogues throughout 2024, including three at CERAWeek in the Innovation Agora on hydrogen, carbon management, and geothermal.
Additional Deploy Dialogues will be held at Deploy24.
Secretary of Energy Jennifer Granholm announced the Next-Generation Geothermal Power Commercial Liftoff report at CERAWeek.
Information about Deploy24 in-person and virtual participation, exhibitor opportunities, and programming is available online.
source_title: Deploy24 | Department of Energy

canonical_name: Department of Energy Grid Deployment Office
facts: The Department of Energy’s Grid Deployment Office (GDO) is responsible for administering $10.5 billion in Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds through the Grid Resilience and Innovation Partnerships (GRIP) Program.
GRIP’s first focus area includes grants to mitigate storm and natural disaster damage to the grid.
GRIP’s second focus area includes grants to increase transmission capacity and to integrate renewable energy sources and other grid-edge devices.
GRIP’s third focus area includes cooperative agreements to support projects enhancing grid resiliency and reliability.
An inspection was initiated to determine whether GDO had adequate internal controls and resources to implement the GRIP Program.
The inspection found that GDO did not have adequate internal controls and may not have adequate staff resources to implement the GRIP Program.
GDO did not develop and document an effective internal controls system, including the control environment and risk assessment, as required.
GDO followed a phased implementation approach for internal controls.
GDO did not distinguish between program-level and project-specific responsibilities.
Without a robust internal controls system, GDO may not identify risks that could negatively impact the GRIP program’s outcomes.
Potential impacts of inadequate controls include improperly reimbursed costs, fraud, waste, and undisclosed conflicts of interest.

GDO may have difficulty identifying and addressing program performance issues, including the performance of organizations such as the National Energy Technology Laboratory, which GDO depends upon for ensuring GRIP Program success.
Two recommendations were made to address the identified issues, which should help ensure that GDO’s internal controls and resources are adequate for implementing the GRIP Program if fully executed.
source_title: Inspection Report: DOE-OIG-25-19 | Department of Energy

facts: U.S. Department of Energy’s Loan Programs Office (LPO) announced the closing of a direct loan of up to $9.63 billion to BlueOval SK LLC (BOSK).
The loan will finance the construction of up to three manufacturing plants to produce batteries for Ford Motor Company’s future Ford and Lincoln electric vehicles.
One plant is located in Tennessee and two plants are located in Kentucky.
Together, the three facilities would enable more than 120 gigawatt hours of U.S. battery production annually.
The construction phase created more than 5,000 construction jobs as the plants were being built.
The facilities will create up to 7,500 BOSK operations jobs.
This announcement represents the largest loan granted through DOE’s Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing (ATVM) Program.
BOSK is a joint venture between Ford Motor Company and SK On, a world-leading Korean EV battery manufacturer.
BOSK was created to supply Ford with batteries for its growing EV product line.
The announcement reinforces President Biden’s efforts to onshore and re-shore domestic manufacturing to ensure American businesses remain global leaders in future technologies.

Expanding domestic battery production is critical to reducing America’s reliance on adversarial foreign nations like China, meeting climate commitments, and achieving the goal of making half of all new vehicles sold in 2030 zero-emissions vehicles.
LPO borrowers are required to develop and implement a comprehensive Community Benefits Plan (CBP).

As part of its CBP, BlueOval SK partnered with Tennessee College of Applied Technology through the state of Tennessee and Elizabethtown Community and Technical College in Kentucky to construct new community and technical colleges.
These new colleges will train community members for quality jobs at the BOSK facilities.
The announcement is one of several recent LPO loans under the ATVM Loan Program, demonstrating leadership in building a strong domestic EV supply chain.
Through ATVM, LPO provides loans to projects related to fuel-efficient vehicles and eligible component manufacturing in the United States.
Recent ATVM announcements have spanned critical mineral and battery supply chains, involving mineral processing, battery manufacturing, and battery recycling.
LPO has been working with BOSK to finalize its loan since June 2023.
Across all LPO programs, DOE attracted 212 applications for projects totaling over $324.3 billion in requested loans and loan guarantees as of November 2024.
source_title: DOE Announces $9.63 Billion Loan to BlueOval SK to Further Expand US Manufacturing of Electric Vehicle Batteries | Department of Energy

canonical_name: Technology for Recovery and Advanced Critical-material Extraction – Gallium (TRACE-Ga) Program
facts: U.S. Department of Energy’s (DOE) Office of Critical Minerals and Energy Innovation (CMEI) announced approximately $5.4 million for five projects to help establish a secure, domestic supply chain for gallium (Ga).
Gallium is a critical material for the defense and semiconductor sectors.
Assistant Secretary of Energy (EERE) Audrey Robertson stated that the five projects support President Trump’s commitment to strengthen America’s critical minerals supply chains.
The projects demonstrate a focus on restarting domestic primary gallium recovery for the first time in almost 40 years.

Technological breakthroughs from these projects will foster the commercialization of cutting-edge technologies, expand the nation’s critical minerals production capacity, and reduce reliance on foreign sources.
The projects are funded through the Technology for Recovery and Advanced Critical-material Extraction – Gallium (TRACE-Ga) initiative.
The TRACE-Ga initiative will complement other federal investments in the gallium supply chain by rapidly prototyping novel technologies for gallium recovery from U.S. metal processing feedstocks.
Currently, the U.S. is 100% net import reliant on gallium and has not domestically produced the critical mineral since 1987.
Projects are divided into two distinct phases where performers recover gallium at defined cost, purity, and quantity.

PHNX Materials Inc. (San Leandro, CA) will integrate a novel processing pathway to extract gallium from wastes not compatible with traditional flow sheets, producing supplementary cementitious materials, alumina, and fertilizer in addition to gallium.

Atlantic Alumina Company, LLC (Gramercy, LA) will use a novel process (counter-current ion exchange and electrochemistry) with commercially demonstrated and innovative resins to recover gallium at higher temperatures and under continuous operation.

Found Energy Co (Charleston, MA) will use the company’s Direct Bayer Extraction (DBE) technology, a continuous electrochemical process that recovers gallium directly from dilute Bayer liquor without ion-exchange preconcentration of chemical modification of the underlying alumina production circuit.

Kunin Technologies, Inc (Chattanooga, TN) will develop a pathway for producing 12 ton per annum gallium utilizing innovative processing methods to recover gallium from metal streams with highest gallium concentration.
Indium Corporation (Clinton, NY) will develop a capability to recover gallium from recycling scrap from a combination of commercial and innovative processes.

The TRACE-Ga initiative is managed by ENERGYWERX in partnership with DOE, a collaboration made possible through a Partnership Intermediary Agreement set up by the DOE's Office of Technology Commercialization.

This agreement enables ENERGYWERX to broaden DOE’s engagement with innovative organizations and non-traditional partners, facilitating the rapid development, scaling, and deployment of energy solutions.
Selection for award negotiations is not a commitment by DOE to issue an award or provide funding.
Before funding is issued, DOE and the applicant will undergo a negotiation process, and DOE may cancel negotiations and rescind the selection for any reason during that time.
DOE award amounts are subject to change pending negotiations.
The description of PHNX Materials’s project has been updated.
source_title: DOE's Office of Critical Minerals and Energy Innovation Selects Five Organizations to Restart Domestic Primary Gallium Recovery | Department of Energy

facts: Archer is partnering with cities across the country to submit multiple applications to launch initial air taxi operations under the White House’s eVTOL Integration Pilot Program (eIPP), a first-of-its-kind public-private initiative led by the U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) and Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to support the safe integration of air taxis in the U.S. beginning as soon as next year.
The applications coincide with the DOT’s unveiling of its Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) National Strategy today, which lays the framework for integrating eVTOL aircraft into the national airspace.
Archer’s applications to the eIPP are proposed public-private partnerships with cities across California, Texas, Florida, Georgia and New York.
Archer’s application with the City of Huntington Beach is exclusive, meaning it is the only air taxi OEM that is part of that application.
This bid supports Archer's plans for early air taxi operations in and around Los Angeles as part of its role as the Official Air Taxi Provider of the 2028 Olympic Games.
The eIPP was established via President Trump’s “Unleashing Drone Dominance” Executive Order.
The program is designed to accelerate adoption of electric air taxis in major cities across the country by creating operational pathways for the top American eVTOL companies.

Archer’s applications focus on establishing the foundation required for long-term electric air taxi services in select U.S. cities, including building local operations teams, developing or upgrading infrastructure to support eVTOL flights and coordinating closely with public safety and emergency response agencies.

Led by the DOT and FAA, the eIPP is intended to bring together industry, regulators and local governments to establish early eVTOL operations and help U.S. cities prepare for the widespread adoption of electric air taxis that is expected in the coming years.

Archer’s founder and CEO, Adam Goldstein, joined the U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy and other industry leaders in Washington, D.C. as the DOT unveiled the nation’s Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) National Strategy.

The National AAM Strategy outlines a coordinated roadmap to safely integrate electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft into the national airspace, aligning certification, operations, infrastructure and early deployment to enable American companies to move from flight testing into early commercial operations.

The National AAM Strategy and its accompanying action plan outline 40 recommendations to support early operations and advance President Trump’s America First agenda, including strengthening domestic manufacturing, creating high-skilled jobs and reinforcing U.S. leadership in next-generation aviation.

U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean P. Duffy stated that the strategy aims to unlock the future of skies, transform how the flying public travels, assist first responders, improve community healthcare access, enable business goods delivery, and support national defense.

Archer founder and CEO Adam Goldstein stated that the industry has moved past the question of “if” into “when and how,” citing the clearest path to market through close work with the Administration, DOT, FAA and other federal agencies, with a current focus on execution—building, deploying and flying these aircraft in the United States.

Huntington Beach Mayor Casey McKeon commented that partnering with Archer to explore a safe eVTOL demonstration will bring new opportunities to the community and keep the city at the forefront of innovation.
For several years, Archer has worked closely with the DOT, the FAA and other federal, state and local agencies to help shape the path to market for air taxis.

That collaboration has included participation in the Advanced Air Mobility Interagency Working Group, contributions to the development of the Special Federal Aviation Regulation (SFAR) covering air taxis and engagement through the crafting of the eVTOL Integration Pilot Program.

The FAA is expected to review applications submitted during this initial phase of the eIPP and announce selections in early to mid-2026, with early operational activity anticipated to begin later in the year.
Archer is designing and developing the key enabling technologies and aircraft necessary to power the future of aviation.

links.context: SEC filings and Annual Report on Form 10-K

source_title: Archer Moves to Launch Air Taxi Trials In U.S. Cities Under White House Executive Order as DOT Unveils National AAM Strategy

entity_name: Critical Minerals in Atlantic Seaboard Plain
canonical_name: Critical Minerals in Atlantic Seaboard Plain
facts: New projects will join previous awardees in developing alternative feedstocks.

The U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Critical Minerals and Energy Innovation announced $15 million for two projects to establish regional consortia to accelerate the development of new critical minerals and materials supply chains from unconventional and secondary feedstocks.
Assistant Secretary of Energy (EERE) Audrey Robertson stated that building domestic supply chains for critical minerals and materials means realizing the value of unconventional feedstocks.

Domestically abundant sources, such as coal, wastewater from oil and gas development, and acid mine drainage, can reinforce supply chains for American manufacturing and the production of essential technologies.
The two newly announced projects join six previous selectees to build upon the ongoing work of DOE’s Carbon Ore, Rare Earth, and Critical Minerals (CORE-CM) Initiative.
The initiative scales its focus from 13 basins in the continental United States to eight broader regions across the nation, including Hawaii, Alaska, and Puerto Rico.
DOE’s National Energy Technology Laboratory will manage the selected projects.
The Critical Minerals in Atlantic Seaboard Plain project is located in Atlanta, Georgia.
Researchers at Georgia Tech Research Corporation will examine valuable minerals within the Atlantic seaboard plain.
The project focuses on sedimentary minerals such as kaolin, bauxite, heavy minerals, and phosphate, as well as residues from mining and coal combustion.

Samples from former industrial sites (known as brownfields) across the region will undergo detailed analysis to identify their composition, mineral types, critical minerals forms, and rare earth element characteristics.
The resulting data will be used in statistical analyses and machine learning to predict where these minerals occur and to develop efficient extraction methods.
The project aims to build a regional innovation system for a domestic supply of critical minerals.
The initiative aims to foster economic growth by utilizing existing infrastructure and available expert knowledge.
Selection for award negotiations is not a commitment by DOE to issue an award or provide funding.
Before funding is issued, DOE and the applicant will undergo a negotiation process, and DOE may cancel negotiations and rescind the selection for any reason during that time.
DOE award amounts are subject to change pending negotiations.
source_title: DOE’s Office of Critical Minerals and Energy Innovation Launches Regional Consortia To Bolster Domestic Critical Minerals Supply Chain | Department of Energy

facts: CC&C is a U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) partnership that fosters collaboration and innovation to advance transportation energy choices nationwide.
More than 75 DOE-designated CC&C coalitions work in urban, suburban, and rural areas to deliver objective technical expertise based on a unique understanding of local markets.

As partners with DOE’s Transportation Technologies Office, coalitions build bridges between national priorities and local needs to create transportation energy systems that are affordable, reliable, and secure.
Together, coalitions create compounding impacts nationwide that support locally driven energy choices and benefit regional economic development and job growth.
This report summarizes the success and impact of partnership activities based on data and information provided in their annual reports.
Authors: Singer, M.; Johnson, C.; Wilson, A.; Reichelt, L.; Abdullah, M.
source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Publications

facts: The Transportation Technologies Office's (TTO) Technology Integration Program supports a broad technology portfolio that includes alternative fuels, energy efficient mobility systems and technologies, and other efficient advanced technologies to reduce transportation energy costs for businesses and consumers.
The program provides objective, unbiased data and real-world lessons learned to inform future research needs and support local decision making.

The program includes projects to disseminate data, information, and insight, as well as online tools and technology assistance to cities and regions working to implement alternative fuels and energy efficient mobility technologies and systems.

For more than 35 years, the Transportation Technologies Office has sponsored advanced vehicle technology competitions (AVTCs) in partnership with the North American auto industry to educate and develop the next generation of automotive engineers.

TTO's advanced vehicle technology competitions provide hands-on, real-world experience focusing on science, technology, engineering, and math to support the development of a workforce trained in advanced vehicle technologies.
The EcoCAR EV Challenge was launched in 2022 as the latest iteration of the advanced vehicle technology competitions.
The EcoCAR EV Challenge tasked 15 North American universities with engineering a next-generation battery electric vehicle (BEV) that utilizes automation and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) connectivity.

General Motors provided a 2023 Cadillac LYRIQ for each team to demonstrate the potential of advanced propulsion systems, connected and automated vehicle (CAV) technologies, and other innovative technologies to analyze energy efficiency.
The Battery Workforce Challenge was launched in 2023 as a three-year engineering competition.

The Battery Workforce Challenge challenges North American universities and their community college partners to design, build, test, and integrate an advanced EV battery pack into a 2024 Ram ProMaster EV from Stellantis.

The Alternative Fuels Regulatory activity provides technical and analytical support for the implementation of federal legislation related to the deployment of alternative fuels and fuel-efficient fleet vehicles.

Relevant federal legislation includes the Energy Policy Act (EPAct) of 1992, EPAct 2005, the Energy Conservation Reauthorization Act of 1998, the Energy Independence and Security Act (EISA) of 2007, and other amendments to EPAct.
EPAct regulated fleets include State & Alternative Fuel Provider Fleets and Federal Fleets managed by the Federal Energy Management Program.
source_title: Technology Integration | Department of Energy

facts: DOE's Loan Programs Office (LPO) offered a conditional commitment to lend up to $2 billion to Redwood Materials.
The funding is for the construction and expansion of a battery materials campus in McCarran, Nevada.

Once fully operational, the project will be the first domestic facility to support production of anode copper foil and cathode active materials in a fully closed-loop lithium-ion battery manufacturing process by recycling end-of-life battery and production scrap.
Redwood Materials is expected to create approximately 3,400 good-paying construction jobs.
The project will employ approximately 1,600 full-time employees, including labor, technical staff, and on-site management.
The construction workforce will be comprised of union, Minority and/or Woman-Owned Business Enterprises.
LPO works with borrowers to create good-paying jobs with strong labor standards during construction, operations, and throughout the life of the loan.
Borrowers must adhere to a strong Community Benefits Plan.
This commitment builds upon recent Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing (ATVM) loan announcements for Syrah Vidalia, Ultium Cells, and Rhyolite Ridge.

The project supports the Biden Administration’s target of making half of all new vehicles sold in 2030 zero-emissions vehicles, including battery electric, plug-in hybrid electric, or fuel cell electric vehicles.
At full production capacity, the project’s anode copper foil and cathode active material output is anticipated to support the production of more than 1 million EVs per year.
The project will reduce annual gasoline consumption by over 395 million gallons.
The project will avoid more than 3.5 million tons of CO2 emissions each year.
Redwood Materials will use both new and recycled feedstocks comprised of critical materials like lithium, nickel, and cobalt.
The facility will produce approximately 36,000 metric tons per year of ultra-thin battery-grade copper foil for use as the anode current collector.
The facility will produce approximately 100,000 metric tons per year of cathode active materials.
Anode copper foil and cathode active material are the two most critical and valuable components of lithium-ion battery manufacturing for EVs.

Onshoring production of these components is critical to America’s energy independence while ensuring the long-term success and sustainability of an advanced battery manufacturing industry domestically.
To date, nearly all anode and cathode production supporting U.S. battery cell manufacturers occurs in Asia.
This conditional commitment makes Redwood Materials one of the first domestic projects that produces cathode materials at scale for battery cell manufacturing.
The project will support a domestic, sustainable supply of anode copper foil as U.S. EV adoption grows exponentially.
The share of annual battery-powered electric vehicles is expected to rise from about 2% in 2020 to 35% by 2030.
On average, Redwood can recover greater than 95% of critical battery elements in an end-of-life battery (including lithium, nickel, cobalt, manganese, and copper).
Recovered metals are used to manufacture anode and cathode components domestically for U.S. battery cell manufacturers.
The Nevada operation recycles end-of-life batteries from consumer electronics like cell phone batteries, laptop computers, power tools, and other electronic waste.
Lowering the cost of critical materials using recycled materials has the potential to make electric vehicles more accessible and affordable to consumers.
Redwood Materials currently has partnerships with several existing and prospective EV battery manufacturers in the United States.
Off-takers include Panasonic Energy of North America, which is located nearby at the Tesla Gigafactory.
Panasonic Energy has another facility under construction in Kansas that will source Redwood Materials’ cathode and anode materials for battery cell production.
Other partnerships include key U.S.-based and global automakers, domestic suppliers of end-of-life batteries and electronic waste, and others.
Proximity to battery manufacturers’ facilities will drive down logistics costs and emissions.
Proximity will enable faster learning cycles between battery makers and Redwood Materials.
Proximity will accelerate production efficiencies and reduce costs in these component technologies.
The project helps meet the goals put forth by the Biden Administration’s Executive Order 14017 on America’s Supply Chains.
Goals include the promotion of domestic critical materials recycling and manufacturing.
Domestic production of these battery components is paramount to national security, building the supply chain, and strengthening the economy long-term.

Shifting the domestic critical materials supply for lithium-ion batteries to meet future demands using recycled materials will help decouple U.S.-based battery production from a shock-prone international supply chain.
This shift will enable the growth of reliable, large-scale U.S.-based EV battery cell production.
While the conditional commitment demonstrates intent to finance, several steps remain for the project to reach critical milestones.
Certain conditions must be satisfied before the Department issues a final loan.

links.context: Link to learn more about LPO's critical materials projects

source_title: LPO Offers Conditional Commitment to Redwood Materials to Produce Critical Electric Vehicle Battery Components From Recycled Materials | Department of Energy

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy’s Vehicle Technologies Office (VTO) partners with the National Park Service (NPS) through the Clean Cities National Parks Initiative.

The initiative supports transportation projects that educate park visitors on the benefits of shifting to affordable, domestic alternative fuels, advanced vehicles, and fuel-saving technologies and strategies.

Since 2010, VTO and Clean Cities and Communities coalitions have partnered with NPS on 35 projects to put alternative fuel and fuel-efficient vehicles on the road, reduce vehicle idling, and improve vehicle efficiency.
These actions support and coincide with NPS's efforts to reduce vehicle congestion, air pollution, and resource degradation, and to preserve the long-term quality of the parks.
Blue Ridge Parkway worked with the Land of Sky Clean Vehicles coalition to shift to domestic fuel use in the park.
In 2012, Blue Ridge acquired four hybrids and four propane pickup trucks to replace less-efficient vehicles dating back to model year 1989.
The vehicles are helping raise awareness of sustainability among the park's visitors.
The park also installed a propane refueling station.
source_title: Clean Cities and Communities: National Parks Initiative

canonical_name: U.S. Department of Energy Transportation Technologies Office
facts: Plug-in electric vehicles (PEVs), including plug-in hybrid electric and all-electric vehicles, have the potential to decrease transportation costs and will play a key role in the country's transportation future.
The Transportation Technologies Office (TTO) supports work to lower the cost and increase the convenience of PEVs.
TTO collaborates with national laboratories and industry to improve batteries and electric drive systems.
Researchers use simulation and modeling software to create virtual vehicles based on data collected from vehicles on the road and in the laboratory.
TTO works with national laboratories and key stakeholders to advance the development and use of PEV charging infrastructure.
Three broad principles guide TTO efforts for future PEV charging availability to support communities, companies, and others in planning.

The office closely coordinates with other Federal agencies, including the U.S. Department of Transportation, state and local governments, industry partners, and other key stakeholders to serve current and future PEV drivers across America.
Community and fleet readiness activities connect local governments and private fleets with more than 75 Clean Cities and Communities coalitions around the country.

The EV Readiness Scorecard helps communities assess how prepared they are for PEV drivers, while cities are already improving planning, increasing training, and cutting red tape through EV community readiness projects.
TTO partners with organizations and companies to train the American workforce to develop, build, repair, and respond to incidents with these vehicles.
TTO has sponsored student competitions in advanced vehicles for more than 35 years.

TTO's Batteries, Charging, and Electric Vehicles program aims to research new battery chemistry and cell technologies that can reduce EV battery pack level cost down to less than $75/kWh by 2030 while maintaining a vehicle range of at least 300 miles.
The program also aims to decrease charge time to less than 15 minutes.
source_title: Batteries, Charging, and Electric Vehicles | Department of Energy

facts: U.S. DRIVE is a non-binding and voluntary government-industry partnership focused on advanced automotive and related energy infrastructure technology research and development (R&D).
The Partnership serves as a forum for pre-competitive technical information exchange among partners to discuss R&D needs, develop joint goals and technology roadmaps, and evaluate R&D progress.

The Partnership accelerates technical progress by enabling peers in the technical community to discuss pre-competitive, technology-specific R&D needs and challenges, identify possible solutions, and evaluate progress toward jointly-developed technical goals.
The Partnership helps avoid duplication of efforts and ensures that publicly-funded research delivers high-value results and overcomes high-risk barriers to technology commercialization.

Government-industry collaboration benefits from multiple perspectives and unequivocal technical expertise to keep efforts at the forefront of the changing world of automotive and related energy infrastructure technology development.

The Automobile industry partner is U.S. Council for Automotive Research LLC (USCAR), which operates as the cooperative research organization for Ford Motor Company, General Motors Company, and Stellantis.
Electric utility industry partners include DTE Energy Company, Southern California Edison Company, Electric Power Research Institute, American Electric Power, and Duke Energy.
The Federal government partner is the U.S. Department of Energy.
Fuels industry partners include BP America, Chevron Corporation, Phillips 66 Company, ExxonMobil Corporation, and Shell Oil Products US.
Technical focus areas for Vehicles include advanced combustion and emission control, electric drive and power electronics, electrochemical energy storage, fuel cells, and lightweight materials.

Technical focus areas for Fuels include hydrogen production, hydrogen delivery, hydrogen fuel pathway integration, low carbon fuels for future high-efficiency engines, or other mobility fuel as agreed to by the Partnership.
Cross-cutting technical focus areas include analysis, energy efficient mobility systems, hydrogen codes and standards, hydrogen storage, and vehicle-grid interaction.
Information about the Partnership's vision, mission, scope, organizational structure, and operating principles is documented in the U.S. DRIVE Partnership Plan.
Highlights of technical accomplishments are available in the 2023 Technical Accomplishments Report or past years' technical reports.
The Fuels Working Group published 2019 High-Octane reports detailing its work.

facts: Constellium N.V. announced it will construct a new greenfield manufacturing facility in Bartow County, Georgia.
The project responds to growing demand for automotive structures in North America.
Constellium will invest $20 million in the facility.
Developer Seefried Properties will invest an additional $12 million.
The total project value is $32 million.
Construction of the plant will begin in early 2016.
Start of production is anticipated in 2017.
Constellium expects to create approximately 150 high-tech manufacturing jobs at the location by 2019.

Paul Warton, President of Constellium's Automotive Structures and Industry business unit, stated the new Georgia plant demonstrates a commitment to partnering with North American automakers and locating near their assembly plants.

The expansion will allow Constellium to better serve automotive customers and respond to industry demand for aluminum structural parts to lighten vehicles, improve fuel economy, and reduce environmental impact.
The planned facility will be 84,000 square feet.
The facility may be expanded up to 220,000 square feet in the future to adapt to customer supply needs.

Eric Krepps, Vice President and General Manager of Constellium Automotive Structures North America, highlighted Georgia's strong business climate and advanced technical education network for providing a skilled workforce.
Constellium expressed gratitude for the welcome received from the State of Georgia, Bartow County, and various partners.
Constellium is a global sector leader developing innovative aluminum products for aerospace, automotive, and packaging markets.
Constellium generated €3.7 billion in revenue in 2014.
source_title: Constellium to build greenfield manufacturing facility for automotive structures in Bartow County, Georgia, USA

canonical_name: U.S. Department of Energy Transportation Technologies Office
facts: The Transportation Technologies Office (TTO) works with the National Laboratories and industry partners to collect and report data on vehicle performance, research projects, and the transportation sector broadly.

TTO publishes the Transportation Energy Data Book annually in PDF and downloadable Excel formats with Oak Ridge National Laboratory, compiling information on petroleum consumption, vehicle use, transportation technologies, household vehicle characteristics, and fleet characteristics.
TTO publishes a weekly Fact of the Week featuring facts with accompanying graphs or charts to highlight useful statistics.
TTO’s FuelEconomy.gov and the Alternative Fuels Data Center host graphs, maps, and downloadable data sets on vehicle fuel economy, alternative fuel use, and alternative fuel vehicles and stations.

TTO supports the Advanced Vehicle Testing Activity, which collects on-road and in-laboratory data on advanced and alternative fuel vehicles and infrastructure to provide benchmark data for technology models and future research and development.

The Advanced Vehicle Testing Activity has tested technologies including hybrid electric, plug-in electric, neighborhood electric, start-stop hybrid, natural gas, clean diesel, and hydrogen internal combustion engine vehicles, as well as electric vehicle supply equipment and batteries.
source_title: Data and Results | Department of Energy

facts: CTA determined that certain Continental VanContact A/S tires do not fully comply with paragraph S5.5(b) of FMVSS No. 139.
CTA filed a noncompliance report dated September 10, 2021, pursuant to 49 CFR part 573.
CTA petitioned NHTSA on September 30, 2021, for an exemption from notification and remedy requirements on the basis that the noncompliance is inconsequential to motor vehicle safety.
Notice of receipt of CTA's petition was published with a 30-day public comment period on April 18, 2024; no comments were received.
Approximately 419 Continental VanContact A/S tires are potentially involved in the noncompliance.
The subject tires are size LT245/70R17 119/116 Q, manufactured between May 31, 2020, and May 22, 2021.
The noncompliance consists of the tire size designation on both sidewalls missing the prefix “LT”.
Sidewalls incorrectly read “245/70R17 119/116 Q” instead of the required “LT245/70R17 119/116 Q.”
CTA states that aside from the missing marking, the tires contain all required markings and meet FMVSS 139 performance requirements for LT 245/70R17 119/116 Q, Load Range E tires.
CTA contends the missing markings do not impact operational safety because correct load index and load capacities for single and dual fitments are marked.
No higher load tire is specified for size 245/70R17 in the TRA yearbook or ETRTO, meaning the tires could not be mistakenly placed in an overloaded application.
CTA cites a similar noncompliance petition granted to Michelin North America Incorporated (78 FR 30963, May 23, 2013) as supporting precedent.
The subject tires were installed on pre-serial production vehicles that were not sold to end consumers or released into the replacement tire market.
All remaining CTA inventory will be scrapped, and no additional noncompliant tires will be manufactured or sold.
In response to NHTSA email correspondence on November 7, 2022, CTA clarified that Rivian owned, controlled, and operated the subject vehicles.
The tires were sent to Toyota Arizona Proving Grounds for prototype vehicle testing.
NHTSA granted the petition, exempting CTA from notification and remedy obligations under 49 U.S.C. 30118 and 30120.
The exemption only applies to subject tires CTA no longer controlled at the time of the noncompliance determination.
The decision does not relieve tire distributors and dealers of prohibitions on selling or introducing noncompliant tires into interstate commerce after being notified.
Otto G. Matheke, III serves as Director of the Office of Vehicle Safety Compliance at NHTSA.

links.context: Federal Docket Management System website to locate docket number NHTSA-2021-0076

source_title:
Federal Register
       :: 
      Continental Tire the Americas, LLC, Grant of Petition for Decision of Inconsequential Noncompliance

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) released an updated Pathways to Commercial Liftoff report on clean hydrogen.
The original Clean Hydrogen report was first released in March 2023 at the start of the Liftoff effort.
The U.S. clean hydrogen market is poised for rapid growth, accelerated by historic commitments like the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act’s Regional Clean Hydrogen Hubs.
Growth is also accelerated by the Inflation Reduction Act’s Clean Hydrogen Production Tax Credit (45V).
DOE has ongoing programs for research, development, demonstration, and deployment of clean hydrogen technologies.
The Loan Programs Office made a $1.04 billion conditional commitment to a natural gas pyrolysis project with Monolith.
The Loan Programs Office provided a $504.4 million loan guarantee to an electrolysis and energy storage facility with Mitsubishi Power Americas, Magnum Development, and Haddington Ventures.
The Loan Programs Office announced a $1.66 billion loan guarantee to finance up to six electrolysis projects nationwide with Plug Power.
The U.S. is on track to reach 7-9 MMTpa in operational production capacity by 2030.
Developers face higher production cost estimates due to higher electricity prices, installation costs, and cost of capital.
Tax credits like 45V or 45Q can help offset some or all of these increases.
Industrial sectors continue to pull near-term demand for clean hydrogen, although an increasing number of projects will service export markets.
Starting in 2030, new end-use applications for clean hydrogen will emerge.
Promising clean hydrogen production technologies continue to develop, including methane pyrolysis and drilling for geologic hydrogen.
This update marks the fifteenth installment in the Department of Energy’s Pathways to Commercial Liftoff series.

The Liftoff series combines best-available data from across DOE and other federal agencies with on-the-ground insight from the private sector to develop a roadmap for transformative clean energy technologies.
These reports serve as “living documents.”
This update serves as an analytical refresh of the original Clean Hydrogen Liftoff report published in March 2023.
DOE develops its Pathways to Commercial Liftoff reports with extensive stakeholder engagement and system modeling.
DOE continues to solicit input through industry forums, requests for information, and regular interaction.
Direct public input can be submitted via official DOE channels.
The analysis in this report was completed following the publication of the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) for 45V.
The analysis was completed prior to the publication of final rules, which were issued by the United States Treasury Department and Internal Revenue Service on January 3, 2025.
source_title: U.S Department of Energy Releases Updated Report on Pathways to Commercial Liftoff for Clean Hydrogen | Department of Energy

facts: In January 2024, the first commercial production facility for converting ethanol into sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) opened in Soperton, Georgia.
The facility is supported by DOE’s Bioenergy Technology Office (BETO).
LanzaJet’s Freedom Pines Fuel Facility will produce nine million gallons of SAF and one million gallons of renewable diesel in its first year of operations.
Dr. Mark Shmorhun, BETO Technology Manager for Systems Development and Integration, is the lead overseeing the project.
The opening of the Freedom Pines Fuel facility is a major milestone in meeting the SAF Grand Challenge.
The SAF Grand Challenge was launched in 2021 by DOE, the U.S. Department of Transportation, the U.S. Department of Agriculture, and industry partners.
The SAF Grand Challenge aims to produce three billion gallons of SAFs by 2030.
The SAF Grand Challenge aims for SAFs to meet 100 percent of U.S. aviation fuel demand by 2050.
Aviation accounts for roughly 2% of global emissions and 11% of all transportation emissions.
The facility uses a modular design that allows scaling if engineering assumptions are validated or challenges are resolved.
Scaling the project dramatically reduces barriers and risks to production.
The Freedom Pines Fuel Facility serves as a blueprint for innovative ways to produce SAF and reduce emissions.
The plant validates engineering assumptions, cost of production, operating cost, supply chains for raw materials, and economic assumptions.
The facility will define with improved certainty what the cost of these plants will be at much larger scales.
The LanzaJet facility is expected to provide dozens of new quality jobs to Soperton's local economy.
The facility is expected to provide an estimated $5 million in new annual wages and benefits.
The facility is expected to have a total economic impact of $70 million annually.
At peak construction, there were 300 people on-site working every day.
More than 30 full-time employees have been added on the site just to run the plant.
Both LanzaJet and LanzaTech, the subsidiary of LanzaTech, will be in Soperton contributing to SAF production.
LanzaJet is training the local fire department and helping add equipment for them.
Local businesses, including a BBQ place and gas stations, have benefited from the facility's presence.
LanzaJet uses ethanol-based technology to scale production of SAF to reduce emissions through feedstocks.
Globally, there are roughly 30 billion gallons of ethanol production around the world.
In the United States, ethanol comes from corn; in Brazil, it comes from sugar cane; and in India, it comes from a variety of sources.
Ethanol is a plentiful and good feedstock and a good basic chemical for downstream products.
Aviation fuel is a good candidate as an ethanol feedstock.
Dr. Shmorhun has been with BETO since 2016.
Dr. Shmorhun has a background in specialty chemicals and biochemical industries in R&D, engineering, and business development roles.
Dr. Shmorhun received a degree in chemical engineering.
The LanzaJet process took more than a decade from basic bench top experimental work at Pacific Northwest National Laboratory to commercial production.
The process was driven by chemical engineers, chemists, synthetic organic chemists, and physical chemists at national labs.
Detailed engineering requires civil, mechanical, and chemical engineers, plus knowledge of electrical engineering, instrumentation, and controls.
All design is done on computers using computer-aided design and 3D modeling for material and energy balance.
Construction requires specialists in concrete, steel, piping, and cabling.
Manufacturing facilities build vessels, valves, and instrumentation.
Scheduling tracks every hour to sequence activities and minimize overall project cost.
Project controls and budget management require individuals with accounting and finance backgrounds.
Dr. Shmorhun has three decades of experience in the renewable energy field.
Dr. Shmorhun considers being part of the SAF Grand Challenge from the start, through the MOU and crafting language, a career highlight.
source_title: World’s First Ethanol-To-Jet Fuel Plant Paves the Way for Commercial Production of SAF | Department of Energy

facts: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (P.L. 119-21) or Working Families Tax Cut was enacted on July 4, 2025.
This act revised dates and provisions in the Internal Revenue Code that affect some or all of the tax credits described on the page.
The Qualifying Advanced Energy Project Credit (§ 48C) program aims to strengthen U.S. industrial competitiveness and clean energy supply chains.

As the nation builds a net-zero economy, the § 48C tax credit program aims to play a critical role to create high-quality jobs, reduce industrial emissions, and increase domestic production of critical clean energy products and materials.
The Inflation Reduction Act provided $10 billion in new funding under § 48C(e).
At least $4 billion is reserved for projects in certain energy communities with closed coal mines or retired coal-fired power plants.
Credits are allocated to projects in three categories: clean energy manufacturing and recycling, greenhouse gas emission reduction, and critical materials refining, processing, and recycling.
Registered applicants will be able to create, view, and manage their applications through the program's portal.
source_title: Home

facts: Georgia combines a diverse energy mix, world-class research, and a culture of collaboration to drive innovation across the state’s clean energy ecosystem.
The state features top solar production.
The state has expanded nuclear capacity at Plant Vogtle.
The state utilizes waste-to-energy conversion technology.
The state conducts advanced research in battery, hydrogen, and solar technologies.
Georgia Tech ranks No. 1 for Energy among public universities and in the top 5 for materials science and engineering, according to U.S. News and World Report.
Plant Vogtle is the largest generator of clean energy in the U.S.
Unit 4 at Plant Vogtle has been online since 2024.
Plant Vogtle is operated by Georgia Power.
The University System of Georgia and the Technical College System offer specialized programs across the state.
Georgia advances next-gen energy and transportation technology through public-private partnerships and cutting-edge research.
Living laboratories like Curiosity Lab and The Ray test real-world solutions.
Research institutions including Georgia Tech and industry partners drive breakthroughs in batteries, semiconductors, and solar.
Energy storage remains a cornerstone of Georgia’s clean energy future to ensure renewable power is reliable, scalable, and available when needed.
Hyundai’s Clean Logistics Project supports a fully integrated, carbon-conscious energy ecosystem.
Zero-emission fuel production facilities support a fully integrated, carbon-conscious energy ecosystem.
Twelve percent of the state's current energy mix comes from renewables.
Thirty-four percent of the state's current energy mix comes from carbon-neutral sources, including Plant Vogtle and Plant Hatch.
The state connects businesses to resources that turn post-consumer materials into valuable assets.
Post-consumer waste plastics are converted into high-grade fuels in the state.
Large-scale recycling of glass and aluminum occurs in the state.
Next-generation vehicles are rolling off the line at Kia Georgia.
Next-generation vehicles are rolling off the line at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
Georgia is emerging as a national leader in electric and hybrid vehicle manufacturing.
Georgia offers a growing, end-to-end advanced mobility supply chain spanning the state, from vehicle assembly to advanced battery and materials production.
source_title: Energy Technology in Georgia | Georgia

entity_name: U.S. Department of Energy Hydrogen Shot Initiative
canonical_name: U.S. Department of Energy Hydrogen Shot Initiative
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) announced the findings of a report highlighting ways to achieve affordable, abundant clean hydrogen through thermal conversion pathways.
The report is the first of three assessments for the Department’s Hydrogen Shot™ initiative.

The Hydrogen Shot was unveiled in June 2021 as the first goal of the Energy Earthshots Initiative™, a set of eight moonshots to accelerate breakthroughs of more abundant, affordable, and reliable clean energy solutions within the decade.
The Hydrogen Shot seeks to reduce the cost of clean hydrogen by 80% to $1 per kilogram by 2031.
Clean hydrogen is produced with zero or near-zero carbon emissions and supports President Biden’s climate and energy security goals.
U.S. Deputy Secretary of Energy David M. Turk announced the report’s findings at COP28 in Dubai, United Arab Emirates on December 5, 2023.

Turk stated that early in the Biden-Harris Administration, the administration invested in Energy Earthshots to bridge gaps on key clean energy technologies and make clean hydrogen a viable, affordable source of energy.

The Hydrogen Shot Technology Assessment: Thermal Conversion Approaches report was led by experts at DOE’s National Energy Technology Laboratory and Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management, coordinated with the Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Technologies Office.

The report presents a snapshot of thermal conversion pathways for clean hydrogen production, including technology status and envisioned approaches for achieving goals through research, development, and deployment advances.

DOE experts will release two additional reports covering hydrogen production from electrolysis pathways and advanced pathways like photoelectrochemical, solar-thermochemical, and biological hydrogen production.
The report found that hydrogen costs could be reduced to between $1.30 and $1.40 per kilogram through technology advancement.

Beyond RD&D improvements, the report identified plant scale, market scenarios, plant site location, optimization of carbon dioxide transport and storage, byproduct sales, CO2 valuation, and integration with other energy systems as factors for further cost reduction.
The report focuses on producing clean hydrogen through thermal conversion of fossil and/or waste feedstocks with carbon capture and sequestration.
Thermal conversion is defined as a process that uses heat to drive chemical reactions converting carbon-based feedstocks into other fuels and chemical energy carriers.
In October, DOE announced $7 billion to launch seven Regional Clean Hydrogen Hubs across the nation to accelerate commercial-scale deployment of low-cost, clean hydrogen.

These efforts align with the U.S. National Clean Hydrogen Strategy and Roadmap as part of an integrated all-of-government strategy for decarbonization, energy security, resilience, and economic growth.

Achieving the 80% cost reduction goal can unlock new markets for hydrogen in steel manufacturing, clean ammonia, energy storage, and heavy-duty trucks, creating jobs and reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
source_title: DOE Releases First in Series of Reports Highlighting Pathways Toward Clean Hydrogen EarthShot | Department of Energy

facts: Governor Brian Kemp signed HB 1015 into law on April 18, 2024, during a ceremony in Augusta, GA, accelerating the largest state income tax cut in Georgia history.
HB 1015 builds upon HB 1437 to provide a step down of 10 basis points in the individual income tax rate starting in 2025.
The accelerated reduction sets the individual income tax rate for Tax Year 2024 at 5.39 percent, down from the 5.49 percent originally established by HB 1437.
This change marks a 36-basis-point reduction from the Tax Year 2023 individual income tax rate of 5.75 percent.

The Office of Planning and Budget estimates approximately $1.1 billion in taxpayer savings for calendar year 2024 due to the tax cut acceleration and HB 1437's 26-basis-point reduction, with total estimated savings of approximately $3 billion over the next 10 years.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that conservative budgeting and a pro-growth, business-friendly environment will keep billions of dollars in Georgians' pockets rather than funding government bureaucracy.
Governor Kemp simultaneously signed HB 581, HB 1021, HB 1023, and SB 496 into law on the same day.

HB 1023 lowers the corporate income tax rate from 5.75 percent to 5.39 percent for Tax Year 2024 by aligning it with the individual income tax rate, aiming to maintain a business-friendly environment that creates good-paying jobs statewide.
HB 581 enables a constitutional amendment allowing counties to implement a statewide homestead valuation freeze that limits property value appreciation to the inflation rate.
HB 581 also authorizes a special local option sales tax for counties and municipalities to fund property tax relief.
HB 1021 increases the state income tax dependent exemption by 33 percent, raising the deductible amount per dependent from $3,000 to $4,000.
SB 496 expands historic home certification criteria and extends tax credits for historic home rehabilitation to December 31, 2029, and for rural zone revitalization to December 31, 2032.
source_title: Gov. Kemp Signs Historic Tax Cut Package Into Law | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy announced $2.7 billion to strengthen domestic enrichment services over the next ten years.
The funding announcement was made on January 5, 2026.
The investment supports President Trump’s commitment to enhance energy security and reduce reliance on foreign suppliers.
The historic investment expands U.S. capacity for low-enriched uranium (LEU).
The investment jumpstarts new supply chains and innovations for high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU).
The goal of the investment is to create American jobs and usher in the nation’s nuclear renaissance.
Secretary of Energy Chris Wright stated that President Trump is catalyzing a resurgence in the nation’s nuclear energy sector to strengthen American security and prosperity.

The Administration is committed to restoring a secure domestic nuclear fuel supply chain capable of producing the nuclear fuels needed to power the reactors of today and the advanced reactors of tomorrow.
Last year, the DOE signed contracts with a total of six companies for LEU and HALEU enrichment that allowed them to bid on future work.
The task order awards will transition the United States away from foreign sources of uranium and diversify the nation’s domestic fuel supply.
Developing new domestic production capacity for LEU and HALEU ensures an adequate fuel supply is available to maintain operations of the nation’s 94 commercial reactors.
The new capacity builds a strong base to supply future deployments of advanced nuclear reactors.
To ensure accountability, the awards will be distributed to companies under a strict milestone approach.
American Centrifuge Operating was awarded a $900 million task order to create domestic HALEU enrichment capacity.
General Matter was awarded a $900 million task order to create domestic HALEU enrichment capacity.
Orano Federal Services was awarded a $900 million task order to expand U.S. domestic LEU enrichment capacity.
The DOE awarded an additional $28 million to Global Laser Enrichment to continue advancing next generation uranium enrichment technology for the nuclear fuel cycle.
The $28 million award is the result of a competitive solicitation issued last December.

source_title: U.S. Department of Energy Awards $2.7 Billion to Restore American Uranium Enrichment | Department of Energy

facts: X-energy announced the start of vertical construction for its TX-1 fuel fabrication facility in Oak Ridge, Tennessee on November 17, 2025.
The TX-1 facility will be the first of its kind in the United States and will manufacture the company’s proprietary TRISO fuel for commercial reactors.

X-energy subsidiary TRISO-X selected Clark Construction Group for a $48.2 million award as part of the U.S. Department of Energy’s Advanced Reactor Demonstration Program (ARDP) to complete vertical construction.
ARDP funding provides up to 50% cost sharing with X-energy for their Xe-100 Advanced Reactor Demonstration Project, including construction of the TX-1 fuel fabrication facility.
Clark Construction Group will build the shell of a 214,812 square foot facility.
The facility will fabricate around 700,000 TRISO fuel pebbles each year, which is enough fuel for 11 Xe-100 small modular reactors.

TX-1 will be the first of two facilities planned for the site to fabricate the company’s ceramic fuel designed to withstand extreme temperatures and retain fission products under all reactor conditions.
The facility is on track to be the first Category II fuel fabrication facility licensed by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission.
The facility is expected to bring more than 400 new jobs to the region.
Joel Duling, President of TRISO-X, stated that the start of vertical construction marks another significant milestone in bringing their bold vision for the future of nuclear energy to life.
Joel Duling added that TX-1 will stand as a symbol of the team’s relentless dedication and determination to bring this transformative project forward in just a few years, not decades.
The U.S. Department of Energy awarded X-energy $9 million in 2018 to support the initial design of the company's fuel fabrication facility that later evolved into TX-1.
TRISO-X anticipates regulatory approval by May 2026.
TRISO-X recently received approval from the Department to spend an additional $30 million to secure long-lead procurement items to adhere to the overall project schedule.
Operations are projected to start the following year with initial fuel production supporting X-energy’s first commercial reactor.
X-energy’s first commercial reactor is a proposed four-unit plant in partnership with Dow Chemical Company at their chemical plant in Seadrift, Texas.
The TX-1 project is one of two demonstration projects supported by the Department to accelerate the deployment of new reactor technologies.
The proposed Long Mott Generating Station at Seadrift would be the first advanced nuclear facility to power an industrial site in the United States.
source_title: X-energy Starts Building Construction for Advanced Nuclear Fuel Facility | Department of Energy

facts: GEDIA Automotive Group is a family-owned international company headquartered in Germany that specializes in manufacturing state-of-the-art automotive parts.
The company announced it will build an advanced manufacturing facility in Whitfield County, Georgia, marking its first site in the Southeast U.S. and second facility in the United States.
GEDIA will invest $85 million in this project.
The facility will create 200 jobs for the greater Dalton area.
Production will focus on parts for electric vehicles.
Operations are expected to begin in the third quarter of 2021.
The company has more than 100 years of experience and currently supplies lightweight structural automotive parts to Volkswagen, BMW, Mercedes-Benz, Volvo, and numerous other world-renowned companies.
GEDIA will use state-of-the-art press hardening, tempering, and automated welding processes to produce its parts.

The plant's core technology will be a hot-forming line featuring the company's patented TemperBox®, which enables the creation of body components with lower weight and significantly higher crash safety performance while conserving energy and material.
GEDIA will occupy a 180,000-square-foot facility on a GRAD Certified property in Carbondale Business Park.

The Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) certification program requires properties to undergo due diligence and complete specific requirements before a prospective business visits, with over 8,000 jobs created on GRAD sites since the program's inception.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson traveled to Germany to meet with GEDIA leadership and other companies finalizing U.S. market entry decisions, supported by Georgia’s government partners in the Free State of Bavaria.
Director of International Relations Nico Wijnberg represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development on the project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Georgia Electric Membership Corporation, Whitfield County, and Georgia Quick Start.
GEDIA will offer opportunities for management and staff positions, highly skilled technicians, and production workers.
Additional information about the project was expected to be available by the beginning of the fourth quarter of 2020.
Globally, GEDIA employs more than 4,300 people across eight production plants and is involved in joint ventures and research companies.
The company reported an annual turnover of $750 million in 2019.
GEDIA develops and produces lightweight body-in-white automotive parts and assemblies, as well as chassis components.
source_title: German Automotive Parts Manufacturer GEDIA to Locate First Southeast U.S. Site in Whitfield County, Create 200 Jobs | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor
