canonical_name: Economic, Greenhouse Gas, and Resource Assessment for Fuel and Protein Production from Microalgae: 2022 Algae Harmonization Update
facts: A new report from the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) national laboratories investigated the economic and environmental implications of siting and deploying large-scale microalgae farms for cultivation and conversion to biofuels and bioproducts.

The report, “Economic, Greenhouse Gas, and Resource Assessment for Fuel and Protein Production from Microalgae: 2022 Algae Harmonization Update,” was developed by Argonne National Laboratory, National Renewable Energy Laboratory, and Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, with funding from DOE’s Bioenergy Technologies Office (BETO).

The harmonization assessment report focused on the technological potential to leverage non-competing available resources for land, saline water, and industrial waste carbon dioxide (CO2) sources across the southern United States to cultivate high-protein algal biomass for conversion into products like sustainable aviation fuels (SAF) and protein for food and feed applications.

Total microalgae biomass production potential across the United States was estimated at 152 million tons per year (ash-free dry-weight, or AFDW), which reflects a CO2 utilization potential of 268 million tons per year.

This cumulative potential could be enabled by nearly 1,000 viable algae farm sites located across southern regions in the United States, with an average targeted minimum biomass selling price of $674 per ton AFDW.
While higher than projected prices for terrestrial biomass resources, this can be offset by the potential for higher fuel yields and high-value coproducts from algal protein or lipid fractions.

The study estimated that fuels could be produced for less than $4 per gallon gasoline equivalent (GGE) from this biomass resource for cases including co-production of algal protein for the food market (subject to protein market sizes and values), excluding any policy incentives.

When coupled with reduced emissions electricity sources such as wind or solar, algal fuel and protein coproduction could achieve a 50% emissions reduction compared to conventional fuel and soy protein or a more substantial 90% reduction compared with conventional fuel and whey protein.
Meanwhile, a fuel-only algal system with reduced emissions electricity could achieve emissions reductions of 70%.

Under today’s U.S. average grid electricity, algal fuel and protein co-production can achieve a 70% reduction in emissions compared with conventional fuel and whey protein; however, the algal fuel and protein emissions are still higher than those for conventional fuel and today’s highly efficient soy protein production, highlighting interdependencies between reduced emissions fuel and power systems.

Algal SAF fuel potential could reach between 5–9 billion GGE/year (with higher total fuel potential) depending on market limitation scenarios for protein co-production, contributing up to 25% of the 2050 SAF Grand Challenge goal of 35 billion gallons SAF per year.
Such volumes could support roughly 1–2 million hours of flight time on SAF annually for a typical commercial airline.
This latest analysis is reflected in BETO’s recently published 2023 Billion-Ton Report in Chapter 7: Emerging Resources: Microalgae, Macroalgae, and Point-Source Carbon Dioxide Waste Streams.
The analysis may be leveraged to help inform jet fuel production from microalgae supporting SAF Grand Challenge 2030 and 2050 goals.
Footnote 1 defines the southern United States for this study as generally below the 37th parallel latitude between southern California through Texas, Louisiana, and Florida.

Footnote 2 notes that displacing whey protein in the market may not eliminate the emissions associated with its production, as whey may still be produced as a byproduct of cheese production even if algal protein attains a significant portion of the protein market.
source_title: Updated Algae Report Analyzes National-Scale Prospects for Converting Microalgae to Biofuels and Bioproducts | Department of Energy

facts: Hyundai Motor Group announced its first fully dedicated electric vehicle (EV) and battery manufacturing facility in the State of Georgia.
The new state-of-the-art U.S. smart factory will be located at the more than 2,900-acre Bryan County Megasite in Coastal Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group is making a $5.54 billion investment for the project.
Non-affiliated Hyundai suppliers are expected to invest approximately another $1 billion in the project.
The facility will deliver approximately 8,100 new jobs to Georgia's coastal region.
Georgia Power joined Governor Brian Kemp and the Georgia Department of Economic Development to celebrate the announcement on May 20, 2022.
Hyundai Motor Group is headquartered in Seoul, Korea, and employs 250,000 people worldwide.
The company sells vehicles in 193 countries through 5,000 dealerships and showrooms.

Chris Womack, chairman, president, and CEO of Georgia Power, stated that the announcement is historic for the state and highlights the foresight of state and local leaders in creating a megasite for international manufacturers.
Chris Womack also serves on the Georgia Ports Authority Board.
The highly connected, automated, and flexible EV smart factory will produce a diverse range of innovative full electric vehicles.
Through the battery manufacturing facility, Hyundai Motor Group aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV batteries and other EV components in the U.S. market.
Georgia is home to more than 200 automotive-related facilities that contribute more than $3 billion annually to the state's economy.
In 2021, Georgia had 977,000 workers supporting its automotive operations and facilities.
Before this announcement, Georgia's EV and battery production industry had already created 13,200 new jobs and $8 billion in new capital investment.
Georgia is recognized as a leader in electric transportation and is near the top in the nation for EV customer adoption and EV charging, with thousands of connections available across the state.

Georgia Power supports proactive efforts to attract multibillion-dollar companies involved in auto manufacturing production, recycling, and development, as original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) shift product lines to meet increasing EV production demands.

Chris Womack noted that as more Georgians choose electric vehicles, Georgia offers an ideal market for auto manufacturers, suppliers, and technology leaders to collaborate on the future of the EV marketplace.

Georgia Power recently partnered with the University of Georgia's College of Engineering to create an e-Mobility Certificate program covering engineering, public policy, public health, and business acumen to prepare the workforce for electrified transportation.
Georgia Power is working closely with design students at Georgia Tech to evaluate environmental benefits and future workforce opportunities of transportation electrification.
In 2021, Georgia Power joined Southern Company and utilities across the South, Midwest, Gulf Coast, Central Plains, and mid-Atlantic to form the Electric Highway Coalition.
The Electric Highway Coalition enables a coordinated network of charging stations along major highway routes from Texas to Washington, DC to provide easier access to EV options for drivers.
Georgia Power is the largest electric subsidiary of Southern Company (NYSE: SO), America's premier energy company.
Georgia Power serves 2.7 million customers in all but four of Georgia's 159 counties.
The company delivers clean, safe, reliable, and affordable energy at rates below the national average.
Georgia Power maintains a diverse generation mix including nuclear, coal, natural gas, and renewables such as solar, hydroelectric, and wind.
Georgia Power is recognized by J.D. Power as an industry leader in customer satisfaction.

links.context: Information about benefits of electric transportation and charging capabilities

source_title: Georgia Power welcomes Hyundai Motor Group to Coastal Georgia

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) published technical targets for polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) fuel cell components, including membrane electrode assemblies (MEAs), membranes, electrocatalysts, and bipolar plates.
These targets were developed with input from the U.S. DRIVE Partnership, which includes automotive and energy companies, specifically the Fuel Cell Technical Team.
The guideline component targets are designed to assist component developers in evaluating progress without testing full systems.
Additional information on these targets is available in the Fuel Cells section of the Fuel Cell Technologies Office's Multi-Year Research, Development, and Demonstration Plan.
For MEAs, the 2020 cost target is $14/kWnet (down from $17/kWnet in 2015), projected to high-volume production of 500,000 80-kWnet systems per year.
The 2020 MEA durability with cycling target is 5,000 hours (up from 2,500 hours in 2015).
The 2020 MEA start-up/shutdown durability target is 5,000 cycles.
The 2020 MEA performance at 0.8 V target is 300 mA/cm2 (up from 240 mA/cm2 in 2015).
The 2020 MEA performance at rated power (150 kPaabs) target is 1,000 mW/cm2 (up from 810 mW/cm2 in 2015).
The 2020 MEA robustness targets for cold operation, hot operation, and cold transient are all set at 0.7.
For membranes, the 2020 maximum oxygen cross-over target is 2 mA/cm2 (down from 2.4 mA/cm2 in 2015).
The 2020 maximum hydrogen cross-over target for membranes is 2 mA/cm2 (up from 1.1 mA/cm2 in 2015).
The 2020 membrane area specific proton resistance target at maximum operating temperature and water partial pressures from 40–80 kPa is 0.02 ohm cm2.
The 2020 membrane area specific proton resistance target at 80°C and water partial pressures from 25–45 kPa is 0.02 ohm cm2.
The 2020 membrane area specific proton resistance target at 30°C and water partial pressures up to 4 kPa is 0.03 ohm cm2.
The 2020 membrane area specific proton resistance target at -20°C is 0.2 ohm cm2.
The 2020 membrane maximum operating temperature target is 120°C.
The 2020 membrane minimum electrical resistance target is 1,000 ohm cm2 (down from >5,600 ohm cm2 in 2015).
For membranes, the 2020 cost target is $20/m2 (up from $17/m2 in 2015), projected to high-volume production of 500,000 80 kW systems per year.
The 2020 membrane mechanical durability target is 20,000 cycles until >15 mA/cm2 H2 crossover (down from 23,000 in 2015).
The 2020 membrane chemical durability target is >500 hours until >15 mA/cm2 crossover or >20% loss in OCV (up from 742 hours in 2015).
The 2020 combined chemical/mechanical membrane durability target is 20,000 cycles.
For electrocatalysts, the 2020 platinum group metal total content target is 0.125 g/kW (down from 0.16 in 2015).
The 2020 PGM total loading target for electrocatalysts is 0.125 mg PGM/cm2 electrode area (down from 0.13 in 2015).
The 2020 electrocatalyst mass activity target is 0.44 A/mg PGM @ 900 mVIR-free (down from >0.5 in 2015).
The 2020 loss in initial catalytic activity target for electrocatalysts is <40% mass activity loss (down from 66% in 2015).
The 2020 loss in performance at 0.8 A/cm2 target for electrocatalysts is <30 mV (down from 13 mV in 2015).
The 2020 electrocatalyst support stability target is <40% mass activity loss (down from 41% in 2015).
The 2020 loss in performance at 1.5 A/cm2 target for electrocatalysts is <30 mV (down from 65 mV in 2015).
The 2020 PGM-free catalyst activity target is >0.044 A/cm2 @ 0.9 VIR-free (up from 0.016 in 2015).
For bipolar plates, the 2020 cost target is $3/kWnet (down from $7/kWnet in 2015), projected to high-volume production of 500,000 80 kW systems per year.
The 2020 bipolar plate weight target is 0.4 kg/kWnet (up from <0.4 in 2015).
The 2020 bipolar plate H2 permeation coefficient target is <1.3x10-14 Std cm3/(sec cm2Pa) @ 80°C, 3 atm, 100% RH.
The 2020 bipolar plate corrosion (anode) target is <1 µA/cm2 and no active peak.
The 2020 bipolar plate corrosion (cathode) target is <1 µA/cm2 (down from <0.1 in 2015).
The 2020 bipolar plate electrical conductivity target is >100 S/cm.
The 2020 bipolar plate areal specific resistance target is <0.01 ohm cm2 (up from 0.006 in 2015).
The 2020 bipolar plate flexural strength target is >25 MPa (down from >34 MPa for carbon plate in 2015).
The 2020 bipolar plate forming elongation target is 40% (up from 20–40% in 2015).
source_title: DOE Technical Targets for Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cell Components | Department of Energy

entity_name: Korea Trade-Investment Promotion Agency (KOTRA) Atlanta Office
facts: A Korean strategic investment agency announced the opening of a new overseas office in Georgia to promote economic relations.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson and KOTRA Senior Executive Vice President Soo Deuk Sohn announced the opening of the new KOTRA overseas office in Atlanta.
KOTRA is the Government of Korea’s trade and investment organization.
GDEcD and KOTRA signed an MOU to support each other's services and networks and promote cooperation in economic relations, trade, and investment between Georgia and Korea.
Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Korea is an established strategic market for investment, trade, and tourism for Georgia.
Commissioner Wilson noted that KOTRA's decision to open an overseas office in Atlanta will strengthen existing partnerships.
Existing connectivity includes daily direct flights between Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport and Incheon International Airport.
Long-standing agreements exist between the Georgia ports of Savannah and Brunswick and five Korean ports.
These connections facilitate joint efforts to create an innovative electric mobility ecosystem in Georgia.
In early March, Commissioner Wilson visited Korea and met with KOTRA President and CEO Jeoung Yeol Yu.
The mission included meetings with key business representatives from Kia and SK Group.
The mission re-confirmed Georgia's commitment to its Korean companies and international partners.
KOTRA Atlanta will serve as an outpost to support Korean businesses in the Southeastern United States.
KOTRA aims to expand cooperation in many industrial fields between Korea and the U.S. and support Korean companies' local investments.
KOTRA acts as the first point of contact to help Korean companies settle in new markets like Georgia.
The agency provides investment and support resources, including information on programs from state or local agencies like GDEcD.
The new KOTRA office is located at 950 East Paces Ferry Road NE, Suite 999 in Atlanta.
The office will host teams dedicated to facilitating trade and investment opportunities between Georgia and Korea.
This Atlanta office is the eighth overseas KOTRA office in the U.S. and the 10th in North America.
The Republic of Korea has maintained a consular presence in Georgia since 1971.
Yoonjoo Park has served as Consul General since June 2021.
Georgia has maintained continuous representation in Korea since 1985 with a Seoul office headed by Managing Director Peter Underwood.
The state has a dedicated Georgia-based director of Korean Investment, Yoonie Kim.
Several Georgia companies, including American Megatrends, First Advantage Corporation, InComm, Novelis, Unicoi Systems, and UPS, have operations in Korea.
Georgia is home to at least 100 Korean facilities.
Korean-affiliated companies in Georgia currently employ more than 9,400 Georgians.
These Korean-affiliated companies include Kia, SK Battery America, Sangsin Technology America, and Q CELLS Korea.
Since 2020, numerous Korean companies have announced projects in Georgia.
SK Group subsidiary SKC will manufacture glass-based substrates for semiconductor chips in Covington.
SUNGDO ENG USA announced its corporate U.S. headquarters in Georgia.
SUNGDO ENG USA provides highly technical construction services to many Georgia-based clients.
Electric mobility parts and storage systems supplier for SK Group, Duckyang, is opening a new facility in Jackson County.
Brake pad supplier KB Autosys will open its first U.S. advanced manufacturing facility in west Georgia.
The KB Autosys facility aims to serve regional customers including GM, Hyundai, and Kia.
The Georgia Ports Authority in Savannah offers seven regular shipping services to the Port of Busan.
GPA's Brunswick Port offers shipping services to Incheon, Masan, Pyongtaek, and Kunsan.
In 2021, Georgia exports to Korea totaled $860.59 million.
Korea is the 10th largest export market for Georgia.
Top exports from Georgia to Korea include gas turbines, aircraft, medical instruments, chemical woodpulp, and chemical analysis instruments.
In 2021, Georgia imports from Korea totaled $8.83 billion.
Korea ranks third among Georgia's importer nations.
Top imports from Korea to Georgia include motor vehicles, tractor parts, bulldozers, engines, and fork-lift trucks.
KOTRA was established in 1962 to promote trade, facilitate investment, and support industrial technology cooperation.
KOTRA is headquartered in Seoul and operates over 127 Overseas Trade Centers in 84 countries.
source_title: KOTRA opens overseas office in Georgia

facts: State officials announced that a maker of automotive electronics will set up its first U.S. factory in northern Georgia.
Duckyang plans to invest $10 million in the new facility located in Braselton, about 45 miles northwest of Atlanta.
The company expects to create 285 new jobs at the site.
The plant will provide battery parts to SK Innovation, a fellow Korean company that will supply electric vehicle batteries to Ford and Volkswagen.
SK Innovation is building a battery plant in nearby Commerce, Georgia.

Duckyang currently supplies cockpit modules, which are integrated components located near the driver’s seat, to Hyundai, its affiliated brands Kia and Genesis, its parts subsidiary Mobis, and Renault Samsung Motors.

Company officials stated that the initial U.S. facility will provide new opportunities to work with North American automakers, including Kia and Hyundai, which operate assembly plants in Georgia and Alabama, respectively.

Duckyang CEO Dong-in Son said the company expects business expansion opportunities to supply not only electric vehicle parts but also interior parts, including cockpit modules, door trim panel modules, and other flagship parts to global automakers based in the United States.
The Associated Press reported that the company could save up to $1.8 million per year in state income tax credits.
Georgia will provide job training but no additional discretionary incentives for the project.
Local governments could provide property tax breaks for the facility.
source_title: Hyundai’s Automotive Electronics Supplier to Establish First U.S. Factory in Georgia-Machinery Insights Insider MingYang

facts: Duckyang Industrial Co. will invest $10 million to produce battery modules and energy storage systems for SK Innovation's electric vehicle plant in Commerce, Georgia.
A Korean auto supplier will invest $10 million and hire 285 people northeast of Atlanta to make parts that will accompany electric vehicle batteries that SK Innovation will produce nearby.
Duckyang Industrial Co. said the plant in Braselton will make battery modules and energy storage systems for SK Innovation's $2.6 billion, 2,600-worker plant in Commerce.
Duckyang did not disclose when it would start production.
The company will occupy a 230,000-square foot (21,000-square meter) building in an industrial park near an Amazon warehouse.
The company already supplies cockpit modules, a dashboard assembly of more than 130 parts that includes the steering wheel, instruments and electronics, to automakers including Kia and Hyundai.
Kia has a factory in West Point and Hyundai has a factory in Montgomery, Alabama.

Duckyang CEO Dong-in Son said entering the U.S. electric vehicle battery market with SKBA provides an opportunity to cooperate with many other automotive companies, including Hyundai and Kia Motors, that already have a manufacturing base in the U.S.
Dong-in Son said the company hoped to supply interior parts and electrical vehicle parts from Braselton.
Duckyang could claim various tax breaks, including an income tax credit allowing it to annually deduct $1,250 per job from state income taxes, up 50% of its tax liability.
The tax break could save up to $1.8 million over five years, as long as workers make at least $28,000 per year.
Georgia will provide job training but will not provide any discretionary incentives, according to Gordon.
Braselton and Jackson County could give property tax breaks.
Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson said the plant shows how a flagship company like SK Innovation can bring other suppliers in its wake.
Pat Wilson said, "In Georgia, we're focused on growing the entire electric mobility supply chain."
SK Innovation announced it would form a joint venture with Ford Motor Co. to make batteries and related equipment starting in 2025 to meet Ford's battery needs.
The joint venture, called BlueOval SK, would make enough batteries to require about two jointly owned plants.
Ford said that it will need the capacity of 10 factories worldwide by 2030.
SK Innovation said it would spend about $5.3 billion to build the plants, on top of the $2.6 billion it said it is spending on the plant in Commerce.

The U.S. International Trade Commission decided in February that SK stole 22 trade secrets from LG Energy, and that SK should be barred from importing, making or selling batteries in the United States for 10 years.
The dispute was settled in April with SK agreeing to pay $1.8 billion and an undisclosed royalty, allowing it to move ahead with production in Commerce.
source_title: Korean firm to invest $10M, hire 285, to make battery parts

facts: Protestors gathered outside the Hyundai Metaplant in Ellabell, Georgia on Saturday, September 6, 2025.
The protest was organized against ICE.
The gathering occurred in response to an ICE raid at the HL-GA Battery Company on Thursday, September 4, 2025.
The ICE raid resulted in the detention of 475 workers at the HL-GA Battery Company.
Approximately 40 protestors from multiple organizations attended the gathering outside the Hyundai Metaplant.
Nat Villasna led a chant during the protest.
Cesar Mosquera spoke during the protest.
Gerald Green, representing The Party for Socialism and Liberation, spoke during the protest.
Kim spoke during the protest.
Nilson Barahona, a member of ICE Breakers who was previously held in ICE detention for 13 months, spoke during the protest.
Angel yelled into a bullhorn during the protest.
A Bryan County Sheriff's Deputy drove past the protest site.
Protestors held signs reading "Communities Over Cages" and "Ice out of SAV".
The Hyundai Metaplant America is located in Ellabell, Georgia.
source_title: Protestors against ICE gather outside Hyundai Metaplant in Ellabell

facts: Korean supplier Duckyang has announced that it will invest USD10 million toward opening its first US plant.
The new facility will be located in Jackson Country, Braselton, Georgia.
The plant will span 230,000 square feet.
The facility is designed to produce battery modules.
The project will create 285 jobs.
The announcement was reported according to a press release by the State of Georgia website.
source_title: Korean supplier Duckyang to build new plant in US to produce battery modules - Automotive Technology Insight | Forecasts | Industry News | Supply Chain

facts: The state of Georgia offers tax incentives that make investing and expanding business in Georgia attractive.
New and expanding companies may earn the Georgia Job Tax Credit for creating new full-time jobs in Georgia.
A qualifying job must have a 35-hour work week, offer health insurance, and pay more than the county’s average wage with the lowest average wage in the state being $602/week as of June 2022.

The employer generating the jobs must be in a specified industry including manufacturing, warehousing, software development, data centers, contact centers, telecommunications, and research and development.
Employers can use the Georgia Job Tax Credit to offset income tax or to reduce a payroll withholding obligation.
The county in which the job is created determines the amount of the credit, which ranges from $750 - $4,000.
The Investment Tax Credit is available to existing manufacturing and telecommunication companies.
Depending on the county, the Investment Tax Credit ranges from 1% - 5% of qualified investment expenses.
Eligible expenses for the Investment Tax Credit include land acquisitions, improvements, buildings, machinery and equipment.
To qualify for the Investment Tax Credit, a company must have operated a manufacturing or telecommunications facility in Georgia for at least three years and make a minimum $100,000 investment.
Companies in certain designated counties may be able to apply excess Investment Tax Credits to payroll withholding.
The Retraining Tax Credit can mitigate the cost of a company’s investment in new technologies and retraining existing employees on the technology or software.
The Retraining Tax Credit is calculated as 50% of the employer’s direct retraining costs, up to $500 per employee per approved retaining program.
The total amount of the Retraining Tax Credit per employee can be at most $1,250 per year.
The Technical College System of Georgia must approve the training program for it to be eligible for the Retraining Tax Credit.
The Research & Development Tax Credit is available to companies performing research and development in Georgia.

To qualify for the Research & Development Tax Credit, the Georgia facility must be engaged in, or the headquarters of, a specified industry including manufacturing, warehousing and distribution, tourism, telecommunications, broadcasting, and research and development.
The value of the Research & Development Tax Credit is determined based on qualifying expenditures each year in Georgia.

Qualifying expenditures for the Research & Development Tax Credit can include the purchase of materials and supplies, fees for Georgia-based contract work, and the portion of wages and salaries of personnel, direct management, and direct support dedicated to R&D activities that occur in the state.
The Research & Development Tax Credit equals 10% of qualified R&D expenditures over a base amount.

The base amount is calculated by multiplying the company’s current year Georgia sales by the average of the ratios of its aggregate qualified R&D expenses to sales in Georgia for the preceding three taxable years, or 0.300, whichever is less.
If the company had no sales in Georgia in one or more of the three preceding tax years, then multiply the current year Georgia sales by 0.300.
The Employer Child Care Assistance / Facilities Tax Credit is available to employers who provide or sponsor childcare at a state-licensed facility.
The Employer Child Care Assistance / Facilities Tax Credit is equal to 75% of the direct costs associated with the childcare services.
Georgia provides an exemption from sales and use tax for certain expenditures that manufacturing facilities, data centers and distribution centers must make for their operations.

Expenditures eligible for the sales and use tax exemption include manufacturing machinery and equipment, repair to industrial machinery, consumables, energy used in manufacturing, primary material handling equipment, pollution control, clean room equipment, and data centers.
Many counties and municipalities offer a personal property tax exemption for inventory held within Georgia under the Freeport Exemption.
The Freeport Exemption generally applies to manufacturers, distributors and fulfillment centers.
The exemption amount for the Freeport Exemption varies by county and municipality.
Companies can claim the Freeport Exemption with the filing of their annual business personal property tax return.
source_title: 2022 Tax Incentives | Atlanta CPA Firm

facts: Enchem America, Inc. has canceled plans for a $152.2 million EV battery facility in Brownsville.
The cancellation occurs two years after the company initially announced construction plans for the new EV battery supplier.
In June 2023, South Korean officials announced intentions to construct the facility.
The planned facility was intended to offer 190 jobs.
The site is located in the I-40 Industrial Park.
The I-40 Industrial Park is located near Ford Motor Company’s BlueOval City in Haywood County.
In August 2025, Enchem delayed construction on the facility prior to its cancellation.
State Rep. Chris Todd attributed the project cancellation to market changes in the EV and battery markets caused by shifting consumer demand.
State Rep. Chris Todd noted that consumers' purchasing preferences change regularly, requiring manufacturers to build what people want to buy.
State Rep. Chris Todd stated that the property is a certified site through the state's site development program and should revert back to the community for marketing to other companies.
State Rep. Chris Todd explained that parameters around certified sites help prospects know what they are getting and how to plan for their facilities.
source_title: EV battery manufacturer Enchem America cancels plans for $152.2 million facility in Brownsville - Talk-N West TN

facts: Georgia’s biggest-ever foreign investment could be poised to grow even larger.

SK Innovation, the Korean oil refiner pivoting into electric vehicle batteries, has announced that it will build a second factory on its site in Jackson County and increase its capital investment to $2.5 billion by the time they’re both operational in 2023.
The company broke ground on the first facility in March 2019.
It had always planned to build in multiple phases in the city of Commerce, with two plants of 1 million square feet apiece set to create a combined 2,000 jobs.

Planned spending initially was pegged at $1.67 billion, which already dwarfed any other single foreign investment project in the state’s history, even the Kia Motors plant that came to West Point more than a decade ago.

Reports from Reuters and other news outlets said the company would invest an additional $727 million on top of $903 million spent on the plant so far, putting its cumulative expenditure close to the budget announced originally.
A news release by SK Innovation clarified that its board of directors had approved plans to spend some $2.5 billion overall.
The investment is aimed at preparing for long-term growth in electric vehicles, despite drastically reduced oil prices and the coronavirus-induced economic slump.
The first plant will be online in 2022 and was expected to have 9.8GWh capacity.

It was reportedly aimed at supplying EV models set to be produced just across the state line at the Volkswagen plant in Chattanooga, Tenn., where the German auto maker is spending $800 million to prepare for new electric models.
The second SK battery factory will more than double the company’s output in Georgia to 21.5 GWh.
The two plants here would represent about 30 percent of SK’s planned 71GWh capacity globally, including plants in Hungary and Korea.

SK Innovation CEO Jun Kim, who visited Georgia last March, said: “While the global community faces challenging times, SK Innovation believes it is important to continue making strategic investments to drive economic growth and meaningful change.”

Jun Kim added: “With this investment, SK Innovation’s battery business will significantly contribute to not only the local Georgia economy but the development of the U.S. EV industry value chain and ecosystem.”
Sources had shared with Global Atlanta that a second plant may be in the works, though the rumors weren’t able to be independently confirmed.
SK’s plant is already spurring ancillary investment by related suppliers.
Gov. Brian Kemp traveled to Korea last June and then to Germany in January.
On the latter trip, Kemp announced EnChem would invest $61 million in two factories in Jefferson to make electrolytes for SK’s batteries.
It is unclear whether the new capital will generate additional jobs.
A spokesperson said the Georgia Department of Economic Development does not comment on “active projects.”
The company said it could eventually invest up to $5 billion in the U.S. and create 6,000 jobs, but not all of that need come to Georgia.
SK meeting its projections is not guaranteed due to a legal battle with Korean rival LG Chem in the U.S.
The legal battle could stop SK Innovation from importing EV batteries and components.
source_title: SK to Build Second Georgia Battery Plant, Increase Investment to $2.5B  - Global Atlanta

facts: Enchem Co., a South Korean battery material maker, has scrapped its plan to invest $143 million to build a plant in the US.
The scrapping is due to the protracted downturn in the world's third-largest electric vehicle market.
Enchem is the world's No. 4 electrolyte manufacturer.
Enchem cancelled its plan to spend 200 billion won ($142.7 million) to construct a production base in Tennessee.
The Tennessee plant was intended to supply electrolytes to BlueOval SK LLC.
BlueOval SK LLC is a joint venture between South Korea's battery maker SK On Co. and US automaker Ford Motor Co.
Supply deals for the Tennessee plant were expected to be signed last year but were repeatedly delayed.
Ford's EV business remains sluggish.
Ford's EV division has lost $12 billion in total.
Ford's EV division lost $2.2 billion in the first half of 2025.
US President Donald Trump's policies are boosting internal combustion engine vehicles and hurting the EV industry.
The US administration eliminated the tax credit benefit of up to $7,500 for EV buyers starting this month.
Ford CEO Jim Farley stated that the elimination and softening of emissions rules will sharply curtail EV demand in the US.
Jim Farley predicted the share of zero-emission vehicles, currently around 10% of the domestic market, could fall by half.
The policies are expected to affect both the EV industry and the battery sector.
BlueOval SK's total capacity is likely to rise to 127 GWh by next year.
BlueOval SK is predicted to underutilize its facilities.
An industry source in Seoul stated that all companies involved in the local EV value chain with Ford are now revising their strategies.
Instead of the Tennessee project, Enchem plans to focus investments on its Georgia facility.
Enchem's Georgia facility supplies electrolytes to LG Energy Solution Ltd.
Enchem also has an Indiana plant that serves Samsung SDI Co.
source_title: Korea’s battery material maker Enchem scraps $143 mn US plant plan - Open Korea

facts: Enchem is a secondary battery electrolyte company.
Enchem will become the first Korean battery materials firm to sign a long-term supply agreement with Contemporary Amperex Technology Co. Limited (CATL).
CATL is the world’s largest battery manufacturer.
Enchem announced on December 23 that its board of directors approved a five-year contract with CATL to supply a total of 350,000 tons of electrolyte.
The supply period will run from 2026 to 2030, with an annual average of 70,000 tons.
Based on current market prices, the contract is valued at approximately 1.5 trillion won.
This amount is more than four times Enchem’s consolidated sales from the previous year.
The annual average supply volume of 70,000 tons far exceeds Enchem’s 2023 annual electrolyte supply of about 50,000 tons.
In terms of a single client, this is the largest contract in Enchem’s history.
While Korean battery companies have struggled to enter the Chinese market, Enchem’s contract to supply electrolyte for CATL’s domestic Chinese production is highly significant.
CATL holds a nearly 38% share of the global electric vehicle (EV) battery market.
CATL supplies batteries to major global automakers in key regions including China, North America, Europe, and Southeast Asia.
Through this agreement, Enchem plans to expand supply beyond China to CATL’s new global production bases in Europe, the United States, and Southeast Asia.
The contract will involve production and supply from Enchem’s Korean and overseas manufacturing sites according to the customer’s global facility requirements.
Revenue generated under this contract will be recognized by Enchem’s Korean headquarters and overseas subsidiaries and reflected in the parent company’s consolidated financial statements.
Full-scale electrolyte supply will begin in the second quarter of 2026.
Enchem expects to generate additional annual sales of over 300 billion won from this contract.
There is potential for further sales growth in regions such as Europe, North America, and Southeast Asia beyond the Chinese market.
An Enchem representative stated that the contract is an official acknowledgment of Enchem’s technological capabilities and global production capacity by CATL.
Enchem plans to seek further opportunities to expand supply across global markets while continuing to pursue sustainable growth and enhanced competitiveness.
source_title: Enchem Becomes First Korean Materials Firm to Secure Deal with China's CATL... Supplying 350,000 Tons of Electrolyte - The Asia Business Daily

facts: Expanding factories around the world to meet needs caused by rapid increase in demand and strategic mid- and long-term management plan to occupy the market in advance.
Completing the global supply network and initiating new business plans such as internalization of raw materials, NMP refining business, and sustainable businesses.
Aiming for complete localization and internalization of raw materials.
Enchem's revenue grew 10 times by 2016 and accomplished another 10 times growth in 2021 compared with other electrolyte production companies for the same period.
Achieved the world’s first commercialization of electrolyte used in the GM Bolt GEN 2 (second generation electric motor vehicle that enables to run more than 200 miles) in 2013.
source_title: Enchem

facts: This Clean Economy Works (CEW) analysis is part of E2’s ongoing monthly tracking of large-scale clean energy project announcements, cancellations, closures, and downsizes across the United States.
The analysis monitors private-sector investment in clean energy manufacturing, generation, and grid infrastructure projects since federal energy tax credits were passed in August 2022.
CEW methodology excludes projects that began, were proposed, sited, or in any way began development prior to the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
CEW excludes projects funded entirely by federal sources or lacking specific geographic data.

CEW measures key indicators including investment value, job creation or losses, project types (manufacturing, generation, research and development), and distribution by sector, state, and congressional district.

Since 2025, the analysis began to include all project cancellations, closures, and downsizings going back to August 2022 due to rising business uncertainty about the future of U.S. clean energy policy.
The tracking culminated in the rollback and restriction of energy tax credits included in the federal tax and spending bill passed in July 2025.
E2’s methodology excludes temporary delays or ownership transfers that do not impact production capacity.

In October, companies canceled, closed, or downsized nine major clean energy projects that would have otherwise resulted in more than $4.4 billion in economic investments and an estimated 8,700 new jobs.
During the same month, only one new project was announced: a battery storage operation in Pennsylvania.
Year-to-date through October 2025, companies canceled, closed, or scaled back 51 large-scale clean energy projects according to E2 analysis of projects tracked by E2 and the Clean Economy Tracker.
The cancellation of these 51 projects represents the loss of nearly $28.8 billion in private investment in new factories and projects nationwide.
The cancellations represent the loss of nearly 30,000 new jobs that were previously announced.

Nine recent project abandonments and downsizes in October include the loss of more than $4.4 billion in investments and at least 8,700 previously announced jobs at major EV, battery, and other clean energy related factories in Mississippi, Tennessee, Michigan, Ohio, and elsewhere.
Nissan (MS) lost 2,000 jobs and abandoned $500M on 9/30.
Enchem (TN) lost 190 jobs and abandoned $152.5M on 10/1.
General Motors (OH) lost 1,520 jobs and abandoned $760M on 10/2.
GAF Energy (CA) lost 138 jobs and closed its R&D facility on 10/15.
Gotion (MI) lost 2,350 jobs and abandoned $2.36B on 10/24.
GM (MI) downsizing impacted 1,200 jobs with an undisclosed investment reduction on 10/29.
GM/LG Energy Solution (OH) downsizing impacted 550 jobs with an undisclosed investment reduction on 10/29.
Xalt Energy (MI) lost 550 jobs and abandoned $600M on 10/31.
Dana (MI) lost 200 jobs and abandoned $54.2M on 10/21.
Fifty-one large-scale clean energy projects have been canceled, closed, or downsized in 2025, which is more than any previous year since the IRA passed.
These 51 projects were expected to create 29,944 new jobs and included $28.77 billion in planned investments that are now lost.
Manufacturing facilities account for nearly all losses, accounting for 45 of the 51 projects abandoned in 2025 and over 29,000 jobs and $24.2 billion in investment lost.
EV and battery/storage projects continue to drive the majority of cancellations: 24 EV projects and 25 battery/storage projects have been abandoned since 2025.

Republican congressional districts experienced the greatest economic fallout with 37 canceled projects, 21,916 lost jobs, and $16.9 billion in abandoned investments compared with Democratic-held districts.
Democratic-held districts saw 24 projects, 13,248 jobs, and $9.9 billion in lost investment.
13 projects, 4,378 jobs, and $4.6 billion in lost investment occurred where the specific congressional district is yet to be determined.
Just a single new clean energy project announcement was made in October, marking the lowest number since E2 began tracking announcements in August 2022.
Eos Energy Storage (PA) announced a battery/storage manufacturing plant expected to create 1,000 jobs and result in $352.9 million in investments.
Since E2 began tracking projects in August 2022, companies have announced a total of 417 major clean energy manufacturing, generation, and grid projects across 42 states and Puerto Rico.
These announced projects represent 124,241 planned jobs and about $132.33 billion in total investment.

The U.S. clean energy economy is entering a period of instability driven largely by shifting federal policies, new federal roadblocks to clean energy development, and the rollback of clean energy tax incentives.
Announcements tracking excludes projects that began development, were proposed, or applied for local and state approval before the passage of the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
Tracking does not include investments in which the federal government has provided financial resources for the complete project, lease sales, or projects lacking specific geographic information.
Cancellations/closures tracking includes all projects canceled or closed since the IRA passage in August 2022.
The list excludes announced layoffs that are not associated with a project downsizing unless there is a stated decrease in production output.
The list excludes transfer of project ownership if production will continue under new ownership, power purchasing agreements, or similar announcements.
Project delays or idling of facilities are not included unless there is an announced decrease in production or investment or the project needs to be restarted to proceed in the future.
source_title: Clean Economy Works | October 2025 Analysis | E2

facts: Carroll Daniel is a construction and engineering firm based in Gainesville, GA.
The company maintains offices in Atlanta, Greenville, Mobile, Nashville, and Savannah.
Carroll Daniel provides project planning, engineering, procurement, and construction services for industrial and public markets across the Southeastern U.S. and beyond.
The firm is currently operating under its third generation of family leadership.
Carroll Daniel consistently ranks among the nation’s Top 400 Contractors and Engineering firms according to ENR Magazine.

The company has earned multiple awards including ENR Top 400 Construction Firms, ENR Top 500 Design Firms, Design-Build Institute of America Award Winner, Design-Build Southeast Multi-Award Winner, Build America Multi-Award Winner, Construction Management Association of America Multi-Award Winner, Construction Management Association Southeast Multi-Award Winner, Associated Builders & Contractors Safety Excellence Award Winner, and Business Facilities Deal of the Year: Impact Award Winner.

Carroll Daniel's core values emphasize Relationships (servant leadership and client advocacy), Integrity, Stewardship (investing in employees and supporting local communities), and Excellence (passion for building and dedication to quality).
source_title: About Carroll Daniel

facts: GrowHub Ltd shares surged approximately 541% to $2.23 (reaching an intraday high of $2.95) on massive volume of roughly 88.5 million shares on Monday, June 1, 2026.
GrowHub filed a Form 6-K with the SEC signaling a strategic pivot via a binding term sheet for a reverse merger with EnChem America.
EnChem America is a U.S. subsidiary of South Korea's EnChem Co Ltd that develops and manufactures battery electrolyte materials.
Under the proposed deal, EnChem would merge into a newly created subsidiary of GrowHub, with the private company emerging as the surviving entity.
EnChem America's shareholders would own 85% of the combined company on a fully diluted basis, while existing GrowHub holders would retain 15%.
The merger assigns a minimum valuation of $400 million to EnChem America.
The term sheet includes a condition allowing EnChem to walk away if GrowHub's volume-weighted average price (VWAP) falls below $4.00 per share at closing.

Transaction milestones include finalizing a definitive merger agreement, securing shareholder approval under Nasdaq rules, obtaining Nasdaq listing approval, completing PCAOB-standard audits for EnChem, and having the SEC declare registration statements effective.
All current GrowHub directors and senior executives are expected to resign at closing, with EnChem's nominees taking control of the board and management.
On May 26, GrowHub received a Nasdaq notice indicating it fell below the minimum stockholders' equity requirement for continued listing on the Nasdaq Capital Market.
GrowHub has until July 10 to submit a plan to regain compliance with Nasdaq.
GrowHub's 2025 annual report revealed a net loss of $13.4 million and a shareholders' deficit of $2.3 million, with revenue dropping sharply to $64,574 from $237,014 the prior year.
The proposed merger represents a dramatic shift in GrowHub's business focus from a blockchain-based supply-chain traceability platform to battery electrolyte materials.
The deal aligns with a broader push by Korean battery materials companies to capture market share in the U.S., as Chinese players face supply chain restrictions.
EnChem CEO Oh Jung-Kang noted in a 2024 interview that Chinese competitors are 'cut off from the American supply chain,' creating a 'window of opportunity for Korean companies.'
Current GrowHub shareholders will see their ownership stake reduced to just 15% if the deal proceeds, highlighting significant dilution risks.
source_title: GrowHub Shares Surge 541% on EnChem Reverse Merger Filing | StockTi

facts: The company/division name is Enchem America.
The parent company is Enchem Ltd.
The type of work is manufacturing.
The company operates as a contract manufacturer.
The reshoring category is Foreign Direct Investment.

facts: GaMEP works with manufacturers across Georgia to implement on-site projects, conduct training, and build connections with Georgia Tech resources and partners throughout the state.
The organization divides the state into ten regions led by region managers who live and work within the region they serve.
GaMEP project managers stay updated on the latest tools and technology to help manufacturers think strategically, operate more efficiently, and grow at a sustainable pace.
Services offered include Business Health & Strategic Planning, Leadership & Team Development, Human Resources, and Technology.
Technology project managers provide expertise in robotics, automation, artificial intelligence, sensors and data, and more.
The program assists small manufacturers with few existing technology applications as well as mid-size to large manufacturers looking to add advanced systems to their plants.
GaMEP offers Advanced Manufacturing services to help companies successfully implement and sustain advanced manufacturing automation and equipment projects.
Industry 4.0 experts assist manufacturers in identifying and applying data and artificial intelligence solutions to maximize productivity and boost revenue growth.
Cybersecurity services help companies identify electronic security risks and develop a cybersecurity plan.

facts: Sierra International Machinery is adding a 24,000-square-foot expansion at their manufacturing facility in Jesup, Georgia.
The expansion is due to overwhelming demand for the Sierra REB line of two-ram balers and conveyors.
This expansion will allow Sierra the ability to increase production and meet the demand for the full Sierra product line.
This is the third expansion Sierra has added to its Georgia facility since it opened in 2008.
John Sacco, President and Owner of Sierra International Machinery, announced the expansion of their facility in Jesup, Georgia.
The success of Sierra's two-ram balers and conveyors in the waste sector has grown exponentially, far more than initially anticipated.
Growth can be largely attributed to the new REB-4 two-ram baler that has surpassed all expectations.
The expansion was necessary to meet the demand for the Sierra catalog.
Every machine in the Sierra product line passes through the Georgia facility.
Every Sierra two-ram baler and conveyor is fully engineered, manufactured, and assembled in Georgia.
The Georgia facility is the birthplace of the Sierra two-ram balers.
Sierra offers three different two-ram models, including the patent pending REB-4 with Dual-Compression Doors, and many conveyor models.
Once completed, Sierra’s Georgia manufacturing facility will be 72,000 square feet.
The expansion will provide increased job opportunities in the Jesup, Georgia area.
source_title: Sierra breaks ground at Georgia manufacturing facility on 24,000-square-foot expansion - Expansion Solutions

facts: Hyundai Motor Group’s sprawling Metaplant campus is located west of Savannah.
Roads around the campus bear the names of the South Korean automaker’s brands: Hyundai, Kia and Genesis.
Models bearing two of those nameplates are now being manufactured inside the factory.
Kia recently began production of a hybrid SUV, the Sportage, marking U.S. manufacturing of the hybrid version of the model for the first time.
Hybrid Sportages were previously made exclusively in Asia.
SeungKyu Yoon, president of Kia North America, stated, “We want to produce where we sell.”
The launch expands the Georgia factory’s portfolio beyond two electric vehicle models.
The plant opened in October 2024, making the Hyundai Ioniq 5.
Manufacturing of a second EV, the Hyundai Ioniq 9, began last year.
South Korea has announced a long-term investment of $5.5 billion to this site.
source_title: Georgia Hyundai EV plant adds Kia Sportage to production, ups target

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is deepening its US manufacturing footprint with a $21bn investment through 2028.
The Group is scaling production, localising components and developing future mobility and energy technologies to strengthen industrial resilience.
Hyundai Motor Group has announced a $21 billion investment across its US operations, signalling its most ambitious expansion in North America to date.

Between 2025 and 2028, the South Korean industrial group aims to accelerate vehicle production, localise critical components, strengthen its supply base and develop the technologies shaping the future of mobility.
The scale of the investment reflects a deepening strategic alignment between the Group and US industrial policy.
Since its entry into the US market in 1986, Hyundai has committed over $20.5 billion in American operations.
A further $6 billion has been earmarked to localise automotive components and increase supply chain robustness.
The company stated that its investment and efforts will further expand operations in the U.S., grow the American workforce, and stand with American partners, employees, and communities.
The largest share of the new investment – $9 billion – will support a significant expansion of automotive production across Hyundai Motor Group’s US facilities.
The Group plans to achieve an annual production capacity of 1.2 million vehicles across its Hyundai, Kia and Genesis brands.
Production facilities in Alabama and Georgia will be upgraded, including Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Autoland Georgia.
These enhancements are expected to improve quality and operational efficiency while reinforcing the company’s ability to adapt to shifting customer demand.
This week, Hyundai is also expected to officially open its new Metaplant in Bryan County, Georgia.
Known as Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA), the facility marks a milestone in US EV localisation.
The $7.6 billion site is the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history and was completed in just two and a half years.
It is expected to support 8,500 direct jobs and over 570,000 jobs indirectly across the US supply chain.
The plant will begin trial production in late 2024, with full EV output ramping up in 2025.
The Group will focus on scaling production of high-value parts, including battery packs for electric vehicles, and forming an integrated auto cluster around its growing US production base.
Hyundai Steel, a key affiliate, will build an Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) steel mill in Louisiana, with an annual capacity of 2.7 million tonnes.
The facility will use locally sourced steel scrap to produce low-carbon steel sheets, providing both environmental benefits and greater supply chain control in a volatile geopolitical environment.
The Group is also investing in logistics infrastructure to support these efforts, aiming to reduce dependencies and increase regional integration.
$6 billion will support Hyundai’s push into future mobility technologies and energy infrastructure.
This includes partnerships in robotics, artificial intelligence, autonomous driving, as well as advanced air mobility (AAM).
Key initiatives include expanding the US robotics ecosystem with Boston Dynamics, developing AI mobility solutions with NVIDIA, and advancing R&D with Supernal, Hyundai’s AAM affiliate.
Supernal plans to commercialise an electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft by 2028.
Hyundai will also continue its collaboration with Waymo, supplying robotaxis, and deepen its ties with Aptiv in developing autonomous driving services.
Through venture funding and strategic investments, the Group aims to bolster American startups in mobility, robotics and AI.
Alongside industrial and technological ambitions, Hyundai is committing significant funds to the development of energy infrastructure.

This includes the expansion of electric vehicle charging networks through the IONNA alliance and the advancement of Small Modular Reactor (SMR) technologies in partnership with Holtec International and Hyundai Engineering & Construction.
Such efforts are designed to support the Group’s electrification strategy while contributing to broader US goals for decarbonisation and energy independence.
The Group expects its investment to create 14,000 direct full-time jobs by 2028.
Over 100,000 total direct and indirect job opportunities are anticipated across manufacturing, technology and infrastructure.
With this latest move, Hyundai Motor Group extends its US footprint and deepens its role in shaping the country’s industrial, technological and energy transformation.
source_title: Hyundai to Invest $21 Billion in US Auto Manufacturing | Automotive Manufacturing Solutions

facts: Electric vehicles (EVs) are upending the status quo of internal combustion vehicles (ICE).
Fueling the shift to electric power requires next-generation battery technology and an ample supply of lithium.
Lithium is the key raw material for lithium-ion batteries.
The industry’s ecosystem starts upstream with lithium miners that extract the metal from the earth.
Raw materials move into the chemical conversion process to produce lithium carbonate or lithium hydroxide.
Battery producers combine carbonate or hydroxide with materials to form a cathode and an anode, together forming an individual battery cell.
Thousands of cells may be combined to create a battery pack for an EV.
Ganfeng Lithium is a leading Chinese lithium mining company that has evolved into refining and processing lithium, battery manufacturing, and recycling.
Panasonic is a top-3 global EV battery manufacturer from Japan.
Livent is a top-5 lithium producer from the US.
Contemporary Amperex Technology Limited (CATL) is a top-3 EV battery manufacturer from China.
Ganfeng Lithium along with Albemarle, SQM, Tianqi Lithium, and Livent control more than 70% of the world’s lithium supply.
Ganfeng was established in 2000.
Ganfeng is the third-largest lithium compound producer in the world and the leading producer in China.

Ganfeng covers a wide swath of the lithium-ion battery supply chain, including lithium resource development, refining & processing (75% of total revenue), battery manufacturing (17% of total revenue), and battery recycling & other (8% of total revenue).
Ganfeng has a broad international footprint with lithium operations in Australia, Argentina, Mexico, and Ireland, in addition to its resources throughout China.
Mount Marion in Western Australia is Ganfeng’s primary source of lithium.
In Australia, Ganfeng uses hard rock lithium extraction processes.
In Argentina, Ganfeng extracts lithium via a brine process as is common in Latin America’s Lithium Triangle (Argentina, Bolivia and Chile).
In Mexico, Ganfeng is exploring new lithium clay extraction methods with an expected production start in 2023.
Ganfeng boasts long-term supply contracts with major battery producers and OEMs like Tesla, Panasonic, LG Chem, Volkswagen, Samsung, and BMW.
Ganfeng expects to double its capacity from 100,000 metric tons in 2020 to 200,000 by 2025.
Surging EV demand should help companies like Ganfeng secure long-term contracts at higher prices.
Ganfeng is focused on growing its lithium hydroxide production and sales because it is better suited than lithium carbonate for next-generation EV battery technology.
Batteries with NMC 811 cathodes and other nickel-rich batteries require lithium hydroxide.
By 2021, Ganfeng expects to produce and sell 50,000–60,000 metric tons of lithium hydroxide and 20,000–30,000 metric tons of lithium carbonate.
Panasonic has more than 100 years of history and is the world’s third-largest supplier of EV batteries.
Panasonic is considered a Tier 1 lithium-ion battery producer according to Benchmark Mineral’s classification standards for automotive uses.
In 2014, Panasonic and Tesla partnered to build Giga Nevada, the world’s largest lithium-ion battery factory.
Panasonic started working on Tesla’s featured battery cell called 4680.
The 4680 format is expected to store more energy and have an easier manufacturing process to further reduce battery costs.
A battery represents approximately 29% of the total cost of an EV.
Panasonic is the sole provider of lithium-ion batteries to Tesla for EVs manufactured in the U.S.
Panasonic recently partnered with Toyota to build a lithium-ion plant in Japan to supply 500,000 EVs starting in 2022.
Livent was spun out of FMC Corporation in 2018 and is a Philadelphia-based company with a lithium history dating back to the 1940s.
Compared to other large Western players like Albemarle and SQM, Livent is a pure-play company focused only on lithium.
Livent operates one of the lowest-cost lithium mineral deposits in the world, the Salar del Hombre Muerto in Argentina.
Livent’s operations sit at the low end of the global cost curve to produce lithium carbonate.
Livent’s strategy focuses on lithium hydroxide, requiring a two-step conversion process from brine-based raw materials that adds costs.
Livent estimates its all-in cost structure for lithium hydroxide to be about $5,800, which remains below today’s depressed lithium prices.
The growing prevalence of nickel-rich batteries supports Livent’s business strategy.
Livent estimates nickel-rich batteries should increase their market share from about 25% today to 75% by the end of the decade.
Livent is known for its relationship with Tesla, being one of its main lithium hydroxide suppliers.
In November, Livent announced the extension of a multi-year supply agreement with Tesla through 2021, committing to deliver greater volumes than in 2020.
Livent management indicated they are working to extend their partnership for 2022 and beyond.
Livent recently announced a 50/50 joint venture with private equity firm Pallinghurst Resources to buy Canada’s lithium projects previously managed by Nemaska Lithium.
Pallinghurst, through Quebec Lithium Partners, is expected to own 50% of New Nemaska Lithium.
Livent is expected to see 25% of the potential economic benefits of New Nemaska Lithium.
The Canadian location can help Livent meet the growing demand of battery grade lithium from North American and Europe.
CATL is the world’s second-largest battery producer, behind LG Chem.
CATL is a Tier 1 battery producer, joining the select group of LG Chem, Panasonic, Samsung SDI, Tesla, SK Innovation, and AESC.
CATL distinguishes itself as the battery maker with the largest number of relationships with OEMs, including Tesla, BMW, Daimler, Geely, Great Wall, Honda, Hyundai, Volkswagen, and NEVS.
In February, CATL and Tesla agreed to produce batteries for EVs manufactured at Giga Shanghai, Tesla’s second battery megafactory.
Tesla is currently producing Model 3’s at an annualized rate of 250,000 EVs.
Helped by CATL’s cobalt-free lithium iron phosphate (LPF) batteries and local procurement, the Model 3 is the lowest priced premium mid-sized sedan in China at $36,800.
CATL recently announced a project to roll out a “million-mile” battery that costs less than $100 kWh by 2021.
Such a battery could support decades of heavy use for an EV, well beyond the lifespan of an internal combustion engine vehicle.
CATL’s relationships with OEMs across multiple regions, especially Chinese and European markets, allow it to remain a key player in the EV supply chain.
Recent reports from the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers show that new energy vehicles sales increased 105% year-over-year in October 2020.
New energy vehicle sales totaled approximately 160,000 during October 2020, marking the fourth consecutive month of gains.
Europe is projected to command the greatest share of EV sales growth.
Research firm Rho Motion estimates EV sales in Europe could double by the end of 2020 from 2019 due to significant policy support from European regulators.
Beyond the four highlighted firms, dozens of other lithium miners and battery producers around the world enable the rapid growth of electric vehicles.
The Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT) invests in the full lithium cycle, from mining and refining the metal through battery production.
source_title: Four Companies Leading the Rise of Lithium & Battery Technology

Manufactures lower and upper arms, which are integral components of a suspension system.
Lower and upper arms connect the wheels to the body of the vehicle.

Ensures these key components perform their functions without fail.

facts: Gov. Brian Kemp announced that more than 24,000 jobs were created across all regions of Georgia by economic development projects during the first three quarters of fiscal year 2021.
These projects total more than $8.43 billion in new investments, representing a 67% increase in new investments compared to the first nine months of the previous fiscal year.
Job creation is up 49% over the same period during Fiscal Year 2020.

Gov. Kemp stated that creating high-paying jobs for Georgians regardless of zip code has been a key priority since day one, and that targeted investments in K-12 education, workforce development, and innovative partnerships are delivering opportunities statewide.

During the third quarter of the fiscal year between January and March 2021, Georgia solidified its position as an internationally recognized tech hub and data center region with commitments from Airbnb, The Adecco Group, and Google.
Microsoft announced plans to build on its existing Georgia footprint to develop one of the company's largest hubs in metro Atlanta.
Microsoft will establish new data centers in Fulton and Douglas counties.
Georgia's fiscal year-to-date investment and job creation totals do not include complete details on expected investments and job creation from Microsoft or Airbnb.
Business expansions accounted for 68% of all projects and total investments as companies chose Georgia as the best location for growth.
New locations to the state accounted for 58% of new jobs in economic development projects from July 1, 2020, through March 31.
Food processing, manufacturing, and logistics and distribution industries continued a robust pace of development, accounting for 63% of jobs created by economic development projects.
Georgia's automotive and information and technology industries accounted for nearly 5,000 new jobs generated three-quarters into the fiscal year.
Freshly opened its first Southeastern U.S. facility in Cobb County.
Sailfish Boats expanded in the southwest Georgia city of Cairo.
Feit Electric invested in their first East Coast distribution facility in McDonough.

Brake pad supplier KB Autosys selected Meriwether County in west Georgia for its first U.S. automotive manufacturing facility, locating near regional customers such as Hyundai, Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, and General Motors.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia's economy is diverse, promising, and continuing to gain momentum after a volatile year, thanking private and public sector partners for helping maintain the state's position as the No. 1 state to live, work, and raise a family.
