facts: Yancey Engineered Solutions will invest $5.7 million to establish a new manufacturing facility in Cordele, Georgia.
The project will create 300 new jobs over the next several years in Crisp County.
Yancey Engineered Solutions provides power generation packaging systems and operates as a division of Yancey Bros. Company (Yancey).
Yancey Bros. Company is the authorized Caterpillar dealer for Georgia.
Trey Googe, CEO of Yancey, stated that Yancey Bros. has served Cordele and Crisp County for decades primarily with Caterpillar earthmoving equipment, parts, and service.
The new facility will be located at 502 South Midway Road in Cordele.
The company plans to refurbish the former Big Tex manufacturing facility on the site.
Hiring plans include welders, quality control technicians, assembly workers, and drivers.
Governor Brian P. Kemp noted that Yancey provides solutions supporting everything from small businesses to large manufacturers across every industry in Georgia.
Senior Regional Project Manager Christy Bozeman represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) on the project.
The project is in partnership with the Cordele-Crisp Industrial Development Authority, Crisp County Power Commission, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Yancey Engineered Solutions represents an exciting addition to Yancey’s portfolio that helps provide reliable energy to users.

Cordele Mayor and Crisp County-Cordele Industrial Development Authority Vice-Chair Wesley Rainey expressed excitement about redeveloping the Big Tex facility and hopes to rehire many of its former employees.

James R. Dowdy, III, Chairman of the Crisp County Board of Commissioners and the Cordele-Crisp Industrial Development Authority, stated that the community is thankful for the decision and it demonstrates economic development efforts paying dividends.
source_title: Yancey Engineered Solutions To Invest $5.7M In Georgia Manufacturing Facility - Business Facilities Magazine

facts: Between July 1, 2023, and June 30 of Fiscal Year 2024, GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 429 facility expansions and new locations statewide.
These FY2024 facility expansions and new locations resulted in more than $20.3 billion in investment and the commitment of 26,900 new private sector jobs across Georgia.
83% of FY2024 facility expansions and new locations are located in communities outside the 10-county Atlanta region, representing more than 18,300 new jobs and $12.5 billion in investment.
Metro Atlanta attracted nearly $7.8 billion in investments during FY2024 as a logistics and business hub.
Statewide, new locations accounted for $12.8 billion in investments, while expansions were responsible for 9,800 new jobs.
Manufacturing, including the aerospace, automotive, and e-mobility sectors, accounted for over 50% of facility expansions or new locations in FY2024.
Kia Georgia added the EV9 to its lineup at its facility in Troup County during FY2024.
Toyota Industries Corporation established a campus in Jackson County during FY2024.
StandardAero expanded operations in Richmond County during FY2024.
International life sciences company Gerresheimer announced its most recent expansion in Fayette County during FY2024.
Suppliers for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America announced new locations across Bryan, Candler, Chatham, Coweta, Effingham, Harris, and Laurens Counties.
SOLARCYCLE, an innovator in solar panel recycling, announced plans for a new location in Polk County to support solar panel manufacturer Qcells’ operations.
Governor Brian Kemp led economic development missions to Korea and Switzerland during FY2024, focusing on strengthening relationships with Hyundai Motor Group and its supplier network.
During the prior fiscal year, Governor Kemp led similar economic development missions to Israel, France, and Switzerland.

Georgia maintained its position as a global gateway for business, landing more than 8,100 new jobs and $5.9 billion in investments from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S. market in FY2024.

Lt. Governor Burt Jones highlighted that the Technical College System of Georgia, University System of Georgia, College and Career Academies, and private-public partnerships are building Georgia’s workforce pipeline through workforce training programs.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns noted that the state has delivered historic investments in education, workforce development, and infrastructure alongside tax cuts and regulatory relief.
The State of Georgia celebrated more than 50 years of continuous economic representation in Europe and Japan during FY2024, which remain among the top sources of foreign direct investment.
The coming fiscal year will mark 40 years of continuous economic representation in Korea, a partnership that earned GDEcD the James A. Van Fleet Award from the Korea Society.
All markets where Georgia maintains business and trade representation accounted for 29% of total new jobs and investments in FY2024.
Investments in food processing and agribusiness totaled more than $1.3 billion in FY2024.
Japanese probiotic beverage company Yakult located in Bartow County during FY2024.
Korean bakery CJ Foodville located in Hall County during FY2024.
Walmart will source milk from local dairy farmers at a future dairy processing facility in Lowndes County.
Cargill Corporation is establishing a new office hub in the City of Atlanta, creating at least 4,400 new jobs as part of FY2024 wins.
U.S. Soccer’s future headquarters and national training center in Fayette County reinforces Georgia’s position within entertainment, sports, and tourism.
Georgia earned the rank of No. 1 state for business from Area Development magazine for 10 consecutive years.
Georgia earned the No. 1 spot in Site Selection magazine’s annual Prosperity Cup rankings for successful investment recruitment in 2023.
Georgia was awarded a Gold Shovel by Area Development for achieving significant success in attracting new business investment and job creation in 2023.
Business Facilities magazine ranked Georgia as the No. 1 state for EV investments, as well as a top 10 state for food processing, cybersecurity, aerospace and defense, and AI growth hubs.
source_title: Kemp, GDEcD announces Georgia job creation remains strong

entity_name: Georgia EV and Battery Supply Chain Funding Ecosystem
canonical_name: Georgia State and Federal Incentive Programs for EV Manufacturing
facts: Georgia historically kept state grant programs lean and relied primarily on tax incentives, workforce training, and direct capital projects to attract business.
The state does not have a GoldenLEAF equivalent or a large discretionary development foundation.
Georgia's funding architecture includes the Quality Jobs Tax Credit (QJTC), Qualified Opportunity Zone tax credits, OneGeorgia Authority, and Georgia Department of Community Affairs (GDCA).

The arrival of Rivian, Hyundai Metaplant, SK Innovation battery plants in Commerce, Jackson, and Covington, and a dozen Korean and German automotive suppliers changed the game for Georgia's rural counties.
Federal co-investment including the CHIPS Act, IRA manufacturing credits, and DOE loan programs are all active in Georgia.
Bryan County went from rural coastal county to one of the largest EV manufacturing hubs in the Western Hemisphere in 18 months.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) is the primary state agency for business attraction and retention incentives.
Georgia's main incentive mechanism is the Quality Jobs Tax Credit (QJTC), not a cash grant.
The QJTC provides a per-employee credit against Georgia income tax for companies creating at least 50 jobs paying 10% above the county average wage.
QJTC credits range from $1,250 to $4,000 per job per year for 5 years, depending on county tier.
In distressed Tier 1 counties, a company creating 100 jobs at $60,000 average salary could earn $400,000 per year in tax credits for five years, or $2 million total.
The QJTC functions as a grant equivalent by reducing state tax liability rather than providing cash-in-hand.
OneGeorgia Authority is the primary vehicle for cash grants and was created to bridge development gaps between metro Atlanta and rural Georgia.
OneGeorgia operates Equity Fund grants that go to local governments for infrastructure supporting job-creating projects.
Water, sewer, broadband, rail spurs, and road improvements qualify for OneGeorgia Equity Fund grants.
OneGeorgia Equity Fund awards typically range from $500,000 to $3 million and require a matched private investment commitment.
Applications for OneGeorgia Equity Fund go through regional development centers (RDCs), not directly to the state.
OneGeorgia Frontier Fund grants address the most severely distressed counties with fewer than 50,000 residents.
The 50 most distressed Georgia counties are eligible for OneGeorgia Frontier Fund assistance.
Frontier Fund can provide slightly more flexible assistance including direct support for industrial building construction.
GDEcD administers the Competitive Grant Program for Rural Counties through the OneGeorgia framework as a closing fund for rural projects.
QuickStart is Georgia's employer-funded workforce training program administered by the Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG).
QuickStart provides custom job training at no cost to qualifying new or expanding businesses and has been running since 1967.

Qualification for QuickStart requires creating a minimum number of new jobs, paying wages above a county baseline, and operating in an eligible sector like manufacturing, distribution, IT, or headquarters operations.
Retail, food service, and hospitality do not qualify for QuickStart.

Businesses using QuickStart receive custom curriculum developed by TCSG instructors, training facilities (including on-site), instructors and equipment, and sometimes paid trainees during the training period.
For rural counties, QuickStart training combined with OneGeorgia infrastructure grants creates a package that lowers startup costs substantially.
The Hyundai Metaplant in Bryan County is receiving QuickStart training for its 8,500-job workforce.
Porsche's US headquarters training and Yamaha's manufacturing training have previously run through QuickStart.
Georgia is one of the top-five IRA beneficiary states because of its advanced manufacturing base.
The IRA's Section 45X Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit applies to clean energy components made in the US.
SK Innovation's battery plants in Commerce, Jackson, and Covington qualify for IRA Section 45X credits.
Rivian's plant in Statesboro and Hyundai Metaplant qualify for related IRA credits.
These IRA credits are worth hundreds of millions of dollars annually for manufacturers operating at scale.
The DOE Loan Programs Office (LPO) Title 17 Clean Energy Financing Program provides loans at below-market rates for clean energy manufacturing projects in Georgia.
The minimum project size for DOE LPO loans is $5 million.
Georgia-based EV component suppliers, battery material processors, and clean energy equipment manufacturers are eligible for DOE LPO programs.
The EPA's Clean Heavy-Duty Vehicles program under the IRA has made awards in Georgia for fleet electrification targeting school districts and port authorities with diesel fleets.
Georgia Ports Authority, which operates Savannah the second-largest container port in the US, received infrastructure funding related to electrification under this program.
SBIR programs at DOD, DOE, and NIH are available to Georgia companies with no special state registration required.
Georgia Tech's Enterprise Innovation Institute (EI2) provides specific support for Georgia small businesses pursuing federal SBIR awards, including proposal assistance and match funding.
EI2 is part of Georgia Tech's MEP affiliate and provides fee-based consulting that DOE MEP partially subsidizes.
The CHIPS and Science Act Section 9003 provided $500 million for semiconductor workforce training nationally.
Georgia Tech received funding for its semiconductor workforce programs through the CHIPS Act.
Wolfspeed has explored Georgia expansions for its semiconductor facility.
The Georgia Department of Community Affairs (GDCA) administers federally funded community development programs across the state as pass-through federal programs.
Community Development Block Grants (CDBG) in Georgia flow to non-entitlement communities: cities under 50,000 population and counties that are not Fulton, DeKalb, Gwinnett, or Cobb.
GDCA runs competitive CDBG rounds for water and sewer infrastructure, economic development, housing rehabilitation, and community facilities.
A typical rural Georgia county can receive $500,000 to $1.5 million per CDBG award.
CDBG applications open annually, usually in spring.
HOME Investment Partnerships Program funds flow through GDCA to local governments and Community Housing Development Organizations (CHDOs) for affordable housing construction and rehabilitation.
HOME awards typically range from $250,000 to $750,000 per project.
Atlanta, Augusta, Savannah, and Macon apply for HOME funds directly to HUD rather than through GDCA.
The State Small Business Credit Initiative (SSBCI) allocated $112 million to Georgia.
Georgia's SSBCI program operates through the Georgia Loans for Underserved Business (GLUE) network, providing access to capital for minority-owned and socioeconomically disadvantaged businesses.
SSBCI in Georgia is administered through approved financial institutions rather than directly by the state.

GDCA runs the Broadband Deployment Initiative for rural Georgia communities as a planning grant and funding guidance program to help counties access USDA ReConnect and FCC Rural Digital Opportunity Fund money.
Applications for most GDCA programs go through Georgia's Grant Management System.
Invest Atlanta is Atlanta's primary economic development authority managing Tax Allocation Districts (TADs), business retention incentives, and small business financing.
The Westside TAD covers English Avenue, Vine City, and neighborhoods adjacent to Mercedes-Benz Stadium.
The Westside TAD captures increment property tax revenue and reinvests it in the district.
Businesses locating or expanding in the Westside TAD can access facade improvement grants typically ranging from $5,000 to $25,000.
Tenant improvement grants for commercial build-outs are available in the Westside TAD.
Invest Atlanta also manages the Atlanta Affordable Housing Fund, a revolving loan fund that provides below-market financing for affordable housing development.
The Atlanta Affordable Housing Fund targets projects serving households at 60% AMI or below.
The City of Atlanta's Office of Small Business and Entrepreneurship provides grants through the Small Business COVID Recovery and Resiliency Grant Program which is active in limited form in 2026.
APD Urban LLC is a public-private partnership focused on Pittsburgh neighborhood redevelopment administering grants for small business tenants in the Pittsburgh Yards development.
Pittsburgh Yards is a 31-acre mixed-use site in southwest Atlanta.
APD Urban LLC grants range from $5,000 to $50,000 for qualifying businesses in creative industries, food and beverage, and light manufacturing.
Georgia's 159 counties include dozens that are persistently poor and receive disproportionate federal rural development funding.
The Appalachian Regional Commission covers 35 Georgia counties in the northern mountains.
ARC grants in Georgia typically go to workforce training programs, broadband infrastructure, and healthcare access in mountain counties like Rabun, Towns, Gilmer, and Pickens.
ARC allocated approximately $12 million to Georgia projects in FY2024.
USDA Rural Development has a Georgia state office in Athens with active programs including Community Facilities Direct Loans and Grants for hospitals, libraries, schools, and emergency services.
USDA Rural Development offers Water and Environmental Program grants for water and sewer infrastructure in rural communities.
The Rural Energy for America Program (REAP) provides grants for agricultural producers and rural small businesses making energy improvements.
REAP grants cover 25% of the cost of qualifying renewable energy systems or energy efficiency improvements.
A rural Georgia agricultural processor installing solar panels on a 5,000 sq ft facility might receive a $15,000-$40,000 REAP grant alongside a REAP loan covering another 50% of the cost.
Applications for REAP open annually and deadlines vary by fiscal year.
The Community Foundation for Greater Atlanta makes grants through several funds to nonprofits in a 23-county metro area.
The Goizueta Foundation focuses on Hispanic/Latino communities in metro Atlanta and provides grants from $25,000 to $250,000 to nonprofits serving those communities.
The Woodruff Foundation is the largest private grant maker in Georgia with roughly $100 million in annual grants for education, health, and community development.
The Woodruff Foundation is invitation-only and does not accept unsolicited applications.

South Arts is headquartered in Atlanta and administers Jazz Road Creative Residencies, Jazz Road Tours, Southern Circuit filmmaker grants, and the Southern Prize and State Fellowships across the 9-state South Arts region.
Current open deadlines include June 30, 2026 for Jazz Road Creative Residencies and the Southern Circuit call for screening partners runs through April 2027.

facts: GSC Steel Stamping, LLC is a minority and family-owned and operated automotive parts manufacturer.
The company provides Body in White frame assembly brackets to some of the world’s leading OEMs and tier one suppliers.
GSC Steel Stamping offers competitively priced metal stamping, engineering, welded assemblies, and tooling services.
In June 2023, GSC acquired Dixien, LLC’s assets.
The acquisition continues the 43-year history of automotive parts stamping at its Lake City, GA location.
The company's facility in Lake City, GA spans 200,000 square feet.
Prior to leading GSC Steel Stamping, CEO Vincent Henderson purchased and led the turn-around of two award-winning automotive stamping operations in Michigan.
Those previous Michigan operations were regularly recognized by customers for excellence in overall performance, quality, and on-time delivery.
The company continuously re-evaluates ways to improve all its processes.
GSC invests in advanced technology and hires top-notch staff.
The plant operations management team alone possesses over 200 years of combined stamping experience.
The company takes pride in consistently delivering high-quality, precision parts on time.
The company's mission is to provide quality parts, shipped on time, every time.

entity_name: Hyundai Meta Plant & Kia West Point Plant
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Georgia (Meta Plant) & Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (West Point Plant)
facts: Georgia is quickly turning into an automotive powerhouse.
Hyundai operates a brand-new manufacturing facility near Savannah called the Meta plant.
Kia operates an established assembly plant in West Point near Atlanta.
Both plants operate under the same corporate umbrella (Hyundai Group).
The Hyundai Meta plant buildings are painted very light sage green to blend with rural surroundings, featuring rounded corners on every building exterior.
The original plan for the Hyundai Meta plant was to only make electric cars, starting with the Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9.
EV sales did not materialize as expected, prompting the addition of the Kia Sportage to increase manufacturing volume.
The Hyundai Meta plant is highly automated but operates at very low volume with lines moving slowly.
Production at the Hyundai Meta plant started in October 2024.
The Hyundai Meta plant does not expect to reach full line speed until 2028.
The plant requires more models to fill assembly lines and its advanced automation needs ongoing tweaking and adjustment.
Metal stamping at the Meta plant uses one massive transfer press line made by Rotem, a division of Hyundai.
There is only one transfer press line at the Meta plant, meaning it produces all panels for all three models with no backup if it fails.
The Meta plant makes all major A-class panels including body sides, roofs, floors, and fenders, while smaller stampings are shipped in from suppliers.
Racks of stampings are stacked to the ceiling at the Meta plant due to the single press line constraint.
No forklifts are used at the Meta plant; all material movement is handled by Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) and heavy-duty electric tuggers.
The welding shop at the Meta plant uses huge Fanuc robots to pick up entire body-in-white structures.
The brand-new Meta plant is still ironing out quality issues, including line workers hammering door sills and bodies being pulled off the line for repair loops.
Cars spend six to eight hours in the Meta plant paint shop using one gallon of paint per vehicle.
The paint shop at the Meta plant is not accessible on tours to prevent dust and dirt contamination.
At the Meta plant final assembly, doors are removed for interior assembly and reinstalled later entirely by robots.
Every car at the Meta plant rides on an AGV equipped with an automatic up-and-down lift to adjust ergonomic height at each workstation.
Hyundai leverages its subsidiaries at the Meta plant: Hyundai Steel supplies steel, Hyundai Rotem makes presses, Hyundai Glovis handles logistics, and Hyundai Mobis handles battery pack assembly.
The Hyundai Meta plant currently employs about 1,800 people.
The Meta plant is aggressively hiring to add a second shift, which will increase employment to 2,600.
Production at the Meta plant remains very low, with only about 6,000 cars shipped in the month prior to the visit.
When the Meta plant reaches capacity, Kia will receive about 40% of the production.
The Hyundai Meta plant has a current capacity of 300,000 units but plans to boost it to 500,000.
Hyundai is planning to add a second plant at the Meta site by knocking out an exterior wall to add another 250,000 units of capacity.
The additional space at the Meta plant will likely be tooled for upcoming midsize body-on-frame trucks and SUVs from both Hyundai and Kia.
Kia’s West Point assembly plant began operations 16 years ago.
The Kia West Point plant spans roughly 1.7 million square feet under roof across stamping, paint, body shop, and final assembly.
The Kia West Point plant is tooled to produce 350,000 vehicles a year.
A single line at the West Point plant sequentially builds the Telluride, Sorento, Sportage, EV6, and EV9.
The West Point plant builds gas, hybrid, and pure EV vehicles sequentially on one line.
The Kia West Point plant employs 3,200 workers across three eight-hour shifts, five days a week.
The West Point plant performs on-the-fly shift handoffs so the assembly line never stops.
Daily maintenance at the West Point plant is scheduled during 30-minute lunch breaks and weekends.
The Kia West Point stamping shop runs two Rotem transfer presses.
Die sets and storage racks for stamped panels at the West Point plant are color-coded to specific vehicle models to prevent mistakes.
Robots at the West Point plant pick stamped panels off the press line and keep a “last hit” panel on each rack for quality verification.
The West Point plant maintains about 36 hours of inventory in stamped panels.
The West Point body shop uses zero batch building, constructing different bodies sequentially to match sales orders.
A modular body buck at the West Point plant adapts on the fly to accommodate different models by indexing parts in or out.
The West Point general assembly area features open sightlines with parts racks no higher than chest-high.
Unlike the Meta plant, the West Point plant uses two separate trim lines where human operators assisted by mechanical lift equipment handle door installation.
Workers on the final line at the West Point plant rotate jobs every two hours to prevent fatigue and boredom.
The West Point plant paints 14 different colors, primarily white, black, and silver.
The final assembly line at the West Point plant is tightly packed with only about four feet of clearance between cars.
The Kia West Point plant produces 1,500 cars per day.
Every vehicle at the West Point plant undergoes testing on a 2.2-mile on-site track vital for calibrating lane-keeping systems.
The Kia West Point facility features an on-site transmission assembly plant.
Kia sells transmissions produced at the West Point plant to outside competitors including Nissan and Volkswagen.
The Kia West Point plant has a 13,000-spot parking lot with protective roofs built over part of the vehicle staging areas.
A severe hail storm caused massive damage to inventory at the West Point plant last year, resulting in significant repair costs.
Management added protective roofs and solar panels over the staging areas at the West Point plant but is still assessing cost-effectiveness for full coverage.
The Hyundai Meta plant relies heavily on vast open space to allow AGVs to maneuver.
The Kia West Point plant uses about 17 labor-hours to make each vehicle, including stamping.
Hyundai and Kia are on track to produce more than 1.4 million vehicles a year in the U.S., surpassing current Toyota and Honda output.
source_title: Insights & Observations: Inside Hyundai’s Meta & Kia’s West Point Plants - Autoline

GDEcD stands for Georgia Department of Economic Development.
Asante Bradford serves as the digital entertainment liaison for the Georgia Film, Music & Digital Entertainment division of the Georgia Department of Economic Development.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development has expanded its mission under Governor Perdue's leadership to include support for entrepreneurship as a critical component of the state's economic development strategy.

facts: Astemo is a global automotive components manufacturer focused on technologies for connected, autonomous, shared, and electric mobility.
The company name was changed from Hitachi Astemo, Ltd. to Astemo, Ltd. on April 1, 2025.
Astemo develops automotive technologies aligned with connectivity, autonomous systems, and electrification trends for passenger and motorcycle applications.
Astemo's ownership structure includes major stakes from Hitachi, Ltd. (40%) and Honda Motor Co., Ltd. (40%), with JIC Capital holding the remaining 20%.
In December 2025, Honda announced it would acquire an additional 21% of Astemo shares from Hitachi, changing the shareholding structure to 61% for Honda, 20% for JIC, and 19% for Hitachi.

Astemo has expanded its research and development activities in electric powertrains, advanced driver-assistance systems, and in-vehicle software to support its strategic focus on electrification and autonomous mobility.
Astemo develops components for electric and autonomous vehicles, with a focus on integration of key systems such as power control units, motors, inverters, and software-defined vehicle platforms.
In 2023, Astemo announced a ¥15.3 billion (~US $100 million) expansion of its Berea, Kentucky plant to support its electrification business.
As of 2025, Astemo is investing $153 million at its Berea, Kentucky manufacturing plant to expand production of electric vehicle components and create over 200 jobs.
In July 2025, Astemo announced a £100 million investment in its Horwich manufacturing facility in Bolton, Greater Manchester.
The project will establish a new production line dedicated to electric vehicle (EV) inverters, making the site the only facility in the United Kingdom with this production capability.

In September 2025, CBS News and The Detroit News reported that Astemo planned to establish a regional headquarters in Wixom, Michigan, as part of a $95 million investment expected to create about 200 jobs.
Astemo maintains business relationships with Nissan Motor Co., reflecting Hitachi's historical ties to the Nissan Group.
Astemo was established in January 2021 through the business merger of Hitachi Automotive Systems, Keihin Corporation, Showa Corporation, and Nissin Kogyo.
In December 2024, the Government of Cantabria awarded the Hitachi Astemo Buelna plant a €1 million grant through SODERCAN’s competitive programme for major industrial competitiveness projects.
The funding supports a €5 million experimental development project focused on advanced braking systems, to be completed over 18 months.
source_title: Astemo - Wikipedia

facts: Hitachi Astemo Americas Inc., one of Kentucky’s largest Tier 1 automotive suppliers, announced plans to expand its operation in Madison County with a $153 million investment creating 167 new, quality Kentucky jobs.

The project will see company leaders invest in the current manufacturing operation in Berea to support site improvements and renovations, including increased production lines and additional equipment.
The facility’s expansion will include an additional 752,000 square feet on 62 acres and respond to increased market demand and growth within the EV industry.
Hitachi’s investment will create 167 full-time jobs for Kentuckians, bringing the company’s total Kentucky employment across the Harrodsburg and Berea facilities to over 2,100 workers.
Tim Clark, president of Hitachi Astemo Americas, stated that as the company approaches 40 years of manufacturing in Kentucky, it is pleased to continue its commitment to growing there.

Tim Clark described the announcement as a bold step toward vehicle electrification and noted it embodies the new company name Hitachi Astemo, which stands for Advanced Sustainable Technology for Mobility.
In January 2021, Hitachi Automotive Systems, Keihin, Showa and Nissin Kogyo completed a business merger to form the global mega-supplier Hitachi Astemo.
The company provides advanced mobility solutions in electric powertrains for automobiles, advanced chassis, autonomous driving/advanced driver assistance systems, and systems for motorcycles.
Headquartered in Tokyo, Japan, Hitachi's entry into the Americas began in 1972 with a subsidiary established in Brazil.
In the mid-1980s, Hitachi expanded into the U.S. market with its first factory in Kentucky.

The company now serves as a global mega-supplier to some of the largest automobile manufacturers in the world and has more than 20 manufacturing sites in the United States, Canada, Mexico, and Brazil.
The company currently operates two locations in Kentucky.

This expansion adds to Kentucky’s thriving manufacturing sector, which has seen nearly 600 private-sector new-location or expansion projects commit to invest over $22.9 billion and create more than 30,000 jobs administration-to-date.
In March 2023, the Kentucky Economic Development Finance Authority (KEDFA) preliminarily approved a 10-year incentive agreement with the company under the Kentucky Business Investment program.
The performance-based agreement can provide up to $2.4 million in tax incentives based on the company’s investment of $153 million and annual targets.
Annual targets for the incentive include the creation and maintenance of 167 Kentucky-resident, full-time jobs across 10 years.
Annual targets also require paying an average hourly wage of $25.71, including benefits, across those jobs.
KEDFA approved Hitachi Astemo for up to $200,000 in tax incentives through the Kentucky Enterprise Initiative Act (KEIA).
KEIA allows approved companies to recoup Kentucky sales and use tax on construction costs, building fixtures, equipment used in research and development, and electronic processing.
By meeting its annual targets over the agreement term, the company can be eligible to keep a portion of the new tax revenue it generates.
The company may claim eligible incentives against its income tax liability and/or wage assessments.

Hitachi Astemo can receive resources from Kentucky’s workforce service providers, including no-cost recruitment and job placement services, reduced-cost customized training, and job-training incentives.
source_title: Hitachi Astemo Americas to expand in Berea with $153 million investment - The Lane Report | Kentucky Business & Economic News

facts: In preparation for Honda EV production in the U.S., Honda Transmission Plant-Georgia (TMP-G) is partnering with a Honda supplier, Hitachi Astemo Americas, Inc., to build e-axles, an essential component of EVs.
TMP-G leads global production of the state-of-the-art 10-speed automatic transmission that powers a number of Acura and Honda vehicles.
Associates at Honda Transmission Plant-Georgia (TMP-G) celebrated production of the 5 millionth automatic transmission since the plant began production in 2006.
TMP-G is partnering with Honda supplier Hitachi Astemo Americas, Inc., to install a new line to build e-axles, an essential component of electric vehicles (EVs).
The e-axle combines the electric motor, gearing components and power electronics.

Arturo Valdes, Manufacturing Division Lead at TMP-G, stated: "This milestone is a testament to the dedication and skill of our Honda associates and their commitment to outstanding quality for our customers."

Arturo Valdes added: "Even as we continue to meet customer demand for Honda and Acura vehicles, the unwavering commitment of the associates is leading us to a new challenge in our electrified future."
Honda started transmission production in Georgia in 2006 with 179 associates.
The TMP-G operation has received a total investment of more than $485 million.
The operation has expanded to include more than 500 associates.
The facility has the capacity to build 375,000 transmissions annually for various Acura and Honda models.
After beginning production of 5-speed automatic transmissions, TMP-G added 6-speed automatic transmissions in 2012.
In 2017, the facility added a second assembly line to become the first Honda plant in the world to produce the advanced Honda 10-speed automatic transmission.
Honda has been producing automobiles in America for over 40 years, beginning in November 1982 with the start of automobile production at the Marysville Auto Plant.
In 2022, more than two-thirds of all Honda and Acura automobiles sold in the U.S. were made in America, using domestic and globally sourced parts.
Honda’s cumulative auto production in America now exceeds 30 million vehicles.
Honda established manufacturing operations in America in 1979 with the start of motorcycle production in Marysville, Ohio.
Honda has one of the largest and most diverse U.S. manufacturing footprints of any international company.
More than 23,000 U.S. associates support the company’s 12 manufacturing plants in America.

These operations have an annual capacity of more than 1.27 million automobiles, 1.52 million engines, 500,000 power equipment products and 300,000 powersports products, as well as the HondaJet advanced light jet and GE Honda HF120 turbofan engines.
Cumulatively, Honda has invested $19 billion in its U.S. manufacturing operations.
This includes more than $3.6 billion over the past five years alone.
The company works with 600 U.S. original equipment suppliers.
U.S.-sourced parts purchases have reached approximately $492 billion since 1979.

links.context: Learn more about Honda in America

source_title: Honda’s Georgia Transmission Plant Produces 5 Millionth Transmission

facts: Hitachi Astemo Ltd. is owned by Hitachi Ltd. and Honda Motor Co. and was created in 2021 from the merger of Hitachi Automotive Systems with Keihin, Nissin Kogyo, and Showa.
The company focuses on electrification, comfort, safety, and sustainability for mobility applications.
Hitachi Astemo is investing heavily in e-axles, inverters, and electric motors to balance traditional ICE and EV automaker demands.
Engineers developed e-axles featuring efficiency, low vibration, low noise, modular design, and optimization control software for motors and gears.
The company developed an inverter with industry-leading power density using a unique cooling module and thin, highly heat-dissipating insulation mounting technology.
Inverters consist of power modules with built-in power semiconductors, control boards, capacitors, and current sensors.

Hitachi Astemo leverages legacy Hitachi technologies in material development, analysis, sound-reduction, and manufacturing to create magnetic circuits for electric motors using magnets, electromagnetic steel sheets, and windings.
At the Japan Mobility Show in Tokyo, the company showcased advanced driver assistance systems including smart brakes and steer-by-wire technology.

The company unveiled 360-degree sensing technology that uses multiple stereo cameras combined with long-range detection and wide viewing angles to detect pedestrians and objects using AI and machine learning.
Hitachi Astemo operates assembly plants in Georgia, Indiana, Kentucky, and Ohio in North America, plus facilities in Canada and Mexico, with its U.S. headquarters in Farmington Hills, MI.

The company's Berea, KY plant conducted a smart factory experiment with Ericsson and Amazon Web Services using Hitachi video analytics over a private 5G network to detect defects earlier and reduce wasted material.
John Nunneley serves as senior vice president of design engineering and IT at Hitachi Astemo Americas Inc.

The company functions independently of Hitachi and Honda but collaborates on R&D projects, leveraging Hitachi's digital technology and former Honda-owned component suppliers to act as a systems supplier for OEMs.
Hitachi Astemo has no plans to exit the ICE market, which generates the bulk of its revenue, acknowledging a long transition period from ICE to hybrid to fully electric vehicles.
The company expects an 85 percent power density increase in its inverters over the next five years.
Hitachi Astemo is combining inverters, motors, power electronics, gearboxes, and software to create three-in-one e-axles that simplify EV drivetrain integration.
Production of the three-in-one e-axle is currently ramping up, with pilot production scheduled for this year and commercial production next year.
The three-in-one e-axle will be built in North America at Hitachi Astemo's factory in Tallapoosa, GA.

Motors and power inverters are larger and more complex than brake and suspension parts, requiring single-station batch builds in early stages before transitioning to new Industry 4.0-equipped assembly lines for higher EV volumes.
Battery pack control modules for EVs can be manufactured on the same surface-mount assembly lines as ICE engine controllers.
The company is electrifying components from hydraulic brakes to smart brakes and brake-by-wire, while also developing steer-by-wire systems and active suspension systems.
A smart chassis system ties these components together dynamically using data from stereo cameras and other sensors to adapt to road conditions like potholes or ice.
Target production timing for the next-generation AI-enhanced 360-degree stereo camera is 2025, with initial assembly in Japan followed by North American production as volumes ramp up.

The Berea, KY plant currently produces suspension products and has started producing electric motors in 2022, with production ramping up over the next few years alongside new EV product assembly lines.
Harrodsburg, KY is Hitachi Astemo's largest U.S. plant, primarily producing components for electronic control units, electronic throttle bodies, fuel rails, and mass air flow sensors.
The Sunbury, OH plant assembles suspension products.
The Greenfield, IN facility, a former Keihin factory making ICE components, is being converted to produce EV products such as power inverters.
All U.S. production sites are implementing Industry 4.0 tools, including data tracking and equipment observation to identify bottlenecks and improve overall equipment effectiveness.
The company is creating an end-to-end digital thread from sales to engineering to production to aftermarket and implementing a standard enterprise resource planning system across global sites.
source_title: Hitachi Astemo Pushes the Envelope on EV and AV Technology | ASSEMBLY

facts: Showa Corporation was a globally recognized Japanese manufacturer specializing in high-performance shock absorbers, suspension systems, steering systems, and other critical components for automobiles, motorcycles, and power products.
Showa Corporation was founded in 1938 and built a strong reputation for quality and innovation while supplying major Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) worldwide.
In January 2021, Showa Corporation merged with Keihin Corporation, Nissin Kogyo Co., Ltd., and Hitachi Automotive Systems, Ltd. to form Hitachi Astemo, Ltd.
The merger integration aimed to create a new global mega-supplier in the automotive and motorcycle industry by combining strengths in powertrain, chassis, and safety systems.
While the Showa brand and its technologies continue within Hitachi Astemo, Showa Corporation as an independent entity ceased operations upon this merger.

The Gyoda headquarters served as the central hub for Showa's global operations, including corporate management, research and development, strategic planning, and oversight of its automotive and motorcycle components business.

The Gyoda facility was known for advanced manufacturing capabilities and R&D centers focused on suspension, steering, and hydraulic technologies, often including proving grounds and testing facilities.

Prior to its merger into Hitachi Astemo, Showa Corporation maintained an extensive global footprint with numerous manufacturing plants, R&D centers, and sales offices across Asia (Japan, Thailand, Indonesia, India, China, Vietnam), North America (USA, Canada, Mexico), South America (Brazil), and Europe (UK, Spain, Germany, Italy).

These global facilities were dedicated to designing, manufacturing, and supplying high-quality shock absorbers, power steering systems, drive train components, and other critical parts for major automotive and motorcycle OEMs.
Showa Corporation's headquarters address was 1 Chome-14-1 Fujiwara, Gyoda, Saitama Prefecture, Japan.
A key US facility was located at 707 W. Cherry St., Sunbury, OH 43074, USA.
North American operations catered directly to OEM clients like Honda and Harley-Davidson, managed regional sales, provided localized engineering support, and ensured just-in-time delivery.
A key Asian facility was located at Rojana Industrial Park, Ayutthaya, Thailand, supplying components to Japanese and international automotive/motorcycle manufacturers in the ASEAN region.
A key European facility was located at Showa Autoparts Spain, S.A.U., Martorelles, Barcelona, Spain, serving European automotive manufacturers with localized production and R&D support.
Hitachi Astemo announced its participation in EICMA 2023 (Milan Motorcycle Show) on November 2, 2023, showcasing advanced suspension systems under the Showa brand for motorcycles.
Hitachi Astemo Americas introduced the new SHOWA G²as (Groove² Gas) Series aftermarket rear cushion units for Harley-Davidson Touring models from 2014 onwards on October 26, 2022.

Hitachi Astemo, Ltd. officially began operations on January 5, 2021, following the successful business integration of Hitachi Automotive Systems, Keihin Corporation, Showa Corporation, and Nissin Kogyo Co., Ltd.
As of April 2025, there have been no executive new hires or exits specifically for Showa Corporation within the last 12 months due to its merger into Hitachi Astemo.
source_title: Where is Showa Corporation Located? HQ, Global Offices & Company Insights

The company generates approximately USD 29,441,610 in revenue annually.
Hollingsworth-Vose employs around 68 people at its Hawkinsville location.
The facility is located at 300 Industrial Blvd, Hawkinsville, GA 31036.
Business hours are Monday through Friday from 8 AM to 5 PM, with weekends closed.
Lee Tompkins serves as the Line Manager and primary contact for the company.

facts: Hollingsworth & Vose Company has been awarded the 2026 BCI Innovation Award by Battery Council International for its development of a unique glass fibrous matrix electrode.

The innovation, known as Glass Electrode Material (GEM), helps generate and support a unique microporous structure within the lead active mass of a battery to enhance chargeability performance and cycle life reliability without impacting water loss.

Roger Miksad, President and Executive Director of BCI, said battery innovation is thriving and H&V is a great example of technological leadership and product evolution that advances the capabilities of the storied lead battery.
GEM technology improves recharge efficiency through enhanced charge acceptance, reducing fuel-related emissions while improving battery performance, durability, and cycle life reliability.
With approximately three times higher Dynamic Charge Acceptance (DCA), GEM enables next-generation start-stop systems that supports fuel economy gains of up to 5%.
Its fast recharge response also makes it well suited for advanced 12-V architectures in hybrid (HEV, PHEV) and electric vehicle auxiliary systems.
The technology platform is applicable across multiple energy storage applications, including automotive batteries, motive power for forklifts, and stationary backup power solutions.
Hollingsworth & Vose developed GEM in partnership with ArcActive.

Nicolas Clement, R&D Director, and Dr. Amogh Karwa, Director of Product management, stated that GEM technology has true potential for creating a more sustainable future and will be a great tool for battery manufacturers for enhanced performance to enable lead batteries to compete better with alternative chemistries.
East Penn Manufacturing and Silicon Joule received Honorable Mention recognition for their joint work on the first commercial bi-pole battery based on lead chemistry and treated silicon wafers.
Battery Council International annually presents this award to celebrate innovation in equipment, processes, services, and products that advance the lead battery industry.
The award was established to honor industry thought leader, Sally Breidegam Miksiewicz, the former CEO of East Penn Manufacturing Co.
Battery Council International (BCI) is the leading trade association representing the global battery industry.
Founded in 1924, BCI advocates and educates on behalf of battery manufacturers and recyclers, marketers and retailers, suppliers of raw materials and equipment, and battery distributors.
H&V is a global manufacturer of advanced materials used in filtration, battery, and industrial applications.
H&V's advanced materials contribute to a cleaner world through their use in products that provide clean air, clean liquids, and energy storage.
Family-owned for seven generations, the company is headquartered in East Walpole, Massachusetts, U.S.A.
source_title: BCI Names Hollingsworth & Vose its 2026 Innovation Award Winner

facts: Hollingsworth and Vose Co's estimated annual revenue is currently $275.2M per year.
Hollingsworth and Vose Co's estimated revenue per employee is $282,800.
Hollingsworth and Vose Co has 973 employees.
Hollingsworth and Vose Co grew their employee count by 11% last year.
H&V products are found in applications such as engine filtration, high efficiency air and liquid filtration, battery separators, gasket materials, and specialty and industrial nonwovens.
The company's expertise and process capabilities include wet laid, dry laid, melt blown, and composite technologies.
Established in 1843, H&V has supplied specialty, industrial, and technical papers and nonwovens for over 150 years.
Key leadership titles at the company include CEO, Chief Scientist Global Battery Technology, VP Managing Director Asia Pacific, VP Global Operations, EVP Global Operations, and VP & General Counsel.

facts: Hollingsworth & Vose (H&V) is a global manufacturer of advanced materials used in filtration, battery and industrial applications.
H&V will invest $40.2 million to expand its operation in Floyd County, Va.
The company will add over 28,000 square feet to its facility at 365 Christiansburg Pike Northeast to accommodate new production equipment to meet increased demand.
Virginia successfully competed with Georgia for the project, which will create 25 new jobs.
Gov. Glenn Youngkin announced the investment and stated H&V has generated positive economic impact and job opportunities in Floyd County for more than four decades.
Autoneum signed an agreement to acquire the automotive business of Borgers Group for an enterprise value of €117 million, expected to close in April 2023 following antitrust clearance.

Borgers specializes in textile acoustics protection, insulation and trim for automobiles, including wheel arch liners and trunk liner product lines, and is the market leader in Europe in textile wheel arch liners.
Borgers generated €610 million in revenue in fiscal year 2021 with around 4,700 employees.
Autoneum will finance the transaction through a new credit facility and a planned CHF 100 million capital increase.
Mann+Hummel Group announced a strategic investment in M-Filter Group, one of the largest filter manufacturing companies in Northern and Eastern Europe.
M-Filter produces HVAC and air filtration including HEPA filters and oil/hydraulic filters for engines, with a strong footprint in OEM products for industrial and engine filtration.
Sonobond Ultrasonics president Janet Devine noted the company's involvement in the growing market for EV vehicles through ultrasonic welding equipment for non-ferrous metals.

facts: Hollingsworth & Vose Company will invest $4.8 million to expand its Floyd County plant.
The expansion will create 25 new jobs over the next three years.
Governor Jim Gilmore approved a $50,000 grant from the Governor's Opportunity Fund to assist the county with the expansion.

Governor Jim Gilmore stated: "The Hollingsworth & Vose facility has been an important employer in Floyd for many years... I am delighted that they have chosen to expand here, and I am sure they will continue to prosper in the New River Valley."
The company specializes in providing technically oriented, high quality, fibrous composite materials primarily for filtration and battery operations.
The Floyd plant produces carded and meltblown nonwoven composites for filtration and other specialty industrial applications.
The company has been in Floyd County since 1976.
Virginia successfully competed with New York and Georgia for the 44,800-square-foot expansion.
The expansion will add a third meltblown line to the existing plant.
Hollingsworth & Vose Company is headquartered in East Walpole, Massachusetts.
The company has six U.S. manufacturing locations, one in the United Kingdom, and a joint venture plant in Mexico.
The company is one of the world's leading producers of meltblown composite materials.
John Hunsinger serves as Plant Manager of the Hollingsworth & Vose Company - Floyd.
John Hunsinger stated: "We are very pleased with the decision to expand our Floyd operations and are very appreciative of all the support that helped to make this possible."
The Virginia Economic Development Partnership, Floyd County, and American Electric Power assisted the company in its decision to expand.
The Virginia Department of Business Assistance will provide workforce training services for the expansion.

facts: Hollingsworth & Vose is a global leader in filtration and energy storage solutions.
The company is introducing PlusZero, a line of high-performance solutions made without PFAS.
PlusZero aims to create a cleaner, healthier, and more sustainable world.
The environmentally friendly solutions are designed to meet performance, reliability, and affordability goals.
Mike Clark serves as H&V’s Division President.
Clark stated that PlusZero is the company's latest breakthrough in innovation and sustainability after years of research.
The PlusZero line utilizes advanced technology to eliminate forever chemicals (PFAS) in manufacturing.
The solutions deliver high performance in most applications and are priced similarly to legacy counterparts.
H&V is committed to providing sustainable business solutions, including PlusZero, despite not yet being required to eliminate PFAS use in product manufacturing.

The company is nationally recognized as a certified minority business under the Hollingsworth name.
Hollingsworth has emerged as a leading supply chain provider to manufacturing and distribution communities.

The company operates over 10 million square feet of warehouse and production space.
Hollingsworth supports over 550 million pieces shipped annually.

facts: The state-of-the-art plant is a zero-to-waste-landfill facility.
The facility earned the 2023 U.S. Environmental Protection Agency ENERGY STAR Certification.
Groundbreaking occurred for the transmission plant in Tallapoosa, Georgia.
Assembly Line 1 starts mass production with the 5-speed transmission.
The facility produced its 100,000th transmission.

facts: Rivian's Georgia plant will have a capacity of 300,000 units initially.
That's up from the 200,000 that Rivian previously planned.
Production there will begin in late 2028, Rivian confirmed on Thursday.
The company also said it now plans to borrow $4.5 billion through the Department of Energy to build the facility, down from $6.6 billion.
Rivian is also drawing on the loan sooner than before, starting in 2027.
The factory will house production vehicles based on Rivian's midsized platform, including the R2.
The facility will produce a robotaxi variant as part of a 50,000-vehicle partnership with Uber.
With the combined capacity of 515,000 vehicles, Rivian expects to reach the scale it needs to achieve positive free cash flow, CFO Claire McDonough said on the company's earnings call on Thursday.
Rivian's initial plan for Georgia called for two phases of construction, each yielding 200,000 units of production capacity.
On Rivian's earnings call, McDonough said there will still be room at the plant for future expansions.
source_title: Rivian Boosts Initial Georgia Plant Capacity To 300,000 Units - Autojunction

facts: KIA Motors Manufacturing Georgia continues to lead the way for one of the world’s fastest growing automobile manufacturers.
February 2013 vehicle sales figures show the KIA Optima and KIA Sorento as the leading units for KIA Motors with over 20,000 units sold during the month.
KIA announced that 2012 was the 18th consecutive year in which the manufacturer has gained U.S. market share.
KIA Motors recently became one of Interbrand’s “Top 100 Global Brands” thanks in large part to the quality products being produced at the West Point, GA manufacturing facility.
Since its groundbreaking, KIA Motors Manufacturing Georgia has invested more than $1.4 billion in Georgia while creating over 11,000 manufacturing and supplier related jobs.
source_title: Georgia Built Vehicles Leading the Way for KIA | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: Last year, Honda gave Ars a tour of its manufacturing facilities in Ohio.
The Anna Engine Plant underwent a transformation adding a massive die-cast operation to make electric vehicle battery packs alongside the lines that make engines at Anna.
The Marysville Auto Plant received a new section filled with robots ready to incorporate three new Honda and Acura EVs into the production mix alongside Accords and Integras.
Earlier today, Honda announced it is facing heavy losses for the financial year between $5.1 billion and $7 billion (820 billion–1.12 trillion yen).
To help stanch these losses, Honda canceled the Honda 0 SUV, Honda 0 sedan, and the electric Acura RSX.
The canceled EVs were revealed at CES last year in a “nearly production” state.

Honda cited ongoing trade war chaos and tariffs as a primary reason for killing off its new EVs before they reach the market, noting they have eaten into the profitability of the cars it imports into the US.
A second reason is the US government’s revanchist decision to cease enforcing emissions and fuel economy standards on the auto industry.
Honda stated that “striving for carbon neutrality” is a “responsibility Honda… must fulfill for the future,” but implied this responsibility only applies when being forced by a government.
source_title: Facing heavy losses, Honda cancels its three US-made electric vehicles - Ars Technica

facts: Volkswagen Group Canada Inc. plans to eventually manufacture solid-state batteries at its planned gigafactory in St. Thomas, Ont., says chief executive Vito Paladino.
Volkswagen AG owns many brands including Audi, where Paladino is also CEO for Canada.
VW has long signaled interest in solid-state battery technology, but lithium-ion and lithium phosphate batteries are more prevalent today.
Many, if not all, of VW's EVs currently use lithium-ion batteries, and one industry source said the company will produce those batteries in the short term.
Paladino stated: "As far as the technology, it is evolving fast. It is incredible."
Paladino noted it is tough to talk specifics but reported that the solid-state battery will allow them to provide electrification in a more affordable way.
Paladino made comments regarding consumer sentiment around EVs, noting climate change makes it necessary to move away from internal combustion engine vehicles.
Paladino said affordability is still an issue for many people when it comes to EVs.
Paladino stated: "The battery plant that we have in Canada will be solid-state batteries."
Paladino added these batteries will have a cost benefit, but also way more range and performance.
Powerco Canada Inc., VW's battery manufacturing subsidiary, said in December that the site was ready for groundbreaking.
Construction of the $7-billion St. Thomas gigafactory would begin this year with a goal of producing batteries by 2027.
A spokesperson for Powerco did not return calls seeking comment by the time of publication.
In December, Powerco described the solid-state cell as "the battery of the future," which would cut charging time, extend driving range and maintain safety standards.
Whereas lithium-ion batteries use liquid electrolytes, solid-state batteries use solid electrolytes to carry charges, which may improve charging times and make them less prone to catching fire.
VW has invested hundreds of millions of dollars into California-based Quantumscape Corp., a tech startup working on solid-state batteries.

In January, Reuters reported that VW was in talks with Blue Solutions SAS, a solid-state battery company with operations in Boucherville, Que., that is now owned by the French conglomerate Bolloré SE.
A spokesperson for Blue Solutions was not immediately available for comment.
Regardless of whether solid-state batteries are currently ready for mass commercial production, VW's planned battery plant in St. Thomas remains a year away from initial production.
VW made headlines in Canada when it announced the plant in 2023.
The announcement marked the first time in years that Canada attracted a new automaker to build out its supply chain here.
The past few decades have been characterized by automakers closing operations in Canada and moving to cheaper jurisdictions, such as the U.S. south and Mexico.
The town of St. Thomas has historically been an auto industry town.
VW's decision to build a battery factory approximately the size of 210 football fields marks a revival of sorts for the area.

It would be the largest EV supply chain investment in Canada, although there are reports that Honda Motor Co. Ltd. may soon announce plans to manufacture battery cells and EVs on an even larger scale.

Such projects have been beset by controversy over whether government incentives that entice automakers to build here end up cancelling out any economic benefits from the jobs and tax base created by the operations.
The Parliamentary Budget Office described the economic impact of VW's proposed plant as "marginal," though that report has also been critiqued by industry advocates as too simplistic.
In 2023, sales of passenger EVs in Canada declined by 12.6 per cent from a year earlier.
Sales of commercial EVs, a larger category that includes SUVs, minivans and other vehicles, increased by 85 per cent.
Overall, total sales increased 49 per cent to 185,300 vehicles sold in 2023 from about 123,500 vehicles sold in 2022.

Paladino said Canada's EV mandate may be creating pushback from consumers and there is still more work to be done to make EVs more affordable, including creating more charging stations, especially in condominiums.
Paladino said EVs offer "a more polished driving experience" and better performance than their internal combustion engine counterparts.

Paladino stated: "We're living it every day and we see the path forward," adding it is more about ensuring consumers come along excited rather than being forced, which is why they are growing the portfolio.
Paladino said Audi Canada Inc. is set to introduce a slate of new EVs in every segment during the next six to eight months.
Audi will also continue to produce internal combustion engine vehicles because some customers are not all the way there yet.
Research is underway to redesign vehicles so that the interior is more like "a moving living room," aiming for extreme comfort for resting, working, and commuting.

A clarification noted that Vito Paladino originally said the plant would make solid-state batteries, but a spokesperson later clarified he meant to say the plant would eventually make solid-state batteries.
source_title: VW hones in on manufacturing EV 'battery of the future' in Canada

facts: Lithium-ion battery pack manufacturing is a crucial process in producing energy storage solutions for various applications, including electric vehicles, consumer electronics, and renewable energy systems.
The process involves carefully assembling individual lithium-ion cells into packs to ensure optimal performance, safety, and longevity.
Key stages of the manufacturing process include cell selection, module assembly, battery management system integration, and quality control.
Advancements in technology have led to improvements in energy density, charging efficiency, and environmental sustainability for lithium-ion batteries.
AceOn specialises in producing bespoke lithium-ion battery packs as a leading supplier in the UK.
AceOn's skilled engineering team has extensive expertise in lithium-ion technology to help clients select ideal battery cells tailored to specific needs.
AceOn provides solutions for industrial use, renewable energy systems, and electric vehicles.
AceOn handles everything from prototype development to small production runs and large-scale mass manufacturing.
AceOn primarily focuses on crafting Lithium-Ion 18650 battery packs.
AceOn utilises reputable cell manufacturers such as Samsung, LG Chem, Panasonic, and Sanyo.
AceOn can produce battery packs using various cells, including Li-ion 21700 cells, Li-ion prismatic cells, and Li-ion polymer cells.
AceOn provides affordable UN38.3 testing for lithium-ion batteries, significantly reducing costs compared to UK and European testing facilities.
AceOn already has several lithium battery packs with UN38.3 transportation approval.
Custom lithium-ion battery packs can be tailored to specific business needs by considering factors such as voltage, capacity, size, and shape.

Custom packs can include advanced Battery Management Systems (BMS) explicitly designed for intended applications to enable enhanced monitoring, control, and protection against overcharging, overheating, and short circuits.
Integrating BMS improves safety, extends battery lifespan, reduces the need for frequent replacements, and provides a cost-effective solution.
Custom battery packs support scalability, allowing businesses to adjust production based on demand from small prototype batches to large-scale runs.
Using custom lithium-ion battery packs can lead to long-term cost savings by optimising design and performance for specific applications to reduce energy consumption and improve overall efficiency.
Custom battery packs may improve brand differentiation by offering unique solutions tailored to specific products in a competitive market.
AceOn operates from a site in Telford, Shropshire.
AceOn prioritises quality control and safety, ensuring all products meet rigorous standards including UN38.3 transportation approval.
AceOn's flexible manufacturing capabilities accommodate small-scale and large-scale production runs without sacrificing precision or quality.
Lead times for production vary depending on the complexity and scale of the order.
Prototypes can generally be provided quickly, while larger production runs may take several weeks.
source_title: Custom Lithium Ion Battery Pack Manufacturing | AceOn Group

facts: A 2,000-acre slice of land in Northern Georgia, about 45 miles east of Atlanta, was pitched for years by state officials to various automakers before being secured by Rivian.
Pat Wilson has served as Commissioner of the State of Georgia’s Economic Development Department since November 2016.
Wilson previously showed the property to Volvo and Toyota/Mazda but could not come to terms with them on the land.
Large-scale automakers were unwilling to join Kia, which operates a massive $1.8 billion, 2.2 million square foot factory just miles away from the Georgia-Alabama border.
Rivian Automotive requested information for the site after rolling its first production units off an assembly line in Normal, Illinois and completing its first deliveries earlier this year.
The project represents Georgia’s single most significant investment in state history at $5 billion.
CEO RJ Scaringe drove around the 2,000-acre site in a Rivian R1T to plot ideas for his company’s second U.S.-located automotive assembly plant.
RJ Scaringe was genuinely concerned about keeping the area environmentally stable and prioritized preserving its natural beauty.
Rivian wanted to preserve the landscape's beauty and integrate its future automotive facility into the topography.
The facility is expected to hit an employment of 7,500 people in 2028.
Local residents gave their blessing for the Economic Development Department to offer the area to large industries because the project would not intrude on them.
Wilson clarified that the department does not propose sites unless they are invited to do so.
Rivian’s elevated expectations and accelerated timeline scared off other regions competing for “Project Terra”.
Construction is scheduled to begin in Georgia in Summer 2022, with production lines ramping up in 2024.
Rivian hopes to launch production around two years after construction crews break ground to meet its speed-to-market needs.
The company locked up the 2,000-acre land plot and announced it during its first quarterly earnings call as a publicly-traded entity.
The plant sits in the Interstate 20 corridor, providing easy access to the Port of Savannah and the State’s 5,000 miles of railway for delivering manufacturing materials.
Georgia’s unemployment rate was listed at just 2.8% for November 2021 by Department of Labor statistics, making it the fourth-lowest rate federally.
Other states' unemployment rates mentioned were Nebraska (1.8%), Utah (2.1%), and Oklahoma (2.5%).
Governor Brian Kemp kept the State’s workforce relatively operational through the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 and 2021 by creating structure for the State.
Georgia committed to addressing Rivian’s employment concerns by securing plans for a Quick Start workforce training program facility at the future automotive plant.
Quick Start is a State-sponsored program created in 1967 that provides customized workforce training for expanding industries.
The program runs through the Georgia Technical College System and gives workers free, hands-on, in-depth training that contributes to the state’s economy.
Wilson stated that the program allows taxpayer dollars to be funded back into local communities through job training.
The program resulted in the first car ever built at the Kia Factory in West Point being fully operational rather than a prototype.
Wilson stated that the Quick Start program retains talent in Georgia, keeping the State’s workforce and brilliant minds local.
Wilson expressed hope that more EV makers will come to Georgia.
Wilson noted there will be more change in the automotive industry in the next ten years than in the last 100.
Wilson noted that these plants will create jobs 60 years down the road.
source_title: EXCLUSIVE: 'Rivian wanted what Georgia had': How the Peach State became Rivian's $5B match

facts: Trip Tollison, CEO and President of the Savannah Economic Development Authority (SEDA), shared details at a Chamber luncheon about how Hyundai chose to locate in Bryan County.
The new facility is reported to be the largest economic development project in the State of Georgia’s history.
The project will bring more than $5.5+ billion in investment from the Hyundai Motor Company.
The project will create 8,000+ jobs.
Thousands of additional jobs will be created by various parts suppliers that will become public over the next few months.
Governor Kemp led the State and the Georgia Economic Development by offering necessary incentives to Hyundai, thereby separating Georgia from many other states considered for the same investment.

In 2014 The Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (JDA) was created and that began the process of searching for an original equipment manufacturer and developing the necessary site for such a project.
The JDA is a joint venture of Chatham, Effingham, Bryan, and Bulloch Counties.
The JDA provided more than $15 million towards the purchase of the property.
Under the Governor’s leadership, the JDA purchased the mega-site in 2021 by leveraging the proceeds of selling properties in Chatham County.

During the luncheon, Tollison and SEDA recognized many who contributed to making this announcement possible including the county governments of the JDA, The City of Savannah, Thomas and Hutton, Georgia Power, and Bouhan Falligant.
source_title: SEDA Outlines Hyundai Project at Chamber's CEO and Owner's Council Luncheon, - Savannah Chamber

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp announced that DAS Corp. (DAS), an automotive parts company, will establish a new manufacturing facility in Metter.
The facility will create 300 new jobs and generate more than $35 million in investment in Candler County.
This initiative will also involve collaborative indirect investments.
Established in Korea in 1987, DAS specializes in designing and manufacturing seating systems, safety seating components, and seating structures.
DAS is a key supplier for Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG) and Hyundai Motor Group.

Sean Kim, Chief Operating Officer at DAS, stated that the strategic location near prominent cities like Savannah and Macon facilitated convenient access and played a pivotal role in the decision-making process.

Looking ahead to future expansion prospects, Metter was deemed an ideal location in anticipation of its role as a global electric vehicle production hub thanks to its close proximity to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Located in the Candler County Industrial Park in Metter, the new DAS manufacturing facility will primarily produce automotive seat structures.
Operations are projected to begin in the latter half of 2024.
Over the course of the next five years, DAS will be recruiting for assembly technicians.
DAS intends to establish collaborations with local employment services as part of its hiring process.
Metter Mayor Ed Boyd stated that the City of Metter is proud to welcome DAS to Metter and Candler County.
The impact on the community will be transformative, stimulating investment in housing, creating other business opportunities, and improving the social economic wellbeing of citizens.
Glyn Thrift, Chairman of the Candler County Board of Commissioners, thanked DAS for choosing them as their new home and noted they will provide a boost to spur more economic development.

Griff Lynch, President and CEO of the Georgia Ports Authority, stated that the authority is proud to support the growing automotive manufacturing presence in the state and work together to drive economic activity and jobs.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was executed in partnership with the Candler County Industrial Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Electric Cities of Georgia, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia is well-positioned for the arrival of Hyundai’s supplier network as a result of years of consistent investment in site preparation, workforce solutions, and connectivity.
DAS is another exciting addition to Georgia’s automotive community.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
As the market transitions to electric vehicles, Georgia has pursued the entire supply chain, creating more than $25 billion in investments and 30,000 jobs since 2018.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
Suppliers for the Hyundai Metaplant resulted in over $2 billion in investment last fiscal year, alone, helping Georgia achieve a third straight year of record-breaking economic growth.
Logistics assets, including a reliable network of rail lines and highways, connect companies like DAS to key business partners in all corners of the state and to markets across the world.
source_title: GA: DAS Corp. to Bring 300 Automotive Jobs to Metter with New Manufacturing Facility | Trade and Industry Development

facts: Georgia has landed its second EV plant in less than a year.
The development is another chess move toward satisfying the state's goal of emerging 'at the forefront of the electric vehicle revolution.'
Hyundai's EV plant in Georgia received $1.8B in tax incentives.
source_title: Hyundai in Georgia: EV plant receives $1.8B in tax incentives - Atlanta Business Chronicle

facts: Honda announced key next steps in the establishment of its EV Hub in Ohio that will lead the company’s journey to an electrified future in North America.

In October 2022, Honda announced that it would invest $700 million to retool several of its existing auto and powertrain plants to establish the new EV Hub in Ohio, to prepare for the production of battery electric vehicles in 2026.
The Marysville Auto Plant (MAP) will be Honda’s first auto plant in the U.S. to transition to making EVs.
As part of the EV Hub in Ohio, Honda will transform its Marysville Auto Plant (MAP), East Liberty Auto Plant (ELP) and Anna Engine Plant (AEP), leading to the start of EV production in North America.
Honda expects to maintain employment stability across all locations during these key next steps in this transition.

MAP will begin preparing for EV production as early as January 2024, by consolidating its two production lines currently making vehicles powered by internal combustion engines and hybrid-electric vehicle systems into one line.
Every MAP associate will have a position as part of this transition.
Starting later in 2023, MAP associates will begin training programs designed to prepare them for the skills required for EV production.
Production of the Honda Accord will transfer to the Indiana Auto Plant (IAP) in 2025 to maintain production volume while enabling MAP to transition to EV production.
AEP will transfer component production for two different generations of engines to the engine plant at the Alabama Auto Plant (AAP).
Machining of the engine head, which ended at AEP in February, for the existing V6 engine that powers many Honda light trucks will be added to existing AAP operations beginning March 2023.
Engine block casting for the all-new V6 engine just introduced in the all-new 2023 Honda Pilot, will begin at AAP in August 2023.
These two moves will allow AEP to re-allocate space for future IPU Case production.
These cases will be combined with battery modules produced at the new joint venture battery facility Honda is establishing in Ohio with LG Energy Solution (LGES).
These EV batteries will power the EVs to be produced at MAP and ELP.

Transmission Plant – Georgia (TMP-G) will partner with a Honda supplier to install a new line to build e-axles, an essential component of EVs that combines the electric motor, gearing components and power electronics.
The supplier will install, own, and operate the new e-axle line in space currently occupied by a transmission production line that is not in operation at TMP-G.
Honda and LGES recently held the official groundbreaking ceremony for the new joint venture EV battery plant to be located in Fayette County, near Jeffersonville, Ohio.

The two companies have committed to invest $3.5 billion in the new JV facility of over 2 million square feet, which is scheduled to be completed by the end of 2024, and aims for approximately 40GWh of annual production capacity.
The overall investment of the two companies in the JV facility is projected to reach $4.4 billion.

As part of its goal to achieve carbon neutrality for all products and corporate activities by 2050, Honda has a vision to make battery-electric and fuel cell electric vehicles represent 100% of its vehicle sales by 2040.

Even as Honda accelerates preparation for EV production, the company plans to sustain current ICE and hybrid-electric vehicle production in order to continue to meet anticipated strong customer demand through 2030 and beyond.
The sustained success of ICE and hybrid-electric vehicle sales also will support the required investment in the electrified future.
Honda has been producing automobiles in America for over 40 years, beginning in November 1982 with the start of automobile production at the Marysville Auto Plant.
In 2022, more than two-thirds of all Honda and Acura automobiles sold in the U.S. were made in America, using domestic and globally sourced parts.
Honda’s cumulative auto production in America now exceeds 30 million vehicles.

Honda established manufacturing operations in America in 1979 with the start of motorcycle production in Marysville, Ohio, and today has one of the largest and most diverse U.S. manufacturing footprints of any international company.

The company’s 12 plants employ more than 22,000 associates in America, supporting operations that have an annual capacity of more than 1.27 million automobiles, 1.52 million engines, 500,000 power equipment products and 300,000 powersports products, as well as the HondaJet advanced light jet and GE Honda HF120 turbofan engines.
Cumulatively, Honda has invested $19 billion in its U.S. manufacturing operations, including more than $3.2 billion over the past five years alone.
The company also works with over 600 U.S. original equipment suppliers, with U.S.-sourced parts purchases of approximately $470 billion since 1979.
source_title: Honda Details Manufacturing Changes In Preparation To Join Other Hopeful Carmakers for U.S. EV Production

facts: Honda Precision Parts of Georgia (HPPG) recently marked the 10th anniversary of manufacturing transmissions at its west Georgia facility.
HPPG started transmission production in 2006 with 179 associates and now has annual capacity to build 375,000 transmissions with more than 500 associates.
Today, HPPG builds transmissions for Honda products including the Odyssey, Pilot and Ridgeline.
