The company's cumulative investment is more than $217 million dollars.
Masahiko Kayama, president at HPPG, said: "We have made great strides during the last decade, and our associates have been a driving force for our success here in Tallapoosa."

Masahiko Kayama added: "Our future is bright and we look forward to sharing even more accomplishments with our associates and the local community as we continue to make high quality products for our customers."

HPPG expanded rapidly, starting with production of a five-speed transmission when the plant opened in 2006 before investing in innovation in 2012 to produce the six-speed transmissions currently made at the facility.
HPPG produced its one-millionth transmission on October 4, 2010, with total cumulative production now reaching approximately 2.7 million transmissions to date.
Honda has manufactured more than 21 million transmissions in America since starting production of automatic transmissions in Ohio in 1989.
source_title: Honda Plant Marks 10 Years of Transmission Manufacturing

facts: President Donald Trump expressed optimism regarding growth in the U.S. auto industry during his address to Congress on Tuesday night.
Trump cited Honda's decision to build a new plant in Indiana as a key example of auto industry growth.
Trump stated that plants are opening up all over the place and deals are being made never seen, attributing this to an election win and tariffs.
Trump claimed numerous car companies have announced they will be building massive automobile plants in America.
Trump specifically mentioned Honda just announcing a new plant in Indiana, describing it as one of the largest anywhere in the world.
Trump stated he spoke to the top people at the three major auto companies that day and found them excited.
Trump's comments reference a recent Reuters report stating Honda will produce its next Civic model in Indiana rather than Mexico due to U.S. tariffs.
WRTV reached out to Honda for clarification regarding the new plant announcement.
Honda denied that any new plans had been announced.
Honda currently operates a production plant in Greensburg, Indiana.
A Honda spokesperson stated the company has made no such announcement and will not comment on the report.
The Honda Civic has been made at Honda's Indiana Auto Plant since the facility opened in 2008.
Honda follows a longstanding approach to build products close to the customer, with flexibility to produce products in each region based on customer needs and market conditions.
A Honda spokesperson acknowledged President Trump's recognition of Honda's commitment to American manufacturing.
The spokesperson emphasized that the company did not announce any plans for a new plant in Indiana or elsewhere in the U.S. at this time.

Honda thanked President Trump for recognizing its commitment to manufacturing vehicles in America and expressed a desire to work with the administration and Congress on pro-growth policies supporting a robust U.S. auto industry.
While not announcing new plant plans, Honda has invested over $3 billion in advanced vehicle manufacturing in America in just the past three years.
Honda's cumulative investment in American advanced vehicle manufacturing totals more than $24.7 billion.
source_title: Trump mentions new Indiana Honda plant, but company denies announcement

facts: Honda Director, President and Representative Executive Officer (Global CEO) Toshihiro Mibe held a press briefing on May 14, 2026, regarding initiatives toward rebuilding its automobile business.

Honda will focus on rebuilding its automobile business structure over the next three years to achieve consolidated operating profit of more than 1.4 trillion yen by the fiscal year ending March 31, 2029.
Honda is strategically reallocating corporate resources and prioritizing North America, Japan, India, and China as priority regions for future growth.
Starting in 2027, Honda will launch next-generation hybrid models featuring an all-new hybrid system and platform.
Honda plans to launch 15 next-generation hybrid models globally by the end of the fiscal year ending March 31, 2030, primarily in North America.
In North America, Honda will launch large-size hybrid models in the D-segment or above in 2029.
Honda presented prototypes of its next-generation hybrid models: the Honda Hybrid Sedan Prototype and the Acura Hybrid SUV Prototype, scheduled for sale within the next two years.
Honda aims to reduce the cost of its next-generation hybrid system by more than 30% compared to the hybrid system introduced in 2023.
Honda will improve the fuel economy of next-generation hybrid models by more than 10% by combining the new hybrid system with a next-generation platform and newly-developed electric AWD unit.
Honda is developing next-generation ADAS with plans for market launch in 2028, applying it to more than 15 models over five years.
Honda will reallocate all excess capacity at its auto plants in Ohio to gasoline and hybrid vehicle production.
Honda will make all of its auto plants in North America capable of producing hybrid models.
Honda and joint venture partner LG Energy Solution will convert part of the EV battery production lines at L-H Battery Company to hybrid battery production.

Honda will increase the local content of assemblies (ASSY) and component parts of motors and inverters by more than four times the current level in North America to prepare for hybrid vehicle expansion and mitigate U.S. tariff impacts.
Honda will pursue a battery procurement strategy with a focus on competitiveness in North America.
Honda will indefinitely suspend the project to build a comprehensive EV value chain in Canada.
Honda will not pursue complete in-house battery sourcing at this time, instead fully utilizing L-H Battery facilities and producing batteries for hybrid vehicles and other applications.
Honda is laying groundwork for introducing a highly competitive EV hardware platform for the future.
Honda continues research and development of all-solid-state batteries.
Honda will apply its ASIMO OS to EVs and hybrid models to increase mobility value.
Honda adopted a domain-based E&E architecture for EVs and hybrids to flexibly address country-specific differences and market conditions.
Honda expects EV-related losses to be resolved by the fiscal year ending March 31, 2029.
Over the three years until the fiscal year ending March 31, 2029, Honda will reallocate resources from EVs to hybrid vehicles and control EV-related investments at approximately 0.8 trillion yen.
Honda will invest 1.0 trillion yen in software technologies and 4.4 trillion yen in gasoline and hybrid vehicles over the three-year period, totaling 6.2 trillion yen in resource investment.
Honda expects to generate more than 7 trillion yen in operating cash flow after R&D adjustment by the fiscal year ending March 31, 2029, excluding EV-related losses.
Honda will maintain stable dividend payments with a target DOE of 3%.
Honda aims to realize its long-standing ROIC (Return on Invested Capital) target of 10% by the fiscal year ending March 31, 2031.
Honda will reduce development cost, timeframe, and workload by half compared to 2025 levels through a “Triple Half” approach.
Honda will improve production efficiency by approximately 20% over the next five years by efficiently executing and allocating resource investments in new models and equipment.
source_title: Summary of 2026 Honda Business Briefing | Honda Global Corporate Website

facts: Hyundai Motor Group broke ground on its first U.S. plant dedicated to making electric vehicles in Bryan County west of Savannah, Georgia.
The manufacturing plant represents a $5.5 billion investment.
Vast areas of the site have already been cleared of trees ahead of the groundbreaking ceremony.
The factory is planned to open in 2025.
The facility will employ at least 8,100 workers.
Production capacity will be up to 300,000 electric vehicles per year.
Euisun Chung, executive chairman of Hyundai Motor Group, stated the plant will be the envy of the industry.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp called it the largest economic development project the state has ever seen.
Gov. Kemp described the project as transformational for the region's current and future generations.
The groundbreaking ceremony took place two weeks before the Nov. 8 election.
A yellow-and-black robotic dog delivered champagne flutes for Gov. Kemp and top Hyundai executives during a celebratory toast.
Officials and dignitaries participated in turning ceremonial scoops of dirt at the event.
Gov. Kemp and Sen. Raphael Warnock shared a brief handshake after the ceremony.
Sen. Raphael Warnock, a Democratic junior U.S. Senator, stated he wants to see more jobs in Georgia.
Warnock's Senate race against Republican Herschel Walker will help determine party control of the U.S. Senate.
Gov. Kemp faces an Election Day rematch with Democrat Stacey Abrams.
State and local officials worked to lure Hyundai to Georgia with a package of $1.8 billion in tax breaks and incentives.
President Joe Biden has embraced boosting U.S. production and sales of electric vehicles as part of his strategy for slowing climate change and adding manufacturing jobs.
The White House recently announced $2.8 billion in grants to ramp up EV battery production in 12 states.
A sweeping climate and health care law passed by congressional Democrats in August includes incentives for EV manufacturing and purchasing.
The law provides tax credits for EV buyers worth up to $7,500.
Changes to the tax credit limit eligibility only to electric vehicles produced in North America.
Hyundai EVs will not qualify for the tax credit until the company starts production in Georgia.
South Korea's ambassador to the U.S., Tae-yong Cho, told dignitaries that his country believes the revised EV tax credit unfairly excludes Hyundai.
Ambassador Cho stated Korean companies are at risk of being disadvantaged by the provision and that limiting consumer choices is not good for Korea’s partnership, Georgia, or climate change efforts.
Sen. Warnock voted in support of the Inflation Reduction Act containing the revised tax credit.
Warnock defends the law overall as proof the U.S. is serious about investing in EV manufacturing.
Warnock introduced a bill to push back the date the tax credit revisions take effect.
Warnock asked the Treasury Department to use maximum flexibility in adopting regulations for the new criteria.
Warnock stated he will continue working with Ambassador Cho and other officials to perfect the project.
Rivian announced plans last year for a $5 billion EV plant east of Atlanta.
Rivian plans to employ 7,500 workers at its Georgia facility.
A Morgan County judge rejected local governments' plan to exempt Rivian from an estimated $700 million in property taxes.
Rivian agreed to pay $300 million in lieu of the exempted property taxes.
State and local officials are weighing an appeal of the judge’s decision regarding Rivian's tax break.
The property tax break was a major component of the $1.2 billion in incentives offered to Rivian.
Rivian, a California automaker, has seen its stock value decline sharply this year.
Rivian recently announced a recall to tighten a loose fastener that could affect drivers' ability to steer.
Gov. Kemp expressed confidence that the Rivian Georgia plant will still happen despite the tax ruling.
Gov. Kemp noted the vast majority of people want the Rivian project to come and added excitement about the whole EV marketplace in Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai breaks ground on $5.5B electric car plant in Georgia

facts: Governor Brian Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp joined President and Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and President and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America José Muñoz to celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s historic impact on Georgia and provide an update on Metaplant America progress.
The facility will officially start vehicle production in the fourth quarter of this year.
Hyundai has been a partner to Georgia for decades, helping recruit businesses, giving back to communities, and working with world-class colleges and universities.
Hyundai and Genesis electric vehicles including the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60, and Electrified GV70 were displayed outside the State Capitol.
The hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck was displayed and will be used for logistics needs between Hyundai’s manufacturing facilities and suppliers.
Governor Kemp officially declared February 26 as Hyundai Day throughout the state.
The Georgia General Assembly passed a Resolution commending Hyundai Motor Group’s dedication to the state and recognizing February 26 as Hyundai Day at the State Capitol.
Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of great paying American jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) project was announced in May 2022.
LG Energy Solution was named as the joint venture partner in the manufacture of battery components.
An additional expansion of the battery facility was announced alongside the joint venture.
Hyundai and SK On are building an EV battery manufacturing facility in Bartow County that will supply Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. plants.
Hyundai Motor Group’s planned investment in Georgia has reached more than $12.6 billion.
The planned investment brings with it the creation of 12,000 direct, long-term jobs.

Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones, House Speaker Jon Burns, legislative leaders, GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson, and officials from the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority joined the celebration.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones praised Governor Kemp, state agencies, and the University System of Georgia, especially Georgia Tech, for their partnership with Hyundai.
Up to 300,000 Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia EVs are expected to be produced annually at the Metaplant.
17 HMGMA suppliers have announced projects across 10 Georgia counties spanning beyond southeast Georgia.
Further supplier announcements are expected.
Supplier facilities are expected to create more than 6,700 new jobs and more than $2.5 billion in investment.
Supplier facilities will be operational prior to the opening of HMGMA.
Speaker Jon Burns credited Governor Kemp and the General Assembly for sound policy direction that enabled Hyundai’s Southeast Georgia investment.
The Metaplant site is nearly 3,000 acres.
Four building exteriors are complete at the site with outfitting for internal operations underway.
The Metaplant’s architecture incorporates technologically advanced elements, design features representing the Savannah area, Hyundai’s brand identity, nature, and clean energy.
HMGMA is hiring full-time employees known as Meta Pros rapidly for training and skills on machinery, office and clerical work, and numerous other employment roles.
HMGMA currently has 566 full-time employees.
Approximately 75 percent of the current workforce are from the Greater Savannah area.
An additional 37 employees are from Georgia but are outside the local area.
Those interested in applying to be a Meta Pro can apply online.
Georgia Quick Start broke ground on its customized training center at the Metaplant site on February 8, 2024.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgians will be trained in high-skill careers as Meta Pros at one of the most advanced vehicle assembly and battery plants in the world.
HMGMA partnered with local technical colleges to prepare students for entry-level employment at the Metaplant.

Electrical Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate of Credit (EVP TCC) training is available at Coastal Pines Technical College’s Baxley and Waycross campuses, Southeastern Technical College’s Vidalia campus, Savannah Technical College, and Ogeechee Technical College.
Technical College System of Georgia Commissioner Greg Dozier highlighted the partnership as a commitment to innovation, excellence, and cultivating a highly skilled workforce for the EV industry.
In September 2023, Georgia Institute of Technology (Georgia Tech) and Hyundai Motor Company signed a memorandum of understanding to collaborate on research and applications for sustainable mobility.
The Georgia Tech collaboration includes developing a hydrogen economy, workforce development, and making cities smarter and more sustainable.
U.S. News & World Report ranked Georgia Tech as the top public university and No. 3 nationally in energy and fuel research.
Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera stated the strategic partnership represents one of their most significant corporate relationships to advance science and produce the next-generation workforce.
Since 1998, Hyundai has directed over $6 million towards local Georgia communities through philanthropic programs and partnerships.
Hyundai is promoting STEM programs in local K-12 schools and hosting educational programs on alternative energy sources.
Hyundai supports local organizations dedicated to affordable homeownership and focuses on environmental stewardship.

Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority’s Trip Tollison noted Hyundai has given more than $1 million to regional nonprofits and formed key partnerships with local educational institutions.
Trip Tollison stated Hyundai committed to $7.59 billion in capital investment in HMGMA and will create 8,500 jobs.
More than 550 Meta Pros have already been hired, with 75 percent coming from the Savannah region.
Beginning with mass production, electricity used to power the Metaplant will come from 100 percent clean power sources.
The clean power initiative aligns with Hyundai’s commitment to carbon neutrality by 2045.
A 41-acre park will be featured in front of the plant including trails, running tracks, environmental art displays, sports fields, and amenities for employee use.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is Hyundai Motor Group’s first dedicated Electric Vehicle mass-production plant located in Bryan County, Georgia.
The plant features a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem.
The facility will operate as an intelligent manufacturing plant optimizing order collection, procurement, logistics, and production utilizing AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system creates a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
Hyundai Motor Group is a global enterprise with about 250,000 employees worldwide and mobility brands including Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.
source_title: State Leaders Celebrate Hyundai Motor Group's Historic Impact on Georgia - Southeast Regional Center

entity_name: Hyundai Georgia EV Manufacturing and Battery Plant
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group Georgia Facility / HL-GA Battery Company Joint Venture Site
facts: 475 immigrants suspected of living and working in the U.S. illegally were detained at a huge Hyundai facility in Georgia on Thursday.
Steven Schrank, special agent in charge of Homeland Security Investigations in Georgia and Alabama, told reporters Friday that the majority of those detained were Korean nationals.
Detainees worked for a variety of different companies at the site, including subcontractors.
South Korea's Foreign Minister Cho Hyun said Saturday that more than 300 South Koreans were among the 475 people detained.
No criminal charges were announced during Friday's news conference.
The sweep was conducted as part of a month-long investigation into allegations of unlawful employment practices and other federal crimes.

Schrank described Thursday's raid as the largest enforcement operation at a single site in the history of Homeland Security Investigations, which is a unit of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement.

Schrank stated: "This operation underscores our commitment to protecting jobs for Georgians and Americans, ensuring a level playing field for businesses that comply with the law, safeguarding the integrity of our economy and protecting workers from exploitation."
Some detainees allegedly entered the U.S. illegally, while others were accused of overstaying visas or violating visa waivers by working.
Most of the detainees were taken to an ICE facility in Folkston, Georgia, near the Florida-Georgia state line.
ICE stated Friday night that those arrested were "found to be working illegally, in violation of the terms of their visas and/or statuses."
One green card holder from Mexico was arrested after being determined to be removable from the U.S. based on multiple criminal convictions.
The investigation is ongoing, and additional arrests and charges are possible.
Schrank said the operation "sends a clear message that those who exploit the system and undermine our workforce will be held accountable."
Schrank noted: "We welcome all companies who want to invest in the U.S., and if they need to bring workers in for building or other projects, that's fine — but they need to do it the legal way."
The president of Georgia's chapter of the AFL-CIO called the raid politically motivated and said ICE has been escalating its presence at workplaces across the state.
Yvonne Brooks stated: "This raid is the latest in an ongoing campaign of harassment that has targeted immigrant Georgians as they try to earn an honest living."

Brooks added: "Arresting and detaining workers, who are exploited every day and risk their lives every day on the job, creates an atmosphere of fear that terrorizes workers and their families and increases the workload burden on their coworkers."

The operation involved law enforcement agents from multiple federal agencies, including ICE, Border Patrol, the FBI, the Drug Enforcement Administration, the IRS, and the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives.
Earlier, the Atlanta office of the ATF said 450 people were detained in a post on social media that included pictures from the operation.

The sweep targeted one of Georgia's largest and most high-profile manufacturing sites, touted by Gov. Brian Kemp and other officials as the biggest economic development project in the state's history.
Hyundai Motor Group began manufacturing electric vehicles a year ago at the $7.6 billion plant.
The plant employs about 1,200 people.
Hyundai partnered with LG Energy Solution to build an adjacent battery plant, slated to open next year.
The search shut down construction on the battery plant.
Hyundai Motor Company said it was aware of the operation and was "closely monitoring the situation while working to understand the specific circumstances."
Hyundai stated that based on current understanding, none of those detained is directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company.
Hyundai committed to full compliance with all laws and regulations in every market where it operates, including employment verification requirements and immigration laws.
Hyundai expects the same commitment from all partners, suppliers, contractors, and subcontractors.
Hyundai is reviewing its processes to ensure that all parties working on its projects maintain high standards of legal compliance.
Hyundai will conduct an investigation to ensure all suppliers and their subcontractors comply with all laws and regulations.
LG stated it was "closely monitoring the situation and gathering all relevant details."
LG couldn't immediately confirm how many of its employees or Hyundai workers had been detained.
LG said its top priority is ensuring the safety and well-being of employees and partners, and that it will fully cooperate with relevant authorities.
ICE spokesman Lindsay Williams confirmed federal authorities conducted an enforcement operation at the 3,000-acre site west of Savannah.
Agents were focused on the construction site for the battery plant.

The South Korean Foreign Ministry dispatched officials to the site and urged the U.S. Embassy in Seoul to exercise extreme caution to ensure legitimate rights and interests of Korean citizens are not infringed upon.
Georgia State Patrol troopers blocked roads to the Hyundai site.
The Georgia Department of Public Safety confirmed it was dispatched to assist federal authorities.
Video posted to social media showed workers in yellow safety vests lined up as an HSI agent told them: "We're Homeland Security. We have a search warrant for the whole site."
The agent instructed: "We need construction to cease immediately" and "We need all work to end on the site right now."
The Trump administration has undertaken sweeping ICE operations as part of a mass deportation agenda, with immigration officers raiding farms, construction sites, restaurants, and auto repair shops.
Pew Research Center cited preliminary Census Bureau data saying the U.S. labor force lost more than 1.2 million immigrants from January through July.
Hyundai and LG's battery joint venture, HL-GA Battery Company, said it is cooperating fully with appropriate authorities and paused construction of the battery site to assist work.
Plant spokesperson Bianca Johnson said operations at Hyundai's EV manufacturing plant weren't interrupted.
Johnson stated: "This did not impact people getting to work."
Johnson added that production and normal office hours had already begun for the day when authorities shut down access.
The Hyundai site is in Bryan County, which saw its population increase by more than a quarter in the early 2020s.
Bryan County stood at almost 47,000 residents in 2023.
The county's Asian population went from 1.5% in 2018 to 2.2% in 2023, with growth primarily among people of Indian descent.
source_title: 475 people detained in Georgia Hyundai raid by ICE, other agencies, officials say - CBS News

facts: Hyundai Motor and Georgia Tech announced a multidecade partnership in August 2023 to work together in research and applications to support sustainable mobility, the hydrogen economy, and workforce development.
About a year and a half after the partnership announcement, Georgia Tech President Angel Cabrera stated the collaboration has been a win-win situation for both sides.
In an interview in Seoul on Jan. 23, Georgia Tech President Angel Cabrera said Hyundai has become synonymous with technology.
Hyundai Motor decided to invest $12.6 billion in constructing a new electric vehicle-dedicated manufacturing plant and battery production sites in Georgia.
The Georgia EV and battery facility construction is being done in partnerships with Korean battery firms LG Energy Solution and SK On.
Georgia Tech President Angel Cabrera noted that Hyundai is already actively recruiting students, and Georgia Tech students want to work for innovative companies like Hyundai.
Cabrera stated that Hyundai's advantage lies in its very exciting sense of purpose and project for the future.

When asked about US President Donald Trump's pledge to remove government subsidies for electric vehicles, Georgia Tech President Angel Cabrera dismissed concerns that it would hinder EV sector growth.

Cabrera said he does not think the new administration is anti-EV but may challenge subsidies, emphasizing the idea is to let the market develop technology and that the future of mobility will be electric.
Georgia Tech President Angel Cabrera purchased an Ioniq 5, Hyundai's award-winning EV, for himself seven months ago as his first pure electric vehicle.
Cabrera expressed confidence that customers will not want to return to internal combustion engine-powered cars once they experience an EV.
Georgia Tech is currently exploring a co-location of Hyundai staff inside its facilities to conduct research there.
There are currently about 10 ongoing joint research projects between Georgia Tech and Hyundai in areas such as EV and battery technologies.

A Georgia Tech delegation visited Hyundai’s research sites and joint labs with top Korean schools including Seoul National University and Hanyang University to understand challenges Hyundai is facing.
During their visit to Korea, the delegation met with officials from Samsung, Doosan, and Hanwha to discuss various topics.
Georgia Tech has collaborated with Samsung and Doosan in research areas of software technology and hydrogen turbines.
Hanwha is building a large solar panel production site in Dalton, Georgia, located about 145 kilometers away from Atlanta, which is home to Georgia Tech.
Georgia Tech's mission to develop leaders who advance technology aligns with Hyundai’s "Progress for Humanity" vision, leading both to always strive for innovation.
Cabrera emphasized that Hyundai is not just building traditional cars but is constantly trying to figure out how to build new vehicle technology.
About 10 years ago, Georgia Tech began offering master’s programs online despite initial concerns that it would hurt the university's brand.
Georgia Tech now offers three online master's courses in computer science, data analytics, and cybersecurity.
There are about 20,000 students enrolled in remote classes at Georgia Tech, making up the largest master's programs in the world.
Cabrera noted that Hyundai cars are now everywhere in the US and have become one of the biggest brands in the country.
source_title: ‘Hyundai has become synonymous with technology’ - THE INVESTOR

facts: Hyundai Motor Group officially opened its new Metaplant in Ellabell on March 27.
Hyundai Motor Group will invest a total of $12.6 billion in Georgia.
The facility will produce 500,000 electric and hybrid vehicles annually.
The project was originally slated as a $5.54 billion investment with a capacity of 300,000 units per year.
The new announcement marks a substantial increase in both scope and impact compared to the original plan.
The Hyundai Metaplant is the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Hyundai’s roots in Georgia date back to 2005 with the construction of Kia’s manufacturing plant in West Point.
Then-Governor Sonny Perdue welcomed Hyundai Motor Group’s first investment in the Peach State twenty years ago.
Governor Brian Kemp announced both the opening of the Hyundai Metaplant and an expansion at the Bryan County facility this week.
Tens of thousands of Georgians are employed in the automotive and electric vehicle (EV) industries.
The automotive and EV industries generate billions in economic activity each year in Georgia.
Georgia is now considered the unquestioned leader in advanced EV manufacturing following the announcement.
The Georgia Chamber thanked Hyundai Motor Group for increasing its record-breaking investment in Georgia.
The Georgia Chamber applauded Governor Brian Kemp for his tireless dedication to pro-growth policies that made the expansion possible.
The Georgia Chamber thanked state and regional economic developers and other state leaders for their commitment to Georgia.
The Georgia Chamber stated that Georgia is the preferred destination for firms to meet customer demand for automobiles for decades to come.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group boosts Georgia investment to $12.6 billion with new EV metaplant - The Augusta Press

facts: Hyundai Motor Group broke ground on its first U.S. plant dedicated to making electric vehicles in Bryan County west of Savannah, Ga.
The groundbreaking ceremony took place two weeks before the Nov. 8 election.
A robotic dog delivered champagne flutes for Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp and top Hyundai executives during the celebration.
Euisun Chung, executive chairman of Hyundai Motor Group, said the plant will be the envy of the industry.
Hyundai announced a $5.5 billion manufacturing plant in Bryan County five months prior to the groundbreaking.
Vast areas of the site have already been cleared of trees following the announcement.
Hyundai plans to open the factory in 2025.
The facility will employ at least 8,100 workers.
The plant is designed to produce up to 300,000 electric vehicles per year.
Georgia officials state the project is the largest economic development project the state has ever seen.
Gov. Brian Kemp called the project transformational for the region and said it will resonate for generations to come.
The groundbreaking ceremony occurred ahead of high-stakes elections where both Kemp and Sen. Raphael Warnock face formidable challengers.
Kemp and Warnock shared a brief handshake after officials turned ceremonial scoops of dirt.
Sen. Raphael Warnock expressed a desire to see more collaboration and emphasized the need for more jobs in Georgia.
Kemp worked with state and local officials to secure a package of $1.8 billion in tax breaks and incentives to lure Hyundai to Georgia.
President Joe Biden has embraced boosting U.S. production and sales of electric vehicles as part of his strategy for slowing climate change and adding manufacturing jobs.
The White House recently announced $2.8 billion in grants to ramp up EV battery production in 12 states.
A sweeping climate and health care law passed by congressional Democrats in August includes incentives for EV manufacturing and purchasing.
The federal law provides tax credits for EV buyers worth up to $7,500.
Congressional changes to the tax credit limit eligibility only to electric vehicles produced in North America.
Hyundai EVs will not qualify for the tax credit until the company starts production in Georgia.
South Korea’s ambassador to the U.S., Tae-yong Cho, told dignitaries that his country believes the revised EV tax credit unfairly excludes Hyundai.
Ambassador Cho stated Korean companies are at risk of being disadvantaged by the provision and criticized limiting consumer choices for climate change efforts.
Warnock voted in support of the Inflation Reduction Act containing the revised tax credit but defends it as proof of U.S. seriousness on EV manufacturing.
Warnock introduced a bill to push back the effective date of the tax credit revisions.
Warnock asked the Treasury Department to use maximum flexibility in adopting regulations for the new criteria.
Warnock stated he will continue working with Ambassador Cho and other officials to perfect the project.
Rivian announced plans last year for a $5 billion EV plant east of Atlanta that would employ 7,500 workers.
A Morgan County judge rejected local governments' plan to exempt Rivian from an estimated $700 million in property taxes.
Rivian agreed to pay $300 million in lieu of the exempted property taxes.
State and local officials are weighing an appeal of the judge's decision regarding Rivian's tax break.
The property tax break was a major piece of a $1.2 billion incentive package offered to California automaker Rivian.
Rivian has seen its stock value decline sharply this year.
Rivian recently announced a big recall to tighten a loose fastener that could affect drivers' ability to steer.
Gov. Kemp expressed confidence that the Rivian Georgia plant will still happen despite the tax ruling.
Kemp stated the vast majority of people want the Rivian project to come and expressed excitement about the whole EV marketplace in Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai breaks ground on $5.5B electric car manufacturing plant in Georgia | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: Hyundai Motor Co.’s chief executive said hybrid assembly will be incorporated into the company’s electric vehicle factory near Savannah.
The hybrid assembly addition is part of a wave of expedited investment planned for Georgia.
Hyundai CEO José Muñoz told The Atlanta Journal-Constitution during his company’s annual investor meeting in New York City that hybrids will start rolling off Georgia assembly lines next year.
He projected hybrids could comprise half of the factory’s output by 2030.
The projection cites shaky American demand for EVs as a driving factor.
“You will see a good balance. I would say roughly 50-50 between hybrids and EVs,” Muñoz said.
“Our strength is the flexibility and the ability to produce different types of vehicles in the same factory.”
Hyundai announced an investment in its Georgia EV plant located in Bryan County.
The facility will produce both electric vehicles and hybrid vehicles on the same assembly lines.
Hyundai plans to ramp up hybrid production significantly at the Georgia plant.
The shift toward hybrids is driven by shaky American demand for pure EVs.
Hyundai announced a new automotive workforce expansion initiative alongside local recruitment efforts.
"Things have changed" regarding vehicle production states, according to Hyundai's announcement.
The plant will begin producing hybrids next year.
By 2030, hybrids could make up 50% of the factory's total output.
Hyundai cited its manufacturing flexibility as a key strength for adapting to market demands.
source_title: Hyundai CEO says hybrids could comprise 50% of Georgia EV plant’s output

facts: Hyundai is teaming up with Yandex spinoff Avride to jointly develop fully driverless cars for autonomous ridehailing.
The partnership will enable Avride to deploy 100 Hyundai Ioniq 5 SUVs retrofitted with autonomous driving technology this year.
Avride currently operates fleets of sidewalk delivery robots in several cities and aims to launch a commercial robotaxi service in the near future.
The company has been using the Ioniq 5 and the Hyundai Sonata as its test vehicles in Austin and Seoul for the past year.
In today’s announcement, Avride said it plans for a “significant expansion” of its fleet ahead of the launch of a commercial service.
The Ioniq 5 vehicles destined for Avride’s fleet will be assembled at the new Hyundai Metaplant America factory in Georgia and then integrated with Avride’s autonomous technology.
The first vehicles will arrive in Dallas later this year, as part of Avride’s deal with Uber to deploy autonomous vehicles exclusively on the ridehailing company’s app.
In addition to the autonomous vehicles, Avride and Hyundai say they plan to explore other opportunities, such as delivery robots and other smart mobility applications.
Avride spun out of Yandex, which is one of Russia’s biggest tech companies.
Yandex had been testing its vehicles in the US up until Russia’s 2022 invasion of Ukraine, which forced most Western businesses to cut ties with the company.

Last year, Yandex self-driving group rebranded as Avride after its parent company (now called Nebius) divested itself in a $5.2 billion sale of all of its Russian holdings and severed ties to its original country.
Avride is now headquartered in Austin.
This won’t be the Ioniq 5’s first self-driving rodeo, as Waymo recently announced plans to buy an unspecified number of Hyundai EVs for its robotaxi business.
The vehicle also serves as a platform for Motional, which is Hyundai’s robotaxi subsidiary.
source_title: Hyundai teams up with Yandex spinoff Avride to develop robotaxis | The Verge

facts: The Hyundai Motor factory will be located in Bryan County.
The factory will produce up to 300,000 Hyundai, Kia and Genesis electric cars and batteries per year.
The South Korean company had previously brought forward the start of construction from the beginning of 2023 to October 2022.
The earlier start of construction was cited as being due to new rules for electric car subsidies in the USA at the time.
The start of production at the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Bryan County was initially announced for the first half of 2025.
The Group maintained the first half of 2025 production start timeline despite the earlier construction start.
The Group has officially announced that the start of production will be brought forward to the fourth quarter.
The accelerated production timeline is presumably due to the time gained from the early start of construction.
A year and a half ago, Hyundai estimated the investment volume for the new vehicle plant at “just over 5.5 billion US dollars”.
Hyundai previously announced the creation of 8,100 jobs for the project.
In the current announcement, the Group cites a 7.59 billion dollar investment.
The current announcement specifies 8,500 direct jobs.
As announced at CES 2024, plant logistics will be partly based on Hyundai’s H2 truck Xcient.
The logistics plan is part of the “Clean Logistics Project”.
The Group is building two battery cell factories in Georgia to localize the value chain and qualify for subsidies.
One battery cell factory is being built together with LG Energy Solution near the EV plant in Bryan County.
The LG Energy Solution joint factory is scheduled to go into operation at the end of 2025 at the earliest.
The LG Energy Solution joint factory will have an annual capacity of 30 GWh.
The South Korean company is planning another battery cell factory with SK On.
The SK On factory will be built in Bartow County in the north-west of Georgia near Atlanta.
The production capacity of the SK On factory is not yet known.
The opening of the SK On factory is also scheduled for 2025.
source_title: Hyundai moves up the opening of its EV factory in Georgia to Q4 2024 - electrive.com

facts: The State of Georgia and the Savannah Harbor–Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority created a 2,284-acre megasite for economic development in May 2021.
Hyundai Motor CEO and President Jaehoon Chang and Governor Brian Kemp signed an agreement on May 20 for Hyundai to invest $5.54 billion into a facility to build electric vehicles and their batteries.
The Bryan County site on Interstate 16 at U.S. 280 expanded to 2,923 acres to accommodate the project.
Trip Tollison serves as president and CEO of the Savannah Economic Development Authority and is part of the regional Joint Development Authority.
Georgia’s largest-ever economic development project promises 8,100 jobs.
Outside suppliers are expected to invest an additional $1 billion.
Hyundai could begin construction as early as January 2023.
Production is scheduled to start in the first half of 2025.
The facility will have an annual capacity of 300,000 vehicles.
The Joint Development Authority is taking bids until June 15 for the first grading contract at the site.
Governor Brian Kemp conducted an economic development mission to Korea in 2019.
Since 2020, Georgia has announced more than 20 projects related to electric vehicles.
These EV projects total $13.54 billion in investment and nearly 18,000 jobs.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development has not announced the incentives it is offering Hyundai.
Rivian is receiving state and local tax breaks and other incentives totaling $1.5 billion.
Rivian will bring 7,500 jobs to Morgan and Walton counties.
Rivian will manufacture electric trucks and SUVs.
Hyundai has not confirmed which specific vehicles it will produce at the new plant.
The new plant will utilize smart factory technologies.
The new plant will rely on renewable energy.
Hyundai Motor North America President and CEO José Muñoz had dealer representatives for Hyundai and its luxury Genesis model attend the signing ceremony.
Kia is a Hyundai subsidiary that builds vehicles in West Point.
In March, Hyundai announced a global annual goal of selling 1.87 million units of 11 Hyundai and six Genesis electric models by 2030.
Including Kia, Hyundai's target is to produce 3.23 million electric vehicles a year.
The Bryan County site is located 16 miles west of I-95 and 28 miles from the port of Savannah.

Georgia Ports Authority Executive Director Griff Lynch stated that ongoing capacity-building at the ports of Savannah and Brunswick positions them to facilitate trade for Hyundai and future suppliers.
Georgia Central Railway runs along the site’s eastern edge.
The railway will provide last-mile freight service for supplies and first-mile service for finished products.
The railway connects to CSX in Savannah and Norfolk Southern in Macon.
Supporting infrastructure includes ports, rails, interstates, and Savannah/Hilton Head International Airport.
Trip Tollison cited the local workforce and quality of life as key factors that helped win Hyundai over.
The original land was purchased from three families last summer.
Purchasing the land allowed proposed projects to bypass initial steps and move directly to construction and prep phases.
source_title: Electric Development - Atlanta Magazine

facts: Hyundai Motor Group appointed Drew Ferguson, a former four-term Republican congressman from Georgia, as the head of its Washington office effective May 1.
Ferguson will assume the role of senior vice president of government affairs at Hyundai Motor Group to represent the company's interests and actively engage with the US government and Congress.
The group previously did not have a centralized government relations position, with Robert Hood handling communications for Hyundai Motor and Christopher Wenk handling them for Kia.

An official from Hyundai Motor Group’s Seoul headquarters stated Ferguson was recruited for his deep understanding of key industrial and trade policies under the Trump administration to establish a more effective communication channel with the US government.
Ferguson is expected to play a key role in strengthening the company’s automotive, robotics, and urban air mobility businesses in the US.
Ferguson represented Georgia’s 3rd congressional district from 2017 to 2024 and served as chief deputy whip for House Republicans between 2019 and 2023.
Prior to his time in the US Congress, Ferguson was mayor of West Point, Georgia, from 2008 to 2016, which is home to Kia’s US manufacturing plant.

Industry insiders note that creating a new senior government affairs position in Washington reflects Hyundai Motor Group’s strategic initiative to enhance its responsiveness to the increasingly volatile US trade environment.
Despite the group’s $21 billion investment pledge in the US, “nonnegotiable” 25 percent tariffs on imported vehicles and parts took effect on April 3.
The tariff move has dealt a blow to Hyundai Motor and Kia, which heavily rely on exports, with over 60 percent of their US sales in 2024 coming from imported vehicles.
Trump signaled a possible exemption for certain imported auto parts, such as engines, transmissions and powertrains, which are set to face the same 25 percent levy starting next month.

Trump stated during a meeting with reporters in the Oval Office that he is looking at something to help car companies with the tariff situation as they switch to parts made in Canada, Mexico and other places and need a little bit of time because they’re going to make them here.
This follows Trump’s recent decision to exempt Mexican and Canadian auto parts from the tariffs, a move seen as a lifeline for US carmakers like General Motors with major factories in those regions.

An industry source familiar with the matter stated that Trump’s indication of a potential waiver highlights the value of strengthening government relations through Ferguson’s appointment, positioning him to monitor developments closely and devise effective strategies.
Hyundai Motor Group has been bolstering its capabilities to proactively address trade challenges posed by Trump’s second term.
Earlier this year, the group appointed Sung Kim, who has advised the company since January 2023, as president of Hyundai Motor Co.

Kim, a former US diplomat and special representative for North Korea, reportedly played a pivotal role in arranging Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Chung Euisun’s announcement of the company’s US investment plans alongside Trump last month.
This marked the first time since Trump’s return to the White House that a Korean conglomerate announced an investment strategy at the White House.
Chung also became the first Korean business leader to have an official meeting with Trump in this term.
Experts believe Ferguson’s appointment will likely accelerate Hyundai Motor and Kia’s tariff countermeasures, including pricing strategies, in response to the turbulent US auto landscape.

Lee Ho-geun, a professor of automotive engineering at Daeduk University, stated that despite the impact of tariffs, Hyundai Motor and Kia have pledged to freeze their vehicle prices in the US for the next couple of months.

Lee Ho-geun noted that while aiming to clear existing inventory, it is critical to negotiate a reduction in tariffs to below 10 percent before the price freeze ends to minimize the blow to profitability.
Hyundai Motor’s US subsidiary has announced that it will maintain current retail vehicle prices until June 2.

During a recent investor meeting, both Hyundai Motor and Kia reportedly hinted at possible price hikes in the US in the latter half of the year, citing increased cost pressures from the ongoing tariff shock.
source_title: Hyundai Motor taps ex-congressman to lead Washington office amid trade tensions - THE INVESTOR

facts: A federal agency plans to reassess its environmental permit for Hyundai's $7.6 billion electric vehicle plant in Georgia after a conservation group complained that regulators failed to properly examine the sprawling factory's potential impacts on the area's water supply.

The Army Corps of Engineers said in a letter Friday that state and local economic development agencies that applied for the project's 2022 permit never mentioned Hyundai wanted to withdraw up to 6.6 million gallons (25 million liters) per day from the underground aquifer that's a major regional source of drinking water.
Details of the plant's needs came out earlier this year as the Georgia Environmental Protection Division considered a proposal for four new wells to supply water to the auto factory.
As a result, the Army Corps said it will revisit its finding that the project would have “negligible impacts.”

The Army Corps sent a similar letter to the Ogeechee Riverkeeper conservation group, which gave notice in June that it planned to sue if the agency refused to revisit the permit issued for the Hyundai project in October 2022.
Ben Kirsch, the riverkeeper group's legal director, said “The concentration of that pumping in one area is going to have some impacts locally, such as on domestic and agricultural wells.”
Ben Kirsch also stated, “The big question we've had throughout all this is what impact will it have on other resources — natural springs in the area, wetlands, tributaries and streams.”
The Army Corps' decision comes as Hyundai pushes to start production before the end of the year at its 2,900-acre (1,170-hectare) plant in Bryan County west of Savannah.
The site will produce EVs and the batteries that power them.
The South Korean automaker plans to employee 8,000 workers at the plant, making it the largest economic development project Georgia has ever tackled.
The Army Corps ordered no delays or disruptions to construction at the plant site as a result of its permit reassessment.

Cheri Dragos-Pritchard, a spokesperson for the Army Corps' Savannah District, said by email Monday that “At this time the permit is still valid and we have not requested that the permittee stop work.”
Cheri Dragos-Pritchard noted it wasn't known how long the additional review might take.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, the automaker’s name for its Georgia factory, said in a statement Monday that it will assist as needed to ensure the Army Corps gets the information it needs.

A Hyundai statement said, “Hyundai has worked tirelessly with the relevant authorities to ensure we are good neighbors to those in the region and that our operations do not negatively impact the community’s water resources.”

Sara Lips, a spokesperson for the state Environmental Protection Division, said the extra scrutiny by the federal government is “unlikely to impact or delay” a final decision by Georgia regulators on whether to permit wells for the Hyundai project.

The Army Corps permit obtained by state and local economic developers authorized the filling or dredging of 221 acres (89 hectares) of wetlands at the plant site just a few months after Hyundai announced plans to build its EV factory in May 2022.
The Army Corps concluded then that the project would have “negligible impacts on municipal and private water supplies.”
The Army Corps' Friday letter said the agency relied on information provided by economic developers.
Trip Tollison, president and CEO of the Savannah Area Economic Development Authority, said, “We never purposefully withheld anything.”
Tollison said he expected the updated information requested by the Army Corps to be submitted within 10 days.
Tollison stated the federal agency would typically complete its review within a month, adding that he's confident the reevaluation won't hold up the project.
Tollison said, “There’s enough water for everyone,” and “We feel really good about where we are.”
Georgia's environmental agency issued draft permits in July for the four wells to supply Hyundai and is now evaluating public comments before reaching a final decision.
The wells would be drilled in neighboring Bulloch County, where some farmers and rural residents have said they worry the auto plant will siphon water away from their crops and homes.
State regulators concluded that water withdrawals by the Hyundai plant would lower water levels in the aquifer up to 19 feet (5.8 meters) for private wells within 5 miles (8 kilometers).
State regulators said most wells won't see any impacts because they reach deeper into the ground.
The state agency has also said that nearby rivers and streams won't be affected because dense layers of rock seal off the aquifer from water at the surface.
Kirsch with the Ogeechee Riverkeeper said he hopes a second look by the Army Corps will provide more details on how state regulators reached those conclusions.
Kirsch said, “We definitely want to see the Corps independently evaluate this,” and “This should have been all considered before the wetlands were filled and buildings went up.”
source_title: U.S. Agency to Reexamine Permit for Hyundai's $7.6 Billion Electric Vehicle Plant in Georgia | Design and Development Today

facts: Professor Juanita (Babet) Villena-Alvarez presented an incredible story of Hyundai's growth from its founding in 1967 to becoming a huge conglomerate during a two-part USCB-OLLI course titled “THE KOREAN WAVE 2024 – CHANGING DEMOGRAPHICS OF THE LOWCOUNTRY”.
The course covered the massive investments by Hyundai in southeast Georgia, located only about an hour drive from Hilton Head.
Georgia Power’s Plant Vogtle, now the largest nuclear generating plant in the U.S., is completed and within 100 miles of the HMG Metaplant.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that in a single year, Georgia broke ground on the largest project in state history.
Georgia landed multiple suppliers across the state for Hyundai’s Metaplant.
LGES was welcomed to Bryan County.
Georgia is continuing to build on success to make itself the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Major investments ultimately go to hardworking Georgians in the form of paychecks, improved schools and infrastructure.
Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) is just one division of the larger Hyundai conglomerate.
South Korean firms including Sam Sung, LG, and SK-Battery have invested over $200 Billion in plants in the U.S.
Hyundai manufactures Hyundai, KIA, and Genesis autos, as well as steel and shipping.
Over 8,100 jobs will be created by HMG in Georgia.
Over 14,300 jobs will be created including supply-chain providers of components.
The Panamax capable Port of Savannah will be enlarged with additional new Ship to Shore Cranes.
Savannah is already one of the busiest ports in the U.S.
The Talmadge Bridge over the Savannah River will be raised to accommodate Panamax Ships.
Hyundai is now the #3 world auto manufacturer, after Toyota and VW.
HMG plans to build 500,000 vehicles/year at the new GA MetaPlant.
BMW produced 412,000 vehicles in 2022 as a comparison point mentioned by the professor.
The Hyundai Meta Plant covers almost 3,000 acres off of I-16.
Hyundai is partnering with Georgia Tech to collaborate on the use of Hydrogen for future Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicles.
In August 2023, Hyundai pulled out of its large plant in China.
Hyundai was founded in 1967 by Chung Ju Yung, who was born in North Korea.
A majority of South Koreans are Christians and share the values of western civilization.

facts: Autos Drive America, the leading trade association representing international automakers with operations in the U.S., celebrated Hyundai Day in Georgia.
The Georgia Assembly recently signed a resolution commending Hyundai Motor Group’s dedication to the state.
Governor Kemp recognized Monday, February 26th as Hyundai Day.
Jennifer Safavian is President and CEO of Autos Drive America.
Hyundai Motor Group announced a more than $7.5 billion investment in 2022 to develop its Metaplant America.
The Metaplant America will create 8,500 direct jobs.
The Metaplant America will assemble six models across Kia, Hyundai, and Genesis.
Hyundai and SK On are building a $5 billion EV battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia.
The Hyundai and SK On EV battery cell plant will employ 3,500 workers.
Hyundai Motor Group announced that its Metaplant will open three months ahead of schedule.
The Metaplant is slated to begin production later this year.

According to the Autos Drive America Economic Impact Report, ten international automakers have a presence in Georgia: Hyundai, Kia, BMW, Honda, Mazda, Mercedes-Benz, Mitsubishi, Nissan, Subaru, and Volkswagen.
In 2022, international automakers, dealers, and suppliers generated 67,906 jobs in Georgia.
In 2022, these entities paid $4.9 billion in total employee compensation.
International automakers invested $2.8 billion in Georgia’s auto industry.
Investments by international automakers in Georgia are growing to produce electric vehicles in the state.
In 2022, international automakers in Georgia produced 340,000 vehicles.
The 340,000 vehicles produced accounted for 100% of production in the state.
International automakers contributed $850 million in state and local revenues.
International automakers contributed $8.6 billion to Georgia's Gross State Product.
Governor Kemp and Georgia lawmakers share a commitment to growing the U.S. automotive industry, manufacturing more vehicles on U.S. soil, and creating career opportunities for the U.S. workforce.
source_title: Autos Drive America Celebrates Hyundai Day, Applauds International Automakers’ Significant Investments in Georgia - Autos Drive America

facts: Between July 1, 2023, and June 30, 2024, GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 429 facility expansions and new locations resulting in more than $20.3 billion in investment and the commitment of 26,900 new, private sector jobs statewide.
83% of expansions and new locations are on their way to communities outside the 10-county Atlanta region, representing more than 18,300 new jobs and $12.5 billion in investment.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the state is committed to continuing momentum by investing in key areas like education, workforce development and infrastructure.

Lt. Governor Burt Jones emphasized ensuring businesses have the necessary workforce, noting that the Technical College System of Georgia, University System of Georgia, College and Career Academies and private-public partnerships are building Georgia's workforce pipeline with their workforce training programs.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns highlighted historic investments in the education system, workforce development and infrastructure as part of sustained economic development success.

During FY2024, Governor and First Lady Kemp led economic development missions to Korea and Switzerland that focused on strengthening existing relationships with key investors like Hyundai Motor Group and its supplier network.
The prior year, Governor and First Lady Kemp led similar missions to Israel, France, and Switzerland.

Georgia maintained its position as a global gateway for business, landing more than 8,100 new jobs and $5.9 billion in investments from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S. market this past fiscal year.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that Georgia's strong partnerships and diverse industry landscape are major advantages for businesses and people.
The State of Georgia celebrated more than 50 years of continuous representation in Europe and Japan in FY2024, which remain among the top sources of foreign direct investment.
The coming fiscal year will mark 40 years of continuous representation in Korea, a partnership that earned the Department of Economic Development the James A. Van Fleet Award from the Korea Society.
All markets where Georgia maintains representation in business and trade accounted for 29% of total new jobs and investments in FY2024.
GDEcD Commissioner Kristi Brigman stated that the state is exploring new heights in mobility and technology, celebrating project milestones across the state during Fiscal Year 2024.
Manufacturing, including the aerospace, automotive, and e-mobility sectors, accounted for over 50% of expansions or new locations in FY2024.
Kia Georgia added the EV9 to its lineup in Troup County.
Toyota Industries Corporation established a campus in Jackson County.
StandardAero operates in Richmond County.
International life sciences company Gerresheimer completed a recent expansion in Fayette County.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America suppliers announced new locations in Bryan, Candler, Chatham, Coweta, Effingham, Harris, and Laurens Counties.
SOLARCYCLE, an innovator in solar panel recycling, announced plans for a new location in Polk County to support solar panel manufacturer Qcells’ operations.
Investments in food processing and agribusiness totaled more than $1.3 billion in FY2024.
Japanese probiotic beverage company Yakult located in Bartow County, and Korean bakery CJ Foodville located in Hall County to support East Coast operations.
Walmart will be sourcing milk from local dairy farmers at a future dairy processing facility in Lowndes County.
Technology and corporate hubs are creating at least 4,400 new jobs as a result of wins in FY2024, including Cargill Corporation establishing a new office hub in the City of Atlanta.
U.S. Soccer’s future headquarters and national training center in Fayette County reinforces Georgia’s position within entertainment, sports, and tourism.
Georgia earned the rank of No. 1 state for business from Area Development magazine for 10 consecutive years.
The state earned the No. 1 spot in Site Selection magazine’s annual Prosperity Cup rankings for successful investment recruitment in 2023.
Georgia was awarded a Gold Shovel by Area Development for achieving significant success in attracting new business investment and job creation in 2023.
Business Facilities magazine ranked Georgia as the No. 1 state for EV investments as well as a top 10 state for food processing, cybersecurity, aerospace and defense, and AI growth hubs.

facts: Top executives of Hyundai Motor Group’s key affiliates met with Georgia Governor Brian Kemp during his visit to South Korea to explore strengthening cooperation.
The State of Georgia expressed willingness to expand investment support for Hyundai Motor Group, including facilitating early operation of its new EV factory, Metaplant America (HMGMA).

Hyundai Motor Group executives attended the "Night of Georgia" event at the Conrad Hotel in Yeouido, Seoul on June 17 to exchange views with Governor Kemp on activating the company's U.S. mobility business.
The "Night of Georgia" event was organized by the State of Georgia to strengthen economic partnerships with Korean companies that have or are interested in establishing bases in the state.

Attending Hyundai Motor Group representatives included Chang Jae Hoon (President & CEO of Hyundai Motor Company), Song Ho Sung (President of Kia), Jose Munoz (Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company), Lee Gyu Suk (President of Hyundai Mobis), Seo Kang Hyun (President of Hyundai Steel), and Yeo Su Dong (President of Hyundai Transys).
Local partners of Hyundai Motor Group, including Daehan Solution and Dongwon Metal, also attended the meeting.

Other attendees included Philip Goldberg (U.S. Ambassador to Korea), Park Won Cheol (President of SKC), Kim Dong Myung (President of LG Energy Solution), and Lee Koo Young (CEO of Hanwha Solution Q CELLS division), all of whom have established operations in Georgia.
Trade between Georgia and South Korea totals approximately $15.8 billion.
This trade volume accounts for about 10% of Georgia's total trade.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Korean companies have become integral to Georgia's local community and expressed gratitude for the ongoing partnership expected to bring prosperity to both regions.
Governor Kemp promised Hyundai Motor Group that the state would help accelerate their product launch timelines in the U.S. market.
Governor Kemp offered full state support for any potential Hyundai Motor Group investments in hydrogen technology.
Georgia Minister of Economic Development Pat Wilson expressed strong interest in future hydrogen cooperation and praised Hyundai Motor's hydrogen-electric Xcient truck as highly innovative.
Hyundai Motor Company President & CEO Chang Jae Hoon stated the company is pleased to partner with Georgia and other major Korean companies on a sustainable future.
Chang noted that Hyundai's new Georgia factory, which will soon produce the latest EVs, marks a significant milestone for eco-friendly mobility.
Chang emphasized that meetings in Seoul have strengthened the partnership between Korean companies and the State of Georgia.
Georgia serves as the primary operational base for Hyundai Motor Group's U.S. business.
Since the 2010 completion of the Kia Georgia plant, Hyundai Motor Group has produced over 300,000 strategic vehicles annually for the U.S. market.
Hyundai Motor Group is constructing Metaplant America (HMGMA), scheduled to begin operations as early as October, alongside a joint battery cell factory and a battery system factory.

facts: Hyundai Motor has missed out on the United States tax incentives for its new $7.6-billion manufacturing plant -- Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America -- in Bryan County, Georgia.
Hyundai Motor said the credit did not affect its investment in the plant.

Industry sources reported Monday that Hyundai Motor did not make the list of companies that will receive tax incentives in the second round allocations of the Qualifying Advanced Energy Project Credit, or 48C.
The 48C program is an allocated tax credit funded by President Joe Biden’s Investing in America agenda through the Inflation Reduction Act.
The Biden administration has assigned a total of $10 billion to the 48C program.
Selected companies could receive up to a 30 percent tax credit for their investments on American soil.
A local report estimated that Hyundai Motor could have received up to 480 billion won ($344 million) in tax incentives.
The Georgia plant’s construction has been smooth sailing as the Korean automaker already began early-stage production of electric vehicles at HMGMA in October.
Hyundai Motor is expected to start full operation of the new plant in the first quarter of next year.
A Hyundai Motor official stated: “It’s true that (we) applied for (the 48C program) but we did not expect to receive benefits, as those tax incentives were made for parts makers not automakers.”
The same official noted: “(The 48C program) was not in consideration when (we) invested in (HMGMA).”
The US Department of Energy announced a list of 35 projects across 20 states selected for a total of $1.93 billion in the first round allocations of the 48C program in April this year.
The projects were categorized into four areas: clean energy and clean vehicle manufacturing, critical minerals and materials, grid components and modernization, and industrial decarbonization.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution will boost their joint investment in a Georgia battery manufacturing plant by $2 billion and add 400 additional jobs, the companies and the state said Thursday.
The facility is a joint venture between the two companies that now includes a total investment of $4.3 billion.

Hyundai, the world's third-largest automaker by vehicle sales, said the two companies now plan to spend a total of $7.59 billion and create 8,500 new jobs over eight years in Bryan County, Georgia.
The $7.59 billion figure includes the battery plant, which has an annual production capacity of 30 gigawatt hours (GWh), as well as a separate electrical-vehicle manufacturing plant.
The auto manufacturing plant is scheduled to begin producing vehicles in January 2025 and will build 300,000 vehicles annually.
The two manufacturing facilities are known collectively as the "Metaplant."
The Metaplant has been incentivized by $7,500 consumer tax credits in the 2022 U.S. Inflation Reduction Act.
The 2022 U.S. Inflation Reduction Act requires electric vehicles to be manufactured in the United States and sets new sourcing requirements for critical minerals and battery components.

facts: Efficiency is especially important in electric vehicles because single-charge distance impacts product selection due to battery capacity and charging problems.
Mileage per charge is ranked 1st place with 45% of considerations when purchasing an electric vehicle.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS has developed the world's first AWD disconnector technology for electric vehicles that can improve driving efficiency.
The disconnector system reduces unnecessary power loss and improves power efficiency (electric fuel efficiency) by about 6% to 8%.
Vehicle mileage increases by the same percentage due to the system.
Mounting the disconnection system on a vehicle that can travel 400 kilometers with a single charge allows it to travel about 430 kilometers with the same battery.
Reducing battery capacity frees up battery space, which can be used to enlarge the interior space of the vehicle.
AWD provides excellent grip force between the road surface and wheels in difficult driving conditions such as snow, rain, and icy roads.
30% of vehicles sold are AWD in North America.
The number of buyers choosing the AWD system is expected to continue increasing in the future due to EV safety.
The AWD system in an electric vehicle consists of main drive wheels and auxiliary drive wheels.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS' disconnector system was installed in Hyundai's IONIQ for the first time.
In the rear wheel-based IONIQ, the rear wheels serve as the main drive wheels and the front wheels serve as the auxiliary drive wheels.
The disconnector system is attached to the front auxiliary wheels to cut off power between the motor and the wheels.
The technology initially failed repeatedly during development because it was the first of its kind applied in electric vehicles.
During the first endurance test, only 20% of the target level was achieved due to excessive gear tooth wear or poor meshing.
Engineers questioned whether the design concept could match existing methods since EV power sources use motors rather than engines.
The product was redesigned by analyzing the power source to control meshing synchronization speed according to motor speed and change mesh length.
These adjustments increased the meshing success rate and solved "differential noise" and "squeaking (switching shock)" issues not found in internal combustion engines.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS is the only company with a full lineup of transmissions in the world.
The company specializes in decelerators that have continuously produced special decelerators for electric vehicles, trains, and subways.
Decelerators are often mistakenly considered less complicated than conventional transmissions requiring no special skills, but this is not actually the case.
Decelerators are considered to have many more possibilities and opportunities in the future as a key component for improving fuel efficiency.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS will continue to develop and introduce reducers suitable for the electric vehicle era, including integrated decelerators.
source_title: HYUNDAI TRANSYS

facts: Chinese startup Greater Bay Technology has claimed that its new electric vehicle (EV) battery can work in any weather.
The Phoenix cell uses superconducting materials and thermal management to bring freezing temperatures to normal room temperature in just five minutes.

Extreme temperatures have been the bane for batteries of electric vehicles since they were introduced, reducing charging efficiency when temperatures dip below 32 degrees Fahrenheit (zero degrees Celsius).
Carmakers have turned to installing heat pumps to keep battery temperatures in a high-efficiency range, but that is considered an inefficient way to manage the battery.
Greater Bay Technology was founded by Huang Xiangdong and industry veteran Pei Feng in 2020.
Huang and Pei were colleagues at GAC, the third most popular EV brand in China after BYD and Tesla.
Huang studied automotive engineering in Italy in the 1980s and worked as a postdoctoral researcher at Fiat’s research center for five years before returning to China in 1991.
From 2006, Huang led the R&D Center at GAC, where he oversaw the development of the GS4 SUV.
Huang retired in 2016 but returned in 2020 to bring in battery technology that can make EVs as easy to own and operate as gasoline-powered cars.
Their first-generation battery can recharge in about 15 minutes and can deliver a range of 310 miles (500 km).
The first-generation battery technology is already being implemented in GAC’s Aion, an electric SUV.
The Phoenix cell can deliver a range of 621 miles (1,000 km) on a single charge.

The use of superconducting materials and thermal management technology ensures the battery can be heated from temperatures as low as -4 Fahrenheit (-20 degrees Celsius) to 77 Fahrenheit (25 degrees Celsius) in just five minutes.
Once within its normal operating temperature range, the Phoenix cell can be rapidly charged and reach full capacity in just six minutes.
Huang stated that the Phoenix battery addresses long charging times for EVs and other pain points, ensuring range is unaffected by hot or cold weather.
Huang is confident that his technology will increase the mass adoption of EVs.
The Phoenix cell could be seen in action as early as next year on GAC’s Aion EVs as well as EVs of other carmakers.
In just two years, Greater Bay Technology has become a unicorn company valued at $1 billion.
Greater Bay Technology is now mentioned alongside battery industry leaders Contemporary Amperex Technology Limited (CATL) and BYD in China.
source_title: New 621-mile range EV battery charges in six minutes and works in any weather

entity_name: Georgia’s Immigration and Labor Migration Reform 2025–2026
canonical_name: Georgia’s Immigration and Labor Migration Reform 2025–2026
facts: Georgia is introducing comprehensive immigration and labor migration reforms starting September 1, 2025, with full implementation from March 1, 2026.

The old "labor migrant registration" system will be abolished in favor of a new requirement for every foreigner intending to work, operate a business, or provide remote services for a Georgian company to obtain the Right to Engage in Labor Activity.
A residence card or visa alone is no longer sufficient for legal employment without the work right, and companies cannot onboard foreign staff until the authorization is issued.

Foreigners outside Georgia have 30 days to apply for a D1 immigration visa after receiving the work right, while those already in Georgia must apply within 10 days for a labor residence permit or the new IT-sector residence permit.
A special three-year IT residence permit will be available, renewable in three-year increments up to a maximum of twelve years.

The IT residence permit applies to IT employees, individual entrepreneurs with small business status in the IT field, executives and representatives of international IT companies operating in Georgia, and their family members.
Qualification for the IT permit requires proof of at least two years of IT-related work or economic activity and an annual income of no less than USD 25,000 in Georgian lari equivalent.
The IT residence permit will be revoked if the holder spends more than 183 consecutive days outside Georgia in any 12-month period, unless exceptional circumstances apply.
Foreign directors of Georgian LLCs and JSCs are legally treated as performing labor or entrepreneurial activity and must obtain the Right to Engage in Labor Activity before exercising their powers.
A residence permit must follow the work authorization for foreign directors, though IT sector directors may alternatively qualify for the IT residence permit.
Companies risk fines of GEL 2,000 per violation if a foreign director lacks required authorization, with penalties increasing for repeat offenses.
Directors already holding another valid residence class, such as an investment residence permit or permanent residency, are exempt from the new labor permit requirement.
The minimum real estate value for property-based short-term residence permits has been raised to USD 150,000, up from the previous threshold of USD 100,000.
Applicants for property-based residency must rely on certified Georgian appraisals to establish the value of their property.
Employers hiring foreigners without authorization and individuals working without it will face fines of GEL 2,000 per violation, with repeat offenders facing doubled and tripled penalties.
Deportation procedures are being tightened with harsher consequences for overstaying or working illegally.
Responsibility for unlawful labor activity is shared among employers, workers, and third parties who facilitate it, all of which can be fined.
Foreigners already in Georgia registered in the labor migrant system by March 1, 2026, have until January 1, 2027 to obtain the new work authorization and correct residence permit.
New arrivals after March 1, 2026, must comply with the new rules immediately.

The government cited a discrepancy between foreign inflows and official registrations as a key reason for the reform, noting nearly a quarter of a million foreigners entered Georgia in just six months of 2024 while only a few thousand were registered over eight years.

The reform aims to close data gaps, reduce the informal labor market, improve oversight, align with international standards, and preserve Georgia's reputation as a transparent, investor-friendly jurisdiction.
Employers are advised to audit foreign staff, train HR departments on 30-day processing times, and ensure contracts reflect immigration compliance.
IT professionals should prioritize assembling proof of experience and income for permit applications.
Company directors should include immigration timelines in corporate governance planning.
Property investors must factor in the new USD 150,000 threshold before committing funds.
source_title: Georgia’s Immigration Reform 2025–2026: What Employers, Investors, and Expats Must Know - AttorneyAtLaw.ge
