facts: Suzuki Motor USA (SMO) announced the production of its 500,000th All-Terrain Vehicle (ATV) at the SMAC facility in Rome, Georgia.
The SMAC facility has been assembling ATVs for 22 years since opening in 2002.
Prior to 2002, Suzuki’s adult-sized ATVs were produced in Japan.
The 500,000th ATV rolled off the assembly line is the KingQuad 750AXi, Suzuki’s flagship model known for its power and reliability.
Masami Haga, President of SMO and SMAC, expressed pride in the achievement of building a half million ATVs in the United States.
Masami Haga stated that North America represents the largest ATV market in the world and emphasized the importance of building these products here by skilled American workers.
Masami Haga highlighted the role of U.S.-based suppliers in providing components essential to Suzuki’s ATVs.
Suzuki introduced the first four-wheel ATV in 1982 and has maintained its leadership in the market for over four decades.
Suzuki ATVs are sold through a network of over 700 franchised dealers across the United States.
Suzuki ATVs are distributed in nearly 25 countries worldwide.
All-Terrain Vehicles produced by Suzuki are used for recreational trail riding, hunting, agricultural work, and light construction.
Suzuki ATVs are especially valued for their reliability and durability, making them a trusted choice for various outdoor and industrial activities.
To commemorate the milestone, Suzuki plans to display the 500,000th ATV at its SMAC headquarters.
Suzuki’s U.S. sales and distribution headquarters are located in Brea, California.
SMAC operates as a subsidiary of SMO.
source_title: Suzuki Celebrates 500,000th ATV Milestone at U.S. Plant - Gearhead Daily

facts: SMAC has become a 100% subsidiary of Suzuki Motor USA LLC (SMO) effective March 1, 2024.
SMAC was founded in 2001 and is based in Rome, Georgia.
SMAC serves as Suzuki’s All-Terrain Vehicle (ATV) manufacturing facility.
For over two decades, all Suzuki adult KingQuad ATVs have been manufactured at the Rome, Georgia location.
Suzuki ATVs are sold in the United States, Canada, and nearly 25 countries worldwide.
The organizational change consolidates ATV sales and production arms to strengthen Suzuki's overall ATV business in the United States and globally.
Masami Haga, President of SMO, stated that a primary goal is to expand commitment to the growth of the entire powersports business.
The move aims to build on relationships with Suzuki dealers and SMAC vendor partners.
Strengthening the supply chain and purchasing policies for locally sourced parts are objectives of the organizational change.
Integrating production and sales functions is expected to improve responsiveness to the ATV market by more accurately linking production to sales demand.
SMO aims to improve inventory management for both the company and its dealer body to protect sales margins, flooring costs, and profitability.
The restructuring will create better, more direct communication between SMO and SMAC, fostering a collaborative environment between production and sales groups.
The change fosters better connection between the Rome- and Brea-based teams.
Suzuki’s United States distribution headquarters are in Brea, California, where SMO relocated in 1981 after entering the U.S. market in 1963.
SMAC becoming a subsidiary sets the stage for new Suzuki ATV offerings and enhancements to existing products in the future.
source_title: Suzuki Cycles

facts: Hyundai Motor Group announced plans to spend $21 billion in the United States between 2025 and 2028.
The announcement signals increasing pressure on foreign automakers to localize operations as trade policy continues to shift under mounting scrutiny from lawmakers and domestic industry advocates.
Hyundai is a South Korean automotive giant seeking to expand its U.S. footprint across manufacturing, electric vehicles, robotics, and autonomous driving technologies.
Hyundai confirmed the plan during a press briefing, stating the investment aims to “fortify [its] presence” in American industry.
The investment will include expansions to Hyundai's EV Metaplant in Georgia.
The investment will include a new steel manufacturing facility in Louisiana.
Hyundai’s push to localize appears closely timed with intensifying rhetoric in Washington around enforcing the Inflation Reduction Act and scrutiny of Chinese-affiliated supply chains.
The Biden administration has recently weighed heightened tariffs on Chinese EVs, citing national security concerns tied to data surveillance risks from connected car technologies.
Hyundai has already committed $7.6 billion in previous U.S. investments.
The new announcement brings Hyundai's total U.S. investment commitment to above $28 billion by 2028.
Around $9 billion of the new commitment is earmarked for expanded vehicle production and local infrastructure.
The remainder of the new commitment will go toward future-facing technologies such as AI, robotics, and autonomous mobility.
In Bryan County, Georgia, Hyundai is ramping up operations at its Metaplant America.
The Metaplant America began partial production in 2024.
The Metaplant America is slated to become fully operational this year (2025).
The Metaplant America is expected to produce 300,000 vehicles annually.
The Metaplant America has already begun hiring thousands of American workers.
Hyundai is partnering with its steel-making subsidiary to build a $5.8 billion steel factory in Louisiana.
The Louisiana steel factory will supply Hyundai's own U.S. production facilities and help reduce reliance on foreign inputs.
The Louisiana steel factory is projected to create more than 1,400 permanent jobs.
In 2023, Hyundai and Kia sold more than 1.5 million vehicles in the United States.
Less than half of the vehicles sold by Hyundai and Kia in the U.S. market were assembled domestically.
Lawmakers are exploring reforms to the de minimis loophole and increasing scrutiny of non-American supply chains, which may put automakers with limited U.S. content at a competitive disadvantage.
source_title: Hyundai Announces $21 Billion U.S. Investment Amid Growing Pressure on Foreign Automakers - AllAmerican.org

canonical_name: United States Electric Vehicle Battery Manufacturing Sector
facts: Ten new electric vehicle battery factories are on track to go online in the United States this year.
The new plants include facilities from global battery giants Panasonic, Samsung, and SK On, as well as automakers Ford, Honda, Hyundai, Stellantis, and Toyota.
If all ten plants open in 2025, U.S. EV battery manufacturing capacity is poised to grow to 421.5 gigawatt-hours per year.
This represents a 90 percent increase from the end of 2024, according to Benchmark Mineral Intelligence.
The industry is facing uncertainty as the Trump administration takes steps that would reduce demand for electric vehicles.
Battery startups Kore Power and Freyr recently canceled plans for new U.S. factories.
Evan Hartley, a battery industry analyst for Benchmark, stated that even if tax credits for battery manufacturing are undone, the plants are too far along in development to be canceled.
Most of the new plants are located in Republican states, making it difficult to take away thousands of jobs promised to key voter bases.
The Trump administration is poised to preside over a banner year for the United States as a major player in EV batteries, largely due to policies from the Biden administration.
LG and Honda have teamed up on a joint venture factory in Jeffersonville, Ohio.
The LG-Honda plant will have capacity to build 40 gigawatt-hours of batteries per year and support 2,200 jobs.
The projected investment for the LG-Honda plant is $4.4 billion.
Construction on the LG-Honda plant is in its final stages, with production expected to begin late this year.
Kore Power, an Idaho-based startup, scrapped plans to build a $1.2 billion battery factory in Buckeye, Arizona.
Kore Power cited failure to finalize a crucial federal loan before Trump took office and freeze new grants and loans as a reason for cancellation.
Kore Power is shifting its focus to finding an existing building to retrofit for battery production.
Freyr, which has roots in Norway, canceled plans to build a $2.6 billion battery plant in Georgia.
Freyr is instead focusing on a solar panel factory in Texas that it acquired last year.
Freyr named Austin, Texas, as its new corporate headquarters.
Analysts note that startup challenges are largely related to technical activity, manufacturing scale and precision, and the difficulty of consistently making high-quality products.

Evelina Stoikou, head of battery technology and supply chain research at BloombergNEF, identified two main unanswered questions for the U.S. market: how potential federal policy changes will affect automakers, battery makers, and consumers, and what the utilization rate of these plants will be after production starts.
Joint ventures between battery manufacturers and automakers are likely to face more certainty around expected demand due to integrated supply chains.
All new plants will be influenced by policy decisions, consumer trends, and economic factors.
Some analysts raised concerns that the building boom could lead to a battery supply exceeding demand for a few years.
The Trump administration could further harm demand by revising or eliminating tax credits for consumers buying EVs.
Jay Turner, a Wellesley College environmental studies professor, expressed uncertainty regarding multibillion-dollar decisions for projects in planning or under construction.
Trump’s tariff policies are a major source of uncertainty due to their effect on prices for essential materials like graphite, which is mainly produced in China.
Trump’s actions could reduce the profitability of new battery plants, potentially allowing China to dominate the near-future battery and EV industries.
The Trump administration has frozen funding for climate, energy, and environmental justice programs through executive orders.
Funding for the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure program was canceled as part of Trump’s executive orders, presenting legal and practical concerns.

facts: Thermal Ceramics is an Augusta-based insulation manufacturer that is a world leader in the production of ceramic fiber products, insulating firebricks, and specialty insulation products.
The company has more than 3,000 employees in more than 30 locations worldwide.
Thermal Ceramics supplies customers in the petrochemical, chemical, automotive, and power generation industries.
In the early 1990s, the Augusta, Ga. facility became ISO certified to be in conformance with quality management standards maintained by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO).
By 2005, the facility's ISO system had begun to lose its relevance and was considered burdensome.
Sherri Pettigrew serves as the environmental health and safety/quality assurance manager for Thermal Ceramics.
Georgia Tech quality specialists Elliot Price, Deann Desai, Don Pital, Holly Grell-Lawe, and Dennis Kelly assisted Thermal Ceramics with re-tooling its quality management system.
The company implemented ISO 9001, an international quality management system standard that presents fundamental management and quality assurance practices applicable to any organization.
Thermal Ceramics decided to become ISO 14001 certified, the international specification for an environmental management system, so Georgia Tech built that system from scratch for the company.
The Georgia Tech team helped Thermal Ceramics integrate the ISO 9001 and ISO 14001 standards into one system and served as the company’s internal auditors.
Thermal Ceramics transitioned its ISO system from a burdensome paper system to something that is online and totally electronic.
As a result of becoming ISO 9001 and 14001 certified, Thermal Ceramics was able to increase its sales by $6 million while saving $2 million in costs.
The Augusta facility employs a total of 450 people and was able to add seven employees following the quality and environmental standards success.

Thermal Ceramics sought assistance from the Enterprise Innovation Institute in the area of lean management principles, a set of tools that helps organizations identify and steadily eliminate waste from their operations.
Georgia Tech lean specialist Paul Todd led an executive introduction to lean, a 5S overview and planning session, and 5S training and implementation.
The company formed four teams that represented each of the three manufacturing areas plus a warehouse/shipping group for aggressive lean implementation.
Lean implementation activities included painting lines for hoppers and pallets, getting rid of clutter, implementing visual cues, and labeling.
Thermal Ceramics is currently conducting lean projects in the areas of energy reduction, downtime reduction, safety, increasing melting efficiency, and shipping error reduction.
Weekly 5S meetings are held at the facility to maintain momentum on lean initiatives.
In December 2007, the Augusta operation sought Georgia Tech’s assistance in the high-impact area of energy management as part of the U.S. Department of Energy’s Industrial Assessment Center program.
The Augusta facility spans 58 acres and 10 buildings.

Georgia Tech energy specialists Mike Brown and Pierpaolo Baldisserotto, along with several Georgia Tech co-op students, visited the plant to evaluate challenges, studied energy consumption history, and measured major energy-consuming equipment.

A report produced by the team included recommendations such as recovering fiber waste, recovering heat from the kiln exhaust, repairing air leaks, and replacing desiccant air dryers with refrigerated dryers.
The report estimates that Thermal Ceramics could save more than $1.8 million, which is 22 percent of the facility’s annual energy costs, by implementing the recommendations.
source_title: Insulation Manufacturer Improves Business with Georgia Tech Assistance - Enterprise Innovation Institute

facts: TI Fluid Systems is an industry leader in thermal solutions and fluid systems for vehicle manufacturers across the world.
The company designs and manufactures thermal management and fluid handling systems that improve efficiency, performance and sustainability for forward-thinking organisations worldwide.
TI Fluid Systems is leading the shift to electrification with tailored solutions that enable customers to lower their environmental impact without sacrificing quality.
The company operates five global technical centers and seven regional support centers to drive its research and development efforts.
The Industrial Products division harnesses TI Fluid Systems’ fluid management technologies for Battery Energy Storage, Fuel Cell, Commercial Vehicle and Off-Highway applications.
The company is seeking the next generation of engineers and innovators to join its team.

facts: TI Fluid Systems has launched new fluid handling products for thermal coolant and fuel systems on the 2021 Hyundai Santa FE SUV hybrid electric vehicle (HEV).
The Hyundai HEV application demonstrates the company’s ability to optimize systems with light weight solutions for improved EV mode efficiency.
Battery, motor drive, and power electronics thermal management applications typically use complex rubber and aluminum line assemblies for coolant thermal loops.
TI Fluid Systems has developed in-house a full range of multiple light-weight, multi-layer nylon plastic constructs with associated quick connections for all vehicle operating temperatures.
In the Hyundai Santa Fe HEV application, TI Fluid Systems collaborated with the OEM to fully switch traditional chassis thermal loops to lightweight optimized materials.
TI Fluid Systems’ Fuel Tank and Delivery Systems (FTDS) division launched their latest ‘Light Pressurized Tank’ (LPT) pressure resistant plastic fuel tank for the HEV.
In a HEV, when electric propulsion runs, the engine shuts off and fuel vapor builds pressure internally in the tank.
A pressure resistant fuel tank is required to maintain in-tank pressure and extend the electric-only driving range during this unique electric only mode.
TI Fluid Systems’ LPT technology integrates mechanical pin columns during the blow molding manufacturing process to stiffen the upper and lower tank shells.

Hans Dieltjens, Chief Operating Officer of TI Fluid Systems, stated that the new Hyundai Santa Fe HEV model and its related SUV platform models demonstrate an outstanding opportunity for increased product content per vehicle with light weight solutions for the hybrid electric vehicle segment.
Hans Dieltjens also noted that TI Fluid Systems is a differentiated supplier that can leverage fluid know-how on the new thermal challenges of electrification.
source_title: TI Fluid Systems Launched 2021 Hyundai Santa FE SUV Hybrid EV - Urja Daily

facts: ABC Technologies has announced the acquisition of TI Fluid Systems for £1.83 billion.

ABC Technologies serves over 25 OEMs across eight countries.
The acquisition unites two automotive manufacturing leaders under one umbrella to enhance the combined entity's global footprint.
ABC Technologies President and CEO Terry Campbell called the deal a strategic industry partnership with the potential to revolutionize automotive sector innovation.
Integration of the two companies aims to drive growth by leveraging shared expertise in sustainable manufacturing solutions and thermal fluid solutions.
The combined entity will address evolving demands in the automotive sector, including the shift toward electrification and sustainability.
The acquisition will be financed through equity and debt.
Financial advisory services for the transaction were led by Lazard, with support from Citi, TD Securities, and Scotiabank.
Legal advice was provided by Kirkland & Ellis International and Paul, Weiss, Rifkind, Wharton & Garrison.
The merger positions ABC Technologies and TI Fluid Systems as key players in the global automotive industry through their combined expertise in light vehicle components and thermal fluid solutions.

facts: TI Fluid Systems is a leading global supplier of automotive fluid systems technology.
TI Fluid Systems has started high volume production of new thermal products, assemblies and technology for the Volkswagen ID.3 and ID.4 battery electric vehicles.
The Volkswagen ID.3 is a compact hatchback.
The ID.3 is the first production model based on the modular electric drive matrix (MEB) from Volkswagen.
The release of the ID.4, an electric compact SUV, will follow the ID.3.
Bill Kozyra serves as President and CEO of TI Fluid Systems.

Bill Kozyra stated that TI Fluid Systems has taken another important step toward becoming an integrated thermal management supplier for the battery electrical vehicle market with the launch of new BEV components.
The new thermal application utilizes heat pump technology using R744 (also called CO2) refrigerant.
The heat pump system provides both heating and cooling for cabin comfort.
The system operates at a higher efficiency in certain conditions to provide significant battery range extension.
TI Fluid Systems assembles the heat pump module with electronic valves, an accumulator, a bracket, a chiller, and associated high pressure aluminum lines.
The heat pump valve assembly is supplied from TI Fluid System’s Jablonec Plant in the Czech Republic.
TI Fluid Systems will supply Chassis Thermal Coolant Loop Line Assemblies manufactured in various materials including lightweight engineered polymers.
TIFS has commissioned a new plant in Tangier, Morocco for the battery electric vehicle market.
The Tangier plant supplies finished hoses to Tauste, Spain for final assembly and verification test.
TI Fluid Systems is the Tier 1 Global Supplier for these BEV products.

facts: TI Fluid Systems has officially opened its all-new e-Mobility Innovation Center (eMIC) in Auburn Hills.

The Auburn Hills eMIC is designed to foster customer collaboration and enable North American automakers to develop thermal management systems for hybrid, plug-in hybrid, and battery electric vehicles more quickly and efficiently.
The new facility spans 48,000 square feet.

The facility was constructed around TI Fluid Systems’ Core Competency model, bringing together virtual engineering, designing, processing, prototyping, product testing, and vehicle testing under one roof.
The center reduces the development time for prototype thermal management components and systems from six months to as little as two weeks.
The facility can achieve system-weight reductions of up to 60 percent.
The facility can achieve overall cost savings of up to 30 percent.
The facility features equipment that enables rapid prototyping of fully functioning components and systems using 3D printing and injection moulding.
The facility includes two vehicle lifts and 42 electric vehicle charging stations.
Hans Dieltjens, president and CEO of TI Fluid Systems, stated that the Auburn Hills eMIC represents a significant step forward in automotive thermal management development in North America.

Dieltjens noted that the center enables speeded-up development, nurtures collaboration, and promotes innovation, which is crucial for the continued progress of hybrid, plug-in hybrid, and battery electric vehicles in North America and beyond.
The Auburn Hills facility is one of five eMICs opened worldwide since 2022.
Each eMIC is located in a key region for automotive and e-Mobility development, including China, Germany, Japan, South Korea, and the USA.

Dieltjens stated that constructing five eMICs in key e-Mobility development regions across the world underlines TI Fluid Systems’ commitment to supporting customers to remain competitive in a fast-changing automotive landscape.
He added that it highlights the difference an innovative approach to thermal management can have on the efficiency, performance, and cost of an electric vehicle.
TI Fluid Systems is listed on the London Stock Exchange under the ticker TIFS.
The company's corporate headquarters is located in Auburn Hills, Michigan.
TI Fluid Systems has 8,600 employees.
The company operates 21 manufacturing locations in the Americas, including 12 locations in the United States.

Alongside thermal management solutions for hybrid, plug-in hybrid, and battery electric vehicles, TI Fluid Systems provides fuel systems, lines and connectors, and thermal management solutions for internal combustion engine vehicles.
source_title: New Michigan e-Mobility Innovation Center to fast-track EV development - EV Engineering & Infrastructure

facts: TI Fluid Systems is an automotive components manufacturer that designs and manufactures thermal management and fluid handling systems.

CEO Hans Dieltjens stated that the bookings highlight the advantages of a product portfolio catering to all propulsions.
Peel Hunt analysts reiterated a 'buy' recommendation for the stock, noting confidence in TI Fluid's ability to outperform in a slower market.
CEO Dieltjens added that the first half performance demonstrates strong operational execution, successful strategic delivery, and the resilience of their propulsion agnostic portfolio.
Through productivity and efficiency measures, the company improved its adjusted EBIT margin, making progress towards a mid-term double-digit target.
The company has a strong pipeline of productivity initiatives and is agile to respond to changing market conditions.
CEO Dieltjens expressed confidence that the company's market-leading positions and ability to adapt will continue.

facts: U.S. immigration authorities (ICE) raided an under-construction Hyundai plant in Georgia on Sept. 4, detaining 475 workers.
About 300 of the detained workers were from South Korea and were chartered home by the South Korean government.
The raid has caused South Korean companies to rethink and pause current U.S. investment plans.
The Hyundai Motor-LG Energy Solution battery joint venture plant (HL-GA) in Savannah, Georgia, was cited as one of the largest U.S. investment projects by a global company.

Kim Gap-song, head of the Korean American Service and Education Consortium in New York, dispatched staff to Savannah to support detained HL-GA workers and reported that some Korean companies have preemptively suspended construction due to fears of further incidents.
Georgia is considered a hub for Korean investment in the U.S., hosting more than 110 Korean companies.
Hyundai Motor Group operates in Savannah, SK On operates in Commerce, and Hanwha Qcells operates in Dalton.
A coordinated crackdown by DHS's ICE, FBI, and DEA has left many Koreans feeling betrayed and caused local businesses like Costco and downtown restaurants to lose Korean customers.
Areas revived by Korean corporate investment could take a direct economic hit from the immigration enforcement actions.
Volkswagen is still interested in investing in the U.S. and is considering building a plant to manufacture Audi cars there.
VW was in advanced talks with the U.S. government over substantial investments in the U.S. auto market amid tariffs that have cost the company billions of euros.

VW Chief Executive Oliver Blume stated the company does not appreciate an asymmetric tariff deal (15% on EU auto imports vs. no tariffs for U.S. industrial goods into Europe) and is counting on its U.S. investment plans to boost local employment and supply chain.
Blume expressed hope for a quick solution to localize business decisions at the IAA Munich car show.
source_title: South Korean Companies Halt Georgia Investments After ICE Raid

facts: Project 2025 is a blueprint for a Republican presidential administration published by deeply conservative think tank The Heritage Foundation.
The document may shape the automotive industry once Donald Trump takes office on January 20.
Many creators of Project 2025 have ended up in positions of power in Trump's new administration, despite his attempts to distance himself from it.
The blueprint outlines plans for electric vehicles, fuel economy standards, emissions requirements, and California's ability to set its own pollution rules.
Diana Furchtgott-Roth, an economist and former Department of Transportation deputy assistant secretary for research and technology, authored parts of Project 2025.
Thomas F. Gilman, the former CEO of Chrysler Financial, also authored parts of Project 2025.

Project 2025 contends that the Energy Policy and Conservation Act of 1975 grants DOT authority to create "maximum feasible" mileage requirements using internal combustion engine technology running on gasoline, diesel fuel, or similar combustible fuels, not EVs.
The document states that DOT must lower fuel economy standards to levels specified by Congress for 2020 model year vehicles, a fleetwide average of 35 mpg according to laboratory test measures.
Real-world miles-per-gallon metrics are typically 20 to 30 percent less than the government measure, according to DOT.

Lowering standards would undo Biden-era levels that the document claims cannot realistically be met by most categories of ICE vehicles and are aligned with an "anti-fossil fuel climate agenda never approved by Congress."

The blueprint characterizes current regulations as designed to "force the auto industry to transition away from traditional technologies" and "compel Americans to accept costly EVs" despite consumer preference.
Project 2025 wants the next Republican administration to ensure DOT exercises priority over EPA in setting fuel economy standards.
The document complains that EPA carbon dioxide emissions rules act as de facto fuel economy requirements, despite neither agency having clear authority to set them in place of NHTSA.

Project 2025 takes a mixed stance on autonomous vehicles and advanced driver-assist systems, claiming liberals are "trying to force [Americans] into electric vehicles and eventually out of the driver's seat altogether in favor of self-driving robots."
The document opposes compulsory data collection mandates for AV manufacturers, specifically targeting NHTSA's standing general order requiring crash reporting for automated driving systems.
It urges NHTSA to reverse the mandate, return to a "successful focus on the voluntary sharing of data," and remove regulatory barriers to AV operation.
The impact of these planned changes could reach much further than when Trump's term ends in January 2029.
source_title: Project 2025 Has A Lot To Say About The Automotive Industry. None Of It Is Good

facts: Georgia voters unseated two Republican utility commissioners over escalating electricity rates on Tuesday night.
The election outcome could signal an early consumer backlash against data centers and Republican President Donald Trump's aggressive push to develop nuclear power.

AI-fueled data centers' energy needs are forcing utility regulators nationwide to address the cost of building billions of dollars in infrastructure projects for new generation and upgraded transmission lines.
Consumers have faced higher rates as demand outpaces available power supply.
Jefferies downgraded Southern Co. stock to a "Hold" rating from a "Buy" rating following the special election outcome.

Jefferies analysts called the election outcome a "resounding Republican loss" and stated it boosts the risk profile of Georgia Power, a unit of Southern Co., making it harder to increase electricity usage rates.

Summer electric bills for Georgia Power customers using 1,000 kilowatts increased 41% to nearly $190 per month compared to the summer of 2021, according to a survey by the Georgia Public Service Commission.
Democrats Alicia Johnson and Peter Hubbard easily unseated Republican incumbent commissioners, each winning more than 60% of the votes cast.
Johnson and Hubbard will take seats on Georgia's Public Service Commission, which regulates major utilities and sets electric rates.
Both candidates ran campaigns championing fair electric rates and more emphasis on renewable energy.
Electricity rates have increased six times in the past two years, according to their online campaign platforms.
Georgia electric bills partly reflect massive cost overruns from the construction of the two Vogtle nuclear reactors, which were about seven years behind schedule and cost about $35 billion.
The Vogtle project cost was more than double the original estimate of $14 billion, according to Georgia regulators.
Patty Durand, director of nonprofit Georgians for Affordable Energy, called the election a referendum on nuclear power.
The Trump administration announced an $80 billion deal to buy nuclear reactors from Westinghouse last week.
Westinghouse previously landed in bankruptcy in 2017 because of cost overruns at the Georgia nuclear projects.

A January 2023 report to the Georgia Public Service Commission estimated electric customers would pay an additional $36 billion to $43 billion over the 60-year lifecycle of the two Vogtle reactors compared to an alternative gas-fired fuel source.
Durand stated that "Georgia electric customers are getting electric bills that look like monthly car payments."
source_title: Georgia voters oust GOP utility commissioners over rising electricity rates - AOL

facts: Manufacturing in Georgia started the year still moving forward but in a lower gear, according to a report from Kennesaw State University.
The manufacturing sector employs more than 377,200 people across the state.
Market conditions for the sector are less encouraging according to the KSU Econometric Center.
The center calculates indices based on survey responses from manufacturing companies across Georgia.
Survey answers are factored into the Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) to summarize manufacturing outlook.
The overall PMI index dipped from 52.5 to 52.0 in January.
Five of the six measured categories showed a decline in the monthly report.
Economist Don Sabbarese, director emeritus of the center, provided the analysis.
A PMI reading above 50 indicates manufacturing activity is expanding, while below 50 indicates contraction.
source_title: Georgia manufacturing stumbles into new year, report says - Coles College of Business

facts: Tn Georgia is a facility located in Cumming, GA, operating in the Fabricated Metals sector.
The parent company of Tn Georgia is Tn Americas Holdings Inc.
The facility has reported 123.1K lbs of toxic releases across 3 chemicals to the EPA Toxic Release Inventory based on annual facility disclosures from 2019 through 2023.
The primary release pathway for toxic chemicals at the facility is off-site transfers, accounting for 100% of total releases.
Tn Georgia files annual Form R disclosures to the EPA Toxics Release Inventory under EPCRA Section 313, classified under NAICS 332991 (Fabricated Metals).
Across five years of records spanning 2019–2023, the site has disclosed 123.1K lbs in cumulative toxic releases across three distinct chemicals.
Between 2019 and 2023, reported toxic release totals at the facility decreased by 70%, dropping from 27.3K lbs to 8.2K lbs.
TRI Form R values are self-reported estimates rather than measured stack emissions and treat every pound of reported chemical equally regardless of toxicity.
Inclusion in the TRI database does not establish a health risk to nearby residents but documents the annual reporting record filed with the EPA under federal community right-to-know requirements.
In 2019, the facility reported 27.3K lbs of total releases across 3 chemicals.
In 2020, the facility reported 26.6K lbs of total releases across 3 chemicals.
In 2021, the facility reported 28.4K lbs of total releases across 3 chemicals.
In 2022, the facility reported 32.6K lbs of total releases across 3 chemicals.
In 2023, the facility reported 8.2K lbs of total releases across 2 chemicals.
Release quantities are self-reported by the facility and may represent estimates rather than exact measurements.
Total releases include releases to air, water, land, and off-site transfers for disposal.
Data is sourced from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Toxic Release Inventory program.
Facilities that manufacture, process, or otherwise use listed toxic chemicals above threshold amounts are required to report annually to the EPA under the TRI program.

facts: HMGMA is located in Bryan County, GA, roughly 25 miles west of the Georgian Coastal town of Savannah.
The facility sits directly off Interstate 16 with fast access to I-95 and the Port of Savannah.
The campus spans 3,000 acres and encompasses 11 buildings and 7.5 million square feet.
One of the main parking lots features over 1,800 solar panels generating up to 5% of the plant’s daily energy needs.
An ecological park for employee recreation is in the works at the facility.
Everyone at HMGMA, from the CEO to the assembly line, carries a single unified title: Metapro.
The term "Meta" means transformative and shapes the company's hiring, culture, and community impact.
Hyundai committed $12.6 billion to build this facility, representing the single-largest economic development investment in Georgia’s history.
The investment will create 8,500 onsite jobs and 7,000 offsite supplier jobs.
Full production at HMGMA launched in October 2024.
The initial 300,000-vehicle annual goal was quickly raised to 500,000 EVs annually.
At the October 2025 Investor Day, Hyundai announced phase two of expansion.
Phase two includes an additional $2.7 billion investment and will create 3,000 more direct and indirect jobs.
The average salary at HMGMA is around $58,000 per year, excluding benefits.
This average salary is approximately 25% higher than the average wage in Bryan County, translating to a local annual payroll of half a billion dollars.
HMGMA currently produces two vehicles: the IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9.
The 2026 IONIQ 5 delivers up to 318 miles of range.
The IONIQ 5 can recharge 10–80% at a DC Fast Charging station in roughly 24 minutes via its 800V architecture.
Power output for the IONIQ 5 ranges from 225-horsepower for a RWD to 320-horsepower for the AWD.
The XRT variant of the IONIQ 5 features rugged styling and raised ride height.
Hyundai added the IONIQ 9 to the lineup in March 2025.
The IONIQ 9 extends range up to 335 miles with ultra-fast 800V charging.
Power for the IONIQ 9 runs from RWD to a dual-motor AWD delivering up to 422-horsepower.
The IONIQ 9 cabin features a fully flat floor, a sliding Universal Island 2.0 console, available relaxation seats, Bose audio, and optional premium H-Tex materials.

Hyundai’s human-centric manufacturing vision was first witnessed at the Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center in Singapore (HMGICS), a seven-story smart mobility lab where roughly 50% of tasks are handled by robots and AI alongside a skilled human workforce.
Every element of production at HMGMA, from order collection, procurement, logistics, and assembly, is optimized in real time through AI and data systems.
Hyundai is developing the IONNA charging network, a joint venture with other major automakers that has 107 live charging sites across North America.
There are 46 more IONNA charging sites under construction and 500+ sites contracted.
A partnership with Circle K promises to bring fast charging to one of the most ubiquitous convenience store footprints in the country.
Hyundai is developing Home energy solutions and in-car payment integration, alongside growing IONNA–NACS compatibility for broad network access.
Hyundai Motor Group climbed from 5th to 3rd in U.S. EV sales rankings between 2021 and 2025, trailing only Tesla and GM.
Electrified vehicles accounted for 33.8% of all Hyundai sales as of Q1 2026, up from just 19.4% in 2023.
Euisun Chung serves as Executive Chair of Hyundai Motor Group.
