facts: The Company provides material technologies for applications in a wide range of markets including automotive, commercial, recreational, packaging, healthcare and building products.
It is a global leader in the development and production of automotive polyurethane products.

For the automotive industry, the Company manufactures molded foam, expanded foam, chemicals, components, and engineered composites for applications including seating, structural support, occupant protection, headliner systems, exterior bumper assemblies, and NVH management.
The Company is privately owned.

Seating products include seatback panel assemblies, seat back bolsters, rear seat stacked interior components, flexible foam, climate-controlled seating foam, flexible foam lamination, low-emission flexible foam, topper pads, molded foam, adaptive comfort and support foams, trimvisible foam products enabling visual transition between exposed seating components, textured surface foams, high-performance foams, and multi-zone foams.

Head restraint products include head restraints, head restraint assembly, energy management polyurethane foam, foam-in-place head restraint assembly, integral skin head restraint, foam-on-rod assemblies, high-resiliency foams, dual hardness foams, comfort safe head restraints, and head restraint mechanisms.

Seating module products include encapsulated StructureLite Module combining seating support with urethane seating pad in a single module, integral skin seat armrests, foam-in-place armrest assemblies, structurelite structural seat foam, and trimvisible BIO reduced GWP foam.

Interior products include cab back panel insulators, interior soft trim spacer foams, thin profile interior foam door armrests, thin profile interior foam console armrests, integral skin door armrests, integral skin console armrests, load floors, rear seat bolster foams, rear seat bolster assemblies, coverstock headliner foam, headliner composites, thin high-strength headliner composites, cargo management solutions, package trays, energy management foam, acoustical foam, and technical foams for filters or NVH applications.
Exterior products include structural exterior foam bumper assemblies, underhood insulators, underbody shields, tailgate liners, compressor shields, acoustical heat shields, and wheel liners.

The Company began its business in 1978 as the Woodbridge Foam Corporation, a privately owned Canadian supplier created by the purchase of the polyurethane foam division of the Monsanto Corporation in Woodbridge, Ontario, Canada.
The Company experienced growth primarily through acquisitions in the early 1980s.
The Company expanded further by building new facilities strategically located near its customers in the late 1980s.
In March 1999, Group and Poliuretanos S. W., de C. V. announced the expansion of their Joint Venture with the addition of a third manufacturing facility in Mexico.
The Company opened a manufacturing facility in Sarnia, Ontario, Canada in April 1999.
In September 1999, the Company signed contracts with the Belgian Group, Recticel, establishing a manufacturing joint venture where Recticel holds a 51% stake and the Company holds 49%.
The Company acquired the Morval division of PFB Corporation of Calgary Alberta for CAD 17 million in February 2000.
In March 2001, Woodbridge and its European partner Recticel together acquired 50% of Gestind.
The Company signed a letter of intent to enter into a Joint Venture with Technicon Industries Inc. of North Carolina in May 2001.
The Company established a joint venture in China with Chinese company GSK in September 2005.

In May 2007, Johnson Controls (JCI) joined with Proseat (a joint venture between Recticel and the Company) to set up a new manufacturing joint venture for the production of molded foam seating cushions in Slovakia and Poland.
In November 2007, The Group and Inoac Corp. announced establishing four joint venture companies in North America.
In July 2008, Proseat (joint venture with Recticel S.A.) signed an agreement for the takeover of all shares of Indepol, a Spanish moulded foam manufacturer.
In July 2011, The Company and Wuxi Jixing Auto Parts Co., Ltd. agreed to form a joint venture "Wuxi Woodbridge Jixing Advanced Polymer Technology Co, Ltd." in China.
In August 2011, The Company and the Summa Group announced expanding their partnership with the establishment of a joint venture "DuraLite Formed Plastics" in Leon, Mexico.
In January 2018, The Company and Inoac set up a new equal joint venture "Woodbridge INOAC Technical Products".
The Company announced entering into an agreement to sell its stake in Proseat, its European joint venture, to partner Recticel S.A. in December 2018.
The Company completed the acquisition of Hematite, a company specializing in NVH technology development, in November 2021.
In December 2023, the Company formed a joint venture with TM Automotive Seating Systems called TMWB Foam Private Limited in India to produce seating systems for commercial vehicles.
In November 2024, the Company formed a joint venture with Chengpeng called Woodbridge (Changzhou) Automotive Components Co., Ltd. in China for commercial vehicle and passenger seat foam.

facts: BASF SE is a German multinational chemical company headquartered in Ludwigshafen am Rhein, Germany.
It is the largest chemical producer worldwide by revenue.
As of end 2023, BASF employed around 112,000 people globally.

Under a strategy called Winning Ways, BASF is reshaping its portfolio and how it manages its businesses distinguishing between core and standalone businesses to optimize focus, capital allocation, and operational efficiency.
In the 2000s, BASF acquired major chemical businesses including Engelhard Corporation (2006), the construction chemicals business of Degussa AG, and Ciba (2008–2009).
More recently, completed January 31, 2024, BASF took over one of the two MDI plants from a joint venture in China previously shared with Huntsman Corporation.

In Asia, BASF invests heavily in regional R&D through its Innovation Campus Shanghai, serving as BASF’s largest R&D site in Asia and collaborating with local institutions for materials, catalysis, specialty chemicals, process and digitalization research.

At the 2025 industry event PU TECH 2025 (Greater Noida, India), BASF showcased high performance PU solutions designed for lower environmental footprint including synthetic leather for automotive seating with reduced greenhouse gas emissions, and PU composites for building insulation and PV frame applications.

BASF is enhancing materials for next generation batteries including Debonding on Demand adhesives, battery potting systems, and advanced surface treatments/coatings for battery packs to support performance, safety, and recyclability.

In 2025, BASF unveiled a new advanced facility in Ludwigshafen dedicated to process catalysts R&D aimed at bringing lab developed catalysts rapidly into production and strengthening its catalyst technology leadership.
In 2024, BASF spent around €2,061 million on R&D globally.
Around 10,000 employees worldwide work in R&D for BASF.
Over the past five years to 2024, innovations newly launched by BASF have generated about €11 billion in sales.
In 2025, BASF presented progress under its Winning Ways strategy confirming financial targets for 2028 and reaffirming its focus on integrated core businesses plus selective standalone units.
As part of that strategy, BASF decided to retain the standalone business BASF Environmental Catalyst and Metal Solutions (ECMS), which generated €7.0 billion in sales in 2024.
The global polyurethane market is experiencing rapid growth motivated by demand across the automotive industry for durable, lightweight, and versatile materials.
Lightweight polyurethane materials improve fuel efficiency and vehicle performance, with applications including automotive panels and EV battery casings.
The automotive segment for polyurethane is seeing a notable increase due to increasingly increasing use of interiors, seating, and coatings for durability and comfort.
The EV trend is driving innovative and higher adoption of PU products in the automotive sector.
North America expects significant growth in the polyurethane market driven by robust demand in the automotive industry.
The U.S. dominates the polyurethane market backed by a thriving automobile sector and expanding infrastructure initiatives, with strong demand for flexible foam coatings and adhesives.
Manufacturers in the U.S. are spending more money on cutting-edge PU technologies to satisfy performance and sustainability standards aligned with stringent environmental laws.
Growing demand for materials in electric vehicles including batteries, lightweight plastics, and coatings represents a key opportunity for chemical suppliers.

facts: Woodbridge Foam Corp. is diversifying its automotive impact-protection technology by acquiring PFB Corp.'s Morval Division.
Morval makes expanded polypropylene and expanded polystyrene energy-absorption products.
Woodbridge is a major in polyurethane energy-absorption protection through its Enerflex business.
Woodbridge of Mississauga, Ontario, agreed to buy Morval for about C$17 million (US$11.7 million) in cash.
Morval represented about 30 percent of PFB's 1999 sales, PFB said Feb. 8.
PFB had sales of C$35.6 million (US$24.6 million) for the nine months ended Sept. 30.
PFB of Calgary, Alberta, will focus on its main business of expanded foam products such as EPS for construction.
It started Morval, based in Kitchener, Ontario, in 1996 to capitalize on emerging markets for side-impact-protection materials among automakers.
Auto energy-management markets are competitive.
PU, expanded thermoplastic beads, ribbed plastics and expanded metals vie for market share, according to Keith Osborne, vice president of Woodbridge's Enerflex business.
He declined to estimate the market's value in North America.

Osborne said demand for side-impact energy-management products is stabilizing, but regulations for offset impacts and head impacts promise continued growth for crash-protection products among automakers.
He would not disclose Woodbridge's Enerflex sales but said they are larger than Morval's auto-protection sales.
"Acquisition of PFB's Morval division is a key component in Woodbridge Foam Corp.'s strategy to grow its energy management division," Osborne said.
PU is preferred in some impact applications, whereas expanded thermoplastics make more sense in others, he added.
Morval and Enerflex have a similar customer base, mainly Tier 1 suppliers, according to Osborne.
The companies expect to finalize the deal about March 31.
Woodbridge Group of Troy, Mich., the parent company of Woodbridge Foam, had 1998 sales of $740 million, according to Crain's Detroit Business.
Osborne said his company will continue and expand Morval's other business, expanded thermoplastic foam packaging for auto dunnage, appliances, electronics and other industries.
He said the business builds on the Kitchener plant's auto applications in terms of technologies and production equipment.
PFB will retain its stake, for a short while at least, in auto toolmaker Hallmark Technologies Inc., said Dan Stearne, PFB executive vice president.
PFB acquired 19.8 percent of the Windsor, Ontario, firm last year to gain mold-making expertise for PFB's auto impact products business.
Stearne said his company will evaluate that holding's status over the long term.
source_title: Woodbridge-buys-Morval-foam-unit-of-PFB - Plastics News

Automotive suppliers Adient plc and Lear Corporation are investing heavily in foam injection moulding facilities dedicated to electric vehicle production.
Material innovators at BASF SE and Covestro AG are developing specialized foam formulations that meet thermal management requirements for battery systems and high-voltage electrical components.
The United States Inflation Reduction Act is providing subsidies and tax incentives for vehicle manufacturers that incorporate lightweight foaming technologies.
Electric vehicles represent a rapidly growing segment, capturing 19.0% of market revenue with growth rates exceeding 15.2% annually through 2034.

Primary demand drivers for electric vehicle foam components include lightweight material requirements to extend driving range, thermal management capabilities for battery systems, and specialized acoustic properties for electric motor noise isolation.
Emerging electric vehicle manufacturers including Tesla, Rivian, NIO, and BYD are establishing new foam component specifications that incumbent suppliers are working rapidly to meet.
Battery thermal management requirements are driving demand for foam materials with specific thermal conductivity ranges of 0.030 to 0.050 watts per meter-Kelvin.

Seating foam systems in electric vehicles require enhanced fire resistance ratings due to high-voltage battery proximity, with suppliers developing formulations that achieve UL-94 V0 fire classification while reducing weight by 12-15%.

Interior packaging efficiency in electric vehicles is generating demand for custom foam moulding solutions that maximize passenger cabin space while providing structural support for integrated battery pack enclosures and thermal conditioning systems.
Suppliers capturing premium pricing in electric vehicle supply chains report gross margins exceeding 25-30% for advanced seating systems compared to 15-18% for conventional applications.
BASF SE maintains a strategic positioning in electric vehicle foaming solutions, including specialized polyurethane systems for thermal management and battery enclosure applications.

Covestro AG's innovation pipeline includes advanced foam formulations incorporating carbon dioxide as a blowing agent to reduce environmental impact while enabling density optimization for lightweight vehicle designs.
Adient plc leverages a global manufacturing footprint of over 200 facilities to enable comprehensive customization and rapid scaling for new electric vehicle programs.
The automotive industry's transition toward electric vehicles demands lightweight materials to extend battery range and improve overall efficiency.
China's New Energy Vehicle promotion policies are providing subsidies and tax incentives for vehicle manufacturers that incorporate lightweight foaming technologies.

Electric vehicle platforms create fundamentally different foam component specifications compared to traditional internal combustion engine vehicles, with higher foam component value per vehicle reflecting the integration of advanced features.
source_title: Automotive Foaming Mould Market Report | Global Forecast From 2025 To 2033

facts: Located in Lithonia, Ga., the Woodbridge Corp. plant is the site of the first strike in UAW Local 472's history.
Approximately 70 union members walked out on Oct. 30 after voting by 98% to authorize strike action in August.
Workers at the facility produce seating and dashboard components for Yamaha and Nissan cars and golf carts.
Strikers report that Woodbridge is hiring temporary workers and attempting to keep one shift operating during the walkout.
The company implemented a two-tier pay system in 2009, which union members are actively trying to eliminate.
Under the current pay structure, new hires start at approximately $15 an hour.
Top pay for a production job at the plant is capped at $20.50 an hour.
Workers cite forced overtime and understaffing as major grievances, noting that employees are sometimes sent home after only four or five hours when work slows down.
Union representatives include Myra Williams, Shawn Milligan (second shift representative), Neal Turner (31-year veteran), and Keith Walker (34-year veteran).
The workforce is predominantly female, with union members originating from Nigeria, Ghana, Ethiopia, and Cuba.
Strikers are demanding better pay and benefits, an end to unfair treatment by supervisors, and improved work-life balance due to forced overtime.
source_title: UAW holds first strike ever at Georgia automotive parts plant – The Militant

facts: In 2012, TrimTech Interior Soft Trim Solutions announced a global cooperation with Multimatic, a Canada-based manufacturer of automotive mechanisms, to provide product design, engineering, prototyping and manufacturing of automotive trim solutions.
TrimTech Solutions produces head restraints, armrests, bolsters and closure panels.

In accordance with the cooperation, TrimTech Solutions launched HeadSmart head restraint mechanism technology, which provides full design capability including flip and fold seats, active head restraints systems and automatic and manual release applications.
In 2012, the Company announced that it licensed its StructureLite technology to JSP, a Japanese supplier of plastic foams, to market and produce expanded polypropylene (EPP) seat frames in Japan.
StructureLite seat frame technology replaces traditional steel stampings and wires frames and can lower seat system weight by up to 35%.
This technology was previously licensed by JSP for manufacture in North America.
In 2014, the Company announced its TrimVisible LE low emission molded foam technology.
The development of TrimVisible LE foams enables the Company to meet extremely low volatile organic compound (VOC) test requirements.
As tested through an independent accredited laboratory, the use of TrimVisible foams provides up to an 80% reduction of certain volatile compounds without compromising seat cushioning performance.

In 2014, the Company developed StrataLiner 3D lightweight composite boards that are used to manufacture automotive headliners and a variety of interior parts including load floors, deck lids and package trays.

The polyurethane composite boards are manufactured by laminating engineered reinforcement layers to custom formulated polyurethane substrates, providing superior rigidity in a product that is up to 35% lighter than the leading competing material.
In November 2013, the Company completed development of its StrataPlush HD polyurethane flexible laminate coverstock foam technology.

StrataPlush HD provides all the benefits of standard StrataPlush foams while also providing superior resistance to mottling, higher firmness range and reduced thickness opportunity as low as 2 millimeters.
This coverstock laminate foam innovation is designed to be used in a variety of automotive interior trim applications including headliners, seats, head restraints and arm rests.
In September 2013, the Company announced the development of its new low-mass TDI high-resiliency foams for automotive seating applications.
This new TDI HR foam can be formulated to precisely match the vibrational response of any MDI foam over an expanded density range.
The formulation enables TDI seating foams to provide higher resiliency, lower hysteresis and superior durability, while delivering significant reduction in mass.

Another benefit of the new TDI formulation is its ability to improve cabin air quality and reduce windshield fogging due to the ultra-low volatile organic compound characteristics of the foam products.
In June 2013, the Company completed development of a new foam material for car seats.
This new foam allows the Company to create a seat design template for different types of vehicles such as sedans and compact cars.
The new foam has improved vibration absorption characteristics and reduced weight compared to a previously developed version.
As many automakers have started to use common seat frames, the Company expects a nearly 20% cost reduction for a single seating unit if the new foam for seat cushioning is introduced as a standard.

By using the new foam as a standard, seat design and production costs will be lowered as manufacturers can reduce the work needed to optimize the suspension for each vehicle model due to the improved vibration characteristics.
An additional benefit of the foam is improved ride comfort for passengers.

In June 2012, the Company announced the development of a new flame retardant foam called WhisperTech V0 for a range of under-hood acoustical and thermal insulating applications to meet UL-94 V0 flammability requirements.

This new material represents a significant development in flammability performance for Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH) solutions, offering enhanced sound absorption and transmission loss properties versus competing materials including metals and engineered fibers.

These flame retardant formulations are available for a wide range of molded components that make contact with the engine including covers and various acoustical insulators for manifolds, valve and cam covers, fuel rails, air intakes and pumps.
Additionally, these products can integrate fasteners and mounting brackets for ease of assembly.

In February 2012, the Company announced several new products under its TrimTech interior soft trim components product line including the ComfortZone dual hardness head restraint, DecoSkin alternative trim technologies, TrimLine components for thin profile parts, and TrimStyle components for complex geometries and tight dimensional tolerances.

In January 2012, the Company announced the development of StrataTrim polyurethane composite board technology with applications including load floors, deck lids, package trays, sun shades and seat backs.

StrataTrim advantages include system cost savings, superior design flexibility, enhanced surface quality, improved acoustics, up to 50% mass reduction, glass and glass-free reinforcement options, low cost composite tooling, shortened tool lead time, quick prototyping and a cost effective production option for low volume programs.
In January 2012, the Company announced that it developed new ComfortSense seat cushioning which delivers improved initial softness without the added expense of a secondary topper pad.
ComfortSense A-surface treatments also deliver optimized air distribution capability in both active and standard seat systems.
In 2014, the Company announced that it will open a new manufacturing facility in Capivari, Sao Paulo, Brazil, which will be its sixth facility in Brazil.
The Capivari facility will offer molded polyurethane technologies and produce high performance automotive seat cushions and interior parts such as head restraints and arm rests.
With two OEMs announcing plans for new assembly plants in the region, the Company hopes that its new plant will be able to capitalize on the opportunities provided by the expansion.
In 2014, the Company announced that its Formed Plastics division will expand its particle foam operations in Germany.
The Company and its joint venture partners currently have particle foam manufacturing facilities in the U.S., Canada, Mexico and France.
In 2013, the Company announced that its Formed Plastics division has expanded their growing business unit with a new particle foam facility in Chattanooga, Tennessee, U.S.

Formed Plastics is a tier 1 particle foam and polyurethane foam solutions provider which manufactures cargo storage, seat frames, occupant safety and acoustical components from materials such as EPP, PEPP, EPE, and EPS.
The Formed Plastics footprint expansion is a strategic complement to the Company's facilities in Kitchener, Canada; Leon, Mexico; and Trilport, France.
As of May 29, 2015, according to the Company's website, the Company has over 8,000 employees.
source_title: Woodbridge Foam Corporation Business Report up until FY ended Dec. 2014 - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: Vaughan is home to many globally recognized tier 1 suppliers, such as Magna International, Martinrea, Multimatic, Hanon Systems, Litens Automotive, IAC, and Woodbridge Foam Corporation.
The city hosts numerous tier 2 and tier 3 automotive suppliers.
The City’s central location is in direct proximity to assembly plants for giants such as Stellantis, Ford, Honda, and Toyota.
Businesses can plug into a strong and well-connected supply chain.
JEMS Coating Ltd. President Robert German states that Vaughan offers highly conscientious support from the city’s economic development team.
Robert German notes that Vaughan provides unparalleled proximity for most of who’s who in the automotive industry.
Robert German highlights excellent access via the 407 to those that are further away.
Robert German mentions challenges amongst peers with finding labour, but finds that Vaughan is well situated for having access to both skilled and unskilled talent pools.
The Automotive Parts Manufacturers sector in Vaughan includes 45 companies and 7,200 employees.
The Motor Vehicle and Parts Dealers sector in Vaughan includes 110 companies and 1,200 employees.
The Vehicle Dealers sector in Vaughan includes 125 companies and 3,400 employees.
source_title: Automotive | Vaughan Economic Development

facts: Georgia offers a wide range of business-friendly tax credits covering job creation, high-wage roles, R&D, film/TV, facility expansion, childcare, retraining, and port activity that can significantly reduce costs.
Georgia maintains a 5.19% corporate tax rate with single-factor apportionment.
Georgia has maintained AAA credit ratings for two decades.

The Job Tax Credit (JTC) rewards job creation in tiered counties or zones, offering $750 to $4,000 per job annually for five years, with unused credits carryable forward up to 10 years and the ability to offset income tax.
JTC Tier 1 provides ± $4,000 per job per year with a minimum requirement of 2 new jobs.
JTC Tier 2 provides ± $3,000 for creating 10 jobs.
JTC Tier 3 provides ± $1,750 for creating 15 jobs.
JTC Tier 4 provides ± $1,250 for creating 25 jobs.

The Quality Jobs Tax Credit targets high-value, higher-wage roles, providing $2,500 to $5,000 per quality job per year for up to five years, applying to 100% of corporate income tax with a 10-year carryforward.
To qualify for the Quality Jobs Tax Credit, a business must create and maintain at least 50 new jobs paying at least 110% of the county’s average wage.

The R&D Tax Credit encourages innovation by providing credits equal to a portion (usually 10%) of qualified R&D spending over a base amount, offsetting up to 50% of Georgia income tax liability with a 10-year carryforward.

The Film/TV/Digital Entertainment credit rewards media production spend with up to a 30% credit including logo inclusion, starting with a base credit of 20% of qualified in-state production expenses exceeding $500,000, plus an additional 10% if the production includes the "Made in Georgia" promotional logo.

The Investment Tax Credit (ITC) reduces upfront facility expansion costs, offering 1–5% for basic equipment and expansions, and 3–8% for recycling, pollution control, or defense-conversion investments, applying against 50% of income tax liability with a 10-year carryforward.
The ITC is available to manufacturing or telecommunications firms making qualified investments equal to or exceeding $50K–$100K.
The Child Care Credit allows employers providing or sponsoring childcare to claim 75% of direct costs over several years, with the ability to carry forward unused credits.
The Retraining Credit supports workforce upskilling by covering half the training cost for approved programs, subject to per-employee limits.
Businesses frequently fail to claim credits because they do not track qualifying expenses properly throughout the year.
Claiming the wrong tier or miscalculating wage thresholds can invalidate an entire tax credit claim.
Many Georgia state tax credits must be applied for on time, and missing documentation deadlines risks losing the credits permanently.
Failing to map and integrate state incentives with federal benefits results in untapped savings for businesses.
source_title: Georgia State Tax Credits: Incentives for Business Growth

facts: Develops urethane, rubber, plastics, and composite materials.
Delivers automotive interior and exterior decorative parts, industrial materials for interior products, cosmetic containers, and housing materials.
Has established joint ventures with Woodbridge Foam based in Canada, and a Chinese supplier Ningbo Huaxiang Electronic Company.
Is an unlisted company.
Products include interior parts such as instrument panels, energy absorption parts, engine covers, molded ceilings, air-intake ducts, and pillar garnishes.
Seat parts products include armrests, seat cushions, and head rests.
Molding parts products include window moldings, roof moldings, aero spats, side mudguards, mudguards, and rear spoilers.

Functional parts products include air intake ducts/resonators, sound deadening parts for resonator, steering column hole covers, hood seals/door weatherstrip, sealing, door cushions, plug holes/packing, and Cellasto (Suspention).
Provides material solutions for EV battery.
1926: Established the Inoue Gomu Seizousyo to produce bicycle tire tubes.
1934: Started overseas operations by exporting the IRC Brand tire tube to Southeast Asia.
1938: Incorporated Inoue Rubber Co., Ltd.
1954: Established MTP Kasei, becoming the first company in Japan to manufacture and sell polyurethane foam.
1955: Adopted ceiling materials / heat insulating materials for engine compartments.
1960: Started to produce vacuum formed polyvinyl chloride sheet products.
1968: Adopted plastic molds for automobiles.
1979: Started to produce plastic bumpers (PP).
1980: Established Inoue MTP Co. Ltd. as the result of the merger of Inoue Rubber and MTP Kasei, launched the 'INOAC' brand, and established Inoue Cellotechs as a joint venture with Jim Walter Inc.
1984: Adopted instrument panels using powder slash molds.
1987: Started to produce blow molded spoilers.
1988: Started to produce Beneron sheets domestically by entering into a technical partnership with Benecke-Kaiko Group.
June, 1990: Changed name of company to Inoac Corp.
1994: Started to produce instrument panels using HPI Technology.
1995: Established Kansai Inoac and Chita Inoac.
1996: Started to produce instrument panels integrated with airbag doors.
1997: Seino Inoac renamed Inoac Elastomer Co., Ltd.
1998: Established IW foam corporation, a joint venture company with Woodbridge of Canada, to manufacture automobile cushions.
1999: Established BASF Inoac polyurethane.
2000: Established SAI Automotive SAI Japan KK, a joint venture company, with Faurecia of France.

2001: Established a joint venture company with RECTiCEL S.A. of Belgium to manufacture automobile interior parts; established Inoac Tokai (Thailand) Co., Ltd., a company to produce medium-pressure and low pressure hoses in Thailand.

2004: Established Tianjin Inoac Polymer Co., Ltd. in China to manufacture and sell automotive components; established Inoac Automotive (Thailand) Co., Ltd. in Thailand to manufacture automotive resin products.
2005: Established the Toyohashi Plant in Aichi Prefecture as an integrated production base for headrests with a seamless production structure including cutting of headrest cover through finishing.

2006: Established Dongguan Inoac Polymer Co., Ltd. in China to manufacture high-performance urethane foam; established Inoac (Thailand) Co., Ltd. in Thailand, which supervises the Group's affiliated companies in Asia.

Nov. 2007: The Company and Woodbridge Foam of Canada signed a joint-venture contract to collaborate in the automotive seats urethane foam business; based on the agreement, Woodbridge Foam will acquire 50 percent of the share capital in Kyusyu Inoac Co., Ltd., while the Company will obtain 50 percent of the share capital in Saltillo Interior Trim S.A. de C.V., which is Woodbridge Foam's production base in Mexico; established the Kitakami Plant in Iwate Prefecture as an integrated production base for automotive interior and exterior components.
2008: Established Saltillo Interior Trim S.A. de C.V. in Mexico to manufacture foam products.
2009: Established Tianjin Inoac Huaxiang Automobile Produces in Tianjin, China.
2011: Acquired 100% of the shares in Inoac Exterior Products LLC based in North America to enhance its automotive spoiler business.
2012: Established ABC Inoac Exterior Systems in USA and Canada to produce automotive exterior components.
2013: Started series production at Inoac Polymer Lanka (PUT) Ltd. in Sri Lanka; initially produced urethane form for apparel business in India.
2014: Established the general foundation named the 'Polyurethane International Technology Promotion Foundation'.
2015: Started volume production on new foaming line at INOAC (THAILAND) CO., LTD.
2016: Started volume production on foaming lines at INOAC VIETNEM CO., LTD; started volume production on new foaming line at PT. INOAC POLYTECHNO INDONESIA; established INOAC LIVING VIETNAM CO., LTD.
2017: Established Inoac Ryukyu Co., Ltd. as the group's first company in Okinawa prefecture.
2021: Built a new production plant at PT Inoac Polytechno Indonesia.
source_title: Inoac Corporation - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: South Korean company investing $18 million in Georgia plant to build heaters for electric vehicles.
Woory Industrial Co., based in Yongin, South Korea, will open the plant in Dublin, Georgia, in November.
The company plans to hire 130 people for the new facility.
The plant will manufacture electric heaters and control units for electric and internal combustion automakers.
This marks Woory's first U.S. manufacturing location.
The company currently operates existing factories in South Korea, China, Thailand, Vietnam, India, Slovakia, and Mexico.
Woory has been developing electric heaters over the last 10 years with hopes of becoming a leader in the market.
Electric heaters are needed in cold weather to keep passengers warm and to keep electric batteries warm for better performance.
Woory is the ninth supplier to locate in the region following Hyundai's 2022 announcement to build a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia.
The Hyundai site in Ellabell could grow to 8,100 employees and is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
Suppliers for the regional project have pledged to invest more than $2 billion and hire nearly 4,700 people.
Woory could qualify for $2.6 million in state income tax credits, calculated at $4,000 per job over five years, contingent on workers earning at least $31,300 a year.
The state will cover the cost to train workers for the facility.
The company could qualify for additional incentives, including property tax breaks from Dublin and Laurens County.
source_title: South Korean company investing $18M in Georgia plant to make heaters for electric vehicles | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: An automotive parts manufacturer will open a new EV parts manufacturing facility in Georgia, creating over 130 new jobs.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Woory Industrial Co., Ltd. will establish a new manufacturing facility in Dublin, Georgia.
The facility will create more than 130 new jobs and an estimated $18 million in investment in Dublin-Laurens County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated pride in welcoming Woory Industrial as a job creator helping Georgia become the "e-mobility capital of the nation."
Governor Kemp cited historic levels of new jobs and investment brought to communities by the emerging industry, thanks to the state's highly-skilled workforce and reliable infrastructure network.
Governor Kemp expressed confidence that Woory will have a long and beneficial partnership with Dublin-Laurens County.
Woory Industrial Company (known as Woory) was established in 1989 and specializes in heating, ventilation, and air conditioning components and systems for the global automotive industry.
Woory currently maintains its traditional product lines while concentrating on developing new products for electric vehicles (EVs) and hydrogen-fueled cars.
CEO Jungwoo Kim stated that the auto industry is facing a revolution for the first time in 100 years and identified the U.S. EV market as the key market for the transition to electric vehicles.
Over the past 10 years, Woory has successfully grown into a specialized EV component company through continuous innovation.
Jungwoo Kim noted that Georgia offers a strategic opportunity for Woory to advance as part of the vanguard of EV development and thanked the State of Georgia and City of Dublin for their partnership.
The new facility, located at 404 Kellam Road in Dublin, will be Woory's first U.S. manufacturing location.
The facility will produce electrical heaters, control units, and actuators for EV and internal combustion carmakers in North America.
Operations are expected to begin in November 2023.
Unlike internal combustion engine vehicles, EVs lack a built-in heating source, making EV heaters necessary for cabin heating and battery temperature maintenance.
Woory's pioneering development of electric heaters over the last 10 years has established it as a leader in this growing market segment.
Dublin Mayor Joshua Kight stated that Woory's commitment to repurposing an existing manufacturing building promotes infill development and creates new career paths for the community.

Dr. Fred Williams, Chair of the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority Board, highlighted Dublin and Laurens County's rapidly growing automotive sector driven by its location on Interstate 16 and access to a skilled workforce.
Dr. Williams noted that partnerships with area education systems and training providers will ensure training programs are in place to fill Woory’s talent pipeline for years to come.

Project Manager Jacob Lee represented the GDEcD Global Commerce team on the project, which was developed in partnership with the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that projects like Woory's first U.S. facility in Dublin justify prioritizing the cutting-edge manufacturing transformation to create jobs and opportunities in rural communities.

Commissioner Wilson noted that Georgia has invested for decades to match competitive projects with prepared development sites and congratulated Dublin-Laurens County on bringing future e-mobility jobs to middle Georgia.
For over a century, Georgia has fostered industry practices, partnership, and innovation to position itself as a leader in developing emerging technologies for the automotive and mobility industry.
From July 1 through December 31, 2022, the automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment in Georgia.
Since 2020, Georgia's pursuit of EV supply chain job creation opportunities has resulted in over $22.7 billion in investment and the creation of more than 28,400 jobs.

Woory began nearly 35 years ago as a small factory producing fuel senders in Suwon, South Korea, and has grown into an international enterprise with global production plants employing over 1,300 people.
Woory's current business focuses on developing and producing HVAC auto components for all vehicle types, including EVs, internal combustion engines, eco-friendly cars, and hydrogen-fueled cars.
Woory has been nominated as a supplier for Tesla, Rivian, Lucid, and Canoo, and currently supplies the HV heater system used to heat EV cabins.
source_title: Woory Industrial Co. to open EV parts facility in GA

facts: Woory Industrial has secured Hyundai Wia as its customer.
The company signed a long-term contract to supply components for the heat management system in electric vehicles.
The deal is worth 21.7 billion won and lasts from 2023 to 2030.
Woory will be supplying its 3-way valves under the deal.
The component is used to distribute cooling water within the vehicle.
Hyundai Wia developed a cooling water distribution and supply module for electric vehicles in January of this year.
Besides 3-way valves, Woory will also be supplying its expansion valve, positive temperature coefficient (PTC), and e-compressor.
Earlier, the company had secured Hanon Systems as its customer as well.
The pair’s heat management system is to be used in Ioniq 5 and EV6 vehicles.
Woory’s PTC is also expected to be supplied to Rivian, Tesla, BMW, Stellantis, and Daimler.
The company recorded 215.3 billion won in revenue up to the third quarter of this year.
The company recorded 3.4 billion won in operating income up to the third quarter of this year.
Revenue increased by 5.28% from the same time period in 2020.
Operating income increased by 37.04% from the same time period in 2020.
source_title: Woory secures Hyundai Wia as customer  < IT·Gaming < 기사본문 - The Elec Inc.

facts: Woory Industrial and Hyundai Wia will collaborate to develop an e-compressor and other auto parts used in the heating system of electric vehicles.

The collaboration stems from Hyundai Motor and Kia reviewing potentially applying a new heating system for their future electric vehicles to increase their drive distance and efficiency, according to a person familiar with the matter.

Woory and Hyundai Wia are collaborating to develop new expansion valves, positive temperature coefficients, and e-compressors for a new integrated thermal management system that can be used in electric vehicles by 2025.
The integrated thermal management system uses refrigerants’ compression, expansion, and vaporization that causes high and low temperatures to activate the car’s heating system.
This system makes the car more efficient and consumes less power, which can extend drive distance of electric vehicles per charge.
In January this year, Hyundai Wia introduced its new cooling water distribution and supply module that it plans to supply to Hyundai Motor in 2023 for electric vehicles made with the E-GMP platform.
Woory Industrial was formed in 2015 and specializes in car heating and ventilation systems.
Woory Industrial supplies heating ventilating air conditioning actuators, clutch coils, and PTC heaters.
Woory Industrial supplies these automobile parts to Hyundai Motor, Tesla, BMW, and Stellantis.
Woory Industrial recently won orders from US electric truck firm Rivian.

The company recorded 146.2 billion won in sales and 4.2 billion won in operating income during the first half of the year, an increase of 6.87% and 110%, respectively, from the same time period a year ago.
source_title: Woory and Hyundai Wia to develop EV compressor < Latest Stories < 기사본문 - The Elec Inc.

facts: The article was written by Craig Lesser and Jorge Fernandez of The Pendleton Group and appeared in Global Atlanta.
This white paper builds on a previous edition published by Insider Advantage.
A new “Georgia Global Energy Policy Center” is proposed to capitalize on the state’s existing assets and cooperate with international partners.
The proposed Georgia Global Energy Policy Center would be funded by state government, private industry, federal grants, and foundations.
The Georgia Institute of Technology could serve as the hub for the Center of Energy Supply Research.
The University of Georgia could form the Center for Rural Energy Research and Policy through its engineering and agricultural schools.
Georgia Southern University could house the Center of Air and Sea Energy Research and Policy.
Georgia State University could become the Center of Urban Energy Research and Policy.
The proposed center would connect global players to address pressing energy issues and form relationships with similar energy think tanks worldwide.
Private sector companies in Georgia include AGCO, Delta Air Lines, Fieldale Farms, Georgia Power Co., Gulfstream, Hyundai, Kia, Lockheed, Mercedes Benz, Porsche, Southern Co., and dozens of others.
These companies could utilize the new policy center to build partnerships with university researchers that further their own energy projects.
They could also cooperate on joint energy work to bring in additional international partners and funding.
Energy-related organizations like AEE and INPO could cooperate with the new policy center to pool resources and knowledge.
The existing Center of Innovation for Energy is led by the Georgia Department of Economic Development.

The Center of Innovation for Energy is working with companies like Stryten Energy, Mitsubishi Turbines, and Siemens Energy and Automation to advance energy technologies for improved generation, storage, and consumption.
This energy innovation center has worked with other partners for about 10 years to lay the groundwork for the electric vehicle (EV) mobility industry in Georgia.
Georgia has positioned itself as a hub for electric transportation with 35 EV-related projects investing in the state since 2018.
Hyundai Motor Group’s fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing “meta plant” facility near Savannah is expected to ramp up to full production in 2025.
Rivian’s largest manufacturing facility in the U.S. is slated to start production in Georgia in 2026.
Georgia is rapidly expanding its full EV supply chain with related manufacturing and solutions.
NVH Korea has announced a $72 million battery parts factory in Henry County.
Woory Industrial Co., Ltd. plans to open an EV parts R&D center and manufacturing facility in Dublin, Ga.
Both NVH Korea and Woory Industrial are coming to support the Hyundai Meta Plant.
LG Energy Solution Ltd. is partnering on a $4.3 billion battery operation at the Hyundai Meta Plant.
Anovion Technologies is building an $800 million plant to manufacture synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries in Bainbridge, Ga.

Georgia’s ambitions to become a hub for clean vehicles underscores its desire to be a clearinghouse for expertise around clean energy in general, forging new paths in EV infrastructure, battery development and commercialization and charging stations.
Georgia’s existing energy innovation center is focusing on identifying EV charging equipment manufacturers, such as Netherlands-based Heliox.
The state is especially interested in enticing global players in hydrogen energy technology and storage to invest in Georgia.
The state could work with Hydrogen Europe on innovation and investment for deploying hydrogen power.
The Center of Innovation for Energy is hoping to put together demonstration hydrogen projects and potentially the first hydrogen living laboratory.
This hydrogen initiative would be similar to its work on the net-zero transportation initiative The Ray and the smart city Curiosity Lab.
Georgia is bidding for funding to be a center for hydrogen expertise via a coalition of regional utilities, including Southern Company.
Georgia Power is conducting research into hydrogen fuels.
Related supplier Atlanta Cylinders Inc., whose parent company is in the Czech Republic, is lining up to support Georgia’s hydrogen endeavor.
Automakers Hyundai and Toyota Motor Corp. are looking to hydrogen as the fuel of the future.
If successful with hydrogen initiatives, the industry will start paying attention to Georgia, as the EV mobility industry did.

The state’s previous initiatives to coalesce expertise and business clusters around fintech and global health are evidence of Georgia’s ability to garner local and international support to boost certain industries.

Existing and future innovation centers in Georgia could contribute important resources to share with a new Georgia Global Energy Policy Center and forge new connections among international and local innovators and between those innovators and public and private sector actors.
The creation of a Georgia Global Energy Policy Center would encourage public education programs around new energy technology.
Educational and community programs would engage children in learning about better ways to create and use energy to cultivate students for universities and technical schools.

With educational programs in place, Georgia can cultivate energy-minded students to feed universities and technical schools, which would develop new programs focused on the energy sector and build leaders who will direct the world’s energy future.

With a growing community of educated professionals, Georgia can further its reputation as the best place for smart, motivated individuals to come to learn about and put into practice new energy technologies, attracting talented entrepreneurs and innovators from around the world.
Georgia’s startup ecosystem could benefit from – and contribute to – new energy development.
The Georgia Research Alliance has been instrumental in accelerating the growth of university research and development in the state.
By bringing private and public research investment to Georgia, the Georgia Research Alliance supports startup companies and creates jobs.
Georgia Research Alliance’s efforts, aligned with those of a new energy policy center, could result in even further economic impact throughout the state.
The Georgia Global Energy Policy Center would be a nonpartisan effort that benefits the entire state.
Research and development on this scale can only benefit every Georgian, whether by creating new jobs or inventing new efficiencies that impact gas and electric bills.
source_title: Powering Ahead: How Georgia Can Become a Global Energy Thought Leader - The Pendleton Group

facts: Woory Industrial Co., a South Korean producer of heating, ventilation and air conditioning systems for vehicles, announced it will construct an $18 million factory in Dublin, Georgia.
The new facility is located roughly 95 miles northwest of Hyundai’s Metaplant near Savannah.
Woory Industrial Co. promised to hire 130 workers for the new plant.
Hyundai suppliers have been moving to rural and Coastal Georgia to partner with the future EV manufacturing plant.
Eight large parts companies, also known as tier one suppliers, have announced factories within the Metaplant’s orbit.

The Pad Construction Company, a Joint Venture between Atlanta Department of Economic Development and Georgia Commerce Commission, is the largest supplier of this project, according to Woory Kim, President of Woory Industrial Co.
Woory Kim stated that they are looking forward to expanding their component supply chain in Georgia as well as other Korean companies that have announced plans to expand operations there.
The Wall Street Journal reported that Hyundai Motor Group will build a new factory near Dublin, County 404.
Hyundai Motor Group is building a $5.54 billion electric vehicle factory near Savannah.
Korean companies have pledged to create over 82,000 jobs and invest $22.7 billion in Georgia since 2021.
This investment marks the first of several new investments in Georgia by Korean companies.
Hyundai Motor Group will also expand its existing battery cell manufacturing facilities at the plant.
source_title: $18M facility coming to Georgia to build HVAC systems for EVs

Facility is located at 565 Beulah Church Rd., Carrollton, GA 30117.
Office hours run from 8:00 AM to 4:30 PM Monday through Friday.

entity_name: Motherson Yachiyo Automotive Systems Co., Ltd.
canonical_name: Motherson Yachiyo Automotive Systems Co., Ltd.
facts: The Company develops and manufactures fuel tanks and sunroofs.
Honda announced on July 4, 2023 that it will sell its stake in its consolidated subsidiary Yachiyo Industry to the Motherson Group.

Honda will first make Yachiyo Industry Kogyo a wholly owned subsidiary through a takeover bid, after which it will transfer 81% of its voting rights to SMRC Automotive Holdings Netherlands B.V., a group company under Samvardhana Motherson International.
The tender offer is scheduled to commence around October 2023.

Honda is working to optimize its business portfolio that includes its consolidated subsidiaries, and will make the Motherson Group a strategic partner for Yachiyo Industry in order to expand sales channels in its existing business and to expand its plastic products business.
Honda will also acquire all shares of Goshi Giken Kogyo held by Yachiyo Kogyo, and will hold 95% of the voting rights in the company.

As a result, Yachiyo Industry will concentrate its management resources on its automotive business and Honda will aim for future business growth in the motorcycle business by optimizing management resources among the group.
The company was delisted on January 10, 2024.
Effective October 1, 2024, the company changed its name to Motherson Yachiyo Automotive Systems Co., Ltd.
After Honda transfers the Company’s stock to the Motherson Group, the voting rights of the Motherson Group are forecast to be 81%, while the voting rights of Honda are forecast to be 19%.
Products include sunroofs, plastic fuel tanks, steel fuel tanks, hydrogen tanks for FCVs, plastic parts, bumpers, instrument panels, and CFRP products.
Eiichi Ohtake, currently a Special Advisor, personally established Ohtake Paint Shop at 7, Fujimi-cho, Itabashi-ku, Tokyo in Apr. 1947 to paint machine parts.
In 1951, the company started painting of motorcycle components along with Honda's business expansion to Tokyo.
Aug. 1953: Established Yachiyo Painting Co., Ltd. to start baking finish business and set up Narimasu Plant in Daiwa-cho, Saitama (currently Wako-shi), which was closed in June 1962.
May 1960: Established Suzuka Plant in Suzuka City, Mie, as a buffing and painting shop for motorcycle parts.
Sep. 1968: Changed the company name to Yachiyo Industry Co., Ltd.
Aug. 1972: Established Kashiwabara Plant in Saitama, starting assembly of Honda's mini-cars on an outsourcing basis, at which point Honda invested in the Company and it became a Honda affiliate.
Apr. 1973: Merged Yachiyo Press Industry Co., Ltd. with Shimizu Seisakusho.
Nov. 1974: Established Goshi Giken Co., Ltd. as a joint venture with Honda and Sankei Giken Kougyou Co., Ltd.
Feb. 1976: Started producing buggies for Honda at the Kashiwabara Plant on an outsourcing basis.
Feb. 1977: Started producing fuel tanks as automotive parts.
Jul. 1981: Relocated head office to Toshima Ward in Tokyo.
Jun. 1983: Established Yokkaichi Factory in Mie Prefecture as an automotive parts manufacturing facility performing stamping and welding operations.
Aug. 1985: Enhanced facilities at Yokkaichi Factory to produce trucks for Honda on an outsourcing basis.
Jan. 1986: Established the Tochigi R&D Center in Tochigi Prefecture.
Jun. 1986: Started producing sunroofs as part of its automotive-parts range.
Jan. 1989: Established Yachiyo of Ontario Mfg., Inc., a consolidated subsidiary, in Canada.
May 1996: Started contract production of Honda's mini passenger cars on a subcontracted basis.
Jun. 1996: Established Yachiyo Industry (U.K.) Ltd. and Unipart Yachiyo Technology Ltd. (a joint venture with several local companies) in the UK.
Jan. 1997: Established Siam Yachiyo Co., Ltd., a consolidated subsidiary, in Thailand.
May 1997: Relocated head office to Sayama, Saitama Prefecture.
Sep. 1997: Established Yachiyo of America Inc., a consolidated subsidiary, in the U.S.A.
Oct. 1997: Established AY Mfg., Ltd., a consolidated subsidiary, in the U.S.A. through its local subsidiary.
Jan. 1999: Established US Yachiyo Inc., a consolidated subsidiary, in the U.S.A.
Sep. 2000: Established Yachiyo Manufacturing of Alabama Llc in the USA.
Apr. 2002: Established Yachiyo Zhongshan Mfg. Co., Ltd. in China.

Aug. 2002: Purchased shares of Goshi Giken Co., Ltd. and made it into its subsidiary, bringing Uemura Tech, Shisui Tech, Goshi Tech, Siam Goshi Mfg. Co., Ltd. (Thailand) and Goshi-Thanglong Auto-Parts Co., Ltd (Vietnam) under Yachiyo's subsidiaries.
Dec. 2004: Cancelled its registration with the Japan Securities Dealers Association for over-the-counter trading and listed its shares on the JASDAC Stock Exchange.
Jul. 2005: Established Yachiyo Wuhan Mfg. Co., Ltd. in China, a consolidated subsidiary.
Oct. 2005: Acquired equities of AY Mfg. and Ltd. in the United States, making it a consolidated subsidiary.
Dec. 2006: Honda Motor Co., Ltd. became the Company's parent company after purchasing the Company's shares through a takeover bid.
Apr. 2008: Established YG Tech Co., Ltd., a current consolidated subsidiary, in Mie Prefecture, Japan through a joint-investment pooled by Group companies.
Apr. 2008: Established Yachiyo India Manufacturing Private Limited, a consolidated subsidiary, in India.
Dec. 2008: Goshi India Auto Parts Private Limited, a consolidated subsidiary, started its operations in India.
Sep. 2009: Established Yachiyo de Brasil Industria e Comercio de Pecas Ltda., a consolidated subsidiary, in Brazil.
Feb. 2012: Established Yachiyo Mexico Manufacturing S.A. de C.V., a consolidated subsidiary, in Guanajuato, Mexico.
Apr. 2012: Established the Saitama R&D Center in Sayama, Saitama Prefecture.
May 2012: Established P.T. Yachiyo Trimitra Indonesia.
Jun. 2014: Yachiyo of Ontario Manufacturing, Inc. in Canada transferred sheet-metal business to Matcor-Matsu Group of Companies.

Oct. 2014: Stamping operations of Yachiyo Manufacturing of Alabama, LLC (U.S.A) were acquired by Unipres, and plastic fuel tank operations were transferred to a new production plant in Georgia, after which the company adopted the new name Yachiyo Manufacturing of America, LLC.
Dec. 2014: Established Yachiyo Germany GmbH.
May 2015: Reached basic agreement with Topre to start discussions that Yachiyo will transfer stamping operations in Japan to Topre.
Jun. 2015: Transferred all operations with H-One at UYT Ltd. (an affiliate company in the U.K.) to N Press Assembly Limited.
Jan. 2016: Completed the transfer of business operations of YG Tech, a consolidated company, to Topre, ending the Company’s operations in the sheet-metal business.
Mar. 2018: Yachiyo Industries Yokkaichi Works Co., Ltd. was consigned the vehicle-assembly business and started operations.
Apr. 2018: All stock shares of Yachiyo Industries Yokkaichi Works Co., Ltd. were transferred to Honda Motor Co., ending its consignment of assembling light vehicles.
Jul. 2023: Announced that it will sell Yachiyo Industry, a consolidated subsidiary, to the Motherson Group based in India.
Oct. 2024: Renamed Motherson Yachiyo Automotive Systems Co., Ltd.
source_title: Motherson Yachiyo Automotive Systems Co., Ltd. (Formerly Yachiyo Industry Co., Ltd.) - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: Honda recently honored 52 North American suppliers with awards for outstanding performance in supplying parts and materials in 2023.
Honda recognized 31 mass production suppliers during its annual Honda Supplier Conference in Columbus, Ohio.
Honda recognized 21 service parts suppliers for supporting customer needs after purchasing a Honda or Acura product.
Honda purchased over $38 billion in parts for mass production operations from North American suppliers in 2023.
The mass production parts supported the sales of more than 1.3 million Honda and Acura vehicles in North America.
Honda purchased more than $1.2 billion in parts from its Service Parts suppliers in North America.

Mike Lapham, vice president of Procurement in the Purchasing & Supply Chain Center at Honda Development & Manufacturing of America, LLC, stated that as Honda prepares for EV production at its North American plants, suppliers continue to support petrol and hybrid-electric vehicle customers.
Mike Lapham emphasized maintaining a focus on stabilizing production with outstanding quality to establish the foundation for the EV future.
31 mass production suppliers were recognized in three categories: Excellence in Quality & Delivery, Value, and Sustainability.

The Excellence in Sustainability Award recognizes a company exemplifying Honda’s commitment to social responsibility based on leadership in compliance, ethics, environmental governance, diversity, health and safety, and social/trade compliance.
Michelin Group, located in Greenville and Lexington, South Carolina, received the Supplier Sustainability Award.
Honda does business with 746 OEM suppliers across North America.
These OEM suppliers provide parts to Honda’s 13 plants in the region that produce Honda and Acura cars, light trucks, engines, and transmissions.

Jessica Hammontree, assistant vice president of the Parts Division at American Honda Motor Co., Inc., stated that future success depends on taking new business approaches focused on customer needs as Honda transitions to an EV future.
Jessica Hammontree noted that after new vehicle sales become 100% electrified, customers will rely on Honda for service parts for petrol and hybrid-electric vehicles for the next twenty years.
Honda works with nearly 1,400 service parts suppliers in North America to provide service and accessory parts for more than 20 million Honda and Acura automobiles in operation in the United States.
Honda also provides service parts for powersports and power equipment products through its supplier network.
Honda manages a national network of parts and distribution facilities to deliver high-quality service parts to dealers nationwide and support global parts operations.

facts: On September 24, 2020, Yamaha Golf-Car President Tom McDonald met with Interior Secretary David Bernhardt at the Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation (YMMC) facility in Newnan, Ga.

Mike Chrzanowski, President of Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation (YMCC), and Ben Speciale, President of Yamaha U.S. Marine Business Unit, signed the President’s Pledge to America’s Workers during the event.
The signing reinforces Yamaha’s commitment to workforce development within its U.S. manufacturing facilities.
Tom McDonald stated that jobs created through Yamaha facilities in Georgia make a positive impact on the state’s economy and contribute to the growth of the outdoor industry overall.
In 2016, the golf industry supported $191.9 billion in total annual activity, including 1.9 million jobs and $58.7 billion in wages and benefits.
U.S. golf participation saw record increases with rounds played up 14% in June and 19.7% in July, with industry experts expecting similar August results.
McDonald emphasized that Yamaha will continue to invest in training and best practices necessary to create successful manufacturing career paths within each of its divisions.
Yamaha operates a technician training facility in Kennesaw, Ga., which supports dealers and distributors across the country.

The President’s Pledge to America’s Workers originated from President Trump’s National Council for the American Worker, established on July 19, 2019, to lead a national strategy for training and re-training workers in high-demand industries like recreational manufacturing.
More than 430 companies have signed the pledge, contributing to over 16 million new training opportunities for American students and workers over the next five years.
Yamaha Motor Corporation, USA (YMUS) offers a wide range of products including Power Assist Bicycles, Golf Cars, Power Assist Wheelchair Systems, and various powersports and marine vehicles.
YMUS maintains corporate offices in California and two locations in Georgia, along with facilities in Wisconsin and Alabama, and factory operations in Tennessee and Georgia.
Yamaha Golf-Car Company is headquartered in Kennesaw, Georgia, with assembly and production of all its cars taking place at its Newnan, Georgia production facility.
The company was the first to develop Electronic Fuel Injection technology in a golf car, producing the best gas mileage and smallest carbon footprint in the industry.
Yamaha Golf-Car Company was also the first to introduce a golf car with fully independent, four-wheel suspension and the first gas-powered car that is almost as quiet as an electric one.
source_title: INTERIOR SECRETARY DAVID BERNHARDT MEETS WITH YAMAHA GOLF-CAR COMPANY PRESIDENT TOM MCDONALD | Golf Cart Resource

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp welcomed the announcement that Yamaha Motor Co., Ltd. has officially decided to move its U.S. headquarters to Kennesaw, Georgia.
The relocation involves moving out of California following roughly 50 years of operation there.
The decision follows a meeting between Governor Kemp and Yamaha executives in October of last year at the Southeast U.S./Japan (SEUS-Japan) annual gathering in Tokyo.
Yamaha Motor Co., Ltd. is a manufacturer of ATVs, boat engines, personal watercraft, and other motorized products.
Yamaha already has a strong presence in Georgia, employing over 2,300 Georgians.
The relocation of the Yamaha Motor Corp. U.S. headquarters will begin this year and is expected to continue through 2028.
Governor Brian Kemp stated: “After many years of great partnership, we are honored and proud to welcome Yamaha’s American headquarters to the No. 1 state for business.”

Governor Brian Kemp stated: “This is another loud and clear testament to what we offer job creators from around the world. To any other California-based companies looking for a better home, we’ll give you plenty of reasons to keep Georgia on your mind.”
The Yamaha Motor Company currently employs thousands of people across various facilities in Georgia.
The 1.3-million-square-foot Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation of America factory in Newnan opened in 1986 and currently employs roughly 2,000 Georgians.
Yamaha relocated its marine division to Kennesaw in 1999.
Yamaha expanded the Kennesaw facility with a 75,000-square-foot Marine Innovation Center in 2023.
In 2019, the company moved its motorsports operations to Georgia to benefit from close proximity to the existing facilities.
Yamaha Motor Co., Ltd. is a world-leading producer of motorcycles, marine products, power products, and robotics.
The company was founded in 1955 and is headquartered in Iwata, Shizuoka, Japan.
The company operates under the corporate mission of being a “Kando Creating Company,” aiming to offer new excitement and a more fulfilling life for people all over the world.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Yamaha to Move HQ to Georgia - Cobb Chamber of Commerce

entity_name: Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation of America
canonical_name: Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation of America
facts: Yamaha Motor Corp., USA (YMUS) announced $30,000 in funding to All Kids Bike for six programs in Georgia and California.
The grant covers costs for kindergarten Physical Education classes teaching children how to ride bikes.
Funding is provided through the Yamaha Outdoor Access Initiative (OAI).
Steve Nessl serves as Yamaha’s Motorsports Marketing Manager.
Ryan McFarland is the founder of All Kids Bike and helped deliver bikes to Morris Elementary in Cypress, California.
YMUS employees from Marietta, Georgia, and Cypress, California corporate offices, along with YMMC personnel in Newnan, Georgia, volunteered to build and deliver bikes and helmets to local schools.
Full All Kids Bike programs delivered included 24 balance bikes, pedal conversion kits, helmets, and a teacher’s bike.
Programs were delivered to Elm Street Elementary in Newnan, Georgia; A.L. Burruss Elementary School in Marietta, Georgia; and Juliet Morris Elementary School in Cypress, California.
Three additional schools in Newnan, Marietta, and Cypress are guaranteed funding through the Yamaha grant for the year.
The All Kids Bike program includes an eight-lesson Kindergarten PE Learn-To-Ride Curriculum, teacher training and certification, and a five-year support plan.
Bob Brown is Vice President, Finance and Operations Support at YMMC.
Newnan schools are the first in Georgia to receive the All Kids Bike program.
The All Kids Bike program operates in 350 schools across 45 states, with 50 additional schools in training.
Ryan McFarland is also founder and CEO of Strider Sports International, Inc., which manufactures the Strider Bikes used in the program.

The Yamaha Outdoor Access Initiative has issued quarterly grants to non-profit organizations for over 12 years to support riding groups, outdoor enthusiasts, land stewardship organizations, and land managers.
Yamaha has contributed more than $4.5 million in aid to nearly 400 projects nationwide through the OAI program.
The YMMC facility was founded in 1986 and produces golf cars, personal watercraft, Side-by-Side vehicles, and all-terrain vehicles (ATVs).
The YMMC factory holds ISO9001 Quality Management System and ISO14001 Environmental Management System certifications.

YMUS product offerings include Power Assist Bicycles, Golf Cars, Motorcycles and Scooters, ATVs, Side-by-Side vehicles, Snowmobiles, WaveRunner Personal Watercraft, Boats, Outboard Motors, Outdoor Power Equipment, Power Assist Wheelchair Systems, Surface Mount Technology (SMT), Robotic Machines, Unmanned Helicopters, Accessories, Apparel, Yamalube products, and more.
YMUS maintains a corporate office in California, two corporate offices in Georgia, facilities in Wisconsin and Alabama, and factory operations in Tennessee and Georgia.

YMUS U.S.-based subsidiaries include Yamaha Marine Systems Company (with Bennett Marine in Florida and Kracor Systems in Wisconsin), Skeeter Boats (Texas) with division G3 Boats (Missouri), and Yamaha Precision Propeller (Indiana).
YMUS products are sold through a nationwide network of distributors and dealers in the United States.
source_title: Yamaha Funds Six All Kids Bike Programs in Georgia and California | The Outdoor Wire

facts: Yamaha has approximately 2,600 employees in Georgia.
The company has operated in Georgia for nearly 40 years since opening its first factory there.
In 1997, Yamaha spun off its music unit and created Yamaha Motor USA, which remains technically based in Cypress, Calif.
The Georgia manufacturing facility is named Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corp. of America and is located in Newnan, Ga.
The Newnan factory initially produced golf carts before expanding to wave runners, all-terrain vehicles, and side-by-side off-roaders.
To date, the Georgia factory has produced 4.5 million units.
North American CEO Michael Chrzanowski began his tenure at the Georgia facility more than 20 years ago.
When appointed CEO of the North American operation in 2021, Mr. Chrzanowski chose to remain in Kennesaw rather than relocate to California.
Sales and marketing functions for Yamaha's marine division relocated to Georgia due to proximity to the boating community and the state's welcoming business environment.
A former parts warehouse on Chastain Road now serves as the hub for Yamaha's U.S. marine, motorsports, and golf cart operations, which are its three largest business units.
The Kennesaw facility also functions as a major technical training hub.
Upon taking the CEO role in 2021, Mr. Chrzanowski was told the company's footprint across two facilities in Kennesaw and Marietta would be sufficient, but space constraints emerged within two weeks.
In April 2023, the company inaugurated a 75,280-square-foot Marine Innovation Center attached to its Kennesaw facility.
The Marine Innovation Center trains representatives from 3,000 nationwide dealers and partners, as well as 2,000 technical specialists annually.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp attended the April 2023 opening of the Marine Innovation Center to celebrate Yamaha's commitment to leadership and innovation.
The company operates 13 facilities across the United States, including three campuses in Georgia.
Each Georgia Department of Natural Resources trailer is equipped with a Yamaha portable generator.
The company has donated six G3 boats to the state of Georgia.
Vehicles are donated annually from the Newnan plant to local communities, including ATVs to fire departments and golf carts for schools.
In September, following Hurricane Helene, Yamaha donated 1,000 generators to assist residents in eastern Georgia and the Carolinas.
Mr. Chrzanowski attributes Cobb County's selection for the company's initial investment to its educated workforce and strong transportation infrastructure.
The Cobb Chamber’s annual International Company Reception featured trade representatives from Ireland and Japan, along with diplomats from the Bahamas, Hungary, Norway, Sweden, Slovakia, and Japan.
Deputy Consul General Hiroyuki Kaneda praised Cobb County as one of Japan's most important partner counties in Georgia.
The Georgia Japanese Language School, established in 1974 to help expatriates maintain their mother tongue, is based in Cobb County.
The reception event was sponsored by The Manely Firm and Freeman Mathis Decisions.
source_title: Yamaha Motor's Impact in Georgia Outlined at Cobb International Reception  - Global Atlanta

entity_name: Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation SMART Warehouse
facts: Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation (YMMC), based in Newnan Georgia, broke ground on a new SMART Warehouse in Newnan, Georgia.
The SMART Warehouse is the first of its kind for Yamaha Motor Company worldwide.
The facility utilizes state-of-the-art automated storage and retrieval systems.
The new warehouse will be 200,000 square feet.
The facility will reduce response time for fulfilling dealer orders for Yamaha WaveRunners, Golf Cars, ATVs, and Side-by-Sides by more than 50 percent.
Mike Chrzanowski, President and CEO of Yamaha Motor Corporation, stated the warehouse will improve operational efficiency and material flow velocity to factory assembly lines.
Chrzanowski noted that demand for all outdoor recreational products produced at this factory continues to exceed supply.
SMART is an acronym for Sequenced Material and Reduced Transportation.
The medium parts automated storage and retrieval system will retrieve and put away 240 containers per hour, compared to the current rate of 170 containers per hour.
The small part automated system will handle 2400 containers per hour, compared to the current capability of 550 containers per day.
The new systems will enable Yamaha to reduce forklifts by 50 percent using automatic conveyors and tuggers.
The significant investment underscores the company's long-term commitment to YMCC employees as well as the City of Newnan and Coweta County.
The facility will bring higher paid and more skilled jobs into Coweta County, including robotics experts, software developers and systems engineers.
Designed by SSI SCHAEFER, the new warehouse will include an observation platform for groups such as students from local schools to learn about manufacturing and automated processes.
Yamaha expects to complete the SMART Warehouse in 2024.
Yamaha is currently hiring for various roles, with most careers starting on a contract-to-perm basis.
YMUS has a corporate office in California, two corporate offices in Georgia, facilities in Wisconsin and Alabama, and factory operations in Tennessee and Georgia.

YMUS products include Motorcycles and Scooters, ATV and Side-by-Side vehicles, Snowmobiles, WaveRunner Personal Watercraft, Boats, Outboard Motors, Outdoor Power Equipment, Power Assist Bicycles, Golf Cars, Power Assist Wheelchair Systems, Surface Mount Technology (SMT) and Robotic Machines, Unmanned Helicopters, Accessories, Apparel, Yamalube products, and much more.
source_title: Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation Breaks Ground on Innovative SMART Warehouse in Newnan Georgia | Yamaha

facts: Yamaha Motor Co., Ltd. has announced plans to relocate its U.S. headquarters to Kennesaw, Georgia.
The relocation marks a significant corporate shift after nearly five decades of operations in California.
The relocation process is expected to begin in 2026 and continue through 2028.
Yamaha already maintains a substantial presence in Georgia, employing more than 2,300 workers across multiple facilities.
Central to the company’s U.S. operations is the 1.3-million-square-foot Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation of America plant in Newnan.
The Newnan plant opened in 1986 and supports large-scale production of recreational vehicles.
Yamaha has expanded its marine operations in Kennesaw, including the development of a Marine Innovation Center in recent years.

The decision to relocate the U.S. headquarters reflects the company’s strategy to align corporate leadership more closely with its manufacturing, engineering and product development operations in the Southeast.
Company leaders noted that Georgia’s logistics connectivity, workforce capabilities and established industry ecosystem were key considerations in the decision.

Yamaha has continued to consolidate and expand U.S. operations in the state over the past decade, including the relocation of its motorsports division to Georgia to enhance collaboration across business units.
Founded in 1955 and headquartered in Iwata, Japan, Yamaha Motor Co., Ltd. is a global producer of mobility and marine technologies, as well as power equipment and robotics solutions.
source_title: Yamaha Motor Corp. U.S. to Relocate Headquarters to Georgia - Expansion Solutions
