facts: Governor Nathan Deal announced that Hyundai Glovis Georgia, a leading logistics services company for the automotive sector, will relocate its parts consolidation center to West Point.
The relocation project will create up to 190 jobs over four years.
Governor Nathan Deal stated that Georgia’s booming automotive and logistics industries play critical roles in creating jobs and bringing investment to the state.
Deal noted that companies like Hyundai Glovis need high-quality resources to serve their global customer base and remain internationally competitive.
The company will work with a general contractor to develop a new, state-of-the-art 310,080-square-foot sub-assembly and sequencing facility in the Northwest Harris Business Park.
The facility will be used to assemble and distribute components to meet the strict demands and timetables of its suppliers.
Hyundai Glovis Georgia Chief Operating Officer Hong Kee Rhee expressed appreciation for the support received from West Point, Harris County, and the state of Georgia.
Yoonie Kim, senior project manager on the international investment team at the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), assisted Hyundai Glovis Georgia on behalf of the state.
The City of West Point Development Authority and Georgia Electric Membership Corp also assisted with the project.
Transwestern represented the company during the site selection process.

Harris County Commission Chairman Harry Lange welcomed Hyundai Glovis Georgia as a new corporate citizen, stating its decision to locate in the Northwest Harris Business Park represents a commitment to the county.
Officials anticipate a grand opening for the facility in the fall of 2015.

GDEcD Commissioner Chris Carr highlighted that the automotive sector has grown significantly in Georgia over the past few years due to a competitive business climate, solid supplier network, direct access to leading original equipment manufacturers, and interconnected transportation infrastructure.
Hyundai Glovis designs logistics strategies and processes tailored to customers’ environments using the latest information systems and advanced logistics technologies.
The company provides total logistics services in domestic and international markets, shipping business operations, and logistics consulting.
source_title: Gov. Nathan Deal: Hyundai Glovis Georgia To Create 190 Jobs In Harris County | GaPundit

facts: Hyundai will be using hydrogen fuel cell heavy-duty trucks to bring parts to its US EV plant in Georgia through a partnership with a Californian-based logistics firm.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) has placed a preliminary order of 21 Class 8 XCIENT trucks onto the road.
The purpose of these vehicles is to transport vehicle parts from suppliers to the factory on a daily basis.
HTWO Logistics, a joint venture between Hyundai and Glovis, has planned to install a hydrogen refuelling station at the Megasite to support this deployment.
The parties involved are currently waiting for the hydrogen production capacity to be announced soon.
Due to the trucks connecting suppliers and HMGMA's on-site consolidation centre, the firms have released a plan to expand the logistics to a broader network of suppliers.
HTWO Logistics was set up earlier this year as a brand that will enable each stage of the entire hydrogen value chain, from production and storage to transportation and utilisation.

Hyundai's North America commercial vehicle and hydrogen business boss Jim Park stated that HMGMA's deployment of XCIENT for logistics and operations is one of the main initiatives of HTWO, delivering on their strategy to showcase a closed-loop hydrogen logistics ecosystem and improve manufacturing environmental sustainability.

facts: Haering Precision USA LP hosted high-level diplomatic representatives from the Georgia Consular Corps for an exclusive visit.

The event was organized in partnership with the Hart County Industrial Building Authority (IBA) to provide insight into the region's economic strength, workforce initiatives, and business-friendly environment.
Diplomats exchanged views with Haering Precision's plant management on state-of-the-art manufacturing technologies, innovative strength, and the company's economic importance for the region.
Guests toured the plant to gain direct insights into Häring's high-precision production processes and advanced technologies.

Hart County leaders in attendance included Amanda Brown, President & CEO of the Hart County Chamber of Commerce; Steve Burton, CEO of the Hart County College and Career Academy; and representatives from the City of Hartwell and Hart County.
Local government, industry, and the education sector collaborate closely to promote skills training and attract new businesses.
Dustin Chambers serves as Plant Manager of Haering Precision USA LP.

A particular focus of the visit was on skills development, with Haering Precision's education and training programs serving as a model for close collaboration between industry and educational institutions.
Partnerships with institutions such as the Hart County College and Career Academy ensure a qualified skilled workforce and increase the region's attractiveness for international companies.
The Hart County Industrial Building Authority held discussions about expanding industrial opportunities, attracting new investment, and expanding infrastructure.

Dustin Chambers stated: “As a global company, we are pleased to welcome the Georgia Consular Corps to Lavonia and showcase our work. This visit demonstrates our commitment to innovation and workforce development, as well as the strength of Hart County's industrial sector and opportunities for international partnerships.”
Haering Precision USA remains a leader in manufacturing excellence and workforce development, strengthening Hart County's position as a leading industrial center in Georgia.

facts: Anton Häring KG is a family-run technology company with more than 4,000 employees across five locations on four continents, including 300 apprentices and students.
The company is a leading specialist in precision manufacturing and simultaneous engineering, developing and producing complex parts and assemblies in large and medium series.
Customers come from around the world, specifically in the automotive industry.
Häring produces around 3 million components per day, each meeting the highest standards.
Components are critical elements of highly complex systems and are used in key technologies such as brake systems, hydrogen valves, and hydraulic cylinders.

Precision parts are often only a few centimeters in size and are manufactured with tolerances in the micrometer range — one-thousandth of a millimeter, about one-hundredth the thickness of a human hair.

Parts perform critical functions: enabling movement, maintaining stable pressure, opening and closing passages, sealing systems reliably, and guiding fluids or gases precisely under extreme conditions.
The company operates 1,300 000 ft² of global production space and around 1,000 production machines to handle a wide range of customer orders.

Core expertise lies in creating optimal production processes for customer-specific parts from concept to series production using a simultaneous engineering approach where production experts are involved early in product development.
Manufacturing competencies include forming and machining of diverse raw materials, hardening, coating and other finishing processes, as well as assembly of complete units.
The company combines processes flexibly rather than relying on a single technology to deliver efficient and precise solutions for customers across a wide range of sectors.
Häring takes ecological, economic, and social responsibility by supporting environmental protection at all locations and assisting communities, public institutions, and schools.
Through its own educational initiatives, the company fosters young talent and creates opportunities for future generations.
Employee support includes housing assistance, modern company restaurants, and other offerings designed to improve everyday work life.
source_title: Website | Anton Häring KG

The acquisition deal is valued at US$86 million.

Jan Wikström serves as the president of Hexpol Thermoplastic Compounding.
The acquisition adds new capabilities, application knowhow and a new customer base to Hexpol Thermoplastic Compounding in the U.S., according to Jan Wikström.
The announcement was made in an Oct. 31 news release.

Hexpol Group is recognized as the world’s largest rubber compounder.
Hexpol operates through two business areas: Hexpol Compounding and Hexpol Engineered Products.

facts: Hexpol Compounding Americas is North America's largest custom mixer.

The company is located in Burton, Ohio.

facts: HEXPOL shifted from automotive roots to specialty compounding through bolt-on acquisitions in 2023–2024.
The firm operates over 50 production units worldwide.
Primary customers include OEMs and Tier-1 suppliers in automotive (including EVs), medical device manufacturers, cable & wire, industrial equipment, renewable-energy, and building-components firms.
North America expansions focus on specialty compounding capacity to shorten lead times for OEMs and Tier-1s.
Key market focus areas include EV e-mobility cables, battery encapsulation, renewable energy sealing, hydrogen infrastructure, and biocompatible TPEs for medical tubing and seals.
Bolt-on acquisitions target specialty elastomers and TPE niches for higher-spec end markets to drive margin uplift within 12–24 months post-close.
Commercial programs bundle materials, application engineering, and rapid prototyping to secure multi-year supply agreements and increase share-of-wallet in top accounts.
Capacity additions are phased to booked programs through 2026–2027 with measurable KPIs on customer qualification cycles, on-time launches, and share-of-wallet gains.

Acquisitions emphasize application know-how, customer stickiness, and ESG-advantaged formulations; integration success is monitored by accelerated qualification cycles and measurable revenue from new high-spec markets such as EV thermal management and medical devices.

Customers demand high-performance, low-emission elastomers with faster qualification cycles, consistent batch quality, and increasing recycled or bio-based content to meet regulatory and OEM sustainability targets.
Investment prioritizes high-performance elastomers, TPEs, silicone compounds, recycled-content masterbatches, and bio-based polymers to address technical and sustainability requirements.
Simulation tools and AI-assisted compound design reduce time-to-qualification by months and enable rapid iteration across customer-specific formulations.
Deployment of inline sensors and IoT-driven process control improves batch consistency and lowers scrap rates through real-time process adjustments.
Robotic material handling, advanced mixing controls, and predictive maintenance upgrades at flagship plants lift throughput and cut energy intensity per ton.
Expanded recycled and bio-attributed feedstocks plus ISCC PLUS-certified grades position the company to capture demand for low-carbon materials.
Patent activity and certifications in medical and aerospace-grade materials underpin premium pricing and long-term supply agreements.
HEXPOL operates across Europe, North America, and Asia with production and R&D hubs serving automotive, industrial, medical, and consumer end markets.
Growing contributions from medical and EV-related applications are expected to lift average selling prices and reduce cyclicality relative to automotive exposure.
Delays in EV adoption or renewable-energy capex slow demand for specialty compounds linked to electrification, affecting near-term sales growth.
Disruptions in supply chains and shortages of specialized compounding talent raise execution risk and can delay capacity expansions or integration.
source_title: What is Growth Strategy and Future Prospects of HEXPOL Company? – PortersFiveForce.com

facts: Hexpol has appointed board member Henrik Elmin as its next CEO, effective August 1.
Henrik Elmin will drive the company's 2030 strategy.
Elmin previously served as Business Area President for Industrial Technique at Atlas Copco, a large-cap industrial machinery company with global scale.
Elmin holds an engineering degree from Lund and an MBA from INSEAD.
Hexpol manufactures compounded polymers including rubber, thermoplastic elastomers, and engineering plastics.
Hexpol's products are used in car tires, wheel rims, seals, and gaskets.
Hexpol operates in two business segments: Hexpol Compounding and Hexpol Engineered Products.
Revenue in the compounding segment fell 6% last year to roughly SEK 17.8 billion.
Total trailing revenue is down about 9%.
First quarter 2026 operating profit missed analyst estimates at SEK 701 million versus SEK 719 million expected, and well below the SEK 839 million a year earlier.
Hexpol's business performance is tied to auto OEM cycles and raw material pricing.
When carmakers slow production or demand cheaper suppliers, Hexpol feels the impact.
Hexpol's feedstock costs swing with oil price fluctuations.
EBIT margins have been falling from the mid-teens toward double digits due to industry-wide margin compression.
Elmin replaces Klas Dahlberg as CEO, who started in July 2024 and left after less than 20 months without a publicly explained reason.
Dahlberg previously worked at NIBE Climate Solutions, a Swedish HVAC and heat pump company.
Hexpol trades at a trailing P/E of about 14, with a market cap around SEK 26 billion.
An analyst recently trimmed Hexpol's stock price target from SEK 85 to SEK 84 while maintaining a neutral rating.
Hexpol's 2030 strategy points toward recycled materials and circular economy products to move away from virgin fossil-based feedstock.
Recycled-content polymers face thin initial margins and heavy certification requirements despite being a real growth trend.
The board appointed Elmin to provide continuity rather than revolution.
source_title: Hexpol's New CEO Can't Fix What's Not a CEO Problem

facts: Piedmont Resin Supply is a manufacturer of Nylon 6 and Nylon 66 custom resin compounds headquartered in Cartersville, Georgia.
Piedmont Resin Supply operates as a HEXPOL company.
The facility maintains an on-site testing lab outfitted with state-of-the-art testing equipment that performs in-depth and precise tests on every product before it leaves the facility.
Piedmont Resin Supply is an ISO 9001:2015 Certified company.
The facility has an annual capacity of 70+ million pounds.
The facility operates 4 Twin Screw production lines.
The facility includes multiple blending stations with capabilities up to 120,000 pounds.
The facility provides custom compounding and toll services supplying a variety of resins at competitive prices to meet customer specifications.
The company occupies 140,000 square feet of operating and warehouse space.
Piedmont Resin Supply is focused on polymers and engineering thermoplastics.
The company's office address is located at 420 S Erwin St, Cartersville, Georgia 30120.
Deliveries are processed at 131 Industrial Drive, Cartersville, GA, 30120.
Pickups are processed at 200 Industrial Drive, Cartersville, GA, 30120.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Industrial Co., Ltd. will create 100 new jobs and invest more than $24 million in a new manufacturing facility near Newnan in Coweta County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that partners like Kia and Hyundai are attracting a large network of suppliers, creating jobs and opportunity for hardworking Georgians across the entire state.

Governor Brian Kemp noted that these suppliers have now committed to bringing over 5,300 jobs to Georgia, mostly to communities outside of large urban areas, since last year’s Hyundai Metaplant announcement.
Hyundai Industrial was established in 1978 and is headquartered in Ulsan, Korea.
The Hyundai and Kia supplier specializes in manufacturing car seats, arm rests, and head rests.

Mr. Woosuck Kim, Team Leader at HDI America Inc., stated that Hyundai Industrial has more than four decades of experience providing comfortable and safe experiences for drivers and passengers with some of the world’s biggest automotive brands.

Mr. Woosuck Kim added that the company is excited to be part of the e-mobility transition happening in Georgia and looks forward to working with Coweta County to create mutually beneficial growth for the community and future employees.
Hyundai Industrial has purchased an existing building at 83 Amlajack Way in Coweta County.
The purchased building will be retrofitted to produce arm and head rests for OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Hyundai Industrial will focus on filling manufacturing production roles.
Job openings will be posted on Indeed.
Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman John Reidelbach stated that they are thrilled to welcome Hyundai Industrial Co., Ltd. to Coweta County.
Chairman John Reidelbach noted that Hyundai joins their thriving manufacturing industries and will have close proximity to I-85, making transport to nearby automobile plants effortless.
Chairman John Reidelbach expressed excitement for the opportunities Hyundai will bring to citizens as workforce opportunities continue to increase within the community.

Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Coweta County Development Authority and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that with Kia in West Georgia and Hyundai on the eastern coast, suppliers like Hyundai Industrial can locate almost anywhere in the state and still be center of at least two major manufacturers.
Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that companies are able to select a community that’s the right fit for them, and vice versa.

Commissioner Pat Wilson described the relationship between Coweta County and Hyundai Industrial as another classic example of Georgia’s partnership approach to economic development, matching a community with an existing asset and plan to a company whose goals aligned.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
As the electric vehicle market continues to grow, Georgia has pursued the entire supply chain, creating more than $25.1 billion in investments and 29,000 jobs since 2020.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to fiscal year 2021.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
source_title: Hyundai, Kia Supplier to Invest in Manufacturing Facility in Coweta County – Gradick Communications

facts: A Sept. 4 raid on Hyundai’s battery plant in Georgia resulted in 475 workers being placed in handcuffs, with more than 300 of them being South Korean.
In 2022, Hyundai announced plans to build a $5.5 billion electric vehicle plant in Georgia.
One year after Hyundai's announcement, LG announced it would invest billions to build electric batteries.

Attorney Charles Kuck, representing more than a dozen detained workers, stated the individuals were brought from abroad to install products and oversee the internal building of the plant rather than perform jobs Americans can do.
President Donald Trump stated the workers were in the country illegally and that the U.S. needs arrangements with countries like South Korea to train U.S. citizens for this work.

Kuck argued that training Americans properly takes three to five years before authorization, whereas using a visa allowed since at least 1990 would allow Hyundai to complete plant installation quickly and hire U.S. workers within four years.
The detained workers were on either ESTA B visas or B-1 visas.
ESTA visas allow individuals from Visa Waiver Program countries, like South Korea, to enter the U.S. for up to 90 days for business or tourism without applying for a visa.
A B-1 visa is roughly equivalent to an ESTA but applies to individuals from countries not on the Visa Waiver program.
Immigration lawyer Rajiv Khanna noted that H-1B visas are annual lotteries with less than a 50% chance of winning, and H-2B visas require months of legal procedures.
B1 and B2 visas are typically classified as tourists-slash-business visas rather than work visas for the United States.

Kuck asserted that the B-1/ESTA visas were the correct and only choice for Hyundai to bring in specific equipment not manufactured in the U.S. to avoid import taxes and build the plant per Georgia's request.

Law professor Kevin Johnson stated that Hyundai did not directly hire the workers, who were instead hired by labor contractors secured by Hyundai, likely preferred for their product knowledge, Korean language skills, and familiarity with Korean corporate operations.
South Korea is reportedly working on chartering a plane to transport the detained workers out of a south Georgia jail back to South Korea.
Johnson noted that government intervention to repatriate citizens is extraordinary but consistent with the Trump administration's stated effort to enforce workplace immigration laws.
Hyundai has not publicly commented on the raid or the next steps at the plant.
Johnson highlighted that the U.S. solicited investment under the previous administration to attract Hyundai to Georgia, citing economic benefits and job creation for U.S. workers as key factors.

The warrant for the Sept. 4 raid originally targeted four Latino workers, and enforcement officials were unprepared for South Korean nationals, bringing no Korean interpreters and only expecting to arrest around 200 Latino workers.
Officials made an on-the-spot decision to arrest all individuals present rather than vet each person's visa status and job duties individually before the raid.
At least one detained individual has a reported criminal history, while none of the others have reported records at this time.
Khanna observed that the administration shifted from targeting criminal aliens to establishing an arrest quota regardless of who is caught.
Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem stated the raid was intended to send a message to other countries about employing workers legally and does not deter foreign investment.
Noem encouraged companies coming to the U.S. to employ U.S. citizens and follow immigration laws when bringing people to work in the country.
Department of Homeland Security data indicates that Asians have not been targeted as heavily as South Americans in immigration enforcement actions.

Johnson compared the raid to ICE operations targeting Latinos in car washes and day labor pickup points, noting this case is different but aligns with the Trump administration's commitment to enforcing immigration laws.
The situation involves South Korea, which Johnson described as very integral to U.S. efforts to navigate challenges in Asia involving China and other nations.
source_title: Two types of visas that didn’t keep South Korean Hyundai workers out of jail

facts: A subsidiary of Hyundai Motor has used child labor at a plant that supplies parts for the Korean carmaker’s assembly line in Montgomery, Ala.
Underage workers, in some cases as young as 12, have worked at a metal stamping plant operated by SMART Alabama LLC.
SMART is listed by Hyundai in corporate filings as a majority-owned unit.
SMART supplies parts for popular cars and SUVs built by Hyundai at its flagship US assembly plant in Montgomery.
Hyundai did not respond to phone calls or emails from Reuters seeking comment on the allegations.
SMART stated it follows federal, state, and local laws and denies knowingly employing anyone ineligible for employment.
SMART relies on temporary work agencies to fill jobs and expects them to follow recruitment and hiring laws.
The allegations followed the brief disappearance in February 2022 of a Guatemalan migrant child from her family’s home in Alabama.
The missing girl, who turns 14 that month, along with her brothers ages 12 and 15, worked at the plant earlier in 2022 and were not attending school.
Their father, Pedro Tzi, confirmed their employment at the plant.
Enterprise police identified the missing girl by name in a public alert during a search for her.
The Enterprise police force, located about 45 miles from the Luverne plant, lacks jurisdiction to investigate labor-law violations and notified the state attorney general’s office instead.
Mike Lewis, a spokesperson at the Alabama attorney general’s office, declined to comment on whether investigators contacted SMART or Hyundai.
Interviews with a dozen former/current employees and labor recruiters indicate a larger cohort of underage workers found jobs at the supplier over recent years.
Several minors forgone schooling for long shifts at the plant, which has a documented history of health and safety violations including amputation hazards.
Most current and former employees spoke on condition of anonymity.
The precise number of children who worked at SMART, their pay, and employment terms remain undetermined.
The child labor revelations could spark consumer, regulatory, and reputational backlash for Hyundai.
Hyundai’s online human rights policy forbids child labor throughout its workforce and suppliers.
Hyundai recently announced plans to expand in the US with over $5 billion in investments, including a new electric vehicle factory near Savannah, Ga.
David Michaels, former US assistant secretary of labor for OSHA, stated consumers should be outraged that cars are being built partly by child workers.
Labor experts noted heightened risks of undocumented migrants ending up in hazardous workplaces during US labor shortages and supply chain disruptions.
Alabama and federal laws limit minors under 18 from working in metal stamping and pressing operations like SMART's.
Alabama law requires children 17 and under to be enrolled in school.
Michaels warned Hyundai executives in 2015 that heavy demand for "just-in-time" parts was causing safety lapses.
The SMART plant builds parts for the Elantra, Sonata, and Santa Fe models, which accounted for almost 37% of Hyundai’s US sales through June.
Federal records show SMART has received repeated OSHA penalties for health and safety violations.
A Reuters review found SMART assessed with at least $48,515 in OSHA penalties since 2013, including a fine this year.
OSHA inspections documented crush and amputation hazards at the factory.
The plant's website states it has capacity to supply parts for up to 400,000 vehicles each year.
The facility has struggled to retain labor to meet Hyundai’s demand.
In late 2020, SMART wrote a letter to US consular officials in Mexico seeking a visa for a Mexican worker due to being "severely lacking in labor."
The late 2020 letter was written by SMART General Manager Gary Sport and stated Hyundai "will not tolerate such shortcomings."
Attorneys filed a class-action lawsuit earlier this year against SMART and several staffing firms supplying workers with US visas.
The lawsuit, filed in the US District Court for the Northern District of Georgia on behalf of about 40 Mexican workers, alleges some hired as engineers were ordered to do menial jobs.
SMART called the lawsuit allegations "baseless" and "meritless" in court documents.
Many minors at the plant were hired through recruitment agencies.
Staffing firms are often criticized by labor advocates for enabling large employers to outsource responsibility for verifying employee work eligibility.
A former adult migrant worker said there were around 50 underage workers across different shifts at SMART.
Another former US citizen worker said she worked alongside about a dozen minors on her shift.
Former employee Tabatha Moultry, who worked on SMART’s assembly line through 2019, noted high turnover and reliance on migrant workers for intense production demands.
Moultry recalled working with one migrant girl who looked 11 or 12 years old but claimed to be 13.
Tzi contacted Enterprise police on Feb 3 after his daughter did not come home.
Police issued an Amber Alert and launched a manhunt for Alvaro Cucul, a 21-year-old Guatemalan migrant and SMART worker.
Using cell phone geolocation data, police located Cucul and the girl in a parking lot in Athens, Georgia.
The girl told officers Cucul was a friend they traveled with to look for work opportunities; Cucul was later arrested and deported.
After local news coverage of the disappearance, SMART dismissed a number of underage workers due to fears of an authority crackdown.
Tzi previously worked at SMART and now does construction and forestry odd jobs, expressing regret that his children worked but noting the family needed the income.
Tzi stated the kids are no longer working and will be in school in the fall.
source_title: Hyundai accused of using child labor at Alabama factory

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) Megasite
facts: In 2022, Hyundai Motor Group announced a $5.5 billion investment to establish Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
HMGMA is Hyundai's first US-based electric vehicle (EV) mass production facility.
The HMGMA facility is located on a 3,000-plus acre site in Bryan County, Georgia.
HMGMA integrates cutting-edge technology and sustainability initiatives.
Total investment in Georgia related to the project has surged to over $8 billion.
HMGMA and its network of Tier 1 suppliers are creating 12,000 direct, long-term jobs.
The project is driving significant economic growth across the coastal region.
In March 2025, the HMGMA Metaplant officially opened, marking the start of commercial production.
The project reinforces Georgia's position as a leader in EV innovation and manufacturing.
Hussey Gay Bell provided architecture, engineering, and surveying services for multiple Tier 1 supplier projects related to the development.
On-site facilities were built for Hyundai Glovis.
On-site facilities were built for Hyundai Mobis.
On-site facilities were built for Hyundai Materials.
An on-site facility was built for the Hyundai Motor Group/LG Energy Solution (LGES) JV EV Battery Cell Manufacturing Plant.
Off-site supplier projects were completed for Seoyon E-Hwa.
Off-site supplier projects were completed for Daechang Seat Corp.
Off-site supplier projects were completed for Seohan Auto Georgia.
Off-site supplier projects were completed for Sewon America.
Off-site supplier projects were completed for PHA.
The supplier projects were designed and constructed in parallel with HMGMA’s state-of-the-art smart factory.
The design and construction ensured seamless integration and operational efficiency to support Hyundai’s next-generation EV production.
source_title: Hyundai Megasite - Hussey Gay Bell

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is now open following its Grand Opening celebration.
HMGMA is the key pillar of Hyundai Motor Group’s $12.6 billion investment in Georgia.
The project is the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
The vehicle assembly and battery plant will produce up to 500,000 electric and hybrid vehicles annually for Hyundai, Kia and Genesis brands.
Hyundai Motor Group commits to an additional $21 billion investment from 2025 to 2028 to drive U.S. manufacturing growth.
Since entering the U.S., Hyundai Motor Group has invested $20.5 billion in the U.S., creating directly and indirectly more than 570,000 American jobs.
The Grand Opening ceremony marks the completion of the project, occurring just two and a half years after breaking ground.

Euisun Chung, Executive Chair of Hyundai Motor Group, stated that HMGMA represents the Group’s advanced manufacturing capabilities, commitment to innovation, and investment in relationships with partners and communities in Georgia.
Chung highlighted the rich history of craftsmanship and manufacturing in the community, along with the talented workforce at HMGMA building the future of mobility with America, in America.

The event was attended by Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung, Governor Brian P. Kemp, U.S. Representative Buddy Carter, Hyundai Motor Group Vice Chair Jaehoon Chang, Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO José Muñoz, Kia Corporation President and CEO Ho Sung Song, company officials, state and local leaders, HMGMA Meta Pro employees, and other distinguished guests.
Governor Brian P. Kemp called HMGMA a once-in-a-generation opportunity made possible by commitment to working with job creators for decades.
Kemp highlighted collaborations between HMGMA and Georgia’s universities and technical colleges, including Georgia Quick Start’s on-site training facility, preparing Georgians for high-quality jobs.
Kemp noted the plant's award-winning infrastructure, such as ports connecting companies to markets nationwide and globally.
Hyundai Motor Group entered a landmark agreement with the State of Georgia in May 2022 to establish an electric and hybrid vehicle production and battery manufacturing facility in Ellabell, Georgia.
The site is located 20 miles outside of historic Savannah on a 2,906-acre Megasite.
HMGMA was officially formed by June 2022.
Within four months of formation, the company broke ground on the project.
On October 3, 2024, less than two years after breaking ground, the first vehicle – a Hyundai IONIQ 5 – rolled off the assembly line.
Hyundai’s IONIQ 9 three-row electric SUV is now in production at HMGMA.
The first Kia model to be built at the facility will join production in 2026.
In addition to EVs for Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia, the plant is equipped to produce hybrid vehicles.
HMGMA initially has the capacity to produce 300,000 vehicles annually.
The Group’s investment in Georgia related to HMGMA and joint battery ventures with LG Energy Solution and SK On is $12.6 billion.
This represents the largest single investment in the state’s history.
The project includes a total of 8,500 jobs at HMGMA by 2031.
The Group’s total investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs.
These investments are expected to generate $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year, according to the Center for Automotive Research.
As of the end of 2024, more than $2.5 billion in capital investment and approximately 6,900 jobs have been announced by HMGMA suppliers in 12 counties across the state.
This supplier investment more than doubles the additional $1 billion investment originally anticipated.
To date, Hyundai Motor Group has invested more than $20.5 billion in the U.S., creating or supporting over 570,000 jobs nationwide.
Earlier this week, the Group announced a significant investment of $21 billion in the U.S. from 2025 to 2028.
$9 billion will be invested to establish an annual production capacity in the U.S. of 1.2 million vehicles.
The Group plans to expand HMGMA’s annual production capacity to a total of 500,000 units, an increase of 200,000 units from the original plan.
The Group’s existing U.S. production sites – Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Autoland Georgia – will also see improvements in production facilities.
A total of $6 billion will be allocated to enhance the Group’s parts and logistics business.
This includes establishing an Electric Arc Furnace-based integrated steel mill in the U.S. through its affiliate, Hyundai Steel.
The mill will have an annual steel production capacity of 2.7 million tons.
The mill will supply high-quality automotive steel plates to HMGMA when commercial steel production begins in 2029.

The Group will invest an additional $6 billion to drive innovation and expand strategic partnerships with U.S. companies in areas including autonomous driving, robotics, artificial intelligence (AI), and advanced air mobility (AAM).
Through this $21 billion commitment to drive U.S. manufacturing growth, the Group anticipates it will create 14,000 new direct full-time jobs in the U.S. by 2028.
The overall economic impact is expected to generate more than 100,000 direct and indirect job opportunities across related industries.
HMGMA’s architecture blends advanced technology with design elements that reflect Hyundai’s brand identity and the natural beauty of the Savannah area.

HMGMA has integrated a variety of proof-of-concept manufacturing technologies and systems from Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center Singapore (HMGICS), scaling these to accommodate larger production volume at the Georgia facility.
All HMGMA’s processes – order collection, procurement, logistics and production – are optimized using AI and real-time data.
Cutting-edge robotics and vision systems ensure high-quality vehicle production.
These innovative manufacturing systems help create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers, improving efficiency.
HMGMA expands its mixed-model production capability and integrates various robot processes to enhance automation.
Automation includes automated guided vehicles for an optimized logistics system.
Boston Dynamic’s Spot is used for exterior quality inspection in the weld shop.
Parking robots are used in the assembly shop.
Automation in HMGMA’s assembly shop is nearly double that of the Group’s existing facilities.
Oscar Kwon, CEO of HMGMA, stated it is a historic moment that their state-of-the-art smart plant is officially open.
Kwon emphasized creating a comfortable and welcoming work environment for Meta Pros alongside an energy-efficient and technologically advanced plant.
Kwon noted it is essential for Meta Pros to be happy, comfortable and safe in their work environment for long-lasting careers at HMGMA.
All work zones, from office to production areas, have been designed to encourage communication and build team collaboration.
Designs feature open layouts with ample daylight and indoor landscaping.
Production areas, such as those in the assembly building, have team centers with skylights delivering natural light at the center of the building.
These centers can be used for employee breaks and meetings.
The 3,050-foot-long front of the HMGMA building’s exterior is lit in two parallel lines, inspired by a stream.
This outlines an area where interstate travelers can view vehicles as they travel by conveyor from paint to general assembly.
A solar parking lot located at the front of the site will generate 5.2 megawatts of energy.
This accounts for roughly five percent of the Metaplant’s total power consumption.
The solar panels are installed above 1,878 parking spaces, providing shade for vehicles while simultaneously producing electricity.
A 41-acre ecological park that includes trails, running tracks, sports fields, and picnic areas will feature prominently in front of the Metaplant.
The park is for Meta Pros to enjoy before or after work.
Construction of the park is ongoing.
HMGMA has deployed Hyundai Motor XCIENT heavy-duty hydrogen fuel-cell electric trucks for clean logistics operations.
A total of 21 XCIENT trucks are currently in operation.
These represent more than a third of the Glovis America truck fleet at HMGMA.
The trucks transport vehicle parts from suppliers across the region to the 2,906-acre Megasite where HMGMA is located on a daily basis.
This initiative represents a significant step forward in HMGMA’s leadership efforts to both reduce its carbon footprint and actively promote sustainable practices in daily logistics operations.

Working under Hyundai Motor Group’s global vision of shaping a better future for humanity through smart mobility solutions and sustainable innovation, Hyundai Motor has supported several non-profit organizations that have had a positive impact on the community, especially in education, safety and sustainability.
Since 2022, Hyundai and Genesis, along with various social responsibility initiatives and nonprofit organizations, have donated nearly $4.5 million to local organizations in Georgia.
Kia’s Autoland Georgia factory in West Point has provided over $22 million in support of Georgia communities since 2009.
This includes more than $6 million to support educational programs such as the THINC College & Career Academy, and SAE’s “A World in Motion.”
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America Celebrates Grand Opening, Powering U.S. Economic Growth - HMGMA

facts: A federal agency plans to reassess its environmental permit for Hyundai’s $7.6 billion electric vehicle plant in Georgia after a conservation group complained that regulators failed to properly examine the sprawling factory’s potential impacts on the area’s water supply.

The Army Corps of Engineers said state and local economic development agencies that applied for the project’s 2022 permit never mentioned Hyundai wanted to withdraw up to 6.6 million gallons (25 million liters) per day from the underground aquifer that’s a major regional source of drinking water.
Details of the plant’s needs came out earlier this year as the Georgia Environmental Protection Division considered a proposal for four new wells to supply water to the auto factory.
As a result, the Army Corps said it will revisit its finding that the project would have “negligible impacts.”

The Army Corps sent a similar letter to the Ogeechee Riverkeeper conservation group, which gave notice in June that it planned to sue if the agency refused to revisit the permit issued for the Hyundai project in October 2022.

Ben Kirsch, the riverkeeper group’s legal director, stated that the concentration of pumping will have local impacts on domestic and agricultural wells, and raised questions about impacts on natural springs, wetlands, tributaries, and streams.
The Army Corps’ decision comes as Hyundai pushes to start production before the end of the year at its 2,900-acre (1,170-hectare) plant in Bryan County west of Savannah.
The site will produce EVs and the batteries that power them.
The South Korean automaker plans to employ 8,000 workers at the plant, making it the largest economic development project Georgia has ever tackled.
The Army Corps ordered no delays or disruptions to construction at the plant site as a result of its permit reassessment.

Cheri Dragos-Pritchard, a spokesperson for the Army Corps’ Savannah District, stated that the permit is still valid and work has not been requested to stop, though it is unknown how long the additional review might take.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America said it will assist as needed to ensure the Army Corps gets the information it needs.
The company stated it has worked tirelessly with relevant authorities to ensure they are good neighbors and that operations do not negatively impact the community’s water resources.

Sara Lips, a spokesperson for the state Environmental Protection Division, said the extra scrutiny by the federal government is “unlikely to impact or delay” a final decision by Georgia regulators on whether to permit wells for the Hyundai project.

The Army Corps permit obtained by state and local economic developers authorized the filling or dredging of 221 acres (89 hectares) of wetlands at the plant site just a few months after Hyundai announced plans to build its EV factory in May 2022.
The Army Corps concluded then that the project would have “negligible impacts on municipal and private water supplies,” relying on information provided by economic developers.
Trip Tollison, president and CEO of the Savannah Area Economic Development Authority, stated they never purposefully withheld anything.
Tollison said he expected the updated information requested by the Army Corps to be submitted within 10 days.
He noted the federal agency would typically complete its review within a month and expressed confidence that the reevaluation won’t hold up the project.
Tollison said, “There’s enough water for everyone,” and added, “We feel really good about where we are.”
Georgia’s environmental agency issued draft permits in July for the four wells to supply Hyundai.
The state agency is now evaluating public comments before reaching a final decision on the wells.
The wells would be drilled in neighboring Bulloch County, where some farmers and rural residents have said they worry the auto plant will siphon water away from their crops and homes.
State regulators concluded that water withdrawals by the Hyundai plant would lower water levels in the aquifer up to 19 feet (5.8 meters) for private wells within 5 miles (8 kilometers).
Regulators said most wells won’t see any impacts because they reach deeper into the ground.
The state agency also said that nearby rivers and streams won’t be affected because dense layers of rock seal off the aquifer from water at the surface.
Kirsch with the Ogeechee Riverkeeper said he hopes a second look by the Army Corps will provide more details on how state regulators reached those conclusions.
He stated, “We definitely want to see the Corps independently evaluate this,” and added, “This should have been all considered before the wetlands were filled and buildings went up.”
source_title: US agency to reexamine permit for Hyundai's $7,6 billion electric vehicle plant in Georgia - Batteries News

facts: EON Reality announced a strategic initiative to cultivate the skilled workforce for Georgia’s automotive and EV manufacturing ecosystem.
The initiative features a specialized “AI Academy for Income – Automotive/EV Track.”
The initiative is amplified by EON Reality’s Spatial AI technology.
EON Reality will forge partnerships with Georgia’s state agencies, leading educational institutions, and key automotive manufacturers.
The goal is to deploy XR and AI training infrastructure to equip thousands of Georgians with skills for modern vehicle production careers.
Georgia is securing its position as a major hub for next-generation mobility.
Hyundai’s $5.5B+ MetaPlant America near Savannah is attracting massive investments.
The Hyundai plant is poised to create over 8,100 jobs.
There are significant expansions in the broader automotive supply chain.

Modern automotive and EV facilities demand proficiency in EV battery assembly, high-voltage systems safety, robotics operation, complex assembly techniques, and collaboration with AI-driven manufacturing systems.
Conventional training methods struggle to deliver these competencies at the required speed, scale, and safety levels.
EON Reality provides an AI-enhanced solution for Georgia’s automotive workforce needs.
The initiative leverages the EON-XR Platform, a cloud-based SaaS solution for creating and deploying photorealistic, interactive 3D training simulations.
The platform features a Spatial AI Text-to-XR engine that transforms complex automotive assembly instructions, EV battery manuals, or SOPs into dynamic XR training modules in under 80 seconds.
This dramatically accelerates curriculum development and updates.
The Specialized AI Academy for Income – Automotive/EV Track integrates immersive XR simulation with AI-driven personalized learning paths.
The program focuses on demonstrable skill mastery, awards industry-recognized micro-credentials, and creates direct hiring funnels with leading automotive employers in Georgia.
EON Career Guide & Compass provides intelligent AI career advisors to help Georgians explore automotive manufacturing roles, assess skills, and map training journeys.
The AI-Era Automotive Curriculum is co-created with industry experts from Georgia’s automotive sector.
Modules build proficiency in technical tasks, interacting with AI, interpreting data from connected machines, and collaborating with robotic systems.
Trainees are immersed in realistic virtual assembly lines and simulated high-voltage environments.
EON Reality’s methodology enables learners to grasp complex skills up to 4x faster.
Graduates are designed to be productive and safe from their first day.
EON Reality is actively engaging with Georgia’s key stakeholders through deep partnerships.

State Agencies like the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) and Georgia Quick Start will provide strategic alignment, integrate with state training programs, facilitate industry connections, and explore aligned funding opportunities.
Educational Institutions like the Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG) and Savannah Technical College will serve as deployment and delivery partners.
TCSG and Savannah Technical College will embed XR/AI training in existing automotive and technical programs, provide skilled instructors, and leverage regional presence.
Automotive Industry Partners like Hyundai, Remington, and Tier Suppliers will collaborate to define skill standards and validate technical accuracy of training content.
Hyundai, Remington, and Tier Suppliers may offer apprenticeships or co-op opportunities and prioritize hiring program graduates.
Deployment models include establishing dedicated AI Academy training hubs, integrating solutions into TCSG campuses, deploying mobile XR training units, and implementing train-the-trainer programs.
Employers can use XR and AI to reduce time-to-competency for critical automotive/EV roles by up to 60%.
Learners can learn essential skills 4x faster to meet urgent production timelines.
The program aims to achieve superior knowledge retention (over 75%) and higher quality output through immersive practice.
It provides zero-risk environments to master high-voltage EV battery handling protocols, robotic interactions, and emergency procedures.
Spatial AI Text-to-XR allows rapid generation or modification of training content from technical documentation in seconds.
The program significantly lowers training costs (up to 50-60% savings) and scales across Georgia via the cloud.

It cultivates a workforce skilled in AI-era manufacturing competencies including human-robot collaboration, data analysis from smart machines, predictive maintenance understanding, and agile problem-solving.
The initiative directly supports the launch and operation of major automotive investments like the Hyundai MetaPlant.
It aims to accelerate job creation, enhance Georgia’s competitiveness for future projects, and solidify its reputation as a leader in advanced mobility manufacturing.
Traditional training content creation is slow, manual, and costly, while EON Reality uses Spatial AI Text-to-XR for instant updates.
Learning speed with EON Reality is up to 4x faster compared to standard classroom pace.
High-voltage safety training shifts from theory or risky hands-on to zero-risk immersive practice.
Robotics training offers unlimited virtual programming and interaction practice versus limited access to expensive robots.
AI and data skills are integrated into training on interacting with smart systems.
Scalability is highly scalable via cloud and diverse devices compared to physical space constraints.
Cost per trainee is significantly lowered through simulation and AI efficiency.
Job-specific readiness offers higher day-one productivity versus significant on-job ramp-up.
Virtual EV Battery Assembly & Installation provides step-by-step immersive guidance on assembling battery modules and packs, including torque specifications, connection sequences, and quality checks.
High-Voltage Safety Procedures Simulation practices lockout/tagout protocols, proper use of insulated tools, and emergency disconnect procedures in risk-free environments.
Robotic Workcell Operation & Programming teaches basic programming, troubleshooting, and safe interaction for virtual robotic arms used in welding, painting, or assembly.
AR-Guided Vehicle Component Maintenance uses augmented reality overlays to guide technicians through maintenance or repair on virtual or physical EV powertrain components.
AI-Assisted Quality Control Inspection trains technicians to use simulated AI vision systems or XR tools to identify subtle defects.
Automated Production Line Troubleshooting diagnoses and resolves simulated faults on a virtual assembly line, responding to AI-driven alerts.
Supply Chain Logistics Simulation visualizes and practices the flow of materials and components within the virtual factory environment.
Ergonomics Assessment & Training uses XR to visualize and practice ergonomically correct movements for specific assembly tasks to reduce injury risk.
Connected Vehicle Systems Diagnostics simulates diagnosing issues related to EV software and connectivity features.
Final Vehicle Inspection Protocols provides immersive walkthroughs of standardized final inspection checklists using virtual vehicle models.
Module 1 covers EV Architecture & Components, Industry 4.0/5.0 in Automotive Production, Advanced Safety, Data Literacy & AI Principles, and Lean Manufacturing & Quality Systems.

Module 2 covers EV Battery Pack Assembly & Testing Simulation, Electric Motor & Powertrain Assembly Simulation, High-Voltage Systems Installation & Verification, and Vehicle Electronics & Sensor Integration.

Module 3 covers Robotic System Operation & Basic Programming, Safe Human-Robot Interaction Protocols, Interpreting Data from Smart Factory Systems (MES, IoT Sensors), AI in Quality Control & Predictive Maintenance, and Basic Troubleshooting of Automated Equipment.

Module 4 covers EV-Specific Diagnostic Tools & Procedures Simulation, Advanced Quality Inspection Techniques (Virtual Metrology, Vision Systems), Root Cause Analysis for Assembly & Component Issues, and Digital Documentation & Traceability.

Module 5 covers Team Communication in Automated Environments, Problem-Solving in Complex Production Systems, Adaptability & Continuous Learning Mindset, and Career Pathways in Advanced Automotive Manufacturing.
Dan Lejerskar, Chairman of EON Reality, stated that Georgia is strategically positioning itself at the vanguard of the EV revolution and requires a workforce trained for the factory of the future.
He noted their Spatial AI technology redefines how quickly tailored training can be created for complex processes like EV battery assembly.
He emphasized enabling learners to master critical skills up to 4x faster in immersive, safe environments to meet demands of landmark investments like the Hyundai MetaPlant.
He stated they are dedicated to partnering with Georgia to build AI-ready technicians who will drive innovation and ensure the state’s long-term leadership in advanced automotive manufacturing.
source_title: EON Reality Accelerates Georgia State's Automotive/EV Future in the US with Spatial AI & XR, Launching Initiative to Build the AI-Ready Workforce - EON Reality - AI Assisted XR-based knowledge transfer for education and industry

facts: Georgia and local governments are on track to give $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives to Hyundai Motor Group.
Hyundai Motor Group and a partner announced last month that it will invest an additional $2 billion at an electric vehicle complex it’s building in Georgia.
Projected incentives will rise by more than $290 million from the $1.8 billion deal originally announced last year.
Only $2.75 million of that represents additional cash from the state.
The rest will come from increases in tax breaks.
The deal calls for Hyundai and battery maker LG Energy Solution to invest $7.6 billion in the Georgia plant.
The deal calls for Hyundai and LG Energy Solution to hire 8,500 workers by the end of 2031.
The original job projection was 8,100 at the sprawling electric vehicle and battery complex being built in Ellabell, west of Savannah.
It’s the largest economic development deal in Georgia history.
It comes with the largest incentive package.
Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson said Hyundai is projected to have a direct payroll of $4.7 billion over the next 10 years.
The company has promised to pay workers a yearly average of $58,105, plus benefits.
Pat Wilson stated on Sept. 8 that Hyundai will deliver a state-of-the-art facility in Bryan County, provide well-paying jobs to Georgians, and invest in schools, families and communities.
Local officials released property tax projections to the AP on Sept. 12.
The Hyundai package has already been described as the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised a single automotive plant.
Good Jobs First is a group skeptical of subsidies to private companies.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the assembly plant through 2048.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the battery plant through 2049.
During that time, Hyundai is projected to pay $523 million in taxes while saving $669 million.
The state projects it will waive an additional $81.8 million in sales taxes on construction materials and machinery.
This brings Hyundai’s savings from those exemptions to more than $478 million.
The company is also projected to receive an additional $10.5 million in state income tax credits, at $5,250 per job over five years, because of the increase in jobs.
That would bring Hyundai’s state income tax savings to $223 million.
If Hyundai didn’t owe that much state corporate income tax, Georgia would instead give the company personal income taxes collected from Hyundai workers.
The state will spend an additional $2.75 million to help fund construction, machinery and equipment, boosting that total to nearly $53 million.
State and local governments spent more than $112 million to buy and prepare 2,913 acres for the plant.
State and local governments spent $175 million on water and sewer facilities.
The state will spend $210 million on road construction and improvements.
The state will spend more than $153 million to recruit and train workers.
The deal requires Hyundai to pay back a portion of the incentives if the company falls below 80% of promised investment or employment.
Hyundai plans to start EV production in 2025.
Initially, Hyundai will make 300,000 vehicles per year.
Production may expand to 500,000 a year.
Since the company announced its first U.S. plant solely dedicated to electric vehicles, suppliers have pledged to invest $2.2 billion and to hire 5,300 people.
The announcements are part of an electric vehicle and battery land rush across the United States.
Under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act, EVs must be assembled in North America to qualify for a full $7,500 EV tax credit.
A certain percentage of their battery parts and minerals must come from North America or a U.S. free trade partner to qualify for the full $7,500 EV tax credit.
Chung Eui-sun is the executive chairman of Hyundai Motor Group.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp attended the groundbreaking for the Hyundai plant on Oct. 25, 2022, in Ellabell, Ga.
source_title: Hyundai Could Get $2.1 Billion in Aid to Make EVs in Georgia - TT

facts: Hyundai Mobis Co. is selling its 500 billion won ($324.5 million) overseas bumper business to three South Korean mid-sized auto parts makers.
Hyundai Mobis is shifting its focus to robotics, software and other future growth businesses.
According to industry sources on Thursday, Hyundai Mobis recently named Seoyon E-hwa Co., Ecoplastic Corp. and Plakor Co. as preferred bidders for different parts of the business.
The assets include bumper subsidiaries and manufacturing facilities in Alabama and Georgia, the United States, Beijing, China, Slovakia, Brazil and Mexico.
Global suppliers, including France’s OPmobility SE, competed for the assets.
Hyundai Mobis chose Korean middle-market suppliers with long-standing business ties to Hyundai Motor Co. and Kia Corp.
Seoyon E-hwa will acquire the U.S. operations, considered the portfolio’s most valuable assets.
The U.S. operations include plants in Montgomery, Alabama, and West Point, Georgia.
Seoyon E-hwa will also acquire the Slovakia operation and the Beijing subsidiary.
The Beijing operation had been viewed as a less attractive asset because of weak local sales by Hyundai Motor and Kia.
Industry sources said Seoyon E-hwa agreed to acquire the China unit as part of the package after securing the U.S. operations.
Ecoplastic was selected as the preferred bidder for the Mexico operation.
Plakor was chosen for the Brazil business.
The acquisitions will allow both companies to immediately join Hyundai Motor and Kia’s global supply chain.

Suppliers located near vehicle assembly plants generally enjoy a strong competitive advantage after making the required upfront investment in production facilities as automotive bumpers are bulky and costly to transport.
Hyundai Mobis sold its domestic bumper business in Asan, South Chungcheong Province, to domestic auto parts maker BMI last year.
With the planned sale of its overseas operations, Hyundai Mobis will exit the bumper manufacturing business.

The divestment reflects Hyundai Mobis’ strategy to reduce its reliance on traditional hardware manufacturing, once a key profit driver, and accelerate its transformation into a software-defined vehicle company focused on autonomous driving and software.
Hyundai Mobis is also seeking to sell its automotive lighting business.
The company and the preferred bidders are expected to sign share purchase agreements next month after completing due diligence.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis to sell overseas bumper business for $325mn - 매일경제 영문뉴스 펄스(Pulse)

facts: Hyundai Mobis will exhibit innovative future mobility technologies at CES 2025.
Hyundai Mobis is introducing human-centric technologies including holographic windshield displays, human-centric interior lighting, and M.Brain.
Hyundai Mobis is strengthening sales efforts for mass-produced new technologies covering electrification, electronics, and lighting systems.
On December 12, 2024, Hyundai Mobis (KRX: 012330) announced its participation in CES 2025.
CES 2025 will be held at the Las Vegas Convention Center in Las Vegas, USA, from January 7 to 10.
Hyundai Mobis has participated in CES for ten consecutive years since 2016, focusing on mobility innovation and mass-produced new technologies.
The exhibition theme is 'Beyond and More,' centered on 'Human Tech' that breaks down boundaries between people and technology to maximize safety and convenience.
Hyundai Mobis designed its exhibition booth with transparent outer walls and ceiling structures to symbolize seamless connections between people, technology, and visitors.
Hyundai Mobis will give the first public unveiling of the 'Holographic Windshield Transparent Display' at CES.
The Holographic Windshield Display is co-developed with German optics company ZEISS.
Hyundai Mobis will equip the Holographic Windshield Display on Kia’s electric vehicle EV9 for its unveiling.
The display projects driving information, navigation, and infotainment data across the front windshield using a special optical film to enhance safety and spatial feel.
As a next-generation display technology not currently in mass production, Hyundai Mobis plans to begin actual mass production as early as 2027.
Hyundai Mobis will introduce 'Human Centric Interior Lighting' that changes like a chameleon based on user mood and situation.
The smart lighting system can implement 32 situational patterns including stress/motion sickness reduction, dismounting hazard prevention, door ding avoidance, and UVC sterilization.
Hyundai Mobis's M.Brain is a brainwave-based driver distraction care system that monitors inattentive states like drowsy driving in real-time.
If driver attention wanes, M.Brain issues warnings via visual LED lights, tactile vibrating seats, and auditory headrest speakers.
Hyundai Mobis will operate a 'Private Zone' at CES to strengthen face-to-face networking with global client company representatives.
The Private Zone will exhibit 16 strategic products across electrification, electronics, chassis, and lighting systems for customized sales activities.
Hyundai Mobis has strengthened customer-centric sales by participating in major global mobility events like the Paris Motor Show and Auto China.
At the ‘CEO Investor Day’ held on the 19th of last month, President Lee Gyu-suk stated the aim to expand sales from global automakers to 40% of total core auto component sales by 2033.
Hyundai Mobis aims to become a global TOP 3 automotive supplier based on its leading technological competitiveness.

Hyundai Mobis plans to invite approximately 40 international students from undergraduate, master’s, and doctoral programs at U.S. engineering schools including MIT, Stanford, and Georgia Tech to the CES site.
The company intends to showcase future technological competitiveness to these students, introduce crucial roles, and offer consultation opportunities to secure top global talent.
source_title: Newsroom | Hyundai Mobis

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Mobis plans to invest $926 million in an Electric Vehicle (EV) Power Electric system plant in Bryan County.
The new plant will eventually employ at least 1,500 people.

Gov. Kemp stated the announcement follows the recent groundbreaking of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County, which he said would unleash transformational job creation and investment in the region.
This marks the second supplier announced in just two weeks to locate in the Bryan County area.
The EV Power Electric system plant will expand Hyundai Mobis’ current footprint in Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing facility with almost 1,200 employees in West Point since 2009.
The West Point facility serves as the largest Tier 1 supplier to Kia Georgia.
The West Point plant supplies automotive manufacturers with complete cockpit modules, chassis modules, and bumper assemblies.
H.S. Oh, vice president of Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit, said the investment reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia’s auto industry.
H.S. Oh stated Hyundai Mobis will be a major production player in the EV market and expects to trigger more growth within the sector.
H.S. Oh noted the company looks forward to providing high-quality work opportunities to the growing local workforce.
Once at full production, the 1.2 million-square-foot facility will supply over 900,000 EV Power Electric systems annually to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Bryan County.
The facility will also supply over 900,000 EV Power Electric systems and 450,000 Integrated Charging Control Units annually to the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA) factory in Montgomery.
The new plant will supply components to the Kia Georgia plant as well.
Construction on the new facility is expected to begin as early as January 2023.
Production at the new facility is expected to begin in 2024.
The facility is located at the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.
Jon Seagraves, chairman of the Development Authority of Bryan County, welcomed Hyundai Mobis to the community.
Seagraves cited the site's close proximity to available housing in nearby Heartwood, a top-performing public school system, interstate access, and rail as key factors in attracting the project.
Seagraves noted the site was purchased about two years ago and highlighted nearly $1 billion of investment and the creation of over 1,500 good-paying jobs.
Seagraves thanked Bryan County, the City of Richmond Hill, and Raydient Places for their support and partnership on the project.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on the competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bryan County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that suppliers like Hyundai Mobis are transitioning to jobs of the future and becoming part of Georgia’s growing EV ecosystem.
In May, GDEcD predicted that non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project would invest an additional $1 billion into Georgia.
Commissioner Wilson noted that this prediction had already been blown past in about two weeks.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
Offsite suppliers connected to the HMGMA project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: Second supplier for Hyundai metaplant to create 1,500 jobs - Coastal Courier

facts: P2H2 is a small business developing hybrid liquid alkaline/AEM electrolysis technology that reduces stack cost by ~65% by eliminating expensive and supply chain-constrained metals like iridium, titanium, platinum, and gold.

P2H2's technology integrates directly with variable renewable energy, produces hydrogen at 250 bar, and solves durability challenges of AEM electrolyzers through a patented cell design that puts the membrane under less stress.

P2H2 has a signed commercial relationship with Shell and a pilot with Enel Green Power, targeting industrial high-pressure hydrogen/oxygen users and large renewable energy developers, markets amounting to over $100B by 2025.
P2H2's business model involves manufacturing/selling equipment or installing it on-site for a hydrogen-as-a-service model.

P2H2's team has 17 members including 5 PhDs, led by CEO Dr. Paul Matter (18 years hydrogen experience, previously led fuel cell technology development for major automotive OEMs) and COO Chris Holt (20+ years materials engineering experience).

H2scan is a small business focused on hydrogen pilot projects and sensing technologies; its Gen 5 technology provides high-accuracy, low-cost hydrogen gas safety sensing to reduce distribution transformer explosion risk.
Denso International America is a large business with expertise in manufacturing fuel cell components and enabling electronic/thermal systems, and is currently demonstrating a SOEC.

JTEKT is a top 20 automotive supplier with over 50 years of manufacturing precision sheet metal components in US plants, operating a Design & Manufacturing Technical Center in South Carolina equipped with a high speed servo press, bearing quality metrology/metallurgy labs, and a machine shop.
JTEKT is exploring bipolar plate manufacturing, capable of stamping plates ~80 cm2 active area, and has access to laser welding, coating, and sealing via an external partner.

GenHydro INC is a minority-owned early-stage renewable energy company developing a novel process for producing clean hydrogen from renewable materials at less than $3/kg using scrap aluminum in a self-sustaining chemical process.
GenHydro's system produces hydrogen, electricity, and green alumina, and is establishing partnerships with Stanley-Black & Decker and Ford Motor Company to reduce their carbon footprint.

Quantron US Inc is developing a Data-Driven Analysis tool to optimize MEA and Stacking for PEMs in Heavy Duty Vehicles using Quantron's Data-Driven Controls to address fuel cell technology shortcomings.

Quantron US Inc, a subsidiary of Germany-based Quantron AG, will bring hydrogen-powered trucks and an industry-first 360° solution platform combining vehicle, service, maintenance, insurance, leasing, financing, infrastructure, green power, and hydrogen to the US market.

University of Oklahoma researchers have extensive experience developing fundamental to system-level computational models for fuel cells using first-principles calculations, machine learning, and statistical analysis.

Molecule Works Incorporated developed a 50-micrometer thin porous ceramic membrane separator/anodic electrode assembly for alkaline water electrolysis and plans to scale up to pilot production as a US-based supplier.

Rochester Institute of Technology investigator Patricia Taboada-Serrano combines modeling with experimental work to guide electrode material synthesis, focusing on synthesizing scalable water electrolysis electrodes from earth-abundant metals using additive manufacturing and electrochemical functionalization.

Skyhaven Systems LLC has locations in Steamboat Springs, CO and Westford, MA, and holds a patented quality control inspection system for non-destructively assessing chemical activity of membrane and electrode assemblies during reel-to-reel manufacturing.

Skyhaven Systems is developing a novel water electrolyzer design with substantially lower capital cost than traditional PEM-based electrolyzers while maximizing operational efficiency, seeking partners for mathematical modeling and scaling to active areas larger than 25 cm2.

Austin Power Engineering LLC provides PEM fuel cell manufacturing cost and system analysis using a proprietary bottom-up activities-based manufacturing cost model validated with commercial clients for new manufacturing lines.

Austin Power Engineering has developed concept designs for 100 MW, 500 MW, 1 GW, and 5 GW H2 production plants and evaluates commercial alkaline, PEM, and SOEC products alongside new electrolysis technologies.

Hydrogen Technologies LLC offers 100% hydrogen fueled commercial and industrial steam boilers emitting zero greenhouse gases, providing steam from 1,500 kg/hr up to 8,000 kg/hr, with plans to scale up to 50,000 kg/hr in collaboration with National Labs.

NC Clean Energy Technology Center, an extension of North Carolina State University founded in December 1987, provides technical assistance, policy analysis, and workforce development training related to clean energy and hosts the DOE's CHP Technical Assistance Partnership in the Southeast.

TreadStone Technologies Inc is located in Princeton, NJ, holds over 60 patents for metallic plate coatings for fuel cells and electrolyzers, and is expanding coating service capabilities to address up to 1 GW by the end of 2023.

University of Missouri researcher Prof. Yangchuan Xing studies corrosion resistant catalyst supports and developed a condensed layer deposition process for low-cost liquid metal oxide nanocoatings that can incorporate iridium for high utilization with reduced PGM loading.

ET Energies, established by Electrolytic Technologies Systems LLC which has over two decades of experience installing skid-mounted modular chlorine and sodium hypochlorite generation plants globally, is developing a scalable high-pressure discharge PEM electrolyzer stack with optimized flow-fields and cooling loops.

Pajarito Powder LLC is a US-based manufacturer of electrocatalyst materials for fuel cells and electrolyzers in Albuquerque, New Mexico, producing proprietary Engineered Catalyst Support and complete catalyst solutions featuring platinum and platinum alloys, and is located in a Disadvantaged Community.
source_title: DOE: CMEI eXCHANGE: Teaming Partners
