facts: Lund International Holding Company, a portfolio company of Highlander Partners, L.P., announced on January 20, 2015, that it has acquired Bushwacker, Inc. and its subsidiaries.
The acquisition announcement was issued from Buford, GA.

Bushwacker, based in Portland, Oregon, is a major manufacturer and marketer of branded automotive aftermarket accessories including fender flares, bedrail and tailgate caps, bumper protection products, and trail armor components for Jeeps.
Bushwacker also manufactures and markets hood protection products and window visors under the Stampede brand acquired in 2012, which is based in Camanche, Iowa.
Operations will continue from existing facilities and be led by Bushwacker’s President and CEO Russ Morgan, who will join the Lund International team.
Mitch Fogle serves as President and CEO of Lund.
Jeff L. Hull serves as Chairman of Lund and Managing Partner of Highlander.
This acquisition marks Lund’s fifth since Highlander acquired the company in 2011 and represents its largest addition to date.
The transaction significantly strengthens Lund’s geographic presence and product depth in the automotive aftermarket accessories market.
Bushwacker will significantly add to Lund's Jeep offering, which was recently enhanced with the December 2014 acquisition of Rampage Products, LLC.
Regions Business Capital, as Administrative Agent, along with a syndicate of participating lenders, provided senior financing for the transaction.
AEA Mezzanine Fund III LP provided mezzanine debt in support of the transaction.
Deloitte Corporate Finance served as the exclusive financial advisor to Bushwacker in connection with this transaction.
Highlander Partners, L.P. is a Dallas-based private investment firm with over $1 billion of assets under management that uses a “buy and build” investment approach.
Lund International Holding Company is a leading designer, manufacturer, and marketer of branded automotive aftermarket accessories for passenger cars, light trucks, and heavy trucks.
Lund's product lineup includes Ventvisors®, hood shields, floor mats, fender flares, tonneau covers, storage boxes, nerf bars, and running boards.
source_title: Lund International, a Portfolio Company of Highlander Partners, Acquires Bushwacker – Highlander Partners

Brian Wiland, Mike Stevens, and Daryl Lundberg continue as sales directors covering the WD channel, automotive retailers, and mail order/sporting goods channels respectively.
Bob Cobb continues as a director of channel management responsible for specialty channels including hardware, home center, tool warehouses, and farm/fleet accounts.
Lund International announced plans to establish a corporate distribution center.
The company plans to relocate its corporate headquarters to the new location in April.
Lund International entered into a long-term lease for a 356,000-square-foot facility in Suwanee, GA.
The new facility is located approximately seven miles from the Ventshade Accessories Division, the company's largest manufacturing operation.
The company will relocate its Lund Accessories Division from Lawrenceville, GA to the new facility.
Georgia-based corporate and sales and marketing staff will move to the new facility.
The new facility is being designed to allow for future growth of the company.
source_title: Lund International Realigns Sales Structure; Plans to Open Corporate Distribution Center - AftermarketNews

facts: Lund International announced further reorganization and restructuring initiatives from Suwanee, GA.
The company first announced changes to its corporate structure in 2006.

The company consolidated its Duluth, GA corporate offices, its Lund Accessories Division in Lawrenceville, GA, and its Ventshade Accessories Division warehousing function into a new Corporate Center in Suwanee, GA.
The consolidation moves were completed in August 2006.
The restructuring includes the closure of two facilities and a new business structure designed to reduce fixed costs, increase management effectiveness, and improve customer service.
The Autotron Accessories Division in Longmont, CO is expected to close by May 31.
Approximately 75 employees work at the Longmont facility producing plastic accessories for light truck and automotive vehicles.
Production from the Longmont facility is being transferred to the company’s Ventshade Accessories Division in Lawrenceville, GA.
The company’s offices in Anoka, MN will close by the third quarter of 2007.
Approximately 35 employees are engaged in administrative support functions at the Anoka facility.
The majority of the Anoka positions will be relocated to the company’s Corporate Center in Suwanee, GA.
Lund International reorganized into three strategic business units for improved management effectiveness.

The Light Truck and Automotive Accessories business unit, marketed under the Lund, AVS, and Nifty brands and sold through warehouse distributors, specialty retail chains, and mail order distributors, is the company's largest business unit.
A separate Light Truck and Automotive Accessories business unit focused on sales through original equipment manufacturers was established to improve the company's position in that channel.
The third strategic business unit focuses on Heavy Truck Accessories sold through original equipment manufacturers and their aftermarket distribution centers.
Management is being realigned into the three business units to improve focus on satisfying customer needs.
Vice president of sales and marketing for Light Truck and Automotive Accessories Terry Cole has left the company.
Corporate vice president responsible for Information Systems and the Corporate Distribution Center Steve Treichel has left the company.
Jerry Schomaker, previously vice president and general manager of the Ventshade Accessories Division, has become vice president of Product Supply and Engineering.
Schomaker joined Lund International in 2000 as vice president and general manager of the Deflecta-Shield Division in Howe, IN.
source_title: Lund International Announces Restructuring Plans - AftermarketNews

facts: U.S. Sen. Bob Casey (D-PA) visited the Mack Trucks assembly plant in Macungie on Friday to discuss recent federal legislation.
The Inflation Reduction Act was set to pass the U.S. House of Representatives around August 2022, alongside the recently signed CHIPS and Science Act.
Both the Inflation Reduction Act and the CHIPS and Science Act include billions of dollars in government investment for technology research and development, plus sustainability incentives.

Sen. Casey stated that manufacturing incentives from these bills will help alleviate inflation, reduce future price shocks by lowering clean energy costs, and relieve supply chain bottlenecks to bring down clean vehicle costs.
Mack representatives showcased their new LR Electric trash-collection truck during the visit.
Mack representatives noted that heavy-duty electric trucks are in their infancy and require government assistance for development and deployment.
The CHIPS and Science Act allocates $53 billion toward domestic semiconductor chip manufacturing to address shortages and foreign import reliance.
Heavy-duty trucks like the LR Electric can require up to 1,500 semiconductor chips per truck to control key safety and control functions.
Scott Wolf, president of United Auto Workers Local 677, stated that supply chain issues and limited chip supplies have challenged the trucking industry and its workers.
Wolf noted that chip shortages are slowing production and delivery times and reducing worker wages due to unscheduled downtime.
Jonathan Miller, senior vice president of public affairs for Mack’s parent company Volvo, stated that federal investments are critical for supporting Mack's operations.

Miller emphasized that the availability of semiconductor chips significantly impacts manufacturing ability and called for increased domestic production of critical infrastructure components like chips, citing the CHIPS Act as helpful.
Miller expressed optimism about sustainable vehicles like the LR Electric and their impact on the region.

Miller stated Mack believes sustainable trucks are the future of the classic trucking industry and is positive about the job outlook in the Lehigh Valley, noting production at the facility since 1976.
Miller indicated the LR Electric is just the first step in Mack's zero-emission truck platform efforts.
Miller highlighted that federal tax credits, increased semiconductor production, and charging infrastructure construction will be crucial for the future of electric trucks like the LR Electric.
Sen. Casey emphasized that domestic manufacturing investment is vital for national security to ensure U.S. control over production rather than economic rivals like China.
Casey warned that failing to make these federal investments in research and development would cause the U.S. to lose to China.
Casey described the Lehigh Valley facility's work as emblematic of the future of the region, America, the economy, and national security.
Casey stated that federal investments will support both a clean energy future and good-paying union jobs.
source_title: In Macungie, Sen. Bob Casey tackles inflation, investment in good jobs | WITF

facts: Mack Trucks made the first public debut of the all-new Mack Anthem at the American Trucking Associations (ATA) Management Conference & Exhibition (MC&E), October 25-28, at the San Diego Convention Center.
The completely redesigned truck was originally launched in June 2025.
The new Anthem delivers up to 10% improvement in fuel efficiency compared with the previous model.
The truck features enhanced maneuverability and industry-leading driver comfort.
Jonathan Randall, president of Mack Trucks North America, stated that the all-new Anthem represents a complete reimagining of what a modern Class 8 truck should deliver.
The truck was designed to address fleet manager challenges including rising fuel costs, driver recruitment and retention, and maximizing productivity.
The new Anthem is available in day cab, 44-inch sleeper, and 64-inch sleeper configurations.
The truck’s enhanced maneuverability comes from a shorter bumper-to-back-of-cab (BBC) length of 113.5 inches.
The redesigned hood improves forward visibility, allowing drivers to see approximately 12% closer to the front bumper compared with previous designs.
Fernando Couceiro, Mack Trucks vice president of highway trucks, noted that fleets need a truck that excels in maneuverability without sacrificing comfort or efficiency.
The Anthem features a completely redesigned cab interior that is nine inches wider than the previous model.
New wide-base seats feature optional ventilation, heating, and massage functions.
Stand-up sleeper options provide enough headroom for drivers to stand upright.
Premium quality mattresses and blackout curtains are included to enable improved rest.
The truck features advanced safety technologies, including an optional digital mirror system that eliminates traditional blind spots.
Standard front airbags and an optional Integrated Curtain Airbag (ICA) system are included.
Additional integrated driver assistance systems have demonstrated a 43% reduction in near-miss incidents during controlled testing.
The new Anthem features Mack Connect, the company’s most advanced telematics platform for fleet operations visibility.
Over-the-air programming enables overnight software updates without service appointments.
The predictive maintenance system has been shown to reduce unplanned stops by 20%.
Integration with Mack’s GuardDog® Connect automatically transmits service data to dealers, resulting in a 37% reduction in overall downtime compared with previous-generation trucks.
The Anthem is particularly well-suited for weight-sensitive applications including bulk haul, food and beverage distribution, and less-than-truckload (LTL) hub & spoke operators.
The new Mack Anthem is available for order now, with production beginning in January 2026.
Mack Trucks has provided purpose-built transportation solutions for 125 years and is one of North America’s largest producers of heavy-duty trucks.
Mack trucks, diesel engines, and transmissions sold in North America are assembled in the United States.
Mack manufacturing locations are certified to ISO 9001 (quality), ISO 14001 (environmental management), and ISO 45001 (health and safety) standards.
Mack Trucks is part of the Volvo Group, which employs about 100,000 people and serves customers in almost 180 markets.
In 2024, the Volvo Group reported net sales of about $47 billion.
source_title: Mack Trucks Makes First Public Debut of All-New Mack Anthem® at ATA MC&E 2025

facts: Mack has debuted an all-new Class 8 tractor and an updated Granite model ahead of ConExpo-Con/Agg 2026.
The Mack Keystone is a purpose-built Class 8 tractor aimed squarely at construction, agriculture, and severe-duty on/off-road applications.

The Keystone represents an all-new model line for Mack and signals the OEM’s push to unify its highway and vocational platforms under a common architecture that blends durability, advanced safety and driver-focused design.
Alongside the Keystone, Mack introduced a fully redesigned Granite vocational truck, expanding the brand’s reach across the Class 8 work spectrum.
The new truck is named after Pennsylvania, the historic home to Mack Trucks and the site of its Lehigh Valley Operations in Macungie, where all North American Class 8 models are built.

Stephen Roy, president of Mack Trucks, stated the Mack Keystone represents a commitment to providing customers in agriculture, construction and vocational markets with a truck that’s as capable on rough terrain as it is on the highway.
Roy said the Keystone builds on more than two decades of Pinnacle production but moves to Mack’s latest common platform shared with the Pioneer, Anthem and the newly redesigned Granite.

Vocational tractor buyers will now get the same advanced safety suite, digital architecture and interior refinements found in Mack’s newest highway offerings without sacrificing heavy-duty capability.
For the first time, Mack will offer this class of vocational tractor in select Latin American markets, expanding the Keystone beyond North America.
The Keystone is engineered for operators hauling logs out of forests, livestock across rural highways, equipment to construction sites or commodities across mixed terrain.

Key features include higher ground clearance and improved approach angle, robust suspension options including Mack’s MaxRide system, axle-forward and axle-back configurations, and rear axle offerings in 6x4, 8x4 and 8x6.
Cab configurations include a day cab, 44-inch sleeper and 64-inch sleeper, each available in both axle positions.

Fernando Couceiro, vice president of highway trucks at Mack, said the Keystone is designed for terrain versatility, thriving where roads are unpaved, uneven or nonexistent before transitioning seamlessly to highway driving.
Powering the Keystone is Mack’s MP13 engine, rated up to 540 hp and 1,950 lb.-ft. of torque, paired with the mDRIVE HD automated manual transmission.
Mack says the powertrain delivers up to 6% improved fuel efficiency compared to the Pinnacle.
The Keystone comes standard with Mack’s integrated uptime tools, including a five-year subscription to Mack Connect telematics for remote diagnostics and proactive service planning.

The cab features wrap-around controls, high-visibility sightlines, ergonomic grab handles, premium seating options, a flat-bottom steering wheel, electronic braking system, available digital mirror system, and integrated ELD plate and device management solutions.

Jonathan Randall, president of Mack Trucks North America, stated customers wanted a truck that could handle the toughest jobs without compromising driver comfort or modern technology, which the Keystone delivers.

The new Granite receives an all-new galvanized steel cab that is nine inches wider at the B-pillar, an updated MP13 engine rated up to 540 hp, faster-shifting mDRIVE transmission options and Mack’s proprietary MaxRide air load distributing suspension.
The Granite offers the Mack Protect safety suite with 270-degree radar coverage, including both driver- and passenger-side radar systems, marking a first for the segment.
The Granite also introduces an electronic park brake with rollaway protection and standard E911 auto-call capability triggered by airbag deployment or rollover detection.
The Granite will be available in day cab and 44-inch sleeper configurations, with axle-forward and axle-back options.
Production of the new Granite is scheduled to begin in January 2027 at Lehigh Valley Operations.
The all-new Mack Keystone will be available for order through Mack dealers in late Q3 2026.
Granite orders will open in the second half of the year.
source_title: Mack Debuts All-New Keystone Vocational Tractor, Unveils Reimagined Granite at ConExpo 2026 | Heavy Duty Trucking

HL Mando provides eco-friendly vehicle solutions such as e-Drive and hydrogen fuel cell converters to drive the future of mobility.

facts: HL Mando America Corp. was recognized on Monday for its support of Auburn University, the Samuel Ginn College of Engineering, and the Department of Mechanical Engineering.

HL Mando committed $200,000 to support up to four graduate students in the accelerated bachelor’s and master’s degree program in mechanical engineering at Auburn University who have a special interest in automotive technologies.
Based in Seoul, South Korea, HL Mando is an electric vehicle and autonomous driving solutions company specializing in core components that are key to vehicle safety.
HL Mando is a global leader in key enabling technologies for future mobility, including redundancy concept and by-wire technology.
HL Mando is strengthening eco-friendly vehicle solutions such as e-Drive and hydrogen fuel cell converters.
HL Mando America operates as the US-based subsidiary of HL Mando with manufacturing operations located in Opelika and Hogansville, Georgia.
HL Mando America produces brake calipers, electric steering systems, and suspension systems.
HL Mando America executives, including CEO Bruce Kim and Head of Americas Region Yun Lee, attended a reception on Monday, Aug. 5.
The reception was hosted by Mario Eden, dean of engineering, where HL Mando presented the college with a ceremonial check.
Yun Lee stated, “We consider it a blessing to have an esteemed institution such as Auburn University in our vicinity.”
Lee noted that HL Mando has had great experiences partnering with the Department of Mechanical Engineering and the Office of International Programs.
Lee expressed hope that HL Mando’s contributions will help prepare tomorrow’s leaders in the automotive industry, both locally and globally.
Yun Lee and other HL Mando representatives have established a long-standing relationship with Song-Yul Choe, the William B. and Elizabeth Reed Professor in the Department of Mechanical Engineering.
Song-Yul Choe’s research interests include modeling/control of power sources, piezoelectric devices, high powered connectors, solenoid valves, and tires.
Mario Eden stated, “We are grateful for the ongoing support of our partners at HL Mando.”

Eden added, “As we strive to prepare our graduate students to enter the workforce as leaders, the support of industry leaders such as HL Mando is vital. Their generosity in supporting our students is inspirational.”
source_title: HL Mando America Corp. recognized for support of Auburn mechanical engineering

canonical_name: Creating Helpful Incentives to Produce Semiconductors (CHIPS) and Science Act
facts: The Creating Helpful Incentives to Produce Semiconductors (CHIPS) and Science Act was signed into law by President Joe Biden in August 2022.
The legislation contains $280 billion in funding designed to boost the domestic manufacture of semiconductor products.
The package contains billions of dollars allocated to improve domestic chip capabilities.
The package includes a new program to incentivize research and development (R&D) in advanced computer technology.
The legislation did not repeal IRC Section 174 R&D expenditure capitalization requirements contained in the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 (TCJA).
TCJA eliminated the ability for companies to deduct R&D expenses in the year they are incurred, effective for tax years beginning in 2022.
The failure to repeal these requirements could be a major barrier to investment in critical R&D activities.
IRC Section 174 has allowed deductions for research and experimental (R&E) expenditures as current business expenses since 1954.

Section 174 defines these expenditures as costs related to the development or improvement of a product, including direct expenses such as salaries, supplies, materials, operating costs, patent expenses, and costs of hiring outside contractors or engineering organizations.

Section 174 originally allowed an option to capitalize and amortize R&E expenditures over five years from when a company first received economic benefit, or a write-off over 10 years from when costs were incurred.
Starting in tax year 2022, domestic research costs must be capitalized and amortized over a five-year period starting with the midpoint of the year they were incurred.
Costs for research conducted outside the U.S. must be amortized over 15 years.
Companies with significant R&D investments may see a significant increase in taxable income on their 2022 returns due to these changes.
The changes couple increased taxable income with increased administrative responsibility to comply with new amortization requirements.
Companies are hoping for congressional intervention to delay or repeal the TCJA changes through year-end tax extenders legislation.
Companies must identify a method for tracking expenditures and where research takes place, as these are now subject to amortization.
Many companies may already track R&D costs to utilize Section 41 R&D tax credit, but Section 174 amortization requirements are more far-reaching.
Further guidance does not exist to clarify or further define the new requirements.
It may be worthwhile for companies to shift the location of some research projects to qualify for the shorter domestic R&D expenses amortization.
source_title: Failure to Repeal R D Expenditure Capitalization Requirements Spoils CHIPS and Science Act | UHY

facts: Study after study indicates that electric trucks are hitting cost and range targets that put them in the running to handle a rising share of U.S. freight-moving needs for routes under a few hundred miles per day.
The four-day Advanced Clean Transportation Expo was held in Long Beach, California, where companies unveiled several big orders for all-electric Class 8 trucks.

Nationwide logistics company NFI Industries announced an order for 60 Volvo VNR Electric trucks, adding to a prior commitment of 20 VNRs and 80 Freightliner eCascadias for its Ontario, California logistics hub.
Quality Custom Distribution ordered 30 additional Volvo VNRs on top of 14 ordered last year for daily resupply routes from La Puente and Fontana, California distribution centers.
Quality Custom Distribution, a subsidiary of Golden State Foods, announced a $20 million project at its La Puente site featuring 16 chargers and a solar-, battery- and generator-powered microgrid.
Penske Truck Leasing and Shell Recharge Solutions North America unveiled plans to deploy Level 2 chargers at 33 sites, with 23 located in California.
WattEV announced plans for a charging plaza in partnership with the Port of Long Beach to support its electric-truck-as-a-service business across Southern and Central California.

The WattEV depot will feature 26 360-kilowatt charging bays to serve 50 Volvo VNR electric trucks received in March, with bays also available for other fleets as part of the port’s zero-emissions fleet goal by 2030.
About 20,000 trucks are registered to operate out of the ports of Long Beach and Los Angeles.
Quality Custom Distribution operates a fleet of 700 Class 8 trucks running more than 36 million miles of deliveries across the U.S. annually.
Jeffrey Osborne, managing director and senior research analyst at Cowen, noted that ACT Expo announcements seem to be moving from testing phases toward planning multiyear rollouts.

Quality Custom Distribution has been road-testing its first Volvo VNR Electric for the past year, with senior director of operations Shane Blanchette stating testing gave them comfort to expand their electric fleet.
Today’s electric heavy trucks still lack range for routes longer than a few hundred miles.
WattEV’s model allows drivers and cargo to transfer from a depleted truck to another that has been charging, addressing range limitations.
Quality Custom Distribution’s last-stage deliveries for Starbucks, Chick-fil-A and Chipotle fit the roughly 150-mile ranges of first-generation Volvo VNR Electrics in Southern California.
QCD’s trucks only make deliveries at night, giving them all day to recharge.
Blanchette noted a 100-mile radius around QCD’s La Puente site hits a majority of customers for the future home of 30 electric trucks.
QCD’s Fontana site serves a more dispersed customer set, but enough are within range to be served by its starter electric fleet of 15 trucks.
Both QCD sites are eligible for grants and incentives from California state programs and the South Coast Air Quality Management District.
Blanchette stated that without grant opportunities, electrification would be very challenging given electric trucks cost about three times as much as diesel counterparts.
Electric trucks promise significantly lower maintenance costs, downtimes, and fueling costs compared to internal combustion engines, with the cost delta widening due to recent diesel price increases.
Supply-chain crunch has slowed QCD’s plans for getting electric trucks on the road and installing high-voltage direct-current chargers, but timing and strategy are working so far.
Grant-making agencies have been flexible with deadlines for using funds, and slower deployment hasn’t threatened QCD’s core business.
More predictable timelines for vehicle delivery and charger installation are needed to enable next steps in QCD’s electrification plans.
Blanchette warned that unchargeable electric trucks represent capital tied up with no return for businesses reliant on fleet uptime.
Mike Roeth, executive director of NACFE, noted other fleet operators face similar uncertainties regarding how fast and in what markets electrification will occur.
Last fall, NACFE partnered with RMI and truck manufacturers/fleet operators to test if various electric truck models can reliably complete shorter-haul routes.
Results indicated electric trucks can meet about half of North America’s current road freight-hauling needs, leading to significant reductions in carbon emissions and local air pollution.
NACFE has followed up with reports on medium-duty vans, panel trucks, and short-haul port terminal tractors.

NACFE’s most recent report covers heavy-duty electric tractor-trailers from Volvo, Peterbilt, BYD and Freightliner tested last fall, focusing on hub-and-spoke operations rather than long-haul electrification.
PepsiCo and Anheuser-Busch have somewhere around 90 percent of their delivery trucks operating less than 100 miles per day.

Heavy-duty trucks are responsible for about 70 percent of carbon emissions from the U.S. medium- and heavy-duty transportation sector, with about 22 percent of those emissions coming from vehicles driving less than 250 miles per day.
These trucks are an important emissions-reduction target via electrification, lower-emissions fuels like natural gas or renewable diesel, or hydrogen fuel cells.
Cowen’s Osborne noted increased interest in zero-emissions solutions relative to lower-emission solutions like propane, natural gas or biodiesel at the show.
California’s Advanced Clean Trucks rule, passed in 2020, requires manufacturers to sell an increasing proportion of zero-emissions trucks starting in 2024, aiming for 100 percent by 2045.
Several other states and D.C. have instituted versions of this rule, representing roughly half the U.S. economy, according to a report from ACT Expo host Gladstein, Neandross & Associates.
California is working on an Advanced Clean Fleet rule expected in late 2022 or early 2023, requiring large fleet owners to increase zero-emissions trucks to 100 percent between 2035 and 2042.
The South Coast Air Quality Management District has ordered about 3,000 large warehouses in its jurisdiction to meet increasingly stringent pollution-reduction rules over the coming decade.
Billions of dollars in public funds are being used to encourage clean fuels and vehicles, with California leading, according to the GNA report.
California’s Hybrid and Zero-Emission Truck and Bus Voucher Incentive Project made nearly $430 million in funding available for heavy-duty vehicles in its March annual reopening.
California’s Low-Carbon Fuel Standard program marked $4.7 billion in transactions last year, offering zero-emissions vehicle owners credits for emissions reductions.
Corporate sustainability goals from Amazon, Coca-Cola, Home Depot, Nestle, PepsiCo, Sysco, Target, Verizon and Walmart are pushing electric trucks onto roads.
GNA surveyed nearly 250 early-adopter fleet companies, finding 57 percent have tested or bought electric trucks and two-thirds plan to order them in the next year.
NACFE data shows credits, grants and incentives can reduce higher upfront costs of battery-electric heavy trucks to close to parity with diesel analogs.
A new RMI report finds half of heavy-duty trucks could be cost-effectively electrified today based on incentive structures in California and New York.
Electric trucks cut refueling costs nearly in half compared to diesel, allowing total cost of ownership savings to flow almost immediately, according to NACFE data.
Andy Brown, product marketing manager for Volvo Trucks, noted the price of gas helps TCO and batteries can be charged at a fraction of diesel fueling costs.
Electric drivetrains have far fewer moving parts and do not need motor oil to cool engines.
Volvo’s one-pedal driving feature uses regenerative braking to slow the vehicle, recharging batteries and reducing brake wear and maintenance costs.
Regenerative braking extends ranges for shorter-haul urban routes by capturing energy back into the system during frequent stops.
A March U.S. National Renewable Energy Laboratory report suggests electric trucks for routes under 250 miles could reach TCO parity with diesel before the end of the decade without subsidies.
Manufacturers are bringing better trucks and battery packs, with the 200-mile range being the current standard in 2022.
NACFE highlighted key concerns for electrification shifts: upfront cost, range, lengthy charging times, and fleet operators’ responsibility for installing and maintaining charging networks.
Fleet operations must be purpose-built to dedicate trucks to specific business parts to deliver on sustainability goals.
Electric heavy-duty trucks cannot be used for long-haul overnight deliveries and local daytime deliveries simultaneously due to lack of charging time, reducing flexibility.
Freight companies tend to lease or own vehicles for about a decade, but technology will look very different in range and capability in 10 years, according to Blanchette.
The maximum range of a Volvo VNR Electric truck grew from about 150 miles to about 230 miles in the past year.
Companies including Volvo are developing vehicle-as-a-service and infrastructure-as-a-service programs to consolidate maintenance and charging installation costs.
QCD is working with Volvo on shorter lease terms and higher residual value to plan for switching out future truck or battery versions.
QCD has a stated goal of zero emissions but hasn’t yet mapped out its strategy, emphasizing the need to take first steps.
source_title: Electric heavy-duty trucks are hitting the roads in… | Canary Media

facts: Georgia Power has received the first of three new state-of-the-art natural gas combustion turbines at Plant Yates in Coweta County, Georgia.
The Mitsubishi Power M501JAC turbines were assembled in Savannah.
The three new turbines will provide 1,300 megawatts of generation capacity when completed by 2027.
This expansion marks Georgia Power's first new natural gas project in over a decade.
The project is creating 600 construction jobs and 15 permanent positions.
The advanced turbines feature 30-minute start-up times, oil backup capability, and future hydrogen-blend compatibility.
This project is part of Georgia Power's broader strategy to meet growing energy demand, with natural gas currently providing 40% of its annual energy generation.
Plant Yates began commercial operation in 1950 as the first Georgia Power plant built to support the post-World War II economic boom.
In 2014, five of the seven coal-fired units at Plant Yates were decommissioned, with the remaining two converted to natural gas generation.
Rick Anderson, senior vice president and senior production officer for Georgia Power, stated that customers depend on reliable and affordable energy available around the clock.
The company continues to work with the Georgia PSC to enhance and expand its diverse generation mix to meet customer needs decades into the future.
The three advanced class gas turbines will provide higher output and greater efficiency than previous generations of simple cycle CT designs.
The air-cooled Mitsubishi Power M501JAC Series design eliminates the need for steam cooling, offering a shorter start-up time of approximately 30 minutes and a lower turn down rate.

As a contingency if natural gas is unavailable, the units have built-in flexibility to run on oil, with on-site oil storage capability to provide reliability and resiliency benefits to the electric system.
With minor future modifications, the M501JAC is capable of using a hydrogen mix as a fuel.
Georgia Power is partnering with Mitsubishi Power for research into advancing blending technology to pursue reduced carbon emissions.
Natural gas currently provides 40 percent of Georgia Power's annual energy generation and has long been a bedrock fuel for the company.

A recent all-source RFP certification filing with the PSC requests certification for five new combined cycle (CC) units, totaling 3,692 MW, to be strategically located across the state at Plants Bowen, McIntosh, and Wansley.
Georgia Power is the largest electric subsidiary of Southern Company (NYSE: SO).

The project completion timeline extends to 2027, with construction risks including potential schedule overruns, labor cost changes, equipment delays, and regulatory actions noted in forward-looking statements.

source_title: Georgia Power Receives First Gas Turbine for 1,300MW Plant Yates Project | SO Stock News

facts: Mercedes-Benz USA LLC confirmed the company will relocate its headquarters from Montvale, New Jersey, to Atlanta.
The relocation is designed to better serve its growing customer base and strengthen the company's position for long-term sustainable growth.
The company will begin moving employees to a temporary location while constructing a new headquarters expected to be completed in 2017.
Some operations will remain in Montvale and nearby Robbinsville, New Jersey.
MBUSA President and CEO Stephen Cannon stated New Jersey has been a wonderful home for their U.S. operations for the first 50 years and still is today.
Cannon noted that sustained profitable growth and efficiencies required locating the headquarters elsewhere, ultimately choosing Atlanta.
Georgia reportedly offered Mercedes-Benz USA LLC an incentive package worth an estimated $50 million to lure it to Atlanta from New Jersey.
The North American division of the luxury German automaker initially refused comment early Tuesday regarding the move.
WSB-TV in Atlanta confirmed the deal through sources and reported Mercedes would make an announcement sometime Tuesday.
The deal is expected to bring 1,000 jobs to Georgia.
The company's move comes at the expense of Bergen County, New Jersey, which has been home to Mercedes-Benz USA headquarters since 1972.
Atlanta offers benefits including easier access to already used ports in Georgia and direct commercial flights into Alabama where Mercedes-Benz operates a large assembly plant.
Mercedes-Benz USA officials were scheduled to meet with employees Tuesday regarding the relocation.
Some employees told the Bergen Record anonymously they expected to hear the announcement during the session.
State Sen. Gerald Cardinale said New Jersey offered a “substantial” tax incentive package to keep the company in the state, but declined to offer details.
Cardinale, a Republican, stated his state is having trouble competing to keep and attract businesses because of high property and income taxes.
Mercedes is being offered an incentive package worth as much as $50 million to relocate to Atlanta, according to the newspaper.
New Jersey and Georgia state officials declined comment on the deal Tuesday.

Foreign automakers including Toyota, Mercedes-Benz, Nissan, Honda, Hyundai, and BMW have been attracted to the South by low labor costs, large incentive packages, and a lack of strong union culture over the past 25 years.

Southern Republicans like Sens. Mitch McConnell of Kentucky, Richard Shelby of Alabama, and Bob Corker of Tennessee opposed the 2009 auto industry bailout and called for lowering wages at U.S. automakers to make them more competitive with nonunion workers at Southern factories run by foreign automakers.
Five years after the multibillion-dollar bailout of General Motors and Chrysler, auto manufacturing has become a less lucrative career choice.
Even in union shops, employees doing the same job can be paid differently due to a two-tiered system based on seniority.
Some auto factory workers make as little as $15 an hour and have had to take second jobs.
Automakers say the wage system is necessary to create and keep jobs.

Last month, the National Labor Relations Board upheld a ruling that Mercedes-Benz violated labor laws by stopping United Auto Workers supporters at its Tuscaloosa, Alabama, plant from passing out literature to recruit union membership.
source_title: Why Mercedes-Benz Is Ditching New Jersey, Moving South | IBTimes

facts: German auto maker Mercedes-Benz is deepening its investment in metro Atlanta at a time of turmoil in the global automotive sector.
Mercedes-Benz moved its U.S. headquarters to a new building known as 1MB in the Sandy Springs suburb in 2018.
The 1MB outpost originally promised 1,000 workers and now employs about 800.
On May 22, Mercedes-Benz announced up to 500 more jobs would join them by next August.
The company is making the Sandy Springs office its mothership for the United States and the whole North American market.
The sleek office includes an EV charging hub.
New Atlanta jobs will be brought to Atlanta in large part from Farmington Hills, Mich., where corporate and financial services are currently located.
Those teams will join sales and marketing at 1MB.
Mercedes-Benz announced it would locate a new research and development hub “near Sandy Springs.”
The R&D hub will bring technical talent to the metro area from around its U.S. system.
The move is designed to situate the company better within Atlanta’s growing tech talent pool, including new engineering grads coming out of universities like Georgia Tech.
The move also aims to deepen collaborations with emerging startups.

Jason Hoff, who took up the newly created Mercedes-Benz North America CEO position on May 1, said: “Bringing our teams closer together will enable us to be more agile, increase speed to market and ensure the best customer experience.”
Details on the R&D hub were scant, other than saying it would be a “multimillion-dollar investment.”
The R&D facility will not be co-located at the Sandy Springs office.

A spokesperson told Global Atlanta: “We will share additional details on the R&D facility and functions as soon as we can. We look forward to our future R&D facility and bringing more technical roles to the state.”
At its original opening seven years ago, Mercedes-Benz unveiled a mobility incubator it was planning for Buckhead.
The Lab1886 office was tasked with dreaming up big ideas for portfolio companies within the Daimler family.
Lab1886 was staffed with leaders from China, Germany and California.
When Lab1886 opened, it parked a Volocopter, an early drone taxi that later went bankrupt, in the courtyard of the Terminus building where it had rented a WeWork space.
Then-Gov. Nathan Deal and Atlanta Mayor Keisha Lance Bottoms attended the Lab1886 opening.
In December 2020, Daimler spun off 1886Ventures from Lab1886, retaining 10 percent of the fund.
The Atlanta office closed less than two years after its star-studded opening featuring marketing expert and technology evangelist Guy Kawasaki and other luminaries.
Mercedes-Benz has continued to grow in the U.S., where the German luxury brand points out that it has been present for more than 120 years.
In April, amid President Trump’s automotive tariffs, Mercedes-Benz announced it would make its GLC midsize SUV at the Tuscaloosa, Ala., factory.
The majority of production at the Tuscaloosa factory is exported.
Mercedes-Benz accounts for 8,000 direct manufacturing jobs in the United States and 11,100 overall.
Mercedes-Benz is heavily concentrated in the Southeast U.S.
Beyond the Alabama factory, Mercedes-Benz makes its Sprinter cargo vans in South Carolina.
Mercedes-Benz sold 374,100 passenger cars and vans in the U.S. in 2024.

facts: Mercedes-Benz USA (MBUSA) held the official grand opening of its new headquarters in Sandy Springs, 14 miles outside of the heart of Atlanta.

The new headquarters building is 200,000 square feet and sits on a 73-acre site, described as a 12-acre campus near Georgia 400 at the intersection of Mercedes-Benz Drive (formerly Barfield Road) and Abernathy Road.
The facility has a capacity for more than 1,000 employees.
MBUSA president and CEO Dietmar Exler stated that the new Atlanta headquarters marks a highpoint for Mercedes-Benz in the U.S. market as the leading luxury brand for the past two years.
The building was designed from the inside out with a 1:1 ratio of collaborative seating to individual workstations to encourage interaction and creativity.
The headquarters is divided into North and South Work Bars joined by an indoor Atrium, with corridors called Bridges on the second and third floors.

On-site amenities include a conference center, a coffee bar with a barista, a restaurant-quality café emphasizing locally sourced ingredients, a 5,000 square foot fitness center with trainers, an on-site child care facility with four classrooms and an outdoor playground, and a furnished roof deck for outdoor collaboration.
The building incorporates environmentally-friendly features targeting LEED Silver Certification, including water reduction using drip irrigation and energy-efficient mechanical systems.
MBUSA is partnering with Second Helpings Atlanta Inc. to eliminate food waste by rescuing surplus food from Metro Atlanta organizations.

To reduce traffic impact and align with sustainability commitments, MBUSA implemented alternative transportation initiatives including flex scheduling, a dedicated MARTA shuttle for employees, bike racks, and electric vehicle charging stations.
Construction of the new headquarters required 425,000 man hours and more than 1,000 workers.
The building consists of 14,000 cubic yards of concrete, 1,500 tons of steel, and 1.6 acres of glass.
More than 780 trees were planted around the site, with a nature trail encircling the building to provide an outdoor campus feel.
Georgia Governor Nathan Deal praised Mercedes-Benz for choosing Georgia as its headquarters and acknowledged the company's community service projects.
During the ceremony, Daimler vice president of sales Axel Harries announced plans to establish a global innovation hub called Lab1886 in Atlanta.
Lab1886 will operate as an incubator-type entity combining corporate expertise with startup spirit and is scheduled to open in the summer of 2018.
The Atlanta Lab1886 location will be the fourth globally, joining existing locations in Beijing, Berlin, and Stuttgart.

Axel Harries cited Atlanta's excellent colleges and universities offering highly skilled graduates, along with a growing number of innovative technology and digital companies, as reasons for the selection.
Atlanta Mayor Keisha Lance Bottoms expressed excitement about the headquarters and the Lab1886 announcement, highlighting partnerships between corporations, nonprofits, and government in the city.
MBUSA is involved with over 50 organizations to address local community needs, including efforts in Atlanta's Westside area surrounding the Mercedes-Benz Stadium.
Sandy Springs Mayor Rusty Paul noted that Mercedes-Benz has been a great partner to the community already.

MBUSA is also responsible for Mercedes-Benz Vans and smart products in the U.S. market.

facts: Michelin Retread Technologies (MRT) is increasing its production capacity by adding a new press at its Covington GA retreading operation.
The new press is capable of producing one million new treads per year.
The new MRT press has been designed to produce new high-technology treads.

The press takes advantage of Michelin's advanced co-extrusion technique, a process where two different rubber compounds are combined to ensure longer life and cooler casing temperatures, maximizing the life of the casing.
Michelin is currently the only manufacturer in the world offering co-extruded treads to its retread customers.
The new press will allow the company to expand its offering of wider treads for Michelin's X One wide-single tire.
Expansion of the Covington tread pressing plant is the first major expansion since the facility opened in 1999.

facts: Murata Electronics launched tight frequency tolerance ceramic resonators for the automotive market.
The low-cost CSTCR, CSTCE, and CSTCV series are the only ceramic resonators that meet the total frequency tolerance of +/- 0.3%.
Tight tolerance versions of the existing CSTCR and CSTCV will be available by the end of 2001.
The newly introduced CSTCE ceramic resonator is one of the world's smallest resonators at 1.3 x 3.2mm.
The CSTCE covers a frequency range of 8.00MHz to 12.5MHz.
The CSTCR resonator, introduced late last year, is the world's smallest resonator at 4.5 x 2.0mm for the frequency range of 4.00MHz to 7.99MHz.
Murata shipped over 28 million continuous non-defective ceramic resonators to Visteon Corporation, a major supplier of integrated automotive systems.
Keith Seamands, STA-Quality Engineer for Visteon, commended Murata's quality in a letter, stating that the excellent quality indicates everyone at all levels is doing an excellent job.

Tom Cho, Piezoelectric Product Manager for Murata Electronics North America, stated that the intelligent design and unrivaled quality of their ceramic resonators for CAN-BUS operations embodies their dedication to exceed customer expectations.
Visteon's praise of Murata's flawless resonators confirms the company's commitment to exceeding customer expectations.
Murata's ceramic resonators provide cost-cutting benefits by serving as smaller, more robust replacements for expensive quartz crystals.
Ceramic resonators are commonly found in automotive applications such as airbags, power train, and Anti-lock Brake System (ABS) modules.
The improved total frequency tolerance capability was designed to fulfill the needs of the expanding automotive electronics business.
While cell phone and wireless consumer product sales are slowing, the automotive industry is continually upgrading car features that rely heavily on high-tech electronics.
Murata is already the market leader in ceramic resonators for high reliability applications such as automotive.
The introduction of tight tolerance resonators allows Murata to sustain its industry-leading position as the market moves toward full adaptation of CAN-BUS in most vehicles.
Previously, only higher cost quartz crystal resonators were considered for CAN-BUS due to frequency tolerance issues.
Murata's tight tolerance resonators free design engineers from using higher cost components while achieving desired performance and reliability targets.
Tight tolerance versions of the CSTCR (4.00 to 7.99MHz), CSTCE (8.00 to 12.50 MHz), and CSTCV (14.70 to 70.00 MHz) are currently available from Murata as samples.
Murata Electronics North America, Inc. is one of 47 wholly-owned subsidiaries of the worldwide organization of Murata Manufacturing Co., Ltd. of Japan (NIKKEI:6981).
U.S operations for Murata are headquartered in Smyrna, Georgia.
Murata has a manufacturing facility in State College, Pennsylvania.
Murata is a leading patent holder and global manufacturer of ceramic electronic components for the telecommunications, computer/peripheral, automotive, communications, and EDP markets.

facts: Murata Electronics North America Inc. is one of 47 wholly owned subsidiaries of Murata Manufacturing Co. Ltd. of Kyoto, Japan.
Murata Manufacturing Co. Ltd. reported $3 billion in worldwide revenue for 1998, with $425 million derived from its North American operations.
Murata Electronics estimates its 1999 sales will increase to about $515 million.
The company controls approximately 30% of the North American market for ceramic chip capacitors.
Ceramic capacitors accounted for 49% of Murata Electronics' 1998 sales, with microwave products accounting for a distant second at 18%.
Murata Electronics pursues a vertical integration strategy, acquiring and processing raw materials, designing manufacturing equipment and software, and manufacturing all its products.
The company's last notable acquisition was in 1981.

Murata developed the Dry Phantom test equipment, which mimics electromagnetic-wave reflection, absorption, and dissipation characteristics of the human head, upper torso, and arm for testing handheld wireless devices like cell phones.
The Dry Phantom design uses dielectric ceramic powder, polymer, and carbon powders, developed in conjunction with NTT of Japan.
Murata is developing other products for the wireless-telecom industry, including the monolithic, surface-mountable LDA82 Chip Multilayer Antenna based on low-temperature co-fired ceramics technology.
Murata Electronics announced a laser-tuned High Frequency Active Filter with high-speed op amps and advanced thick-film technology for cable-modem routers and HDTV set-top boxes.

The company plans to evolve from a materials company and commodity supplier to an application-solutions and high-end design partner, customizing chip antennas to specific customer design features rather than producing standard products.
Price erosion has been significant in ceramic caps, prompting Murata's shift to higher-margin, application-specific parts.
Capacitor prices have stabilized due to short supply, with lead times at Murata Electronics increasing from eight to 10 weeks to an average of 14 to 18 weeks.
Products like SAW filters, oscillators, and capacitors are heavily booked with none in oversupply.
Murata's plant in State College, Pennsylvania produces about 1.1 billion chip capacitors per month.
The company has seen approximately a 50% increase in capital investment this year.
Murata Electronics is phasing out its reliance on expensive palladium, with a complete transition to nickel expected by the end of next year.
North American sales to contract electronics manufacturers (CEMs) have risen from 8% to 20% of total sales over the past three years.
Murata is setting up a full-time business unit to handle major CEM contractors such as Flextronics, Jabil, SCI, and Solectron.
Another 20% of Murata Electronics' North American sales goes through distributors including Arrow, Avnet, Future, Pioneer-Standard, TTI, Capsco, and Tonar.
Major OEMs are increasingly partnering with big distributors, forcing suppliers to win business through third-party distributors who may carry competing lines.
source_title: At Murata, the 'cap guys' target higher-margin markets - EE Times

facts: Murata Manufacturing Co. Ltd. was founded in 1940 and follows a strategy of organic growth using research and development to build capacitors, filters, resistors, microwave parts, and other components.
The company has become a multibillion-dollar global supplier.
A prolonged industry downturn caused Murata's revenue and earnings to tumble, forcing workforce trimming and lower capital investment.
Increasing OEM pressure forced Murata to continue reducing commodity part prices.
The Kyoto, Japan-based company is leveraging core ceramic expertise to develop higher-margin products such as Bluetooth modules and higher-performance capacitors for emerging applications.
The company is increasing its presence in China.
John Denslinger serves as senior vice president of Murata Electronics North America Inc., located in Smyrna, Ga.
Murata Electronics North America Inc. is one of the company's 47 wholly owned subsidiaries across Asia, Europe, and North and South America.
Murata remains the world's leading manufacturer of multilayer ceramic capacitors (MLCCs), accounting for more than 20% of the global market by revenue.
The company considers itself a market leader in SAW filters with a 20% share and dielectric filters with a 40% share.
Murata remains vertically integrated, forming its own ceramic materials and building its own machines instead of outsourcing.
The company consistently reinvests 5% to 8% of its revenue in research and development.
Murata's last notable acquisition occurred in 1981.
For the fiscal year ended March 31, 2002, Murata's revenue was $2.97 billion, down 32.4% from the previous year, with net income of $394 million, down 66%.
The company projects revenue and earnings for fiscal 2002 to be roughly flat with fiscal 2001.
Capital investment for fiscal 2002 is projected at $200 million, down from $260 million in fiscal 2001.
Denslinger expects Murata's revenue to slowly improve over the next year but cites price erosion in commodity parts as the main hurdle to recovery.
A lot of OEMs now negotiate price contracts on a quarterly rather than yearly basis.
Capacitors have experienced 12 consecutive quarters of price reductions.
Denslinger expects prices to erode 10% in the first half of 2003.
A weak end market for communications products, Murata's largest market segment, saw its percentage of revenue slide to 37% in fiscal 2001 from 41% in fiscal 2000.

Murata remains committed to the communications sector long-term and expects to supply EMC filters, thermistors, and DC/DC converters to OEMs building broadband, home networking, and digital access equipment.
Capacitors account for 40% of Murata's revenue.
The company is expanding its capacitor offerings to include low-equivalent-series resistance and capacitance values exceeding 1 microfarad.
Murata hopes to target the RF market and applications in which tantalum capacitors now dominate.
The company is leveraging low-temperature co-fired ceramic technology to manufacture integrated Bluetooth modules.
Murata announced a newest Bluetooth module measuring 9.3 x 7.8 x 1.8mm, which is 25% smaller than the previous version introduced in August.
The Bluetooth module for wireless handset and PDA applications sells for $15 to $20, which is two to three times more than component-based solutions from suppliers like Texas Instruments Inc.
Murata's group product manager Scott Klettke stated the higher price is justified because the module speeds time-to-market for designers of Bluetooth products.
Revenue percentage from China was 14% in fiscal 2001 and is expected to increase as more OEM and EMS customers locate there.
Last month, the company began mass production of capacitors, isolators, and thermistors at a plant in Wuxi.
Murata's other China plants are located in Beijing, Suzhou, and Shenzhen.
source_title: Murata banks on ceramic technology - EE Times

facts: Murata became one of the earliest Japanese investors in Georgia in 1973, setting up a plant to make capacitors in car radios.
The Kyoto-based company has global annual revenues of approximately $17 trillion yen.
Murata holds about 40 percent of the world’s market share for capacitors, small ceramic parts produced by the trillions each year to store and release power within electronic devices.
More than 1,000 of Murata's parts go into a typical smartphone.
Potentially 30,000+ of Murata's parts are slated for a fully autonomous electric vehicle.
Murata diversified its sourcing around the world, including in Southeast Asia, to be closer to end customers.
Murata deepened research spending to ensure it could serve advanced industries, like increasingly computerized cars, with innovative solutions.
Murata Electronics North America has a new headquarters in Atlanta, Ga.
Murata Electronics North America maintains a distribution center in Rockmart, Ga.
The company is dealing with increasing volatility in high-value industries where the smallest parts can make the biggest impact.

A webinar hosted by GSU-CIBER and Global Atlanta on Thursday, April 24, 2025, from 11:00 AM to 12:00 PM EST will cover Murata's diversification of manufacturing locations in partnership with customers and its impact on U.S. operations.

The webinar will cover Murata’s approach to continuous improvement in research and development, its moves up the value chain over the last 50 years, and efforts to showcase innovation through robots and consumer-facing items.

The webinar will cover the company's approach to trade uncertainty and supply chain volatility and how it balances Japanese sensibilities, including long-term strategy, with a nimble approach in global markets.
Speaker David Kirk serves as VP of Murata Manufacturing Co Ltd. and President & CEO of Murata Electronics North America.
David Kirk is responsible for Sales/Marketing, Operations, Corporate Technology, and Innovation across the Americas.
The Americas region is critical for design-in activity that results in global sales within the electronics industry.
Murata has made multiple acquisitions and integrations globally, including Peregrine Semiconductor (pSemi), Vios Medical, Eta Wireless, Resonant, and RF Monolithics (RFM).
David Kirk started his career as a Product Engineer with Murata and moved into the Director of Marketing role.
He previously served as President and CEO of RFM, which was a public company from 1999 to 2011.
Upon returning to Murata in 2012, Kirk was instrumental in RFM’s acquisition by the parent company in Japan.
Kirk is the first non-Japanese person to lead Murata in the Americas and become an officer of the parent company, Murata Manufacturing Company headquartered in Kyoto, Japan.

facts: Novelis Inc. plans to invest $365 million to build a highly advanced recycling center for automotive in North America, adjacent to its existing automotive finishing plant in Guthrie, Kentucky.
The new facility is expected to support the expansion of Novelis’s automotive closed-loop-recycling programs and reduce carbon emissions by more than one million tons each year.
Steve Fisher, president and CEO of Novelis, Inc., stated that Novelis aims to be the world’s leading provider of low-carbon, sustainable aluminum solutions advancing a circular economy.
Through this investment, Novelis will increase recycled content in its products, reduce CO2 emissions, and move closer to carbon neutrality.
The recycling center will have an annual casting capacity of 240,000 tons of sheet ingot.
The facility will be equipped with industry-leading processes including advanced shredding and sorting technology.
It will feature energy-efficient innovations to support Novelis’s sustainability goal to reduce energy intensity by 10% by 2026.
Advanced technologies at the plant will enable growth of closed-loop-recycling programs for more automotive customers in North America.
Through closed-loop recycling, Novelis takes back aluminum remaining after automotive parts are stamped from sheet and reprocesses it into the same product for new vehicle production.
The center will also have the capability to process aluminum from vehicles at the end of their lifecycle.
When fully operational, the expansion will add approximately 140 new jobs in Guthrie.
Novelis has a 37-year history of creating jobs in Kentucky and currently employs 1,600 people in the state.
Novelis recently held the grand opening ceremony of its new automotive finishing plant in Guthrie, which currently employs 150 people and will grow to 190 people over the next two years.
The company also operates an aluminum beverage can recycling plant in Berea and the Logan Aluminum joint venture in Russellville, both in Kentucky.

Tom Boney, executive vice president and president of Novelis North America, stated that the investment will help achieve sustainability goals and support the carbon-reduction targets of automotive customers.
Tom Boney noted that the Commonwealth of Kentucky has a strong, highly skilled workforce to meet the growing need for high-strength, low-carbon aluminum.
Groundbreaking on the new recycling facility is scheduled for early 2022, with commissioning expected in 2024.
source_title: Novelis Invests in New Recycling Center for Automotive - Light Metal Age Magazine

facts: Novelis filed a lawsuit in Georgia federal court against Denham-Blythe Co. Inc. alleging mismanagement of a recycling facility intended to serve as a sister facility to an automotive aluminum plant in Guthrie, Kentucky.
Denham-Blythe was reportedly obliged to indemnify Novelis from losses caused by construction failures but allegedly failed to pay damages even after a third-party engineer found it at fault.

Novelis stated that Denham-Blythe repeatedly breached contracts, failed to indemnify Novelis for claims, failed to cure breaches, and persistently delayed final completion, denying Novelis full use of its plant as planned.

Novelis made costly repairs of Denham-Blythe's defective work and breaches, and the delays caused improper liens to be filed against Novelis's project and tens of millions in ongoing and increasing damages.
Denham-Blythe did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Friday.

Novelis set out several years ago to construct a sprawling recycling facility that would furnish aluminum for its auto finishing plant, with an intended investment of $365 million per the company's website.
The facility was expected to produce 240,000 tons of aluminum sheet metal per year at peak capacity.
Denham-Blythe was tasked with the overall design and engineering of the project and held final responsibility for its outcome, though it could subcontract portions.
During construction, Denham-Blythe allegedly botched key parts, including improperly installing the electrical system of a shredder that turns aluminum scrap into fines.
When attempting to repair the mistake, Denham-Blythe sent metal shavings into the shredder's transformer, causing a serious failure of the system in July 2024.
This failure occurred when the facility was expecting to start ramping up production, forcing Novelis to buy scrap materials from third parties at a premium.
Later that year, an overhead crane within the factory allegedly broke and failed, causing a serious safety event.
An independent review commissioned after the incident found that Denham-Blythe's installation failed to meet industry standards for construction.

Despite its own investigation findings and notice from Novelis, Denham-Blythe failed to indemnify or compensate Novelis for repairs associated with the crane rail failure or losses due to inability to produce ingot.
Novelis alleged two counts of breach of contract against Denham-Blythe, noting screwups were not limited to those two incidents but included a range of other delays and faulty installations.
Novelis is seeking recovery of $15 million in damages it says it sustained, along with attorney fees and other litigation costs.
Novelis is represented by Michael J. Breslin, Daniel B. Swaja, Daniel K. Johnson, and Kurtis G. Anderson of Kilpatrick Townsend & Stockton LLP.
The case is Novelis Corporation v. Denham-Blythe Company Inc., case number 1:25-cv-01748, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia.
source_title: Novelis Seeks $15M For Recycle Facility Construction Delays - Law360

facts: Novelis Inc. completed a cash tender offer for its 3.250% Senior Notes due in November 2026.
Approximately $738 million of the Senior Notes were validly tendered at the close of the offer.
The tendered amount represents 98.4% of the $750 million total principal amount currently outstanding.
Successful participants will receive a tender offer consideration of $997.50 for every $1,000 principal value of the notes.
Holders will also receive accrued interest from the latest semi-annual payment leading up to the settlement date.
Novelis plans to proceed with the redemption of the notes not purchased during the tender offer in accordance with governing indenture rules.
BNP Paribas Securities Corp. serves as the Dealer Manager for this tender offer.

During the latest fiscal year, Novelis achieved net sales of $17.1 billion.

facts: Novelis Inc., a leading sustainable aluminum solutions provider and world leader in aluminum rolling and recycling, announced plans to partner with Southern Company on decarbonization efforts.
The partnership's initial focus is on Novelis' new Bay Minette, Alabama, plant, which aims to be carbon neutral for scope 1 and 2 greenhouse gas emissions.
The recycling and rolling plant at the Bay Minette site is currently under construction to help meet growing demand for sustainable beverage packaging.
The Bay Minette facility will support the automotive industry in North America, including increased electric vehicle production.
Novelis will work with Southern Company subsidiary Alabama Power to provide renewable energy to the Bay Minette project to reduce its scope 2 greenhouse gas emissions.
By participating in Alabama Power's Renewable Subscription Program, Novelis will directly support the creation of two new 80-megawatt solar power generation plants in Alabama.
These solar plants will cover over half of the Bay Minette facility's renewable energy needs while avoiding 192 kilotons of CO2 emissions per year.

Novelis and Southern Company will collaborate to advance new technologies to reduce Novelis' Scope 1 carbon emissions, including hydrogen fuels, carbon capture, energy storage, and electrification of thermal processes.
Through their corporate venture and R&D teams, Novelis and Southern Company are exploring projects for the Bay Minette facility as well as other Novelis sites in North America.
Suzanne Lindsay-Walker, Novelis' Vice President of Sustainability, stated that strong partnerships are critical to advancing commitments to the environment, economy, and communities.
Novelis seeks to meet goals to reduce carbon emissions by 30% by 2026 and be carbon neutral by 2050 or sooner.

Chris Cummiskey, Executive Vice President and Chief Commercial and Customer Solutions Officer for Southern Company Services, emphasized that unlocking partnerships is key to advancing renewable power availability and reaching respective decarbonization goals.
Novelis Inc. is a global leader in the production of innovative aluminum products and solutions and the world's largest recycler of aluminum.

Novelis aims to be the leading provider of low-carbon, sustainable aluminum solutions and achieve a fully circular economy by partnering with suppliers and customers in aerospace, automotive, beverage can, and specialties industries across North America, Europe, Asia, and South America.
Novelis had net sales of $18.5 billion in fiscal year 2023.

Novelis is a subsidiary of Hindalco Industries Limited, an industry leader in aluminum and copper, and the metals flagship company of the Aditya Birla Group, a multinational conglomerate based in Mumbai.
Southern Company (NYSE: SO) is a leading energy provider serving 9 million residential and commercial customers across the Southeast and beyond through its family of companies.

Southern Company has electric operating companies in three states, natural gas distribution companies in four states, a competitive generation company, a leading distributed energy distribution company with national capabilities, a fiber optics network, and telecommunications services.
Southern Company is advancing its goal of net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.
source_title: Novelis partners with Southern Company in pursuit of decarbonization goals :: Novelis Inc.

facts: Novelis captured strong demand for aluminum flat rolled products across end markets, including electric vehicles.

Novelis EBITDA per ton at levels above $500 is very well sustainable.
Industry hot mill capacity for aluminum is becoming extremely tight outside of China.
Sustainability trends are driving continued growth in automotive and packaging demand.
Novelis has identified approximately $1.5 billion in capex projects over the next five years, including debottlenecking, capacity expansion, and recycling initiatives.
Novelis plans to spend an average of about $300 million per annum on maintenance over the next five years.

facts: Hindalco Industries Ltd reported an almost 16% drop in net profit attributable to owners to ₹13,391 crore from ₹16,001 crore in FY25 for FY26.
The company's FY26 earnings missed Street expectations despite higher metal prices.
Profit fell well short of the ₹16,164 crore estimate in a Bloomberg poll of 28 analysts.
In the fourth quarter, net profit attributable to owners dropped 51% to ₹2,597 crore, down from ₹5,283 crore a year ago.
The decline stemmed mainly from ₹6,963 crore in one-off expenses linked to the Oswego plant disruption in New York last year.
Operations of Novelis, a key supplier of rolled aluminium to can makers and auto companies, were affected by the fire.
Novelis accounted for 59% of Hindalco’s revenue in FY26.
Hindalco reported a 15% jump in consolidated revenue from operations of ₹2,74,944 crore on the back of higher aluminium sales and moderate copper sales in the domestic market.
Managing director Satish Pai stated that the outage was largely timing-related, with current year headwinds expected to substantially recover in the next fiscal year.
The company reported a record India business Ebitda of ₹22,671 crore in FY26, up 6% from FY25.
Novelis saw Ebitda decline by 5% to ₹14,546 crore in FY26 as the fire incident shrank volumes.
In the June quarter, Hindalco expects near-term input cost escalations of about 5% sequentially due to the West Asia war.
Analysts expect higher metal prices to cushion the impact of cost escalations.
Aditya Welekar, metals and mining analyst at Axis Securities, noted that earnings were largely impacted by the one-time fire incident at Novelis’ Oswego plant.
Management has guided for a $1.7 billion restructuring expense, which will weigh on free cash flow, although 70–80% is expected to be recovered through insurance.
The Oswego plant is set to restart, putting the company on a gradual path to recovery.
In India, aluminium cost pressures are expected to rise in Q1 due to higher furnace oil and coal tar pitch prices, even as realizations remain firm.
The near-term outlook remains steady as strong aluminium and copper prices should offset cost pressures while operations gradually stabilize.
Hindalco plans a ₹12,000 crore capex in India for FY27.
Novelis capex will be $2.3-2.4 billion, mostly at Bay Minette.
Bay Minette is the site of a new $5.5 billion low-carbon aluminum recycling and rolling plant being built by Novelis Inc, expected to be completed this year.
Satish Pai indicated that going into FY28, Novelis capex will sharply drop once Bay Minette is commissioned, shifting to maintenance capex.
India capex will be much higher than ₹12,000 crore in the future due to ramp-ups in the copper smelter and other projects.
In FY26, Novelis shipments declined to 3,557 kt from 3,757 kt in FY25, reflecting the impact of the Oswego plant disruption.
The copper business reported a marginal dip of 1% in volumes, with metal sales at 487 kt versus 491 kt a year ago.
Aluminium upstream operations showed a modest improvement, with shipments rising to 1,350 kt from 1,327 kt in FY25.
Downstream aluminium shipments were up 11% to 446 kt, supported by steady operational performance and capacity utilization.
