facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Sewon America, a subsidiary of Korean-based Sewon Precision Industry Co., will create 740 new jobs in Rincon.
Sewon America will invest more than $300 million in a new Effingham County manufacturing facility.
This project is the largest known private investment in the City of Rincon to-date.

The announcement fulfills the commitment to create jobs and investment in all four Savannah Harbor Interstate-16 Joint Development Authority (JDA) counties as a result of Hyundai’s Metaplant location in Bryan County.
Suppliers for the Metaplant have now committed over $1.8 billion in investment, surpassing initial estimates by at least $800 million.
Governor Brian Kemp's first international trip as governor was to Korea, where he congratulated Chairman Kim on the company’s expansion and discussed future collaboration.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns, who represents part of Effingham County, played a role in securing the project and engaging Sewon America executives.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones commended the announcement, noting it builds on Georgia’s commitment to remain the No. 1 state in which to do business while attracting a robust workforce.
Sewon America operates one of the largest automobile body parts manufacturing plants in North America.
The company opened a manufacturing facility in LaGrange in 2008, which has since doubled in size.
Following the completion of the Rincon facility, Sewon is expected to support 1,600 jobs in Georgia.
Vice Chairman Kim stated that Sewon America was founded 15 years ago in west Georgia and expressed excitement about building another facility in east Georgia as one family in two locations.
Sewon’s new facility will be located at the Grande View industrial park in Rincon.
The new manufacturing location will produce electric vehicle (EV) body parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in 2025.
Rincon Mayor Ken Lee welcomed Sewon America, calling it the largest employment and private investment project in the city’s history.
Chairman of the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority (IDA) Troy Smith stated that the IDA selected Grande View industrial park as the best location for Sewon’s significant investment.
Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority Griff Lynch called the announcement terrific news for Effingham County and the State of Georgia.

Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on the project in partnership with the Effingham County IDA, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that Sewon has integrated into Georgia’s automotive community through working with local colleges, Georgia Quick Start, and statewide initiatives like Georgia Made.
On October 25, 2022, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and 26,700 jobs in Georgia.
Sewon America was established in 2008 in LaGrange as a Georgia company.
Sewon primarily operates in the Motor Vehicles and Car Bodies industry within the Transportation Equipment sector, producing stamped body components.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Sewon America Announces 740 New Jobs in Effingham County - Effingham County GA

The Group strengthened its production structure in North America in response to increased requirements from Japanese automakers in the region.

The Company optimally allocated production among its three U.S. facilities, with Shiroki North America, Inc. specializing in seat components, Shiroki-GA, LLC specializing in door sashes, and Shiroki GT, LLC specializing in window regulators.

Financial performance at China operations significantly increased year-on-year due to streamlining initiatives by Guangzhou Shiroki to improve production operations and procure dies and facilities locally, alongside aggressive sales activities with OEMs in China.

Shiroki Corp. (Thailand) Ltd.'s revenue temporarily dropped due to lower customer production volumes from flooding, but business performance is forecasted to recover due to rising domestic demand and greater export demand to neighboring countries.
The Company established P.T. Shiroki Indonesia in August 2011 and began constructing a plant to mass-produce window regulators and seat adjusters for local OEMs in Bekasi, West Java.

The 19,000-square-meter Indonesian facility is going to employ 70 people by 2015.

The Company provided greater technical support to its subsidiary Technico Industries Ltd., which launched commercial production of window regulators and seat adjusters for Maruti Suzuki India "Swift", ensuring profits through effective investment.

(Korea) for door frames, RG Brose (South Africa) and Brose do Brasil (Brazil) for window regulators, and Technico Industries Ltd. (India) for seat recliners, seat adjusters, and window regulators.

facts: SK Battery America paid $31 million to Commerce-based recycling center Metro Site Inc. in late September as a settlement for a fire that destroyed the family-owned business's facility.
The settlement was reached just before the case was set to go before a Jackson County jury.
SK Battery spokesperson Joe Guy Collier stated that SK Battery America added an extra layer of oversight to the disposal of unused material at the site more than a year ago.

Collier noted that the agreement provides a generous settlement for Metro Site to rebuild and relaunch its business, and questioned why Metro Site continued arguing the case after agreeing to settle a month prior.

Attorneys for Metro Site Inc. alleged that SK Battery America disguised more than 600 explosive, hazardous lithium-ion batteries as regular recyclable material inside a roll-off dumpster shipped to their non-hazardous recycling facility in Commerce.
The delivery of the dumpster allegedly triggered a chemical reaction known as thermal runaway, causing a catastrophic fire at the Banks County facility.
According to the lawsuit, the fire burned for almost a week before flames were extinguished, emitting toxic fumes for miles and depleting Commerce’s water reserves to unsafe levels.
State Senator Bo Hatchett reported that Banks County used more than 3 million gallons of water over five days to extinguish the blaze.

Plaintiff attorneys claimed this was allegedly the second time SK Battery tampered with evidence, citing a 2021 case where an International Trade Court judge determined SK systematically destroyed evidence in a patent dispute with LG Energy Solution, resulting in a settlement of over $1 billion.
Collier disputed the evidence tampering claim, stating the referenced previous case was filed in 2019 before SK Battery America started manufacturing operations.

Metro Site attorneys questioned the millions of dollars in tax breaks SK received to build its Commerce facility, with the lawsuit specifically citing more than $300 million in tax breaks for the Commerce-based gigafactory.
Plaintiff attorneys cited 911 service call records showing an exorbitant number of internal fires at the gigafactory as evidence of a pattern of issues.

Collier countered that the cited fire events occurred over three years, with many triggered only by smoke alarms without actual fires, and none resulted in significant damage or burn injuries to employees.
SK Battery America established an on-site emergency response team in 2021 that is staffed around the clock by trained firefighters using sophisticated equipment, including a company-owned fire truck.
A second lawsuit regarding reimbursement for resources expended during the blaze remains pending between SK Battery and Banks County government.

Banks County retained the McDonald & Cody law firm for legal representation in the pending case, which was transferred to federal court before a motion was filed to move future proceedings to Jackson County Superior Court.

Hatchett stated that during the fire, county officials had to monitor water going into a nearby treatment facility and evacuate a fire station due to extraordinary measures taken to protect government property.
SK Battery is known as one of the largest employers in Jackson and Banks counties.
Collier emphasized that SK Battery America maintains compliance with safety guidelines and has stringent procedures for shipping and disposing of production-related material.
source_title: SK Battery pays $31M to family-owned business after ‘catastrophic’ fire in Commerce - Now Georgia

facts: SK On, the battery subsidiary of SK Innovation, operates a plant in Commerce, Georgia.
The plant announced layoffs on the 6th (local time).
SK Battery America dismissed 968 employees.
The layoffs represent 37% of the plant's total workforce of 2,566 workers.
The restructuring was carried out in response to sluggish electric vehicle sales and changing market conditions.
SK Battery America stated it reduced its workforce to adjust operations to current market conditions.
SK Battery America remains committed to honoring promises to Georgia and building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
The plant supplies EV batteries to Germany’s Volkswagen.
The plant supplies EV batteries to South Korea’s Hyundai Motor Company.
The plant supplied batteries for Ford Motor Company’s electric pickup truck, the Ford F-150 Lightning.
Profitability was negatively impacted after Ford canceled production of the F-150 Lightning model.
Ford shifted focus to profitable hybrid and internal combustion engine vehicles after the administration of Donald John Trump ended tax credit benefits for EV purchases.
The South Korean battery industry faces difficulties due to a slowing shift from internal combustion engine vehicles to EVs in major markets like the United States.
SK Innovation is constructing a second battery plant in Georgia.
The second Georgia facility will supply batteries to Hyundai Motor Company.
The second Georgia facility is scheduled to begin production in the first half of this year.
A joint venture plant with Ford in Tennessee is expected to start production in 2028.
The Tennessee plant may supply both automotive batteries and energy storage batteries.
source_title: Why Did SK Battery America Lay Off 37% of Workers at Its Georgia Plant? "Impact of Sluggish EV Sales" - MK

facts: SK Battery America Inc. has laid off nearly 1,000 workers at its battery manufacturing plant in Commerce, Georgia.
The layoffs coincide with automakers revising their electrification strategies and uncertainty around consumer demand for electric vehicles.
A notice was filed under the Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act (WARN) by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
Friday marked the final working day for exactly 958 employees.
The affected workers will continue to receive pay through May 6.
SK Battery America Inc. opened its $2.6 billion plant in January 2022.
The Commerce, Georgia plant supplies batteries for the electric pickup Ford F-150 Lightning produced by Ford Motor Company.
In December, Ford Motor Company announced it would cancel the fully electric version of the F-150 model and instead introduce an extended-range variant.
Industry observers note the decision reflects slower-than-expected growth in the U.S. electric vehicle market and shifts in federal policy.
Under the administration of Donald Trump, the government has moved away from aggressive electrification targets while supporting the oil and gas sector.
The Trump administration is proposing to ease emissions standards for automakers.
SK Battery America Inc. and Ford Motor Company had previously announced a joint investment of $11.4 billion to build battery plants in the United States.
The partnership between SK Battery America Inc. and Ford Motor Company ended in December.
In 2020, SK Battery America Inc. revealed plans to invest $940 million to expand battery manufacturing in the Atlanta area.
The 2020 Atlanta area expansion plan was expected to create around 600 jobs.
SK Group and Hyundai Motor Company are continuing development of a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville.
The state of Georgia has attracted a $5 billion EV factory investment by Rivian.
The state of Georgia has attracted a $7.6 billion EV manufacturing complex investment by Hyundai Motor Company.
EV demand continues to grow but has fallen short of earlier projections.
Electric vehicles accounted for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the United States in 2025.
The 8% EV market share in 2025 was roughly unchanged from the previous year.
Automakers such as Ford Motor Company, General Motors, and Stellantis have begun reassessing multibillion-dollar electrification strategies.
These automakers are delaying or revising factory projects and investments due to shifting demand patterns.
Hybrid and more fuel-efficient gasoline vehicles are gaining popularity among buyers concerned about driving range.
Buyers are also concerned about the availability of charging infrastructure for electric vehicles.
The U.S. government has removed tax credits of up to $7,500 for new and used EV purchases.
The removal of EV tax credits could further affect the pace of EV adoption in the market.
source_title: SK Battery America Inc. Lays Off Nearly 1,000 Workers at Georgia Plant

SK Group's affiliates, including SK On, SK Battery America, and Absolix, are key players in the battery and technology industry, according to Governor Kemp.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the contribution SK Group's affiliates make to the technology industry is very important beyond Georgia's state borders.
Governor Brian Kemp expressed looking forward to continuing a cooperative relationship with SK Group's affiliates in the future.
An SK On official described Georgia as a reliable partner with an innovative business environment and strong policy support.
An SK On official added that the company will do its best to create a sustainable electrification ecosystem with the state of Georgia.

facts: A battery company plans to spend $19 million on a new regional IT hub facility in north Fulton County, but it's unclear whether Georgia taxpayers are on the hook for any incentives.
SK Battery America expects to create 200 high-tech jobs at an integrated IT management center on Sanctuary Parkway in Roswell.
The integrated IT management center will serve the company's battery manufacturing facilities in Georgia and the country.
Georgia Department of Economic Development officials did not respond to a request for information about what incentives the state offered or what it might cost taxpayers.
The company is spending roughly $2.6 billion on a pair of lithium-ion battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County to serve electric vehicles.

Jason Choi, SK Battery America's head of information technology, stated that the company decided to open its IT regional center in Roswell to strengthen SK's position as a leader in the battery industry and help build and operate an advanced manufacturing system in accordance with further expansion of battery production bases in the United States.
SKBA is part of SK Group, the second-largest conglomerate in South Korea.
In 2019, SK On established SK Battery America as its U.S. production base.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the new IT regional center is a natural result of the company's commitment to Georgia.

entity_name: Electric Vehicle (EV) Market and Supply Chain
canonical_name: Electric Vehicle (EV) Market and Supply Chain
facts: The expansion of the electric vehicle (EV) market has brought with it nuanced opportunities to create a cleaner and transformative new energy carrier.
Demand for EVs is projected to continue to expand globally.
Higher oil prices, spurred by the Russian invasion of Ukraine, have made EVs seem suddenly appealing to many car consumers, accelerating adoption globally.
The US Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) includes $369 billion in renewable energy investment and tax credits for EVs, which should put automakers on a growth path for the next decade or so.
Sales of internal combustion engine vehicles declined by 8.5% year-over-year during the first half of 2022, while sales of EVs grew by 60%+, according to LMC Automotive, led by BYD and Tesla.
Bloomberg New Energy Finance (BNEF) projected EV sales to hit 10.7 million in 2022 and 20.6 million by 2025.
Surges in EV sales are driven by policy support, improvements in battery technology, more charging infrastructure, and new compelling models from automakers.
Electrification is spreading to new segments of road transport.
China and Europe will account for nearly 80% of EV sales by 2025, while the US is projected to represent 15%.
The major gap in EV adoption is supply, with demand so strong that some models are effectively sold out and there are long waits for others.
Carmakers, battery suppliers, and semiconductor chip manufacturers must build and equip new factories to meet demand.
The sweeping new US climate bill apportions nearly $400 billion over 10 years to encourage the clean energy transition and the growth of factories.

Tesla CEO Elon Musk admitted on a recent earnings call that the company does not have a demand problem but a production problem, reflecting an industry-wide focus on scaling operations and improving supply chain management.
Production is taking place at a Georgia factory spanning 3 football fields, where SK Battery America employees work 24/7.

The new Ford F-150 Lightning is highlighted as part of an all-around win for manufacturing revitalization, gas savings, and curbing the transportation sector's leading 27% share of US greenhouse gas emissions.

China’s EVE Energy will supply BMW with large cylindrical batteries for its electric cars in Europe, with contracts signed to be BMW’s primary supplier of battery cells in Europe for new EVs hitting the market from 2025.
Metals are an important component of EV batteries, with prices rising but expected to trend downward due to new supply coming to market.

The most common EV battery type uses a combination of nickel, cobalt, and magnesium in lithium-ion cells, while other metals feature in the revolution including graphite, silicon, tin, copper (in charging stations), and aluminium and lightweight steels (in car bodies).
Long-term prospects stay healthy for commodities like nickel and lithium used in EV batteries.

At least 40% of critical metals in batteries (lithium, nickel, cobalt, and manganese) must come from the US or a Free Trade Agreement partner to comply with the IRA, rising to 80% in 2026, necessitating more factories within US borders.
Commodity suppliers have to open new mines and build refineries, with mining companies already reacting to high prices by announcing expansions to stabilize prices in the next few years.

Charging companies are struggling to install stations fast enough, which is especially important for fleet owners representing more than 2.5 million vehicles on the road and claiming more than $1.1 trillion in annual revenue.
Tesla is researching methods to cull out multiple uses from battery cells and expanding its R&D in 4680 batteries, including for energy storage to improve range.

A managed EV battery charging and discharging profile with the national grid, known as the Vehicle-to-Grid system (V2G), is projected to reduce renewable energy intermittency impacts, with Australia’s REVS project serving as an example of commercially available EVs and chargers contributing to energy stability.

Technological changes in the automobile industry accelerate automated driving development, including a project at Cornell University where researchers developed a way to help autonomous vehicles create “memories” of previous experiences for future navigation in adverse weather.
The emergence of plastics in EV development is considered pivotal for efficiency and profitability, giving engineers freedom to design various parts and sizes by replacing bulkier materials.

The latest investment will create another 600 jobs in Commerce, Georgia.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp confirmed the creation of the additional 600 jobs.
The new investment is in addition to a previously announced $1.67 billion planned expenditure by SK Innovation.
The prior announcement included plans to develop two manufacturing facilities.
The prior announcement also included plans to create 2,000 jobs.

facts: Somerville, Massachusetts-based startup Form Energy announced the chemistry for an iron-air-exchange battery.
The battery could offer long-duration storage at a price of less than $20/kWh.
The technology relies on thousands of small iron pellets which rust when exposed to oxygen, then revert back to iron when oxygen is removed.
Form claims the battery can deliver electricity for 100 hours.
Form announced a $200 million Series D funding round led by an investment from the innovation fund of steelmaker ArcelorMittal.
ArcelorMittal is one of the world’s leading iron ore producers.
ArcelorMittal will non-exclusively supply iron materials developed jointly with Form for use in the batteries.
Mateo Jaramillo is Form CEO and co-founder.
Jaramillo does not consider the technology long-duration storage, instead preferring the term 'multi-day storage'.
The capacity to dispatch energy for 100 hours puts it in a different category than long-duration storage, generally defined as systems with at least 10 hours of duration.
Jaramillo previously led Tesla’s energy storage arm.
Jaramillo considers the Form Energy technology complementary, not in competition, with shorter-duration lithium-ion batteries.
Experts say this balance will be essential to transition the grid to renewable energy.
Lithium-ion batteries can store energy for hours and distribute it throughout the day.
A 100% renewable grid will need larger storage systems to tackle the day-to-day or seasonal variability in renewable production.
High cost and infrastructure difficulties have limited widespread penetration of long-duration technologies on the market.
Mark Jacobson is director of the Atmosphere/Energy program at Stanford University.
Jacobson said a $20/kWh cost, if commercial costs end up that low, would be a 'substantial breakthrough'.
Jacobson stated it 'would enable the rapid transition to 100% clean, renewable electricity on a worldwide scale, while avoiding blackouts, at lower cost than previously thought'.
Jacobson added in an email that it 'would eliminate the need for any more fossil fuels, nuclear power and bioenergy for electricity faster than we thought'.
BloombergNEF found that lithium-ion battery pack prices fell to $137/kWh in 2020.
Projected costs were close to $100/kWh by 2023.
Manufacturers like Tesla and CATL have dropped prices as low as $80/kWh.

A March study published in Nature Energy found that the energy capacity cost of long-duration storage technology must fall below $20/kWh to reduce total carbon-free electricity system costs by at least 10%.
The study found capacity costs would have to drop even lower to displace nuclear and natural gas plants.
Form will partner with Minnesota electric cooperative Great River Energy on a test project for the iron air exchange battery.
Construction for the test project is expected in 2023.
Jaramillo said other test projects are in the works but have not been announced.

Jaramillo said early on they had an indication that this type of technology had great potential, and now they have to prove the bankability of the asset itself, proving that it is durable and meets the needs of the utilities.
Jaramillo stated the only way to compare one type of storage to another is in real-world operating conditions.
Besides the investment from ArcelorMittal, Form Energy has been backed by Breakthrough Energy Ventures, the Bill Gates-led climate investment fund.
A November 2020 Series C funding round raised $76 million.
The Series C included investments from Energy Impact Partners, Temasek and NGP Energy Technology Partners III.
source_title: Form Energy's $20/kWh, 100-hour iron-air battery could be a 'substantial breakthrough' | Utility Dive

facts: SK Battery America is conducting a comprehensive operational restructuring at its Commerce, Georgia facility while maintaining full production capability.
The facility's pre-restructuring total employee count was 2,566.
The facility's post-restructuring total employee count will be 1,600.
The workforce reduction represents a 37.3% decrease in total employees.
The annual production capacity remains unchanged at 22 GW.
Workers per GW capacity decreased from 116.6 to 72.7, representing a 37.7% drop.
Labour efficiency is projected to improve by 59%.
The facility is reducing its workforce by exactly 958 employees.

Modern battery manufacturing at the facility relies on automated systems for precision-critical operations including cell assembly, formation operations, stacking and winding procedures, and quality assurance.
The maintenance of 22 GW annual capacity despite a 37% workforce reduction indicates successful implementation of technological solutions.
The operational model suggests manufacturing efficiency improvements exceeding 40%.
The Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas documented that United States electric vehicle sales experienced growth followed by market uncertainty.
Ford announced a $19.5 billion write-down related to electric vehicle strategy shifts.
Ford cancelled electric F-150 Lightning production due to insufficient sales volume.
The BlueOval SK battery joint venture partnership was dissolved.
Major automakers are strategically pivoting toward hybrid vehicle development and energy storage business expansion.

A SK Battery America spokesperson confirmed the company is pursuing future customers in the Battery Energy Storage System arena, indicating a formal strategic commitment to market diversification beyond electric vehicle applications.
The facility implemented a 60-day pay continuation for all 958 affected workers extending through May 6, 2026.
Benefits maintenance during the transition period provides healthcare and related coverage to affected workers.
Written notification compliance with the Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) Act requirements was implemented.
SK Battery America provided WARN Act layoff notification on March 6, 2026.
Industry reports indicate that major battery plants are cutting nearly 1,000 jobs as the sector adjusts to changing market conditions.
source_title: SK Battery America Cuts 958 Jobs at Georgia Plant

facts: Barton Malow partnered with Gray Construction as design-build contractors to deliver two battery plants in Glendale, Kentucky.
The facilities total 9 million square feet under roof and are built on BlueOval SK Battery Park Kentucky’s 1,500-acre campus.
The project includes 1.8 million square feet of highly controlled cleanroom space across two floors.
The project includes essential outbuildings for hazardous storage, waste management, security, and testing.
Construction began in early 2022.
Construction reached substantial completion in February 2025.
The project met an aggressive speed-to-market schedule for a project of unprecedented scale and technical complexity.
The first concrete placement occurred in August 2022.
The first steel column was erected ahead of schedule in October 2022.
Cleanroom certification was achieved by July 2024.
At peak, 4,700 craft professionals were engaged on site.
The team utilized advanced scheduling, earned value management, and real-time coordination across trades.
The construction team moved 4.3 million cubic yards of soil.
The team laid 283,000 tons of stone.
The team poured 66,000 cubic yards of concrete.
The team erected over 40,000 tons of steel while staying under budget.
Barton Malow partnered with local organizations to support food insecurity relief, park revitalization, workforce development, and environmental stewardship.
In 2025, the GE2 plant was awarded The Association of Union Constructors’ (TAUC) Joseph R. La Rocca Project of the Year Award in the “Above $50 Million” category.
The award recognizes outstanding work performed under labor-management cooperation regarding collaboration and teamwork, quality and craftsmanship, execution, and safety.
The park is part of Ford and SK On’s $5.8 billion manufacturing investment to bring EVs at scale to American consumers.
This represents the single largest manufacturing investment at one time by any automaker in the nation.
source_title: Electric VehicleBlueOval SK Battery Park Kentucky - Barton Malow

entity_name: US Electric Vehicle Battery Manufacturing Industry
facts: Ford Motor is constructing a Blue Oval electric battery plant on 1,500 acres of former farmland in Marshall, Mich.
Marshall officials believe the plant will diversify the local economy and attract newcomers to the town of just under 7,000 residents.

James Durian, chief executive of the Marshall Area Economic Development Alliance, warns that without fighting for economic development, the community will continue to decline and young people will leave.
Across the United States, at least $100 billion has been invested or promised for EV battery plants.
These investments will create capacity to produce 18 million battery packs per year.
TechCrunch estimates the plants will employ nearly 65,000 people in total.
The investment surge is due in part to generous tax credits in the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022.
Economic development agencies have been chasing battery investments for much of the past decade to give their communities an edge on future technology.
EV battery plants in the 2020s are comparable to international car plants from the 1980s through the 2000s as billion-dollar investments providing thousands of jobs.
A major peril for these projects is Americans’ reluctance to commit to electric vehicles.
When the United Auto Workers struck Ford for six weeks last fall, construction on the Marshall battery plant was halted.
After the strike, Ford pushed back the launch of the Marshall plant (one of three planned) and reduced its expected workforce due to weaker than expected EV demand.
In April, Ford slashed production of the F-150 Lightning electric pickup and pushed back the introduction of a new electric SUV planned for Canada.
Marshall Area Economic Development Alliance CEO James Durian states that communities are at the whim of the economy and electric vehicles but believes the future is electric vehicles.
Toyota has begun hiring workers for a vast battery complex in Liberty, N.C., featuring 10 production lines.
The Toyota Liberty plant will employ 5,100 people working in a high-tech clean-room environment.
The facility will manufacture batteries for both electric cars and hybrid vehicles powered by batteries and gasoline engines.
Since its initial announcement in 2021, Toyota has boosted its Liberty investment twice, increasing it from $1.9 billion to nearly $14 billion.
The Liberty plant will be the sole battery supplier for Toyota’s hybrid vehicles made in North America, including the top-selling Camry sedan and 13 other models.
By 2030, company officials project that about 30 percent of Toyota’s US sales will be EVs.
Sean Suggs, president of the North Carolina venture, notes that Toyota is preparing for customer demand but acknowledges customers are not yet asking for all EVs.
The CEO of General Motors once declared her company would go entirely electric by 2035.
North Carolina previously pushed hard to win a Toyota-Mazda joint venture factory but lost out to Alabama in 2018 before shifting focus to the battery plant.
Greensboro Chamber of Commerce president Brent Christensen helped lobby for the plant and describes EV batteries as "the gasoline of the future."
The Environmental Defense Fund estimates that national capacity for 18 million battery packs will far exceed the 10 million needed to power all vehicles announced for the US market.
There are currently 30 plants under construction with new investments still being announced, alongside potential foreign plants serving the US market.
Honda recently announced plans to spend $11 billion on a factory for EVs and batteries in Canada, where Volkswagen has also made a major battery investment.
Although 2.5 million EVs are now in use in the United States, that represents less than 1 percent of the 282 million vehicles registered there.
A Gallup Organization survey found that only 35 percent of respondents might consider buying an EV in the future, down from 43 percent a year ago.
The proportion of people who say they’d never buy an EV jumped to 48 percent from 41 percent in 2023.
source_title: Automakers and towns make a $100 billion bet on EV batteries

facts: Fulton County moved from #11 last year to #4 in the national Top 20 Best Counties in America rankings for economic development projects.
Fulton County achieved the #1 position in the I-85 Corridor Report.
Site Selection Magazine published the rankings in its July issue, utilizing the Conway Data Project Database to evaluate 3,142 U.S. counties based on new investments and job creation.
Fulton County is ranked as the #1 county in the Southeast and is the only county in Georgia to make the top 20 rankings.
Robb Pitts, Chairman of the Fulton County Board of Commissioners, stated the recognition highlights collaborative work with partners at the State of Georgia, the region, and its 15 cities.

Kristi Brigman, Deputy Commissioner of Global Commerce at the Georgia Department of Economic Development, noted that diverse, well-educated talent and a collaborative ecosystem are fostering growth in innovative industries such as tech and life sciences.

Recent major corporate wins and investments in Fulton County include Adobe, SK Battery America, Amazon Music, Cummins, Resia Manufacturing, Microsoft, GCI Restaurant Group, Mastercard, Newegg Commerce, Nike, PrizePicks, and Cargill.
These recent projects span various sectors including technology, manufacturing, and finance, strengthening the regional economy and creating job opportunities for Georgians.
Fulton County is the most populous county in Georgia and is home to 15 distinct cities, including Atlanta.
Metro Atlanta is currently the country’s sixth-largest region and continues to attract top talent as businesses choose locations with strong workforces.

Samir Abdullahi, Director of Economic Development and Select Fulton, emphasized that Select Fulton serves as the central hub for economic and workforce development services throughout the county, cultivating new job prospects and investment opportunities.
The article was published courtesy of Georgia CEO on July 10, 2024.

links.context: Site Selection Magazine rankings and I-85 Corridor Report

source_title: Fulton County Soars to #4 Nationally for Economic Development Projects - The ATL Airport Chamber

facts: Ford and South Korea’s SK Innovation (SKI) announced they will manufacture next-generation battery cells and arrays in the US.
Canada’s First Quantum Minerals is in talks with South Korea’s Posco Group about a potential joint battery venture.
Norway’s Morrow Batteries will jointly develop anode materials for its planned lithium-ion battery cell facility.
Australia-listed Jadar Resources and European Lithium will work together on the development of their respective lithium projects.
Canadian cathode materials producer Nano One entered an advanced lithium-ion battery cathode materials coating development agreement.
International separator group Entek is acquiring the majority stake in the lead battery separator business of Tokyo-based Nippon.
Canadian Pacific (CP) will retrofit a line-haul diesel freight locomotive with hydrogen fuel cells and Leclanché’s European-manufactured lithium-ion batteries.
Leclanché will deploy proprietary energy management software for the CP project to evaluate combined technologies’ readiness for the freight rail sector.
CP operates a 21,000 km rail network across Canada and the US that serves deep-water ports on the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans.
CP will conduct rail service trials and qualification testing once the locomotive is operational.

Leclanché CEO Anil Srivastava stated battery systems and energy management software significantly increase total energy efficiency compared to standalone fuel cell modules, reducing total cost of ownership.
Leclanché is evaluating the freight train market in Europe, Australia, Brazil and South Africa for diesel stock transformation.
Leclanché unveiled a high-energy, 65 Ah lithium-ion pouch cell designed to meet demands of bus and truck manufacturers for increased range and operating time.
Solid Power’s Series B investment round will enable production of full-scale automotive batteries and expand in-house production capabilities.
Auto manufacturers aim to utilize Solid Power’s low-cost, high-energy all solid-state technology in forthcoming electric vehicles and have expanded existing joint development agreements.
Solid Power is producing 20 ampere hour (Ah) multi-layer all solid-state batteries on a continuous roll-to-roll production line utilizing industry standard lithium-ion processes.
CEO Doug Campbell plans to start producing automotive-scale batteries on the pilot production line in early 2022.
Solid Power delivered hundreds of production line-produced battery cells validated by Ford and BMW late last year, formalizing commercialization plans.
Ford and BMW will receive full-scale 100 Ah cells for automotive qualification testing and vehicle integration beginning in 2022.
Volta Energy Technologies CEO Dr Jeff Chamberlain noted Volta invested early in Solid Power due to promising technology and a fundamental focus on manufacturability.
Volta is a venture capital firm focused on breakthrough energy storage and battery innovations, spun out of the US Department of Energy’s Argonne National Laboratory.
Leipzig is BMW’s pioneer plant for electromobility, building the BMW i3 since 2013, and will now produce electric drive train components.
Battery modules will be produced in a 10,000 sqm production area previously reserved for the BMW i8 at Leipzig.
BMW’s first of four coating lines for fifth-generation high-voltage batteries went on stream at its Regensburg plant, occupying over 40,000 sqm.
High-voltage batteries will be produced in Regensburg from 2022.
BMW battery modules are produced in a highly-automated process with plasma cleaning and specialized coating for optimal insulation.
BMW uses different battery cells from supply partners depending on which provides the best properties for each vehicle concept.
Michael Nikolaides, senior VP of production engines and e-drives at BMW, expects at least 50% of vehicles delivered worldwide to be fully electric by 2030.
BMW is investing more than €250m (£217m) in Regensburg and Leipzig locations to supply electrified vehicles with high-voltage batteries.
BMW produces high-voltage batteries and components at three German locations: Dingolfing, Leipzig, and Regensburg.
Akasol, being acquired by US-headquartered BorgWarner, has an additional supply deal worth the high double-digit-million-euro range from mid-2022 until at least 2024.
The Akasol order volume could increase to a low three-digit-million-euro amount if a potential option for further battery systems is taken up by 2027.
Akasol CEO Sven Schulz stated the order for AKM CYC lithium-ion ultra-high energy systems is the second extension of cooperation with its long-term electric bus manufacturing customer.
The Akasol systems use new cylindrical-cell battery modules allowing electric vehicles to increase range by more than 60%.
The module achieves an energy density of approx. 221 Wh/kg with liquid-cooled, scalable battery modules at the system level.
Commissioned Akasol battery systems will be produced on fully-automated lines at Gigafactory 1 in Darmstadt.
Gigafactory 1 will have a production capacity of 2.5 GWh from that year onwards, potentially increasing up to 5 GWh.
Akasol senior VP Klaus-Dieter Nagel stated follow-up orders will gradually increase production capacities at Gigafactory 1 over the coming quarters.
Gigafactory 1 lines can produce up to 70 ultra-high-energy battery systems daily, each with about 100 kWh storage capacity.
Porsche CEO Oliver Blume stated a factory is to be built in the southwest of Germany.
Blume said Porsche will purchase battery cells from the Volkswagen Group but require high-performance cells.
Porsche is researching high-performance batteries with silicon instead of graphite anodes to achieve higher energy density and better fast-charging capability.
The new Porsche batteries will be produced in Europe and initially used in limited-production, high-performance vehicles and customer motorsport.
BASF will hold a 51% stake in a venture with Shanshan to produce cathode active materials (CAM) and precursors (PCAM) in China for e-mobility and consumer electronics markets.
The BASF-Shanshan move strengthens position in Asia and increases annual CAM capacity to 160 kilotons by 2022, with further expansions under way.
BASF catalysts division president Dr Peter Schuhmacher stated the partnership brings two powerhouses in the battery materials market together.
Shanshan chairman Yonggang Zheng stated the deal strengthens competitiveness in the Chinese market and accelerates integration into the global market.
Shanshan’s portfolio includes CAM and PCAM for lithium-ion batteries, a raw materials supply chain, and a battery recycling operation.
Shanshan operates four production sites for CAM and PCAM in Hunan and Ningxia, China, with an annual capacity of 90 kilotons by 2022.
The BASF-Shanshan partnership deal is set for completion later this summer, subject to various approvals.
source_title: Business News Energising the Battery Industry and Beyond

facts: Residents, environmental organizations, and members of local faith communities joined a virtual hearing held by the Georgia Environmental Protection Division to convey concerns about Solvay's plans to significantly expand its operations and drastically increase toxic air emissions in Augusta.
Solvay Specialty Polymers USA, LLC operates a large chemical plant in South Augusta located in the middle of a predominantly Black community where half of the population earns a lower income.

Decades of legacy pollution in the areas surrounding Solvay's facility have led to residents facing serious health problems, including asthma and other respiratory conditions, increased risk of heart disease, and a lower overall life expectancy.
Solvay’s facility is a major source of hazardous air pollutants with the potential to emit approximately 50 tons of hazardous air pollutants per year if allowed to expand operations.
The proposed expansion would potentially include more than 25 tons of toxic chlorobenzene emissions annually.
Nearly 80 people attended the hearing to ask state regulators about public health implications, citing Augusta’s air quality issues and high asthma rates.
Hearing participants questioned how the plant’s emissions would further impact Augusta’s noncompliance status with federal air pollution standards.
The Augusta metro area has been named by the American Lung Association as one of the nation’s worst 25 metro areas for particle pollution.
The Augusta metro area was ranked as the worst in the southeastern United States for particle pollution.
SELC submitted written comments on behalf of Savannah Riverkeeper and 13 local organizations and churches regarding the permit application.
Comments pointed out that Solvay has been improperly classified as a minor rather than major source of criteria pollutants.
Comments highlighted significant issues with Solvay’s compliance with the Clean Air Act more broadly.

SELC Staff Attorney Patrick Anderson stated that especially given the potential for additional significant pollution, Solvay should be classified as a major source and held to stricter emissions standards.

Patrick Anderson urged EPD to deny the permit or address glaring issues and lack of information in the company’s application, and to release a revised draft permit for additional public notice and comment.

Savannah Riverkeeper Tonya Bonitatibus noted that while communities have heard from the company that it wants to be a good neighbor, the permit application omits critical data on how much more pollution residents will face.
Tonya Bonitatibus stated that EPD should require Solvay to provide all information in its application to the public before allowing any expansion to move forward.
source_title: Augusta communities speak out against Solvay chemical plant expansion - Southern Environmental Law Center

The company produces nearly 15 gigawatt-hours of energy storage annually through domestic manufacturing facilities.
It develops solutions for transportation, essential power, and motive power applications.
The company is committed to environmental sustainability, achieving a 99% recycling rate for lead batteries.
Stryten Energy uses up to 90% recycled materials.
Its product portfolio spans advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium battery technologies for industries from automotive to military.

facts: Stryten Energy has been named a 2022 Silver Military Friendly® Employer and a Military Friendly® Brand.

The recognition is part of an annual list from militaryfriendly.com that identifies companies creating sustainable career paths, community outreach, brand enthusiasm, and enduring partnerships benefiting the military community.
Stryten hires military members across every level of the company, from executive management to the plant floor.
Stryten maintains a dedicated section on its Jobs website specifically for military and veteran job seekers.
Mike Judd serves as President and Chief Operating Officer of Stryten Energy.
Mike Judd stated that Stryten values veterans' service and proactively translates their military skills into jobs, announcing the recognition on Veterans Day.
The Military Friendly Employers list is produced annually by Viqtory, Inc., a service-disabled, veteran-owned small business.

Ratings for the list are awarded based on data from public sources, proprietary survey data, and personal surveys of veterans, processed through an algorithm overseen by The Military Friendly® Advisory Council and audited by Ernst & Young.
Stryten maintains high retention rates for its military personnel, with many progressing through multiple promotions within the company.
Stryten currently has job openings available for military members across numerous U.S. locations.
Mike Judd reiterated the company's commitment to hiring military community members and integrating them into the Stryten community.
Stryten sponsors Rivers of Recovery, a national nonprofit that uses outdoor-based activities to treat PTSD, depression, and anxiety in combat veterans and active-duty military.
Stryten Energy provides energy storage solutions across the transportation, motive power, essential power, and military sectors.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
The company partners with major recognized firms to meet demand for reliable and sustainable energy storage capacity.
Stryten's products power applications ranging from submarines to subcompacts, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains, and trucks.
Stryten's stored energy technologies include advanced lead and lithium batteries, intelligent chargers, and cloud-based software.
Stryten Energy is an industry leader backed by more than a century of expertise in delivering energy solutions.

facts: Stryten Energy LLC, a U.S.-based energy storage solutions provider, announced the signing of agreements by its affiliate Stryten Critical E-Storage LLC with Largo Clean Energy Corp. (LCE) to form Storion Energy LLC.
LCE is a subsidiary of Largo Inc., which trades on NASDAQ and TSX under the symbol LGO.

The new company’s mission is to remove barriers for battery manufacturers to domestically source price competitive electrolyte used in vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFB) for long-duration energy storage (LDES) applications in the U.S.
Storion utilizes the Earth to Energy™ model which combines access to high-quality vanadium supply from the only western hemisphere vanadium mine with domestic electrolyte production.
This model establishes a fully integrated vertical supply chain for utility-scale flow battery LDES solutions.

Storion will leverage Stryten’s proprietary reactor design and Largo Physical Vanadium Corp.’s (LPV) unique vanadium electrolyte leasing capabilities to deliver vanadium electrolyte at less than $0.02 per kilowatt-hour (kWh).

This cost-effective domestic supply chain for vanadium electrolyte positions the U.S. as a leader in critical energy infrastructure and supports the Department of Energy’s Long Duration Storage Shot goal to reduce the levelized cost of storage (LCOS) to $0.05/kWh by the end of the decade.

Mike Judd, President and CEO of Stryten Energy, stated that VRFB technology will completely replace lithium in the utility arena in the next five years to support deployment of renewables for data centers, AI, green hydrogen production, and commercial-industrial applications.
Vanadium electrolyte typically constitutes a large portion of the overall VRFB system cost depending on market price.

LPV provides a unique vanadium leasing model that allows investors to invest in physical vanadium assets while stored in VRFBs, aiming to alleviate acquisition costs and drive additional vanadium demand.
Storion will have exclusive rights to offer LPV-owned vanadium to the market in the form of vanadium electrolyte.

Francesco D’Alessio, President of LCE, noted that utilizing LPV’s leasing capabilities is expected to create a competitive VRFB pricing model to challenge lithium dominance for utility-scale deployments of 10 MWhs and greater.
Following the installation of the largest VRFB in Europe by the LCE team, Storion’s cost-effective domestic supply chain presents an opportunity to rapidly grow adoption of this technology.

VRFB technology provides long-duration energy storage greater than four hours to help ensure grid stability and facilitate increased utilization of renewables for businesses and consumers across the U.S.
With proper maintenance, VRFBs can last more than 20 years without their electrolyte losing storage capacity.
The electrolyte is infinitely recyclable, and the battery offers a near-limitless cycle life.
These systems are independently scalable in power and capacity, with potential for substantial cost reduction as discharge time increases.
Storion Energy will be a joint venture between a Stryten Energy affiliate and Largo Clean Energy Corp., a subsidiary of Largo Inc., one of the world’s largest and highest quality vanadium suppliers.
Storion will have locations in Wilmington, Massachusetts and Alpharetta, Georgia.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, and partners with major companies to meet demand for reliable and sustainable energy storage capacity.
Stryten’s stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.
LPV aims to provide a secure, convenient, and exchange-traded investment alternative for direct exposure to physical vanadium, a metal essential to steel, aerospace, and energy storage industries.
Vanadium is non-degrading and fully recyclable when used as electrolyte in VRFBs and offers carbon-reducing attributes when used in steel alloying applications.
LPV’s strategy includes owning and actively supplying vanadium to end users of VRFBs to advance renewable energy integration in long duration storage.
Largo is a globally recognized supplier of high-quality vanadium and ilmenite products sourced from its Maracás Menchen Mine in Brazil.
As one of the world’s largest primary vanadium producers, Largo produces critical materials for steel, aerospace, defense, chemical, and energy storage sectors.
Largo holds a 50% ownership stake in Storion Energy through its strategic investment in the clean energy storage sector.
source_title: Stryten Energy and Largo Launch Long-Duration Energy Storage Company

facts: Stryten Energy LLC announced the launch of expanded functionality in the Monitor module of Stryten Energy inCOMMAND™ on March 20, 2023.
Stryten Energy inCOMMAND, formerly GNB Cloud, is an energy performance management software tool that makes it easy to design and deploy web-connected battery and charger solutions.
The platform includes modules for executing power studies, modeling battery and charger solutions, monitoring systems, and managing asset configuration.
The enhanced Monitor module enables users to remotely manage motive power systems at scale.
Multi-site customers can view battery health data organized by region or location and drill down to the individual asset level for complete visibility.
Dealers can manage rental and service customers from a single dashboard within the software.

The system utilizes self-adaptive IoT technology enabled by smart gateways to automatically update batteries equipped with PowerLoggers in the software when they move between customer sites for location tracking.
The enhanced Monitor module tracks fleet energy usage, allowing customers to monitor site efficiency and identify opportunities to optimize performance and reduce costs across corporate fleets.

For dealers, the software enables new monetization opportunities such as a “Power by the Hour” model that monitors energy usage and bills customers only for battery use instead of charging expensive monthly fees.
Battery licenses are issued upfront with an easy one-time payment that guarantees data access for the life of the battery and includes capability for multiple users.
Matt Gould serves as Vice President of Sales and Service, Industrial at Stryten Energy.
Stryten Energy provides a broad range of energy storage solutions across the Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military, and Government sectors.
The company is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
Stryten Energy partners with recognized companies to meet the growing demand for reliable and sustainable energy storage capacity.
Stryten powers applications ranging from submarines to subcompacts, microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains, and trucks.
The company's stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.

facts: On August 2, 2023, Stryten Energy and Snapping Shoals EMC celebrated the installation of a vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) system.
A ribbon-cutting ceremony for the installation was held at Snapping Shoals EMC's office on Brown Bridge Road in Newton County, Georgia.
The installed VRFB system is the first of its kind in Georgia.
The partnership between Stryten Energy and Snapping Shoals EMC will demonstrate the VRFB’s energy storage capabilities and evaluate other uses for its long-duration technology.
The battery system promotes clean, renewable energy and holds a capacity of 100 kilowatt hours, which equates to 26,000 AA batteries.

Ceremony attendees included U.S. Rep. Mike Collins, representatives from the Georgia General Assembly, a representative from Senator Jon Ossoff’s office, Georgia Public Service Commissioner Tim Echols, and leaders from both Stryten Energy and Snapping Shoals EMC.
Georgia Public Service Commissioner Tim Echols expressed enthusiasm for clean energy, comparing the benefits of the VRFB to bacon.
Tim Echols stated that devices like the VRFB represent a "very high tech time" in the energy world that improves solar, resiliency, and grid performance.
Tim Echols quoted the VRFB as being "bacon in a box" that makes solar, resiliency, and the Snapping Shoals grid better.
The VRFB system is currently operating as a pilot project but will directly impact Snapping Shoals EMC customers.
The battery is connected to Snapping Shoals EMC's utility grid but has a limited transmission range due to its small demo size.

Snapping Shoals EMC Vice President of Engineering and Developing Technologies Robbie Young stated that the VRFB's effectiveness will be assessed using monitoring devices that provide energy values for engineer analysis.
Robbie Young aims to gain a better understanding of the battery through this installation to improve it for future applications.
Robbie Young expressed hope to learn significantly about the VRFB technology itself.
Snapping Shoals EMC engineers will monitor the system with the goal of increasing reliability and efficiency to make it cost-effective for larger-scale use.
Stryten Energy designed the VRFB over an 18-month period focusing on safety, reliability, and recyclability.
Stryten Energy Vice President of Essential Power Scott Childers expressed hope regarding the project's future and community benefits.
Scott Childers noted that Snapping Shoals operates in an active daily energy trading market.
Childers explained that Snapping Shoals' daily buying and selling of energy allows them to test the VRFB's performance and economic viability.
Scott Childers stated the project has far-reaching impacts ranging from reducing electric bills to driving energy independence and national security.
Childers described the joint relationship between Stryten Energy and Snapping Shoals EMC as a "one of a kind scenario" that enables solving major energy challenges together.
source_title: Snapping Shoals unveils new battery system demo with Stryten Energy - The Covington News

facts: Dragonfly Energy inked a $30 million Strategic Partnership with Stryten Energy, a leading North American battery manufacturer.
The partnership aims to expand the Battle Born Batteries® brand into new markets including military, automotive, marine, power sports, lawn & garden, and golf carts.
Expansion will be achieved through brand licensing, contract manufacturing, and battery pack design.
Stryten Energy will leverage its vast distribution network to introduce Battle Born Batteries® branded products to new retail markets.
The partnership aims to establish a domestic circular economy for lithium batteries.
Stryten Energy will leverage its existing lead battery recycling model for end-of-life Battle Born Batteries products.
The agreement allows Stryten Energy to manufacture and distribute Battle Born Batteries under license, leveraging the brand’s strong reputation.
The agreement is expected to significantly increase brand awareness for Battle Born Batteries® and unlock tremendous growth for both companies.
The agreement paves the way for future collaboration where Stryten’s numerous U.S. lithium battery initiatives could incorporate Dragonfly Energy’s cell manufacturing technology.
Future collaboration aims to reduce reliance on foreign suppliers and create a vertically integrated North American lithium battery production chain.
Denis Phares serves as Chief Executive Officer of Dragonfly Energy.
Mike Judd serves as Chief Executive Officer of Stryten Energy.
Dragonfly Energy Holdings Corp. (Nasdaq: DFLI) is a comprehensive lithium battery technology company specializing in cell manufacturing, battery pack assembly, and full system integration.
Through the Battle Born Batteries® brand, Dragonfly Energy has deployed hundreds of thousands of reliable battery packs through top-tier OEMs and a diverse retail customer base.

Dragonfly Energy’s patented dry electrode manufacturing process can deliver chemistry-agnostic power solutions for applications including energy storage systems, electric vehicles, and consumer electronics.
Dragonfly Energy's mission includes the future deployment of its proprietary, nonflammable, all-solid-state battery cells.
Stryten Energy provides energy storage solutions across the Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military, and Government sectors.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
Stryten Energy partners with recognized companies to meet growing demand for reliable and sustainable energy storage capacity.
Stryten Energy powers applications ranging from submarines to subcompacts, microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains, and trucks.
Stryten Energy’s stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.
Stryten Energy is backed by more than a century of industry expertise.
Benzinga reports that the licensing agreement is a game-changer for Dragonfly Energy.
RV Pro reports the agreement grants Stryten Energy a license to market and distribute Battle Born Batteries globally with a potential value of up to $30 million.
KTVN Channel 2 News reports that a local lithium-ion battery manufacturing company says its products are about to be distributed across the world.
KOLO 8 News Now notes that Nevada is the only place in the country where lithium is extracted, processed, and later recycled.
Atlanta Business Chronicle reports that Stryten and Dragonfly's deal is the latest step in advancing the battery supply chain in the US.
source_title: Dragonfly Energy Enters into $30M Licensing Agreement with Stryten Energy

facts: Effective Friday, January 31, 2025, Largo Clean Energy Corp. and Stryten Critical E-Storage, LLC finalized the formation of a joint venture named Storion Energy LLC.

Storion Energy is focused on removing barriers to entry for battery manufacturers by providing domestically sourced, price-competitive components for the flow battery industry, including vanadium electrolyte.
The partnership leverages Largo’s high-quality vanadium supply, Largo Physical Vanadium Corp.’s innovative electrolyte leasing model, and Stryten’s U.S.-based manufacturing expertise.

Francesco D’Alessio, President of LCE, stated that the transaction closing represents a transformational step for Largo as it advances its investment strategy into the fast-growing vanadium flow battery market.
Storion Energy now provides a direct channel for Largo’s high-quality vanadium products into the long duration energy storage sector.
Through Storion, the partners are establishing a fully integrated vanadium electrolyte supply chain in North America to address key challenges for vanadium flow battery adoption.
Stryten paid US$1 million in cash directly to Largo and will contribute an additional US$6 million over time to fund Storion’s operations.
LCE and Stryten each hold a 50% equity interest in Storion, with customary pre-emption rights and anti-dilution protections.
Storion’s board composition will be generally proportional to ownership, with Stryten holding one additional seat.
Largo amended its Safekeeping and Supply Agreements with LPV and assigned its Safekeeping Agreement to Storion.
Largo and Storion entered a separate supply agreement granting Storion a right of first offer for vanadium products.
Required third-party consents were received as closing conditions for the transaction.

Storion Energy intends to support scalable domestic electrolyte production to establish a fully integrated vertical supply chain for utility-scale vanadium redox flow battery long-duration energy storage solutions in the U.S.
Vanadium redox flow battery technology is described as a safe and reliable option for Battery Energy Storage Systems that need to provide energy storage of four or more hours.
Storion has locations in Alpharetta, Georgia and Wilmington, Massachusetts.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
Stryten Energy’s stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.

Largo Physical Vanadium Corp.’s strategy includes owning and actively supplying vanadium to end users of vanadium flow batteries to advance the integration of renewable energy in long duration storage.
Vanadium is non-degrading and fully recyclable when used as electrolyte in vanadium flow batteries and offers carbon reducing attributes when used in steel alloying applications.
Largo Inc. is one of the world’s largest primary vanadium producers, sourcing materials from its Maracás Menchen Mine in Brazil.
Largo maintains a 50% ownership stake in Storion Energy as part of its strategic investment in the clean energy storage sector.
