facts: During a May gathering of headquarters employees, Stryten Energy presented Rivers of Recovery with a check for $256,000.
The donation amount was a combination of the company’s annual contribution and the results of winning a cornhole match.
Mike Judd serves as President and CEO of Stryten Energy.
Rodger Meyer holds the title of Executive Vice President of Operations at Stryten Energy.
Petar Oklobdzija is Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer at Stryten Energy.
In January 2024, the Stryten Energy leadership team attended the annual Atlas Holdings leadership meeting.
The January 2024 Atlas leadership meeting included leaders from approximately 30 other Atlas companies.
All participating Atlas companies engaged in January Madness, an annual cornhole tournament that raises money for charity.
Stryten Energy split its tournament winnings with Georgia-based Iconex, which finished as the second-place team.
Herff Jones, also an Atlas company, provided rings and banners for the winners' ring ceremony.
source_title: Stryten Energy Donation Helps Change the Lives of Veterans

facts: On January 19, 2022, lead and lithium battery maker Stryten Energy announced the acquisition of Storion Energy to take on vanadium flow battery technology.
The acquisition marks a shift in markets toward redox flow batteries used for longer-duration storage applications such as utilities, microgrids, and military bases.
Storion Energy is a commercial spin-off from technology firm ITN Energy, which began research into redox flow batteries in 2010 and formed Storion in 2015 to bring the technology to market.

Stryten Energy states that Storion’s VRFB design solves the energy-to-power tradeoff for batteries by allowing operation for many hours without sacrificing power or release speed through simply adding more electrolytes.
Stryten Energy is based in Georgia, US.
Stryten Energy CEO Tim Vargo stated that the clean energy transition in the US requires reliable battery solutions across multiple chemistries to meet growing demand for renewable power.
According to Tim Vargo, adding Storion’s proprietary vanadium redox flow battery technology to Stryten’s portfolio will expand capabilities to solve current and future US energy challenges.

Mike Judd, president and chief operating officer at Stryten Energy, noted that the Storion facility will complement Stryten’s four R&D centres in North America that focus on lead and lithium storage solutions.

Stryten has created an Essential Power division to provide advanced lead and lithium battery technologies and services for applications ranging from telecommunications to microgrids, railways, and renewable energy storage.
The company states that Storion’s VRFB technology will expand the portfolio of Stryten’s Essential Power division.
source_title: Lead battery firm Stryten shifts into vanadium flow battery technology – Batteries International

facts: The Kansas House of Representatives issued House Resolution No. 6036 honoring Stryten Energy for 50 years of manufacturing at its Salina, Kansas plant.
The resolution acknowledges the company's long-standing contributions to the economic vitality, workforce development, and community strength of Salina and the State of Kansas.
Stryten Energy has manufactured batteries in Salina for five decades.
The company assembles batteries for key industry sectors including automotive, marine, recreation, heavy-duty truck, and lawn-and-garden for use across the United States.
Stryten Energy currently employs more than 700 people in Saline County.

The resolution highlights the company's civic engagement and volunteer service, including participation in the Smoky Hill River Festival, the downtown Salina Christmas Parade, the Parade of Lights, the Fe for a Cure Race, and contributions to Project Salina’s annual food drive.
Stryten Energy maintains ongoing engagement with Kansas technical schools and universities to promote advanced manufacturing careers and workforce development.

Rodger Meyer, Executive Vice President of Operations, stated the company is honored by the recognition and looks forward to continuing its mission of innovation, resilience, and leadership in energy storage technologies.
Stryten Energy's contributions to the domestic energy supply chain include battery recycling and closed-loop manufacturing technologies.
These contributions were recognized as important for strengthening U.S. energy security.
Representative Steven Howe sponsored the resolution and formally presented it, noting the company's commitment to the community, domestic supply chain, and national energy and security independence.
The Kansas House formally adopted House Resolution No. 6036 on March 11, 2026.
source_title: Stryten Energy Recognized by Kansas House for 50 Years of Manufacturing Excellence  - Professional Services

facts: Stryten Energy LLC is a U.S.-based energy storage solutions provider.
Tim Vargo, CEO, will transition to chairman of the board for Stryten Energy before the end of the company’s fiscal year, March 31, 2023.
Mike Judd, president and chief operating officer, will be promoted to CEO and president, also effective before the end of the company’s fiscal year.
Petar Oklobdzija joins the company as executive vice president and CFO, effective Feb. 1, 2023.
Oklobdzija will lead the company’s Finance, Accounting, Treasury, Information Technology and Strategic Sourcing functions.
Dan Autey joined the company as executive vice president, Commercial, on Jan. 3, 2023.
Autey is responsible for leading commercial strategies across the Stryten Energy business.
Tim Vargo stated that Mike Judd’s appointment to CEO is the culmination of a succession plan developed by the board and himself over several years.
Vargo expressed confidence in Judd to achieve the company’s strategy for increased profitability and growth.
Mike Judd stated he is excited to welcome Petar Oklobdzija and Dan Autey to the senior management team.
Judd noted that Oklobdzija’s tenure in operations and finance in the automotive and battery industries will help accelerate the company’s growth strategy into new and existing energy storage markets.
The past year marked a significant period of transformation for Stryten Energy with the integration of three strategic acquisitions.
These acquisitions added advanced lithium-ion and vanadium redox flow battery technologies to its energy storage chemistry portfolio.
The acquisitions also added a vertically integrated supply chain for engineered energy components to answer growing capacity needs.
Ongoing capital investments in its manufacturing plants position the company to meet increased demand for domestically produced, clean and sustainable energy storage solutions.
source_title: Stryten Energy Announces Executive Leadership Appointments

facts: A South Korean electric battery recycler is the latest clean-energy firm pulling back on investment plans for Georgia amid flagging demand for electric vehicles and the impending elimination of federal EV subsidies.
SungEel Recycling Park Georgia LLC had planned to spend $37 million and hire more than 100 people at a facility in Toccoa.
Stephens County officials announced in April that the project was stalled.
As of July 3, the industrial authority was reportedly in the process of buying back two sites in the Hayestone Brady Industrial Park developed with the help of a $700,000 state grant.

Stephens County Development Authority president and CEO Brittany Ivey told WLHR radio that the county looks forward to marketing those sites and finding another company they are excited to work with as they were Sungeel.

The news came months before the passage of President Trump’s “One Big Beautiful Bill Act” on July 3, which phases out $7,500 subsidies for new electric-vehicle purchases by Sept. 30, sooner than earlier versions of the bill.
The EV purchase subsidies had previously been set to sunset in 2032.

Republicans had long telegraphed the repeal of what they called EV “mandates” and tax breaks for solar energy even as some of Georgia’s most conservative members of Congress urged President Trump to preserve at least the production tax credits for solar to counter China and bolster American “energy dominance.”
Critics say the bill threatens progress in the South’s so-called “Battery Belt,” in which Georgia is a key node thanks to South Korean investors that have committed to at least three separate sites.
Only one of those South Korean investor sites, SK Battery in Commerce, is operational so far.

The bill also impacts solar energy, which has been a booming business in Georgia largely thanks to billions in investment by Qcells, a Korean firm that has become the largest maker of panels in North America.

Qcells’ decision to set up its solar production base in Georgia appears to have served it well, as future subsidies seem contingent on when factories started construction and how well they decouple from Chinese supply chains, another stated goal of parent company Hanwha for its Georgia plants.

On the EV battery side, Hyundai is developing two separate factories with SK (Cartersville) and LG Energy Solution (next to its Bryan County factory) potentially worth a combined $10 billion for the state.
Kia and Hyundai have invested heavily in electric vehicles but also boast flexible assembly lines that can make hybrids, EVs, and internal combustion vehicles with little disruption.
Record sales in June for Kia saw hybrid sales rise alongside slowdowns for its EV6 and EV9 models, the production of which was brought to Georgia last year.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp has often touted the state’s emerging ecosystem for electric mobility even as he has criticized Biden-era subsidies for picking market winners and losers.

The governor has argued that market forces should shape electric-vehicle demand and pointed out that many signature projects, including the Hyundai Meta Plant near Savannah, were announced before the passage of President Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act in August 2022.
SungEel HiMetal’s announcement came Aug. 18, two days after the IRA was signed into law.
Georgia has announced 33 clean-energy projects worth $12.8 billion since 2022, meaning it has much to lose from policies that discourage new factory development, according to advocacy group E2.
Bob Keefe, E2’s executive director, called the bill a “stunningly reckless blow to America’s economy and our environment.”

Keefe added that at a time of skyrocketing energy demand, Congress is dismantling policies driving a manufacturing renaissance in America that are critical to 90 percent of the new power being added to the grid.
SungEel’s cancellation follows Norway’s Freyr Battery pulling the plug on a $2.6 billion factory in Newnan.
Aspen Aerogels also nixed a south Georgia investment.
Nippon Denkai canceled a copper foil factory in Augusta that was largely targeted toward meeting rising demand for electric vehicle batteries.
E2’s Mr. Keefe noted these cancellations are the tip of the iceberg, stating that $15.5 billion in U.S. projects worth 12,000 jobs have been canceled since January, mostly in Republican states.

The New York Times reported in June that a $500 million Solarcycle panel recycling facility promising up to 1,200 jobs in Cedartown, announced in February 2024, was hanging in the balance due to uncertainty around tax credits.
source_title: Pullback on Korean Battery Recycling Plant Could Portend More Trouble for Georgia's Clean Energy Sector - Global Atlanta

facts: SungEel Recycling Park Georgia, LLC is a subsidiary of Korea-based SunEel HiTech Co., LLC.
The company plans to invest more than $37 Million in a new battery recycling facility at Hayestone Brady Industrial Park in Toccoa-Stephens County, Georgia.
The project will bring 104 new jobs to Stephens County, including positions for technicians, operators, administrative and sales staff, with no management positions mentioned.
Average pay for the new roles is above the county’s current average wage of $22.85 per hour.
Groundbreaking was originally scheduled for March 2023 but has been delayed while the company upgrades equipment and selects a general contractor.
A new groundbreaking date will be announced once the general contractor is selected.
The company expects to start production in December 2023.
SungEel plans to utilize North Georgia Technical College’s services for employee training and hiring.
The company fully expects to hire locally qualified individuals to fill the new positions.
Governor Brian Kemp announced the project in August 2022.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development joined Governor Kemp in welcoming the facility.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that SungEel is at the cutting edge of sustainable technology and its proximity to Georgia’s electric mobility ecosystem will lead to success.
Stephens County Development Authority (SCDA) Chairman Brian Akin noted the SCDA had been working with SungEel for eight months prior to the announcement.
SCDA President and CEO Brittany Ivey updated the timeline in September 2023 and discussed ongoing negotiations for a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU).
The MOU includes incentives, job creation commitments, investment timelines, and land purchase terms.
Unlike previous deals where land was given away, the SCDA is asking full price for the land in Hayestone Brady Industrial Park.
If the MOU remains unchanged, the facility will be placed on the tax digest within the first two years of operation.
The recycling process dismantles battery modules, heats cells to dry them, and shreds the material into battery powder that ships to Korea.
The facility will use an electric-powered dryer instead of natural gas combustion or positive pressure type vessels.
Equipment is being upgraded to vacuum-type vessels for US facilities to ensure safety.
SungEel confirmed it has used this recycling process since 2008, with recent equipment upgrades.
No incinerator will be used at the facility, and the process operates without combustion.
All materials will be recycled for beneficial reuse, with nothing going to a landfill or city wastewater system.
The Georgia facility will recover more electrolyte material compared to other proposals.
An explosion in July 2022 at SungEel HiTech’s Hungary plant was caused by faulty equipment installation by an outside vendor during test operations, not facility operation.
The process is fully compliant with Federal and State permitting regulations.
An air permit will be obtained prior to starting operation, and the application will be available to the public upon submission.
SungEel is actively working with Georgia’s Environmental Protection Division (EPD) and Department of Natural Resources (DNR) throughout the permitting process.
No PFAs will be released, and emissions will not exceed state or federal regulations.
The facility will install state-of-the-art fire suppressant systems that satisfy all building codes and insurance requirements.
Local fire personnel will conduct employee fire precaution training every six months, as required at all other SungEel facilities.
The site is not a storage facility but will have separate areas for raw materials and short-term finished goods storage.
source_title: Q&A with SungEel representatives, state and local officials - WNEG

facts: Construction of the €45 million battery recycling plant in Gera will start in March 2024.
The first of two production lines should go into operation in early 2025, the second in early 2027.
Old batteries will be dismantled, crushed and dried on site.
Materials are subsequently separated in a mechanical process to leave only raw materials that can be used for new batteries.
The joint venture between SungEel HiTech and Samsung had initially been planned for a site in the Thuringian municipality of Rudolstadt.
The initial plan in Rudolstadt failed partly due to massive local objections.
In Gera, the city council supported the project in a decisive vote only by a narrow majority.
The plant is to be built in the Gera-Cretzschwitz industrial park on an area of around 30,000 square meters.
The plant will provide 100 jobs.
The planned construction by the South Korean partners will be the second large recycling plant for electric vehicle batteries in the German state of Thuringia.
Finnish company Fortum is also building a battery recycling hub in Thuringia.
SungEel HiTech previously initiated a battery recycling joint venture within Korea with SK Innovation.
The SK Innovation JV is expected to combine SK Innovation’s independently developed lithium hydroxide recovery technology and SungEel HiTech’s nickel, cobalt and manganese recovery technology.
According to SK Innovation’s statement at the time, the JV aims to “secure a differentiated competitive advantage in the fast-growing market.”
SungEel HiTech made its stock market debut in the summer of 2022.
SungEel HiTech is the only company in Korea that recovers cobalt, nickel, manganese and copper from lithium-ion batteries in a large-scale hydrometallurgical plant.
An update dated 25 March 2025 states that the planned battery recycling plant in Gera-Cretzschwitz industrial estate between SungEel HiTech and Samsung has come to nothing.
source_title: SungEel HiTech & Samsung plan battery recycling in Germany - electrive.com

facts: South Korean battery recycler SungEel HiTech announced its first U.S. battery recycling plant in Georgia, set to go on-line in 2024.
source_title: News from SungEel HiTech, Trinity Metals and more - Resource Recycling

facts: TCI Powder Coatings is a subsidiary of RPM International Inc.
Drew Spencer has been hired as the new account manager for TCI Powder Coatings’ Ohio/Western Penn./N.Y. territory.
Spencer previously worked with Mercedes Benz.
Spencer previously served as a sales and marketing manager with ZeroBase Energy.

Spencer completed a comprehensive three-month training period in Georgia that included hands-on experience in TCI’s Operations, Product Development, Customer Service, Quality, and Field Technical Service departments.
VP of Sales Jack Bostock stated that Spencer has a unique set of skills to serve customers and advance the company's premium brand position in the powder coatings market.
source_title: TCI Powder Coatings Welcomes New Account Manager - Coatings World

facts: TCI Powder Coatings, a subsidiary of RPM International, and BASF have entered into an agreement that allows TCI to sell the BASF CathoGuard electrocoat technology to specified TCI market segments.
Jack Bostock, TCI vice president of sales and marketing, stated that the alliance builds on TCI’s commitment to market differentiation.
Bostock said pairing the CathoGuard technology series with premium powder coatings is a win-win for customers looking for performance.

The partnership aims to deliver an innovative solution meeting global standards to make a significant difference for customers’ production lines, helping them increase throughput while reducing costs.
The BASF CathoGuard coating combines unique performance qualities for parts that are more difficult to paint efficiently, including those with multiple angles and crevices.
CathoGuard improves the application process, coverage, and corrosion edge protection while lowering total material costs by nearly 20 percent.
TCI market segments with access to this technology through the new alliance include off-road and heavy equipment, general finishing, office furniture, and appliance.
CathoGuard will also be available to custom coaters that supply major subassemblies and modular components for the automotive market.
Thaddeus Lepkowski, BASF tier metals market manager, stated they are pleased that TCI can deliver their innovative product line more broadly to customers.
Lepkowski noted that the chemistry in the CathoGuard coating achieves a flawless coat on hard-to-paint parts found in many of TCI’s markets where finish quality and durability are critical.
source_title: TCI Powder Coatings, BASF Announce Strategic Alliance - Coatings World

facts: TEKLAS is opening a USD 6.5 million headquarters and manufacturing facility in Calhoun, Georgia.
The facility is scheduled to open in 2021.
The TEKLAS USA facility will span 200,000 square feet.
The facility will include assembly lines to serve OEMs.
source_title: TEKLAS to open USD 6.5 million HQ, manufacturing facility in Calhoun, Georgia in 2021 - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: Teklas Kaucuk is an International Buyer & Supplier primarily engaged in the import and export business of products such as therm, tati, forming.
The company's address is kaucuk metal tesisigosb hsandede cd. no:12141400 - gebze/kocaeli.
As of 2022-04-19, the company has completed 55 international trades.
Its main trading regions cover the United States, Russia, and Mexico.
Core products include HS846594, HS39174001, and HS8708999709.
Products are often transported via cagliari, port gebze, and port bremerhaven.
Main trading partners include thyssenkrupp automotive systems, thyssenkrupp elevator manufacturing, and teklas usa inc.

One core product consists of rubber hoses reinforced with textile materials with fittings for assembly of Volkswagen model series cars, specifically a connecting pipe of an engine cooling system with connecting fittings made of vulcanized rubber.

canonical_name: Northwest Georgia Economic & Clean Energy Corridor
facts: TEKLAS, a Turkish supplier of electric vehicle parts, invested $6.5 million in its first North American facility and headquarters.
The TEKLAS facility will be housed in a 200,000-square-foot building in Calhoun, Gordon County.
The TEKLAS facility will create more than 120 jobs.
TEKLAS produces rubber hoses and plastic tubes for automotive fluid systems for companies including General Motors, Volkswagen, and Mercedes-Benz.
GEDIA Automotive Group is building an $85 million advanced manufacturing facility in Dalton to make parts for electric vehicles.
The GEDIA facility will eventually add 200 jobs when operating at full capacity.
GEDIA is expected to start shipping products to Mercedes-Benz for a new electric crossover vehicle later in the year (relative to March 2022).
Carl Campbell, executive director of the Dalton-Whitfield County Joint Development Authority, notes significant activity and high demand from component makers and battery manufacturers in the region.
GE Appliances announced plans to invest $118 million to expand manufacturing operations at its subsidiary Roper Corp. in LaFayette, Walker County.
The GE Appliances/Roper expansion will result in the creation of more than 600 new jobs.
The investment will improve material flow and increase assembly-line efficiency by adding cutting-edge advanced manufacturing technology.
Trenton, a small industrial city at the far northwest corner of the state, saw about $15 million in expansions over the last 18 months, with anticipated wage increases of about 60% over four years.

The Development Authority of Polk County received Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) certification for phase three of the Cedartown North Business Park to enable fast-track construction.
Oldcastle APG purchased a 100,000-square-foot building and 17.35 adjacent acres in the Cedartown North Business Park last year.
Oldcastle APG will initially create 20 jobs in Polk County, growing to 45 at full capacity.
Murray County’s Appalachian Regional Port serves as a rail-connected pathway to and from the Port of Savannah, reducing truck traffic and bypassing congested seaports and Metro Atlanta.
Companies including GE Appliances, Huali Floors, and United Surface Solutions have invested millions in facilities in Murray County due to the port's location.
In calendar year 2020, the Appalachian Regional Port handled 32,816 containers, an increase of nearly 14,000 boxes (73%) compared to 2019.
Logistics and distribution facilities are growing rapidly along the I-75 corridor in Bartow County due to its location north of Metro Atlanta.
Amazon developed a Last Mile Delivery Station on Acworth’s Third Army Road in southern Bartow County.
Rome and Floyd County saw more than $810 million in capital investments adding 581 jobs in the last three years.
Ball Corp.’s aluminum cup plant opened in Rome last year, producing up to 700,000 recyclable beverage cups a day with 115 employees, and is the sole Ball cup plant in the U.S.
A second production line at the Ball Corp. facility is set to be added in the spring of 2022.
Pirelli’s Rome plant is producing the world’s first FSC-certified tire using sustainably produced rubber verified by the Forest Stewardship Council.
source_title: Northwest: A Greener Path - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that TEKLAS will invest $6.5 million in opening its first North American facility and headquarters in Gordon County.
The project will deliver 120 jobs to Calhoun.
TEKLAS is a Turkish advanced research and development, manufacturing company and supplier of electric vehicle (EV) parts.
Founded in Turkey in 1971, TEKLAS designs, develops, and manufactures fluid circulation systems for cooling and heating applications.
The company produces hoses and pipes from rubber, plastic, and metal for the global automotive industry.
TEKLAS uses highly innovative and automated manufacturing processes using advanced water molding technology.
TEKLAS USA will include assembly lines to serve prominent original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
Operations at the new facility are scheduled to start in the spring of 2021.
The 200,000-square-foot facility and headquarters will be located at 320 South Industrial Boulevard in Calhoun, Georgia.
Hiring will cover a variety of skill levels, including positions in advanced manufacturing, management, and supervision.
Regional Director Arcan Ergur stated that the Calhoun location will help the company be closer to its main customers.
Larry Roye, chairman of the Development Authority of Gordon County, applauded state and local partners for helping encourage the company's decision.
Kathy Johnson, Gordon County Chamber of Commerce President and CEO, commended TEKLAS for its research and advancements in electric vehicle manufacturing.
GDEcD Project Manager Fernanda Kirchner represented the Global Commerce division on this competitive project.
The project was executed in partnership with the Economic Development Authority of Gordon County and Georgia Power.
The Center of Innovation for Logistics, a division of GDEcD, provided freight analysis to support the project and company planning.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia is focused on attracting and growing the jobs of the future.
Wilson noted the automotive industry's transformation toward electric vehicles and e-mobility and pledged to foster an ecosystem of manufacturers and suppliers on the leading edge of the transition.
TEKLAS serves major car original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) with more than 45 years of experience in design, development, and production capabilities.
The company provides innovative solutions for thermoplastics, rubber, and metal products to its customers.
source_title: Electric Vehicle Manufacturer TEKLAS to Open North American Headquarters in Georgia - Expansion Solutions

facts: At a factory 10 minutes from the famed Augusta National Golf Club in Georgia, brightly colored golf carts are being assembled, inspected and prepped for shipment.
E-Z-GO carts come in vivid reds, blues, greens, classic whites and a subtle gold color as part of Textron Specialized Vehicles' expanding product line.
The expansion was made possible in part by the firm's decision to cut its maintenance staff and hire Advanced Technology Services (ATS) to handle that work instead.
Over the last five years, Textron Specialized Vehicles launched 40 new vehicles to market and expanded its engineering workforce by 138 percent, according to plant manager Kerri Thompson.

In July of 2003, the Textron unit decided it was not very good at doing internal maintenance required in its Augusta, Georgia plant and hired ATS to maintain and repair machines that build E-Z-GO golf carts, Cushman industrial carts, and Bad Boy Buggies all-terrain vehicles.
The production facility is 750,000 square feet.
Brian Rabe, ATS's vice president of heavy equipment industry and international operations, stated that demand for their product has been accelerating over the last five years.
Spun off from Caterpillar in 1985, ATS is looking to add 500 jobs this year to its 3,000-person workforce.
Finding workers with basic mechanical, electrical, hydraulic, and computer skills can prove challenging for ATS.
ATS developed an internal recruiting team that relies on social media, job postings, and current employees to advertise available jobs while monitoring plants being shut down.
An ATS technician will earn between $21 to $51 an hour with full benefits.
Due to high demand for skilled workers, ATS pays a premium in areas with lots of manufacturing or remote locations where attracting workers is difficult.
Over the last five years, pay has increased in these regions by 10 percent to 20 percent.
Nationally, ATS said wages have been flat because of labor slack in other parts of the country.

Jonathan Register, a 48-year-old Georgia native hired by ATS after it took over maintenance at his former employer Georgia Iron Works, noted he gets more support and training working for a firm where maintenance is one of many services rather than the only service.
Peoria, IL-based ATS assesses potential employees' skills and provides additional training to fill gaps before sending them to customer factories.
Micah Statler, head of training programs for ATS, stated they work closely with clients to understand their equipment and processes and tailor training to meet specific needs.
Training needs are met by ATS's 300 mobile training simulators that are shipped to clients' factories for in-house training.
Today, ATS supports over 100 factories in the U.S., the U.K., Mexico, and Canada for clients including Dean Foods, Georgia Pacific, and Eaton Corporation.
ATS technicians work in teams of six to 60 and typically start at a client's factory under a three-year contract, with fees depending on the type of jobs taken over.
Brian Rabe noted that reducing machine downtime from 5 percent to 1 percent allows customers to make product and profits.
source_title: This firm helps others by growing its own workforce

facts: Textron Inc. (NYSE: TXT) announced today that it has completed the acquisition of Arctic Cat Inc. by means of a short-form merger under Minnesota law.
As a result, Arctic Cat has become an indirect wholly owned subsidiary of Textron.
Going forward, Arctic Cat will operate as a subsidiary of Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc.
Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc. is based in Augusta, Ga.
Textron Specialized Vehicles designs and manufactures vehicles and equipment for a range of uses and industries.
The company's product portfolio includes E-Z-GO golf cars and personal transportation vehicles.
The company's product portfolio includes Cushman commercial utility vehicles.
The company's product portfolio includes Textron Off Road side-by-sides.
The company's product portfolio includes Dixie Chopper zero-turn mowers.
The company's product portfolio includes Jacobsen professional turf-care equipment.
The company's product portfolio includes TUG, Douglas, Premier and Safeaero ground support equipment.
The acquisition brings the Arctic Cat brand lineup of snowmobiles, side-by-sides and ATVs into the Textron Specialized Vehicles product family.
Textron president and CEO Scott Donnelly stated that Arctic Cat is an ideal fit with their growing range of off-road recreational vehicles.

The addition of Arctic Cat instantly gives Textron a deeper product line for customers, greater potential for innovation, and introduces new sales opportunities for the combined worldwide dealer network.
Arctic Cat’s operations will remain in Minnesota following the acquisition.
The business will gain valuable new capabilities through its integration with Textron Specialized Vehicles and the global resources of Textron Inc.
Textron Inc. is a $13.8 billion multi-industry business with operations in more than 25 countries.
The Arctic Cat brand designs, engineers, manufactures and markets all-terrain vehicles (ATVs), side-by-sides and snowmobiles.
Arctic Cat also markets related parts, garments and accessories under the Arctic Cat and Motorfist brand names.
Textron Specialized Vehicles markets products under the E-Z-GO, Cushman, Arctic Cat, Textron Off Road, Jacobsen, Dixie Chopper, Ransomes, TUG, Douglas, Premier and Safeaero brands.
Textron Specialized Vehicles vehicles are found in environments ranging from golf courses to factories, airports to planned communities, and theme parks to hunting preserves.
Textron Inc. is a multi-industry company that leverages its global network of aircraft, defense, industrial and finance businesses to provide customers with innovative solutions and services.

Textron is known around the world for brands such as Bell Helicopter, Cessna, Beechcraft, Hawker, Jacobsen, Kautex, Lycoming, E-Z-GO, Greenlee, Textron Off Road, Textron Systems, and TRU Simulation + Training.
source_title: Textron Inc. Announces Acquisition of Arctic Cat Inc., a Leading Company in the Recreational Vehicle Industry - ATVILLUSTRATED

facts: Textron, the owner of EZ-GO, plans to lay off 400 workers in its Textron Specialized Vehicles division nationwide.
The layoffs are expected to happen fast, by the end of this month.
Textron operates two manufacturing plants in Augusta, Georgia.
In January of 2018, Textron laid off 60 employees locally in Augusta.
A local representative told News 12 in January 2018 that the company did not anticipate any additional job cuts at that time.
The company filed a new document with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) stating it was not getting the expected return after acquiring Arctic Cat.
The SEC filing outlines restructuring changes including cutting 400 jobs, closing several branches, and closing one manufacturing facility.
This restructuring plan is expected to be mostly complete by the end of 2018.
Textron previously invested $40 million in Augusta when it purchased an old Proctor and Gamble plant on the corner of Marvin Griffin Road and Mike Padgett Highway in 2016.
The 2016 Augusta expansion brought 400 jobs to the site.
The SEC filing states the company expects to pay out up to $85 million in closing costs, including at least $10 million in severance.
Textron Specialized Vehicles maintains locations all over the world.
Local representatives stated they cannot comment beyond what is included in the SEC filing.
The company told News 12: "Unfortunately, I can’t offer any comment beyond what is included in the SEC filing at this time. We will share more info when we can."
It remains unclear whether any jobs in the Augusta area will be affected or if the Augusta plants themselves are in jeopardy.
source_title: UPDATE: Textron Specialized Vehicles planning to layoff 400, close one manufacturing facility

facts: E-Z-GO vehicles are designed and manufactured by Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., a Textron Inc. company.
E-Z-GO produces the EliTE series of electric vehicles powered by Samsung SDI lithium-ion technology.
Gunnar Kleveland serves as President and CEO of Textron Specialized Vehicles.
E-Z-GO was founded in Augusta, Ga. in 1954.
E-Z-GO is globally renowned for its innovation in sustainable electric-vehicle and powertrain technology.
E-Z-GO models include RXV and TXT fleet golf cars; Liberty, Freedom and Valor personal golf cars; and E-Z-GO Express personal utility vehicles.
E-Z-GO became part of Textron Inc. in 1960 and currently operates as part of Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc.

The employees of Textron Specialized Vehicles are committed to a culture of compliance and pollution prevention, aiming to sustain premier status by integrating environmental performance into their business processes.
E-Z-GO partnered with Rollick to utilize the RollickDR digital engagement module to enable consumers to complete more of their transaction online.
E-Z-GO added Rollick’s Inventory Tools to its Aimbase lead management solution powered by Rollick prior to January 2022.

facts: Augusta, Georgia-based Textron Specialized Vehicles has recalled about 34,000 E-Z-GO RXV Personal Transportation Vehicles (PTV) or golf carts due to injury and crash hazards.
The PTV's steering knuckles and A-arms can break during or after an impact and cause loss of steering control, posing crash and injury hazards.
The company received 335 reports of incidents of the steering knuckles and A-arms on the PTVs breaking, including one incident that resulted in two reports of abrasion injuries.
The recall includes model year 2022 and 2023 Freedom and Valor E-Z-GO RXV personal transportation vehicles.
Recalled units have serial numbers within the range of 5656874 through 5656886 and 5659215 through 5700741.
The company has asked its customers to immediately stop using the recalled PTVs and contact Textron Specialized Vehicles for a free repair.
The recalled golf carts were sold at dealerships nationwide and online from January 2022 through September 2022 for between $10,000 and $17,000.
source_title: Textron Specialized Vehicles Recalls 34K Golf Carts

facts: Textron Specialized Vehicles, the manufacturer of the recalled ATVs, is located in Augusta, Georgia.
The recall involves model years 2022 and 2023 Arctic Cat Alterra 600 and Tracker 600 all-terrain vehicles (ATVs).
Approximately 3,800 units were sold in the United States, with an additional 1,200 sold in Canada.
The recalled ATVs were manufactured in the United States.
Arctic Cat Alterra 600 ATVs were sold at dealers nationwide from May 2021 through January 2023 for between $9,200 and $11,600.
Tracker 600 ATVs were sold at Bass Pro Shops, Cabela’s, and Tracker dealers nationwide from May 2021 through January 2023 for between $7,700 and $9,900.
The vehicles pose a crash hazard because the A-arm can separate from the frame, causing loss of steering control.
Consumers are instructed to immediately stop using the recalled ATVs and contact Textron Specialized Vehicles to arrange for a free inspection and repair.
Dealers will inspect the main frame A-arm mount brackets for cracks before adding a support bracket, and replace the main frame weldment along with strengthening brackets if cracking is found.
The recall was announced on March 23, 2023, under recall number +23-739.
Arctic Cat and Tracker ATVs were previously recalled in July 2022.
No incidents or injuries have been reported related to this recall.
Recalled VIN ranges for 2022 and 2023 Arctic Cat Alterra 600 models include NT200101 to NT202064 and PT200101 to PT200679.
Recalled VIN ranges for 2022 and 2023 Tracker 600 models include NT200101 to NT201929 and PT200101 to PT200697.
The Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) is located on the front right-hand frame tube beside the right-hand shock.

links.context: Recall information page for Arctic Cat owners
Recall information page for Tracker off-road vehicles
Toll-free consumer contact line (8 a.m. to 5 p.m. ET, Monday through Friday)

source_title: Textron Specialized Vehicles Recalls Arctic Cat Alterra 600 and Tracker 600 All-Terrain Vehicles (ATVs) Due to Crash Hazard - Motor Sports NewsWire

facts: Ajin Georgia is a stamped-metal auto body parts manufacturer building a factory in Bulloch County’s Bruce Yawn Commerce Park.
The facility will serve as a supplier to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
During a luncheon, Ajin Georgia presented a total of $40,000 in donations to 12 local community service, public safety, and educational organizations.
The project was originally announced in November 2022 under the name Joon Georgia plant.
The plant under construction operates under the name Ajin Georgia as part of the South Korea-based Ajin Corporation.
The company is committed to creating more than 600 jobs.
The project was initially announced last year as a $317 million investment.
The projected cost of building and equipping the facility has been revised to $278 million.
The plant will be 853,000-square-foot.
Ajin executives stated that October is now the target month for the factory to begin production.
Hiring for production jobs is expected to begin in the early part of 2024.
Jamie Calloway serves as the Human Resources General Manager for Ajin Georgia and acted as master of ceremonies at the luncheon.
Senior Human Resources Manager Jaewook “Jake” Jeong was present at the luncheon.
Five other Ajin Georgia executives and managers originally from Korea were introduced at the event.
Ajin USA CEO Jung-Ho Sea toured the plant site the week of Thanksgiving.
Each of the 12 recipient organizations received a check (amounts varied) and a small “Ajin Georgia” sign for display on their premises.
Public safety recipients included the Bulloch County Fire Department, Bulloch County Sheriff’s Office, and Bulloch County Emergency Medical Service.

Community service recipients included the Statesboro Kiwanis Club, Safe Haven (Citizens Against Violence shelter), the Boys & Girls Club of Bulloch County, the Bulloch County Recreation and Parks Department, and the city of Statesboro’s Village Builders youth initiative.
Education recipients included Bulloch County Schools, Evans Region College and Career Academy (based in Claxton), Metter College and Career Academy, and the Ogeechee Technical College Foundation.
Aerial photos from October showed the factory buildings taking shape behind the TA Travel Center off U.S. Highway 301 at the I-16 interchange.
A video was shown highlighting operations at the established Ajin USA plant in Cusseta, Alabama, which has been in production since 2009.
The new Georgia facility will use blanking presses, stamping presses, and die cutters to shape metal into parts that comprise much of the framework of passenger vehicles.
Parts-handling robots will be used at almost all of the work stations in the new facility.

Ajin Georgia is one of three plants being built in Bulloch County by companies that will supply different kinds of components to Hyundai Motor Group's Metaplant electric vehicle and battery manufacturing complex under construction in northern Bryan County.
As of December 7, job listings showed seven managerial and administrative positions available, with no production jobs listed yet.
source_title: Ajin gives $40K to community, school and public safety agencies here while factory is being built - Statesboro Herald

facts: AlbaForm Inc. officially opened its doors September 23rd at a ribbon cutting for a new Hall County manufacturing plant.
The Czech Republic based manufacturer makes metal components for the automotive industry.
The Flowery Branch facility is AlbaForm's first plant in the United States.
Currently the Hall County location employs 16 people but is expecting to hire an additional 40 employees, primarily computer programmers, during the next year.
According to the Hall County Chamber of Commerce, the county is now home to more than 40 international companies.

facts: Anovion Technologies will make Decatur County, Georgia, the location for its latest manufacturing facility.
The 1,500,000-square-foot plant will produce 40,000 metric tonnes per annum of synthetic graphite anode material for lithium-ion batteries.
According to the company, Decatur County was selected because of its abundance of low- and carbon-free energy sources, existing infrastructure, and pipeline of skilled workers.
Anovion’s $800 million investment will create over 400 jobs.

Eric Stopka, Chief Executive Officer of Anovion, said the company conducted a thorough search to identify a location that addressed energy, transportation, logistics, and human capital needs to ensure an ecosystem for producing the highest quality and sustainable synthetic graphite anode material on the market today.

Stopka stated that existing infrastructure makes Southwest Georgia an attractive location for Anovion’s new facility and that proximity to other battery and EV manufacturing plants will allow them to further reduce their carbon footprint.
source_title: Anovion Technologies invests $800 million to build new synthetic graphite anode materials manufacturing plant in Georgia | Plant Services

facts: Anovion Technologies is trying to become a major American-based supplier of graphite for electric batteries.
The company announced it will invest $800 million to build a factory in southwest Georgia.
The company plans to hire 400 workers.
Anovion Technologies is based in Chicago.
The company will make synthetic graphite anode in Bainbridge.
Production of the key ingredient for lithium ion batteries is supposed to start in 2025.
The plant builds on Georgia’s push to recruit makers of electric vehicles and their suppliers.
Republican Gov. Brian Kemp said the announcement furthers his goal of making Georgia the nation’s electric mobility capital.
Most battery graphite is currently made outside the United States.
Georgia economic development officials say more than 40 electric vehicle-related projects have been announced in Georgia since 2020, pledging $22 billion of investment and 28,000 jobs.
Anovion was created last year by combining the graphite businesses of two existing companies, Pyrotek and Amsted Graphite Materials.
The company received new investment from Monomyth Group, a private equity firm.
Anovion has existing facilities in Sanborn, New York, and Clarksburg, West Virginia.
The company earlier announced plans to build a graphite factory with a 35,000-ton yearly capacity near Muscle Shoals in northwest Alabama.
Anovion won a $117 million grant under the new federal infrastructure law last year to help finance the new Alabama plant, as well as improvements at its existing New York plant.
Anovion CEO Eric Stopka said the company was attracted by Georgia’s workforce, research universities and growing solar energy production.
Stopka stated: “We are looking forward to providing solutions that will further secure the country’s electrified future with the support of our partners in Georgia and the federal government.”
Anovion executives have said they are trying to reach 150,000 tons of production in coming years, starting from almost nothing.
Synthetic graphite is made from carbon products like petroleum coke, a byproduct of oil refining, heating it up to super-high temperatures for long periods of time to refine it.
Such furnaces usually require huge amounts of electricity, making power costs a key consideration in locating factories.

Anovion could claim various tax breaks including a state income tax credit allowing it to annually deduct $3,000 per job from state income taxes for five years, as long as workers make at least $31,300 a year.
That tax credit could be worth $6 million.
The company could also qualify for property tax breaks from Bainbridge and Decatur County.
Monday’s announcement is the second big industrial win in two years for Bainbridge, located in the far southwest corner of the state.
Danimer Scientific, a maker of biodegradable plastic, announced in 2021 that it would build a $700 million factory, hiring 400 employees.
Decatur County Commission Chairman Pete Stephens said: “We have always known that in order to remain competitive and to grow, we needed to diversify our industry mix.”
source_title: Anovion Technologies plans to build $800M factory in southwest Georgia | Chicago Financial Times

facts: Anovion Technologies is a supplier of premium synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries.
Anovion Technologies will build a new manufacturing facility in Bainbridge, Georgia.
The project involves an $800 million investment.
The project will bring over 400 jobs to Decatur County.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp welcomed Anovion to Georgia, stating the state is fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that job creators are recognizing every corner of Georgia has the resources and workforce needed for success.
Governor Brian Kemp expressed looking forward to celebrating the partnership with the hardworking Georgians of Decatur County after meeting their team personally.
The new facility will be 1,500,000 square feet in size.
The facility will be located at Downrange Industrial Park in Bainbridge.
The facility will be the first of its size and scale in North America.
The facility will produce synthetic graphite for use in batteries that power EVs, electric storage systems, electronics, military applications, and other products.
Bainbridge Mayor Edward Reynolds welcomed Anovion to the city.
Mayor Edward Reynolds highlighted the $800 million investment and the creation of over 400 high-paying, quality jobs as a huge win for the community.
Mayor Edward Reynolds stated that the local workforce is ready to embrace the opportunity and expressed confidence in shared prosperity.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in late 2025.
The company will be hiring for technical, production, and engineering roles, along with administrative and management positions.
