facts: OTR’s product portfolio includes tires, wheels, assemblies, tracks, tubes, ballast and more.

The tire lineup comprises over 1,700 models and 300+ distinctive treads, with bias and radial pneumatic (directional and non-directional); semi-pneumatic; solid (directional and non-directional); non-marking; and airless.
The portfolio includes over 1,000 wheels, with single- and multi-piece versions in steel and aluminum, as well as cast disc units.
There are over 100,000 standard items available, many of which can be customized for unique applications.
In 1987, OTR Wheel Engineering was founded in Rome, Georgia by Fred B. Taylor.
OTR has been a long-standing preferred supplier to AGCO since 1997 and an instrumental part of their supply chain to grow their AG equipment business in North America.
In 2020, AGCO introduced OTR as its wheel and tire supplier for its cornerstone small HP tractor range.
OTR has supported Honda since 2016 and expanded its scope in 2019 to include mounting for Honda's Side by Side lines.
OTR wheels are used on CAT 795 haul trucks at Nevada Gold Mines mine sites, specifically running 7 piece Speed Wheels and 5 piece standard wheels.
Aros Chile has worked with OTR Wheel Engineering for more than 15 years as an essential supplier in the mining wheel category, manufacturing wheels for CAT Haulers and Loaders.
DCML uses OTR products on Komatsu 830 E dump trucks at Vale Mining Carajas and CAT 777 haul trucks at Vale Mining South, noting that product quality ranks among the very best in the industry.
In 1995, a facility was opened in Chambersburg, Pennsylvania for first major OEM customer JLG, launching a complete line of aerial work platform tires.
In 1996, OTR Canada was formed via acquisition of the business Northshore.
In 1997, Silverstone was acquired and is based in Omaha, Nebraska.
In 1997, a facility was opened in Ackerman, Mississippi for OEM customer Taylor Machine Works.
In 1999, OTR Wheel in Quincy, Illinois was acquired and folded into OTR Wheel Engineering, providing additional manufacturing capability.
In 2005, OTR China was established in Wuxi.
In 2005, a facility was opened in Shady Grove, Pennsylvania to support Grove International.
In 2005, the company moved to a new facility in Louisville, Mississippi.
In 2007, OTR Europe was established in Derby.
In 2009, OTR Thailand was established in Chon Buri.
In 2011, OTR Sri Lanka was established in Colombo.
In 2013, Long’s Wheel & Rim was acquired.
In 2015, SolidBoss was acquired.
In 2016, the Rome facility was expanded to include new corporate headquarters.
In 2017, Southern Wheel & Rim was acquired and OTR International was formed; Silver Diamond Services (SDS) in Mansfield, TX and Trans American Rubber (TAR) in Denver, CO were also acquired.
In 2018, Fieldens OTR was acquired and established in Suffolk, U.K.
In 2019, a partnership was established with Owners Resource Group with OTR configured as a member company.
In 2019, OTR Germany was established in Sande, located near Wilhelmshaven in northeast Germany.
In 2020, OTR Europe expanded to include operations in the Czech Republic.
In 2020, SolidBoss OTR U.S. East operations were established.
In 2021, Silverstone and its businesses, SDS and Trans American Rubber, were fully integrated into OTR, forming the basis for OTR U.S. (with SolidBoss included).
In 2021, Tom Rizzi became the new CEO and president of OTR.
source_title: OTR Wheel Engineering, Inc. - About OTR

facts: OTR announced the appointment of Oscar Torres as President and Chief Executive Officer.
The leadership transition is effective January 5, 2026.
The organizational announcement was issued from Rome, GA.
Oscar Torres brings more than 25 years of executive leadership experience in private equity–backed organizations.
He has deep expertise in the aerospace aftermarket.
He spent the majority of his career at Kellstrom Aerospace, a global distributor and technical services provider.
At Kellstrom, he advanced through senior leadership roles including Chief Financial Officer, Chief Operating Officer, and Chief Executive Officer.
During his tenure at Kellstrom, he led initiatives that strengthened operational performance and drove sustainable growth for the company and its business partners.
He played a key role in multiple strategic acquisitions and divestitures.
He helped position Kellstrom as a leading global supply-chain solutions provider serving airlines, OEMs, MROs, and aircraft lessors worldwide.

Hector Ramirez, Interim President of OTR, stated that Oscar’s proven leadership, collaborative approach, and deep industry knowledge make him exceptionally well suited to lead OTR into its next phase of growth.
Hector Ramirez expressed excitement about welcoming Oscar and confidence in the value he will bring to customers, partners, and stakeholders.
Oscar Torres is a Certified Public Accountant.
He holds a Bachelor of Accounting from Florida International University.
He holds an MBA from the University of Miami.
source_title: OTR ORGANIZATIONAL ANNOUNCEMENT: NEW CEO INTRODUCTION | OTR Engineered Solutions

facts: PAI Industries partnered with ECM to bring heat treatment in-house with a NANO vacuum furnace system.
Adding the in-house system expands PAI’s capabilities to expedite production and increase capacity.
PAI operates an already impressive 112,000ft2 manufacturing facility with state-of-the-art machinery in Suwanee, GA.
Prior to project acceptance, PAI relied on exemplary outsourced heat treating.
Transitioning from primarily out-sourced heat treatment to in-house vacuum technology allows for low pressure carburizing and through-hardening 24/7.
The ECM NANO system features modularity, an external loader, a 3-position temper oven, and queuing positions.
Additional benefits provided by the vacuum technology include zero CO2 furnace emissions, no fire hazards or open flames, increased repeatability, and improved metallurgical results.
Testing was conducted in the ECM NANO located in the ECM USA Synergy Center in Pleasant Prairie, WI to validate improved metallurgical properties prior to project acceptance.
PAI Industries manufactures and distributes quality service parts to the heavy-duty truck industry.
PAI has been supplying distributors throughout the world for over 50 years.
ECM Technologies is an innovative low pressure vacuum furnace manufacturer with headquarters in Grenoble, France.
ECM USA is located in Pleasant Prairie, Wisconsin.
ECM serves industries including automotive, aerospace, nuclear, energy, electronic, induction, and 3D additive manufacturing.
ECM provides service capabilities including advanced automation, robotics, after sales support, spare parts, and on-site training.

facts: PLI (Pak-Lite) was founded in Atlanta, Georgia in February 1972 by Frank M Pritchard.
The company began as a manufacturer of protective packaging products and in May of 1983 opened its second manufacturing facility in Mebane, NC.
In 1985, the leadership team at Pak-Lite consisting of David M. Pritchard, Stanley J. Beard and William H. Beard purchased the company from Frank Pritchard.
David and William still remain the owners of PLI and they continue to operate the company with an emphasis on simplicity and the basic principle of quality above all.
PLI (Pak-Lite, Inc.) is a leading North American converter/manufacturer of foams, nonwovens, foils, films, specialty materials, and fabrics.
The company specializes in various markets including automotive, marine, HVAC, flooring, construction, packaging, transportation, and manufacturing.
PLI's customer base includes many of the top OEM and industrial companies in the world.
Manufacturing facilities are located in Georgia, North Carolina, and Taiwan with a variety of distribution affiliates on both the East and West Coasts of the United States and Canada.
The company has over 300+ dedicated professionals committed to making projects a success.
In July 2004, PLI opened its 3rd state of the art facility in Suwanee, Ga., and moved its corporate offices to that location.
Recent purchases of skiving, cam cutting, laminating, and spooling equipment have greatly strengthened PLI's commitment and value to customers.
The company's most recent state-of-the-art flooring plant opened in April 2023.
The company has enjoyed sales growth in the past years and continues to grow at an exceptional level to this day.

facts: The automotive industry in Virginia is diverse and thriving, with companies operating in multiple niches from automotive component manufacture to innovative materials technology.
These companies range from subsidiaries of global leaders to unique specialists.
Each company plays a critical role in engineering and producing essential components that constitute a functioning vehicle.
Components may include items such as ball joints for suspension and steering systems, or utilizing ultra-light, strong materials to create fuel-efficient, environmentally friendly vehicles.
As the automotive industry moves towards a more sustainable future, these Virginia-based companies are at the forefront of innovation and development.
Somic America Inc. is headquartered in Wytheville, Virginia, founded in 1916, with a headcount of 201-500.
Somic America is a subsidiary of Somic Ishikawa, a global leader in design, development, and manufacture of automotive components.
Somic America specializes in the engineering, design, and manufacturing of quality ball joints for suspension and steering systems, and rotary dampers for seat and tailgate applications.

They offer a range of services including prototyping, PCB assembly, testing, box build and final assembly.
They focus on providing solutions tailored to their customers' needs and offer supply chain management, program management, and engineering support.

facts: PPG Industries is a global supplier of paints, coatings and specialty materials that serves industrial, transportation, consumer and construction markets.

Founded in 1883 as the Pittsburgh Plate Glass Company, PPG has evolved from its origins in glass manufacturing into a diversified coatings and materials company headquartered in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
The company develops and manufactures a broad array of products used to protect and enhance surfaces, from consumer paints to highly engineered coatings for demanding industrial applications.

PPG’s product portfolio includes architectural and decorative paints, automotive original equipment and refinish coatings, industrial coatings for machinery and equipment, protective and marine coatings, aerospace and defense coatings, and packaging coatings and materials.
The company provides color and design services, technical support, application systems and coatings-related services for manufacturers, distributors, contractors and retailers.
Its operations encompass research and development centers, manufacturing facilities and distribution networks aimed at delivering tailored coating solutions and color consistency for customers.
PPG operates on a global scale, with manufacturing, sales and technical support spanning the Americas, Europe, Asia, the Middle East and Africa.
The company sells to a wide range of end markets including automotive, aerospace, marine, industrial manufacturers, commercial builders and retail consumers.
As of mid-2024, PPG is led by Chief Executive Officer Michael H. McGarry.
Under CEO Michael H. McGarry's leadership, the company has emphasized product innovation and operational performance.

Strategically, PPG places emphasis on innovation, sustainability and application expertise, investing in new coating chemistries, color technologies and formulation improvements that address durability, appearance and environmental regulations.

Its offerings target performance and lifecycle benefits such as corrosion resistance, chemical and UV protection, weight reduction opportunities in transportation, and efficient application systems for contractor and industrial customers.
The company’s combination of global scale, R&D capability and diverse end-market exposure positions it as a major provider of coatings and specialty materials worldwide.
source_title: PPG Industries (PPG) Stock Price, News & Analysis

facts: Last year, electric vehicle startup Rivian Automotive had a proposal for the state of Georgia.
source_title: Will Rivian's big factory help swing Georgia to direct sales? - Crain's Chicago Business

facts: Rivian is building a new plant east of Atlanta, Georgia, which will come online to expand R2 sales to other regions after production at the Normal, Illinois plant ramps up to full speed.
The midsize electric SUV R2 is expected to bring international scale to the EV brand and is designed to meet global regulations.
Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe has promised a $45,000 base price for the R2, though destination charges will add $1,495 to the cost.
The Standard entry model is scheduled for late 2027 as a 2028 model, with potential inflation possibly increasing the price by launch.
The base RWD model features a single 350-hp, 355-lb-ft motor mounted in back, achieves 0-60 mph in 5.9 seconds, and includes a standard-range battery providing over 275 miles of range.
The Standard Long Range RWD will arrive in the first half of 2027 with an 87.9-kWh battery pack for up to 345 miles of range at a cost of $49,985.

Standard R2 trims include a 525-watt five-speaker audio system, power heated front seats and steering wheel, four drive modes (All-Purpose, Conserve, Snow, Sport), 19-inch wheels, and only a Black Crater interior.

Optional equipment for Standard models includes 20-inch wheels, a spare tire, an Autonomy+ L2+ hands-off/eyes-on cruise control subscription, and an upgrade to the Premium model’s dual-motor AWD setup (450-hp, 537-lb-ft).
The R2 Premium trim opens at $55,485, arrives in late 2026 as a 2027 model, and comes standard with a dual-motor AWD setup (450 hp, 537 lb-ft) achieving 0-60 mph in 4.6 seconds.

The Premium trim uses a long-range 87.9-kWh battery pack for up to 330 miles of range and includes standard upgrades like 20-inch wheels, tow hooks, All-Terrain drive mode, a premium interior, 975-watt nine-speaker audio, matrix-LED adaptive headlights, a Rivian Torch flashlight, and rear glass that motors down into the hatchback.
Premium options include an Autonomy+ subscription, spare tire, 21-inch wheels, a towing package, and a Performance upgrade.
The R2 pricing straddles America’s average transaction price.

Transitioning from the R1’s body-on-skateboard architecture to the R2’s unitized construction with the battery as the structural floor pan makes adding a pickup bed exponentially harder, resulting in no truck variant.
source_title: 2027 Rivian R2: Pricing, Trims, Range, and Specs Revealed

facts: A massive new factory for electric trucks is coming to Georgia.
The factory will be five times larger than the Pentagon.
It is expected to produce 400,000 emissions-free trucks per year.
Rivian will invest $5 billion in the project.
Rivian hopes to jump-start the transition to a cleaner energy future.
Rivian hopes to create new jobs in Georgia.
Georgia’s government has stepped up efforts to help the EV industry grow.
Tesla has opened a second service center in recent months in Atlanta.
Emory and Georgia Tech are supporting the growing electric vehicle market.
Companies like Coca-Cola and UPS are building charging stations for their workers.
Nonprofits play a critical role in educating consumers about alternative fuel technology.
Rivian is an EV startup backed by Amazon.
Rivian is planning to build a massive manufacturing facility near Atlanta.
The plant is expected to create thousands of jobs and cost $5 billion.
Rivian’s factory is expected to produce 400,000 electric trucks a year.
Rivian has announced plans to build a second factory in Georgia where it will assemble up to 400,000 electric trucks a year.
The plant will be located in Morgan and Walton counties about 45 miles east of Atlanta.
It is estimated to cost $5 billion to build and hopes to open in 2024.
The new factory will be one of the largest economic development deals to occur in Georgia in a decade.
The state has actively sought the company and state officials have been working to make the investment a reality.
The factory is expected to employ 7,500 people.
President Trump and Vice President Biden have both announced multi-billion-dollar plans to build EV charging stations and batteries.
Several companies are vying for the coveted jobs including Hyundai which is considering Georgia for an electric vehicle manufacturing plant.
Rivian has secured a five-billion-dollar investment in site development and manufacturing facilities in Morgan and Walton counties.
The company is expected to hire at least 7,500 people at the plant according to an executive.
The company plans to begin production in 2024.
The state of Georgia and local governments are providing incentives to attract the company.
The company will build a $5 billion electric vehicle assembly plant on 2,000 acres in Morgan and Walton counties.
It is expected to create thousands of jobs with an average salary of $56,000.
The campus will be located in Stanton Springs a community north of Atlanta with abundant natural space surrounding the site.
It will also be located along the I-20 corridor making it easy to reach the city and other resources.
Officials say the factory is the largest economic development project in the history of the state.
Some residents are concerned about the impact on the environment traffic and urban sprawl.
The company says it will follow local ordinances and take all necessary steps to mitigate the effects on the area.
The company has stated that it intends to build 20 million square feet of buildings on 2,000 acres of land in Morgan County.
The plant will be three times the size of Disneyland and four times the size of Vatican City.
Rivian is building an assembly and battery plant near Interstate 20.
While the company has not yet revealed the exact location it has acquired some property in Stanton Grove.
The property is owned by the Joint Development Authority of Jasper Newton Morgan and Walton Counties which made the deal possible.
The company’s purchase price was not disclosed and an attorney with the Joint Development Authority declined to comment on the news.
The Georgia government is trying to lure the next generation of electric trucks with incentives that are aimed at making the electric vehicle industry more competitive.
The incentive package is expected to be worth hundreds of millions of dollars and includes infrastructure improvements training and job creation.
Rivian has negotiated with state and local officials in secret.
Rivian says it plans to build a $5 billion factory in Decatur Georgia employing around 7,500 people.
It has been called the biggest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Rivian is expected to break ground within the next few months.
The company has not shared a date for community meetings and one community meeting in the area was cancelled after the residents left frustrated.
This factory will generate a billion-dollar industry for electric vehicles in Georgia.
It will also create about 7,500 new green jobs.
The state hopes that this new plant will accelerate the transition away from fossil fuels.
Rivian Inc. is putting up a $5 billion plant on 2,000 acres east of Atlanta.
The company has been searching for a location for a year and announced its plans on December 16.
The site is called Stanton Springs North and will create about 7,500 new jobs.
Rivian has partnered with Amazon to build its second factory.
Its decision is expected to prompt the state to relax its laws regarding automaker sales.
It will begin construction on the $5 billion facility in the summer of 2022 and is aiming to start building vehicles in 2024.
Rivian’s investment in Georgia has raised concerns about its long-term viability.
The company was given government incentives to build the factory which was valued at $150 million.
Rivian’s plans have also been criticized by local residents because of the high cost of property.
Despite its initial investment Georgia is expected to attract more electric car manufacturers and suppliers.
The Rivian Electric Truck features four electric motors a range of over 300 miles and is capable of lightning-quick acceleration.
Rivian has been steering away from the more common vans and sports cars and focusing on trucks.
As of the summer of 2016 Amazon owned about 12% of Rivian and plans to invest $700 million in the company.
Rivian’s biggest shareholder is Amazon which has placed an order for over 100,000 of its electric delivery trucks.
The company is committed to becoming carbon neutral by 2040.
Ford also invested in Rivian in 2019 as part of a strategic partnership.
The companies planned to co-develop an electric truck but the plans fell apart and the two companies instead focused on developing an electric F-150 Lightning.
The companies have similar timelines and spec sheets and are expected to start shipping the electric F-150 Lightning to customers in early May.
Last quarter Rivian Automotive Inc. posted a huge loss of $1.5 billion and negative gross margins.
Rivian has been struggling to meet demand as it scales up its production.
The company’s production numbers are less than half of what they hoped for this year.
It’s not clear if the company will reach their target of fifty thousand cars by the end of next year.
Rivian recently announced plans to build 25,000 electric vehicles this year and another 25,000 by 2022.
It had previously lowered guidance in March and delivered fewer than 4,000 vehicles in the first quarter.
Ford owns 11.4% of Rivian.
It’s not clear whether Amazon will sell its shares.
source_title: How Electric Truck Became Georgia? [Answered 2026] | Prettymotors

facts: Rivian Automotive shares rose 11% in early trading on July 2 to $19 and change.
Rivian released Q2 2026 production and delivery figures before market open.
Rivian produced 12,613 vehicles in the quarter ended June 30.
Rivian delivered 12,194 vehicles in the quarter ended June 30.
The Q2 delivery figure topped Rivian's own delivery outlook of 9,000 to 11,000 vehicles.
Quarter-over-quarter growth occurred in EDV and R1 models during the quarter.
Rivian introduced R2 deliveries during the quarter ended June 30.
Rivian raised its full-year 2026 delivery guidance to 65,000 to 70,000 vehicles.
The new full-year 2026 delivery guidance is up from a previous range of 62,000 to 67,000 vehicles.
Management cites the R2 ramp as the key growth and demand driver for the lower-priced SUV.
Rivian delivered 10,365 units in Q1 2026.
Rivian reported $1.38 billion in revenue for Q1 2026.
Rivian's adjusted EPS was -$0.54 for Q1 2026, beating the consensus of -$0.7162.
Software and Services revenue at Rivian jumped 49% year over year to $473 million.
The Software and Services revenue growth was aided by a Volkswagen joint venture.
Rivian received a $1 billion equity infusion from Volkswagen.
Rivian has secured a $4.5 billion Department of Energy loan for its Georgia facility.
Rivian expects a $300 million initial Uber equity investment in Q2 2026 tied to the R2 robotaxi program.
Rivian will report its Q2 2026 financial results on July 30 after market close.
The margin picture for Q2 deliveries is expected to be critical due to a mix shift toward R2 and continued cash burn.
The bull case for Rivian points to genuine volume growth and potential operating leverage as the Normal, Illinois plant scales.
There were 28 recent RIVN insider transactions netting to buying.
Analyst ratings for Rivian tilt positive with 11 buys, 10 holds, and 5 sells.
Rivian remains unprofitable and burned through $1.08 billion in free cash flow in Q1 2026 alone.
Rivian faces meaningful R2 execution risk in a choppy EV demand environment.
Rivian stock was down 13% year to date heading into July 2.

facts: Electric vehicle stocks retreated in the week ending May 10 amid profit taking, with earnings reports weighing down several stocks.
Tesla executive Rich Otto, head of product, departed after a seven-year stint, citing that recent layoffs rocking the company and its morale have thrown harmony out of balance.
Tesla removed most job postings from its website, with North American sites listing just three open manufacturing jobs as of May 1 (down from 3,400 openings in the U.S., Mexico, and Canada).
Tesla nearly disbanded its Supercharger network team but may expand the network according to CEO Elon Musk.
Elon Musk stated Tesla will spend well over $500M expanding the Supercharger network to create thousands of new chargers this year.
Musk noted that the $500M expansion cost covers only new sites and expansions, not counting operations costs which are much higher.
Tesla shipped 62,617 made-in-China EVs in April, down 18% year-over-year according to China Passenger Car Association data.
Of the April shipments, 30,746 units were exports while domestic sales totaled 31,421 units, marking a decrease of approximately 50% from the previous month and over 21% compared to last year.
Tesla sent former China head Tom Zhu back to China as it struggles with sagging sales amid competitive pressure.
Tom Zhu was promoted to Tesla’s top leadership team last year and was stationed in Austin before his return to China.
Rivian reported first-quarter revenue that exceeded estimates but widened loss per share compared to a year ago.
Rivian maintained its 2024 production forecast of 57,000 units.
Rivian CFO Claire McDonough stated the 2024 capital expenditure guidance was reduced by $550 million to $1.2 billion due to moving the first R2 production line to the Normal, Illinois plant.
DigiTimes reported Apple is assessing a partnership with U.S. EV startup Rivian, though neither company clarified the rumor.
Fisker's Austrian subsidiary Fisker GmbH voluntarily filed for bankruptcy protection on Tuesday, clarifying no other Fisker entities are included in the restructuring.
NHTSA opened another investigation into potential issues with the Fisker Ocean electric SUV's automatic emergency braking.
The Biden administration plans to announce tariffs on Chinese imports in strategic sectors including EVs to protect the domestic EV industry from competition.
The U.S. will maintain existing tariffs on many Chinese goods set by former President Donald Trump while adding new tariffs to semiconductors and solar equipment.
The government is reportedly planning to hike EV tariffs, which are likely to quadruple.
Ford Motor Co. reversed its plan to fully transition to battery EVs in Europe by 2030 due to softer-than-expected demand for pureplay EVs.

Martin Sander, head of Ford’s passenger cars business in Europe, stated the company will continue selling internal combustion engine cars beyond 2030 if customers demand them and will focus on plug-in hybrids.
The KraneShares Electric Vehicles and Future Mobility Index ETF (KARS) ended Friday down 1.87% at $22.14, falling 2.55% for the week.

EV stock performances for the week included Tesla (-7.02%), Nio (-8.98%), XPeng (-14.68%), Li Auto (-4.68%), Fisker (-32.69%), Workhorse Group (+12.50%), Hyzon Motors (-12.50%), Canoo (-7.58%), Rivian (-0.79%), Lucid Group (-4.49%), Faraday Future (+12.71%), Nikola Corp. (-18.33%), and VinFast Auto (+4.15%).
source_title: Tesla's Ongoing Austerity Measures, Rivian-Apple Partnership Buzz, Biden Administration's Clampdown: Biggest EV Stories Of The Week — TradingView News

facts: Shares of Rivian Automotive jumped to $18.63, up 8.44%.
The stock rose after second-quarter deliveries beat its target and management lifted full-year guidance.
Investors will be watching R2 SUV deliveries and 2026 delivery momentum next.
Trading volume reached 77.1 million shares, coming in about 155% above its three-month average of 30.2 million shares.
Rivian Automotive IPO'd in 2021 and has fallen 82% since going public.
Rivian Automotive raised its 2026 delivery guidance to 65,000 to 70,000 vehicles after reporting 12,194 deliveries in the second quarter.
The EV maker had previously told investors it expected to deliver between 62,000 and 67,000 units this year.
The company announced that it will release its second-quarter 2026 financial results on July 30.
Stronger-than-expected second-quarter demand and the launch of Rivian’s R2 SUV deliveries supported the higher outlook.
Investors who hope to see Rivian shares increase in value should be laser-focused on what the company says about production and, especially, demand for the new R2.
source_title: Stock Market Today, July 2: Rivian Jumps on Raised 2026 Delivery Guidance | The Motley Fool

facts: Rivian is expanding plans for its second U.S. production facility in Georgia, increasing the site’s planned annual production capacity to 300,000 vehicles from an earlier target of 200,000.

The California-based company partners with Volkswagen and currently operates a manufacturing plant in Normal, Illinois that produces the EDV electric delivery van, R1 models, and the recently introduced R2 midsized electric SUV.
The Illinois facility has a total annual production capacity of 215,000 vehicles.
Rivian broke ground on the Georgia site near Atlanta in September, with early work focused on infrastructure and civil engineering rather than building construction.
A project delay enabled Rivian to redesign the Georgia facility for larger-scale production.

The expansion is supported by a $4.5 billion loan package from the U.S. Department of Energy, consisting of $4.006 billion in financing and $494 million in capitalized interest to be repaid after construction.
RJ Scaringe, founder and CEO of Rivian, stated excitement about partnering with the DOE to grow Rivian's manufacturing footprint in Georgia.

Scaringe noted that the R2 model dramatically expands market opportunity and emphasized that thousands of workers at the Georgia plant will be instrumental to scaling American manufacturing and maintaining U.S. leadership in innovation and technology.
The Georgia facility is expected to produce the R2 midsized electric SUV and the upcoming R3 compact SUV, which Rivian positions as a more affordable offering.
Rivian plans to manufacture 50,000 robotaxis for ride-hailing partner Uber at the facility.
Production at the Georgia site is scheduled to begin by the end of 2028.
Rivian reported first-quarter 2026 financial results showing 11% revenue growth to $1.38 billion, based on the production of 10,236 vehicles and 10,365 deliveries.
Automotive revenue declined 2% to $908 million, attributed by the company to lower sales of regulatory credits and a larger share of commercial vehicle deliveries.
Vehicle deliveries rose 20% year-on-year.
The software and services segment grew 49% to $473 million, driven largely by the development partnership with Volkswagen.
Following successful winter testing of a zonal vehicle architecture platform, Volkswagen invested an additional $1 billion in Rivian via equity, increasing its stake from 12.3% to approximately 16%.
The increased ownership likely makes Volkswagen Rivian's largest shareholder, surpassing Amazon, another key strategic partner.
Rivian reported a quarterly operating loss of $881 million (compared to $655 million a year earlier), citing lower gross margins and higher operating costs.
Net loss narrowed 23% year-on-year to $416 million.
source_title: Rivian Expands Georgia EV Plant Plans With DOE Loan and Volkswagen Backing

facts: Phase one capacity is going from 200,000 vehicles a year to 300,000, a 50% jump.
The DOE loan also got smaller, from up to $6.6 billion down to up to $4.5 billion.
Production is still on track for late 2028.
The new package is up to $4.5 billion total, $4,006 million in principal plus $494 million in capitalized interest.
Rivian also expects to start drawing on the loan by early 2027, subject to meeting certain conditions.
The earlier loan that closed in January 2025 was structured around two construction blocks.
The new structure funds the optimized Phase 1 only, with future phases of Georgia still on the table but not financed yet.
Block 1 capacity is increasing from 200,000 units a year to 300,000 units a year.
The mid-sized platform that Block 1 will produce is R2 first, then R3.
By the time Phase 1 is online, Georgia alone will be bigger than Rivian’s current Normal, Illinois plant, which sits at 215,000 units of total annual capacity covering R1, R2, and the EDV.
Georgia is becoming the center of gravity for Rivian’s manufacturing operation.
R2 production just started last week in Normal, and that is the demand signal Rivian is responding to.
Designing for 300,000 from day one is cheaper than retrofitting Phase 1 later.
Block 1 construction through 2026 and 2027 is the part to watch.
The Block 2 question is the bigger one nobody is answering today, but it can wait.
R2 has only just started rolling out the doors in Normal, and Georgia is what comes after that proves out.
A comment assumes Rivian is believing the European and overseas markets are going to provide a new fertile ground for growth needed to pay off these loans.
source_title: Rivian’s Georgia Plant Just Got Bigger, Even If the Headlines Say Otherwise

facts: A planned $5 billion electric vehicle plant is billed as the largest economic development project in Georgia's history.
The planned electric vehicle plant is drawing opposition from former Republican U.S. Sen. David Perdue.
Former Republican U.S. Sen. David Perdue, also a former corporate executive, is looking to unseat Georgia governor and fellow Republican Brian Kemp in this year's gubernatorial race.
The campaigns have traded attacks on a range of issues in a contest spurred by former President Donald Trump following his election defeat in the state.
source_title: Kemp, Perdue spar over planned Georgia electric car plant | Chattanooga Times Free Press

facts: Rivian stock jumped Tuesday morning on news related to a planned second manufacturing facility.
In December 2021, Rivian announced it planned to build a $5 billion manufacturing facility -- its second -- in Georgia.
Local residents initially pushed back against the facility plan.
State and local officials have agreed to provide Rivian with $1.5 billion in incentives, including tax breaks, to move the project forward.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated the plant is expected to eventually employ more than 7,500 workers.
Governor Kemp called the plant the largest economic development project ever undertaken in the state.
Governor Kemp defended the incentive package by citing a 2006 offer to automaker Kia worth about $570 million in today's dollars that resulted in great-paying jobs in rural Georgia.
Rivian is currently ramping up production and expects to produce only 25,000 vehicles this year due to supply chain challenges.
Georgia included protections in the incentive offer requiring Rivian to succeed over the long term to realize the benefits.
Rivian has until 2028 to fulfill 80% of its employment goal and investment plans.
Rivian must maintain its employment goal and investment plans through 2047.
Failure to meet the requirements will subject Rivian to state claw-back provisions.
Rivian has yet to prove it can manufacture at scale and fully execute its plans.
source_title: Here's Why Rivian Stock Jumped Tuesday Morning | The Motley Fool

facts: Rivian Automotive leaders say they’re confident as the company starts long-delayed work on a $5 billion facility in Georgia.
The money-losing California-based company breaks ground Tuesday east of Atlanta despite President Donald Trump's successful push to roll back electric vehicle tax credits.
Starting Sept. 30, buyers will no longer qualify for savings of up to $7,500 per car.
Rivian Chief Policy Officer Alan Hoffman said the company believes it can sell electric vehicles not for environmental or tax incentive reasons, but because they're superior.
"We did not build this company based upon federal tax incentives," Hoffman said. "And we're going to prove that we're going to be successful in the future."
The Georgia plant, first announced in 2021, is Rivian's key to reaching profitability.
Now the company makes the high-end R1T pickup truck and the R1S sport utility vehicle in Normal, Illinois, as well as delivery vans for Amazon and others.
Its truck prices start at $71,000.
The Illinois plant will begin making smaller R2 SUVs next year, with prices starting at $45,000.
An expanded Illinois plant will be able to assemble 215,000 vehicles yearly.
If the R2 is a hit, and if Rivian successfully produces an even smaller R3, it will need more capacity.
The company has said the Georgia operation will be able to make 200,000 vehicles yearly starting in 2028.
It plans another 200,000 in capacity in phase two, volume that would spread fixed costs over many more vehicles.
The projections would be a big leap from the 40,000 to 46,000 vehicles Rivian expects to deliver this year, down from 52,000 last year.
The company says it's limiting production now in part to launch 2026 models.
"For Rivian, it's do-or-die time," said Alex Oyler, North American director of auto research firm SBD Automotive.

"We saw with Tesla that the key to profitability is scale, and you can't scale if your cheapest vehicle is $70,000. So they need that plant online to achieve a level of scale of R2 and ultimately R3."
Sales growth is slowing for electric vehicles in the United States, rising only 1.5% in 2025's first half, according to Cox Automotive.
Tesla accounted for almost 45% of U.S. electric vehicle sales in that period, according to Cox.
But the giant is losing market share as others gain: General Motors' slice of American EV sales has climbed to 13%.
By comparison, Rivian had a 3% share in the first half of the year, behind Tesla and six traditional automakers.
But excluding Tesla, Rivian is the most successful of the startup automakers.
The company initially tapped a largely unfilled niche: demand for electric pickups and SUVs.
But the competition now includes Ford's F-150 Lightning and the electric Chevrolet Silverado.
After an initial public offering in 2021, Rivian shares have fallen by more than 80%, while automaker shares overall have outpaced the broader stock market.
Rivian lost $1.66 billion in 2025's first half.
At the same time, some automakers' ardor for electric vehicles is cooling.
Stellantis last week canceled Ram's electric truck program.
Ford has delayed production at a new Tennessee plant.
And General Motors abandoned plans to build electric vehicles at a suburban Detroit plant.
"With all the competition out there in this market and the slowing growth of EVs, it does not play in Rivian's favor," said Sam Fiorani, a vice president at AutoForecast Solutions.
"However, there still is an EV market out there."
Georgia has pledged $1.5 billion of incentives to Rivian in exchange for 7,500 company jobs paying at least $56,000 a year on average.
Rivian can't benefit from most incentives unless it meets employment goals, but the state is already spending $175 million to buy and grade land and improve roads.

Georgia Republican Gov. Brian Kemp, who has said he wants to make Georgia "the electric mobility capital of America," acknowledges Rivian faces bumps, but says he remains confident the company can fulfill its promises.
While Tesla has thousands of employees in California and Texas, some new electric vehicle plants have sputtered.
Two separate EV makers that hoped to assemble vehicles in a former GM plant in Lordstown, Ohio, went bankrupt.
Georgia's Hyundai complex near Savannah is faring better, with production underway.
However, a battery plant there has been delayed by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement arresting 475 people on site, including more than 300 South Koreans.
Rivian was supposed to be making trucks by now at the 2,000-acre (800-hectare) site near Social Circle, about 45 miles (70 kilometers) east of Atlanta.
As the company burned through cash in 2024, it paused construction.
But German automaker Volkswagen agreed to invest $5.8 billion in Rivian in exchange for software and electrical technology.
And then-President Joe Biden's administration in November agreed to loan Rivian $6.6 billion to build the Georgia plant.
Despite the Trump administration's hostility toward EVs, Hoffman said Rivian hopes the U.S. Department of Energy will distribute the loan money, arguing it will boost domestic manufacturing.
Rivian also faces opposition from some residents who say the plant is an inappropriate neighbor to farms and will pollute the groundwater.
"I planned on dying and retiring on the front porch and the biggest project in Georgia has to go next door to me, of all places in the country?" asked Eddie Clay, who lives less than a mile away.
He says his well water turned mud-choked after excavation at the Rivian site.

There are other challenges for Rivian, including tariffs costing $2,000 per vehicle, the Trump administration ending a tax-credit program that will cost the company $140 million in revenue this year, and long-term threats from low-priced, cutting-edge Chinese EVs.
But Hoffman says Rivian is "in this for the long haul."
"We think that we can compete with anyone out there and that once given the opportunity, we're going to excel," he said.
source_title: It's 'do or die' for electric vehicle maker Rivian as it breaks ground on a $5 billion plant - News.net

canonical_name: State of Georgia Advanced Manufacturing Ecosystem
facts: Parts holding rockets together are made in Dodge County, Georgia.
Carbice in Atlanta produces thermal tape for satellites.
Saft America in Valdosta makes advanced batteries that power airplanes, satellites and race cars.
Manufacturing of all types employs over 11,000 companies with an estimated 393,500 workers in 2021 – about 9% of Georgia’s workforce.
Approximately three-quarters of Georgia's manufacturing workers are production workers.

Companies employing advanced manufacturing practices had a total output of nearly $62 billion in 2019, representing approximately 10% of Georgia’s Gross State Product according to National Association of Manufacturers (NAM) data.
John Morehouse is the director of the Georgia Center of Innovation for Manufacturing at the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).
Georgia's strategic location allows companies to reach 80% of the U.S. market via truck, rail or plane.
The Port of Savannah serves as a gateway to and from the world for Georgia manufacturers.
Georgia Quick Start is the nation’s No. 1 workforce training program that has provided customized training for more than 1 million workers.
John Avery is the director of the Advanced Technology Development Center (ATDC) at Georgia Tech.
Southwire Company, a maker of wire and cable, is in the midst of a $1 billion investment to add capacity and improve factories.
Rich Stinson is the CEO of Southwire Company.
William Berry is the Senior Vice President at Southwire Company.
Southwire has developed better control of motors and products such as servo motors that rotate machine parts with high efficiency.

GDEcD’s innovation center has teams working in agricultural technology, energy technology, information technology, logistics, manufacturing, and aerospace to connect companies to academic experts, other companies, or government agencies.
Georgia is pushing adoption of Manufacturing 4.0 (Industry 4.0), integrating technologies like the Internet of Things (IoT), cloud computing, and artificial intelligence (AI) into operations.

Georgia Tech’s Advanced Manufacturing Pilot Facility (AMPF) is a 20,000-square-foot, reconfigurable R&D facility in Midtown Atlanta that provides space for industrial, academic, and government agencies to develop innovative manufacturing projects.
AMPF serves as a teaching laboratory for Georgia Tech graduate students.
AMPF was made possible by a $3 million donation from the Delta Air Lines Foundation.
The facility can house advanced manufacturing innovation projects including industrial robotics and additive, hybrid, composite, and digital manufacturing.

Aaron Stebner is an associate professor in the George W. Woodruff School of Mechanical Engineering at Georgia Tech working on research integrating data informatics and machine learning for new manufacturing processes.
Georgia Tech launched the Georgia Manufacturing 4.0 Consortium to work with businesses in defense and related industries.
The Georgia Manufacturing 4.0 initiative emphasizes helping small manufacturers adopt Industry 4.0 technologies and provides assistance with workforce training.
Gulfstream equips workers with wearable devices to provide remote step-by-step instructions when supervisors are not on hand.
Shreyes Melkote is a mechanical engineering professor at Georgia Tech and executive director of the Novelis Innovation Hub at Georgia Tech.
The partnership between Georgia Tech and aluminum manufacturer/recycler Novelis focuses on basic and applied research related to sustainable processes.
ProcessMiner opened in 2014 with an original goal to optimize manufacturing processes for the pulp and paper industry.
ProcessMiner’s platform now predicts problems in real time using AI within the manufacturing process for companies in the pulp, paper, plastics, water treatment, and energy sectors.
Kamran Paynabar is the co-founder and chief science officer at ProcessMiner.
Karim Pourak is the co-founder and CEO at ProcessMiner.

Rayonier Advanced Materials is developing high-tech applications for wood byproducts, such as purified cellulose used in baby diapers, adult incontinence products, plastics, run-flat tires, sausage casings, timed-release pharmaceuticals, and LCD screens.
Larissa Fenn is the director of research and development into new high-purity cellulose (HPC) products at Rayonier’s Marketing and Research Center.
Rayonier partnered with the University of Georgia’s poultry science department to test a wood-derived prebiotic product in chickens to increase gut health and disease resistance.
Carbice is an Atlanta-based start-up that spun out of a lab at Georgia Tech to address heat control problems for satellite circuit boards.
Carbice produces thermal tape and created a first-of-its-kind Space Pad that adheres to heat sources on satellites, reducing application time from three weeks to three minutes.

CEO Baratunde Cola states Carbice’s material will improve performance across space, power trains in electric vehicles, semiconductor manufacturing, data centers, cloud computing, graphics cards, and high-performance gaming.

The Atlanta Drone Group uses advanced manufacturing processes including 3D printing of prototype and production parts, 3D scanning of sensors, and cutting-edge carbon fiber lay-up methods for its unmanned aircraft.
Matt Sloane is the CEO of the Atlanta Drone Group.
Eastman’s Kencoa Aerospace is a supplier of multi-axis precision-machined and sheet metal-fabricated components serving customers including Lockheed Martin, Gulfstream Aerospace, and Boeing.
Aerotech Machining is located in Chatham County near the Port of Savannah and designs/produces parts using CNC machine tools for aerospace, agricultural, power generation, and rail industries.
Joey Jones is the President of Aerotech Machining.
PHX Holdings owns Phoenix Stamping Group, which makes sheet metal and rod component parts.
Brandyn Chapman is the CEO of PHX Holdings and states the company started its lean manufacturing journey in 2003 and implemented an ERP system to streamline and automate functions.

Saft America is a wholly owned subsidiary of France’s TotalEnergies that specializes in advanced technology battery solutions for industry, with products used in satellites and airplanes where reliability is critical.
Kirk Rosenlund is the general manager at Saft America, noting that more than 500 of their batteries have been in space since 1966.

facts: Seohan Auto Georgia held its Grand Opening Ceremony on Friday, September 6th, 2024.
The new manufacturing facility is located in the Tradeport East Business Center in Liberty County, Georgia.
The facility will produce H/shafts, axles, and brake systems for Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs).
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is identified as an OEM customer for the produced components.
Operations at the new facility will create at least 180 new jobs.
The project represents a total investment of more than $72 million in Liberty County.
The site selection process to locate the manufacturing facility in Liberty County began in November 2022.
Liberty County won the competitive site selection against other considered locations through efforts by the Authority staff, board members, and community/state partners.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that economic development does not happen by accident and highlighted the community's investment in the Tradeport East Business Center.
Pat Wilson noted that local infrastructure investments allowed Seohan Auto Georgia to meet a challenging timeline to get up and running.
State Representative Al Williams, LCDA Board Chairman, welcomed Seohan to Liberty County following the competitive location process that started in November 2022.

Opening ceremony attendees included Seohan Group Chairman Yong Suk Kim, Seohan Auto Georgia CEO Jung Kee Koo, Seohan Auto USA President Myon Han, Seohan Group Vice Chairman Woon Dae Paek, GDEcD COO Sydne Smith, GDEcD Deputy Commissioner Kristi Brigman, GDECD Commissioner Pat Wilson, Mayor Karl Riles, Liberty County Commission Chairman Donald Lovett, and LCDA Board members Luke Moses, Melissa Carter Ray, Willa Lewis, Marcus Sack, and State Representative Al Williams.

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Seohan Auto Georgia Corporation, a Tier-1 automotive parts supplier for Hyundai Motor Group America, will be investing more than $72 million in a new manufacturing facility in Liberty County.
The investment is expected to create at least 180 new jobs for the region.
Governor Kemp stated that the Hyundai Metaplant is and will continue to be an economic driver for the entire region, creating more jobs for hardworking Georgians in communities like Liberty County.
Governor Kemp added that they are proud to welcome Seohan as the latest supplier in a growing list to invest in Georgia and look forward to the continued growth of this fast emerging sector.
Seohan Auto Georgia Corporation is based in South Korea and has production facilities around the world, including multiple facilities in the United States.
Jung Kee Koo, CEO of Seohan Auto Georgia Corporation stated that sustained growth of the EV market over the past few years suggests accelerated changes to the automotive market are inevitable.
Jung Kee Koo also stated that they believe Georgia will be the center of the EV industry and a new frontier for Seohan's future with limitless opportunities and potential.
Jung Kee Koo expressed confidence that their experience and technological capabilities would contribute to the prosperity of the local community.
The new facility is scheduled to begin production in late 2024.
The facility will be located at Dunwoody Court Site in Tradeport East Business Center, a GRAD Certified site.
The facility will produce H/shafts, axles, and brake systems for OEMs including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.

Allen Brown, Chairman of Liberty County Development Authority commented on negotiations with Seohan Auto Georgia, describing them as responsible, thorough, and progressive organizations that are striving to advance innovative technology.
GDEcD Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented GDEcD's Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project involved collaboration alongside the Liberty County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson commented that the ongoing transition to e-mobility and sustainable technology creates a window of opportunity for companies and states that remain on the forefront.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America broke ground on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility earlier this year in Bryan County.
The Hyundai Metaplant project was predicted to bring approximately $1 billion worth of investments from offsite suppliers around the state.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion worth of investments as well as 27,800 jobs across Georgia.

Governor Brian Kemp stated he previously announced the largest economic development project in Georgia history last year.
Governor Brian Kemp promised to bring more jobs and opportunity to hardworking Georgians.
Governor Brian Kemp stated Georgia is working to ensure its state is the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Governor Brian Kemp noted projects like this will continue to choose Georgia as the No. 1 state for business.
Seoyon E-Hwa was established in 1972.
Seoyon E-Hwa operates 32 corporations in North America, South America, Europe, and Asia.
Seoyon E-Hwa supplies door trim, headlining, seats, C/PAD, bumpers, and other parts to Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, Mercedes-Benz, Ford, Volkswagen, and more.
Seoyon’s LaGrange location will continue to support 630 full time jobs.
Seoyon E-Hwa Chairman Mr. Ryu Yang-seok stated Seoyon E-Hwa is an automobile manufacturer in various countries such as Korea, Europe, and India.
Seoyon E-Hwa Chairman Mr. Ryu Yang-seok expressed honor to supply electric vehicle parts in Chatham County, Georgia.

Seoyon E-Hwa Chairman Mr. Ryu Yang-seok stated the establishment of Seoyon E-Hwa Savannah will provide new jobs in the region and contribute to making Savannah a central city for automobile development and production.
Seoyon E-Hwa Chairman Mr. Ryu Yang-seok committed to cooperating with the local government continuously.
Seoyon’s new facility will be located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The site is a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site.
The new manufacturing facility will produce automotive interior parts such as door trims, headlining, and tailgate trims.
Production is expected to begin in October 2024.
More than two-thirds, or approximately 500, of the announced jobs will be permanent roles in human resources, development, quality, and production personnel.
Savannah Economic Development Authority President and CEO Trip Tollison stated the announcement positions Seoyon E-HWA in close proximity to the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Trip Tollison noted proximity to the Port of Savannah, Interstates I-16 and I-95, and more.
Trip Tollison highlighted Seoyon E-HWA has a proven track record of supplying the automotive industry.
Trip Tollison stated the facility will provide well paying jobs for the region.
Georgia Ports Authority Executive Director Griff Lynch stated Seoyon is a valued customer using the Port of Savannah to supply its manufacturing facility in West Georgia.
Griff Lynch expressed excitement to help support Seoyon’s growth in this new location as the Hyundai announcement continues to have positive ripple effects throughout the regional economy.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was in partnership with the Savannah Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Seoyon is committing to nearly doubling the number of jobs it supports in the state with its second Georgia facility.
Pat Wilson noted Hyundai’s new EV facility is drawing an experienced and dedicated supplier network to the region.
Pat Wilson stated this adds to the state’s industry expertise and shapes a larger skilled workforce.
Building on automotive industry assets, Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced over $21 billion in investment and 26,700 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers for Hyundai's project have already exceeded the $1 billion in investment predicted from other locations connected to the project around the state by more than $600 million.

Seoyon E-HWA said it will build the plant to make interior and exterior parts including door trim, headlining and tailgate trim.
The company plans to begin production in October 2024.
The company already has a plant with 630 employees in LaGrange, near Hyundai’s plant in Montgomery, Alabama, and Kia’s plant in West Point.
Seoyon E-hwa operates 32 units worldwide.

The available skilled workforce for businesses is located within a 60-mile radius of the Savannah region.
More than 4,000 military members transition to the civilian workforce annually in the Savannah region.
There are 16 area colleges and universities within the Savannah region.

Bearings range in size from 2.4" outside diameter weighing 0.3lbs up to 55" in outside diameter and weighing 5000lbs.
The Flowery Branch facility was constructed in 1975.
The facility was originally operated by the TRW Company as an MRC (Marlin Rockwell Corporation) Ball Bearing Factory.
SKF Flowery Branch is in the process of major upgrades at the facility.
The Flowery Branch facility produces 9 million bearings per year.

facts: SRG Global Inc. is one of the largest manufacturers of chrome plated plastic parts for the automotive and commercial truck industries.
The company is headquartered near Detroit in Troy, Michigan.
SRG Global is a Tier 1 supplier with manufacturing operations across North America, Europe and Asia.
The company’s Advanced Development Center is located in Taylor, Michigan and serves as a dedicated facility for developing new plastics coating technology.
SRG Global Inc. is a wholly owned subsidiary of Guardian Industries, a privately held company based in Auburn Hills, Michigan.
Guardian Industries began in 1932 as a small automotive windshield fabricator in Detroit.
As of 2009, Guardian was operating on five continents with more than 18,000 employees.

Guardian is an approximately $5 billion conglomerate and an industry leader in float glass, fabricated glass products, fiberglass insulation, and building materials for commercial, residential and automotive markets.
SRG Global was established in 2009 after Guardian acquired Siegel-Robert Automotive, a manufacturer of electroplated plastic parts for the North American automotive industry.
The formation of SRG Global brought together the global trim operations of Guardian Automotive and Siegel-Robert, forming one of the largest manufacturers of chrome plated plastic parts in the world.
SRG Global has manufacturing operations in five states across the Midwestern and Southern U.S.
SRG Global operates two manufacturing plants in Spain, one in China, and a facility in Poland.
Expansion into Irapuato, Mexico occurred in 2011.
In 1932, Guardian Glass Company was established as a small automotive windshield fabricator in Detroit, Michigan.
In 1957, William Davidson was named president of Guardian and remained under his leadership for more than five decades.

In 1968, Guardian became a public company and changed its name to Guardian Industries Corp., which included windshield fabrication operations, 10 automotive glass distribution centers and a photofinishing division.
In 1970, float glass operations commenced in Carleton, Michigan, making Guardian the first company to enter the U.S. primary glass industry in nearly 50 years.
In 1981, Guardian’s float glass business extended overseas with a new facility in Luxembourg.
In 1985, Guardian completed the transition to a private company after 17 years on the public stock exchange.
In 1982, Guardian acquired Windsor Plastics, an Evansville, Indiana-based manufacturer of decorative thermoplastics for business machines, household appliances, automobiles and plumbing fixtures.
In 1996, Guardian purchased a 70 percent interest in Automotive Molding Company (AMC), a manufacturer of plastic and metal exterior body components based in Warren, Michigan.
AMC had manufacturing facilities in Michigan and Georgia, with products including body side moldings, backlite and windshield trim, and rocker panels.
AMC was established in 1948.

The acquisition of Windsor Plastics and AMC positioned Guardian as the only supplier in the automotive industry with the capability to supply the entire exterior trim and molding systems for cars and trucks.
In 1998, Guardian acquired trim manufacturer Lab. Radio in Valencia, Spain.
Lab. Radio was founded in 1977 and produced wheel covers, interior trim panels, emblems, cowl panels, door belt moldings, radiator grilles and air bag covers.
In 1997, a new trim plant was opened in Morehead, Kentucky with processes including injection molding, painting and electroplating.
The Morehead plant's initial primary customer was Ford, for which it manufactured parts for the Econoline, F-150, Expedition, Explorer and Lincoln platforms.
Subsequent expansions at the Morehead plant led to increased opportunities with Ford, as well as with DaimlerChrysler.
In 1999, Guardian received the General Motors Supplier of the Year Award and was ranked 85th on the Automotive News list of the top 100 global automotive suppliers.
In 2008, Guardian acquired Siegel-Robert Automotive, a Fenton, Missouri-based manufacturer of decorative trim products including grilles, nameplates and door handles.
Siegel-Robert was founded in 1946 by Henry Siegel and Bruce Robert.

The privately held company pioneered chrome-plated plastics in the 1960s and had plating and injection-molding expertise serving major manufacturers like Toyota, General Motors, Honda, DaimlerChrysler, Nissan, Ford and Harley-Davidson.
At the time of the acquisition, Guardian had automotive trim operations in Indiana, Kentucky, Georgia, Michigan and Spain.
Siegel-Robert had plants in Missouri, Kentucky, Tennessee and China.
In 2009, the formation of SRG Global Inc. was announced at the Geneva International Motor Show in Geneva, Switzerland.
The new company brought together the trim operations of Guardian Automotive and the recently purchased Siegel-Robert Automotive.
SRG Global employs about 4,000 people in manufacturing plants and technical centers located across the U.S., and in China, Japan and Europe.
In June 2009, SRG Global broke ground on a new $25 million automotive trim manufacturing facility in Bolesławiec, Poland.
In 2010, SRG Global opened its Advanced Development Center in Taylor, Michigan to advance the development of new plastics coating technology.
Also in 2010, the company announced its investment in a new 175,000-square-foot plastic injection molding and chrome plating plant in central Mexico.
In February 2011, SRG Global started construction on its new manufacturing facility in Irapuato, Mexico.
In June 2011, the Bolesławiec Poland location started production and shipped its first parts to customers.
In February 2012, one year after groundbreaking, the new Irapuato Mexico facility entered production and started shipping to customers.
SRG Global's Advanced Development Center opened in May 2010 in Taylor, a suburb of Detroit.

The 46,000-square-foot Advanced Development Center houses a chrome plating line and four injection molding presses ranging from 165 to 2,200 tons to create parts for coating and testing in on-site laboratories.
The Advanced Development Center is also equipped to produce custom, limited-run parts for concept vehicles.
The History Channel featured SRG Global's Morehead, Kentucky plant in a program entitled "Modern Marvels: Chrome" which aired on cable TV in March 2010.
The episode followed the injection molding and chrome plating process of a Ford F-150 grille, one of the company's flagship products.
In August 2010, Ford Motor Company awarded SRG Global’s Ripley, Tennessee plant with the Ford Q1 quality award.
Other SRG Global facilities that have achieved Ford Q1 designation include Newbern, Tennessee; Evansville, Indiana; Morehead, Kentucky; Covington, Georgia; Valencia, Spain; and Suzhou, China.
In February 2011, Chrysler unveiled their new "Imported From Detroit" campaign featuring ads with Detroit rapper Eminem, one of which aired during the Super Bowl XLV.
The two-minute spot featuring the new Chrysler 200 showcased SRG Global products originating from five SRG Global locations.

In September 2014, General Motors awarded five of its manufacturing facilities across three continents a Supplier Quality Excellence Award for consistently meeting or exceeding performance standards during the 2013-2014 award year.

SRG Global facilities that received this GM designation were: Portageville, Missouri; Newbern, Tennessee; and Evansville, Indiana, SRG Global's European facility in Lliria, Spain and the Suzhou facility in Jiangsu Province, China.
In October 2014, SRG Global announced the expansion of its operations in China with the development of a new manufacturing facility in east-central Hubei province.

The 23,000-square-meter facility will be the company's second in the region and will have a fully automated plating line including Chrome III technology, painting, plastic injection molding and built-in assembly.
The new facility will be SRG Global's 14th manufacturing facility in the world.

In 2021, SRG was named as a defendant in a number of lawsuits filed by Covington, Georgia residents that allege the company released trichloroethylene into the community’s ground water, causing an increase in cancer cases amongst Covington residents.
source_title: SRG Global - Wikipedia

facts: Located in Rome, GA.
Offers a variety of flexible shafts with standard female couplings and set screws.
Shafts have breaking strength ranging from 50 in-lb. up to 1,350 in-lb.
Shafts are available with sleeve or ball bearings in almost any length.
Products transmit rotary motion from a power source, motor or hand wheel to where the motion is required.
Flexible shafts are rugged, high-longevity components that permit continuous work operations even up to 50,000 rpm.
source_title: Flexible Shafts for Continuous Work at High Speeds From: Suhner | Industrial Equipment News

facts: Suhner Manufacturing, Brugg Cables, and Brugg Wire Rope are located off U.S. 27 south in Rome, Georgia, and ship products worldwide.
All three companies are under the ownership of Otto Suhner.
The three companies were honored by Georgia Northwestern Technical College for their nomination in the state Manufacturer of the Year Award competition.

Pete McDonald, vice president for economic development at GNTC, noted that manufacturing remains the foundation of the American economy and praised the machinery humming at the Suhner facility on Suhner Drive off U.S. 27 South.
Suhner manufactures flexible shafts used in the power movement of automotive components like car seats and sunroofs.
Suhner Vice President Guido Broder stated that the company's primary products evolved from speedometer cables to a broader repertoire due to technological changes.
Suhner relocated to Rome after a Georgia Department of Economic Development Red Carpet Tour more than 34 years prior to the article's publication.
The company was founded in 1854 by Gottlieb Suhner in Herisau, Switzerland.
Suhner was the first Swiss company to open a manufacturing plant in Georgia in 1976.
Suhner currently employs close to 150 people.
Plant manager Lee Coleman reported that Suhner produces close to 30 million feet of flexible cable annually, amounting to more than 14 million cable shafts of varying sizes.
The company adapts its systems to meet manufacturer demands for cable systems wrapped in various coatings and fitted with custom connections.
Suhner's engineering division develops shafts that allow continuous operation across several high-speed ranges.
Suhner supplies flexible shaft products to automotive industry companies including Johnson Controls, Webasto, and CRH.
Suhner also supplies the lawn and garden industry, with customers such as Echo, Stihl, and MTD.
Otto Suhner acquired controlling interest in the Brugg group of companies in 1991.
Brugg Cables was brought to Rome by Otto Suhner 12 years prior to the article's publication.
Brugg Wire Rope followed to Rome in 2002.
Kevin Heling serves as president-general manager of Brugg Wire Rope, located at 1501 Parrish Drive.
Brugg Wire Rope is an exclusive manufacturer of cables used in the elevator industry.
Leading North American customers for Brugg Wire Rope include Otis and Schindler elevator companies.
Brugg Wire Rope recently completed a 14,000 square foot warehouse and engineering addition to its Rome plant.
Heling indicated there is sufficient land at the facility to accommodate future growth for the Brugg group.
Brugg Wire Rope employs approximately 25 people and produces more than 12 million feet of elevator cable annually.
The company's output is primarily sold to stock service companies, where it is cut to length.
Brugg Wire Rope recently installed a new high-tech tubular stranding machine manufactured in China, with Chinese personnel assisting in Rome for several weeks during installation.
Heling cited efficiency as a key competitive advantage for the company.
The manufacturing process involves winding nineteen wires into one strand, then winding eight strands around a sisal core rope.
Virtually all wire used by Brugg Wire Rope is imported from the Czech Republic.
The facility receives 10 to 14 heavy 20-foot freight containers monthly via trucking companies.
In addition to rope systems, Brugg Wire Rope produces new flat belt elevator lift systems.
These belts are manufactured in Switzerland and China, then imported to Rome for custom cutting and logistics before customer shipment.
Brugg Cables is located at 25 Anderson Road in Rome and is the newest member of the Swiss family there.
Karl Zimmermann serves as plant manager for Brugg Cables.
Brugg Cables manufactures optical ground wire cable used to transmit data at super high speeds across the country.
The company was founded by Gottleib Suhner in 1896 and is now the largest within the Brugg Group.
The highly automated Brugg Cables plant employs only 10 to 15 full-time workers, with employees sometimes shared between facilities based on demand.
Heling emphasized that the Georgia Work Ready program focuses on proper training for hard work.
Heling stated the company is working harder and smarter.
Suhner and Brugg were among the first companies in Rome to adopt the Georgia Work Ready program.
Broder noted that hiring personnel ready for jobs is a priority due to the Work Ready program's influence.
source_title: Brugg Honored

facts: TDK Corporation has expanded its MPZ1608-PH series of large-current multilayer chip beads for automotive and commercial power supply lines, measuring 1.6 x 0.8 x 0.6 mm in length, width, and height.
Mass production of the MPZ1608-PH series began in May 2025.
The 1608-size chip beads achieve a rated current of 8 A, which is the industry’s highest value as of May 2025.
These chip beads function as noise suppression components in power and signal circuits.
Conventional circuits handling 8 A or more typically require two or more chip beads connected in parallel, which causes uneven current distribution between the ferrite beads.
TDK’s new product simplifies circuit structure by requiring fewer components compared to conventional parallel methods.
The new design improves the overall quality of power circuits.
The MPZ1608-PH series halves the component footprint compared to circuits that use two conventional 1608-sized chip beads.
The components are highly reliable with a specified operating temperature range up to +125 °C.
The chips are designed for high-temperature environments, specifically targeting automotive and industrial equipment applications.
TDK utilizes proprietary materials and structural designs adapted to market needs for this product line.
The company is committed to expanding its lineup of high rated current products across the automotive, industrial equipment, and consumer equipment sectors.
These multilayer chip beads serve as EMC components that enhance power circuit quality.
source_title: EMC Components: TDK Offers Multilayer Chip Beads with the Industry's Highest Rated Current of 8 A for Power Supply Lines

facts: Panel Built, Inc. was recognized by the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) for doing business internationally.
The GDEcD presented the Georgia Launching Opportunities by Exporting (GLOBE) award to Panel Built at the State Capitol building during their sixth annual ceremony on December 6th.
The GLOBE award ceremony was created by GDEcD to recognize Georgia businesses' work in international markets within the past year.
This year's ceremony featured 36 individual Georgia businesses ranging from medical devices to software development to craft brewing.
The awards ceremony placed an additional emphasis on the contributions of Georgia's small businesses to the international climate.
Three out of four GLOBE award winners were considered small businesses with less than 100 employees, and nearly half have 20 employees or less.
Panel Built has worked on projects for companies abroad in Canada, Mexico, South Korea, Morocco, Angola, Niger, and more.
As a modular construction company, Panel Built offers value to overseas companies by ensuring quality structures that may require American-made standards.

International projects include ballistic rated security booths, multi-story modular offices, bolt together steel mezzanines for scientific labs in Greenland, exterior buildings for oil production in the Democratic Republic of Congo, and rappel training towers in north Africa.
A good portion of these international projects are for the United States government, involving military bases or US embassies.
All of Panel Built's buildings are fabricated in one of its specialized modular construction facilities.
Panel Built has worked in the modular construction industry for nearly 25 years.
GDEcD offers assets and insights to help companies navigate international markets, with global knowledge and specialized representatives.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson, Deputy Commissioner Mary Waters, and Georgia Governor Brian Kemp were present at the GLOBE Awards to give remarks and take photos with winners.
GDEcD was established in 1949 and acts as a resource for current and future Georgia businesses.

The department has seven distinct divisions: Global Commerce, International Trade, Workforce, the Georgia Centers of Innovation, Tourism, Film, Music & Digital Entertainment, and the Georgia Council for the Arts.
GDEcD is one of the main contributing factors to Georgia's status as a top state to do business.
Georgia's economic production spans from automotive manufacturing to movies, being ranked near the top states for film in recent years.

facts: TE Connectivity plc is a global industrial technology leader creating connectivity and sensor solutions.

TE's connectivity and sensor solutions enable the distribution of power, signal, and data to advance next-generation transportation, renewable energy, automated factories, data centers, and medical technology.
TE has more than 85,000 employees, including 8,000 engineers, working alongside customers in approximately 140 countries.
Sam Eldessouky has been appointed to TE Connectivity's board of directors.
TE Connectivity Chairman John Davidson stated that Eldessouky brings valuable expertise to the board, including accounting and financial leadership at public companies.
Eldessouky has served in his current role at Bausch + Lomb Corporation, a global company developing, manufacturing, and marketing products primarily in eye health, since January 2022.
Eldessouky previously served as executive vice president and chief financial officer of Bausch Health Companies from 2021 to 2022.

From 2016 to 2021, Eldessouky was controller and chief accounting officer at Bausch Health Companies, responsible for overseeing global controllership functions including financial reporting, regional finance, and global policies.
Eldessouky spent more than 12 years at Tyco International plc, most recently serving as senior vice president, controller, and chief accounting officer from 2012 to 2016.
Prior to Tyco, Eldessouky spent 10 years at PwC holding roles of increasing responsibility and serving in the National Office providing technical accounting guidance on complex accounting matters.
Eldessouky holds a Bachelor of Science in accountancy from Ain Shams University and a master's degree in accounting and finance from the University of Liverpool.
Eldessouky is a certified public accountant (inactive) and chartered global management accountant.
source_title: Sam Eldessouky Appointed To TE Connectivity Board Of Directors
