facts: Work on Hyundai's Georgia battery plant will be delayed by up to three months due to an immigration raid.
Hyundai operates the Georgia battery plant jointly with LG Energy Solutions.
CEO José Muñoz stated the enforcement action leaves the plant short of workers and will cause a minimum two to three month delay.
Muñoz explained that filling the vacant positions will be difficult because most detained workers are not currently in the U.S.
U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) detained approximately 475 people suspected of illegally living and working at the Georgia plant earlier this month.
More than 300 of the detained individuals were South Korean nationals, according to South Korea's Foreign Minister Cho Hyun.
Following a deal between the U.S. and South Korea, the detained South Korean nationals were set to be repatriated on a charter flight.
A poll in South Korea found that almost 60% of respondents were disappointed by the U.S. crackdown, calling the enforcement action 'excessive.'
Roughly 31% of poll respondents called the ICE action 'inevitable' and said they understood why it took place.
South Korean officials warned that the immigration enforcement action could chill South Korea's investments in the U.S.
source_title: Hyundai CEO says immigration raid will delay Georgia battery plant's construction - CBS News

facts: The Hyundai metaplant is under construction in Bryan County.
Official announcements for the project include 8,100 jobs, at least $5.5 billion in construction spending, and suppliers planning to hire thousands more employees.

Hyundai Motor Group states its arrival will create 8,100 new jobs at its site located at Interstate 16 and Georgia 280 in Bryan County when the electric vehicle and battery plant is fully operational.
The metaplant's workforce size will place it among the largest private employers in the region.
According to Georgia Department of Labor 2022 data, only 11 private companies employ more than 1,000 people across Chatham, Bryan, and Effingham counties.
Gulfstream is the largest private employer in the area with roughly 11,500 workers around Savannah and is currently hiring.
The Savannah-Chatham Public School System employs approximately 5,500 staff members.
Approximately 3,500 civilians work at Fort Stewart and 640 civilians are employed at Hunter Army Air Field.
Roughly 27,000 people work in the hospitality industry across Chatham, Bryan, and Effingham counties, spread among approximately 1,600 small businesses.
Hyundai and the adjacent battery plant will pay an average of at least $58,105 per job, plus benefits.
This wage level is required to qualify for state and local grants, tax breaks, and land valued at approximately $228,000 per job.
Hyundai suppliers can negotiate separate pay, investment, and subsidy agreements.
Ecoplastic's plant near Register must create 456 jobs over eight years when fully operational producing automotive plastic parts.
Ecoplastic workers must be paid at least the county's average wage of $21.65 per hour as established in a February 2023 agreement with Bulloch County.
A 40-hour workweek at that rate equals approximately $43,300 annually.
In exchange for building in the county, Bulloch County provided Ecoplastic with a $2.3 million site purchase discount and a 15-year county property tax break.
The property tax discount excludes school and fire district taxes.
Ecoplastic may also receive a $1.6 million state grant.
Joon Georgia has a similar agreement with Bulloch County and the state, matching the county average wage for 630 promised jobs at its new facility.
Joon Georgia received a $3.1 million land discount near I-16, a $2.7 million state grant, and a 10-year county property tax break.
The property tax abatement for Joon Georgia does not cover school and fire district rates.
The Hyundai Metaplant site spans more than a mile of Black Creek, approximately three miles upstream from where it joins the Ogeechee River.
Hyundai and its suppliers are locating near interstates and Ogeechee watershed waterways.
A new wastewater treatment plant for the site and surrounding areas will discharge into the Ogeechee River.
The wastewater treatment plant located across the interstate from Hyundai will process the facility's wastewater and north Bryan County's sewage before discharging it into the Ogeechee River.
Environmentalists at Ogeechee Riverkeeper are tracking water quality in Black Creek and the Ogeechee River due to distrust of state pollution monitoring.
Eight Hyundai suppliers will be located across Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham, Effingham, and Liberty counties.
None of the supplier factories are currently served by public transportation lines.
Hyundai production at the metaplant is scheduled to begin in the first half of 2025.
The metaplant site is located on the south side of I-16, approximately 22 miles northwest of Savannah.
Site clearance for the project is already visible via satellite imagery.
Satellite imagery shows industrial site development in the area has been ongoing since 2005.
Daniel Defense and ORAFOL facilities are located directly across the interstate from the metaplant site.
State and local governments view the job influx as highly significant, comparing it to winning a College Football National Championship.
The team that attracted Hyundai included state and county leaders plus the newly formed Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority.
Georgia competed against other regional states, anticipating manufacturers would demand billions in subsidies.

Georgia's Department of Economic Development highlighted its "Best State for Business" ranking in its offer letter to Hyundai, citing outstanding logistics, a well-trained workforce, low business costs, a pro-business climate, and high quality of life.
Worker quality of life is not included in the "Best State for Business" rankings.
Coastal Georgia laborers will quickly benefit from tens of millions of dollars in state-funded job training tailored to the new employers' needs.
source_title: The numbers behind Hyundai's metaplant in Bryan County  - Grice Connect

entity_name: Ford Motor Company and SK Innovation Joint Venture
facts: Ford is building three new EV battery plants with one located in Tennessee and two in Kentucky.
The project is an $11.4-billion deal between Ford and the South Korea-based company SK Innovation.
The first Kentucky plant will complete assembly in 2025.
A sister plant in Kentucky is slated for completion in 2026.
Combined, the three new facilities will create 11,000 new jobs.
The combined output of the three facilities will be 129 GWh.
The 129 GWh capacity is enough to power 1 million all-electric vehicles.
Ford committed $7 billion to the project.
SK Innovation committed $4.4 billion to the project.
The Tennessee facility is a $5.6-billion mega-campus located in Stanton, Tenn., called Blue Oval City.
Blue Oval City will offer 6,000 new jobs.
Blue Oval City will include community-designed technical training programs to strengthen the local community.
Ford and SK Innovation plan to build a $5.8-billion battery manufacturing complex in Kentucky called BlueOvalSK Battery Park.
BlueOvalSK Battery Park will require filling 5,000 new jobs.
The new facilities are component plants for battery manufacturing, not automotive assembly plants.
The battery manufacturing effort will run parallel with ambitions to build a more reliable means of recycling lithium-ion batteries at minimal cost to the environment.
The aim is to develop a manufacturing line to supply Ford’s North American car assembly plants with locally assembled batteries.
The batteries will be needed for future Ford and Lincoln EVs, including next-generation all-electric F-Series trucks.

Bill Ford, Executive Chair of Ford, called the project a transformative moment where Ford will lead America’s transition to electric vehicles and usher in a new era of clean, carbon-neutral manufacturing.

Bill Ford stated that with this investment and innovation, they can achieve mutually exclusive goals: protect the planet, build great electric vehicles Americans will love, and contribute to the nation's prosperity.
The deal responds to strong demand for the Ford F-150 Lightning truck, E-Transit, and Mustang Mach-E vehicles.
Ford has not single-handedly built an entirely new plant from the ground up since its first Kentucky plant was commissioned in 1969.
Jim Farley, CEO and President of Ford, called it their biggest investment ever to help build a better future for America.

Jim Farley stated they are moving to deliver breakthrough electric vehicles for the many rather than the few, creating good jobs supporting American families, an ultra-efficient carbon-neutral manufacturing system, and value for communities, dealers, and shareholders.
This is Ford's largest single manufacturing investment in its 118 years of operation.
The project is part of Ford's more-than-$30 billion investment in EVs through 2025.
Ford aims to develop the country’s manufacturing ecosystem for the 21st century while committing to meet the Paris Climate Agreement deadline with zero carbon no later than 2050.
source_title: Ford Is Building Three New EV Battery Plants With 129 GWh Capacity

facts: Lucid Motors has officially begun phase-one construction of its EV manufacturing facility in Arizona.
The facility represents an investment of more than USD 300 million.

The facility’s “ground-building” ceremony was held Tuesday in Casa Grande, Arizona, with Arizona Governor Doug Ducey, representatives from the Arizona Commerce Authority, and other local officials in attendance.
The facility is scheduled for completion in late 2020.
Production of Lucid’s first car, the Lucid Air luxury sedan, is scheduled to follow the facility's completion.
According to the company website, the Lucid Air will have a 400-mile range.
The Lucid Air will be capable of accelerating from 0-60 mph in under 2.5 seconds.
The company has announced the formation of 4,800 direct and indirect jobs by 2029.
More than USD 700 million in capital expenditures are expected from Lucid over the next approximately five years.
The project will have an estimated USD 32 million revenue impact on the region over the next 20-year period.
The project includes new training programs and related curriculum at local community colleges.
Lucid Motors CEO and CTO Peter Rawlinson stated that the Lucid Air is a cutting-edge electric vehicle designed, engineered, and destined for manufacture entirely in America.
Peter Rawlinson expressed pride in moving forward on the commitment to manufacturing the Lucid Air in Casa Grande.

Rawlinson noted that with supportive investors, an outstanding team of designers and engineers, and a product strategy extending well beyond the Air, they expect today to be just the start of a longstanding presence in the city.
According to the press release, Lucid visited more than 60 sites spanning 13 states during its search for a location.
The company cited business climate, infrastructure, workforce, and proximity of the automotive supply chain in the Arizona-Sonora region as factors for its final selection.
source_title: Lucid Motors sees EV facility groundbreaking

facts: Kia Motors unveiled a new midsize sedan called the K5 to replace the outgoing Optima.
The K5 will arrive in U.S. dealerships this summer.
Kia Motors America stated that nearly a decade after the 2011 Kia Optima paved the way for significant growth, the reveal of the all-new K5 resets expectations.
The 2021 K5 features more standard and available safety features, additional technology, and a new design with elements similar to Kia's Stinger performance sedan.
Exterior design changes include a new interpretation of Kia's "tiger face" front, a sleeker grille flowing into the headlights, and "heart beat" daytime running lights extending to the side.
The redesigned interior includes an optional 10.25 inch infotainment touchscreen and wireless Apple CarPlay and Android Auto that mirror phone functions without a cord.
The K5 is powered by a 1.6 liter turbocharged GDI four-cylinder engine producing 180 horsepower and 195 pound-feet of torque, managed by an all-new eight-speed automatic transmission.
A GT model with a 2.5-liter four-cylinder engine making 290 horsepower and 311 pound-feet of torque will be available in the fall.
All-wheel-drive versions of the car will be available in late 2020.
The K5 will be built at Kia's plant in West Point, Georgia.
Pricing for the K5 was not announced, while the Optima currently starts at about $24,000.

After being introduced in 2010, the Optima quickly became Kia's best-selling vehicle in the U.S., with sales surging to nearly 160,000 units in 2015 before declining 39.4% through 2019 and another 16% through the first five months of 2020.
Kia's overall U.S. sales last year were up 4.4% to more than 615,000 vehicles compared to 2018, while sales this year are down about 13% through May compared to the same period in 2019.
source_title: Kia unveils new K5 in attempt to halt skidding sedan sales

entity_name: Georgia DOT, Kia Georgia, Panasonic and The Ray Connected Vehicle Partnership
canonical_name: Georgia DOT, Kia Georgia, Panasonic and The Ray Connected Vehicle Partnership
facts: GDOT, The Ray, Kia Georgia, and Panasonic demonstrated a fully operational connected vehicle environment along 18 miles of Interstate 85 known as “The Ray Highway.”
The demonstration utilized the Cirrus by Panasonic connected vehicle data management platform that detects real-time roadway events.
The platform communicates safety-critical messages to travelers about driving conditions.
The partnership between the four organizations is focused on raising awareness of the life-saving potential of connected vehicle technology.
The partnership aims to deploy real-world use cases with measurable benefits.
GDOT and Kia Georgia vehicles equipped with vehicle-to-everything (V2X) technology were showcased during the demonstration.
GDOT can now deliver real-time situational awareness to drivers on equipped roadways in this key logistics and manufacturing corridor, a fast-growing region of West Georgia.
GDOT, The Ray, and Panasonic are providing “ride-along” experiences for stakeholders.
Drivers participating in the ride-alongs will be warned of hazardous conditions ahead on the roadway before they come into view.
The experiences showcase how connected vehicle technology can detect and warn drivers about weather events, work zones, hard braking, and vehicle crashes in a real-world highway environment.
The experience includes an in-vehicle visualization of traveler warnings.
The experience showcases the capabilities of V2X technology installed on and along roadways.
The experience highlights the digital communication that enables the intelligence of connected roadways.
The Cirrus cloud platform manages the data for GDOT to detect events and provide real-time alerts to drivers.
Russell McMurry, Commissioner of the Georgia Department of Transportation, stated it is important that Georgia DOT leads in the deployment of transportation technology that benefits all drivers.
McMurry noted this technology helps move freight across the region more efficiently and safely.
McMurry said advancing Georgia as the technology epicenter of the east coast is a goal.
McMurry expressed determination to see connected vehicle technology come to life in Georgia as soon as possible.
McMurry emphasized that the day is about the power of partnerships with organizations like Kia, The Ray, and Panasonic to advance roadways for Georgians.
Stuart Countess, Kia Georgia’s president and CEO, stated Kia Georgia is proud to partner with each organization while showcasing products built by the Kia team in West Point, Georgia.
Countess said customer safety is the number one priority for Kia Georgia team members.

Countess described the V2X program as a forward-thinking initiative that puts Kia at the heart of solutions where vehicles and infrastructure communicate seamlessly to enhance travel safety and efficiency.
Allie Kelly, Executive Director of The Ray, stated connected vehicle technology creates an opportunity to increase travel efficiency and driver safety simultaneously.
Kelly noted investments in physical and digital infrastructure are necessary to enable connected and autonomous vehicles.
Kelly said Georgia DOT and The Ray made those investments to improve and modernize Georgia’s transportation infrastructure.

Kelly mentioned the goal is to offer the traveling public real-time, real-place warnings about critical driving conditions such as crashes, construction work zones, hard braking, and weather impacts like black ice, flooding, and hydroplaning.
Chris Armstrong, Vice President of Cirrus at Panasonic’s Smart Mobility Office, stated the tech industry constantly talks about building technology with purpose.
Armstrong expressed pride in partnering with mission-driven organizations in Georgia DOT, The Ray, and Kia Georgia.
Armstrong highlighted solving transportation challenges by bringing forward perspectives from public, private, and philanthropic organizations united in improving safety.
Armstrong stated each organization plays a unique role in championing the wide-scale adoption of this life-saving technology as quickly as possible.
Panasonic Corporation of North America is headquartered in Newark, NJ.
Panasonic is a leading provider of Consumer Lifestyle technologies, Smart Mobility, Sustainable Energy, Immersive Experiences, and Integrated Supply Chain solutions.
Panasonic is the principal North American subsidiary of Osaka, Japan-based Panasonic Holdings Corporation.
Panasonic was named one of Interbrand’s Top 100 Best Global Brands of 2021.
Panasonic serves as a leading technology partner and integrator to businesses, government agencies, and consumers across the region.
source_title: Georgia DOT Demonstrates Safer, Connected Roadways with Kia Georgia and Panasonic Smart Mobility Technology on "The Ray Highway" - AllOnGeorgia

facts: Green Li-ion launched North America’s first commercial-scale plant to produce battery-grade materials from recycled lithium-ion batteries.
The facility is located in Atoka, Oklahoma.
The plant utilizes Green Li-ion’s patented multi-cathode-producing GREEN HYDROREJUVINATION™ technology.
This technology processes unsorted battery waste, or “black mass,” directly into battery-grade precursor cathode active material (pCAM) and anode materials.
The approach eliminates the need to export materials overseas for further processing, significantly reducing production time and greenhouse gas emissions.
Green Li-ion offers modular hardware solutions that allow clients to integrate the technology within existing facilities through licensing agreements.
Clients can alternatively utilize Green Li-ion’s tolling services by sending black mass to Green Li-ion-owned plants.
The technology enables rapid deployment, allowing operations to begin within a few months rather than years.
Green Li-ion's technology converts black mass into high-purity pCAM in approximately 12 hours.
The process generates up to 90% fewer greenhouse gas emissions compared to the production of virgin materials.
The Atoka plant's initial production capacity can process four to six metric tons of end-of-life batteries per day.
This daily processing volume is equivalent to 72,000 smartphone batteries.
Plans are already in place to quadruple the plant's capacity within the calendar year.
The facility closes a critical gap in North America’s battery recycling supply chain.
By producing battery-grade materials domestically, the plant supports the growing demand for electric vehicles (EVs) and other battery-powered technologies.
The advancement aligns with provisions in the Inflation Reduction Act, which incentivizes domestic battery manufacturing and the use of recycled materials through substantial tax credits.
Establishing the Atoka plant has created over 20 local jobs.
There are plans to expand the workforce as the facility grows.
Green Li-ion is committed to using local suppliers and partnering with educational institutions to strengthen integration with the Atoka community.
The plant boosts the local economy and enhances Atoka’s visibility on the international stage.
source_title: Green Li-ion's plant in Atoka, Oklahoma is a Groundbreaking Achievement in the Battery Recycling Industry - Green Li-ion

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) has broken ground on a new $5.54bn electric vehicle and battery manufacturing plant at Bryan County, Georgia, US.
The ground breaking ceremony was attended by Hyundai Motor Group executive chair Euisun Chung, Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp, and company officials, and state and local leaders.
HMGMA and its affiliate suppliers announced plans to construct the plant in May.
Over the next few years, HMGMA plans to generate more than 8,100 jobs as it intends to set up a steady supply chain for EV battery and other components in US.
The factory is anticipated to start commercial production in the first half of 2025, with an annual production capacity of 300,000 units.

Hyundai Motor Group executive chair Euisun Chung said: “Hyundai and the people of Georgia share many qualities: respect for our histories, ingenuity, creativity, and determination to make the world better for the next generation.”

Chung added: “Today, our EVs are recognized as best in class, and with this partnership, we are determined to be the global leader in electrification, safety, quality, and sustainability. With the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, we will continue to evolve beyond an automaker to the world’s leading mobility solutions provider.”

Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp said: “With more Georgians working than ever before, record jobs and investment coming to all parts of our state, and award-winning workforce development programs and infrastructure, the Peach State’s economy is reaching new heights”.
Non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers are expected to invest around $1bn in connection to the project in other locations.
This plant is part of Hyundai Motor Group’s electrification efforts to achieve a global target of selling 3.23 million completely electric vehicles annually by the end of this decade.

The yet to-built facility in Georgia will become an intelligent manufacturing plant, with all processes of production, right from order collection, procurement, logistics and production to be optimised leveraging artificial intelligence and data.
The facility will primarily depend on clean energy sources and use emission-reduction technologies.
source_title: Hyundai breaks ground on $5.54bn EV and battery plant in Georgia, US - World Construction Network

facts: Hyundai is the latest OEM to build a new EV plant in the U.S.
The announcement is associated with the Inflation Reduction Act.
A forum discussion regarding this topic was started on Oct 29, 2022.
source_title: Inflation Reduction Act – Hyundai is Latest OEM to Build New EV Plant in the U.S. | CleanMPG

facts: Ascend Elements is a U.S.-based engineered materials and lithium-ion battery recycling company.
The company announced it has secured $300 million in equity and debt financing.
This funding includes $200 million in Series C equity investments from an international group of strategic and financial investors.
The funding round was led by Fifth Wall Climate.
SK ecoplant, the environmental unit of South Korean conglomerate SK Group, joined the funding round.
Other new investors include Oman Investment Authority, Lithium Americas Corporation, GLy Capital Management’s New Mobility Fund, Mirae Asset Capital & LS and Shinhan GIB.

Many investors from previous funding rounds also participated in the latest round, including Hitachi Ventures, Jaguar Land Rover’s InMotion Ventures, TDK Ventures, Orbia Ventures, At One Ventures, TRUMPF Venture, and Doral Energy-Tech Ventures.
This funding is in addition to two recently awarded grants totaling $480 million from the Department of Energy.
The funding and grants will accelerate commercialization of Ascend Elements’ proprietary Hydro-to-Cathode™ direct precursor synthesis process.
This process establishes a closed-loop, EV battery materials supply chain in North America.
The announcement follows plans to invest up to $1 billion to build a sustainable lithium-ion battery materials facility in Hopkinsville, Ky.
The first-of-its-kind manufacturing facility is known as “Apex 1”.
Apex 1 will produce enough lithium-ion battery pCAM and sustainable CAM to equip up to 250,000 electric vehicles per year.
Mike O’Kronley serves as the CEO of Ascend Elements.
O’Kronley stated that their patented, closed-loop process goes beyond simple battery recycling to produce sustainable, high-performance cathode active materials for new EV batteries.
Ascend Elements is already generating revenue and processing end-of-life batteries and manufacturing scrap at its “Base 1” facility in Georgia.
The Base 1 facility will have 30,000 metric tons of EV battery recycling capacity by the end of 2022.
The Base 1 facility will ultimately feed Apex 1 in Kentucky.
The company expects to recycle more than 150,000 metric tons of lithium-ion batteries per year globally by 2026.
Recycled materials will be transformed into high-performance, customized EV battery cathode active material precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).
These materials meet or exceed the performance requirements of top-tier battery manufacturers.
Peter Gajdoš is a Fifth Wall Partner and Co-Lead of the Climate Investment team.
Gajdoš stated that securing recyclability of critical metals in batteries is essential to achieve energy independence from fossil fuels.
Park Kyung-il serves as the CEO of SK ecoplant.
Park stated the investment lays the foundation for SK ecoplant as the 1st shareholder to dominate the global battery recycling industry.
Ibrahim Al Eisri is Director – Private Equity at Oman Investment Authority (OIA).
Al Eisri stated the investment aligns with OIA’s strategy of investing in transformative companies complementing Oman’s Vision 2040 priorities.
Ascend Elements is based in Westborough, Mass.
The company is the leading provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery materials solutions.
Its proprietary Hydro-to-Cathode™ direct precursor synthesis technology produces new CAM from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods.
This technology results in reduced cost, improved performance, and lowered GHG emissions.
The announcement was made on October 26, 2022.
source_title: Ascend Elements, US Engineered Materials and Lithium-ion Battery Recycling Company, Secures $300 Million in Funding - Batteries News

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy has unexpectedly canceled more than $700 million in federal battery grants, abruptly halting projects intended to boost domestic EV battery production.
Among the affected projects are major initiatives in Kentucky, Nevada, and Missouri.
Senate Democrats accused the DOE of unlawfully rescinding funds that were already approved under previous administration initiatives.
In early October, the Department of Energy withdrew more than $700 million in grants aimed at expanding domestic battery manufacturing.
The canceled grants were part of a broader effort to stimulate the electric vehicle supply chain within the United States and reduce dependency on foreign materials.
The affected grants had been awarded to companies including Ascend Elements, American Battery Technology Company, Anovion, ICL Specialty Products, and LuxWall.
Ascend Elements was awarded $316 million to build a $1 billion facility in Kentucky focused on manufacturing battery components from recycled electric vehicle batteries.
American Battery Technology Company had received $57.7 million to develop a Nevada plant for producing lithium hydroxide.
Anovion was set to receive $117 million for synthetic graphite production used in battery anodes.
The Department of Energy justified its decision by stating that the canceled projects had not met essential milestones and were unlikely to deliver the expected returns.

DOE spokesperson Ben Dietderich said the initiatives “did not adequately advance the nation’s energy needs, were not economically viable, and would not provide a positive return on investment of taxpayer dollars.”
Energy Secretary Chris Wright has supported the cancellations, arguing that public funding must be spent wisely.
Chris Wright stated, “If they’re not in the interest of the taxpayers, if they’re not a good expenditure of the money, you always have the ability to cancel these projects.”
Reports suggest this may be part of a broader reassessment, with a circulating list among lobbyists hinting that up to $20 billion in additional awards could be at risk.
In a letter addressed to Energy Secretary Wright, 37 Democratic and independent senators accused the department of exceeding its legal authority.
The senators stated that the cancellations violate congressional intent and may break laws governing federal fund allocation.
The senators wrote, “The illegality of your cancellations is the only thing as indisputable as the harm your cancellations will wreak.”
The senators emphasized that the DOE has a legal obligation to execute appropriated funds in accordance with the law and contractual agreements.
This challenge introduces complexity into the Biden administration’s clean energy goals, especially given that many of the affected grants were part of the administration’s broader climate agenda.
There is increasing concern that such policy reversals could allow China to consolidate its lead in EV battery innovation and production.
Industry stakeholders fear that the gap between American and Chinese capabilities may widen further due to the U.S. backtracking on critical investments.
The domestic push to strengthen the battery supply chain has been central to reducing reliance on overseas markets, especially as geopolitical tensions rise.
The DOE’s decision has raised alarms not only among politicians but also within the EV manufacturing sector, where long-term planning depends heavily on stable public-private partnerships.
Despite the growing unease, the DOE maintains that its priority is fiscal responsibility and that all grants are subject to periodic review.
source_title: By Cancelling $700M in EV Grants, Washington Just Gifted China Another Advantage in the EV Race

entity_name: Georgia NEVI DC Fast-Charging Infrastructure Project
canonical_name: Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT) NEVI Charging Infrastructure Program
facts: Georgia allocated $24.4 million for 26 new DC fast-charging stations across the state.
The Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT) selected private-sector partners to build and operate the new stations along federally designated Alternative Fuel Corridors.
Each site will feature four DC fast chargers available 24/7 with a minimum output of 150 kW per port.
The chargers are capable of delivering a full recharge in as little as 20 minutes, depending on the EV.
This award represents the second round of Georgia’s NEVI funding, following the first round in 2024.
GDOT mapped out 33 priority sites near highway exits and interchanges in mostly rural areas to close charging gaps left after the first round.
The EV charging industry submitted 41 proposals to cover 26 of those mapped locations.
Six private-sector winners were selected for the project: Pilot Travel Centers, Silver Comet Energy, Universal EV, PowerUp America, Love’s Travel Stops, and EnviroSpark Energy Solutions.

Governor Brian Kemp (R-GA) stated that Georgia appreciates GDOT for fulfilling the state’s commitment to a robust, reliable fast-charging network that meets federal standards and serves communities across Georgia.
Georgia was allocated approximately $135 million through the NEVI program, which is part of President Joe Biden’s Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
The federal NEVI program covers up to 80% of project costs, with private partners responsible for covering the remaining balance.

Round 2 funding follows a legal battle earlier this year when a lawsuit filed by several states (excluding Georgia) compelled the Trump Administration to release funds owed from the NEVI Formula Program.
A federal judge blocked the Trump administration’s attempt to obstruct the NEVI program in June, clearing the way for planned NEVI EV charging projects to continue.
source_title: Georgia gets 26 new DC fast-charging stations with $24.4M of NEVI funds | Electrek

facts: Georgia DOT is advancing the federally approved Georgia National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) Deployment Plan, which has reached a significant milestone.
Georgia DOT conditionally awarded apparent successful proposers for five locations during Round 1 of Georgia NEVI deployment.
The electric vehicle chargers will be deployed through a Public-Private-Partnership (P3) utilizing federal and private funding.

Under the P3 agreement, each team will design, install, finance, operate, and maintain EV charging stations equipped with Direct Current Fast Charging (DCFC) ports for a minimum of five years in compliance with federal rules.
The Tifton location (PI. No. 0019828) is awarded to EnviroSpark Networks, Inc. at Waffle House (706 7th Street West, Tifton, GA 31794).
The Fort Valley location (PI. No. 0019829) is awarded to Francis Energy Charging, LLC at Wendy’s (318 GA-49, Byron, GA 31008).
The Dublin location (PI. No. 0019830) is awarded to Silver Comet Energy, Inc. at RaceTrac Gas Station (2262 US-441, Dublin, GA 31021).
The Metter location (PI. No. 0019831) is awarded to EnviroSpark Networks, Inc. at Shell Super Stop Gas Station (1205 S Lewis Street, Metter, GA 30439).
The Brunswick location (PI. No. 0019832) is awarded to Love’s Travel Stops & Country Stores, Inc. at Love’s Travel Stop (2766 Hwy 17, Brunswick, GA 31523).
Georgia DOT Commissioner Russell R. McMurry, P.E. stated that Georgia's mission is to provide a transportation system focused on innovation, safety, sustainability, and mobility.
Commissioner McMurry noted Georgia is a leader in E-Mobility with KIA in West Point producing the EV9, as well as the Hyundai KIA Genisis Meta plant and Rivian plant both under construction.
Commissioner McMurry said deploying the first five charger locations is a good start to ensure fast charging infrastructure is in place as more electric vehicles are on the road.
Commissioner McMurry expressed that Georgia DOT looks forward to the next round of procurements starting very soon.
Georgia is among the top 10 states for EV sales.
Three of the top five largest investments in the EV supply chain are in Georgia.
Georgia has approximately 1,193 publicly available EV charging stations.
Only 14 stations have DCFC ports that meet both distance and power requirements under federal rules (minimum of four 150 kW ports and located within 1 mile of an Alternative Fuel Corridor).

Georgia DOT Deputy Chief Engineer Andrew Heath, P.E., stated the Georgia NEVI Deployment Program outlines the state's approach to using federal funding to address EV infrastructure gaps in the state’s Alternative Fuel Corridors.

Andrew Heath noted that to be successful, Georgia DOT is turning to the private sector to deliver innovative infrastructure at strategic locations across the state through Public-Private Partnerships.
Georgia DOT developed the Georgia NEVI implementation approach with broad industry stakeholder input for EV charging infrastructure across the state in accordance with federal law.

The program aims to establish an interconnected EV charging network that meets customer demands, reduces range concerns, facilitates data collection, and ensures secure, convenient access to publicly available charging infrastructure.
The Georgia NEVI Deployment Program is derived from the 2021 Bipartisan Infrastructure Law enacted as the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA).
The program provides nearly $5 billion nationally over five years, which started in the fall of 2022.
Georgia’s allotment from the formula program is approximately $135 million to develop its portion of the national network.
The Georgia NEVI Deployment Plan covers the entire state of Georgia and garnered approval from the Federal Highway Administration (FHWA) in January 2023.

Georgia DOT developed the state’s NEVI plan in consultation with 57 different entities, including other state agencies, MPOs, utility providers, private businesses, and other regional/national groups.
source_title: Georgia DOT Continues to Advance the Deployment of Electric Vehicle Infrastructure - Columbus CEO

facts: Morgan County is denying the electric automaker $1.5 billion in bonds.
One of Georgia’s biggest economic development projects in history could be at risk due to a Thursday ruling by a Morgan County judge.

Ocmulgee Superior Courts Chief Judge Brenda Holbert Trammell ruled the local development authority trying to lure a Rivian electric vehicle automotive plant to Morgan County has failed to establish the bonds at the center of the project are reasonable.
Those bonds are part of the $1.5 billion incentive package offered to the company.
Trammell’s ruling is likely to be appealed.
In exchange for those bonds, Rivian is promising to bring more than 7,000 jobs to the area as part of the $5 billion plant.

The Irvine, California-based electric vehicle manufacturer announced last year it would build the facility on a 2,000-acre site in Morgan and Walton counties about 45 miles east of Atlanta along Interstate 20.
It plans to produce up to 400,000 vehicles a year there.
Rivian said it would bring 7,500 new jobs to Georgia.
Thanks to the ruling, that plan is now in jeopardy.

Attorney John Christy, who represents residents who contested the approval of the bond money, stated: “The court ruled that this project is just not going to be entitled to subsidization from the taxpayers of the the three towns which is what the pilot program was all about.”
Christy argued the property tax breaks offered to Rivian take away from important potential benefits for the residents of the community where the plant is located.

Christy stated: “All they have to do is just go ahead and build the plant that they want to with their own money, with their loan from a bank or whatever and pay their taxes, but they wanted to have this pilot program which was a very big subsidy by the taxpayers of these three counties.”
JDA and Georgia Department of Economic Development released a joint statement in response to the ruling.

The joint statement said: “As the entire country is looking to revitalize and grow domestic manufacturing, protect American jobs, and secure the country’s economic independence, we are disappointed and respectfully disagree with Judge Trammell’s decision. This is a transformational project for the people of this community, the State of Georgia, and the United States of America. We remain undeterred in our efforts to bring high-paying, American manufacturing jobs to Georgia, and are currently assessing all legal options. The Joint Development Authority and the State intend to work with Rivian to move this project forward and see it through to completion.”
Rivian also has a plant in Normal, Illinois.
Rivian hoped to break ground this past summer and begin production in 2024.
source_title: Huge Georgia Rivian project threatened by judge’s ruling

facts: A Georgia judge rejected an agreement that would have provided a huge property tax break to Rivian Automotive.
The rejection clouds the upstart electric truck maker's plans to build a plant east of Atlanta.
Morgan County Superior Court Judge Brenda Trammell rejected what is normally a routine request by a local government to validate a bond agreement.
The ruling was issued on Thursday.
The development authority that brought the case hadn't proved that the $5 billion plant, projected to hire 7,500 people, was “sound, reasonable and feasible” as required under state law.
Trammell ruled that under state law, Rivian should be required to pay regular property taxes because of its level of control over property it would be leasing from the development authority.
This ruling undermines the reason the legal action was brought in the first place.
Rivian declined to comment.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development and a local four-county joint development authority that recruited Rivian said they were “disappointed and respectfully disagree with Judge Trammell’s decision.
Officials stated they aren't giving up on their plans and are considering an appeal.
The groups said: “We remain undeterred in our efforts to bring high-paying, American manufacturing jobs to Georgia, and are currently assessing all legal options.”

The Irvine, California-based electric vehicle manufacturer announced last year that it would build the facility on a 2,000-acre (800-hectare) site in Morgan and Walton counties about 45 miles (70 kilometers) east of Atlanta along Interstate 20.
Rivian plans to produce up to 400,000 vehicles a year at the facility.
Rivian, which also has a plant in Normal, Illinois, had hoped to break ground as early as this summer and begin production in 2024.
By maintaining ownership of the property and leasing it to Rivian, local governments would exempt Rivian from a projected $700 million in property taxes over 25 years.
Rivian has agreed to make $300 million in payments in lieu of taxes during the period.
The property tax break is a key part of the $1.2 billion in tax breaks and incentives that Georgia and local officials offered for Rivian to build a plant in the state.
The long-used maneuver circumvents a ban in Georgia’s state constitution on giving “gratuities” to companies or individuals.
If Trammell’s order requiring normal property taxes is upheld, it could call other big tax breaks into question and keep officials from using the tool in the future.
The state also plans to spend $200 million to buy the site and prepare it.
Rivian could claim a projected $200 million income tax credit.
Rivian could claim $280 million in sales tax breaks on machinery and construction materials.
The state also plans to spend $90 million to build a job training center and train workers.

The judge found persuasive the arguments of a group of local residents who oppose development of the plant, saying it will spoil their quality of life in a rural area that Atlanta's sprawl is now encroaching upon.

JoEllen Artz, president of opposition group Morgan Land, Sky & Water Preservation, said: “It is very fulfilling that we local citizens were able to band together to do so much research in order to bring a great legal team on board and deliver us fantastic results like these.”
Artz and other opponents intervened in the lawsuit to question the appropriateness of the tax break deal proposed by a four-county joint development authority that helped recruit Rivian.
The company has encountered difficulties in ramping up production in Illinois.
Rivian's once soaring stock price has tumbled with some key investors dumping shares.
Trammell wrote that local and state officials appeared not to have considered the higher costs of services that local governments would incur.
Trammell also questioned whether Rivian had the money to complete the project.
Trammell wrote: “Rivian's cash reserves are quickly drying up, thus casting serious doubt on whether it will be able to commence, let alone complete the project.
source_title: Georgia judge nixes tax break for electric truck firm Rivian | The Independent

facts: Development of the Rivian factory near Social Circle east of Atlanta is underway again after a pause in construction last year.
Construction crews are scheduled to break ground on the Georgia facility in 2026.
The first vehicles from the Georgia factory are expected to roll off the production line in 2028.
Georgia offered Rivian $1.5 billion in incentives to develop the 2,000-square-foot factory and promote economic development.
The deal with the state requires the company to deliver 80% of its promised jobs and investments by 2030.
Georgia will produce the new R2 and R3 midsize SUV models at the facility.
Production of the R2 began at Rivian’s existing plant in Normal, Illinois, to make the cars available to consumers more quickly.
Rivian spokesperson Peebles Squire said the company is anticipating the new car will attract new buyers.

Peebles Squire stated: “We're opening up the Rivian brand to a whole new group of folks with a much more competitively priced vehicle that sits like right in that midsize SUV segment, and hopefully continues to invite people to make the switch to electric and really experience the brand firsthand.”
Like many auto manufacturers, U.S.-based Rivian is waiting to see what impacts tariffs will have on the industry overall.
Peebles Squire said the stated goals of the Trump administration support the company's current strategy.
Peebles Squire stated: “The narrative here is all really about ensuring those jobs, bringing U.S. manufacturing back, and we're certainly aligned with the administration on that.”
The Georgia project aims to create 7,500 jobs by 2030.
Rivian recently opened a showroom in Alpharetta where consumers can see vehicles in person.
source_title: Georgia Rivian factory development continues as auto industry prepares for tariff impacts | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: Autonomous vehicles are expected to hit the road by 2025.
Autonomous vehicles will function more as public transport environments rather than privately owned machines.
The business model for autonomous vehicles will shift significantly toward predictive maintenance compared to traditional cars and trucks.
OEMs may redefine themselves as manufacturers of the vehicle environment, with separate entities owning the operational environment.
Tier-1, tier-2, and tier-3 suppliers will need to deliver more data for prognostics but will maintain their existing positions in the supply chain relative to the vehicle maker.
A major challenge for predictive maintenance is processing the massive volume of generated data without enough engineers to review it manually.
Smart data processing environments are required to filter out 90% of raw data and isolate component health metrics for targeted maintenance triggers.
Maintenance will be triggered only when a component risks breaking down, performance drops, or fuel efficiency decreases.
ISO and SAE have stepped up standardization efforts for connectivity and system architecture, moving away from isolated regional initiatives toward international committee collaboration.
The industry is on the right track for standardization after years of misunderstandings between OEMs and suppliers.
OBD served as the first diagnostic system in vehicles, establishing foundational methodologies for handling large-scale Type I and Type II errors (false PASS and false FAIL results).
Modern diagnostic systems are adopting lessons learned from OBD regarding statistical application and data communication to end users.
Safety diagnostics and security diagnosis face similar underlying problems and rely on methods comparable to prognostic predictive environments.
source_title: Roben Automotive on autonomous vehicles

facts: South Korean battery manufacturer SK On has announced significant layoffs at its Georgia-based battery plant due to a decline in electric vehicle (EV) demand.
SK On's U.S. subsidiary, SK Battery America Inc., terminated 968 employees at its Commerce, Georgia facility.
The reduction represents approximately 37% of the plant's total workforce of around 2,500 employees.
The company attributes the layoffs to a slowdown in EV sales and changing market conditions, which have necessitated restructuring efforts.
The layoffs were disclosed through a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) filed on a Georgia state website.
source_title: SK On Lays Off Nearly 1,000 Workers at Georgia Battery Plant Ami

facts: On May 31, 2025, Hyundai Motor Group inaugurated its highly anticipated Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Savannah, Georgia.
The $7.6 billion facility is one of the largest of its kind in the nation.
It is central to Hyundai’s goal of expanding its EV footprint across North America.
Located approximately 20 miles from the Port of Savannah, the plant will manufacture a range of EVs and plug-in hybrids for Hyundai, its luxury brand Genesis, and affiliate Kia.
The facility is expected to ramp up production quickly and play a pivotal role in Hyundai’s push toward a fully electrified vehicle lineup.
The HMGMA is designed to be one of the most technologically sophisticated automotive plants in the world.
The complex incorporates state-of-the-art automation, including nearly 300 Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) that move components seamlessly through the production line.

With an initial annual output target of 300,000 vehicles, the company has since raised its goal to 500,000 units, driven by strong demand forecasts and the strategic need to reduce reliance on overseas manufacturing.
In addition to the main assembly line, the plant includes a Hyundai Steel production facility on-site, allowing for just-in-time steel delivery and reduced logistics costs.
Plans are also underway to construct a battery production facility in partnership with LG, aiming to ensure supply chain security and streamline EV battery integration.

This vertically integrated model enhances operational efficiency and supports Hyundai’s broader mission to produce vehicles with a smaller carbon footprint while meeting growing consumer demand for EVs.
The new Metaplant is poised to become a key driver of economic growth in Georgia.
It is projected to create more than 1,300 full-time jobs, with most roles intended for local residents.
Hyundai has been working closely with workforce development programs and local community colleges to train new employees and build a skilled labor pipeline.

The facility is a core part of Hyundai’s broader $21 billion investment in the United States, which also includes other manufacturing hubs, research centers, and product innovation labs across the country.
Notably, Kia’s $3.2 billion facility in West Point, Georgia further complements Hyundai’s strategy to establish a robust presence in the Southeast U.S.

Local leaders have praised the move as a major win for Georgia’s manufacturing industry and a demonstration of the state’s increasing attractiveness to global innovators in clean energy and technology.
The decision to invest in a massive U.S.-based EV production facility comes amid growing trade complexities, particularly rising tariffs on imported steel, aluminum, and finished vehicles.
By establishing a local manufacturing footprint, Hyundai aims to mitigate the impact of international trade tensions and ensure pricing stability for its North American customers.
The localized supply chain also shortens delivery times and allows the company to adapt more quickly to market changes, regulatory shifts, and consumer preferences.

Moreover, the facility enhances Hyundai’s ability to comply with evolving U.S. incentives for domestic EV production, such as tax credits tied to North American assembly under federal clean energy policies.
The Savannah Metaplant symbolizes a broader trend reshaping the Southern U.S. economy—the rise of clean tech and advanced manufacturing.
States like Georgia, Alabama, and Tennessee are quickly becoming epicenters for EV production thanks to strategic infrastructure, competitive incentives, and a skilled workforce.

With other automakers and battery firms also investing heavily in the region, Hyundai’s entry adds momentum to what some are calling the “Battery Belt”—a corridor of innovation set to transform the nation’s transportation future.
source_title: Hyundai Opens $7.6B Electric Vehicle Metaplant in Georgia - The Leader Report

facts: GeoDigital International Inc. will move its North American headquarters from Canada to Atlanta.
The relocation will create approximately 50 new high-tech jobs in Fulton County over the next year.
GeoDigital has been based in Ontario since its founding in 2005.
The company will occupy an 11,200-square-foot, state-of-the-art office space in Atlantic Station.
GDEcD Commissioner Chris Carr stated that Georgia is a hub for headquarter relocations, especially for technology companies.
Since the beginning of the fiscal year (which concluded not even three weeks ago), GDEcD has announced ten corporate headquarter relocations and more than 4,200 technology jobs in Georgia.

The newly created jobs will encompass executive management, information technology, research and development, sales, marketing, project management, finance and accounting, and human resources functions.
GeoDigital plans to work with Kennesaw State University and the Georgia Institute of Technology to create apprenticeship programs to help fill future employment opportunities.
GeoDigital operates at the forefront of the fast-growing autonomous, or self-driving, vehicle industry.

The company uses precision technology called LiDAR, a kind of ‘laser range finder’, combined with high-definition imagery to create the industry’s most detailed 3D maps of the entire North American highway system.
These ‘digital roads’ include data on topography, road surfaces, lanes, and other information critical to the safe and smooth driving experience of autonomous cars.
GeoDigital is providing these mapping services to a major automaker, as well as several Tier 1 suppliers.
The company is also using this technology to help efficiently inspect thousands of miles of power lines for asset and vegetation issues.
GeoDigital serves the vast majority of the country’s largest utility companies, including PPL, Xcel Energy, Tennessee Valley Authority, and many Southern Company entities.
By digitizing vegetation management, utility companies can better predict and respond to areas at risk of causing outages and better inform their crews while keeping them safer.
GeoDigital solutions assist with compliancy with recent federal regulation to protect the nation’s power grid against vegetation outages.

Chris Warrington, president and CEO of GeoDigital, stated that Atlanta meets their needs for greater access to high-tech talent and international travel routes due to the company's growth and market potential.
Shirlynn Brownell, GDEcD project manager, assisted GeoDigital on behalf of Georgia and partnered with the Metro Atlanta Chamber and Georgia Power.

Brian P. McGowan, executive vice president and chief operating officer for the Metro Atlanta Chamber, welcomed GeoDigital to the growing base of international companies choosing Atlanta as an operating hub.
Founded in 2005, GeoDigital operates in the United States, Canada, and Australia.
Its investors include NGP Energy Technology Partners, North Sky Capital, EnerTech Capital and Emerald Technology Ventures.
source_title: GA: GeoDigital to Move from Canada to Atlanta, Hire 50 for High-Tech Jobs | Trade and Industry Development

entity_name: EV Battery Cooling Plate Material Market
canonical_name: EV Battery Cooling Plate Material Market
facts: The global EV Battery Cooling Plate Material market size reached USD 2.35 billion in 2024.
The market is projected to expand at a CAGR of 13.8% from 2025 to 2033.
The forecasted market size is USD 7.41 billion by 2033.
Market growth is driven by the rapid electrification of transportation, stringent government regulations on emissions, and advancements in battery thermal management systems.
The industry is shifting towards high-performance, lightweight, and thermally efficient materials to meet next-generation EV battery demands.
Exponential rise in EV production and sales in China, Europe, and the United States is a primary growth factor.
Advanced battery cooling solutions are necessary to enhance battery longevity, safety, and performance as energy densities increase.
Automakers and battery manufacturers are focusing on materials offering superior thermal conductivity, corrosion resistance, and lightweight properties.
Technological advancements in battery chemistry and architecture are directly influencing the evolution of cooling plate materials.
High-capacity lithium-ion and solid-state batteries lead to higher operating temperatures, escalating the need for robust thermal management systems.
Material scientists and manufacturers are investing heavily in R&D to engineer new alloys, composites, and hybrid materials.
Stringent regulatory frameworks and government incentives for clean mobility are accelerating the transition towards electric vehicles.
Asia Pacific remains the dominant market, accounting for over 45% of the global market share in 2024.
APAC leadership is attributed to major EV manufacturers, extensive battery production infrastructure, and supportive government policies in China, Japan, and South Korea.
Europe follows closely, driven by ambitious emission reduction targets and a rapidly expanding EV ecosystem.
North America is witnessing substantial growth underpinned by increasing investments in EV production and battery manufacturing.
Aluminum has emerged as the most widely used material due to excellent thermal conductivity, lightweight properties, and cost-effectiveness.
Aluminum is extensively preferred by OEMs for mass-market EVs, striking an optimal balance between performance and affordability.
Rising demand for high-energy-density batteries solidifies aluminum's position for efficient heat dissipation without adding significant weight.
Manufacturers are exploring advanced aluminum alloys and surface treatments to enhance corrosion resistance and mechanical strength.
Copper offers superior thermal conductivity but is typically utilized in premium or high-performance EV applications due to higher cost and weight.
Ongoing miniaturization and densification of battery packs are creating niche opportunities for copper-based cooling plates in sports cars and luxury EVs.
Stainless steel is valued for exceptional durability and corrosion resistance, making it suitable for harsh environmental conditions where longevity is paramount.
Composite materials are rapidly gaining traction as lightweight, high-strength alternatives, including carbon fiber-reinforced polymers.
Composites offer reduced weight and tailored thermal properties, particularly in next-generation EVs and high-performance vehicles.
The "Others" category includes ceramic-based plates and hybrid materials addressing extreme thermal loads, chemical compatibility with battery electrolytes, and integration with smart sensors.
Liquid cooling remains the dominant technology due to superior heat transfer capabilities and ability to maintain uniform battery temperatures under high-load conditions.
Liquid cooling systems utilize a network of channels and plates for coolant circulation, enabling higher charging rates, improved battery longevity, and enhanced safety.
Air cooling is favored for simplicity, lower cost, and ease of maintenance in entry-level or compact EVs where thermal loads are manageable.
Phase change cooling leverages phase change materials (PCMs) to absorb and dissipate heat through latent heat transfer, offering passive thermal management and compact system design.
Lithium-ion batteries currently dominate the battery type segment, accounting for over 80% of EV battery installations in 2024.
Li-ion batteries are sensitive to temperature fluctuations, necessitating advanced cooling plate materials to prevent thermal runaway, enhance performance, and extend battery life.
Rapid evolution of lithium-ion chemistries like NMC and LFP is influencing the selection of cooling plate materials for compatibility and optimal thermal performance.
Solid-state batteries represent the next frontier with higher energy densities, improved safety, and faster charging, requiring specialized cooling plate materials tailored to specific requirements.
Nickel-metal hydride (NiMH) batteries continue use in certain hybrid models, generally more tolerant of temperature variations but still benefiting from effective thermal management.
Passenger vehicles constitute the largest application segment driven by mass adoption of electric cars in developed and emerging markets.
Commercial vehicles including electric buses, trucks, and delivery vans represent a rapidly growing application area for battery cooling plate materials.
Commercial EVs feature larger battery packs and operate under more demanding duty cycles, making efficient cooling systems essential for maintaining battery health and minimizing downtime.
OEMs constitute the dominant distribution channel, accounting for over 75% of market share in 2024.
Aftermarket segment is gaining traction as the global EV fleet expands and the need for maintenance, repair, and replacement of battery cooling components increases.
Market threats include volatility of raw material prices for metals like aluminum and copper, which can impact supplier profitability.
Complexity of integrating new materials and technologies into existing manufacturing processes poses technical and operational challenges.
Asia Pacific market size reached USD 1.06 billion in 2024, accounting for approximately 45% of global revenues.
China is the world's largest EV market with aggressive electrification targets and significant investments in battery manufacturing infrastructure.
Europe market size reached USD 0.68 billion in 2024, fueled by stringent emission regulations and ambitious electrification targets.
North America market size reached USD 0.41 billion in 2024, supported by increasing investments in EV production and charging infrastructure.

The competitive landscape features intense rivalry among major companies investing heavily in R&D for new materials with enhanced thermal performance, lightweight properties, and environmental sustainability.

Major companies operating in the market include Constellium SE, Norsk Hydro ASA, Novelis Inc., UACJ Corporation, Gränges AB, Capchem Technology, SGL Carbon SE, DuPont de Nemours, Inc., 3M Company, and Mitsubishi Materials Corporation.
Constellium SE and Norsk Hydro ASA are leading suppliers of high-performance aluminum solutions.
SGL Carbon SE and 3M Company are renowned for expertise in advanced composites and thermal management materials.
Novelis Inc. and UACJ Corporation leverage global manufacturing capabilities to supply OEMs and battery manufacturers worldwide.
Capchem Technology and Mitsubishi Materials Corporation are investing in the development of next-generation materials for emerging battery technologies.

Key trends include the shift toward lightweight and high-performance materials, increased R&D in composites and hybrid materials, digitalization of manufacturing, and adoption of eco-friendly solutions.
source_title: EV Battery Cooling Plate Material Market Research Report 2033

entity_name: Georgia Electric Vehicle Market and Policy Landscape
canonical_name: Georgia Electric Vehicle (EV) Market and Policy Framework
facts: Union of Concerned Scientists released a statement about the benefits of driving on electricity to the State of Georgia.
In 2014 alone, Georgia drivers saved 4.5 million gallons of gasoline thanks to electric vehicles.
This savings provided an extra $10 million to put back into the local economy.
A UCS analysis shows driving 100 miles in the average conventional vehicle costs a Georgia driver $13.57.
Driving those same 100 miles in an electric vehicle costs $3.53 or less.
The UCS analysis emphasizes the importance of forward-thinking policies to expand EV access in Georgia.
Georgia is a national leader in electric vehicles due to strong state policies.
Georgia has the second-highest number of electric vehicles on the road in the country.
Atlanta passed Seattle to become the second-biggest metro area for electric vehicles.
Joshua Goldman, lead policy analyst for UCS, states EVs are cheaper to fuel, cleaner to operate, and benefit Georgia drivers.
Goldman notes Georgia has been a pioneer in putting electric cars on the road.
Electricity rates in Georgia have been relatively stable for years while gas prices rise and fall.
Electric vehicles protect drivers from future changes in gas prices.
Anne Blair, Clean Fuels Director for Southern Alliance for Clean Energy, expresses encouragement over EV progress.
Blair hopes Georgia leaders recognize the importance of EVs and what a standout Georgia is on adopting them.
Georgia has thousands of electric vehicles on the road but still has room for growth.
Policies encouraging consumers to choose EVs will continue to pay dividends for Georgians.
The $5,000 ZEV Georgia State Income Tax Credit faced pressure to be killed, saved, or transformed.
All three options were debated at the State Capitol during the 2015 Georgia State Legislative Session.
HB 176 sponsored by Tommy Benton (R-Jefferson) seeks to broaden the tax credit to include gas-hybrids, high mileage cars, and future hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicles.
The annual cap for HB 176 is $10 million.
A $2,000 credit only applies to Hydrogen Fuel Cell vehicles in 2017 and beyond under HB 176.
The House Transportation Committee passed HB 170 bill which includes language from HB 122 to eliminate the EV Tax Credit as part of a transportation funding proposal.
HB 170 also assesses EV drivers a $200.00/year road use fee.
All three bills in the Ways and Means Committee were expected to be addressed next week.
2014 smashed the 100,000 annual unit sales mark for EVs in November.
More new EV models launched in 2014 than in the prior 3 years combined.
Electrified vehicles were expected to reach 1% of vehicles sold nationally in 2015.
Projections estimated 165-170,000 electrified vehicles added to US roads in 2015 based on total industry sales of 16.5-17.0 million.
Many states added electric vehicle or charging station incentives.
Expanded charging infrastructure and high satisfaction rates among EV owners should propel EV sales growth close to 1%.
Nissan LEAF approaches the 200,000 Federal Tax Credit phase out threshold.
Nissan launched the all-electric LEAF in the US in 2011 and did the best job marketing it.
By the end of 2015, cumulative LEAF sales were close to 170,000, just 30,000 units shy of phase out requirements.
An all-new vehicle was slated to launch in the 2017 Model Year, on sale as early as January 2016.
Carlos Ghosn had a plan for marketing the all-new LEAF without its $7,500 tax credit.
Tesla was expected to FINALLY launch the Model X with Falcon Wing Doors in 2015.
Elon Musk was expected to announce first deliveries of the 2015 Model X on the Tesla blog around Merry Christmas 2015.
The Model X targets the high end 6-7 passenger SUV market and will begin to impact Tesla sales in 2016.
The Model X attracts new buyers to the Tesla marque without hurting Model S sales, as most owners already traded up to the Model D.
Savvy used car buyers were expected to snap up discarded Model S 1.0 offerings at reasonable ($50,000 – $60,000) prices.
Elon Musk promised a future battery upgrade for the Model S.
Software upgrades continue for the Model S.
Chevrolet VOLT 2.0 was expected to be a lower cost version than VOLT 1.0.
Former GM CEO Dan Akerson pushed to get $10,000 of cost out of the VOLT for the 2.0 version.
The 2015 Chevrolet Cruze provided an idea of what the 2016 VOLT would look like: compact.
Range may improve modestly to 45-50 electric miles.
The vehicle was expected to have a $29,995 base price with useable gauges and a 3 person “Cruze Sized” rear seat.
Unveiling was expected at the NAIAS the week of January 12, 2015.
There was hope for using more than 60% of the 17.1kWh battery.
Georgia and metro Atlanta garnered headlines in 2014 as the fastest growing EV market in the US.
The handling of the current ZEV/LEV tax credit (currently $5,000/$2,500 with no sunset) was expected to become a national story.

Nissan was expected to have its best sales month ever in December 2014 for the LEAF given metro Atlanta dealers were selling/leasing against fear that the $5,000 ZEV tax credit would disappear in 2015.
Metro Atlanta/Georgia needs more EVs on roads to help get annual CO2 emissions well below the 150 million metric tons emitted in Georgia.
"PV2EV" (tying EV charging to renewable power generation) was expected to have its day in the sun in 2015.
Separate and uncoordinated incentives between solar power and EV charging station infrastructure kept these technologies apart for years.
2015 might see enough solar powered charging stations built to move the needle toward sustainable PV2EV deployment.
Solar power costs were falling while the cost to retrofit parking lots and garages with EV charging stations remained expensive.
Georgia was expected to become a leader in electric vehicle charging infrastructure.
NRG pushed into the metro Atlanta market from its home base in Houston TX.
Georgia Power announced construction of its own charging islands.
Both firms offered DC Fast Charge and 240 V Level 2 charging.
Metro Atlanta was expected to become a model for deployment of fast and convenient EV charging by the end of 2015.
Metro Atlanta was expected to have at least 50 charging islands including installations in Athens and other outlying cities by the end of 2015.
Public charging stations funded by GEFA were expected to improve EV charging station availability in the Atlanta suburbs.
A legislator asked what Georgia received for the almost $15 million invested in electric cars via the tax credit.
The response may determine whether Georgia’s $5000 ZEV income tax credit lives or dies.

According to the Georgia Department of Economic Development, for every one percent of petroleum-based miles travelled in Georgia displaced by EVs, approximately $201 million dollars remains in the state annually.
Each pure electric vehicle purchased keeps $2,242 annually in the state of Georgia by fueling with electricity rather than petroleum-based products.
The tax credit received comes back as a refund after filing taxes and gets spent on clothes, appliances, and services in Georgia, creating a multiplier effect.
Georgia Power set up a special tariff called Plug In Electric Vehicle Time of Use Rate Plan to encourage overnight charging when power is super cheap and plentiful.
A study of 1000 Georgia electric car owners on the PEV rate plan showed they save $180 per year by shifting usage to the evening and overnight period.
This helps utilize extra electric capacity available overnight.
During the day, electric vehicles and equipment are quiet, clean, and efficient, offering users opportunities to save money on fuel and maintenance costs and reduce environmental impact.
Atlanta was out of compliance with the EPA rule, and the metro area needed help to attain the standard and save everyone the cost of an emission sticker.
Gasoline or diesel engines deteriorate over time, leading to higher emissions with vehicle age, whereas EVs potentially get cleaner over time as electricity generation gets cleaner.
Electric cars send a message to young people about regional priorities.
Mayor Reed stated the investment sent a strong message to millennials and makes Atlanta a more livable city drawing talented young people back to live and work.
He likened it to the Beltline and other quality of life projects.
Georgia is the 2nd largest market in the US for all EVs behind Nissan's success with the LEAF.
BMW, Kia, and many other manufacturers are coming with electric cars.
The recommended message to the legislature was to hold off for another year before taking action on the tax credit.
If eliminating the credit, it should be done slowly and phased out over the next decade.
Georgia's business climate is great in part because it avoids knee-jerk regulations causing uncertainty and confusion in the marketplace.

links.context: Facts on benefits of driving on electricity in Georgia

source_title: #cleanair | Atlanta Electric Vehicle Development Coalition

facts: Oscar (Ohchung) Kwon, president and CEO of Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA), will retire at the end of August after 33 years with Hyundai Motor Company.
Kwon oversaw the establishment of HMGMA's new manufacturing plant and initiated automobile production in October 2024 with the Hyundai IONIQ 5.
Production continued in March with the addition of the Hyundai IONIQ 9.
Hyundai Motor Company appointed Tony Heo as the new president and CEO of HMGMA, effective September 2.
Heo joined Hyundai Motor Company in 1995 and has demonstrated leadership in manufacturing operations, strategic planning, and production coordination.
Since 2021, Heo has served as executive coordinator of production at Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama.
Heo was on the team that selected the Ellabell, GA megasite as the location for HMGMA.
Heo majored in mechanical engineering at Pusan National University in Busan, South Korea.
Chris Susock, Chief Manufacturing Officer of Hyundai Motor North America, stated that Kwon's leadership laid the foundation for the future of the Metaplant.
Susock expressed excitement about welcoming Heo to the "Meta Pro family" at HMGMA.
HMGMA is Hyundai Motor Group's first dedicated electrified vehicle mass-production plant, located in Bryan County, GA.
The plant features a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that connects all elements of the EV ecosystem to realize customer value.
The Georgia facility will become an intelligent manufacturing plant.
All production processes—including order collection, procurement, logistics, and production—are optimized utilizing AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system creates a human-centered work environment where robots assist human workers.
source_title: HMGMA Announces Leadership Transition - HMGMA

facts: Hyundai Motor Company is aware of the immigration enforcement action that took place at the construction site of its supplier, the HL-GA Battery Company in Bryan County, Georgia.
Hyundai is closely monitoring the situation while working to understand the specific circumstances.
Based on current understanding, none of those detained are directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company.
Hyundai is committed to full compliance with all laws and regulations in every market where it operates, including employment verification requirements and immigration laws.
Hyundai expects the same commitment from all partners, suppliers, contractors, and subcontractors.
The safety and well-being of everyone working at Hyundai sites, whether directly employed or working for suppliers, is a top priority.
Incidents like this remind Hyundai of the importance of robust oversight throughout its entire supply chain and contractor network.
Hyundai is reviewing its processes to ensure all parties working on its projects maintain high standards of legal compliance.
The review includes thorough vetting of employment practices by contractors and subcontractors.
North America Chief Manufacturing Officer Chris Susock will now assume governance of the entire megasite in Georgia.
Hyundai will conduct an investigation to ensure all suppliers and their subcontractors comply with all laws and regulations.
Hyundai has zero tolerance for those who don’t follow the law.
Hyundai continues to invest in American manufacturing and create thousands of jobs in full accordance with U.S. law.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Company Statement on Immigration Enforcement Action at Supplier Facility - HMGMA
